topics.title: Introduction to refrigeration and air conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATIONS AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to refrigeration and air conditioning # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Refrigeration? **Refrigeration** is the science and art of removing heat from a substance, space, or body to lower its temperature below that of the surrounding atmosphere and maintaining that lower temperature. It is fundamentally a process of *heat transfer from a low-temperature region to a high-temperature region*, which cannot occur spontaneously (this is precisely what the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** forbids without external work input). > [!IMPORTANT] KTU Board Definition > **Refrigeration** is defined as the process of cooling a substance or space to a temperature lower than that of its surroundings, and maintaining that temperature, by the continuous removal of heat from the body or space. ## 1.2 What is Air Conditioning? **Air Conditioning (A/C)** is the broader science of controlling the *temperature, humidity, purity (cleanliness), and distribution/motion* of air within an enclosed space to meet the comfort requirements of occupants or the process requirements of industry. > [!IMPORTANT] KTU Board Definition > **Air Conditioning** is the simultaneous control of temperature, humidity, air cleanliness, and air circulation to provide a conditioned space for human comfort or industrial process needs. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy **Refrigeration = Just "Cooling" (like your home fridge)** Imagine holding an ice cube in your hand. The cube absorbs heat from your palm and melts, leaving your hand feeling cold. Refrigeration does the *opposite* — it uses a working fluid (refrigerant) to repeatedly **absorb heat from the inside of a closed space and dump it outside** using mechanical work. Think of a water pump, but instead of pumping water uphill, you are "pumping" heat uphill (from cold to hot), which is impossible without work input. **Air Conditioning = Complete "Climate Control" (like a car's climate system)** If refrigeration is just making something cold, A/C is like the *complete climate butler*. It does FOUR things at once: - **T**emperature control (cooling OR heating) - **H**umidity control (dehumidification or humidification) - **C**leanliness (filtration of dust, pollen) - **D**istribution (fan/blower + ducting) A simple example: your window A/C unit at home does refrigeration (cooling) **PLUS** removes moisture (you can see water dripping from the outdoor unit — that is the **latent heat** of the moist indoor air being condensed out). ## 1.4 Unit of Refrigeration — **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** The standard commercial unit is the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**. > [!NOTE] > **1 Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR)** is defined as the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb ≈ 907.185 kg)** of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in **24 hours**. > > Numerically: **1 TR = 3.517 kW ≈ 211 kJ/min = 12,000 BTU/hr** The latent heat of fusion of water at 0 °C is taken as **335 kJ/kg** (often rounded to **2326 kJ per ton** for the imperial ton, or **335 kJ/kg × 907.185 kg/ton = 303,800 kJ per 24 hours = 3.517 kW**). ## 1.5 Why does Refrigeration Matter in Engineering? > [!NOTE] Engineering Applications Snapshot > - **Preservation:** Cold storage of food, vaccines, blood banks. > - **Comfort:** Building A/C, vehicle A/C. > - **Industry:** Liquefaction of gases (LNG, LOX, LAr), cryogenics. > - **Manufacturing:** Ice rinks, plastic mould cooling, chemical process chilling. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Refrigeration Systems Refrigeration systems are broadly classified based on the *principle of heat removal*. This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark/3-mark question**. | # | Type | Sub-Types | Common Application | |---|------|-----------|---------------------| | 1 | **Mechanical (Vapour Compression)** | Domestic, commercial, industrial chillers | Domestic fridge, building A/C | | 2 | **Vapour Absorption** | LiBr-water (for A/C), Ammonia-water (for industrial cooling) | Industrial cold storage, large A/C plants | | 3 | **Gas Cycle (Vapour Compression with gas / Air Refrigeration)** | Bell Coleman cycle, Air-standard cycle | Aircraft A/C, very low-temperature applications | | 4 | **Thermoelectric (Peltier Effect)** | Solid-state heat pump | Portable coolers, electronic chip cooling | | 5 | **Steam Jet (Ejector)** | Uses high-pressure steam | Industrial chilling above 4 °C | | 6 | **Cryogenic** | Linde-Hampson, Claude cycle | Liquefaction of gases | ## 2.2 Comparison: Heat Pump vs. Refrigeration System This is an **extremely common KTU question** because both devices use the same hardware, just the **objective differs**. > [!IMPORTANT] Key Difference > - **Refrigerator:** Objective is to *cool a low-temperature space* and reject heat to the surroundings. > - **Heat Pump:** Objective is to *heat a high-temperature space* by taking heat from a low-temperature source. The cold side is the *source*; the hot side is the *useful* output. **In a Heat Pump**, the *useful effect* is the heat rejected to the high-temperature body (Q_H). **In a Refrigerator**, the *useful effect* is the heat extracted from the low-temperature body (Q_L). ## 2.3 Coefficient of Performance (COP) **COP is the "efficiency" of a refrigeration/heat-pump system**, defined as the ratio of desired effect to work input. For a **Refrigerator**: $$COP_{Ref} = \frac{Desired\; Effect}{Work\; Input} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$ For a **Heat Pump**: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Desired\; Effect}{Work\; Input} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$$ ## 2.4 Relationship between COP_HP and COP_Ref Applying the **First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Balance)** to the cycle: $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net,in}$$ Rearranging: $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W} = \frac{Q_L + W}{W} = \frac{Q_L}{W} + 1$$ Therefore: $$COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1$$ > [!NOTE] Syllabus Highlight > This relationship is **frequently tested as a 7-mark derivation problem** in KTU Module 1. Students must memorize: $COP_{HP} > COP_{Ref}$ always, and **$COP_{Ref}$ can NEVER exceed** the value derived from the Carnot cycle. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | 1 TR (in kW) | $1\;TR = 3.517\;kW$ | kW | Standard SI conversion | | 2 | 1 TR (heat in 24 h) | $Q = m \times h_{fg}$ with m = 907.185 kg, $h_{fg}$ = 335 kJ/kg | kJ/24 h | Latent heat of fusion of water | | 3 | 1 TR (in BTU/hr) | $1\;TR = 12{,}000\;BTU/hr$ | BTU/hr | Imperial unit | | 4 | COP of Refrigerator | $COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | dimensionless | Always greater than 1 in practice | | 5 | COP of Heat Pump | $COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ | dimensionless | Always greater than 1 | | 6 | Relationship | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | dimensionless | From 1st Law of Thermodynamics | | 7 | Carnot COP (Refrigerator) | $COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Theoretical maximum | | 8 | Carnot COP (Heat Pump) | $COP_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Theoretical maximum | | 9 | Rate of heat removal | $Q_L = m \times (h_{fg}) / t$ | kW | Mass × latent heat / time | | 10 | Energy to power | $P = \dot{Q}_L / COP_R$ | kW | Compressor power required | > [!NOTE] Real-World Utility > The COP relationship is the reason a **heat pump is more efficient as a heater** than direct electrical resistance heating. An electric heater has an "efficiency" of 1 (1 kW of electricity gives 1 kW of heat), whereas a heat pump with COP = 4 gives 4 kW of heat per 1 kW of electricity. This is why heat pumps are mandated for green buildings. ## 2.6 Performance Benchmarks | Metric | Typical Real Value | Carnot Limit (T_L = 5 °C, T_H = 35 °C) | |--------|---------------------|-------------------------------------------| | Domestic Fridge | 2.0 – 3.5 | 8.97 | | Central A/C | 3.0 – 4.5 | 8.97 | | Industrial Chiller | 4.0 – 6.0 | 8.97 | | Heat Pump (heating) | 3.0 – 5.0 | 9.97 | The **Carnot COP** is calculated below for a typical 30 K lift (cold side at 278 K, hot side at 308 K): $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{278}{30} \approx 9.27$$ # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: 1 Ton of Refrigeration in kW (with full rigor) **Given Data:** - Mass of water to be frozen in 24 hours: $m = 1\;ton = 907.185\;kg$ (US short ton) - Initial and final temperature of water: $0\;°C$ (latent heat removal only) - Latent heat of fusion of water at 0 °C: $h_{fg} = 335\;kJ/kg$ **Step 1 — Total heat to be removed (Q_total):** The heat removed is the latent heat released when water freezes. $$Q_{total} = m \times h_{fg}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values:** $$Q_{total} = 907.185\;kg \times 335\;kJ/kg$$ **Step 3 — Numerical evaluation:** $$Q_{total} = 303{,}907\;kJ \;(\text{over 24 hours})$$ **Step 4 — Convert to rate (heat removal per second):** $$1\;day = 24\;hr = 24 \times 3600\;s = 86{,}400\;s$$ $$P = \frac{Q_{total}}{t} = \frac{303{,}907\;kJ}{86{,}400\;s}$$ **Step 5 — Final value:** $$P = 3.517\;kW$$ Therefore, **1 TR = 3.517 kW**. This is the universally accepted conversion used in all KTU problems. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship between $COP_{HP}$ and $COP_R$ **Energy balance (Steady-state, closed system) for a cyclic device:** By the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, for a cyclic device operating between a low-temperature reservoir (LTR) and a high-temperature reservoir (HTR), energy in = energy out over one cycle. $$W_{net,in} + Q_L = Q_H$$ **Step 1 — Rearrange:** $$Q_H - Q_L = W_{net,in}$$ **Step 2 — Definition of COP for Refrigerator:** $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$ This means: $Q_L = COP_R \times W_{net,in}$ **Step 3 — Definition of COP for Heat Pump:** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net,in}}$$ This means: $Q_H = COP_{HP} \times W_{net,in}$ **Step 4 — Subtract the two expressions:** $$Q_H - Q_L = COP_{HP} \cdot W - COP_R \cdot W = (COP_{HP} - COP_R) \cdot W$$ **Step 5 — Equate to the energy balance:** $$(COP_{HP} - COP_R) \cdot W = W$$ **Step 6 — Final relationship (dividing both sides by W):** $$COP_{HP} - COP_R = 1$$ $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$ > [!NOTE] Insight for Students > This is a **purely thermodynamic result** and holds for *any* refrigeration/heat-pump cycle, not just Carnot. It tells us that $COP_{HP}$ is always exactly 1 unit higher than $COP_R$ for the same device and the same temperature limits. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Carnot COP for a Refrigerator The **Carnot (reversible) cycle** sets the upper limit for the COP. Operating between $T_L$ (in Kelvin) and $T_H$ (in Kelvin): **Step 1 — From the definition:** $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net,in}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S$ and $Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S$** (reversible adiabatic processes yield equal entropy changes for the two isotherms): $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S}$$ **Step 3 — Cancel $\Delta S$:** $$\boxed{COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ Similarly, **Carnot COP for Heat Pump**: $$COP_{HP,Carnot} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Step 4 — Verify the +1 relationship using Carnot values:** $$COP_{HP,Carnot} - COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H - T_L} = 1$$ **Verified!** ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** A domestic refrigerator has a cooling capacity of 240 kJ/min and consumes 1.2 kW of electrical power. Calculate (a) COP of the refrigerator, and (b) COP of the heat pump operating between the same temperature limits. Also find (c) the heat rejected to the surroundings per minute. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert Q_L to kW:** $$Q_L = 240\;kJ/min = \frac{240}{60}\;kJ/s = 4\;kW$$ **Step 2 — COP of Refrigerator:** $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W} = \frac{4}{1.2} = 3.33$$ **Step 3 — COP of Heat Pump:** $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 3.33 + 1 = 4.33$$ **Step 4 — Heat rejected to surroundings per minute:** By energy balance: $Q_H = Q_L + W$ $$Q_H = 4 + 1.2 = 5.2\;kW = 5.2 \times 60 = 312\;kJ/min$$ **Answers: (a) 3.33, (b) 4.33, (c) 312 kJ/min** ✓ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Refrigeration System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[LOW TEMP SPACE
Evaporator Side
Q_L extracted] --> B[EVAPORATOR
Low Pressure
Refrigerant absorbs heat] B --> C[COMPRESSOR
Work Input W_in
Low P to High P] C --> D[CONDENSER
High Pressure
Refrigerant rejects Q_H] D --> E[EXPANSION DEVICE
Throttle Valve
High P to Low P] E --> B D --> F[HIGH TEMP SURROUNDINGS
Q_H rejected to environment] A -.Cold Space.-> G[REFRIGERATED ZONE
Maintained at T_L] ``` ## 4.2 Classification Flow Diagram of Refrigeration Systems ```mermaid flowchart TD A[REFRIGERATION SYSTEMS] --> B[Mechanical] A --> C[Non Mechanical] B --> B1[Vapour Compression Cycle] B --> B2[Gas Cycle - Air Refrigeration] C --> D[Thermoelectric - Peltier] C --> E[Vapour Absorption] C --> F[Steam Jet Ejector] C --> G[Cryogenic Systems] B1 --> B1a[Domestic Fridge] B1 --> B1b[Central Air Conditioning] B1 --> B1c[Industrial Chillers] B2 --> B2a[Aircraft Air Conditioning] B2 --> B2b[Bell Coleman Cycle] E --> E1[Ammonia Water] E --> E2[LiBr Water] G --> G1[Linde Hampson] G --> G2[Claude Cycle] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: Heat Pump vs Refrigeration — Same Hardware, Different Goal ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DEVICE[REVERSIBLE HEAT PUMP DEVICE] D1[Compressor] --> D2[Condenser] D2 --> D3[Expansion Valve] D3 --> D4[Evaporator] D4 --> D1 end D4 -.Q_L Cold Side.-> S1[REFRIGERATOR MODE
Cold Space is USEFUL
Hot Side is waste] D2 -.Q_H Hot Side.-> S2[HEAT PUMP MODE
Hot Space is USEFUL
Cold Side is source] S1 --- USAGE1[Useful output: Q_L] S2 --- USAGE2[Useful output: Q_H] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Comfort Air Conditioning Block ```mermaid flowchart TD A[INDOOR RETURN AIR
Warm and Humid] --> B[FILTER
Removes dust] B --> C[COOLING COIL
Evaporator of Vapour Compression System
Sensible and Latent cooling] C --> D[DEHUMIDIFICATION
Moisture condenses on coil and drains out] D --> E[FAN OR BLOWER
Distributes conditioned air] E --> F[CONDITIONED SUPPLY AIR
Cool dry clean air to room] F --> G[ROOM - HUMAN OCCUPANCY] G -.Recirculation.-> A C -.Q_L absorbed.-> H[OUTDOOR UNIT
Condenser rejects heat to ambient] ``` ## 4.5 Schematic: Reversed Carnot Cycle on T-s Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[STATE 1: Saturated Vapour at T_H] --> A2[STATE 2: Superheated Vapour at T_H] A2 --> A3[STATE 3: Saturated Liquid at T_L] A3 --> A4[STATE 4: Two Phase Mixture at T_L] A4 --> A1 ``` | Process | Description | Heat Transfer | |---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 → 2 | Isentropic Compression | Work in, no heat | | 2 → 3 | Isothermal Heat Rejection at T_H | Q_H rejected (at constant high T) | | 3 → 4 | Isentropic Expansion | Work out, no heat | | 4 → 1 | Isothermal Heat Absorption at T_L | Q_L absorbed (at constant low T) | > [!IMPORTANT] Conceptual Note > The **Carnot refrigeration cycle** is *theoretical* (it uses a wet compression that is impractical), but it gives the *maximum possible COP*. Real systems (Vapour Compression) always operate at a *lower* COP than Carnot. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Define Refrigeration. State the unit of refrigeration.** **Model Answer:** **Refrigeration:** It is the process of removing heat from a substance or space to lower its temperature below that of the surroundings and maintaining that lower temperature, by the continuous extraction of heat using external work. **Unit of Refrigeration:** The standard commercial unit is the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**. One TR is the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 ton of water (907.185 kg)** at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours. **Conversion:** $\mathbf{1\;TR = 3.517\;kW = 12{,}000\;BTU/hr}$. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **Distinguish between Refrigeration and Air Conditioning.** **Model Answer:** | # | Refrigeration | Air Conditioning | |---|---------------|------------------| | 1 | Only concerned with **cooling** (heat removal) | Concerned with **temperature, humidity, purity, and motion** of air | | 2 | Primary goal: Maintain a low temperature | Primary goal: Human comfort / industrial process control | | 3 | No humidity control is a primary aim | Humidity control is essential (dehumidification) | | 4 | Example: Domestic fridge, deep freezer | Example: Central A/C, window A/C | | 5 | Single parameter (Q_L) | Multi-parameter control | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between the Coefficient of Performance of a Heat Pump and a Refrigerator operating between the same temperature limits. **(b) [7 Marks]** A refrigerator operates between 5 °C and 35 °C. It has a cooling load of 30 kW and a COP of 2.5. Find (i) the power required by the compressor, (ii) the heat rejected per hour, and (iii) the percentage improvement in COP compared to a Carnot refrigerator operating between the same limits. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Given:** Reversed heat engine operating between $T_L$ and $T_H$, with work input W and heat $Q_L$ absorbed from cold space, $Q_H$ rejected to hot space. **Energy Balance (1st Law for a cycle):** $$Q_H = Q_L + W \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Definition of COP of Refrigerator:** $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_L = COP_R \cdot W \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Definition of COP of Heat Pump:** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_H = COP_{HP} \cdot W \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Subtracting (2) from (3):** $$Q_H - Q_L = (COP_{HP} - COP_R) \cdot W$$ **Using (1):** $$W = (COP_{HP} - COP_R) \cdot W$$ $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $T_L = 5\;°C = 278\;K$, $T_H = 35\;°C = 308\;K$, $Q_L = 30\;kW$, $COP_R = 2.5$ **(i) Compressor power:** $$W = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{30}{2.5} = 12\;kW$$ **(ii) Heat rejected per hour:** $$Q_H = Q_L + W = 30 + 12 = 42\;kW$$ $$Q_{H,per\;hour} = 42 \times 3600 = 151{,}200\;kJ/hr = 151.2\;MJ/hr$$ **(iii) Carnot COP and percentage improvement:** $$COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{278}{30} = 9.267$$ **Percentage improvement if the system ran at Carnot COP:** $$\% \;improvement = \frac{9.267 - 2.5}{2.5} \times 100 = 270.7\%$$ > [!WARNING] Examiner's Valuation Pitfall > - Many students forget to **convert °C to Kelvin** for Part (b)(iii). Kelvin temperatures are mandatory in Carnot formulas. > - Students often write $Q_L$ instead of $W$ in Part (b)(i). Be careful: COP = desired effect (Q_L) / work input (W). > - Always state the *units* in your final answer (kW, kJ/hr). A common KTU convention awards 0.5 mark for correct units. --- ### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain briefly the various methods of refrigeration. List the applications of refrigeration and air conditioning in different fields. **(b) [7 Marks]** A food storage room requires a refrigeration system of 10 TR capacity. The system operates on a vapour compression cycle with a COP of 3.0. Calculate (i) the mass of refrigerant circulated per hour if the refrigeration effect per kg is 150 kJ/kg, and (ii) the power required to drive the compressor. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Methods of Refrigeration:** 1. **Vapour Compression Refrigeration (VCR):** Uses a mechanical compressor to circulate refrigerant; most common for domestic and commercial use. 2. **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration (VAR):** Uses heat energy (instead of mechanical work) as input; refrigerant is absorbed in a secondary fluid. Common in industrial and large A/C applications. 3. **Gas Cycle / Air Refrigeration:** Uses air as the working fluid; used in aircraft A/C and gas liquefaction. 4. **Thermoelectric (Peltier) Refrigeration:** Uses solid-state semiconductors; small portable coolers, electronic cooling. 5. **Steam Jet Refrigeration:** Uses high-pressure steam; for industrial chilling above 4 °C. 6. **Cryogenic Refrigeration:** For very low temperatures below -100 °C; used in gas liquefaction. **Applications of Refrigeration and A/C:** - **Domestic:** Fridges, freezers, water coolers, window A/C. - **Commercial:** Cold storage, ice rinks, supermarkets, malls. - **Industrial:** Petrochemical cooling, plastic mould cooling, food processing, dairy industry. - **Medical:** Blood banks, vaccine storage, MRI machines, operation theatres. - **Transport:** Marine refrigeration, refrigerated trucks, A/C in cars/trains. - **Aerospace:** Aircraft A/C, spacecraft thermal control. - **Building Services:** Centralized HVAC, clean rooms, data centres. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** Cooling capacity = 10 TR, COP = 3.0, Refrigeration Effect (RE) per kg = 150 kJ/kg. **Step 1 — Convert cooling capacity to kW:** $$\dot{Q}_L = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17\;kW$$ **(i) Mass flow rate of refrigerant:** $$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{RE} = \frac{35.17\;kW}{150\;kJ/kg}$$ Converting 35.17 kW to kJ/hr: $35.17 \times 3600 = 126{,}612\;kJ/hr$ $$\dot{m} = \frac{126{,}612}{150} = 844.08\;kg/hr$$ **(ii) Compressor power:** $$W = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{COP} = \frac{35.17}{3.0} = 11.72\;kW$$ **Answers: (i) 844.08 kg/hr, (ii) 11.72 kW** > [!WARNING] Examiner's Valuation Pitfall > - Converting **TR to kW** (multiply by 3.517) is a step many students skip, leading to a 1-mark deduction. > - For Part (b)(i), ensure that the **units of Q_L and RE match** (both in kJ/s, or both in kJ/hr). Mixing them is the most common KTU error. > - In Part (a), students often write *only* "VCR and VAR" and miss the others. Listing **at least 4 methods** is required for full marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist - **Refrigeration** = *cooling only*. **Air Conditioning** = *temperature + humidity + cleanliness + motion* (full climate control). - **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 12,000 BTU/hr = 3.0258 × 10⁶ J per 24 hours** (latent heat of fusion method). - **COP_R = Q_L / W** (refrigerator, useful effect = Q_L). - **COP_HP = Q_H / W** (heat pump, useful effect = Q_H). - **COP_HP = COP_R + 1** (universal, derived from 1st Law). - **Carnot COP (Refrigerator) = T_L / (T_H - T_L)** — always use **Kelvin**. - **Carnot COP (Heat Pump) = T_H / (T_H - T_L)**. - **Methods of Refrigeration:** VCR, VAR, Gas cycle, Thermoelectric, Steam jet, Cryogenic. - A **heat pump and a refrigerator** are the *same device* with the *objective changed*; cold side useful = fridge; hot side useful = heat pump. - The **COP of a refrigerator is always greater than 1** in practice (unlike efficiency which is < 1). - **Ton of Refrigeration** is a *rate* of heat removal, NOT a total quantity of heat. - The higher the **COP**, the more efficient the system. A higher COP means **less work input** for the same cooling effect. - **Sensible heating/cooling** changes temperature; **latent heating/cooling** changes moisture content (e.g., dehumidification on cooling coil). - **Reversed Carnot cycle** is theoretical, not realizable in practice due to wet compression, but it gives the **upper limit** of COP. - For KTU problems, always **draw the system sketch**, **state assumptions**, **list given data**, **show formula substitution**, and **box the final answer**.

topics.title: Ideal refrigeration cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Ideal refrigeration cycles # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Refrigeration Cycle? A **refrigeration cycle** is a thermodynamic closed loop that uses external work (mechanical or thermal) as input to transfer heat from a low-temperature body (cold space) to a high-temperature body (atmosphere). It is essentially a **reversed heat engine** running in the opposite direction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):** > *An ideal refrigeration cycle is a reversible thermodynamic cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs (cold space at temperature T_L and surroundings at temperature T_H) in which the working fluid undergoes a series of processes to extract heat Q_L from the cold space and reject heat Q_H to the surroundings, consuming net work W in the process.* ## 1.2 Why "Ideal"? The term **"Ideal"** implies: - All processes are **internally reversible** (no friction, no pressure drop). - No heat losses to surroundings except at the intended reservoirs. - Working fluid behaviour is treated as ideal gas (for air cycles) or as a pure substance on P-h charts (for vapour cycles). Two principal ideal refrigeration cycles are studied in KTU Module 1: | S.No. | Cycle Name | Working Fluid | Realisation | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Bell-Coleman (Simple Air Refrigeration)** | Air (Ideal Gas) | Reversed Brayton / Joule Cycle | | 2 | **Simple Vapour Compression Cycle (VCC)** | Refrigerant (Phase-changing) | Saturated Vapour Compression | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Refrigerator as a Heat Mover > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy (The Sponge and the Bucket):** > Imagine a delivery boy carrying water in a **sponge** from a cold well (T_L) to a hot sunny field (T_H). > - The **sponge soaks up cold water** at the well (= evaporator absorbing Q_L). > - The boy **walks uphill (= compressor doing work W)** carrying the heavy, wet sponge. > - At the field, he **squeezes the sponge dry** (= condenser rejecting Q_H to atmosphere). > - The dry sponge returns downhill (= expansion valve drops pressure). > > The net effect: **water (heat) is moved against its natural direction** by spending energy (work). ## 1.4 Units & Performance Metric - **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is the figure of merit: $$\text{COP} \;=\; \frac{\text{Desired Output (Refrigerating Effect, RE)}}{\text{Net Work Input, W}} \;=\; \frac{Q_L}{W}$$ - **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** $\equiv$ **3.517 kW** $\equiv$ **211 kJ/min** of heat extraction. > [!IMPORTANT] > For KTU numericals, always express mass flow rate as: $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\text{Refrigeration Load (kW)}}{\text{RE per kg (kJ/kg)}}$ # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Cycle A — Simple Air Refrigeration Cycle (Bell-Coleman) ### 2.1.1 Operating Principle Air is used as the working fluid. It undergoes four **reversible** processes around a closed loop on the **T-s** and **P-v** diagrams. ### 2.1.2 The Four Processes | Process | Description | Constant? | Reversibility | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1 → 2** | Compression in compressor (cold air → hot air) | **Isentropic (S = const)** | Reversible adiabatic | | **2 → 3** | Cooling in air cooler (heat rejected to atmosphere) | **Isobaric (P = const)** | Heat transfer at P_2 | | **3 → 4** | Expansion in expander (hot → very cold) | **Isentropic (S = const)** | Reversible adiabatic | | **4 → 1** | Heat absorption in cold space / refrigerator | **Isobaric (P = const)** | Heat transfer at P_1 | ### 2.1.3 Refrigerating Effect & Compressor Work (per kg of air) $$RE = C_p\,(T_1 - T_4) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ $$W_{comp} = C_p\,(T_2 - T_3) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$ ### 2.1.4 The Core Insight — Pressure Ratio Dictates COP Using isentropic relations $T_2/T_1 = T_3/T_4 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ where $r_p = P_2/P_1$: $$T_2 - T_3 \;=\; T_2 - T_4\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) \;=\; T_2\!\left(1 - \dfrac{T_4}{T_1}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\,(T_1 - T_4)$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{air}} \;=\; \dfrac{T_1 - T_4}{T_2 - T_3} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** *The COP of the ideal Bell-Coleman cycle depends ONLY on the pressure ratio — not on the cold-space temperature! That is why cold air refrigeration plants perform poorly at low cold-space temperatures (high $r_p$ required → low COP).* ## 2.2 Cycle B — Simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCC) ### 2.2.1 Operating Principle A pure refrigerant (R-134a, R-12, ammonia, etc.) circulates, **changing phase** through evaporation and condensation. The cycle is depicted on the **P-h** diagram — the most important diagram for KTU VCC problems. ### 2.2.2 The Four State Points & Processes | State | Location | Condition | Process Entering | Process Leaving | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1** | Compressor inlet | Saturated / Superheated vapour at P_e | Evaporation (4→1) | Compression (1→2) | | **2** | Compressor outlet | Superheated vapour at P_c | Compression (1→2) | Condensation (2→3) | | **3** | Condenser outlet | Saturated liquid at P_c | Condensation (2→3) | Expansion (3→4) | | **4** | Evaporator inlet | Two-phase mixture at P_e | Expansion (3→4) | Evaporation (4→1) | ### 2.2.3 Energy Equations (Steady-Flow, per kg) **Compressor (1 → 2):** $$W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1$$ **Condenser (2 → 3):** $$Q_{cond} = h_2 - h_3$$ **Expansion Valve (3 → 4):** Isenthalpic (throttling) $$h_4 = h_3$$ **Evaporator (4 → 1):** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$$ ### 2.2.4 Coefficient of Performance $$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{VCC} \;=\; \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \;=\; \dfrac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}\;}$$ ## 2.3 Modifications of the VCC (KTU High-Yield) ### 2.3.1 VCC with Subcooled Liquid Liquid below saturation temp enters expansion valve. - $h_3' < h_3$ → $h_4' < h_4$ → **RE increases** → **COP increases** ✓ - Compressor work unchanged (compressor inlet state 1 unchanged). - Requires an extra **subcooler** (e.g., shell-and-coil using cold water). ### 2.3.2 VCC with Superheated Vapour Vapour above saturation temp enters compressor. - $h_1' > h_1$ → $h_2'$ even larger (isentropic compression). - $RE' = h_1' - h_4$ increases marginally. - $W' = h_2' - h_1'$ increases more than RE. - **COP decreases** slightly, but **specific volume increases** → less refrigerant mass flow → **volumetric efficiency improves**, and **no liquid slugging** at compressor inlet. - KTU emphasises: *Superheating is mandatory for compressor safety even though COP drops slightly.* ### 2.3.3 VCC with Both Subcooling & Superheating Combines the volumetric benefit of superheating and COP benefit of subcooling. Most real plants operate on this cycle. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Refrigerating Effect (Air) | $RE = C_p(T_1 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | | 2 | Compressor Work (Air) | $W = C_p(T_2 - T_3)$ | kJ/kg | | 3 | COP (Bell-Coleman) | $\text{COP} = 1/[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$ | — | | 4 | COP (Carnot Refrig.) | $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ | — | | 5 | RE (VCC) | $RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | | 6 | Compressor Work (VCC) | $W = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | | 7 | Heat Rejected (VCC) | $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | | 8 | COP (VCC) | $(h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$ | — | | 9 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \text{Load (kW)} / \text{RE (kJ/kg)}$ | kg/s | | 10 | 1 TR | $1\,\text{TR} = 3.517\,\text{kW} = 211\,\text{kJ/min}$ | kW | | 11 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = P_2 / P_1 = P_c / P_e$ | — | | 12 | Volumetric RE | $\text{VRE} = (h_1 - h_4)/v_1$ | kJ/m³ | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Bell-Coleman cycles** were historically used in air-craft pressurisation and cold storage (pre-1930s). Modern aircraft use VCC. - **VCC** is the workhorse of domestic refrigerators, AC units, cold-chain logistics, cryogenic storage, and industrial chillers. - Understanding ideal cycles allows engineers to predict real-cycle performance using **isentropic efficiencies** of compressors and turbines. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — COP of the Simple Air Refrigeration Cycle **Given:** Reversible Bell-Coleman cycle with air as ideal gas; processes 1-2 and 3-4 are isentropic; 2-3 and 4-1 are isobaric. **Energy entering each control volume per kg of air:** - Compressor (1→2): $w_c = h_2 - h_1 = C_p(T_2 - T_1)$ - Cooler (2→3): $q_H = h_2 - h_3 = C_p(T_2 - T_3)$ (rejected) - Expander (3→4): $w_e = h_3 - h_4 = C_p(T_3 - T_4)$ (delivered) - Cold Space (4→1): $q_L = h_1 - h_4 = C_p(T_1 - T_4)$ (absorbed) **Step 1:** Net work input (per kg): $$W_{net} = w_c - w_e = C_p(T_2 - T_1) - C_p(T_3 - T_4)$$ $$W_{net} = C_p(T_2 - T_1 - T_3 + T_4) = C_p[(T_2 - T_3) + (T_4 - T_1)]$$ But because $T_4 < T_1$, the term $(T_4 - T_1)$ is negative, hence: $$W_{net} = C_p(T_2 - T_3) - C_p(T_1 - T_4) = W_{comp} - W_{exp}$$ **Step 2:** Refrigerating Effect: $$RE = C_p(T_1 - T_4)$$ **Step 3:** COP definition: $$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{C_p(T_1 - T_4)}{C_p(T_2 - T_3) - C_p(T_1 - T_4)} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{T_2 - T_3 - (T_1 - T_4)}$$ Wait — careful with sign convention. The expander **delivers** work back, reducing net work input. So: $$W_{net} = C_p(T_2 - T_3) - C_p(T_1 - T_4)$$ Therefore: $$\text{COP}_{\text{air}} = \frac{C_p(T_1 - T_4)}{C_p(T_2 - T_3) - C_p(T_1 - T_4)}$$ **Step 4:** Apply isentropic relations for ideal gas: $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ Hence $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \dfrac{T_3}{T_4}$, which implies $T_2 T_4 = T_1 T_3$. **Step 5:** Define pressure ratio $r_p = P_2/P_1$ and let $\beta = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$. Then $T_2 = \beta T_1$ and $T_3 = \beta T_4$. Compute $(T_2 - T_3) - (T_1 - T_4)$: $$= (\beta T_1 - \beta T_4) - (T_1 - T_4) = (\beta - 1)(T_1 - T_4)$$ **Step 6:** Substitute back: $$\text{COP}_{\text{air}} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(\beta - 1)(T_1 - T_4)} = \frac{1}{\beta - 1}$$ $$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{air}} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}\;}$$ **Alternative form:** If we approximate $T_1 \approx T_4 = T_L$ (cold space) and $T_2 \approx T_3 = T_H$ (hot side), then $\text{COP} \approx T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — *identical in form to a Carnot refrigerator*. --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — COP of the Simple Vapour Compression Cycle **Given:** Steady-flow VCC with isentropic compression and isenthalpic expansion. **Step 1:** Energy balance on compressor (control volume, neglect KE/PE): $$h_1 + w_{in} = h_2 \;\Longrightarrow\; w_{in} = h_2 - h_1$$ **Step 2:** Energy balance on condenser (heat rejected): $$h_2 = h_3 + q_H \;\Longrightarrow\; q_H = h_2 - h_3$$ **Step 3:** Energy balance on throttling valve — adiabatic, no work: $$h_3 = h_4 \;(\text{isenthalpic})$$ **Step 4:** Energy balance on evaporator (heat absorbed): $$h_4 + q_L = h_1 \;\Longrightarrow\; q_L = h_1 - h_4$$ **Step 5:** Since $h_4 = h_3$: $$q_L = h_1 - h_3 \quad \text{and} \quad \text{RE per kg} = h_1 - h_4$$ **Step 6:** COP by definition: $$\text{COP}_{VCC} = \frac{q_L}{w_{in}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ $$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{VCC} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}\;}$$ **Verification of energy balance (1st Law for cycle):** $$q_H = h_2 - h_3 = h_2 - h_4 = (h_2 - h_1) + (h_1 - h_4) = w_{in} + q_L \;\checkmark$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 1 (Air Refrigeration) > **Problem [KTU Style]:** *An air refrigeration plant operates between $-10^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $40^{\circ}\text{C}$. The pressure ratio is 4. Assuming isentropic compression and expansion ($\gamma = 1.4$), determine the COP and the mass of air circulated per TR per minute. Take $C_p = 1.005\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$.* **Solution:** **Step 1:** Convert to absolute temperatures (cold space limit). Cold space air enters cold space at $T_4$ and leaves at $T_1$. Idealising $T_1 \approx T_4 \approx T_L = 263\,\text{K}$. $T_H = 313\,\text{K}$. (Approximation; the actual values are computed from $r_p$.) **Step 2:** Compute $r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$: $$\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$ $$\beta = 4^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \ln 4} = e^{0.2857 \times 1.3863} = e^{0.3961} = 1.486$$ **Step 3:** COP: $$\text{COP} = \frac{1}{\beta - 1} = \frac{1}{1.486 - 1} = \frac{1}{0.486} = 2.058$$ **Step 4:** Refining with actual temperatures: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \beta = 263 \times 1.486 = 390.8\,\text{K} = 117.8^{\circ}\text{C}$$ $$T_4 = T_3 / \beta \quad \text{with } T_3 = 313\,\text{K}$$ $$T_4 = 313/1.486 = 210.6\,\text{K} = -62.4^{\circ}\text{C}$$ $$RE = C_p(T_1 - T_4) = 1.005(263 - 210.6) = 1.005 \times 52.4 = 52.66\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 5:** Mass flow rate per TR (1 TR = 211 kJ/min): $$\dot{m} = \frac{211}{52.66} = 4.007\,\text{kg/min}$$ > **Answer:** COP ≈ **2.06**, $\dot{m} \approx$ **4 kg/min per TR**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 2 (Vapour Compression Cycle) > **Problem [KTU Style]:** *In a vapour compression refrigeration cycle, R-134a enters the compressor at $-10^{\circ}\text{C}$ (saturated vapour) and leaves the condenser at $30^{\circ}\text{C}$ (saturated liquid). Using refrigerant tables: $h_1 = 241.3\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_2 = 273.9\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_3 = 93.6\,\text{kJ/kg}$. Find the COP and the refrigeration effect per kg.* **Solution:** **Step 1:** Throttling is isenthalpic: $h_4 = h_3 = 93.6\,\text{kJ/kg}$. **Step 2:** Refrigerating effect: $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 241.3 - 93.6 = 147.7\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3:** Compressor work: $$W = h_2 - h_1 = 273.9 - 241.3 = 32.6\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4:** COP: $$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W} = \frac{147.7}{32.6} = 4.53$$ > **Answer:** RE = **147.7 kJ/kg**, COP = **4.53**. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (VCC vs Air Cycle) | Parameter | Air Cycle (Bell-Coleman) | Vapour Compression (VCC) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Working fluid | Air (ideal gas) | Phase-change refrigerant | | COP depends on | Pressure ratio $r_p$ | Enthalpy differences $(h_1 - h_4), (h_2 - h_1)$ | | Cycle type | Reversed Brayton | Reversed Rankine | | Typical COP (domestic) | Low (1 – 3) | High (3 – 6) | | Cold-space temp range | Above $-50^{\circ}\text{C}$ limited | Down to cryogenic with cascade | | Mass flow requirement | High (low RE/kg) | Low (high RE/kg via latent heat) | | Compressor type | Reciprocating / Centrifugal | Reciprocating / Scroll / Screw | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic A — Simple Air Refrigeration Cycle (Bell-Coleman) ```mermaid flowchart LR CSP["Cold Space /

The enclosed area under 4-1 represents $q_L$, the area under 2-3 represents $q_H$, and the enclosed cycle area represents net work. --- ## 4.2 Schematic B — Simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (P–h Block Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EVAP["Evaporator Region Low Pressure P_e"] ST4["State 4

The throttling process 3→4 is a **vertical drop** (constant $h$), and the compression process 1→2 is a **near-vertical line** going up and slightly right (isentropic). [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "Coefficient of Performance" of a refrigerator. Why is the COP of a vapour compression refrigeration cycle higher than that of a Bell-Coleman air refrigeration cycle operating between the same temperature limits?* **Model Answer (Board-Key):** The **COP** of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of desired refrigerating effect (heat extracted from cold space) to the net work input required. $$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}}$$ The COP of VCC is higher than Bell-Coleman for the same temperature limits because: - In **Bell-Coleman**, the refrigerant (air) remains in gaseous phase throughout — heat is absorbed/rejected via **sensible heat only** (limited by $C_p$). - In **VCC**, the refrigerant undergoes **phase change**; heat absorption in the evaporator occurs at **constant temperature** equal to the evaporator saturation temperature, leveraging the **large latent heat of vaporisation**. This delivers a much higher refrigerating effect per kg of refrigerant for the same work input, raising COP. **[3 Marks: Definition 1M + Reason 2M]** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is throttling (not an expander) used in the simple vapour compression refrigeration cycle? State the property assumed constant during throttling.* **Model Answer (Board-Key):** A throttling valve is used in place of an expander because: 1. **Cost & simplicity:** A throttling valve is a cheap, passive restriction; an expander is a costly rotating machine. 2. **Refrigerant condition:** The refrigerant at the condenser exit is a **two-phase liquid-vapour mixture at high pressure**. Operating an expander on a wet mixture causes **erosion, cavitation, and mechanical damage**, making expander use impractical. 3. **Energy recovery negligible:** The work extractable from expanding a mostly-liquid stream is minimal compared to the cost of the device. The property assumed **constant** during throttling is **enthalpy** (isenthalpic process: $h_3 = h_4$). **[3 Marks: Any 3 reasons 2M + Constant property 1M]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, KTU ESE Module Choice) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Pattern]** **(a)** *Derive the expression for the COP of an ideal Bell-Coleman air refrigeration cycle in terms of the pressure ratio. Show that the COP depends only on the pressure ratio, not on the cold-space temperature.* **[7 Marks]** **(b)** *An air refrigeration plant works between $-5^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $35^{\circ}\text{C}$. The air enters the compressor at a pressure of 1 bar and leaves at 4 bar. Calculate:* - *(i) The COP of the cycle.* - *(ii) The mass of air circulated per minute per TR of refrigeration.* *Take $C_p = 1.005\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$ and $\gamma = 1.4$.* **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1:** Define the four processes (1-2 isentropic compression, 2-3 isobaric cooling, 3-4 isentropic expansion, 4-1 isobaric heat absorption). **Step 2:** Write energy per kg: - $RE = C_p(T_1 - T_4)$ - $W_{comp} = C_p(T_2 - T_3)$ - $W_{exp} = C_p(T_3 - T_4)$ - $W_{net} = C_p[(T_2 - T_3) - (T_1 - T_4)]$ **Step 3:** COP: $$\text{COP} = \frac{C_p(T_1 - T_4)}{C_p[(T_2 - T_3) - (T_1 - T_4)]} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_3) - (T_1 - T_4)}$$ **Step 4:** Apply isentropic relations $T_2/T_1 = T_3/T_4 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = \beta$: $T_2 - T_3 = \beta(T_1 - T_4)$ Substituting: $$\text{COP} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{\beta(T_1 - T_4) - (T_1 - T_4)} = \frac{1}{\beta - 1}$$ **Step 5:** Final expression: $$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{air}} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}\;}$$ Since $r_p$ and $\gamma$ are the only variables, **COP depends only on pressure ratio**, independent of cold-space temperature $T_L$. > **[7 Marks: Steps 1-5 distributed — 1.5M each step.]** --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1:** Compute $\beta = r_p^{0.2857}$ with $r_p = 4$: $$\beta = 4^{0.2857} = 1.486$$ **Step 2:** Determine temperatures. Take $T_3 = 35 + 273 = 308\,\text{K}$ (hot side, air-cooler outlet). Then: $$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{\beta} = \frac{308}{1.486} = 207.3\,\text{K} = -65.7^{\circ}\text{C}$$ $$T_1 = T_3 / \beta^0 \cdot \beta^0 \text{ consistency: find from } T_2 = T_1 \beta$$ For consistency, use $T_1 = -5 + 273 = 268\,\text{K}$ (cold-space temperature idealisation): $$T_2 = 268 \times 1.486 = 398.2\,\text{K} = 125.2^{\circ}\text{C}$$ **Step 3:** Compute RE and W: $$RE = C_p(T_1 - T_4) = 1.005(268 - 207.3) = 1.005 \times 60.7 = 61.0\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ $$W = C_p(T_2 - T_3) = 1.005(398.2 - 308) = 1.005 \times 90.2 = 90.65\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 4:** COP: $$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W - RE} = \frac{61.0}{90.65 - 61.0} = \frac{61.0}{29.65} = 2.057$$ (Alternatively, using the shortcut: $\text{COP} = 1/(\beta - 1) = 1/0.486 = 2.058$. Both match.) **Step 5:** Mass flow rate per TR (1 TR = 211 kJ/min): $$\dot{m} = \frac{211}{61.0} = 3.46\,\text{kg/min per TR}$$ > **Final Answer:** COP = **2.058**; $\dot{m}$ = **3.46 kg/min per TR** > **[7 Marks: Calculation correctness and unit awareness tested.]** --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Pattern — Alternative Choice]** **(a)** *With the help of a P-h diagram, explain the working of a simple vapour compression refrigeration cycle. Derive the expression for its COP.* **[7 Marks]** **(b)** *A vapour compression refrigeration cycle uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The following data are given:* - *Evaporator pressure $P_e = 2.0\,\text{bar}$ (saturation temp $= -10^{\circ}\text{C}$): $h_1 = 392\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated vapour), $h_4 = 199\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (after throttling from saturated liquid $h_3 = 199\,\text{kJ/kg}$).* - *Condenser pressure $P_c = 10\,\text{bar}$: $h_2 = 426\,\text{kJ/kg}$ after isentropic compression.* - *Refrigeration load = 5 TR.* *Calculate:* - *(i) Refrigerating effect per kg.* - *(ii) Mass flow rate of refrigerant.* - *(iii) COP.* - *(iv) Heat rejected in condenser per minute.* **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1:** **Description of the cycle on P-h diagram:** - Point 1: Saturated (or superheated) vapour at low pressure $P_e$ entering compressor. - Point 2: Superheated vapour at high pressure $P_c$ after isentropic compression. - Point 3: Saturated liquid at $P_c$ after condensation. - Point 4: Two-phase mixture at $P_e$ after isenthalpic throttling. - (Diagram: see SECTION 4.2 — block-level mapping.) **Step 2:** **Component roles:** - Compressor: $1 \to 2$, raises pressure (and temperature) by consuming work. - Condenser: $2 \to 3$, rejects heat $Q_H = h_2 - h_3$. - Expansion valve: $3 \to 4$, drops pressure at constant enthalpy. - Evaporator: $4 \to 1$, absorbs heat $Q_L = h_1 - h_4 = RE$. **Step 3:** **Derivation:** Apply SFEE to each component (neglecting KE/PE): Compressor: $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ Throttle: $h_4 = h_3$ Evaporator: $RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$ COP: $$\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ > **[7 Marks: Description + Diagram 4M + Derivation 3M]** --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $h_1 = 392\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_2 = 426\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_3 = h_4 = 199\,\text{kJ/kg}$, Load = 5 TR. **Step (i) — RE per kg:** $$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 392 - 199 = 193\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step (ii) — Mass flow rate:** $$5\,\text{TR} = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585\,\text{kW} = 17.585\,\text{kJ/s}$$ $$\dot{m} = \frac{17.585}{193} = 0.0911\,\text{kg/s} = 5.466\,\text{kg/min}$$ **Step (iii) — COP:** $$W = h_2 - h_1 = 426 - 392 = 34\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ $$\text{COP} = \frac{193}{34} = 5.676$$ **Step (iv) — Heat rejected per minute:** $$Q_H = h_2 - h_3 = 426 - 199 = 227\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_{H,\text{total}} = 227 \times 5.466 = 1240.8\,\text{kJ/min}$$ > **Final Answers:** RE = 193 kJ/kg; $\dot{m}$ = 5.47 kg/min; COP = 5.68; $Q_H$ = 1240.8 kJ/min. > **[7 Marks: 1.5M + 1.5M + 2M + 2M distributed.]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Sign Error in Bell-Coleman COP:** Students write $\text{COP} = (T_2 - T_3)/(T_1 - T_4)$ (inverted). **Correct:** RE in numerator, work in denominator. > 2. **Missing expander work term:** Some students compute $W = C_p(T_2 - T_3)$ directly as compressor work only. **Correct (ideal cycle):** Net work is $(T_2 - T_3) - (T_1 - T_4)$ since the expander recovers work. > 3. **Confusing temperature limits with state temperatures:** $T_L$ and $T_H$ are *reservoir* temperatures, NOT state temperatures $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ directly. > 4. **Forgetting units in TR-to-kW conversion:** Always convert 1 TR = 3.517 kW. > 5. **VCC — assuming $h_1 = h_g$ at evaporator pressure** when the question says "superheated": read the stem carefully! > 6. **P-h diagram orientation:** Drawing compressor process $1\to 2$ as horizontal — it is **near-vertical** (isentropic). Drawing throttle $3\to 4$ at an angle — it must be **exactly vertical** (constant $h$). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Ideal Refrigeration Cycles** - [ ] **COP definition** = $Q_L / W$, dimensionless. **NOT** an efficiency. - [ ] **1 TR** = 3.517 kW = 211 kJ/min = 50.4 kcal/min. - [ ] **Bell-Coleman COP** = $1/[r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$, depends ONLY on pressure ratio. - [ ] **VCC COP** = $(h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$, depends on refrigerant properties. - [ ] **Throttling** is **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$), not isentropic or isothermal. - [ ] **Compression** in VCC is **isentropic** (ideal) — vertical line on P-h. - [ ] **Refrigerating effect** = $h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3$ (since $h_4 = h_3$). - [ ] **Subcooling** ↑ RE, ↑ COP (good); **Superheating** ↓ COP slightly, but ↑ safety and volumetric efficiency. - [ ] VCC requires **phase-change refrigerant**; air cycle does not (sensible heat only). - [ ] VCC uses **throttle valve** (not expander) because expander on wet mixture causes damage. - [ ] **Carnot COP** is the upper bound: $T_L/(T_H - T_L)$; both real cycles operate below this. - [ ] **Mass flow rate formula:** $\dot{m} = (\text{TR} \times 3.517) / \text{RE}$ in kg/s. - [ ] Always check **T-s and P-h diagrams** in KTU problems — they carry **1–2 marks** just for correct drawing. - [ ] For air cycle, $C_p = 1.005\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$ — standard values to use unless specified. - [ ] **Pressure ratio** $r_p = P_c / P_e$ for VCC; $r_p = P_2/P_1$ for air cycle (compressor outlet ÷ inlet).

topics.title: Aircraft refrigeration cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aircraft refrigeration cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ✈️ AIRCRAFT REFRIGERATION CYCLES — SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aircraft Refrigeration Cycle (ARC):** An *open/closed gas refrigeration cycle* in which **air** is used as the working fluid (refrigerant) to provide cooling for aircraft cabins, electronic equipment bays, and crew compartments. It is a **non-condensing, non-evaporating** cycle, since air remains in the gaseous phase throughout the entire process.

The cycle operates on the **reverse Brayton principle** (also called the **Bell–Coleman cycle** in its simplest form), which is a practical approximation of the **Reversed Carnot Cycle** that uses a *gaseous refrigerant* instead of a vapor.

## 1.2 Why Air is Chosen for Aircraft Refrigeration

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike domestic refrigeration (which uses R-134a, R-410A, etc., that change phase), aircraft systems prefer **air** for the following critical engineering reasons:

- **Air is freely available** at any altitude — no need to carry refrigerant tanks, reducing payload and explosion hazards.
- **Wide operating range:** Air does not condense at typical aircraft operating pressures, so it works across a huge temperature envelope (from −60 °C at 10 km altitude to +45 °C on the tarmac).
- **Low mass flow rate** per ton of refrigeration is possible.
- **No phase change** → simpler components, no evaporator/condenser flooding problems.
- **Compact & lightweight** — essential for aviation where every kilogram matters.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Mountain Bicycle Pump":**
> Imagine pumping a bicycle tire. You *compress* air (it gets hot) — that's the **compressor stage**. Hold the hot pump near a cold water tap to cool it — that's the **heat rejection (ram cooler)** stage. Then release the air through a nozzle — it expands and becomes ice-cold — that's the **expander stage**. Direct this cold air onto your face in summer — that's the **refrigeration effect**.
>
> An aircraft does the same thing, but with continuous airflow: a turbine (instead of a nozzle) recovers expansion work, and a heat exchanger dumps heat to the *ram air* (outside air that hits the aircraft at high speed).

## 1.4 Classification of Aircraft Refrigeration Cycles

Aircraft refrigeration cycles are categorized by complexity and performance:

| **#** | **Cycle Type** | **Key Feature** | **Typical Application** |
|:-----:|----------------|-----------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Simple / Bell–Coleman Cycle** | One compressor, one expander, one cooler | Subsonic aircraft, basic cooling |
| 2 | **Bootstrap Air Refrigeration Cycle** | Uses *two* compressors with intercooler; main expander on cold line | High-performance subsonic jets |
| 3 | **Reduced Ambient Temperature (RAT) Cycle** | Two expanders to drop temperature further | Supersonic aircraft, high-altitude jets |
| 4 | **Regenerative (Recuperative) Cycle** | Heat exchanger between cold expanded air and hot compressed air | Energy-efficient systems |
| 5 | **Evaporative / Vapour-Cycle Hybrid** | Uses water evaporation + mechanical refrigeration | Modern large commercial aircraft (Boeing 787 uses this) |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET751 Module 1):**
> The focus is on the *simple air refrigeration cycle* and the *bootstrap / reduced ambient* variants — their **P–h & T–s diagrams**, COP expressions, and **C.O.P. improvement strategies**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Simple Air Refrigeration Cycle (Bell–Coleman Cycle)

### 2.1.1 The Four Ideal Processes

The Bell–Coleman cycle on a **P–v** and **T–s** diagram consists of four reversible processes:

| **Process** | **Component** | **Thermodynamic Path** | **Heat/Work Exchange** |
|:-----------:|---------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 → 2 | **Compressor** | Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic) Compression | Work input **W_C** added; temperature rises from T₁ to T₂ |
| 2 → 3 | **Ram-Air Cooler** | Isobaric (constant pressure) heat rejection | Heat **Q_R** rejected to ambient; temperature drops from T₂ to T₃ |
| 3 → 4 | **Expansion Turbine** | Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic) Expansion | Work output **W_T** extracted; temperature drops from T₃ to T₄ |
| 4 → 1 | **Cabin / Cooling Coil** | Isobaric (constant pressure) heat absorption | Heat **Q_A** absorbed from cabin; temperature rises from T₄ to T₁ |

### 2.1.2 The T–s Diagram (Visual Logic)

> **Reading the T–s Diagram:**
> - Points **1** and **4** lie on the **lower isobar** (low pressure = cabin pressure).
> - Points **2** and **3** lie on the **upper isobar** (high pressure).
> - **Vertical lines** (1→2 and 3→4) represent *isentropic* compression & expansion.
> - **Horizontal lines** (2→3 and 4→1) represent *isobaric* heat rejection & absorption.

### 2.1.3 Performance Metrics

**Refrigerating Effect (RE) per kg of air:**

$$ RE = C_p \cdot (T_1 - T_4) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} $$

**Compressor Work (per kg):**

$$ W_C = C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} $$

**Turbine Work Output (per kg):**

$$ W_T = C_p \cdot (T_3 - T_4) \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} $$

**Net Work Input:**

$$ W_{net} = W_C - W_T = C_p \cdot [(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)] $$

**Coefficient of Performance (COP):**

$$ \text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)} $$

### 2.1.4 COP Expressed as a Function of Pressure Ratio

Using the isentropic relations for an ideal gas with constant specific heats:

$$ \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} $$

$$ \frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} $$

> Let $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ = **pressure ratio**, and $\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \frac{n-1}{n}$ for an isentropic index $n = \gamma = 1.4$ for air.

By substituting and simplifying (see Section 3 for full derivation), the COP reduces to a beautifully simple form:

$$ \boxed{\text{COP} = \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1}} $$

## 2.2 The Bootstrap Air Refrigeration Cycle

### 2.2.1 Why We Need a Bootstrap Cycle

In the simple cycle, **T₃** is limited by the *ambient* air temperature (the heat sink). At high altitudes, however, the ram-air entering the heat exchanger is extremely cold, so the simple cycle's cooling capacity drops dramatically.

The **Bootstrap** cycle solves this by using:
- A **secondary (bootstrap) compressor** that compresses the *ram air* used to cool the main compressed air.
- A **secondary after-cooler** rejects heat from the ram-air compressor to the atmosphere.
- This allows the **main compressed air to be cooled below ambient**, vastly improving performance.

### 2.2.2 Process Flow of the Bootstrap Cycle

| **Process** | **Component** | **Description** |
|:-----------:|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 → 2 | **Main Compressor** | Cabin return air compressed from P₁ to P₂ |
| 2 → 3 | **Primary Heat Exchanger (Cooler)** | Air cooled by ram air (compressed) |
| 3 → 4 | **Secondary / Bootstrap Compressor** | Ram air compressed to cool the main loop better |
| 4 → 5 | **Secondary After-Cooler** | Ram air rejects heat to atmosphere |
| 5 → 6 | **Ram Air Expansion** | (Optional) — cooled ram air goes back to primary HX |
| 6 → 7 | **Main Expander (Cooling Turbine)** | Main compressed air expands → very cold air |
| 7 → 1 | **Cabin** | Cold air absorbs heat from cabin |

## 2.3 The Reduced Ambient Temperature (RAT) Cycle

The **Reduced Ambient** or **Dual-Expander** cycle uses *two* expansion turbines in series, with a flash-type heat exchanger between them. This drives the cabin temperature far below what a single expander can achieve.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The RAT cycle is the precursor to modern environmental control systems (ECS) used in fighter jets (e.g., MiG-29, Su-30MKI) and supersonic airliners (e.g., Concorde).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Subsonic commercial aviation** (Boeing 737, A320): Modern aircraft use *vapor-compression* cycles (R-134a) for better COP, but the *air cycle* remains critical for pneumatic-driven units and emergency cooling.
- **Military aircraft & helicopters:** Pure air cycle is preferred due to fire safety (no flammable refrigerants), high-altitude operation, and rapid response.
- **Spacecraft:** Modified air cycles (with multi-stage expansion) condition lunar/Mars habitats.
- **Naval & submarine ECS:** Air cycle variants are used for closed-loop life support.

---

## 2.5 📊 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bookmark this table — it is the master reference for all numerical problems on this topic.**

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Applicable Cycle** |
|:-----:|--------------|:----------:|-------------|:--------:|---------------------|
| 1 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p$ | $P_2 / P_1$ | dimensionless | All |
| 2 | Isentropic Temperature Relation (Compression) | — | $T_2 / T_1 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | — | All |
| 3 | Isentropic Temperature Relation (Expansion) | — | $T_3 / T_4 = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | — | All |
| 4 | Refrigerating Effect | $RE$ | $C_p (T_1 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | All |
| 5 | Compressor Work | $W_C$ | $C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | All |
| 6 | Turbine Work | $W_T$ | $C_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | All |
| 7 | Net Work Input | $W_{net}$ | $C_p [(T_2-T_1) - (T_3-T_4)]$ | kJ/kg | All |
| 8 | COP (Function of T) | $\text{COP}$ | $(T_1 - T_4) \div [(T_2-T_1)-(T_3-T_4)]$ | dimensionless | All |
| 9 | **COP (Function of Pressure Ratio)** | $\text{COP}$ | $1 \div [r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$ | dimensionless | **Simple / Bell–Coleman** |
| 10 | Mass of Air Flow Rate | $\dot{m}$ | $RE_{required} \div [C_p (T_1 - T_4)]$ | kg/s | All |
| 11 | Power Required | $\dot{W}$ | $\dot{m} \cdot W_{net}$ | kW | All |
| 12 | Volumetric Efficiency (Compressor) | $\eta_v$ | Actual vol. flow / Swept vol. | dimensionless | All |
| 13 | Tonnage of Refrigeration (TR) | — | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | kW | All |
| 14 | Specific Heat of Air | $C_p$ | **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)** | kJ/(kg·K) | All |
| 15 | Ratio of Specific Heats | $\gamma$ | **1.4** (for air) | dimensionless | All |
| 16 | Exponent in Isentropic Eq. | $(\gamma-1)/\gamma$ | **0.2857** (for air) | dimensionless | All |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $(\gamma-1)/\gamma$ as $0.286$ — but for KTU valuation, use the exact fraction $\frac{0.4}{1.4} = \frac{2}{7}$ when working symbolically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: COP of the Simple Air Refrigeration Cycle as a Function of Pressure Ratio

### Starting Premise

We have the four state points of the Bell–Coleman cycle, with air behaving as a perfect gas with constant $C_p$ and $\gamma$.

**Given:**
- State 1: $P_1, T_1$ (entrance to compressor)
- State 2: $P_2, T_2$ (exit of compressor)
- State 3: $P_2, T_3$ (exit of cooler)
- State 4: $P_1, T_4$ (exit of expander)

### Step 1 — Isentropic Compression (1 → 2)

For an isentropic process in an ideal gas with constant specific heats:

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}
$$

Solving for $T_2$:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}
$$

> **Logic:** The compressor does work on the gas, raising its temperature in proportion to the pressure ratio raised to the isentropic exponent.

### Step 2 — Isobaric Cooling (2 → 3)

At constant pressure $P_2$, heat $Q_R$ is rejected to the ram air:

$$
T_3 = T_2 - \frac{Q_R}{C_p} \quad \text{(by energy balance on the cooler)}
$$

> **Logic:** The hot compressed air gives up its heat to the cooler surroundings, dropping in temperature at constant pressure.

### Step 3 — Isentropic Expansion (3 → 4)

By symmetry with compression:

$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}
$$

Solving for $T_4$:

$$
T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}
$$

> **Logic:** The turbine extracts work from the gas, causing it to expand and cool.

### Step 4 — Compute the COP

By definition:

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{C_p (T_1 - T_4)}{C_p [(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)]}
$$

Cancelling $C_p$:

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)}
$$

### Step 5 — Express in Terms of Pressure Ratio

Let $k = \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}$ and $r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1}$. Then:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{k} \quad \text{and} \quad T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_p^{-k}
$$

Substitute into the numerator and denominator, and **assume** $T_3 = T_1$ (ideal case where the cooler brings the air back to the *cabin-side* temperature, i.e., the isobars are at the same temperature on the cold side). Wait — that's not correct; let us use a more general approach.

> **Refined Approach:** We define $\frac{T_3}{T_1} = \tau$ (a non-dimensional cooling ratio). After rigorous simplification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{T_1 - T_3 \cdot r_p^{-k}}{(T_1 r_p^{k} - T_1) - (T_3 - T_3 r_p^{-k})} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{T_1(1 - \tau \cdot r_p^{-k})}{T_1(r_p^{k} - 1) - T_3(1 - r_p^{-k})} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1 - \tau \cdot r_p^{-k}}{(r_p^{k} - 1) - \tau(1 - r_p^{-k})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Special case — Perfect Cooling ($T_3 = T_1$):** When the cooler is so effective that it returns the gas to the cabin inlet temperature:

$$
\tau = \frac{T_3}{T_1} = 1
$$

Substituting $\tau = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{\text{simple}} &= \frac{1 - r_p^{-k}}{(r_p^{k} - 1) - (1 - r_p^{-k})} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1 - r_p^{-k}}{r_p^{k} - 1 - 1 + r_p^{-k}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1 - r_p^{-k}}{r_p^{k} + r_p^{-k} - 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $r_p^{k}$:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{simple}} = \frac{r_p^{k} - 1}{r_p^{2k} - 2r_p^{k} + 1} = \frac{r_p^{k} - 1}{(r_p^{k} - 1)^2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{simple, perfect cooling}} = \frac{1}{r_p^{k} - 1}}
$$

> **Q.E.D.** The compact formula $\text{COP} = \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}$ is now rigorously established.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Effect of Pressure Ratio on COP

Let $r_p = 4$ and $\gamma = 1.4$ for air, so $k = 0.2857$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{1}{4^{0.2857} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $4^{0.2857}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(4) &= 1.3863 \\
0.2857 \times 1.3863 &= 0.3961 \\
e^{0.3961} &= 1.486
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{COP} = \frac{1}{1.486 - 1} = \frac{1}{0.486} = 2.058
$$

> **Conclusion:** A higher pressure ratio **reduces** the COP. The optimum is at the lowest $r_p$ that still delivers the required $RE$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: COP of the Reversed Carnot Cycle (Theoretical Benchmark)

For comparison, the *reversed Carnot* cycle between temperatures $T_L$ (cabin) and $T_H$ (ambient) has:

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}
$$

> **Why the air cycle's COP is lower:** The Bell–Coleman cycle uses *isentropic* compression/expansion, which is *internally irreversible* compared to the *isothermal* processes of Carnot. This is the cost of using a *non-phase-changing* working fluid.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** Air enters the compressor of a Bell–Coleman refrigeration system at $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}$, $T_1 = 290 \text{ K}$. The pressure ratio is $r_p = 5$. The air is cooled to $T_3 = 310 \text{ K}$ in the cooler. Determine:
> (a) The COP of the system
> (b) The mass of air flow required for a refrigeration load of **10 TR**
> (c) The net work of the cycle per kg

### Given Data

- $P_1 = 1$ bar, $T_1 = 290$ K
- $r_p = 5$ → $P_2 = 5$ bar
- $T_3 = 310$ K
- $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$, $k = 0.2857$

### Step (a): Find $T_2$ and $T_4$

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{k} = 290 \times 5^{0.2857}
$$

Compute $5^{0.2857}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(5) &= 1.6094 \\
0.2857 \times 1.6094 &= 0.4598 \\
e^{0.4598} &= 1.584
\end{aligned}
$$

So $T_2 = 290 \times 1.584 = 459.4 \text{ K}$

For $T_4$:

$$
T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_p^{-k} = \frac{310}{1.584} = 195.7 \text{ K}
$$

### Step (b): Refrigerating Effect and Net Work

$$
RE = C_p (T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (290 - 195.7) = 1.005 \times 94.3 = 94.77 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{net} = C_p [(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)] = 1.005 \times [(459.4 - 290) - (310 - 195.7)]
$$

$$
W_{net} = 1.005 \times [169.4 - 114.3] = 1.005 \times 55.1 = 55.38 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

### Step (c): COP

$$
\boxed{\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_{net}} = \frac{94.77}{55.38} = 1.711}
$$

### Step (d): Mass Flow Rate for 10 TR

$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517$ kW, so:

$$
\text{Refrigeration Load} = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{35.17 \text{ kW}}{94.77 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 0.371 \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step (e): Power Required

$$
\dot{W} = \dot{m} \cdot W_{net} = 0.371 \times 55.38 = 20.55 \text{ kW}
$$

> ✅ **Final Answers:** COP = 1.711, $\dot{m} = 0.371$ kg/s, $\dot{W} = 20.55$ kW

---

## 3.5 📋 ANALYTICAL COMPARISON MATRIX

| **Parameter** | **Reversed Carnot** | **Simple Air (Bell–Coleman)** | **Bootstrap** | **RAT (Dual-Expander)** |
|---------------|--------------------|--------------------------------|---------------|--------------------------|
| Processes | 2 Isothermal + 2 Isentropic | 2 Isentropic + 2 Isobaric | 2 Isentropic + 1 Isobaric + 1 Second Comp. | 2 Stages of Isentropic Exp. |
| T₃ achievable | $T_H$ (ambient) | $T_H$ (ambient) | **<** $T_H$ | **<<** $T_H$ |
| COP (typical) | Highest (benchmark) | ~1.5–2.5 | ~2.0–3.5 | ~2.5–4.0 |
| Complexity | Theoretical only | Low | Medium | High |
| Real Aircraft Use | None | Trainer aircraft | Fighter jets | Supersonic jets |
| Main Advantage | Maximum efficiency | Simple, reliable | Better cooling at altitude | Very low cabin temp |
| Main Drawback | Impractical (gas can't be isothermal) | Limited by ambient | Requires ram-air compression | Heavy, complex controls |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Bell–Coleman Cycle — T–s Diagram (Mermaid Block Functional Map)

The following block diagram maps the functional flow of energy and state transformations in the **Simple Air Refrigeration Cycle**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STATE 1: Cabin Return\nP1, T1\nLow Pressure"] -->|Isentropic\nCompression| B["STATE 2: Compressor Exit\nP2, T2\nHigh Temp"]
    B -->|Isobaric\nHeat Rejection| C["STATE 3: Cooler Exit\nP2, T3\nCooled High-P"]
    C -->|Isentropic\nExpansion| D["STATE 4: Turbine Exit\nP1, T4\nVery Cold"]
    D -->|Isobaric\nHeat Absorption| A

    E["Work Input Wc\nCp x T2-T1"] -.->|Drives| B
    C -.->|Heat Rejected QR| F["Ram Air Sink\nAtmosphere"]
    D -.->|Work Output Wt| G["Turbine Shaft\nCp x T3-T4"]
    D -.->|Refrigeration Effect RE| H["Cabin / Cold Air Supply"]

    style A fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style B fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style D fill:#80deea,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style F fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,color:#000
    style H fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Bootstrap Air Refrigeration Cycle — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MAIN["MAIN REFRIGERATION LOOP"]
        M1["Cabin Return\nAir P1 T1"] -->|Compress| M2["Main Compressor\nExit P2 T2"]
        M2 -->|Cool| M3["Primary HX Exit\nP2 T3"]
        M3 -->|Expand| M4["Main Cooling Turbine\nExit P1 T4"]
        M4 -->|Supply Cold Air| M1
    end

    subgraph RAM["RAM AIR SUBSYSTEM"]
        R1["Ram Air Inlet\nAtmospheric"] -->|Compress| R2["Bootstrap Compressor\nExit P-ram2 T-Ram2"]
        R2 -->|After-Cool| R3["After-Cooler Exit\nP-ram2 T-Atm"]
        R3 -->|Heat Exchange| M3
    end

    R2 -.->|Provides\nCooling| M2

    style M1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style M2 fill:#ff7043,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style M3 fill:#fff176,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style M4 fill:#4dd0e1,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style R2 fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style R3 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Reduced Ambient Temperature (RAT) Cycle — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Cabin Air\nP1 T1"] -->|Main Comp| A2["P2 T2"]
    A2 -->|Primary HX| A3["P2 T3"]
    A3 -->|First Expander| A4["P-int T4\nIntermediate"]
    A4 -->|Flash HX\nSelf-Cooling| A5["P-int T5\nBelow Ambient"]
    A5 -->|Second Expander| A6["P1 T6\nVery Cold"]
    A6 -->|To Cabin| A1
    A4 -.->|Heat Exch.| A5

    style A1 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

## 4.4 P–v Diagram of Bell–Coleman Cycle (Conceptual Sketch Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L["ISOBAR P2 (High Pressure)\nStates 2 and 3"] -->|Process 2-3 Isobaric Cooling| L
    M["Process 1-2: Isentropic Compression\nCurve 1 to 2"] --> M
    N["Process 3-4: Isentropic Expansion\nCurve 3 to 4"] --> N
    O["ISOBAR P1 (Low Pressure)\nStates 4 and 1"] -->|Process 4-1 Isobaric Heat Abs| O

    P["Work Input WC = Area 1-2-C-B-1"] -.-> M
    Q["Work Output WT = Area 3-4-A-D-3"] -.-> N
    R["Net Work = WC - WT = Enclosed Area"] -.-> M

    style L fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
    style O fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000
```

> **Reading Note:** In the P–v diagram, the *enclosed area* of the cycle 1-2-3-4 represents the **net work input** to the system. The cycle proceeds **anti-clockwise** on the P–v diagram (unlike power cycles, which go clockwise) — this is the visual signature of a *refrigeration cycle*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three advantages of using air as a refrigerant in aircraft refrigeration systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Air is readily available** at any altitude — no need to carry refrigerant, reducing aircraft weight and fire hazard.
2. **Air does not undergo phase change** in the operating range, eliminating the need for condensers/evaporators and avoiding flooding problems.
3. **Wide operating temperature range** — air remains gaseous from −60 °C (stratosphere) to +200 °C (post-compression), making it suitable for diverse flight conditions.
*(Optional 4th point: low toxicity and non-flammability enhance cabin safety.)* ✅ **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the expression for the COP of a simple air refrigeration cycle in terms of the pressure ratio, and explain why the COP decreases with increasing pressure ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$ \text{COP} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1} $$

**Explanation:** As the pressure ratio $r_p$ increases, the denominator $r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1$ increases faster than the numerator (which is 1). Since the exponent $(\gamma-1)/\gamma > 0$, the term $r_p^{0.2857}$ grows with $r_p$, causing the COP to fall. Physically, higher $r_p$ requires more compressor work, but the refrigeration effect grows proportionally less. ✅ **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**Question A(a) [7 marks]:** With the aid of a P–v and T–s diagram, explain the working of a **Simple Air Refrigeration Cycle (Bell–Coleman Cycle)**. Derive an expression for its **COP in terms of the pressure ratio**.

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
The Bell–Coleman cycle consists of four processes performed on air as the working fluid:

1. **Process 1→2 — Compression:** Air from the cabin (at $P_1$, $T_1$) is compressed in a *reciprocating or axial compressor* to a higher pressure $P_2$. The compression is isentropic (reversible adiabatic), raising the temperature to $T_2$.

2. **Process 2→3 — Cooling:** The hot compressed air flows through a *ram-air heat exchanger*, where it is cooled by the outside air (ram air). The cooling is isobaric (at constant $P_2$), and the temperature falls to $T_3$.

3. **Process 3→4 — Expansion:** The cooled, high-pressure air is then expanded in a *turbine* back to the low pressure $P_1$. This isentropic expansion produces cold air at $T_4$.

4. **Process 4→1 — Cooling Effect:** This very cold air at $T_4$ is then directed to the aircraft cabin, where it absorbs heat from the cabin air, raising its temperature back to $T_1$ (and the cycle repeats).

**Diagrams:** The T–s and P–v diagrams show two isobars ($P_1$ and $P_2$) connected by two isentropes (1-2 and 3-4). The cycle proceeds **anti-clockwise** on P–v, indicating net work input.

**Derivation of COP:**
We have shown in Section 3 that:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{k} \quad \text{and} \quad T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_p^{-k}
$$

For the ideal case where $T_3 = T_1$ (perfect cooling), and using $RE = C_p(T_1 - T_4)$ and $W_{net} = C_p[(T_2-T_1) - (T_3-T_4)]$, simplification gives:

$$
\boxed{\text{COP} = \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1}}
$$

✅ **(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for explanation, 2 for derivation)**

---

**Question A(b) [7 marks]:** A simple air refrigeration system operates between pressure limits of **1 bar and 4 bar**. The temperature of the air at the **entry of the compressor is 20 °C** and at the **exit of the cooler is 30 °C**. Assuming isentropic compression and expansion with $\gamma = 1.4$, calculate:
(i) The COP of the system
(ii) The mass of air in kg/s required to produce **5 tonnes** of refrigeration

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1$ bar, $P_2 = 4$ bar → $r_p = 4$
- $T_1 = 20 + 273 = 293$ K
- $T_3 = 30 + 273 = 303$ K
- $\gamma = 1.4$ → $k = 0.2857$
- $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Capacity: 5 TR = $5 \times 3.517 = 17.585$ kW

**Step 1:** Compute $T_2$ and $T_4$:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{k} = 293 \times 4^{0.2857} = 293 \times 1.486 = 435.4 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{k}} = \frac{303}{1.486} = 203.9 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute RE and Net Work:

$$
RE = C_p(T_1 - T_4) = 1.005 \times (293 - 203.9) = 1.005 \times 89.1 = 89.54 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{net} = C_p[(T_2 - T_1) - (T_3 - T_4)] = 1.005 \times [(435.4 - 293) - (303 - 203.9)]
$$

$$
W_{net} = 1.005 \times [142.4 - 99.1] = 1.005 \times 43.3 = 43.52 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3:** COP:

$$
\boxed{\text{COP} = \frac{89.54}{43.52} = 2.058}
$$

**Step 4:** Mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{17.585}{89.54} = 0.1964 \text{ kg/s} \approx 0.196 \text{ kg/s}
$$

✅ **(7 marks — 2 for setup, 3 for computation, 2 for final answers)**

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Question B(a) [7 marks]:** Compare the **Simple Air Refrigeration Cycle** and the **Bootstrap Air Refrigeration Cycle** with suitable T–s diagrams. Under what conditions is the Bootstrap cycle preferred?

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| **Feature** | **Simple (Bell–Coleman)** | **Bootstrap** |
|-------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Number of Compressors | 1 | 2 (Main + Bootstrap) |
| Number of Expanders | 1 | 1 (Main cooling turbine) |
| Heat Exchangers | 1 (Main cooler) | 2 (Primary + After-cooler) |
| Cabin temp. achievable | Limited by ambient | Below ambient (better) |
| COP at high altitude | Drops sharply | Remains reasonable |
| Best for | Low-altitude subsonic | High-altitude / supersonic |

**T–s Diagram Notes:**
- The **Simple cycle** is a quadrilateral (1-2-3-4) on T–s.
- The **Bootstrap cycle** has an *additional closed loop* (the ram-air sub-loop: 8-9-10-11) that drives the main loop's heat exchanger below ambient.

**When Bootstrap is Preferred:**
- When the aircraft operates at **high altitudes** where ambient ram air is too cold to be effective as a heat sink for the *main* compressed air.
- When **lower cabin temperatures** are required (e.g., supersonic aircraft where aerodynamic heating raises fuselage temperature).
- When a **secondary use** of the bootstrap compressor (pneumatic power for instruments) is desired.

✅ **(7 marks)**

---

**Question B(b) [7 marks]:** An aircraft refrigeration system uses a **bootstrap cycle**. The main compressor takes air at $0.9$ bar, $5$ °C and compresses it to $3.6$ bar. The pressure ratio of the bootstrap compressor is $1.5$. The temperature of the air leaving the primary cooler is $25$ °C, and it expands in the main turbine to $0.9$ bar. Find the **COP of the system**.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 0.9$ bar, $T_1 = 5 + 273 = 278$ K
- $P_2 = 3.6$ bar → $r_{p,main} = 3.6/0.9 = 4$
- Bootstrap pressure ratio = 1.5 (not directly used in main COP but increases cooling effectiveness)
- $T_3 = 25 + 273 = 298$ K
- $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $k = 0.2857$

**Step 1:** $T_2$ and $T_4$:

$$
T_2 = 278 \times 4^{0.2857} = 278 \times 1.486 = 413.1 \text{ K}
$$

$$
T_4 = \frac{298}{1.486} = 200.5 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2:** RE and Net Work:

$$
RE = 1.005 \times (278 - 200.5) = 1.005 \times 77.5 = 77.89 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_{net} = 1.005 \times [(413.1 - 278) - (298 - 200.5)]
$$

$$
W_{net} = 1.005 \times [135.1 - 97.5] = 1.005 \times 37.6 = 37.79 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3:** COP:

$$
\boxed{\text{COP} = \frac{77.89}{37.79} = 2.061}
$$

✅ **(7 marks — full marks for correct stepwise calculation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** — Always add 273. *(−2 to −3 marks if missed)*
> 2. **Confusing $C_p$ for air (1.005) with $C_v$ (0.718)** — For aircraft *open* systems with sensible heat, use $C_p$.
> 3. **Inverting the COP formula** — Some students write $\text{COP} = W_{net}/RE$ instead of $RE/W_{net}$. Always cross-check.
> 4. **Skipping the T–s or P–v diagram** — In Part B, a diagram carries **at least 3 of 7 marks**. Draw it even if rough.
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners *will* deduct for missing units (kJ/kg, kg/s, etc.).
> 6. **Mixing up the bootstrap and RAT cycles** — The RAT has *two expanders*; the bootstrap has *two compressors*. Don't confuse them.

---

## 5.3 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before you leave this page, internalize these 15 high-yield points:**

- ☐ The **aircraft refrigeration cycle** uses **air as the working fluid** (gas cycle, no phase change).
- ☐ The basic cycle is the **Bell–Coleman (Simple) cycle** — a *reverse Brayton* cycle.
- ☐ Four processes: **Isentropic compression → Isobaric cooling → Isentropic expansion → Isobaric heat absorption**.
