- ☐ The T–s diagram is a **quadrilateral** with the *upper isobar* at $P_2$ and *lower isobar* at $P_1$.
- ☐ The cycle runs **anti-clockwise** on the P–v diagram.
- ☐ Key constants: **$C_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)**, **$\gamma = 1.4$**, **$k = 0.2857$**.
- ☐ Master formula: **$\text{COP} = 1 / [r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1]$** for perfect cooling.
- ☐ **Higher pressure ratio → Lower COP** (compressor work grows faster than RE).
- ☐ **Net work input** = Compressor work − Turbine work.
- ☐ **Bootstrap cycle** = Simple cycle + extra (bootstrap) compressor on the ram-air side → enables cooling *below ambient*.
- ☐ **RAT cycle** = Simple cycle + *two expansion turbines* in series → extremely low cabin temperature.
- ☐ **Reversed Carnot** gives the *theoretical maximum* COP, but it is *impractical* because gases cannot undergo isothermal heat transfer with finite-area heat exchangers.
- ☐ Aircraft air cycle is preferred for **fire safety**, **altitude flexibility**, and **low mass** — even though its COP is *lower* than vapor-compression cycles.
- ☐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW** (memorize!).
- ☐ In a numerical problem: **always convert temperatures to Kelvin first**, then compute $r_p^k$, then $T_2$ and $T_4$, then $RE$ and $W_{net}$, and finally COP.

> **🌟 Pro-Tip for KTU Exam Day:** When the question says *"air refrigeration system"*, the examiner expects you to default to the **Bell–Coleman framework** unless the question explicitly says *Bootstrap* or *RAT*. The choice of cycle is a *14-marker* differentiator — picking the right cycle and drawing the right T–s diagram is worth 4–5 marks by itself.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition of refrigeration and air conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definition of refrigeration and air conditioning # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Refrigeration **Refrigeration** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the process of **removing heat from a low-temperature body/space and transferring it to a high-temperature body/space** with the help of an external agency (work input), so that the temperature of the low-temperature body is maintained **lower than the surrounding atmosphere**. In simpler thermodynamic terms, refrigeration is a **non-spontaneous cooling process** which is made to occur continuously only by **supplying external work** to the system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > *Refrigeration is the process of producing and maintaining a temperature lower than that of the surroundings in a given space or body by removing heat from that space or body.* ### 1.2 Definition of Air Conditioning **Air Conditioning (A/C)** is the process of **conditioning the air** inside a closed space to simultaneously control its four primary properties — **Temperature, Humidity, Cleanliness (Purity), and Motion (Velocity/Distribution)** — to provide **human comfort** or to maintain conditions favourable for industrial/commercial processes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** > *Air conditioning is the simultaneous control of temperature, humidity, cleanliness, and motion of air within an enclosure to meet the comfort requirements of occupants or the requirements of a process.* ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 1 — Refrigeration = A Water Pump for Heat** > Just as a water pump lifts water from a lower level to a higher level (which cannot happen naturally), a refrigeration system **pumps heat from a cold region to a hot region** (which again cannot happen naturally). The pump needs external energy (electricity) to do this. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy 2 — Air Conditioning = A Doctor's Prescription** > Just as a doctor doesn't just check your temperature but also your blood pressure, oxygen level, and pulse rate, **Air Conditioning doesn't just cool the room**. It also controls **moisture (humidity), filters dust/microbes (cleanliness), and circulates fresh air (motion)**. In short, A/C = Comfort Package. ### 1.4 Unit of Refrigeration The standard metric used to rate a refrigeration system is the **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)**. - **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min ≈ 12600 kJ/hr** - It is defined as the rate of heat extraction required to **freeze 1 ton (1000 kg) of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**. ### 1.5 Key Difference Between Refrigeration and A/C | Feature | Refrigeration | Air Conditioning | |---|---|---| | **Primary Goal** | Cool a space/substance below ambient | Provide total comfort (T, RH, purity, motion) | | **Scope** | Narrow — only cooling | Wide — cooling + heating + humidification + dehumidification + filtration | | **Application** | Cold storage, ice plants, food preservation | Buildings, theatres, cars, hospitals, clean rooms | | **Human Comfort?** | Not necessary (often for preserving goods) | Primary objective | | **Example** | Domestic fridge, deep freezer | Central A/C, split A/C, window A/C | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Do We Need Refrigeration? Refrigeration is fundamentally required because **heat naturally flows from hot to cold**. To reverse this flow (i.e., to move heat from cold to hot), **external work input is required** — this is the Second Law of Thermodynamics (Clausius Statement). ### 2.2 Working Principle of a Refrigeration System A refrigeration system operates on a **reversed heat engine cycle**. The four basic components of any vapour compression refrigeration system are: 1. **Compressor** — Receives low-pressure refrigerant vapour and compresses it to high pressure (work input is added here). 2. **Condenser** — High-pressure hot refrigerant rejects heat to the surroundings and condenses into liquid. 3. **Expansion Device (Throttle Valve / Capillary Tube)** — Liquid refrigerant expands to low pressure, causing temperature drop (Joule–Thomson effect). 4. **Evaporator** — Low-pressure cold refrigerant absorbs heat from the space to be cooled, evaporating into vapour. ### 2.3 Coefficient of Performance (COP) — The Key Metric COP is the **figure of merit** for any refrigeration/heat pump system. It tells us how efficiently the system converts work input into cooling/heating effect. **For a Refrigerator (Refrigeration COP):** $$COP_{R} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_{L}}{W_{net,in}}$$ **For a Heat Pump (Heating COP):** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_{H}}{W_{net,in}}$$ **Important Relationship:** $$COP_{HP} = COP_{R} + 1$$ ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Term | Expression | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **1 Ton of Refrigeration** | $1 \, TR = 3.517 \, kW$ | kW | Heat removal rate | | 2 | **1 TR in kJ/hr** | $1 \, TR = 12600 \, kJ/hr$ | kJ/hr | Standard board value | | 3 | **Refrigeration COP** | $COP_{R} = \dfrac{Q_{L}}{W_{net}}$ | Dimensionless | Always > 1 | | 4 | **Heat Pump COP** | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_{H}}{W_{net}}$ | Dimensionless | Always > $COP_{R}$ | | 5 | **COP Relationship** | $COP_{HP} = COP_{R} + 1$ | — | Direct derivation | | 6 | **Energy Balance** | $Q_{H} = Q_{L} + W_{net}$ | kW | First Law | | 7 | **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** | $RE = h_{1} - h_{4}$ | kJ/kg | Across evaporator | | 8 | **Compressor Work** | $W_{c} = h_{2} - h_{1}$ | kJ/kg | Isentropic compression | | 9 | **Heat Rejected** | $Q_{H} = h_{2} - h_{3}$ | kJ/kg | At condenser | | 10 | **Heat Pumped (energy)** | $Q_{L} = m \times RE$ | kW | Mass flow basis | | 11 | **Actual COP vs Carnot COP** | $COP_{Carnot,R} = \dfrac{T_{L}}{T_{H} - T_{L}}$ | — | Theoretical max | | 12 | **Carnot Heat Pump COP** | $COP_{Carnot,HP} = \dfrac{T_{H}}{T_{H} - T_{L}}$ | — | Theoretical max | > [!NOTE] > All temperatures used in COP formulas must be in **Kelvin (K)**. Never use °C — this is the #1 silly mistake KTU examiners deduct marks for. ### 2.5 Applications of Refrigeration - **Domestic:** Refrigerators, deep freezers, water coolers. - **Commercial:** Cold storages, ice plants, dairy cooling, breweries. - **Industrial:** Petrochemical, liquefaction of gases (LNG, LOX, LN₂). - **Specialised:** Cryogenic surgery, tissue preservation, vaccine storage. ### 2.6 Applications of Air Conditioning - **Comfort A/C:** Homes, offices, hotels, cars, theatres. - **Industrial A/C:** Textile mills (humidity control), printing presses, pharmaceutical plants. - **Clean Room A/C:** Hospitals (OT), semiconductor fabrication, computer server rooms. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Heat Pump COP and Refrigerator COP This is a **favourite KTU derivation question** (often asked for 7 marks). **Step 1 — Start with First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Balance)** For a refrigeration cycle operating as a control volume, energy entering = energy leaving. Heat is absorbed from cold space ($Q_L$) plus work input ($W_{net}$) equals heat rejected to hot space ($Q_H$). $$Q_{H} = Q_{L} + W_{net}$$ **Step 2 — Write the COP definition for a Refrigerator** By definition, the desired effect in a refrigerator is the heat extracted from the cold space. $$COP_{R} = \frac{Q_{L}}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 3 — Write the COP definition for a Heat Pump** For a heat pump, the desired effect is the heat delivered to the hot space (i.e., warming the room). $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_{H}}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $Q_H$ from Step 1 into Step 3** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_{L} + W_{net}}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 5 — Split the numerator into two fractions** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_{L}}{W_{net}} + \frac{W_{net}}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 6 — Simplify** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_{L}}{W_{net}} + 1$$ **Step 7 — Recognise that the first term is $COP_R$ (from Step 2)** $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_{R} + 1}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Carnot Refrigeration COP The Carnot (reversed) cycle gives the **maximum possible COP** for a refrigeration system operating between two given temperature reservoirs. **Step 1 — For a Carnot (reversible) cycle operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$:** The ratio of heat transfers equals the ratio of absolute temperatures. $$\frac{Q_{H}}{Q_{L}} = \frac{T_{H}}{T_{L}}$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to express $Q_H$ in terms of $Q_L$** $$Q_{H} = Q_{L} \cdot \frac{T_{H}}{T_{L}}$$ **Step 3 — From First Law: $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$** $$W_{net} = Q_{L} \cdot \frac{T_{H}}{T_{L}} - Q_{L} = Q_{L}\left(\frac{T_{H} - T_{L}}{T_{L}}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into $COP_R = Q_L / W_{net}$** $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{Q_{L}}{Q_{L}\left(\dfrac{T_{H} - T_{L}}{T_{L}}\right)}$$ **Step 5 — Cancel $Q_L$ and simplify** $$\boxed{COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_{L}}{T_{H} - T_{L}}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Analytical Matrix (KTU Standard) **Problem:** A refrigeration system extracts $1500 \, kJ$ of heat from a cold space per minute. The work input to the compressor is $300 \, kJ$ per minute. Calculate: - (i) COP of the refrigerator - (ii) COP of the heat pump - (iii) Heat rejected to the surroundings per minute **Given Data:** $$Q_{L} = 1500 \, kJ/min, \quad W_{net} = 300 \, kJ/min$$ **Step (i) — COP of Refrigerator:** $$COP_{R} = \frac{Q_{L}}{W_{net}} = \frac{1500}{300} = \boxed{5.0}$$ **Step (ii) — COP of Heat Pump (using derived formula):** $$COP_{HP} = COP_{R} + 1 = 5.0 + 1.0 = \boxed{6.0}$$ **Step (iii) — Heat Rejected to Surroundings (First Law):** $$Q_{H} = Q_{L} + W_{net} = 1500 + 300 = \boxed{1800 \, kJ/min}$$ **Verification using $COP_{HP}$:** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_{H}}{W_{net}} = \frac{1800}{300} = 6.0 \quad \checkmark$$ --- ## 3.4 Conversion Logic — Analytical Matrix | Step | What we did | Why we did it | |---|---|---| | 1 | Identified desired effect as $Q_L$ | Refrigeration's purpose is cooling | | 2 | Identified input as $W_{net}$ | Work is required (2nd Law) | | 3 | Applied First Law | Energy is conserved always | | 4 | Divided desired / input | Definition of COP | | 5 | Compared both cycles | To show $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Refrigeration Cycle The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional interactions** between the four major components of a refrigeration system. (Note: a true P-h diagram is complex; this functional block architecture conveys the same conceptual flow clearly.) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Comp1
Low Pressure
Vapour] -->|Work Input W| B[Comp2
High Pressure
Hot Vapour] B -->|Heat Rejected QH| C[Cond3
High Pressure
Liquid] C -->|Throttling| D[Exp4
Low Pressure
Cold Liquid Mix] D -->|Heat Absorbed QL| E[Evap5
Low Pressure
Vapour] E --> A F[Surroundings Hot TH] -.rejects QH.-> B G[Space to be Cooled TL] -.supplies QL.-> E ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Air Conditioning Control Parameters ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Input["INDOOR AIR"] IA1[Temperature] IA2[Humidity] IA3[Dust and Particles] IA4[Air Velocity] end subgraph ACU["AIR CONDITIONING UNIT"] ACC[Cooling Coil] ACH[Heating Coil] ACF[Filter] ACB[Blower and Ducts] end subgraph Output["CONDITIONED AIR"] OA1[Comfortable Temperature] OA2[Controlled Humidity] OA3[Clean Purified Air] OA4[Proper Circulation] end IA1 --> ACC IA2 --> ACC IA3 --> ACF IA4 --> ACB ACC --> OA1 ACH --> OA1 ACF --> OA3 ACB --> OA4 IA2 --> ACH ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Mapping — Refrigeration vs Heat Pump ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CY["SAME PHYSICAL CYCLE"] R[Refrigerator Mode] H[Heat Pump Mode] end QL[Heat QL absorbed from Cold Space] QH[Heat QH rejected to Hot Space] W[Work Input W] QL --> R R -->|Desired Output is QL| COPR["COPR = QL divide W"] H -->|Desired Output is QH| COPHP["COPHP = QH divide W"] QH --> H W --> R W --> H COPR --> EQ["COPHP = COPR plus 1"] COPHP --> EQ ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Solid arrows (→) indicate the actual flow of refrigerant. Dotted arrows (-.->) indicate the **direction of heat transfer** with surroundings. Both directions are critical for understanding. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper] **Define refrigeration. State its unit and SI equivalent.** **Model Answer:** Refrigeration is the process of producing and maintaining a temperature below that of the surroundings in a given space or body by continuously removing heat from it using an external work input. **Unit:** Ton of Refrigeration (TR) **SI Equivalent:** $1 \, TR = 3.517 \, kW$ --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model] **Differentiate between refrigeration and air-conditioning.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Refrigeration | Air Conditioning | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Cooling only | Temperature, humidity, purity & motion control | | Application | Food/cold storage | Human comfort / process control | | Human Comfort | Secondary | Primary | --- --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### ⭐ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q. (a)** Define the term **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** for a refrigerator and a heat pump. **Derive the relation** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The Coefficient of Performance is defined as the ratio of the desired effect to the work input required to achieve it. **For a Refrigerator:** $$COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **For a Heat Pump:** $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **First Law of Thermodynamics applied to the cycle:** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} \quad \text{...(3)}$$ Substituting (3) into (2): $$COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_L + W_{net}}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1$$ From equation (1), $\dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = COP_R$. Therefore: $$\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}$$ --- **Q. (b)** A refrigeration system has a **COP of 4.5** and extracts heat at the rate of **$45 \, kW$** from a cold space. Determine: **(i)** Work input to the compressor, **(ii)** Heat rejected to surroundings, **(iii)** COP if the same system is used as a heat pump. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $COP_R = 4.5$, $Q_L = 45 \, kW$ **(i) Work input:** $$W_{net} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{45}{4.5} = \boxed{10 \, kW}$$ **(ii) Heat rejected (First Law):** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} = 45 + 10 = \boxed{55 \, kW}$$ **(iii) COP as Heat Pump:** $$COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 4.5 + 1 = \boxed{5.5}$$ --- ### ⭐ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q. (a)** Explain any **four essential differences** between a refrigeration system and a heat pump. Show that a heat pump with $COP_{HP} = 6$ consumes **less work** than an electric heater for the same heating effect. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | # | Refrigerator | Heat Pump | |---|---|---| | 1 | Desired effect: $Q_L$ (cooling) | Desired effect: $Q_H$ (heating) | | 2 | $COP_R = Q_L / W$ | $COP_{HP} = Q_H / W$ | | 3 | Operates in summer (cooling load) | Operates in winter (heating load) | | 4 | Cold space is the useful region | Hot space is the useful region | **Comparison with electric heater:** For heat pump with $COP_{HP} = 6$: $$W_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{COP_{HP}} = \frac{Q_H}{6} = 0.167 \, Q_H$$ For electric heater (efficiency = 1): $$W_{heater} = Q_H$$ Since $0.167 \, Q_H < Q_H$, the heat pump consumes **6 times less electricity** than an electric heater for the same heat output. ✓ --- **Q. (b)** Define **Ton of Refrigeration**. A refrigerator operates between $-10 \, °C$ and $40 \, °C$. Calculate the **Carnot COP**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** 1 TR is the rate of heat extraction required to freeze 1 metric ton (1000 kg) of water at $0 \, °C$ into ice at $0 \, °C$ in 24 hours. $$1 \, TR = 3.517 \, kW = 12600 \, kJ/hr$$ **Given temperatures (convert to Kelvin):** $$T_L = -10 + 273 = 263 \, K$$ $$T_H = 40 + 273 = 313 \, K$$ **Carnot COP formula:** $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Substitution:** $$COP_{R,Carnot} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = \boxed{5.26}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Kelvin Mistake:** Using °C instead of K in Carnot COP formulas is the **#1 mark-loser**. Always add 273. > 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kW and kJ/min in TR problems. Stick to one unit system per question. > 3. **Sign Confusion:** $Q_H > Q_L$ always. If your answer shows the reverse, the energy balance is wrong. > 4. **No "what is refrigeration" definitions without stating the work input** — KTU examiners demand the **"external agency"** keyword. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Refrigeration** = Cooling below ambient using external work (non-spontaneous process). - ✅ **Air Conditioning** = Control of **T + RH + Purity + Motion** simultaneously. - ✅ **Ton of Refrigeration (TR):** $1 \, TR = 3.517 \, kW = 12600 \, kJ/hr$. - ✅ **COP formula:** $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W}$, $\quad COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W}$. - ✅ **Key relation:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ (must derive if asked). - ✅ **Carnot COP:** $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — use **Kelvin** strictly. - ✅ **First Law:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ — energy cannot be created or destroyed. - ✅ **Components:** Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator (cycle). - ✅ **Heat is the energy transferred**, **Temperature** is the level — never confuse them. - ✅ **Two COP values possible for the SAME cycle** — depending on desired output. - ✅ **Always cite $T_L$ and $T_H$ in Kelvin** — examiners check this first. - ✅ **Heat pump is more efficient than electric heater** — explain with COP > 1 logic.

topics.title: Relationship of refrigeration and air-conditioning fields
Applications of refrigeration and air-conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of refrigeration and air-conditioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigeration** is the process of removing heat from a low-temperature body or space and transferring it to a higher-temperature surroundings, against the natural flow of heat (i.e., against the Second Law of Thermodynamics), through the expenditure of external work. **Air-conditioning** is the simultaneous control (within a prescribed enclosure) of **temperature, humidity, purity (cleanliness)**, and **air motion (circulation)** to maintain the comfort and well-being of human beings or to meet the requirements of a specific industrial process.

The two terms, although overlapping, are distinct in scope. **Refrigeration** primarily aims at *cooling* (heat removal), while **Air-conditioning** aims at the *comprehensive conditioning of air* — cooling, heating, humidification, dehumidification, filtration, and ventilation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket-Pass" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine you are standing at the bottom of a staircase with a bucket of water. Gravity naturally wants the water to flow *downwards* (high → low). To lift that bucket back up the staircase, you must *spend energy*. Refrigeration does precisely this with **heat** — heat naturally flows from hot to cold, but a refrigerator uses *work input* to **pump heat from a cold region back to a hot region**. The "bucket" is the **refrigerant**, the "staircase" is the **refrigeration cycle**, and your "energy" is the **compressor work**.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning?

The universal human need for controlled environments drives these applications:

- **Preservation:** Food, blood, medicines, chemicals, films.
- **Comfort:** Human thermal comfort in tropical/residential/commercial buildings.
- **Process Industry:** Liquefaction of gases, petrochemical refining, polymer production, and metal heat treatment.
- **Transport:** Air-conditioning of cars, ships, aircraft; refrigerated trucks/railways for perishable goods.
- **Cryogenics:** Medical cryosurgery, superconductivity (requiring temperatures below **−150°C**), space research.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** This topic belongs to **Module 1** and is purely **descriptive/applicative**. It is a frequent **2-mark / 3-mark short answer** area in the End Semester Examination (ESE), and serves as a strong **introduction or conclusion** to 14-mark analytical questions on the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** and **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)**.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (KTU 2024 Board)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Human Comfort Temperature | $T_{comfort}$ | **22°C – 27°C** (DBT) |
| Standard Human Comfort RH | $\phi_{comfort}$ | **40% – 60%** |
| Domestic Refrigerator Internal Temp | $T_{fridge}$ | **2°C – 5°C** |
| Freezer Compartment Temp | $T_{freezer}$ | **−18°C – −25°C** |
| Deep Cryogenic Range | $T_{cryo}$ | **Below −150°C (123 K)** |
| Air-conditioning Reference Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** |
| Approximate COP of Domestic Fridge | $COP_{actual}$ | **1.5 – 2.5** |
| Theoretical Carnot COP | $COP_{carnot}$ | $\frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Refrigeration Applications by Temperature Level

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly categorizes refrigeration applications by the **temperature level maintained**. This is fundamental for selecting the appropriate refrigerant and cycle.

### A. High-Temperature Applications (Above **0°C**)
- **Air-conditioning of buildings** (human comfort cooling, **T = 20°C – 25°C**)
- **Industrial process cooling** (textile, plastic molding, paper)
- **Cold storage of fruits and vegetables** (**T = 0°C – 10°C**)

### B. Medium-Temperature Applications (**0°C to −20°C**)
- **Domestic refrigeration / commercial chillers** (food preservation)
- **Beverage cooling** (soft drinks, dairy products)
- **Ice manufacturing plants** (**T = 0°C – −5°C**)
- **Cold storage of fish, meat, and dairy** (**T = −5°C to −15°C**)

### C. Low-Temperature Applications (**−20°C to −80°C**)
- **Industrial freezers** for frozen food storage (**T = −18°C to −25°C**)
- **Frozen food transport (reefer trucks/containers)**
- **Pharmaceutical and blood plasma preservation** (**T = −30°C to −40°C**)
- **Low-temperature chemical processes**

### D. Cryogenic Applications (Below **−150°C / 123 K**)
- **Liquefaction of gases:** Oxygen (**−183°C**), Nitrogen (**−196°C**), Hydrogen (**−253°C**), Helium (**−269°C**)
- **Superconductivity and MRI magnets**
- **Rocket propulsion and space research**
- **Cryo-surgery** (tissue destruction at ultra-low temperatures)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Examiners expect students to write the **temperature range** alongside each application. Writing *"refrigeration is used in food preservation"* without specifying **T = −18°C** loses marks. Always pair application ↔ temperature.

## 2.2 Classification by Sector (KTU 2024 Board Standard)

### 1. Domestic / Household Applications
- Domestic refrigerators, freezers, water coolers, air-conditioners, deep freezers.
- Typical capacity: **0.1 TR – 5 TR**.

### 2. Commercial Applications
- Hotel and restaurant refrigeration, supermarket display cases, walk-in cold rooms, ice plants.
- Capacity: **5 TR – 100 TR** (using centralized chilled-water plants).

### 3. Industrial Applications
- Process cooling in petrochemical, chemical, pharmaceutical, food processing, textile, paper, and steel industries.
- Often use **chilled water (T = 5°C – 15°C)** as secondary coolant.

### 4. Transport Refrigeration
- Reefer trucks, reefer ships, refrigerated railway wagons, aircraft galley cooling.
- Use **containerized refrigeration units** (≈ 10–30 kW each).

### 5. Air-Conditioning Applications
- Centralized AC for malls, hospitals, airports (using **chillers**).
- Window/split AC for residential use.
- Clean room AC for semiconductor/pharmaceutical industries (**T = 22°C ± 0.5°C**, RH **45% ± 5%**).

### 6. Cryogenic & Specialty Applications
- Gas liquefaction, superconducting magnets, space simulators, cryosurgery.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **descriptive introduction topic**, the formulas here are the *universal refrigeration metrics* every KTU paper assumes you know.

| Concept | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** | $RE = \dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_4)$ | Heat extracted per unit time, in **kW** |
| **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Efficiency metric of refrigerator |
| **Carnot COP (Refrigerator)** | $COP_{carnot,R} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Maximum theoretical COP |
| **Heat Rejected at Condenser** | $Q_H = RE + W_{in}$ | Energy balance |
| **1 Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** | $1 \, TR = 3.517 \, kW$ | Standard cooling capacity unit |
| **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)** | $EER = \dfrac{Cooling \, Output \, (BTU/hr)}{Power \, Input \, (W)}$ | Used for AC rating |
| **Indian Seasonal EER (ISEER)** | $ISEER = \dfrac{Total \, Cooling \, (kWh)}{Total \, Power \, Input \, (kWh)}$ | BEE Star Rating metric |
| **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** | $SHF = \dfrac{SH}{SH + LH}$ | Used in air-conditioning coil selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Do not confuse **EER (instantaneous ratio)** with **ISEER (seasonal average ratio)**. BEE Star ratings in India are based on **ISEER**, not EER.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Strict cold chain (**T = 2°C – 8°C**) for vaccines (e.g., COVID-19 mRNA vaccines require **−70°C** — driven by ultra-low temperature refrigeration).
- **Data Centers:** Server rooms require year-round cooling at **T = 18°C – 22°C** to prevent equipment failure.
- **Food Security:** India loses **~₹92,000 crores/year** in post-harvest spoilage — solved by proper **cold storage at 0°C – 4°C**.
- **Sports & Mega Events:** Stadiums and athlete villages require **central HVAC** with capacities of **2000–5000 TR**.
- **Aerospace:** Cryogenic liquid hydrogen and oxygen (LOX/LH2) used in rocket engines must be stored at **−253°C** and **−183°C** respectively.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Application vs. Temperature vs. Refrigerant

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to link each application to its **temperature range, working fluid, and required tonnage**. Below is a board-ready analytical matrix.

| Sector | Application | Required Temp. (°C) | Capacity Range | Typical Refrigerant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Domestic | Refrigerator (food) | **2 – 5** | 0.1 – 0.5 TR | R-134a, R-600a |
| Domestic | Deep Freezer | **−18 to −25** | 0.2 – 1 TR | R-134a, R-404A |
| Commercial | Cold Storage (fruits) | **0 – 10** | 5 – 100 TR | R-22, R-404A |
| Commercial | Ice Plant | **−5 to −10** | 10 – 50 TR | R-22, Ammonia |
| Industrial | Process Cooling (chilled water) | **5 – 15** | 100 – 5000 TR | R-134a, R-1234ze |
| Transport | Reefer Container | **−20 to −30** | 10 – 30 kW | R-404A, R-410A |
| Medical | Blood / Vaccine Storage | **2 – 6** (and −80) | < 0.1 TR | R-134a, cascade |
| Cryogenics | LNG Storage | **−162** | 50 – 500 TR | Mixed cascade |
| Cryogenics | LOX / LIN Production | **−183 / −196** | Multi-stage cascade | Multi-fluid |
| Building AC | Central HVAC | **22 – 25 (room)** | 100 – 5000 TR | R-134a, R-1234yf |

## 3.2 Derivation of the Fundamental COP Formula (Energy-Balance Approach)

Although the topic itself is descriptive, KTU 2024 frequently begins 14-mark questions with a definition like *"Define COP"* and then connects it to the application. Here is the **board-ready derivation**:

**Statement:** The *Coefficient of Performance* (COP) of a refrigerator is the ratio of the desired output (refrigerating effect) to the required input (work input).

**Step 1 — Energy Balance at Condenser:**
By the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (steady-state, control volume):

$$
Q_H = Q_L + W_{in}
$$

where $Q_L$ is the heat absorbed from the cold space (= **Refrigerating Effect, RE**) and $Q_H$ is the heat rejected at the condenser.

**Step 2 — Definition of COP:**

$$
COP = \frac{\text{Desired \, Output}}{\text{Required \, Input}} = \frac{RE}{W_{in}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{in}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $W_{in} = Q_H - Q_L$:**

$$
COP = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

**Step 4 — For a Reversible (Carnot) Cycle,** the heat ratio equals the temperature ratio (in Kelvin):

$$
\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Carnot COP expression:**

$$
COP_{carnot} = \frac{T_L / T_H}{1 - (T_L / T_H)} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation reveals the **single most important design rule** — *the smaller the temperature difference $(T_H - T_L)$, the higher the COP*. This is why domestic refrigerators maintain **T_L ≈ −5°C** rather than −40°C; a colder evaporator would drastically reduce efficiency.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — Application-Based

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU-style 3-mark / 7-mark question solved below.**

**Problem:** A cold storage is to be maintained at **T_L = −5°C** in a region where the ambient temperature is **T_H = 35°C**. The plant capacity is **10 TR**. Calculate: (a) the Carnot COP, (b) the actual power required, and (c) the heat rejected at the condenser.

### Part (a): Carnot COP

Convert to Kelvin: $T_L = -5 + 273 = 268 \, K$, $T_H = 35 + 273 = 308 \, K$.

$$
COP_{carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{268}{308 - 268} = \frac{268}{40} = 6.70
$$

### Part (b): Actual Power Required

$$
RE = 10 \, TR \times 3.517 \, \frac{kW}{TR} = 35.17 \, kW
$$

$$
W_{in} = \frac{RE}{COP} = \frac{35.17}{6.70} = 5.249 \, kW
$$

### Part (c): Heat Rejected at Condenser

$$
Q_H = RE + W_{in} = 35.17 + 5.249 = 40.42 \, kW
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Notice that the condenser rejects **~15% more energy** than the refrigerating effect — this is because the compressor's work becomes additional heat that must be dumped. This is why an AC's outdoor unit is always *warmer* than the room it's cooling.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Master Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchical Classification of Applications

```mermaid
graph TD
    R["Refrigeration and Air-Conditioning"]
    R --> D["Domestic Applications"]
    R --> C["Commercial Applications"]
    R --> I["Industrial Applications"]
    R --> T["Transport Applications"]
    R --> A["Air-Conditioning Applications"]
    R --> CR["Cryogenic Applications"]

    D --> D1["Domestic Refrigerator"]
    D --> D2["Deep Freezer"]
    D --> D3["Window or Split AC"]
    D --> D4["Water Cooler"]

    C --> C1["Cold Storage"]
    C --> C2["Ice Plant"]
    C --> C3["Supermarket Display"]
    C --> C4["Beverage Chiller"]

    I --> I1["Process Cooling"]
    I --> I2["Petrochemical Refining"]
    I --> I3["Pharmaceutical Mfg"]
    I --> I4["Textile Humidification"]

    T --> T1["Reefer Truck"]
    T --> T2["Reefer Ship"]
    T --> T3["Aircraft Galley"]

    A --> A1["Central HVAC"]
    A --> A2["Cleanroom AC"]
    A --> A3["Data Center Cooling"]

    CR --> CR1["LNG Liquefaction"]
    CR --> CR2["Medical Cryosurgery"]
    CR --> CR3["MRI Superconductors"]
    CR --> CR4["Space Research"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — HVAC System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Return Air from Room] --> B[Filter Section]
    B --> C[Cooling and Dehumidification Coil]
    C --> D[Heating Coil]
    D --> E[Blower or Fan]
    E --> F[Conditioned Air Supply]
    F --> G[Occupied Zone]
    G --> H[Heat Gains and Losses]
    H --> A
    I[Chilled Water from Chiller] --> C
    J[Hot Water or Steam] --> D
    K[Condensate Drain] --> C
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Application to Temperature Spectrum

```mermaid
graph TD
    SP["Spectrum of Refrigeration Applications"]
    SP --> P1["High Temp 0C to 30C"]
    SP --> P2["Medium Temp 0C to minus 20C"]
    SP --> P3["Low Temp minus 20C to minus 80C"]
    SP --> P4["Cryogenic Below minus 150C"]

    P1 --> A1["Building AC, Chilled Water"]
    P2 --> A2["Food Preservation, Ice Plants"]
    P3 --> A3["Industrial Freezers, Blood Banks"]
    P4 --> A4["LNG, Liquid Nitrogen, MRI"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams:** The KTU 2024 Board Examiner often awards **extra marks** for clean, well-labeled flow diagrams. The first diagram gives a *bird's-eye view* of all applications; the second illustrates the *closed-loop* nature of HVAC (air returns, gets conditioned, supplied back); the third aligns directly with the **temperature classification** that KTU emphasizes in the syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Expected]**
**"List any six major applications of refrigeration and air-conditioning, and classify them based on the temperature range maintained."**

**Model Answer (Board-Style, 3 Marks):**

1. **Domestic refrigeration** — for food preservation, **T = 2°C to 5°C** (high-temp).
2. **Industrial process cooling** — chilled water at **5°C to 15°C** (high-temp).
3. **Cold storage of fish and meat** — **T = −5°C to −15°C** (medium-temp).
4. **Deep freezers for frozen food** — **T = −18°C to −25°C** (low-temp).
5. **Central air-conditioning of buildings** — **T = 22°C to 25°C** (comfort AC).
6. **Cryogenic liquefaction of gases** — **T = −183°C (LOX), −196°C (LIN)** (cryogenic).

> **Mark Split:** (i) Any 3 applications × 0.5 = 1.5 marks. (ii) Any 3 temperature ranges × 0.5 = 1.5 marks. **Total = 3 marks.**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**"Define refrigeration and air-conditioning. How do they differ?"**

**Model Answer (Board-Style, 3 Marks):**

- **Refrigeration:** The process of removing heat from a body or space to maintain it at a temperature *below the surrounding ambient*, requiring external work input (against the natural heat flow direction). *Primary goal: COOLING.*
- **Air-conditioning:** The simultaneous regulation of **temperature, humidity, air purity, and air motion** within an enclosed space for human comfort or industrial process requirements. *Primary goal: TOTAL COMFORT / PROCESS CONTROL.*
- **Key Difference:** Refrigeration *primarily* deals with temperature reduction only; air-conditioning is a broader umbrella that includes refrigeration (cooling) + heating + humidification + dehumidification + filtration + ventilation.

> **Mark Split:** Definition 1.5 marks + Difference 1.5 marks = **3 marks.**

---

## 5.2 PART B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected]**

**"With neat sketches, explain the various applications of refrigeration and air-conditioning. Classify them according to the temperature range maintained, and explain at least two cryogenic applications in detail."**

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Classification of applications by temperature range.**

*(Student should present the table from Section 3.1 with all four temperature levels: High, Medium, Low, Cryogenic. Each level should be explained with 2-3 application examples and the corresponding temperature values.)*

**Model Structure (Board-Valued):**

- **High-Temperature (Above 0°C):** Central AC of buildings (T = 22°C – 25°C), cold storage of vegetables (T = 0°C – 10°C), industrial chilled water plants (T = 5°C – 15°C).
- **Medium-Temperature (0°C to −20°C):** Domestic refrigerators, ice plants (T = −5°C – −10°C), dairy industry (T = 0°C – 5°C), pharmaceutical storage.
- **Low-Temperature (−20°C to −80°C):** Industrial freezers, blood banks (T = −30°C), frozen food transport, vaccine preservation.
- **Cryogenic (Below −150°C):** LNG liquefaction, space research, cryosurgery, MRI, superconducting magnets.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: Two cryogenic applications explained in detail.**

**Application 1: Liquefaction of Gases (LNG / LOX / LIN)**
- Natural gas is liquefied to LNG at **−162°C** for ease of transport. Air is separated into Oxygen and Nitrogen by *Linde's double-column* at **−183°C and −196°C**.
- Uses: Rocket propulsion (LOX), medical oxygen, industrial nitrogen.

**Application 2: MRI and Superconductivity**
- MRI magnets use **Niobium-Titanium (NbTi) superconducting coils** cooled to **4.2 K (−269°C)** by liquid helium.
- Once energized, the magnet operates *persistently* with zero electrical resistance, producing the strong uniform field (1.5 T – 3 T) required for medical imaging.

> **Mark Split for Q-A:** (a) 7 marks [4 levels × 1.5 marks + neat table/flow = 1 mark]. (b) 2 applications × 3 marks = 6 marks + diagram = 1 mark.

---

### ✅ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Expected]**

**"Explain the major applications of refrigeration in: (a) Food Preservation, and (b) Chemical and Process Industries. Also discuss the role of refrigeration in the medical and transport sectors."**

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Food preservation.**

- **Refrigerated storage** slows bacterial and enzymatic activity. Fishes/meat stored at **−18°C**, fruits at **0°C – 4°C**, dairy at **2°C – 6°C**.
- **Cold chain** from farm to consumer ensures 80% spoilage reduction.
- **Freeze-drying (lyophilization)** at low temperature preserves food, used in space missions.
- **Quick Freezing** at **−40°C** prevents ice-crystal damage to cells, used in IQF (Individually Quick Frozen) products.
- Example: India needs **~70 million tonnes** of cold storage capacity but has only **~32 million tonnes** — a key infrastructure gap.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: Chemical, medical, and transport sectors.**

**Chemical & Process:**
- **Petrochemical refining** uses propane/ethane chillers at **T = −40°C** to separate hydrocarbons.
- **Polymer (polyethylene, nylon) manufacturing** requires precise cooling at **5°C – 15°C**.
- **LNG processing** at **−162°C** is the largest cryogenic process industry.

**Medical:**
- **Blood plasma storage** at **−30°C**, vaccines at **2°C – 8°C**, and mRNA vaccines (e.g., Pfizer) at **−70°C** in ultra-low freezers.
- **Cryosurgery** uses liquid nitrogen spray to destroy cancerous tissue.
- **MRI superconductors** operate at **4.2 K** using liquid helium.

**Transport:**
- **Reefer containers** (≈ 30 kW refrigeration unit) maintain **T = −25°C** for frozen goods over 30-day sea voyages.
- **Aircraft galley chilling** at **5°C – 10°C** for in-flight meals.
- **Refrigerated trucks** use eutectic plates for **2°C – 8°C** pharma transport.

> **Mark Split for Q-B:** (a) Food preservation = 7 marks (4 points). (b) Chemical + Medical + Transport = 7 marks (3 sectors × 2.3 marks each).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT write only application names** without specifying the **temperature range** — loses up to **30% marks**.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse "refrigeration" and "air-conditioning"** — they are related but not identical.
> 3. **Do NOT write vague statements** like *"refrigeration is used in industries"* — be specific (e.g., *"cryogenic liquefaction of natural gas at −162°C"*).
> 4. **Do NOT skip neat diagrams** — KTU 2024 explicitly awards **1–2 marks** for labeled flow diagrams in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **Do NOT forget units** — temperature in °C, capacity in TR, COP is dimensionless.
> 6. **Do NOT list 15 applications without categorization** — examiners expect **classification by temperature** as the primary structure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save before ESE!):**

- ✅ **Refrigeration** = heat removal; **Air-conditioning** = temperature + humidity + purity + air motion control.
- ✅ Applications are classified by **temperature level**: High (above 0°C), Medium (0°C to −20°C), Low (−20°C to −80°C), Cryogenic (below −150°C).
- ✅ **Domestic:** Refrigerator (2°C – 5°C), Freezer (−18°C), Window/Split AC (22°C – 25°C).
- ✅ **Commercial:** Cold storage (0°C – 10°C), Ice plant (−5°C), Supermarket display (−2°C).
- ✅ **Industrial:** Chilled water (5°C – 15°C), Process cooling, LNG (−162°C).
- ✅ **Transport:** Reefer container (−25°C), Aircraft galley (5°C – 10°C).
- ✅ **Medical:** Blood bank (−30°C), Vaccine cold chain (2°C – 8°C), Cryosurgery, MRI (4.2 K).
- ✅ **Cryogenic gases:** LOX at **−183°C**, LIN at **−196°C**, LH2 at **−253°C**, LHe at **−269°C**.
- ✅ **Capacity unit:** 1 TR = **3.517 kW** of cooling.
- ✅ **COP formula:** $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ (Carnot).
- ✅ **EER vs ISEER:** EER is instantaneous; ISEER is seasonal (BEE Star Rating basis).
- ✅ **Design rule:** *Smaller temperature difference = Higher COP.*
- ✅ Always pair **application ↔ temperature range ↔ typical refrigerant** in answers.
- ✅ Include a **neat labeled diagram** in any 14-mark question for **1–2 extra marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit of refrigeration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unit of refrigeration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Refrigeration?

**Refrigeration** is the process of removing heat from a low-temperature body (or space) and transferring it to a high-temperature body (or surroundings) with the help of an external work input (mechanical, electrical, or heat).

Mathematically, refrigeration is the **inverse of the natural flow of heat**. While heat naturally flows from hot → cold (Second Law of Thermodynamics), refrigeration forces heat to flow from cold → hot using external energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (PEMET751 - Module 1):**  
> *"Refrigeration is the process of maintaining a temperature lower than that of the surroundings by removing heat from a body."*  
> — The heat removed is termed **refrigeration effect** or **cooling effect (Q̇_L)**.

---

### 1.2 The Need for a "Unit" of Refrigeration

Just as we measure **power in Watts (W)** and **force in Newtons (N)**, the cooling capacity of a refrigerator or A/C system must also have a standardized unit. The most widely used commercial and industrial unit is the **Tonne of Refrigeration (TR)**.

Without a standard unit, a manufacturer in Kerala cannot communicate the cooling capacity of a 5-star AC to a buyer in Germany — units provide a **common engineering language**.

---

### 1.3 The Tonne of Refrigeration (TR) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tonne of Refrigeration (TR) — Standard KTU Definition:**  
> One tonne of refrigeration is defined as the **rate of heat removal required to freeze (or melt) one metric ton (1000 kg) of pure water into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**, from and at a temperature of 0 °C.

**Reference conditions:**
- Mass of water/ice: **1 tonne = 1000 kg** (metric ton, as per SI-aligned practice used in KTU)
- Initial/final temperature: **0 °C**
- Time: **24 hours (86,400 seconds)**
- Latent heat of fusion of water at 0 °C: **335 kJ/kg** (or ≈ 144 BTU/lb in imperial units)

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Ice Block Story"

Imagine you have a **1,000 kg block of ice** at 0 °C sitting in a 0 °C room. To melt that entire block back into water at 0 °C, you need to "pump" heat **into** the ice at a very specific rate.

Now flip the picture — imagine a **refrigerator's job** is to freeze 1,000 kg of water (already at 0 °C) into ice in exactly 24 hours. The fridge must **remove** exactly the same amount of heat that the block absorbed while melting. That rate of heat removal is — by definition — **1 TR**.

In simple words:  
🧊 *"1 TR = the cooling power of a machine that can freeze 1 tonne of water into ice in 1 day."*

---

### 1.5 The SI Unit — Kilowatt (kW)

Since refrigeration is fundamentally a **rate of heat transfer** (Energy / Time), its SI unit is:

$$\text{Unit of refrigeration (SI)} = \frac{\text{Joule (J)}}{\text{Second (s)}} = \text{Watt (W)}$$

For practical commercial systems, the unit is expressed in **kW (kilowatts)**.

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU Exams:**  
> *"**3.5 kilo-Watt** = **1 Ton**" → a 1.5 TR split AC ≈ **5.27 kW** of cooling capacity.*

---

### 1.6 Other Units in Practice

| Unit | Symbol | Region | Equivalence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tonne of Refrigeration | TR / TOR | India, Europe, Asia | **1 TR = 3.517 kW** |
| Kilowatt | kW | SI (Global) | 1 kW = 0.2843 TR |
| British Thermal Unit/hr | BTU/hr | USA, UK | 1 TR = 12,000 BTU/hr |
| Kilojoule/hour | kJ/hr | Scientific | 1 TR = 12,662.8 kJ/hr |
| Horsepower (Refrigeration) | HP | Older texts | 1 TR = 4.716 HP |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students confuse **BTU (energy)** with **BTU/hr (power)**. In refrigeration, ALWAYS use **BTU per hour** (a rate), never BTU alone.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Behind the TR Definition

The Tonne of Refrigeration is **not arbitrary** — it is rooted in the **latent heat of fusion of water**. Here is the underlying logic:

**Step 1 — Identify the reference mass:**  
The metric system uses **1 tonne (1000 kg)** as the reference mass.

**Step 2 — Identify the thermodynamic property:**  
To convert water at 0 °C → ice at 0 °C, we must remove the **latent heat of fusion** ($L_f$).

**Step 3 — Look up the physical constant:**  
For pure water at 0 °C and 1 atm:
$$L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg} = 335{,}000 \text{ J/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total heat to be removed:**
$$Q_{\text{total}} = m \times L_f = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg} = 335{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by the reference time (24 hours = 86,400 seconds):**
$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{t} = \frac{335{,}000 \text{ kJ}}{24 \text{ h}} = 12{,}662.8 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kW (kJ/s):**
$$1 \text{ TR} = \frac{335{,}000}{86{,}400} \text{ kW} \approx 3.876 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the value 3.517 kW instead of 3.876 kW?**  
> The **3.517 kW** value is the **standard KTU / international convention** that uses the imperial ton (2000 lb = 907.185 kg). Indian engineering colleges (and ISHRAE) follow this imperial-based TR. The exact derivation in Section 3 will clarify this.

---

### 2.2 The Imperial Tradition Behind "Tonne"

The unit "ton" in refrigeration originates from the **pre-electric era**, when natural ice blocks harvested from frozen lakes (US, Scandinavia) were the primary cooling source. A "ton of refrigeration" literally meant the cooling provided by **one ton of melting ice per day**. When mechanical refrigeration was invented, engineers preserved the unit for continuity.

KTU uses the **"commercial TR"** standard, which is internationally accepted:

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} = 3.5168 \text{ kW} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW}}$$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Numercial Value | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1 \text{ TR}$ (definition) | Heat to freeze **1 ton** water in **24 h** | — | Definition |
| 2 | $1 \text{ TR} = \dfrac{m \cdot L_f}{24}$ | $12{,}000$ | BTU/hr | Quick MCQ |
| 3 | $1 \text{ TR} = \dfrac{m \cdot L_f}{24 \times 60}$ | $211.05$ | kJ/min | Part A derivations |
| 4 | $1 \text{ TR} = \dfrac{m \cdot L_f}{86{,}400}$ | $3.517$ | kW | Most KTU problems |
| 5 | $1 \text{ kW}$ | $0.2843$ | TR | Inverse conversion |
| 6 | $1 \text{ kW}$ | $3412.14$ | BTU/hr | SI ↔ Imperial |
| 7 | $1 \text{ BTU}$ | $1055.06$ | J | Energy conversion |
| 8 | $1 \text{ TR}$ | $50.4$ | kJ/min (using $L_f = 80$ kcal/kg) | Older Indian textbooks |
| 9 | $1 \text{ HP (refrigeration)}$ | $2545$ | BTU/hr | Old units |
| 10 | $1 \text{ kcal}$ | $4.1868$ | kJ | Energy conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always carry out unit conversions using the **dimensional consistency check** — the units on both sides of an equation MUST match. KTU examiners love to deduct marks for unit mismatches in 14-mark problems.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the "TR" Unit

1. **AC Selection for Buildings:** A 1.5 TR AC is recommended for a 100–150 sq. ft. room in Indian climate conditions.
2. **Cold Storage Design:** A cold storage for 100 tonnes of potatoes requires a refrigeration unit rated around 5–8 TR.
3. **Industrial Chillers:** Pharmaceutical and dairy plants use chillers rated from 5 TR to 500 TR.
4. **Energy Audits:** TR is the standard unit in **BEE star rating** labels in India (ISEER = Indian Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio, measured in BTU/Wh).

---

### 2.5 The "Why" — Why Not Just Use Watts?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Historical Continuity** | Industry is familiar with "tonnage"; changing it would confuse technicians and operators. |
| **Intuitive Sizing** | A "1 TR AC" is easier to visualize than a "3.517 kW AC." |
| **Commercial Standard** | Manufacturers, BEE, ISHRAE, and HVAC designers all use TR. |
| **Numerical Convenience** | 1 TR ≈ 12,000 BTU/hr is a clean round number for load calculations. |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Just as we still use **horsepower (HP)** for car engines despite having **kilowatts (kW)** as the SI unit, the refrigeration industry retains "TR" as a practical, intuitive commercial unit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Converting 1 TR to kW (The Most Common KTU Problem)

> **Given:**  
> - 1 ton (imperial, refrigeration) = **2000 lb** (US/UK standard)  
> - 1 lb = **0.4536 kg**  
> - Latent heat of fusion of ice ($L_f$) = **335 kJ/kg** = **144 BTU/lb**  
> - 1 day = **24 hours** = **86,400 seconds**

**Step 1 — Convert 1 ton into kg:**
$$1 \text{ ton} = 2000 \text{ lb} \times 0.4536 \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{lb}} = 907.185 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — Total heat to be removed in 24 hours:**
$$Q = m \cdot L_f = 907.185 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Performing the multiplication:
$$Q = 907.185 \times 335 = 303{,}907 \text{ kJ (approx.)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert time to seconds:**
$$t = 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 86{,}400 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the rate (Power = Energy / Time):**
$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{303{,}907 \text{ kJ}}{86{,}400 \text{ s}}$$

$$\dot{Q}_L = 3.5175 \text{ kJ/s} = 3.5175 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} \approx 3.517 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "2000 lb" and not "1000 kg"?**  
> The 3.517 kW value is the **international commercial standard** used by ASHRAE, ISHRAE, and KTU. The "metric ton" (1000 kg) approach gives ≈ 3.876 kW, which is **not** the KTU convention. KTU students must memorize and use **3.517 kW** in all problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Converting 1 TR to kJ/min (For KTU Part A)

Using the same values as above:

**Step 1 — Total heat in 24 h:**
$$Q = 303{,}907 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Divide by 24 × 60 minutes:**
$$t = 24 \times 60 = 1440 \text{ min}$$

$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{303{,}907 \text{ kJ}}{1440 \text{ min}}$$

$$\dot{Q}_L = 211.05 \text{ kJ/min}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} \approx 211 \text{ kJ/min}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Converting 1 TR to BTU/hr

**Step 1 — Use imperial units directly:**
- Mass: 2000 lb  
- $L_f$ in imperial: **144 BTU/lb**  
- Time: 24 h

**Step 2 — Total heat in BTU:**
$$Q = 2000 \text{ lb} \times 144 \frac{\text{BTU}}{\text{lb}} = 288{,}000 \text{ BTU}$$

**Step 3 — Rate in BTU/hr:**
$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{288{,}000 \text{ BTU}}{24 \text{ h}} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **definitionally exact** value used in HVAC load sheets. KTU examiners will accept 12,000 BTU/hr as the standard equivalence.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Converting 1 TR to Horsepower (Older Engineering Practice)

**Step 1 — Convert BTU/hr to BTU/s:**
$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}}{3600 \text{ s/hr}} = 3.333 \text{ BTU/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert BTU/s to hp (1 hp = 2545 BTU/hr = 0.7067 BTU/s):**
$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{3.333 \text{ BTU/s}}{0.7067 \text{ BTU/s per hp}} = 4.716 \text{ hp}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} \approx 4.716 \text{ HP (refrigeration)}}$$

---

### 3.5 Master Conversion Table (Analytical Matrix)

| To Convert | Multiply by | Quick Formula |
|---|---|---|
| TR → kW | 3.517 | $\text{TR} \times 3.517$ |
| TR → BTU/hr | 12,000 | $\text{TR} \times 12{,}000$ |
| TR → kJ/hr | 12,662.8 | $\text{TR} \times 12{,}662.8$ |
| TR → kcal/hr | 3,024 | $\text{TR} \times 3024$ |
| kW → TR | 0.2843 | $\text{kW} \div 3.517$ |
| BTU/hr → TR | $8.33 \times 10^{-5}$ | $\text{BTU/hr} \div 12{,}000$ |
| BTU → kJ | 1.055 | $\text{BTU} \times 1.055$ |
| kJ → kcal | 0.239 | $\text{kJ} \times 0.239$ |
| 1 TR (in 1 minute) | 211 kJ | (used for rate problems) |
| 1 kWh thermal | 0.2843 TR·h | (energy, not power) |

---

### 3.6 Quick Numerical Practice (For Reinforcement)

**Example 1:** A cold storage plant has a refrigeration load of **150 kW**. Express it in TR.
$$\text{TR} = 150 \div 3.517 = 42.65 \text{ TR}$$

**Example 2:** A 1.5 TR split AC operates for 8 hours. What is the total heat removed in kJ?
$$\dot{Q}_L = 1.5 \times 211.05 = 316.58 \text{ kJ/min}$$
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 316.58 \times 8 \times 60 = 151{,}958 \text{ kJ} \approx 152 \text{ MJ}$$

**Example 3:** A milk chilling plant has a load of 50,000 BTU/hr. Convert to kW.
$$\text{Load (kW)} = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}}{3412 \text{ BTU/hr per kW}} = 14.65 \text{ kW}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of "Unit of Refrigeration"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reference Phenomenon: Melting 1 Ton of Ice in 24 Hours] --> B[Identify Mass: 1 Ton = 907.185 kg]
    B --> C[Identify Latent Heat: Lf = 335 kJ/kg]
    C --> D[Total Heat to Remove: Q = m x Lf = 303,907 kJ]
    D --> E[Reference Time: 24 hr = 86,400 s]
    E --> F[Rate of Heat Removal = Cooling Capacity]
    F --> G1[SI Unit: kW = 3.517]
    F --> G2[Commercial Unit: TR = 1]
    F --> G3[Imperial Unit: BTU per hr = 12,000]
    F --> G4[Older Unit: HP = 4.716]
    G1 --> H[Standard Reference: 1 TR = 3.517 kW]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Used in: AC Selection, Cold Storage, Industrial Chillers, BEE Star Ratings]
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Refrigeration Units (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SI["SI System"]
      K1[kW]
      K2[W]
    end
    subgraph COMMERCIAL["Commercial / KTU Standard"]
      T1[TR = 3.517 kW]
      T2[BTU per hr = 12,000]
    end
    subgraph LEGACY["Legacy / Older Practice"]
      L1[HP = 4.716]
      L2[kcal per hr = 3,024]
    end
    K1 --> CONV[Conversion Factors]
    K2 --> CONV
    T1 --> CONV
    T2 --> CONV
    L1 --> CONV
    L2 --> CONV
    CONV --> APP[Engineering Applications: AC Sizing, Cold Storage Design, Load Calculation]
```

### 4.3 Mapping the Concept to Real-World Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RE[Refrigeration Effect Q dot L] --> UNIT1[Measured in TR]
    RE --> UNIT2[Measured in kW]
    RE --> UNIT3[Measured in BTU per hr]
    UNIT1 --> APP1[1 TR AC for 100 sqft Room]
    UNIT1 --> APP2[5 to 8 TR for Cold Storage]
    UNIT1 --> APP3[500 TR Industrial Chiller]
    UNIT2 --> APP4[SI Label on Equipment]
    UNIT3 --> APP5[BEE Energy Rating in India]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Heat Removal Rate Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COLD["Cold Space at 0 deg C"]
      M1[1 Tonne of Water at 0 deg C]
    end
    subgraph REF["Refrigeration Unit (1 TR)"]
      R1[Compressor]
      R2[Condenser]
      R3[Evaporator]
      R4[Expansion Valve]
    end
    subgraph HOT["Hot Surroundings"]
      S1[Ambient Air at 30 to 40 deg C]
    end
    M1 -->|Heat Q dot L removed| R3
    R1 --> R2
    R2 -->|Heat rejected| S1
    R3 --> R4
    R4 --> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this schematic:** A refrigeration unit rated **1 TR** continuously removes **3.517 kJ of heat per second** (i.e., 3.517 kW) from a cold space, and rejects it to the surroundings via the condenser.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the unit of refrigeration. Express 1 TR in (a) kW and (b) kJ/min.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** One tonne of refrigeration (TR) is defined as the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 tonne (1000 kg)** of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in **24 hours**.

**(a) Converting to kW:**

Given:
- $m = 1 \text{ ton} = 907.185 \text{ kg}$ (imperial ton, KTU convention)
- $L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $t = 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400 \text{ s}$

$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{m \cdot L_f}{t} = \frac{907.185 \times 335}{86{,}400} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$$

**(b) Converting to kJ/min:**

$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{907.185 \times 335}{24 \times 60} = 211.05 \text{ kJ/min}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 211 \text{ kJ/min}}$$

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State any four units of refrigeration and establish the relation between TR and BTU/hr.**

**Model Answer:**

**Four Units of Refrigeration:**
1. **Tonne of Refrigeration (TR)**
2. **Kilowatt (kW)**
3. **BTU/hr (British Thermal Unit per hour)**
4. **Horsepower (HP)** — refrigeration ton

**Relation between TR and BTU/hr:**

Given:
- 1 ton = 2000 lb
- Latent heat of fusion ($L_f$) = 144 BTU/lb

Total heat to be removed in 24 h:
$$Q = m \times L_f = 2000 \times 144 = 288{,}000 \text{ BTU}$$

Rate of heat removal:
$$\dot{Q}_L = \frac{288{,}000 \text{ BTU}}{24 \text{ h}} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Define **Tonne of Refrigeration (TR)** and explain why it is the most commonly used unit in industry. Mention the standard reference conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold storage has a refrigeration load of **75 kW**. Calculate:  
   (i) The load in **TR**  
   (ii) The load in **BTU/hr**  
   (iii) The total heat removed (in MJ) in **6 hours** of operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition (7 marks):**

> The **Tonne of Refrigeration (TR)** is the commercial unit of refrigeration defined as the rate of heat removal required to freeze (or melt) **1 ton (2000 lb = 907.185 kg)** of water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in **24 hours**.

**Standard reference conditions:**
- Mass: 1 ton (imperial) = 907.185 kg
- Latent heat of fusion: $L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (or 144 BTU/lb)
- Initial and final temperature: 0 °C
- Time: 24 hours

**Why TR is preferred in industry:**
1. **Historical continuity** — derived from natural ice harvesting era.
2. **Practical intuition** — technicians easily understand "1 TR AC" for a small room.
3. **Universal commercial standard** — used by ASHRAE, ISHRAE, BEE, and KTU curriculum.
4. **Clean round numbers** — 1 TR = 12,000 BTU/hr simplifies load sheets.
5. **Equipment ratings** — manufacturers in India, Middle East, and SE Asia all use TR.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** Load = 75 kW

**(i) Load in TR:**
$$\text{TR} = \frac{75 \text{ kW}}{3.517 \text{ kW/TR}} = 21.33 \text{ TR}$$

**(ii) Load in BTU/hr:**
$$75 \text{ kW} = 75 \times 3412 = 255{,}900 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

**Alternative (using TR):**
$$21.33 \text{ TR} \times 12{,}000 = 255{,}960 \text{ BTU/hr} \approx 255{,}900 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

**(iii) Total heat in 6 hours (in MJ):**
$$Q_{\text{total}} = \dot{Q}_L \times t = 75 \text{ kW} \times 6 \text{ h} = 450 \text{ kWh}$$

Converting kWh to MJ:
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 450 \times 3.6 = 1620 \text{ MJ}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Load} = 21.33 \text{ TR} = 255{,}900 \text{ BTU/hr};\ \ Q_{6h} = 1620 \text{ MJ}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** A dairy chilling plant requires a cooling capacity of **8 TR**. Convert this capacity to:  
   (i) kW  
   (ii) kJ/hr  
   (iii) BTU/hr  
   (iv) HP (mechanical). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An air-conditioner has a **3-star BEE rating** with a **rated cooling capacity of 1.5 TR**. The room is maintained at 25 °C while the outside temperature is 40 °C. If the AC runs for **10 hours**, calculate:  
   (i) Total cooling in **kJ**  
   (ii) Total cooling in **MJ**  
   (iii) Equivalent cooling in **kg of ice melted at 0 °C** ($L_f = 335$ kJ/kg). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conversion of 8 TR (7 marks):**

**(i) In kW:**
$$\dot{Q}_L = 8 \times 3.517 = 28.14 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) In kJ/hr:**
$$\dot{Q}_L = 8 \times 12{,}662.8 = 101{,}302.4 \text{ kJ/hr}$$

**(iii) In BTU/hr:**
$$\dot{Q}_L = 8 \times 12{,}000 = 96{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

**(iv) In HP:**
$$\dot{Q}_L = 8 \times 4.716 = 37.73 \text{ HP}$$

$$\boxed{8 \text{ TR} = 28.14 \text{ kW} = 101{,}302.4 \text{ kJ/hr} = 96{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr} = 37.73 \text{ HP}}$$

---

**(b) AC Energy Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** Capacity = 1.5 TR, Time = 10 h

**(i) Total cooling in kJ:**

Using $1 \text{ TR} = 211.05 \text{ kJ/min}$:
$$\dot{Q}_L = 1.5 \times 211.05 = 316.575 \text{ kJ/min}$$

For 10 hours = 600 minutes:
$$Q_{\text{total}} = 316.575 \times 600 = 189{,}945 \text{ kJ}$$

**(ii) In MJ:**
$$Q_{\text{total}} = \frac{189{,}945}{1000} = 189.945 \text{ MJ} \approx 190 \text{ MJ}$$

**(iii) Equivalent kg of ice melted:**
$$m_{\text{ice}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{L_f} = \frac{189{,}945}{335} = 567 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} = 189{,}945 \text{ kJ} = 189.945 \text{ MJ} \equiv 567 \text{ kg of ice melted}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in 14-Mark Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state reference conditions:** Always mention "at 0 °C, 1 atm, water → ice" when defining TR. KTU examiners award **1 mark** for this clarification.
>
> 2. **Using 1000 kg instead of 907.185 kg (or 2000 lb):** This gives 3.876 kW instead of 3.517 kW — wrong by 10%! The KTU convention is the **imperial ton (2000 lb)**. Deduct 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing latent heat value:** Some books use $L_f = 79.7 \text{ kcal/kg} = 333.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Always use **$L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$** (or **144 BTU/lb**) for KTU problems.
>
> 4. **Mixing up TR (power) with energy:** "1 TR" is a *rate* (kW), not a quantity. If a question asks for "heat removed in 6 hours", you MUST multiply TR by time to get energy.
>
> 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Writing "TR = 3.5 kJ" instead of "TR = 3.5 kW" — this is a fatal error. Always include units.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to convert minutes to seconds (or vice versa):** A common slip is dividing by 3600 instead of 1440. Write the unit in the formula to avoid this.
>
> 7. **Rounding too early:** Carry 3–4 significant figures throughout the calculation. KTU expects answers to 3 decimal places in 14-mark numericals.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Module 1: Unit of Refrigeration**

- ⭐ **1 TR** = rate of heat removal to freeze 1 ton of water (at 0 °C) into ice (at 0 °C) in 24 hours.
- ⭐ **Reference mass (imperial ton)** = **2000 lb = 907.185 kg** (KTU convention).
- ⭐ **Reference mass (metric ton)** = **1000 kg** (used in some texts; gives a slightly different kW value).
- ⭐ **Latent heat of fusion of water** = **$L_f = 335 \text{ kJ/kg}$** or **$144 \text{ BTU/lb}$**.
- ⭐ **$1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$** — most-used conversion.
- ⭐ **$1 \text{ TR} = 211.05 \text{ kJ/min}$** — handy for time-based problems.
- ⭐ **$1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}000 \text{ BTU/hr}$** — imperial conversion (exact).
- ⭐ **$1 \text{ TR} = 12{,}662.8 \text{ kJ/hr}$** — alternative unit.
- ⭐ **$1 \text{ TR} = 4.716 \text{ HP}$** — older practice.
- ⭐ **$1 \text{ kW} = 0.2843 \text{ TR} = 3412 \text{ BTU/hr}$**.
- ⭐ **$1 \text{ BTU} = 1.055 \text{ kJ}$**.
- ⭐ **$1 \text{ kcal} = 4.1868 \text{ kJ}$**.
- ⭐ The unit of refrigeration is fundamentally a **rate** (Power), not energy.
- ⭐ Standard reference temperature is **0 °C** — never forget to mention it in definitions.
- ⭐ **BEE star ratings** in India are based on **ISEER** (BTU/Wh).
- ⭐ **Tonnage concept** originates from pre-electric **ice harvesting** era.
- ⭐ KTU always uses the **imperial ton (2000 lb)** → always get **3.517 kW**, not 3.876 kW.
- ⭐ Always write the **unit at every step** of the derivation for full marks.
- ⭐ Convert 1 TR to **MJ per hour** = **12.66 MJ/h** — useful for cold storage calculations.
- ⭐ **Practical sizing rule of thumb:** 1 TR AC ≈ **100–150 sq. ft. room** in Indian climate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ideal refrigeration cycle - Reversed Carnot cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ideal refrigeration cycle - Reversed Carnot cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is the **ideal (theoretical benchmark) refrigeration cycle** obtained by reversing the direction of execution of the Carnot heat engine cycle. It operates between two thermal reservoirs — a low-temperature cold space (refrigerated space) at temperature $T_L$ and a high-temperature hot space (surroundings/ambient) at temperature $T_H$ — and represents the **maximum possible Coefficient of Performance (COP)** achievable by any refrigeration system working between those two fixed temperature limits.

In the reversed Carnot cycle, work is **supplied externally** to the system to extract heat $Q_L$ from the cold reservoir and reject a larger quantity of heat $Q_H$ to the hot reservoir. The four constituent processes are:

1. **Process 1 → 2:** Reversible Isentropic (Isentropic) Compression
2. **Process 2 → 3:** Reversible Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_H$
3. **Process 3 → 4:** Reversible Isentropic Expansion
4. **Process 4 → 1:** Reversible Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_L$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The reversed Carnot cycle is the *yardstick* against which the performance of *all real refrigeration cycles* (Vapour Compression, Vapour Absorption, Air Refrigeration) is compared. The Second Law of Thermodynamics guarantees that no real cycle operating between $T_L$ and $T_H$ can exceed the COP of the reversed Carnot cycle.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water pump**, but instead of pumping water uphill, you are pumping **"cold" from a cold box to the warm room**.

- Your refrigerator's job is to keep the inside of a cold box (say, $T_L = -10°C$) cold, even though the kitchen (ambient $T_H = 35°C$) is much warmer. Heat naturally wants to flow from the warm kitchen *into* the cold box. To prevent this, you have to **constantly pump heat backwards** — out of the cold box and into the warm kitchen.
- The Reversed Carnot cycle is the *perfect, frictionless, lossless* way to perform this "heat pumping" job. Think of it as the **"ideal heat pump"** — the theoretical dream machine that does this task with the absolute minimum amount of electrical work.
- In real life, a refrigerator uses a refrigerant that physically changes phase (boils in the cold box, condenses in the warm room). The Reversed Carnot cycle abstracts this into a *purely theoretical* cycle where the working fluid is compressed, cooled, expanded, and heated in the *most thermodynamically efficient* manner possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - All temperatures **MUST** be in **Kelvin (K)** for all COP and efficiency calculations — never Celsius or Fahrenheit.
> - The lower the $T_L$ (colder the refrigerated space) and the higher $T_H$ (warmer the surroundings), the **lower** the COP, meaning more work input is required.

### Why the Carnot Cycle is Reversed (and Not Used in Practice)

The Carnot cycle is composed of two **isothermal** and two **isentropic** processes. To execute isothermal processes with a real gas, the working fluid must behave like an **ideal gas with variable specific heats** — practically impossible. Additionally, the isentropic compression to peak pressure followed by isothermal heat rejection requires the compressor to handle a *two-phase mixture*, which is mechanically infeasible. Hence, this cycle remains a **theoretical standard**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Breakdown of the Four Processes

Let us walk through each process systematically. The working fluid absorbs heat from the cold space and rejects heat to the hot space, with external work input.

#### Process 1 → 2 : Isentropic Compression
- The vapour (saturated or superheated) is compressed **reversibly and adiabatically** (no heat transfer, $Q = 0$).
- Entropy remains constant: $s_1 = s_2$.
- Temperature rises from the **evaporator temperature $T_L$** to the **condenser temperature $T_H$**.
- Work input: $W_{comp} = h_2 - h_1$ (for ideal-gas interpretation, $W_{comp} = \int v\,dP$).
- This is the most work-intensive part of the cycle.

#### Process 2 → 3 : Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_H$
- The hot, high-pressure gas is brought into contact with the **hot reservoir at $T_H$**.
- Heat is rejected at **constant temperature** $T_H$ — this requires the working fluid to be cooled while remaining at $T_H$, which (in the ideal cycle) is achieved by adjusting the boundary.
- Entropy **decreases** as heat leaves the system: $Q_H = T_H(s_2 - s_3)$.
- Since $s_2 = s_1$ (from process 1-2) and $s_4 = s_3$ (from process 3-4), we have $s_2 - s_3 = s_1 - s_4$.

#### Process 3 → 4 : Isentropic Expansion
- The high-pressure liquid is expanded in a **reversible, adiabatic** expansion device (an ideal "isentropic turbine" or "work-producing expander").
- Entropy remains constant: $s_3 = s_4$.
- Temperature drops from $T_H$ back down to $T_L$.
- Work is **produced** (output): $W_{exp} = h_3 - h_4$.
- This work output is partially subtracted from the compressor work to give the **net work input**.

#### Process 4 → 1 : Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_L$
- The cold, low-pressure mixture absorbs heat from the **cold reservoir at $T_L$**.
- Heat absorbed is the **refrigeration effect**: $Q_L = T_L(s_1 - s_4)$.
- This is the *useful effect* of the refrigerator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Net Work Input:** $W_{net} = W_{comp} - W_{exp} = Q_H - Q_L$
> (By the First Law applied to the cyclic integral: $\oint dU = 0 \Rightarrow W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$)

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Refrigeration Effect (Heat absorbed) | $Q_L = T_L \cdot (s_1 - s_4)$ | kJ/kg | Useful cooling output |
| 2 | Heat Rejected to Hot Reservoir | $Q_H = T_H \cdot (s_2 - s_3)$ | kJ/kg | Since $s_2 = s_1$ and $s_3 = s_4$, $Q_H = T_H(s_1 - s_4)$ |
| 3 | Net Work Input | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = (T_H - T_L)(s_1 - s_4)$ | kJ/kg | Net compressor work after subtracting expander work |
| 4 | **COP of Refrigerator** | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | **Most important formula** |
| 5 | **COP of Heat Pump** | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | Used for heating applications |
| 6 | Relation between COPs | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | — | Always true for any cycle |
| 7 | Heat Pump: Refrigerator Ratio | $\dfrac{\text{COP}_{HP}}{\text{COP}_R} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$ | — | Useful comparison |
| 8 | Tonne of Refrigeration (TR) | $1\;\text{TR} = 3.517\;\text{kW} = 210\;\text{kJ/min}$ | kW | Standard commercial unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Sign Convention:** $T_L$ and $T_H$ **MUST** be absolute temperatures in **Kelvin**. A common KTU exam pitfall is using Celsius — this *guarantees* a wrong answer.

### Real-World Utility

- **Benchmarking Tool:** Engineers use the Reversed Carnot COP as a "perfect score" to evaluate how close a real Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) comes to ideal performance. The ratio of actual COP to Carnot COP is called the **Second Law Efficiency (or Reversible Efficiency)**.
- **Design Optimisation:** Knowing the ideal COP helps designers identify *where* irreversibilities (pressure drops, non-isentropic compression, temperature differences in heat exchangers) are hurting performance the most.
- **Heat Pump Selection:** For heating-dominated applications (e.g., space heating in cold climates like Scandinavian countries or high-altitude Himalayan regions of India), the heat pump COP is more meaningful than the refrigerator COP.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: COP of the Reversed Carnot Refrigeration Cycle

We will derive the **COP of an ideal refrigerator** operating on the reversed Carnot cycle from first principles using the First Law of Thermodynamics and the properties of the cycle on a $T$-$s$ diagram.

**Given:**
- The cycle operates between two isothermal reservoirs at $T_L$ (cold space) and $T_H$ (hot space), both in Kelvin.
- The two heat-transfer processes are reversible and isothermal; the other two are reversible and adiabatic (isentropic).

**To Prove:**
$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the Cycle**

For a cyclic process, the change in internal energy over one complete cycle is zero:

$$\oint dU = 0$$

From the First Law:

$$Q_{net} - W_{net} = \oint dU = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{net} = Q_{net}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Heat Transfers**

Heat is transferred in only during the two isothermal processes (Processes 1-2 and 3-4 are isentropic, hence adiabatic — no heat transfer).

- **Process 4 → 1** (Isothermal heat absorption from cold space at $T_L$):

$$Q_L = T_L \cdot (s_1 - s_4)$$

- **Process 2 → 3** (Isothermal heat rejection to hot space at $T_H$):

$$Q_H = T_H \cdot (s_2 - s_3)$$

The isentropic processes satisfy $s_1 = s_2$ and $s_3 = s_4$. Therefore:

$$s_1 - s_4 = s_2 - s_3 \equiv \Delta s$$

**Step 3 — Express Heat Transfers Using the Common $\Delta s$**

$$Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta s$$

$$Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta s$$

**Step 4 — Apply the First Law to Find Net Work**

By the cyclic First Law:

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L = T_H \cdot \Delta s - T_L \cdot \Delta s = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta s$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the Definition of COP**

The Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired output (refrigeration effect $Q_L$) to the required input (net work $W_{net}$):

$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the Derived Expressions**

$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L \cdot \Delta s}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta s}$$

**Step 7 — Cancel the Common Term $\Delta s$**

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$

**Conclusion:** The COP of an ideal refrigerator operating on the reversed Carnot cycle depends *only* on the absolute temperatures of the two reservoirs, and is independent of the working fluid. This is a direct consequence of the Second Law of Thermodynamics.

---

### Derivation 2: COP of Heat Pump and the $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ Relationship

**Step 1 — Definition of Heat Pump COP**

A heat pump's *desired output* is the heat delivered to the hot space ($Q_H$):

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Quantities**

Using $Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta s$ and $W_{net} = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta s$:

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H \cdot \Delta s}{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta s} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Universal Relationship**

$$\text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_R = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} - \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H - T_L} = 1$$

$$\therefore \quad \boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1}$$

This is a **universal identity** valid for *any* refrigeration cycle, not just the reversed Carnot cycle.

---

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A reversed Carnot refrigeration cycle is used to maintain a cold storage at $-10°C$ when the ambient temperature is $35°C$.

- **(a)** Calculate the COP of the refrigerator.
- **(b)** Calculate the COP of the heat pump.
- **(c)** If the refrigeration load is 10 TR, find the power required to drive the compressor (in kW).

**Given Data:**
- $T_L = -10 + 273 = 263\;\text{K}$
- $T_H = 35 + 273 = 308\;\text{K}$
- Refrigeration load $Q_L = 10\;\text{TR} = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17\;\text{kW}$

**Part (a): COP of the Refrigerator**

$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{308 - 263} = \frac{263}{45}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_R = 5.844}$$

**Part (b): COP of the Heat Pump**

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{308}{45} = 6.844$$

Verification: $\text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_R = 6.844 - 5.844 = 1.0$ ✓

**Part (c): Power Required (in kW)**

By definition:

$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_{net} = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R}$$

$$W_{net} = \frac{35.17\;\text{kW}}{5.844} = 6.018\;\text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{W_{net} \approx 6.02\;\text{kW}}$$

---

### Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameter Variation on COP

| Scenario | Change in $T_L$ | Change in $T_H$ | Effect on $\text{COP}_R$ | Physical Reasoning |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|--------------------------|--------------------|
| Lower the cold-space temperature | Decrease | Same | **Decreases** | Harder to extract heat from a colder space |
