| Raise the cold-space temperature | Increase | Same | **Increases** | Easier to maintain near-ambient cold box |
| Higher ambient (hot) temperature | Same | Increase | **Decreases** | Larger temperature lift → more work needed |
| Lower ambient temperature | Same | Decrease | **Increases** | Smaller temperature lift → less work needed |
| Reduce temperature lift $(T_H - T_L)$ | — | — | **COP increases non-linearly** | As $\Delta T \to 0$, $\text{COP}_R \to \infty$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymptotic Behaviour:** As $T_H \to T_L$, the temperature difference approaches zero and $\text{COP}_R \to \infty$. This is **physically correct** — infinitely small work is needed to move heat across an infinitely small temperature difference (the Clausius statement of the Second Law).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 T-s Diagram (Temperature-Entropy) of the Reversed Carnot Cycle

This is the **primary diagram** KTU examiners expect. The cycle appears as a *rectangle* on a $T$-$s$ chart because both heat-transfer processes are isothermal (horizontal lines) and both compression/expansion processes are isentropic (vertical lines).

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["State 1: Sat Vap at T_L, High Entropy"] -->|Process 1 to 2: Isentropic Compression| B["State 2: High Pressure Gas at T_H, High Entropy"]
    B -->|Process 2 to 3: Isothermal Heat Rejection at T_H| C["State 3: High Pressure Fluid at T_H, Low Entropy"]
    C -->|Process 3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion| D["State 4: Low Pressure Fluid at T_L, Low Entropy"]
    D -->|Process 4 to 1: Isothermal Heat Absorption at T_L| A
```

**Reading the T-s Diagram:**
- **State 1:** Saturated (or superheated) vapour leaving the evaporator at $T_L$, with the **highest entropy** of the cycle.
- **State 2:** After isentropic compression, the temperature has risen to $T_H$, but entropy is unchanged.
- **State 3:** After isothermal heat rejection at $T_H$, entropy has dropped to its minimum value.
- **State 4:** After isentropic expansion, temperature has dropped back to $T_L$.

The **area enclosed by the rectangle** on the $T$-$s$ diagram equals the **net work input** $W_{net} = (T_H - T_L)(s_1 - s_4)$.

---

### 4.2 P-v Diagram (Pressure-Volume) of the Reversed Carnot Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1["State 1: Low P, High v, T_L"] -->|Process 1 to 2: Isentropic Compression, P rises, v drops| B1["State 2: High P, Moderate v, T_H"]
    B1 -->|Process 2 to 3: Isothermal at T_H, P drops, v rises along isotherm| C1["State 3: Moderate P, Higher v, T_H"]
    C1 -->|Process 3 to 4: Isentropic Expansion, P drops, v rises| D1["State 4: Low P, Highest v, T_L"]
    D1 -->|Process 4 to 1: Isothermal at T_L, P rises, v drops along isotherm| A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> Unlike the $T$-$s$ plot (which is a perfect rectangle), the $P$-$v$ plot shows **curved isotherms** because for an ideal gas $Pv = mRT$ (isotherms are hyperbolas). The $T$-$s$ diagram is preferred for analysis due to its geometric simplicity.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Components

This block diagram maps the **functional interactions** of the four hardware components of a refrigeration system, regardless of working fluid. In the *ideal* reversed Carnot cycle, the expansion device is an **isentropic expander** (a turbine), not a throttling valve.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Evaporator\nCold Space T_L\nAbsorbs Q_L] -->|Vapour exits at state 1| B[Compressor\nIsentropic Compression\nWork Input W_comp]
    B -->|Hot high pressure gas at state 2| C[Condenser\nHot Reservoir T_H\nRejects Q_H]
    C -->|High pressure liquid at state 3| D[Isentropic Expander\nProduces Work W_exp]
    D -->|Cold low pressure mixture at state 4| A
    B -.Net Work Input W_net.-> D
```

**Component Functions (Block-Level):**
- **Evaporator:** Located *inside* the cold space. Absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the refrigerated space at constant $T_L$.
- **Compressor:** Receives low-pressure vapour from the evaporator and raises its pressure isentropically. **Consumes** work $W_{comp}$.
- **Condenser:** Located *outside* in the warmer surroundings. Rejects heat $Q_H$ to the ambient at constant $T_H$.
- **Isentropic Expander:** Reduces the pressure of the condensed liquid isentropically, producing a small amount of work $W_{exp}$. (In real VCRS, this is replaced by a *throttling valve* that is *not* isentropic.)

---

### 4.4 Schematic Comparison: Carnot Heat Engine vs. Reversed Carnot Refrigerator

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ENG["CARNOT HEAT ENGINE (Forward)"]
        E1[Q_H from Hot Source] --> E2[Engine]
        E2 --> E3[Q_L to Cold Sink]
        E2 --> E4[W_net Output]
    end
    subgraph REF["REVERSED CARNOT REFRIGERATOR (Reverse)"]
        R1[Q_L from Cold Source] --> R2[Refrigerator]
        R2 --> R3[Q_H to Hot Sink]
        R4[W_net Input] --> R2
    end
```

In the forward Carnot engine, heat flows from hot to cold *spontaneously*, producing work. In the reversed cycle, work is **supplied externally** to *force* heat from cold to hot — this is the essence of refrigeration.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q1.** Define the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigerator. How is it different from the **COP of a heat pump**?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator** is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (heat extracted from the cold space, $Q_L$) to the net work input ($W_{net}$) required to achieve it:

$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity. For a Reversed Carnot cycle:

$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

The **COP of a heat pump** is defined as the ratio of the desired heating effect (heat delivered to the hot space, $Q_H$) to the net work input:

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$

**Key Difference:** A refrigerator's "useful output" is the heat removed from the cold space; a heat pump's "useful output" is the heat delivered to the hot space. They are related by $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q2.** State the **limitations of the reversed Carnot cycle** when used as a practical refrigeration cycle.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Reversed Carnot cycle, though thermodynamically ideal, has the following limitations that prevent its practical implementation:

1. **Isothermal processes with a real fluid:** The Carnot cycle assumes the working fluid undergoes isothermal heat transfer. With a real gas, this requires the *specific heat* to vary during the process, which is not feasible.
2. **Two-phase compression:** State 1 is typically a saturated vapour. Isentropic compression of a wet mixture (liquid + vapour) is mechanically impractical — compressors are designed for *dry compression* to avoid cylinder damage.
3. **Isentropic expansion device:** The cycle demands a work-producing isentropic expander. In practice, expansion is carried out via a throttling valve, which is *irreversible* and *non-isentropic* (replaced in VCRS).
4. **Low COP despite ideal status:** Real refrigerants exhibit pressure-temperature relationships that deviate strongly from ideal gas behaviour, making the cycle physically unrealizable.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the **COP of a Reversed Carnot refrigeration cycle** in terms of the source and sink temperatures. Clearly state the assumptions made.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A reversed Carnot refrigerator operates between a cold space at $-20°C$ and a hot space at $40°C$. The refrigerator has a capacity of **5 tonnes of refrigeration (TR)**. Calculate:
   - (i) The COP of the refrigerator
   - (ii) The COP of the heat pump
   - (iii) The power required to drive the compressor in kW

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

*(Follow the step-by-step derivation in Section 3 of these notes. The full derivation must include:)*

- **Assumptions:** (1) All processes are reversible; (2) No friction or pressure drop; (3) Working fluid behaves ideally; (4) Heat transfer occurs only at constant temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$.
- **Step 1:** First Law for cycle: $\oint dU = 0 \Rightarrow W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$
- **Step 2:** Heat absorbed in cold isotherm: $Q_L = T_L(s_1 - s_4)$
- **Step 3:** Heat rejected in hot isotherm: $Q_H = T_H(s_2 - s_3) = T_H(s_1 - s_4)$ (using $s_1 = s_2$, $s_3 = s_4$)
- **Step 4:** Net work: $W_{net} = (T_H - T_L)(s_1 - s_4)$
- **Step 5:** Substituting into $\text{COP}_R = Q_L/W_{net}$:

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

*Given:*
- $T_L = -20 + 273 = 253\;\text{K}$
- $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313\;\text{K}$
- Capacity = $5\;\text{TR} = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585\;\text{kW}$

*(i) COP of the Refrigerator:*

$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{253}{313 - 253} = \frac{253}{60}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_R = 4.217}$$

*(ii) COP of the Heat Pump:*

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{313}{60}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = 5.217}$$

*Verification:* $\text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_R = 5.217 - 4.217 = 1.0$ ✓

*(iii) Power Required:*

$$W_{net} = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{17.585\;\text{kW}}{4.217}$$

$$\boxed{W_{net} = 4.170\;\text{kW}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]:**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Draw the **$T$-$s$ and $P$-$v$ diagrams** of a Reversed Carnot cycle. Explain each process in detail, indicating the energy transfers in each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A refrigerator working on a reversed Carnot cycle has a COP of **4.5**. The heat rejected to the hot space is **30 kJ/min**. Find:
   - (i) The refrigeration effect produced per minute
   - (ii) The work input per minute
   - (iii) The temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$ if $T_H = 1.2\,T_L$

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Diagrams and Process Description (7 Marks):**

*(Refer to Section 4 diagrams in these notes. The verbal description should cover:)*

- **Process 1-2 (Isentropic Compression):** Vapour compressed from $T_L$ to $T_H$ at constant entropy. Work is done *on* the system. $Q = 0$. Pressure and temperature rise.
- **Process 2-3 (Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_H$):** Hot, high-pressure gas rejects heat $Q_H$ to the hot reservoir at constant temperature $T_H$. Pressure and entropy decrease.
- **Process 3-4 (Isentropic Expansion):** High-pressure liquid expands isentropically to low pressure. Work is done *by* the system. $Q = 0$. Temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$.
- **Process 4-1 (Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_L$):** Cold, low-pressure mixture absorbs heat $Q_L$ from the cold space at constant temperature $T_L$. Pressure and entropy increase.

The $T$-$s$ diagram is a **rectangle** with horizontal isotherms and vertical isentropes. The enclosed area equals the net work.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

*Given:*
- $\text{COP}_R = 4.5$
- $Q_H = 30\;\text{kJ/min}$
- $T_H = 1.2\,T_L$

*(i) Refrigeration Effect per Minute:*

We know $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1 = 4.5 + 1 = 5.5$

Using $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$:

$$W_{net} = \frac{Q_H}{\text{COP}_{HP}} = \frac{30}{5.5} = 5.455\;\text{kJ/min}$$

Now, $Q_L = \text{COP}_R \times W_{net} = 4.5 \times 5.455$

$$\boxed{Q_L = 24.545\;\text{kJ/min}}$$

*Verification:* $Q_H - Q_L = 30 - 24.545 = 5.455 = W_{net}$ ✓

*(ii) Work Input per Minute:*

$$\boxed{W_{net} = 5.455\;\text{kJ/min}}$$

*(iii) Temperatures $T_L$ and $T_H$ in Kelvin:*

Using $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ with $T_H = 1.2\,T_L$:

$$4.5 = \frac{T_L}{1.2\,T_L - T_L} = \frac{T_L}{0.2\,T_L} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5.0$$

This gives a contradiction (4.5 ≠ 5.0), which means the stated condition $T_H = 1.2\,T_L$ is inconsistent with the given COP of 4.5. The *consistent* $T_H/T_L$ ratio for $\text{COP}_R = 4.5$ is:

$$\text{COP}_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = 4.5 \Rightarrow T_H = T_L + \frac{T_L}{4.5} = T_L\left(1 + \frac{1}{4.5}\right) = T_L \times 1.2222$$

$$\therefore \quad T_H = 1.2222\,T_L \quad (\text{not } 1.2\,T_L)$$

> **Final consistent values (assuming the correct ratio is 1.2222):**

Assuming $T_L = 250\;\text{K}$ (a reasonable design temperature, e.g., $-23°C$):

$$T_H = 1.2222 \times 250 = 305.55\;\text{K} \approx 305.6\;\text{K}\;(32.6°C)$$

$$\boxed{T_L = 250\;\text{K} \;(-23°C), \quad T_H \approx 305.6\;\text{K} \;(32.6°C)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **#1 Most Common Mistake:** Forgetting to convert Celsius to Kelvin. **Always** use $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. This single error will cost **3–4 marks** out of 7 in Part (b) and will cascade into every subsequent calculation.
> - **#2 Sign Errors:** Students often confuse the direction of $Q_L$ and $Q_H$. Remember: in a refrigerator, $Q_L$ is the *useful* cooling output (extracted *from* the cold space), and $Q_H$ is the *undesired* heat dumped to the hot space.
> - **#3 Units of TR:** $1\;\text{TR} = 3.517\;\text{kW}$ (NOT 3.5, NOT 3.516, the standard value is 3.517). Watch this in numerical problems.
> - **#4 Missing Diagrams:** Part (a) questions on the Reversed Carnot cycle are *worth 0 marks* without a properly labelled $T$-$s$ diagram. KTU examiners typically allocate **2 marks** for the diagram alone.
> - **#5 Confusing $\text{COP}_R$ and $\text{COP}_{HP}$:** A heat pump is judged on the heat *delivered*, not the heat *extracted*. Use the correct ratio for the application.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **Reversed Carnot cycle** is the *theoretical benchmark* — the most efficient refrigeration cycle between two given temperatures.
- [x] It consists of **two isotherms** (heat transfer at constant $T$) and **two isentropes** (reversible adiabatic compression and expansion).
- [x] The four states on the $T$-$s$ diagram form a **rectangle**, with the enclosed area equal to the **net work input**.
- [x] **Master Formulas:**
  - $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$
  - $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \text{COP}_R + 1$
- [x] **Always convert temperatures to Kelvin** before any COP calculation. This is the single most important rule.
- [x] The **Carnot COP is independent of the working fluid** — it depends only on the reservoir temperatures (Second Law consequence).
- [x] **Lower COP** when $T_L$ is very low OR $T_H$ is very high (large temperature lift).
- [x] **Higher COP** when $T_L$ and $T_H$ are close (small temperature lift).
- [x] As $T_H \to T_L$, $\text{COP}_R \to \infty$ (theoretically zero work needed).
- [x] **Practical Limitations:** Isothermal processes with a real gas are impossible; two-phase compression is mechanically infeasible; isentropic expanders are replaced by throttling valves in real cycles.
- [x] The Reversed Carnot cycle serves as the **yardstick** for the **Second Law Efficiency** ($\eta_{II} = \text{COP}_{actual}/\text{COP}_{Carnot}$) of real refrigeration systems.
- [x] **Tonne of Refrigeration (TR):** $1\;\text{TR} = 3.517\;\text{kW} = 210\;\text{kJ/min}$ — must be memorised.
- [x] **Power input to a Carnot refrigerator:** $W_{net} = \dfrac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigerator and heat pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Refrigerator and heat pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Refrigerator — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigerator (KTU 2024 Definition):** A refrigerator is a *cyclic device* operating in a **reversed thermodynamic cycle** whose primary purpose is to **extract heat (Q_L) from a low-temperature body / cold reservoir (T_L)** and **reject it to a high-temperature body / hot reservoir (T_H)**, with the balance of energy supplied from outside as **net work input (W_net)**. By KTU convention, the *useful effect* of a refrigerator is the heat removed from the cold space.

The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** dictates that heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder body to a hotter body; therefore, an external energy input in the form of work is mandatory. The device does **not** create cold — it merely *transports* heat from where it is undesirable to where it is less undesirable.

## 1.2 The Heat Pump — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Pump (KTU 2024 Definition):** A heat pump is also a *cyclic device* operating in a *reversed thermodynamic cycle*, but its primary objective is **heat supply (Q_H) to a high-temperature body / hot reservoir (T_H)**. The *useful effect* of a heat pump is the heat delivered to the hot space. The same physical hardware that serves as a refrigerator in summer can function as a heat pump in winter.

The distinction between a refrigerator and a heat pump is therefore **NOT in the hardware**, but purely in **the choice of the "useful effect"** chosen by the designer / user.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Bucket on the Hill"

Imagine a leaky bucket of water sitting at the **bottom of a hill (cold reservoir)**. The water you want to lift back to the **top of the hill (hot reservoir)**. Gravity, however, pulls it back down. You must constantly spend **energy (work input)** carrying buckets of water uphill.

- The **water that reaches the top** = total heat rejected to hot space, **Q_H**.
- The **water scooped from the bottom** = useful refrigeration effect, **Q_L**.
- The **extra water you carried in the first place (now gone)** = work input, **W_net**.

For a *refrigerator*, you care about how much water you successfully lifted *from the bottom* per unit of effort. For a *heat pump*, you care about the *total water now sitting at the top* per unit of effort. Same hill, same water — different way of *measuring performance*.

## 1.4 Coefficient of Performance (COP) — The Performance Metric

> [!NOTE]
> **Why COP and not Efficiency?** A refrigerator / heat pump is *not* a heat engine. Its objective is **heat transfer**, not work conversion. Therefore, the KTU 2024 syllabus uses the dimensionless **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** in place of thermal efficiency. Mathematically: $\text{COP} = \dfrac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Required Input}} = \dfrac{\text{Output desired}}{\text{Input required}}$.

| Device | Desired Effect | Required Input | COP Symbol |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Refrigerator | Heat extracted from cold space, **Q_L** | Net work input, **W_net** | $\text{COP}_R$ |
| Heat Pump | Heat delivered to hot space, **Q_H** | Net work input, **W_net** | $\text{COP}_{HP}$ |

## 1.5 Standard Units & The Ton of Refrigeration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tonne of Refrigeration (TR):** A standard commercial unit of refrigeration capacity, defined as the rate of heat removal equivalent to the melting of **1 tonne (1000 kg) of ice at 0 °C into water at 0 °C in 24 hours**.
>
> 1 TR = **3.517 kW ≈ 3.5 kW** (this constant is universally tested in KTU papers).
>
> Standard reference temperatures: Cold space T_L = **−18 °C (255 K)** and Condenser T_H = **30 °C (303 K)** — unless otherwise specified.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First Law Energy Balance (Per Cycle)

For both a refrigerator and a heat pump operating as a **cyclic steady-flow device**, the First Law requires that energy in = energy out per cycle:

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$

This is the **single most important energy equation** for Module 1. All COP expressions are derived from this identity.

## 2.2 Why Heat Cannot Flow Spontaneously — The Second Law

A 100 % efficient refrigerator (Q_L = W_net, no Q_H) **violates the Second Law** (Clausius statement: heat cannot flow from cold to hot without external work). Hence, **W_net > 0** is mandatory, and the COP of any real device is always finite and bounded.

## 2.3 The Reversed Carnot Cycle — The Theoretical Benchmark

> [!NOTE]
> The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** consists of **two isothermal processes** (heat transfer at constant temperature) and **two isentropic (reversible adiabatic) processes** (no heat transfer). It is the **most efficient** refrigeration cycle possible between two given temperature reservoirs. KTU 2024 questions frequently ask: *"Show that no refrigerator working between two given reservoirs can have a COP greater than that of a reversed Carnot cycle."*

**Process breakdown of the Reversed Carnot cycle:**

1. **Process 1 → 2 (Isentropic Compression):** Refrigerant vapour is compressed from low pressure to high pressure with no heat transfer. Temperature rises from T_L to T_H.
2. **Process 2 → 3 (Isothermal Heat Rejection at T_H):** Refrigerant condenses at constant high temperature T_H, rejecting heat Q_H to the surroundings.
3. **Process 3 → 4 (Isentropic Expansion):** High-pressure liquid expands through an expansion device to low pressure. Temperature drops from T_H to T_L.
4. **Process 4 → 1 (Isothermal Heat Absorption at T_L):** Refrigerant evaporates at constant low temperature T_L, absorbing heat Q_L from the cold space.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table. Every Module 1 problem in the KTU 2024 ESE / Internal will use **at least one** of these expressions.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ | First Law energy balance per cycle | kJ |
| 2 | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | Refrigerator COP (desired = Q_L) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$ | Heat Pump COP (desired = Q_H) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | Relationship between two COPs | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot refrigerator COP (T in K) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Carnot heat pump COP (T in K) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1}$ | Alternate form of Carnot Refrigerator COP | dimensionless |
| 8 | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | Standard refrigeration unit | kW |
| 9 | $1 \text{ TR} = 211 \text{ kJ/min} = 12600 \text{ kJ/h}$ | TR in kJ terms | kJ/h |
| 10 | $\text{HP} = 2545 \text{ Btu/h} = 0.7457 \text{ kW}$ | Horsepower (legacy unit) | kW |
| 11 | $\text{SHF} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_t}$ | Sensible Heat Factor (preliminary) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Reversible (Carnot) heat ratio | dimensionless |

> ⚠️ **CRITICAL TEMPERATURE RULE (KTU Examiner's Favourite Trap):** All temperatures in Carnot COP expressions **must be absolute** (Kelvin or Rankine). If the problem gives 27 °C and 5 °C, convert first: T_H = 300 K, T_L = 278 K. Substituting Celsius directly is the **#1 cause of zero marks** in KTU papers.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concepts of refrigerator and heat pump form the *theoretical foundation* of every practical cooling/heating system:
- **Domestic:** Window AC, split AC, frost-free refrigerators, geysers (heat pump water heaters).
- **Commercial:** Cold storage warehouses, supermarket display cases, chillers for data centres.
- **Industrial:** LNG liquefaction, food processing, pharmaceutical cold chain, district heating.
- **Automotive:** Cabin AC, battery thermal management in EVs (heat pump).

A modern inverter-driven **VRF (Variable Refrigerant Flow) heat pump** used in Keralite commercial buildings can achieve a seasonal COP (SCOP) of 4–5 — meaning 1 kW of electricity delivers 4–5 kW of heating/cooling. The *theoretical* Carnot limit sets the *upper bound* against which all real machines are benchmarked.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General COP of a Refrigerator

> **Starting Point:** A refrigerator extracts Q_L from a cold space at T_L, rejects Q_H to a hot space at T_H, and consumes net work W_net.

**Step 1: Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics** to the cyclic device (energy in = energy out per cycle):

$$W_{net} + Q_L = Q_H$$

**Step 2: Rearrange** to express the work input as a function of the two heat transfers:

$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 3: Apply the COP definition** — desired effect (Q_L) divided by required input (W_net):

$$\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

**Step 4: Invert** the expression to obtain the alternate (very common KTU) form:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{\text{COP}_R} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L} - 1}$$

This form is exceptionally useful in KTU problems where **Q_H / Q_L is given directly** (e.g., ratio problems).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — General COP of a Heat Pump

**Step 1:** Use the same First Law balance: W_net + Q_L = Q_H.

**Step 2:** Apply the heat-pump COP definition (desired effect = Q_H):

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

**Step 3: Divide numerator and denominator by Q_L**:

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H / Q_L}{Q_H/Q_L - 1}$$

Let $r = Q_H / Q_L$. Then $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{r}{r - 1}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Critical Relationship: COP_HP = COP_R + 1

This is the **single most-tested identity** in KTU 2024 Module 1.

**Step 1: Write down both COPs side by side**:

$$\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} \quad ; \quad \text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

**Step 2: Add 1 to the refrigerator COP**:

$$\text{COP}_R + 1 = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} + \dfrac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} = \dfrac{Q_L + Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$$

**Step 3: Observe that the right-hand side is exactly $\text{COP}_{HP}$**. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1}$$

**Engineering Implication:** Since the heat pump delivers *more* useful energy (Q_H) for the *same* work input, **the heat pump COP is always exactly 1 unit greater than the refrigerator COP** for the same device operating between the same two reservoirs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Carnot (Reversible) COP of a Refrigerator

> **Starting Point:** Reversed Carnot cycle between T_L (K) and T_H (K), operating reversibly (i.e., the second law holds with equality).

**Step 1: Apply the Second Law** — for a reversible cycle, the cyclic integral of δQ/T is zero (Clausius equality):

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L} = \dfrac{Q_H}{T_H}$$

**Step 2: Solve for the heat ratio**:

$$\dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the general refrigerator COP expression** (Step 1 of Derivation 3):

$$\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{Q_H}{Q_L} - 1} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1}$$

**Step 4: Simplify by multiplying numerator and denominator by T_L**:

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Carnot COP of a Heat Pump

Using the identity from Section 3.3, **add 1 to the Carnot refrigerator COP**:

$$\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} + \dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{T_L + T_H - T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Ton of Refrigeration (TR) in kW

> **Definition:** 1 TR = heat required to melt 1 tonne (1000 kg) of ice at 0 °C to water at 0 °C in 24 hours.

**Step 1: Latent heat of fusion of ice** = **335 kJ/kg** (standard value).

**Step 2: Total heat to melt 1000 kg of ice**:

$$Q = m \cdot L_f = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg} = 335{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 3: Time period = 24 hours = 24 × 3600 = 86,400 s**.

**Step 4: Rate of heat removal in kJ/s (= kW)**:

$$\dot{Q} = \dfrac{335{,}000 \text{ kJ}}{86{,}400 \text{ s}} \approx 3.876 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5: Note the convention** — KTU and most textbooks use the round value **1 TR ≈ 3.517 kW**, which corresponds to using **L_f = 2326 kJ/tonne-refrigeration-hour** (i.e., 211 kJ/min). This is the value you should write in KTU exam answers.

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 211 \text{ kJ/min} = 12{,}600 \text{ kJ/h}}$$

---

## 3.7 Analytical Proof — Carnot COP is the Upper Bound

> **Statement (Proved in KTU Module 1):** No real refrigerator operating between T_L and T_H can have a COP greater than $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$.

**Proof by Contradiction (sketch):** Assume a refrigerator R_real with COP > COP_Carnot exists. Couple it to a Carnot heat engine operating between the same reservoirs. Because R_real's COP is higher, it extracts more Q_L per unit of work than the Carnot refrigerator would. Combine the two devices — the net result violates the Clausius statement of the Second Law. **Hence the Carnot COP is the maximum possible.** ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** The KTU 2024 paper routinely asks: *"A refrigerator has a COP of X. Can it operate between T_L and T_H?"* The answer requires comparing the given COP with the Carnot COP at the same temperatures. If given > Carnot, the case is **thermodynamically impossible**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Heat and Work Flows in a Refrigerator

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional energy flows** between the reservoirs, the cyclic device, and the surroundings. It is the *standard schematic* KTU examiners expect in Module 1 answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ColdRes[Low Temperature Reservoir T_L] -- Q_L heat absorbed --> Dev[Reversed Cycle Device Refrigerant]
    Dev -- Q_H heat rejected --> HotRes[High Temperature Reservoir T_H]
    WorkSrc[External Work Source W_net] -- W_net mechanical electrical input --> Dev
    classDef res fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000;
    classDef dev fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000;
    classDef src fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000;
    class ColdRes,HotRes res;
    class Dev dev;
    class WorkSrc src;
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **refrigerant cycle device** sits at the centre, connected to both reservoirs.
- **Q_L** flows *inward* (from cold reservoir to device) — this is the *useful refrigeration effect*.
- **Q_H** flows *outward* (from device to hot reservoir) — this is the *unwanted waste heat* (useful only for a heat pump).
- **W_net** is supplied from outside to drive the cycle.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Refrigerator vs. Heat Pump (Same Hardware, Different Useful Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REFRIGERATOR_MODE
        R1[Cold Room T_L] -- Q_L is USEFUL --> Cycle1[Reversed Cycle Device]
        Work1[Work Input W_net] --> Cycle1
        Cycle1 -- Q_H is WASTE --> Out1[Ambient T_H]
    end
    subgraph HEAT_PUMP_MODE
        R2[Outside Air T_L] -- Q_L is WASTE --> Cycle2[Reversed Cycle Device]
        Work2[Work Input W_net] --> Cycle2
        Cycle2 -- Q_H is USEFUL --> Room2[Heated Room T_H]
    end
    classDef useful fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000;
    classDef waste fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000;
    class R1,Room2 useful;
    class Out1,R2 waste;
```

**Key takeaway:** The *physics* is identical. Only the *label of "useful"* changes.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Reversed Carnot Cycle on T–s Plane (Functional Mapping)

Since a Mermaid diagram cannot accurately render thermodynamic charts, the following **functional flow** maps the four processes of the Reversed Carnot cycle to the corresponding heat/work interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[State 1 Low P Low T Vapour] -- Isentropic Compression W_in --> P2[State 2 High P High T Vapour]
    P2 -- Isothermal Rejection Q_H to T_H --> P3[State 3 High P Saturated Liquid]
    P3 -- Isentropic Expansion W_out --> P4[State 4 Low P Low T Two Phase]
    P4 -- Isothermal Absorption Q_L from T_L --> P1
    classDef state fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000;
    class P1,P2,P3,P4 state;
```

**Process Table (T–s Plane Coordinates):**

| Process | From → To | Nature | Heat Transfer | Work Transfer |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 → 2 | Vapour → Superheated vapour | Isentropic compression | Zero | W_in (positive) |
| 2 → 3 | Superheated → Saturated liquid | Isothermal condensation at T_H | Q_H (rejected) | Zero |
| 3 → 4 | Saturated liquid → Two-phase | Isentropic expansion | Zero | W_out (negative) |
| 4 → 1 | Two-phase → Saturated vapour | Isothermal evaporation at T_L | Q_L (absorbed) | Zero |

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU exam, always draw the **T-s diagram** of the Reversed Carnot cycle showing: a *rectangle* with T_H on top and T_L on the bottom, with processes 4→1 and 2→3 horizontal (isothermal) and 1→2 and 3→4 vertical (isentropic). Mark all four state points (1, 2, 3, 4) and all three energy flows (Q_L, Q_H, W_net).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define a refrigerator and a heat pump. State the relationship between their COPs.**

**Model Answer:**

A *refrigerator* is a cyclic device that extracts heat Q_L from a low-temperature body and rejects it to a high-temperature body with the help of external work input W_net. The *useful effect* is Q_L, so

$$\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$

A *heat pump* is a similar device whose *useful effect* is the heat Q_H delivered to the high-temperature space:

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$

The relationship is:

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall (A1):** Many students write "heat pump and refrigerator are the same device" without stating the *relationship* between their COPs. Marks are lost for missing the explicit formula. Always end your answer with the boxed relationship above.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define 1 TR (Tonne of Refrigeration). What is its equivalent in kW?**

**Model Answer:**

*Tonne of Refrigeration* is defined as the rate of heat removal required to freeze **1 tonne (1000 kg) of water already at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**, i.e., to remove the latent heat of fusion at the rate:

$$1 \text{ TR} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ kg} \times 335 \text{ kJ/kg}}{24 \text{ h}} = \dfrac{335{,}000 \text{ kJ}}{86{,}400 \text{ s}} \approx 3.876 \text{ kW}$$

However, by the standard KTU convention (using L_f = 2326 kJ/tonne-refrigeration-hour):

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 211 \text{ kJ/min} = 12{,}600 \text{ kJ/h}}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 1, Q1 with Choice]**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — OR — ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

> **Solve either (A) or (B). Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** A Carnot refrigerator operates between a cold space at **−10 °C** and a hot space at **30 °C**. The heat extracted from the cold space is **1000 kJ per cycle**.
Find:
(i) The COP of the refrigerator.
(ii) The work input per cycle.
(iii) The heat rejected to the hot space per cycle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the same device is operated as a heat pump between the same reservoirs, find:
(i) The COP of the heat pump.
(ii) The heat delivered to the hot space per cycle for the same work input.
(iii) Comment on the relative performance.

---

##### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A:

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert all temperatures to Kelvin** (mandatory).

$$T_L = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K} \quad ; \quad T_H = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Carnot refrigerator COP** using Formula #5 from the cheat sheet:

$$\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{263}{303 - 263} = \dfrac{263}{40} = 6.575$$

**Step 3: Compute the work input** using Formula #2:

$$W_{net} = \dfrac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ kJ}}{6.575} = 152.09 \text{ kJ/cycle}$$

**Step 4: Compute the heat rejected** using the First Law (Formula #1):

$$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} = 1000 + 152.09 = 1152.09 \text{ kJ/cycle}$$

> **Answer Box (a):** (i) COP_R = **6.575**, (ii) W_net = **152.09 kJ/cycle**, (iii) Q_H = **1152.09 kJ/cycle**.

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1: COP of heat pump** using Formula #6:

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{303}{40} = 7.575$$

> **Quick check:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1 = 6.575 + 1 = 7.575$ ✓

**Step 2: Heat delivered for the same work input** (152.09 kJ):

$$Q_H^{HP} = \text{COP}_{HP} \times W_{net} = 7.575 \times 152.09 = 1152.09 \text{ kJ/cycle}$$

(consistent with Part (a), as expected — same energy balance).

**Step 3: Comment on relative performance:**

The *same hardware* delivers **1152.09 kJ to the hot space** when run as a heat pump, versus **1000 kJ extracted from the cold space** when run as a refrigerator. The heat pump appears "more efficient" by 15.2 % because its useful effect (Q_H) includes the work input. In absolute terms, both modes are equally energy-intensive.

> **Answer Box (b):** (i) COP_HP = **7.575**, (ii) Q_H = **1152.09 kJ/cycle**, (iii) Heat pump COP is greater by exactly 1 (universal identity).

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** A household refrigerator has a COP of **3.5** and a power consumption of **2 kW**.
Find:
(i) The rate of heat removal from the cold space (in kW and in TR).
(ii) The rate of heat rejected to the kitchen (in kW).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Can this refrigerator maintain a cold space at **−20 °C** if the kitchen temperature is **32 °C**? Justify your answer using the Carnot COP limit.

---

##### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B:

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Rate of heat removal (refrigeration effect)**:

$$\dot{Q}_L = \text{COP}_R \times \dot{W}_{net} = 3.5 \times 2 = 7.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Convert to TR** (Formula #8):

$$\dot{Q}_L = \dfrac{7.0 \text{ kW}}{3.517 \text{ kW/TR}} \approx 1.99 \text{ TR}$$

**Step 3: Rate of heat rejected** to the kitchen (First Law):

$$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{net} = 7.0 + 2.0 = 9.0 \text{ kW}$$

> **Answer Box (a):** (i) **7.0 kW ≈ 1.99 TR**, (ii) **9.0 kW**.

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin**:

$$T_L = -20 + 273 = 253 \text{ K} \quad ; \quad T_H = 32 + 273 = 305 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Compute the maximum possible (Carnot) COP** for these reservoir temperatures:

$$\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{253}{305 - 253} = \dfrac{253}{52} \approx 4.865$$

**Step 3: Compare with actual COP**:

$$\text{COP}_{R,\text{actual}} = 3.5 \;\; < \;\; \text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = 4.865$$

**Step 4: Conclusion:**

> Since the actual COP (3.5) is **less than** the Carnot COP (4.865), the refrigerator **CAN** maintain the cold space at −20 °C. The Second Law is not violated, and the device is thermodynamically feasible (though not ideal — an ideal machine could achieve up to 4.865).

> **Answer Box (b):** **Yes, it is feasible** because $3.5 < 4.865$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Q-B Part b):** This is a *very common question type* in KTU 2024 ESE. Students frequently **forget to convert °C to K** before substituting, giving a wrong Carnot COP. If the examiner's model answer is 4.865 and a student writes $\dfrac{-20}{32 - (-20)} = -0.385$ (a *negative* COP!), the student gets **zero marks** for the entire sub-part. **Always convert to Kelvin first.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> 📌 **MODULE 1: REFRIGERATOR AND HEAT PUMP — REVISION BULLET POINTS**

- ✅ A **refrigerator** removes Q_L from cold space; a **heat pump** delivers Q_H to hot space. **Same hardware, different useful effect.**
- ✅ **First Law:** $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$ (universal).
- ✅ **COP_R = Q_L / W_net**   |   **COP_HP = Q_H / W_net**.
- ✅ **Identity to memorize:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ (proved by adding numerators).
- ✅ **Carnot COP_R = T_L / (T_H − T_L)** — *always in Kelvin.*
- ✅ **Carnot COP_HP = T_H / (T_H − T_L)** = COP_R + 1.
- ✅ **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 211 kJ/min = 12,600 kJ/h** (use 3.517 kW, not 3.876, in KTU answers).
- ✅ **Reversed Carnot cycle** = 2 isothermals + 2 isentropics. Draw the *rectangle* on T-s plane.
- ✅ **Real cycle COP < Carnot COP** (Second Law). A COP greater than Carnot ⇒ thermodynamically impossible.
- ✅ The **cold reservoir Q_L is the *useful effect* of a refrigerator** but the *waste* of a heat pump.
- ✅ T–s diagram of reversed Carnot: processes 1→2 (isentropic compression, vertical), 2→3 (isothermal rejection at T_H, horizontal right-to-left), 3→4 (isentropic expansion, vertical), 4→1 (isothermal absorption at T_L, horizontal left-to-right).
- ✅ For *ratio problems*, the form $1/\text{COP}_R = Q_H/Q_L - 1$ is often the fastest path.
- ✅ **Maximum efficiency of a heat pump** is **infinite** only when $T_H - T_L \to 0$ (impractical). Real heat pumps have COP_HP ≈ 3–5.
- ✅ **KTU-specific traps:** (1) forgetting Kelvin conversion, (2) using 3.876 instead of 3.517 for TR, (3) omitting the COP_HP = COP_R + 1 derivation step, (4) labelling the wrong heat as "useful" in a heat-pump question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coefficient of performance (COP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Coefficient of Performance (COP) # ⚙️ COEFFICIENT OF PERFORMANCE (COP) — KTU Module 1 Study Notes --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is defined as the ratio of the *desired refrigeration or heating effect* to the *net work input* supplied to the system. It is a pure dimensionless number that measures the *effectiveness* of a refrigerator or a heat pump. ### Formal KTU Definitions **1. COP of a Refrigerator (COP_R):** It is the ratio of the rate of heat removal from the cold space (Refrigerating Effect, RE) to the net work input to the compressor. **2. COP of a Heat Pump (COP_HP):** It is the ratio of the rate of heat rejected to the hot space (Heating Effect, HE) to the net work input to the compressor. > [!NOTE] > **Why dimensionless?** > COP is a ratio of two energy quantities (both in **kW** or **kJ/s**), hence the units cancel out. It is *never* expressed as a percentage. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠 Think of COP as the **"Mileage"** of a refrigerator: | Vehicle Analogy | Refrigerator Analogy | | :--- | :--- | | Distance travelled per litre of fuel (km/litre) | Cooling produced per kW of electricity (RE / W) | | Higher mileage = efficient car | Higher COP = efficient refrigerator | | A car giving 25 km/l is more efficient than one giving 15 km/l | A refrigerator with COP 3.5 removes more heat per unit power than one with COP 2.0 | In **plain English**: *"For every 1 unit of electrical energy you give to a refrigerator, how many units of heat does it pull out from your cold room?"* That multiplier is the COP. > [!TIP] > **Quick Reality Check:** A typical domestic refrigerator has a COP between **2.0 and 3.0**. A well-designed cold storage unit may achieve **3.5 to 4.5**. The *theoretical maximum* (Carnot COP) is higher but never reached in practice due to irreversibilities. ### Two Different Devices — One Cycle The **same vapor compression cycle** can function as either: - 🔵 A **Refrigerator** (goal: produce cooling) → use **COP_R** - 🔴 A **Heat Pump** (goal: produce heating) → use **COP_HP** The difference lies only in **what you call the "desired output"**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🔵 Logical Breakdown — Why COP Exists A refrigerator consumes work `W_net` to accomplish two things: 1. Extract heat `Q_L` from the cold reservoir (at low temperature T_L) 2. Reject heat `Q_H` to the hot reservoir (at high temperature T_H) By the **First Law of Thermodynamics (Energy Balance)**: $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$$ The desired output depends on *which perspective* you adopt: - If you want **cooling** → Desired output = `Q_L` - If you want **heating** → Desired output = `Q_H` This single distinction creates two different COP values. --- ### 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorise these equations — they appear in almost every KTU ESE question on this topic. **All temperatures are absolute (Kelvin or Rankine).** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Remarks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **COP of Refrigerator** | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Dimensionless | | 2 | **COP of Heat Pump** | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | **Relationship** | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | Always holds true | | 4 | **Carnot COP of Refrigerator** | $\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | For Reversed Carnot Cycle | | 5 | **Carnot COP of Heat Pump** | $\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | For Reversed Carnot Cycle | | 6 | **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** | $\text{RE} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$ | kW (per kg/s) | | 7 | **Power Input (Actual)** | $W_{act} = \dfrac{\text{RE}}{\text{COP}_R}$ | kW | | 8 | **Heat Pump Heating Rate** | $Q_H = W_{net} \cdot \text{COP}_{HP}$ | kW | | 9 | **Second Law Limit** | $\text{COP}_R \le \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Irreversibilities reduce COP | | 10 | **COP Performance Ratio** | $\dfrac{\text{COP}_{HP}}{\text{COP}_R} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_L}$ | Useful for comparisons | > [!NOTE] > **Trick to remember:** For a *refrigerator*, the **smaller temperature** (T_L) appears in the numerator. For a *heat pump*, the **larger temperature** (T_H) appears in the numerator. Both have the **same denominator** `T_H - T_L`. --- ### 🔬 Real-World Engineering Utility - **HVAC Industry**: Heat pumps (COP_HP 3 to 5) are widely used as energy-efficient space heaters — they deliver 3–5 units of heat per unit of electricity consumed. - **Cold Chain Logistics**: Selecting refrigerant and cycle parameters is driven by maximising COP_R to reduce operating cost. - **District Cooling Plants**: Comparing two competing refrigeration systems — the one with higher COP_R is the more economically viable choice. - **Energy Auditing (BEE Star Ratings)**: The Indian Bureau of Energy Efficiency uses COP-derived metrics for rating air-conditioners. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🧮 Derivation 1: Relationship between COP_HP and COP_R **Statement to Prove:** $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$$ **Starting Point — Energy Balance on the Refrigeration Cycle:** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} \quad \text{...(First Law of Thermodynamics)}$$ **Step 1: Write the definition of COP_HP.** $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 2: Substitute the value of Q_H from the energy balance.** $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_L + W_{net}}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 3: Split the numerator.** $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + \frac{W_{net}}{W_{net}}$$ **Step 4: Identify the first term as COP_R.** Since $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$, we substitute: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$$ **Hence Proved. ∎** > [!TIP] > **Why is COP_HP always greater than COP_R?** > Because `Q_H > Q_L` always (heat pump delivers the *whole* rejected heat, while refrigerator only cares about the extracted heat). Numerically, COP_HP is always at least 1 unit higher than COP_R. --- ### 🧮 Derivation 2: Carnot COP of a Refrigerator **Statement to Prove:** $$\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Starting Point — Reversed Carnot Cycle on a T-s Diagram:** The Reversed Carnot Cycle consists of: - **Process 1→2**: Isentropic compression (T_L → T_H) - **Process 2→3**: Isothermal heat rejection at T_H (rejects Q_H) - **Process 3→4**: Isentropic expansion (T_H → T_L) - **Process 4→1**: Isothermal heat absorption at T_L (absorbs Q_L) **Step 1: Apply the Second Law for a Reversible (Carnot) Cycle.** For a reversible cycle, the cyclic integral of dQ/T equals zero (Clausius Equality): $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ **Step 2: Evaluate the only two processes where heat transfer occurs** (the two isotherms): $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0$$ (Sign convention: heat rejected is positive from system viewpoint; heat absorbed is negative. Taking magnitudes, the two terms balance.) **Step 3: Rearrange to obtain Q_H in terms of Q_L.** $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$ $$Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$ **Step 4: Substitute into the COP_R definition.** $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}$$ $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L}$$ **Step 5: Factor out Q_L from the denominator.** $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \left(\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1\right)}$$ **Step 6: Cancel Q_L.** $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H}{T_L} - 1}$$ **Step 7: Bring the denominator to a common form.** $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}}$$ **Step 8: Invert the fraction.** $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ **Hence Proved. ∎** --- ### 🧮 Derivation 3: Carnot COP of a Heat Pump **Using the relationship COP_HP = COP_R + 1:** $$\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} + 1$$ $$\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L + (T_H - T_L)}{T_H - T_L}$$ $$\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ **Hence Proved. ∎** --- ### 📊 Analytical Matrix — COP Comparison Table | Scenario | Refrigerator (COP_R) | Heat Pump (COP_HP) | Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Numerical Example (T_L = 273 K, T_H = 303 K) | $\dfrac{273}{30} = 9.1$ | $\dfrac{303}{30} = 10.1$ | Diff = exactly 1.0 | | Same temperatures, but different desired output | 9.1 | 10.1 | Same cycle, different label | | Lowering T_L (deeper cooling) | Decreases | Decreases | Harder to refrigerate | | Increasing T_H (hotter surroundings) | Decreases | Decreases | More heat to reject | | Reversible vs Irreversible | Irreversible COP < Carnot COP | Irreversible COP < Carnot COP | 2nd Law limit | --- ### 🎯 Sample Solved Numerical (For Pattern Recognition) **Problem:** A refrigerator maintains a temperature of **−10 °C** in its freezer compartment while the ambient temperature is **27 °C**. The heat removal rate from the freezer is **2 kW**. Calculate: 1. The Carnot COP of the refrigerator 2. The Carnot COP of the heat pump 3. The minimum power input required **Solution:** **Convert temperatures to Kelvin:** - T_L = −10 + 273 = **263 K** - T_H = 27 + 273 = **300 K** **Part (a) — Carnot COP_R:** $$\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{300 - 263} = \frac{263}{37} = 7.108$$ **Part (b) — Carnot COP_HP:** $$\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{300}{37} = 8.108$$ *(Verify: 7.108 + 1 = 8.108 ✓)* **Part (c) — Minimum Power Input:** $$W_{net,\text{min}} = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{2 \text{ kW}}{7.108} = 0.2814 \text{ kW} = 281.4 \text{ W}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Pattern:** Such numericals are usually asked for **7 marks** under Part B, requiring all three sub-parts with proper units and temperature conversions. Skipping K conversion = direct loss of 1–2 marks. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 🔁 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Refrigeration Cycle (for COP Analysis) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressor
Work Input Wnet] --> B[Condenser
Heat Rejected QH at TH] B --> C[Expansion Valve
Throttling Process] C --> D[Evaporator
Heat Absorbed QL at TL] D --> A A -.->|COP_R = QL / Wnet| E[Refrigerator
Desired Cooling Output] B -.->|COP_HP = QH / Wnet| F[Heat Pump
Desired Heating Output] E -.->|Relationship| G[COP_HP = COP_R + 1] F -.->|Relationship| G ``` ### 🌡️ Reversed Carnot Cycle on T-s Diagram (Conceptual Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["State 1: Saturated Vapour at TL
End of Evaporator"] -->|Isentropic Compression
Wnet in| P2["State 2: Superheated Vapour at TH"] P2 -->|Isothermal Heat Rejection
QH out at TH| P3["State 3: Saturated Liquid at TH"] P3 -->|Isentropic Expansion
No Heat Transfer| P4["State 4: Liquid-Vapour Mix at TL"] P4 -->|Isothermal Heat Absorption
QL in at TL| P1 P1 -.->|COP_R = QL divided by Wnet| M1["Maximum Possible COP
Carnot Limit"] P2 -.->|COP_HP = QH divided by Wnet| M2["Heat Pump Performance
Carnot Limit"] ``` ### 📐 Energy Flow Sankey-Style Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Low Temperature Source TL
QL extracted] --> SYS((Refrigeration
System)) SYS -->|Wnet input| POW[Work Source
Electricity] SYS -->|QH rejected| SNK[High Temperature Sink TH] SYS -.->|COP_R metric| MET1[Cooling Performance] SYS -.->|COP_HP metric| MET2[Heating Performance] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]** > **Q1.** Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator and a heat pump. How are they related? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **COP of Refrigerator:** It is defined as the ratio of the refrigerating effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the net work input to the compressor. $$\text{COP}_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$$ - **COP of Heat Pump:** It is defined as the ratio of the heating effect (heat rejected to the hot space) to the net work input to the compressor. $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}$$ - **Relationship:** By the First Law, $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$, therefore: $$\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$$ > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]** > **Q2.** Why is the COP of a Carnot refrigerator always greater than 1? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - COP_R is defined as $\dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$. For any refrigerator, the heat extracted `Q_L` is greater than the work input `W_net`, since by the First Law: $$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net}$$ - For Carnot cycle: $\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$. - Since both `T_L` and `T_H` are positive absolute temperatures and `T_H > T_L`, the denominator `(T_H − T_L)` is positive and less than `T_L`, making the ratio always greater than 1. - For example, at `T_L = 273 K` and `T_H = 303 K`, COP_R = `273 / 30 ≈ 9.1`, which is significantly greater than 1. - This indicates that a refrigerator is more effective at moving heat than converting work directly into heat. --- ### 📘 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) --- #### 🔹 QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive an expression for the COP of a Carnot refrigerator and a Carnot heat pump. Show that $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$. **(b)** [7 Marks] A refrigerator operates between temperature limits of **−5 °C** and **35 °C**. If the actual COP is **40%** of the Carnot COP, calculate: 1. The Carnot COP of the refrigerator 2. The actual COP of the refrigerator 3. The power required to maintain a cooling load of **10 kW** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation:** - **Step 1:** For a Reversed Carnot Cycle, apply the Second Law (Clausius equality for a reversible cycle): $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = 0 \implies Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$ - **Step 2:** Substituting into COP definitions: $$\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ - **Step 3:** By the relationship derived in the notes: $$\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} + 1 \quad \text{(Proved)}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Convert temperatures:** - T_L = −5 + 273 = **268 K** - T_H = 35 + 273 = **308 K** **Step 1 — Carnot COP_R:** $$\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{268}{308 - 268} = \frac{268}{40} = 6.70$$ **Step 2 — Actual COP_R:** $$\text{COP}_{R,\text{actual}} = 0.40 \times 6.70 = 2.68$$ **Step 3 — Power Required:** $$W = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_{R,\text{actual}}} = \frac{10 \text{ kW}}{2.68} = 3.73 \text{ kW}$$ **Final Answer:** - Carnot COP_R = **6.70** - Actual COP_R = **2.68** - Power input = **3.73 kW** --- #### 🔹 QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** [7 Marks] A cold storage is to be maintained at **−20 °C** while the ambient temperature is **40 °C**. The heat leakage into the cold storage is estimated at **120 kW**. Assuming a Carnot refrigerator: 1. Calculate the COP of the refrigerator and the heat pump. 2. Determine the power input to the compressor. 3. Determine the heat rejected to the surroundings. **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain why actual refrigerators have a COP lower than the Carnot COP. List at least **four irreversibilities** present in a practical vapor compression cycle. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Numerical:** **Convert temperatures:** - T_L = −20 + 273 = **253 K** - T_H = 40 + 273 = **313 K** **Step 1 — COP_R (Carnot):** $$\text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{253}{313 - 253} = \frac{253}{60} = 4.217$$ **Step 2 — COP_HP (Carnot):** $$\text{COP}_{HP,\text{Carnot}} = \text{COP}_{R,\text{Carnot}} + 1 = 5.217$$ **Step 3 — Power Input:** $$W_{net} = \frac{Q_L}{\text{COP}_R} = \frac{120}{4.217} = 28.46 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 — Heat Rejected to Surroundings:** $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{net} = 120 + 28.46 = 148.46 \text{ kW}$$ **Final Answer:** - COP_R = **4.217**, COP_HP = **5.217** - Power input = **28.46 kW** - Heat rejected = **148.46 kW** **Part (b) — Explanation (4 Irreversibilities):** 1. **Non-isentropic compression** in the actual compressor (entropy increases due to friction and heat losses). 2. **Non-isentropic expansion** through the throttling valve (large pressure drop causes entropy rise). 3. **Finite temperature difference** during heat transfer in the condenser and evaporator (heat transfer across finite ΔT is irreversible). 4. **Pressure drops** in the piping and heat exchangers due to fluid friction. These irreversibilities increase entropy, demanding more work input for the same refrigerating effect, thereby reducing the COP below the Carnot limit. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** — `T_L` and `T_H` must be in **absolute units (K)**. Using `−5` instead of `268` will yield a *negative* COP and zero marks. > 2. **Confusing Q_H and Q_L** — In a refrigerator, `Q_L` is the *extracted* heat; in a heat pump, `Q_H` is the *delivered* heat. > 3. **Saying "COP is in %"** — COP is dimensionless. Writing "COP = 350%" loses the conceptual mark. > 4. **Not verifying the relation** — Always cross-check $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ after calculation; failure to do so indicates a unit/definition error. > 5. **Skipping units in the final answer** — Every numerical must carry units (kW, K, etc.). Missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark even if the value is correct. --- ### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - 📌 **COP_R = Q_L / W_net** — Always extract heat divided by work input. - 📌 **COP_HP = Q_H / W_net** — Always rejected heat divided by work input. - 📌 **Golden Relation:** $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ — *always true*, for any cycle. - 📌 **Carnot Limit:** $\text{COP}_{R,\text{max}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — applies only to reversible cycles. - 📌 **Temperatures must be in Kelvin** (or Rankine) — not °C or °F. - 📌 **COP is dimensionless** — never write %, ppm, or any unit. - 📌 **Higher COP = Better performance** — but bounded above by Carnot limit. - 📌 **Heat pump is always more efficient than electric resistance heating** (COP_HP > 1 implies heat delivered > electricity consumed). - 📌 **Reducing T_L (deeper cooling) decreases COP_R** — designing for very low temperatures is energy-expensive. - 📌 **Increasing T_H (hotter condenser ambient) decreases COP_R** — placing condenser outdoors in summer reduces efficiency. - 📌 **Actual COP < Carnot COP** — due to friction, non-isentropic processes, finite ΔT, and pressure drops. ---

topics.title: Gas and vapour as refrigerant in reversed Carnot cycle
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gas and vapour as refrigerant in reversed Carnot cycle

# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Reversed Carnot Cycle — Formal Definition

The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is a theoretical, idealized thermodynamic cycle that operates in the *reverse direction* of the standard Carnot heat engine cycle. It serves as the benchmark standard of perfection for **all refrigeration and heat pump systems** because it yields the **maximum possible Coefficient of Performance (COP)** for a given pair of operating temperature limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In a reversed Carnot cycle, the working fluid (refrigerant) absorbs heat from a *low-temperature* reservoir (cold space) and rejects it to a *high-temperature* reservoir (surroundings). All four processes are internally **reversible**.

### The Four Reversible Processes
| Step | Process | Description |
|:----:|:--------|:------------|
| 1 → 2 | Isentropic Compression | Refrigerant compressed reversibly & adiabatically |
| 2 → 3 | Isothermal Heat Rejection | Heat $Q_2$ rejected to hot reservoir at $T_2$ |
| 3 → 4 | Isentropic Expansion | Refrigerant expanded reversibly & adiabatically |
| 4 → 1 | Isothermal Heat Absorption | Heat $Q_1$ absorbed from cold reservoir at $T_1$ |

---

## 1.2 Gas vs. Vapour as Refrigerant — Conceptual Analogy

### 🧊 The "Sponge" Analogy (Plain English)
Imagine you are trying to cool a room. You have two options to "carry heat" out of it:

- **A GAS is like a feather pillow being squeezed and released.** You push the gas (compress), it gets a little hotter. You cool it. You let it expand inside the room, and it gets a little cooler. It absorbs a **small** amount of heat because it only changes *temperature* — there is no phase change. The amount of heat absorbed per kg is tiny — this is **sensible cooling**.

- **A VAPOUR is like a dry sponge that becomes wet when it evaporates.** You compress the vapour (it condenses into liquid), release the heat. You let the liquid evaporate inside the cold room — and in doing so, it absorbs a **massive** amount of heat (the **latent heat of vaporization**). This is like the sponge "soaking up" heat to turn from liquid to gas. Per kilogram, the vapour carries away *hundreds of times* more heat than a gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** A vapour refrigeration system needs a *much smaller* compressor and *less* refrigerant flow rate to achieve the same cooling effect, which is why every practical refrigerator and AC you have ever seen uses a vapour, not a gas.

---

## 1.3 Why Vapour is Preferred — A Quantitative Glimpse

| Property | Gas Refrigerant | Vapour Refrigerant |
|:---------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| Phase change? | ❌ No (always gaseous) | ✅ Yes (liquid ↔ vapour) |
| Heat absorption mechanism | Sensible heat only (small $c_p \Delta T$) | Latent heat of vaporization (very large $h_{fg}$) |
| Refrigeration effect per kg | **Low** | **Very High** |
| Compressor work required | High | Comparatively low |
| Practical feasibility | Poor | Excellent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Reversed Carnot Cycle is identical in *form* for both gas and vapour (same four processes, same COP formula), but the **magnitude** of refrigeration effect is dramatically different. This is the central KTU takeaway from this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Reversed Carnot Cycle with a GAS as Refrigerant

In a **gas refrigeration cycle**, the working fluid remains in the **gaseous state** throughout the entire cycle. There is **no condensation or evaporation** — heat is absorbed and rejected purely through **sensible temperature changes** during the two isothermal processes, assisted by adiabatic compression and expansion.

### Process-by-Process Logical Breakdown

- **Process 1 → 2 (Isentropic Compression):**
  The gas at low temperature $T_1$ is compressed *reversibly and adiabatically* to a higher temperature $T_2$. No heat transfer occurs. Work is done *on* the gas (compressor input).
  *Why?* To raise the gas temperature so that it can reject heat to the hot reservoir.

- **Process 2 → 3 (Isothermal Heat Rejection):**
  The hot gas at $T_2$ is kept at constant temperature by an external cooler. Heat $Q_2$ is rejected to surroundings.
  *How?* The gas must be compressed further to maintain constant $T$ (since compression tends to heat it). This is the **reversed of the isothermal expansion** in a heat engine.

- **Process 3 → 4 (Isentropic Expansion):**
  The gas expands adiabatically in a *reversible* expander, dropping its temperature back from $T_2$ to $T_1$.
  *Why?* To lower the gas temperature below the cold space so it can absorb heat.

- **Process 4 → 1 (Isothermal Heat Absorption):**
  The cold gas at $T_1$ absorbs heat $Q_1$ from the refrigerated space.
  *How?* The gas must be expanded further to maintain constant $T$ (since expansion tends to cool it). This is the **reversed of the isothermal compression** in a heat engine.

---

## 2.2 Reversed Carnot Cycle with a VAPOUR as Refrigerant

The *form* of the cycle is identical to the gas cycle, but now the working fluid undergoes **phase change** (condensation during heat rejection, evaporation during heat absorption) within the **two-phase (wet) region** of the dome.

### Key Conceptual Difference
Because the fluid is inside the wet-dome, the **isotherms are also isobars** (pressure remains constant during phase change at saturation). Hence:
- The isothermal heat rejection occurs at *constant pressure* (and constant temperature) — this is the **condensation** process.
- The isothermal heat absorption occurs at *constant pressure* (and constant temperature) — this is the **evaporation** process.

> [!NOTE]
> The P-V and T-S diagrams of a vapour reversed Carnot cycle take the **same shape** as the gas version, but the isothermal lines lie *inside* the saturation dome, where horizontal lines on the T-S diagram are ALSO isobaric.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:------|
| 1 | COP of Reversed Carnot Refrigerator | $\text{COP}_{R} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1}$ | dimensionless | $T_1$ = cold reservoir, $T_2$ = hot reservoir (in K) |
| 2 | COP of Reversed Carnot Heat Pump | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_2 - T_1}$ | dimensionless | Always $\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ |
| 3 | Refrigerating effect (gas) | $RE_{gas} = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Purely sensible |
| 4 | Refrigerating effect (vapour) | $RE_{vap} = h_{fg}$ at $T_1$ | kJ/kg | Purely latent, dominates |
| 5 | Compressor work (per kg) | $W_{comp} = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Same for both at same $T$-limits |
| 6 | Heat rejected | $Q_2 = Q_1 + W$ | kJ | Energy balance |
| 7 | Ratio comparison | $\dfrac{RE_{vap}}{RE_{gas}} = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{c_p (T_2 - T_1)}$ | — | Typically 50 – 500 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **COP formula is mathematically identical** for both gas and vapour reversed Carnot cycles operating between the same two temperature limits. The difference is in the *mass flow rate* and *physical size* of equipment, not in the theoretical COP.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: COP of Reversed Carnot Cycle (Refrigerator)

**Starting Point:** The Carnot cycle is reversible, so the entropy change of the universe over one complete cycle is zero.

$$
\Delta S_{\text{cycle}} = 0
$$

**Step 1 — Apply entropy balance to the two isothermal processes:**

For the **isothermal heat absorption** at $T_1$ (process 4 → 1), the heat absorbed is $Q_1$ and the entropy change of the refrigerant is:

$$
\Delta S_1 = \frac{Q_1}{T_1}
$$

For the **isothermal heat rejection** at $T_2$ (process 2 → 3), the heat rejected is $Q_2$ and the entropy change is:

$$
\Delta S_2 = -\frac{Q_2}{T_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Isentropic processes have zero entropy change:**
The two adiabatic (isentropic) processes 1 → 2 and 3 → 4 contribute nothing to entropy change.

**Step 3 — Sum of entropy changes must equal zero (cyclic process):**

$$
\frac{Q_1}{T_1} - \frac{Q_2}{T_2} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the ratio of heats:**

$$
\frac{Q_1}{T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{T_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{T_1}{T_2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the First Law to the cycle:**
Net work input equals net heat rejected minus net heat absorbed:

$$
W_{\text{net}} = Q_2 - Q_1
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the definition of COP for a refrigerator:**

$$
\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{\text{Desired effect}}{\text{Work input}} = \frac{Q_1}{W_{\text{net}}}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $W = Q_2 - Q_1$:**

$$
\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2 - Q_1}
$$

**Step 8 — Divide numerator and denominator by $Q_1$:**

$$
\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} - 1}
$$

**Step 9 — Substitute $Q_2/Q_1 = T_2/T_1$ from Step 4:**

$$
\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - 1}
$$

**Step 10 — Final simplification:**

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1}}
$$

> **Conversion logic used:** This single expression is valid for **both** gas and vapour reversed Carnot cycles because the derivation only used the first and second laws — it makes no assumption about the working fluid's phase behaviour.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: COP of Reversed Carnot Heat Pump

**Step 1 — Definition of COP for a heat pump:**

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{\text{Desired effect (heat rejected)}}{\text{Work input}} = \frac{Q_2}{W_{\text{net}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $W = Q_2 - Q_1$:**

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_2 - Q_1} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Q_1/Q_2 = T_1/T_2$:**

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}} = \frac{T_2}{T_2 - T_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Prove the universal relationship:**

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_{R} = \frac{T_2}{T_2 - T_1} - \frac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_2 - T_1} = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_{R} + 1}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Gas vs. Vapour Refrigerant

For the **same** $T_1$ and $T_2$, the theoretical COP is identical. But the **refrigeration effect per unit mass** is enormously different.

| Parameter | Gas Refrigerant | Vapour Refrigerant |
|:----------|:----------------|:-------------------|
| Refrigeration effect per kg | $q_1 = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | $q_1 = h_{fg} \text{ at } T_1$ |
| Compressor work per kg | $w_c = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ (isentropic) |
| Mass flow rate for given cooling | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_1}{c_p (T_2 - T_1)}$ | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_1}{h_{fg}}$ |
| Compressor size required | **Very large** | **Compact** |
| Practical application | Aircraft A/C (Bell-Coleman) | Domestic/industrial refrigeration & A/C |

### Sample Numerical Comparison
For an air-standard cycle with $T_1 = 270\text{ K}$ and $T_2 = 310\text{ K}$:
- $c_p = 1.005\text{ kJ/kg·K}$
- $q_{1,\text{gas}} = 1.005 \times 40 = 40.2\text{ kJ/kg}$

For a vapour cycle using R-134a at $T_1 = 270\text{ K}$ (i.e., $-3^\circ\text{C}$):
- $h_{fg} \approx 198\text{ kJ/kg}$

Ratio = $198 / 40.2 \approx 4.9$ — vapour carries nearly 5× more heat per kg. In real high-temperature spans, the ratio is far higher (often 50–500×).

---

## 3.4 Limitations of Reversed Carnot Cycle (KTU High-Frequency Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reversed Carnot cycle is **theoretically perfect but practically impossible**. KTU examiners almost always test *why* it cannot be built.

| # | Limitation | Explanation |
|:-:|:-----------|:------------|
| 1 | Isentropic compression not achievable | Real compression generates friction and heat → irreversibility |
| 2 | Isothermal compression not possible | Pistons cannot run slow enough; gas heats up during compression |
| 3 | Wet vapour compression damages compressor | Liquid droplets in compression cylinder cause cylinder scoring |
| 4 | Turbine/expander in wet region is inefficient | Two-phase expansion produces erosion and poor work recovery |
| 5 | Bulky and expensive equipment | Theoretically ideal but economically unviable |

> **Real-world utility:** These limitations are exactly why all **practical** refrigeration cycles (Vapour Compression, Vapour Absorption) deviate from reversed Carnot — they trade *some* COP for *practical* operability. The reversed Carnot cycle still serves as the **theoretical benchmark** to measure real cycles against.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Reversed Carnot Refrigeration Cycle (Logical Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressor: Isentropic Compression 1 to 2] --> B[Hot Reservoir: Isothermal Heat Rejection 2 to 3 at T2]
    B --> C[Expander: Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4]
    C --> D[Cold Reservoir: Isothermal Heat Absorption 4 to 1 at T1]
    D --> A
    H1[Heat Q1 absorbed from cold space] -.-> D
    H2[Heat Q2 rejected to surroundings] -.-> B
    WIN[Work W input] -.-> A
```

## 4.2 Process-State Architecture for GAS Refrigerant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["State 1: Low T, Low P gas at T1"]
        S1
    end
    subgraph S2["State 2: High T, High P gas at T2"]
        S2
    end
    subgraph S3["State 3: High T, Medium P gas at T2"]
        S3
    end
    subgraph S4["State 4: Low T, Low P gas at T1"]
        S4
    end
    S1 -->|Isentropic Compression: W in| S2
    S2 -->|Isothermal Heat Rejection: Q2 out| S3
    S3 -->|Isentropic Expansion: W out| S4
    S4 -->|Isothermal Heat Absorption: Q1 in| S1
```

## 4.3 Process-State Architecture for VAPOUR Refrigerant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph V1["State 1: Saturated Vapour at T1"]
        V1
    end
    subgraph V2["State 2: Superheated Vapour at T2"]
        V2
    end
    subgraph V3["State 3: Saturated Liquid at T2"]
        V3
    end
    subgraph V4["State 4: Liquid-Vapour Mixture at T1"]
        V4
    end
    V1 -->|Isentropic Compression in dry region| V2
    V2 -->|Isothermal plus Isobaric Condensation: Q2 out| V3
    V3 -->|Isentropic Expansion through throttle or turbine| V4
    V4 -->|Isothermal plus Isobaric Evaporation: Q1 in| V1
```

## 4.4 Comparison Flow: Why Vapour Wins

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{What is the refrigerant phase during heat absorption?}
    Q -->|Always gas, no phase change| GAS[Gas Refrigerant Cycle]
    Q -->|Liquid to Vapour phase change| VAP[Vapour Refrigerant Cycle]
    GAS -->|Sensible heat only| LOW[Small Refrigeration Effect: cp times dT]
    VAP -->|Latent heat of vaporization| HIGH[Large Refrigeration Effect: h_fg]
    LOW --> BIG[Large mass flow, bulky compressor]
    HIGH --> SMALL[Compact compressor, low mass flow]
    BIG --> IMPRAC[Impractical for domestic use]
    SMALL --> PRACT[Practical: used in all real refrigerators and AC units]
```

## 4.5 P-V and T-S Schematic Representation

| Diagram | Description | For Gas | For Vapour |
|:-------:|:-----------|:--------|:-----------|
| P-V | Rectangle-like shape (isotherms are hyperbolic in gas) | Pure gas region, no dome | Inside wet dome — isotherms are also isobars |
| T-S | Two horizontal lines (isothermal) connected by two vertical lines (isentropic) | Identical shape | Identical shape, but inside saturation dome |

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the four processes of a reversed Carnot refrigeration cycle. Write the expression for the COP of the cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four processes of a reversed Carnot refrigeration cycle are:
1. **Process 1 → 2:** Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) compression in the compressor
2. **Process 2 → 3:** Isothermal heat rejection to the hot reservoir at temperature $T_2$
3. **Process 3 → 4:** Isentropic (reversible adiabatic) expansion in the expander
4. **Process 4 → 1:** Isothermal heat absorption from the cold reservoir at temperature $T_1$

The coefficient of performance is:

$$
\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1}
$$

where $T_1$ and $T_2$ are the cold- and hot-reservoir absolute temperatures (in K). **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why is a vapour preferred over a gas as a refrigerant in practical refrigeration systems, even though both have the same COP in a reversed Carnot cycle?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a reversed Carnot cycle, both gas and vapour refrigerants have the same theoretical COP for given temperature limits. However, in a **gas** cycle, heat is absorbed purely as **sensible heat** ($q_1 = c_p \Delta T$), which is small per unit mass. This requires a **very large mass flow rate** and a **bulky compressor** for any practical cooling load.

In a **vapour** cycle, heat is absorbed as **latent heat of vaporization** ($q_1 = h_{fg}$), which is typically 50–500 times larger than $c_p \Delta T$ for the same temperature span. This means a much smaller mass flow rate and a much more compact compressor can deliver the same cooling effect, making vapour cycles practically feasible. **(3 marks)**

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the COP of a reversed Carnot refrigeration cycle. Show that the COP of a reversed Carnot heat pump is one more than the COP of a reversed Carnot refrigerator operating between the same temperature limits.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reversed Carnot refrigerator operates between temperature limits of $-10^\circ\text{C}$ and $40^\circ\text{C}$. Determine: (i) the COP of the refrigerator, (ii) the COP of the heat pump, and (iii) the heat rejected per kW of work input.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

We apply the **Second Law** over one complete reversible cycle:

$$
\frac{Q_1}{T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{T_2}
$$

By the **First Law** for the cyclic device:

$$
W = Q_2 - Q_1
$$

COP of refrigerator:

$$
\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{Q_1}{W} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_2 - Q_1}
$$

Dividing by $Q_1$ and using $Q_2/Q_1 = T_2/T_1$:

$$
\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} - 1} = \frac{T_1}{T_2 - T_1}
$$

COP of heat pump:

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_2}{W} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_2 - Q_1} = \frac{T_2}{T_2 - T_1}
$$

Difference:

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} - \text{COP}_{R} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_2 - T_1} = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

Given:
- $T_1 = -10 + 273 = 263\text{ K}$
- $T_2 = 40 + 273 = 313\text{ K}$

**(i) COP of refrigerator:**

$$
\text{COP}_{R} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = 5.26
$$

**(ii) COP of heat pump:**

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \text{COP}_R + 1 = 5.26 + 1 = 6.26
$$

**(iii) Heat rejected per kW of work input:**

$$
\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_2}{W} \implies Q_2 = \text{COP}_{HP} \times W = 6.26 \times 1 = 6.26\text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_R = 5.26, \quad \text{COP}_{HP} = 6.26, \quad Q_2 = 6.26\text{ kW}} \quad \textbf{(7 marks)}
$$

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*
