\end{aligned}$$

But $h_1 > h_6$ by the regenerator balance (hot liquid gives heat to cold vapour), so the regenerator **always decreases the available refrigerating effect on a per-kg basis** but increases compressor work — this shows that the simple enthalpy-based comparison is not the right one. The **correct** KTU-level comparison uses the second law.

A more useful textbook result (Arora & Domkundwar standard):

$$\boxed{\,COP_{reg} > COP_{simple} \;\;\text{when}\;\; \frac{T_1}{T_6} < \frac{T_2}{T_3}\,}$$

where $T_1, T_2, T_6$ are absolute temperatures at states 1, 2 and 6, and $T_3$ is the condensation temperature. **This condition is satisfied for most practical refrigerants like R-134a and R-22, which is why regenerative cycles are widely used.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: COP of a Two-Stage Cascade Refrigeration System

### Step 1: Define the system
A cascade system has:
- **Lower cycle** (Cycle L): Evaporator temperature $T_{EL}$, condenser temperature $T_{CL}$.
- **Upper cycle** (Cycle U): Evaporator temperature $T_{EU} = T_{CL}$, condenser temperature $T_{CU}$ (ambient).
- A heat exchanger between the condenser of L and the evaporator of U, where heat is transferred at rate $Q_{int}$.

### Step 2: Lower cycle (the cycle that produces the useful refrigeration)
The useful refrigerating effect is delivered by the lower cycle:

$$Q_{EL} = \dot{m}_L (h_{1L} - h_{4L})$$

The work consumed by the lower compressor:

$$W_L = \dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{1L})$$

The heat rejected by the lower cycle (and absorbed by the upper cycle):

$$Q_{CL} = \dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{3L})$$

### Step 3: Upper cycle (the cycle that pumps the heat to ambient)
The upper evaporator absorbs the lower cycle's rejected heat:

$$Q_{EU} = \dot{m}_U (h_{1U} - h_{4U}) = Q_{CL} = \dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{3L})$$

The work consumed by the upper compressor:

$$W_U = \dot{m}_U (h_{2U} - h_{1U})$$

The heat rejected to the true ambient:

$$Q_{CU} = \dot{m}_U (h_{2U} - h_{3U}) = Q_{EU} + W_U = Q_{CL} + W_U$$

### Step 4: Overall COP
By definition, the cascade COP is the useful refrigeration divided by the total work:

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{cascade} &= \frac{Q_{EL}}{W_L + W_U} \\
COP_{cascade} &= \frac{\dot{m}_L (h_{1L} - h_{4L})}{\dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{1L}) + \dot{m}_U (h_{2U} - h_{1U})}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Express the upper mass flow rate in terms of the lower mass flow rate
From $Q_{EU} = Q_{CL}$:

$$\dot{m}_U (h_{1U} - h_{4U}) = \dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{3L})$$

$$\dot{m}_U = \dot{m}_L \cdot \frac{(h_{2L} - h_{3L})}{(h_{1U} - h_{4U})}$$

Substituting back:

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{cascade} &= \frac{h_{1L} - h_{4L}}{(h_{2L} - h_{1L}) + \dfrac{(h_{2L} - h_{3L})}{(h_{1U} - h_{4U})} \cdot (h_{2U} - h_{1U})} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{h_{1L} - h_{4L}}{(h_{2L} - h_{1L}) + \dfrac{(h_{2L} - h_{3L})(h_{2U} - h_{1U})}{h_{1U} - h_{4U}}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **closed-form expression for the cascade COP** in terms of the four end-state enthalpies of each cycle and the linking heat transfer. The result is rigorous and fully derived from the First Law.

### Step 6: Comparison with single-stage at same temperature lift
For the same evaporator and ambient temperatures, a two-stage cascade has **higher COP than a single stage** for very low evaporator temperatures (below approximately –30 °C) because:

- The compression work in each stage is closer to the ideal isothermal compression path.
- The discharge temperature of each compressor stays within safe limits.
- The volumetric efficiency of each compressor stays reasonable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check (textbook example, R-22 in upper, R-13 in lower):**
> For a –40 °C cold room with 35 °C ambient, cascade COP ≈ 1.8 to 2.1, while a single stage would have COP ≈ 1.1 to 1.3 — the cascade is clearly superior at very low temperatures.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Regenerative Vapour-Compression Cycle (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Compressor] -->|state 2 superheated vapour| nodeB[Condenser]
    nodeB -->|state 3 saturated liquid| nodeC[Regenerator Hot Side]
    nodeC -->|state 4 subcooled liquid| nodeD[Expansion Valve]
    nodeD -->|state 5 two phase mixture| nodeE[Evaporator]
    nodeE -->|state 6 saturated vapour| nodeF[Regenerator Cold Side]
    nodeF -->|state 1 superheated vapour| nodeA
    nodeG[Heat flow Qreg] -.-> nodeF
    nodeC -.-> nodeG
```

**Reading guide:**
- Outer loop: main refrigerant path Compressor → Condenser → Regenerator → Expansion Valve → Evaporator → Regenerator → back to Compressor.
- Dotted lines inside: the internal heat transfer $Q_{reg}$ from the hot liquid stream (nodeC) to the cold vapour stream (nodeF) inside the regenerator.

## 4.2 Cascade (Reduced Ambient) Refrigeration System (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LOWER["LOWER CYCLE Cold Side"]
        compL[Compressor L] -->|state 2L| condL[Condenser L]
        condL -->|state 3L| expL[Expansion Valve L]
        expL -->|state 4L| evapL[Evaporator L]
        evapL -->|state 1L| compL
    end

    subgraph UPPER["UPPER CYCLE Warm Side"]
        compU[Compressor U] -->|state 2U| condU[Condenser U to ambient]
        condU -->|state 3U| expU[Expansion Valve U]
        expU -->|state 4U| evapU[Evaporator U]
        evapU -->|state 1U| compU
    end

    HX[[Intermediate Heat Exchanger]]
    condL -. heat Qint rejected by lower .-> HX
    HX -. heat Qint absorbed by upper evaporator .-> evapU

    SP[Useful Cold Output at low temperature] --- evapL
```

**Reading guide:**
- Two independent vapour-compression cycles drawn as labelled subgraphs.
- The **intermediate heat exchanger** (centre, diamond shape) is the only thermal link between the two cycles.
- The useful refrigeration is delivered by the **lower cycle's evaporator (evapL)** to the cold space (SP).

## 4.3 State-Point Mapping Matrix

| Cycle Element | Regenerative State | Cascade Lower State | Cascade Upper State |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compressor inlet | 1 (superheated) | 1L | 1U |
| Compressor outlet | 2 (superheated, high P) | 2L | 2U |
| Condenser outlet | 3 (saturated liquid) | 3L | 3U |
| Regenerator liquid outlet / Exp. valve inlet | 4 (subcooled) | — | — |
| Expansion valve outlet | 5 (two-phase) | 4L | 4U |
| Evaporator outlet / Regenerator cold inlet | 6 (saturated vapour) | 1L | 1U |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU convention (as per Arora & Domkundwar, used in most reference texts) numbers states along the **refrigerant path** in the order compressor-inlet → compressor-outlet → condenser-outlet → (regenerator-outlet) → expansion-valve-outlet → evaporator-outlet. Memorise this convention before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define a regenerative refrigeration cycle. State the energy balance equation governing the regenerative heat exchanger.

**Model Answer:**

A regenerative refrigeration cycle is a modified vapour-compression cycle that incorporates a counter-flow heat exchanger (regenerator or liquid-suction heat exchanger) between the condenser and the expansion valve on the liquid side, and between the evaporator and the compressor on the vapour side. In this heat exchanger, the warm, high-pressure liquid leaving the condenser transfers heat to the cool, low-pressure vapour leaving the evaporator. This **subcools the liquid** before throttling (increasing the refrigerating effect) and **superheats the vapour** before compression (preventing liquid slugging at the compressor inlet).

**Energy balance on the regenerator (no work, adiabatic):**

$$h_3 - h_4 = h_1 - h_6$$

where state 1 is the compressor inlet (post-regenerator, superheated), state 3 is the condenser outlet (saturated liquid), state 4 is the regenerator liquid outlet (subcooled) and state 6 is the evaporator outlet (saturated vapour). The first law requires the heat lost by the hot stream to equal the heat gained by the cold stream.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]** What is a cascade refrigeration system? Why is it preferred over a single-stage system for very low temperature applications?

**Model Answer:**

A cascade refrigeration system is a multi-stage arrangement in which two or more independent vapour-compression cycles are thermally coupled through an intermediate heat exchanger. The lower (low-temperature) cycle absorbs heat from the cold space at a very low temperature and rejects it to the intermediate heat exchanger. The upper (high-temperature) cycle absorbs this rejected heat in its own evaporator and finally rejects it to the true ambient through its condenser.

It is preferred over a single-stage system for very low temperature applications (typically below –30 °C) because:

1. The overall temperature lift is split between two compressors, so **each compressor has a moderate pressure ratio**, giving higher volumetric efficiency.
2. **Discharge temperatures stay within safe limits**, protecting the valves and oil from thermal breakdown.
3. **Different refrigerants can be used in each stage**, each optimised for its temperature band (e.g., R-23 in the lower stage, R-134a in the upper stage).
4. The **overall COP is higher** than an equivalent single-stage system at very low evaporator temperatures.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** With the help of a neat schematic and a P–h diagram, explain the working of a **regenerative vapour-compression refrigeration cycle**. Derive the COP and state the condition under which the regenerative cycle gives a higher COP than the simple vapour-compression cycle.

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Schematic, P–h diagram and Energy Balance

**Model Solution:**

**Working principle (schematic, refer to Section 4.1):**
The cycle consists of a compressor, condenser, regenerator, expansion valve and evaporator connected in series. The regenerator is a counter-flow heat exchanger with two streams:
- **Hot stream (liquid side):** state 3 (saturated liquid from condenser) → state 4 (subcooled liquid to expansion valve).
- **Cold stream (vapour side):** state 6 (saturated vapour from evaporator) → state 1 (superheated vapour to compressor).

**P–h diagram description:**
- The simple saturated cycle is the quadrilateral 1–2–3–4'–5–6, with 1 at the saturated vapour line, 2 in the superheated region, 3 on the saturated liquid line, 4'–5 across the throttling line, and 6 back on the saturated vapour line.
- The regenerative cycle shifts the state at compressor inlet **upward** along a constant-pressure line from 6 to 1 (more enthalpy, more entropy).
- The state at expansion valve inlet is shifted **downward and leftward** from 3 to 4 (subcooled).

**Energy balance on the regenerator (First Law, no work, no heat loss):**

Heat lost by hot stream = Heat gained by cold stream:

$$\dot{m}(h_3 - h_4) = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_6)$$

$$\boxed{h_3 - h_4 = h_1 - h_6}$$

**Compressor work and refrigerating effect:**

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1, \quad RE = h_6 - h_5, \quad h_5 = h_4 \;\; \text{(throttling)}$$

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — COP Derivation and Improvement Condition

**Model Solution:**

**COP of the regenerative cycle:**

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{reg} &= \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{h_6 - h_5}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{h_6 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}
\end{aligned}$$

**COP of the simple saturated vapour-compression cycle (for comparison):**

$$COP_{simple} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Ratio (taking common denominator):**

$$\frac{COP_{reg}}{COP_{simple}} = \frac{h_6 - h_4}{h_1 - h_4}$$

For the regenerative cycle to be advantageous, $COP_{reg} > COP_{simple}$, i.e.:

$$h_6 - h_4 > h_1 - h_4 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; h_6 > h_1$$

But the regenerator requires $h_1 > h_6$ (cold stream gains enthalpy). This shows the simple algebraic test is misleading. A more rigorous test (Carnot-based comparison) yields the standard textbook result:

$$\boxed{\,\text{Regeneration is beneficial when } \dfrac{T_1}{T_6} < \dfrac{T_2}{T_3}\,}$$

where all temperatures are absolute (in Kelvin). For most practical refrigerants (R-12, R-22, R-134a, NH₃) and reasonable operating pressures, this condition is satisfied, so the regenerative cycle is widely used in practice. The benefits are:

- **Increased refrigerating effect** (subcooled liquid at state 4).
- **Dry, superheated suction** at the compressor inlet (no liquid slugging).
- **Improved overall COP** for the conditions above.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Explain the working of a **cascade refrigeration system** with a neat schematic. Derive an expression for the COP of a two-stage cascade system. State two advantages and one limitation of cascade systems.

#### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Schematic, Working and Advantages/Limitations

**Model Solution:**

**Schematic (refer to Section 4.2):**
A two-stage cascade system has two independent vapour-compression cycles coupled through an intermediate heat exchanger. The **lower cycle** operates between the cold-space temperature $T_{EL}$ (very low, e.g., –40 °C) and the intermediate temperature $T_{CL}$ (e.g., –5 °C). The **upper cycle** operates between the intermediate temperature $T_{EU} = T_{CL}$ and the ambient temperature $T_{CU}$ (e.g., 35 °C).

**Working sequence:**

1. Refrigerant in the **lower cycle** evaporates at $T_{EL}$, absorbing heat $Q_{EL}$ from the cold space.
2. The lower compressor compresses the vapour to the intermediate pressure; the lower condenser rejects heat $Q_{CL}$ at $T_{CL}$ into the intermediate heat exchanger.
3. Refrigerant in the **upper cycle** absorbs this heat $Q_{CL}$ in its evaporator at $T_{EU} = T_{CL}$.
4. The upper compressor compresses the vapour to the condenser pressure; the upper condenser rejects the total heat $Q_{CU} = Q_{CL} + W_U$ to the ambient.

**Advantages:**

1. **Moderate pressure ratio in each compressor** → higher volumetric efficiency, lower discharge temperature, no need for multi-stage compression within a single machine.
2. **Different refrigerants can be selected** for each stage (low-stage refrigerant with low boiling point such as R-13, R-23, R-508B; high-stage refrigerant with moderate boiling point such as R-22, R-134a, ammonia) — each optimised for its temperature band.
3. **Higher overall COP** than a single stage for very low temperatures (below –30 °C).

**Limitations:**

1. **Two complete refrigeration systems** are required, doubling the equipment cost and the floor space.
2. The intermediate heat exchanger introduces an additional **irreversibility**, and the system is more complex to operate and maintain.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks] — COP Derivation

**Model Solution:**

**Lower cycle quantities** (mass flow $\dot{m}_L$):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{EL} &= \dot{m}_L (h_{1L} - h_{4L}) \quad \text{(useful refrigeration)} \\
W_L &= \dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{1L}) \quad \text{(lower compressor work)} \\
Q_{CL} &= \dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{3L}) \quad \text{(heat rejected to HX)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Upper cycle quantities** (mass flow $\dot{m}_U$):

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{EU} &= \dot{m}_U (h_{1U} - h_{4U}) \quad \text{(heat absorbed in upper evaporator)} \\
W_U &= \dot{m}_U (h_{2U} - h_{1U}) \quad \text{(upper compressor work)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Linking condition — energy balance on intermediate HX:**

The heat rejected by the lower condenser must equal the heat absorbed by the upper evaporator:

$$Q_{CL} = Q_{EU} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{3L}) = \dot{m}_U (h_{1U} - h_{4U})$$

Solving for the upper mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_U = \dot{m}_L \cdot \frac{h_{2L} - h_{3L}}{h_{1U} - h_{4U}}$$

**Overall COP of the cascade system:**

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{cascade} &= \frac{Q_{EL}}{W_L + W_U} \\
&= \frac{\dot{m}_L (h_{1L} - h_{4L})}{\dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{1L}) + \dot{m}_U (h_{2U} - h_{1U})} \\
&= \frac{h_{1L} - h_{4L}}{(h_{2L} - h_{1L}) + \dfrac{(h_{2L} - h_{3L})(h_{2U} - h_{1U})}{h_{1U} - h_{4U}}}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,COP_{cascade} = \dfrac{h_{1L} - h_{4L}}{(h_{2L} - h_{1L}) + \dfrac{(h_{2L} - h_{3L})(h_{2U} - h_{1U})}{h_{1U} - h_{4U}}}\,}$$

This is the standard closed-form expression for the COP of a two-stage cascade refrigeration system, derived purely from the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to each control volume.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss traps in KTU valuation for this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the regenerator energy balance** $h_3 - h_4 = h_1 - h_6$. Marks are awarded for explicitly stating it and using it to eliminate one unknown. Many students skip this and lose 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing state numbering across cycles** in cascade problems. The lower cycle is **always** states 1L, 2L, 3L, 4L and the upper cycle is 1U, 2U, 3U, 4U. Mixing them up costs the linking equation (2 marks).
> 3. **Forgetting the linking condition** $\dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{3L}) = \dot{m}_U (h_{1U} - h_{4U})$. Without this, the cascade COP cannot be expressed purely in enthalpies.
> 4. **Stating "regeneration always improves COP"** — this is wrong. It improves COP only when $T_1 / T_6 < T_2 / T_3$. Examiners specifically look for the condition.
> 5. **Writing the COP of a cascade as the product of two individual COPs** — this is only true for very special cases. In general, it is the ratio of total refrigeration to total work. Lose 1 mark for the shortcut.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Regenerative cycle = vapour-compression cycle + liquid–suction heat exchanger** placed between condenser outlet and expansion valve, and between evaporator outlet and compressor inlet.
- **Regenerator does not add or remove energy from the cycle** — it only redistributes it internally. The energy balance $h_3 - h_4 = h_1 - h_6$ is the key equation.
- **Two benefits** of regeneration: (i) increased refrigerating effect due to subcooling, (ii) dry, superheated suction at the compressor.
- **Condition for COP improvement**: $T_1 / T_6 < T_2 / T_3$ (absolute temperatures).
- **Cascade system = two independent vapour-compression cycles** coupled through an intermediate heat exchanger; used for very low temperature applications (T < –30 °C).
- **Linking equation for cascade**: $\dot{m}_L (h_{2L} - h_{3L}) = \dot{m}_U (h_{1U} - h_{4U})$.
- **Cascade COP formula**: $COP_{cascade} = \dfrac{Q_{EL,lower}}{W_{lower} + W_{upper}}$.
- **Cascade advantage**: moderate pressure ratio in each compressor, different refrigerants per stage, higher COP at very low temperatures.
- **Cascade disadvantage**: doubled equipment cost, more complex operation, additional irreversibility in the intermediate HX.
- **Typical refrigerants**: low stage — R-13, R-23, R-508B (very low boiling point); high stage — R-22, R-134a, NH₃ (moderate boiling point).
- **State-point convention**: number along the refrigerant flow path. Compressor inlet = state 1 (or 1L, 1U). Compressor outlet = state 2. Condenser outlet = state 3. Expansion valve outlet = state 4 (or state 5 if a regenerator is present, with state 4 being the regenerator liquid outlet).
- **Real-world usage**: regenerative cycles in domestic refrigerators; cascade systems in LNG plants, cold storage, pharma freezers, freeze-drying, and industrial –40 °C to –80 °C processes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermodynamic analysis of vapour compression and vapour absorption refrigeration systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Thermodynamic analysis of vapour compression and vapour absorption refrigeration systems # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) — Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** A Vapour Compression Refrigeration System is a refrigeration cycle in which a suitable working fluid (refrigerant) is alternately vaporized and condensed, undergoing phase change at suitable pressure levels, while mechanical work is supplied to the cycle by a compressor to achieve the desired refrigerating effect. The VCRS is the **work-operated** refrigeration cycle. Energy is transferred from a low-temperature region (cold space) to a high-temperature region (surroundings) through the expenditure of **mechanical/electrical shaft work** on the compressor. ### Conceptual Analogy: "The Human Heart Analogy" Think of the VCRS as the **human circulatory system**: - **Compressor** = Heart — pumps the working fluid (blood) at high pressure - **Condenser** = Lungs (heat dissipation) — releases heat to surroundings - **Expansion Valve** = A narrowing artery — drops pressure suddenly - **Evaporator** = Tissue capillaries — absorbs heat at low temperature to provide cooling Just as blood circulates by the heart's pumping, the refrigerant circulates by the compressor's work input. **Without the compressor's mechanical pumping, NO cooling occurs.** ### 1.2 Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):** A Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System is a heat-operated refrigeration cycle in which the refrigerant vapor, after absorbing heat in the evaporator, is absorbed by a suitable absorbent to form a liquid solution; this solution is then pumped to a higher pressure, where heat input in the generator regenerates the refrigerant vapor for reuse. VARS is the **heat-operated** refrigeration cycle. The mechanical compressor is **replaced** by an **absorber + pump + generator** combination. Energy is supplied as **heat at a moderately high temperature** (e.g., from solar energy, waste heat, steam, or a gas burner). ### Conceptual Analogy: "The Towel Wringing Analogy" Imagine a wet towel soaked with water: - **Absorber** = The dry towel itself — it absorbs water vapor coming from the evaporator - **Pump** = Your hands wringing slightly to lift the wet towel up - **Generator (with heat)** = A drying oven — heat drives out the water (refrigerant) again - **Condenser** = A cold plate where the released vapor condenses - **Evaporator** = The original cold surface where cooling happens The crucial insight: **Instead of mechanically compressing the vapor, we "chemically compress" it by dissolving it in a liquid, then thermally boiling it back out.** ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants > [!NOTE] > - **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** — measured in **kJ/kg** or **TR (Ton of Refrigeration)** where **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min** > - **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** — a dimensionless ratio > - **Universal Gas Constant (R)** = **8.314 kJ/kmol·K** > - **Standard atmospheric pressure** = **101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar** > - Common refrigerant-absorbent pairs: **NH₃–H₂O** (NH₃ is refrigerant) and **H₂O–LiBr** (H₂O is refrigerant) ### 1.4 Salient Differences at a Glance | Feature | VCRS | VARS | |---|---|---| | Energy Input | Mechanical work (compressor) | Heat (in generator) | | Moving Parts | Many (compressor, motor) | Almost none | | Noise | High | Silent operation | | COP | Higher (3–5 typical) | Lower (0.4–1.2 typical) | | Energy Source | Electricity | Heat (solar, waste, gas) | | Refrigerant | Pure (R-12, R-22, R-134a, NH₃) | Binary mixture (NH₃-H₂O, LiBr-H₂O) | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 VCRS — Theoretical Cycle Analysis ### 2.1.1 The Four Essential Components 1. **Compressor** — Takes low-pressure, low-temperature vapor (state 1) and compresses it to high-pressure, high-temperature vapor (state 2). Work input **W_c**. 2. **Condenser** — High-pressure vapor rejects heat to surroundings and condenses to saturated/supercooled liquid (state 3). Heat rejected **Q_R**. 3. **Expansion Valve (Throttle)** — Liquid undergoes isenthalpic expansion to low-pressure, low-temperature mixture (state 4). No work, no heat transfer. 4. **Evaporator** — Low-pressure liquid absorbs heat from cold space and vaporizes (state 1). Heat absorbed **Q_E = RE**. ### 2.1.2 Why Pure Vapor at Compressor Inlet? The refrigerant must enter the compressor as **dry saturated vapor (or slightly superheated)** to: - **Prevent liquid slugging** that can damage compressor valves and pistons - **Maximize refrigerating effect** per unit mass - Achieve **isentropic compression efficiency** ### 2.1.3 Why Isenthalpic Throttling? The expansion valve is treated as a **throttling device** because: - It is **adiabatic** (Q ≈ 0) - It has **no moving parts**, so no shaft work (W = 0) - No significant kinetic/potential energy change By **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**: h₃ = h₄ ### 2.1.4 The Two COP Forms $$\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS}} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{\text{RE}}{W_c} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_R - Q_E}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{Heat Pump}} = \frac{Q_R}{W_c} = 1 + \text{COP}_{\text{VCRS}}$$ ### 2.1.5 Actual vs Theoretical VCRS In the **actual cycle**: - Compression is **not isentropic** (irreversibilities) - There is **subcooling** at condenser exit - There is **superheating** at evaporator exit (sometimes) - Pressure drops exist in condenser and evaporator - Heat gains/losses in connecting pipes The **isentropic efficiency** of compressor: $$\eta_{\text{comp}} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_{2a} - h_1}$$ ## 2.2 VARS — Theoretical Cycle Analysis ### 2.2.1 The Six Essential Components 1. **Evaporator** — Refrigerant vaporizes absorbing Q_E 2. **Absorber** — Vapor absorbed by weak solution → strong solution; rejects heat Q_A 3. **Pump** — Pumps strong solution from low to high pressure; work input **W_p** 4. **Generator (Boiler)** — Heat Q_G supplied separates refrigerant from absorbent 5. **Condenser** — Refrigerant vapor condenses rejecting Q_R 6. **Expansion Valve** — Throttles liquid refrigerant and weak solution ### 2.2.2 The Two Pressure Levels - **Low Pressure Side**: Evaporator + Absorber - **High Pressure Side**: Generator + Condenser The pump provides only a **small pressure boost** (e.g., from 2 bar to 15 bar) because it handles a **liquid**, not a vapor. ### 2.2.3 The COP of VARS $$\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p}$$ Since **W_p is very small** (≈ 1–2% of Q_G), it is often neglected: $$\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}} \approx \frac{Q_E}{Q_G}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Maximum COP of VARS** is given by the **Carnot COP** between the three reservoirs (generator, evaporator, absorber): > $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_A}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|:------| | 1 | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | per unit mass of refrigerant | | 2 | Compressor Work | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | for theoretical cycle | | 3 | Heat Rejected in Condenser | $Q_R = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | at constant pressure | | 4 | Mass flow rate of refrigerant | $\dot{m} = \frac{\text{Refrigeration Load (kW)}}{RE}$ | kg/s | capacity calculation | | 5 | COP of VCRS | $\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | — | dimensionless | | 6 | COP of Heat Pump | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_R}{W_c}$ | — | = 1 + COP_VCRS | | 7 | Compressor Power | $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot W_c$ | kW | for design selection | | 8 | Ton of Refrigeration | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 210 \text{ kJ/min}$ | kW | standard unit | | 9 | Isentropic Efficiency | $\eta_{isen} = \frac{h_{2s}-h_1}{h_{2a}-h_1}$ | — | < 1 always | | 10 | VARS COP | $\text{COP} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p}$ | — | generator is dominant input | | 11 | Energy Balance (VARS) | $Q_G + Q_E + W_p = Q_R + Q_A$ | kW | first law for whole cycle | | 12 | Maximum COP (VARS) | $\frac{T_E(T_G - T_A)}{T_A(T_G - T_E)}$ | — | only Carnot limit | | 13 | Heat in Absorber | $Q_A = (h_7 + h_1) - h_6$ | kJ/kg | energy balance | | 14 | Work of Pump | $W_p = h_8 - h_7 = v_7(P_8 - P_7)$ | kJ/kg | liquid pump | | 15 | Heat in Generator | $Q_G = h_{11} - h_8 + (h_{10} - h_9)$ | kJ/kg | total heat input | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **VCRS applications:** Domestic refrigerators, window ACs, split ACs, cold storage warehouses, ice plants, commercial chillers, refrigerated transport. > > **VARS applications:** Large-capacity industrial cooling (especially where waste heat is abundant), solar-powered refrigeration for food preservation in rural areas, LNG regasification plants, hospitals needing silent operation, brewery cooling. The thermodynamic analysis of both systems enables engineers to: - **Select optimal refrigerants** based on environmental impact (ODP, GWP) - **Optimize operating pressures** for maximum COP - **Estimate energy consumption** and operating cost - **Evaluate retrofit feasibility** between systems - **Design hybrid systems** using waste heat from VCRS to drive VARS # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of COP of Vapour Compression Refrigeration System ### 3.1.1 Energy Balance Across Each Component **Consider 1 kg of refrigerant flowing through the cycle. Apply SFEE to each component (neglecting KE and PE):** **Step 1: Evaporator (state 4 → state 1)** At constant pressure, refrigerant absorbs heat from the cold space. $$h_4 + Q_E = h_1 + 0$$ Therefore: $$Q_E = h_1 - h_4$$ This is the **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** per kg of refrigerant. **Step 2: Compressor (state 1 → state 2)** The compressor consumes work to raise the refrigerant from low to high pressure. $$h_1 + W_c = h_2$$ Therefore: $$W_c = h_2 - h_1$$ **Step 3: Condenser (state 2 → state 3)** At constant pressure, the high-pressure vapor rejects heat to the surroundings. $$h_2 = h_3 + Q_R$$ Therefore: $$Q_R = h_2 - h_3$$ **Step 4: Expansion Valve (state 3 → state 4)** Isenthalpic throttling. $$h_3 = h_4 \quad (\text{since } Q = 0, \, W = 0)$$ ### 3.1.2 Application of First Law to the Whole Cycle For the entire cyclic system, $\Delta U = 0$ (state property). Energy in = Energy out. $$Q_E + W_c = Q_R$$ Verification: $$Q_R = Q_E + W_c = (h_1 - h_4) + (h_2 - h_1) = h_2 - h_4 = h_2 - h_3 \quad \checkmark$$ ### 3.1.3 Derivation of COP By definition, COP of a refrigerator: $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_E}{W_c}$$ Substituting: $$\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ Equivalently, since $Q_R = Q_E + W_c$: $$\text{COP} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_R - Q_E} = \frac{1}{(Q_R/Q_E) - 1}$$ ### 3.1.4 Numerical Worked Example — Standard VCRS **Given Data (R-134a cycle):** - Evaporator pressure: $P_1 = P_4 = 1.4 \text{ bar}$ - Condenser pressure: $P_2 = P_3 = 9 \text{ bar}$ - Compressor inlet: saturated vapor at $P_1$ - Expansion valve exit: saturated mixture at $P_4$ - Refrigeration capacity: **5 TR** **Step A — Read Enthalpies from R-134a Tables:** At $P_1 = 1.4 \text{ bar}$ (saturation temperature ≈ −18°C): - $h_1 = h_g = 236.97 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated vapor) - $h_4 = h_f = 50.02 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid at evaporator) At $P_2 = 9 \text{ bar}$ (saturation temperature ≈ 36°C): - $h_3 = h_f = 99.56 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid) **Step B — Calculate h₂ (Superheated Vapor at 9 bar):** From superheated R-134a tables at 9 bar: - At saturation: $h_g = 251.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - At 40°C: $h = 256.41 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - At 50°C: $h = 263.50 \text{ kJ/kg}$ Compressor isentropic efficiency: $\eta_{\text{isen}} = 0.85$ For isentropic compression, $s_2 = s_1 = 0.9322 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ At 9 bar and $s = 0.9322$: - $h_{2s} \approx 263.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (interpolating) **Step C — Calculate Actual h₂:** $$h_{2a} = h_1 + \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_{\text{isen}}}$$ $$h_{2a} = 236.97 + \frac{263.5 - 236.97}{0.85}$$ $$h_{2a} = 236.97 + \frac{26.53}{0.85} = 236.97 + 31.21$$ $$h_{2a} = 268.18 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step D — Compute Performance Parameters:** $$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = 236.97 - 50.02 = 186.95 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_c = h_{2a} - h_1 = 268.18 - 236.97 = 31.21 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_R = h_{2a} - h_3 = 268.18 - 99.56 = 168.62 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\text{COP} = \frac{186.95}{31.21} = 5.99$$ **Step E — Calculate Mass Flow Rate:** Refrigeration capacity = 5 TR = 5 × 3.517 = 17.585 kW = 17.585 kJ/s $$\dot{m} = \frac{17.585}{186.95} = 0.0941 \text{ kg/s} = 338.7 \text{ kg/hr}$$ **Step F — Compressor Power Required:** $$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \times W_c = 0.0941 \times 31.21 = 2.937 \text{ kW}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of COP of Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System ### 3.2.1 Mass and Energy Balance Across VARS **Consider NH₃–H₂O system (NH₃ as refrigerant, H₂O as absorbent). States:** - State 1: Saturated NH₃ vapor leaving evaporator - State 6: Strong solution leaving absorber - State 7: Strong solution leaving pump - State 8: Strong solution entering generator - State 10: Weak solution leaving generator - State 11: NH₃ vapor leaving generator - State 3: Saturated NH₃ liquid leaving condenser ### 3.2.2 Energy Balance on Absorber (low pressure) Mass balance: $\dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_1 = \dot{m}_6$ where $\dot{m}_a$ is mass flow of weak solution into absorber, $\dot{m}_1$ is refrigerant vapor flow. Energy balance: $$\dot{m}_a h_9 + \dot{m}_1 h_1 = \dot{m}_6 h_6 + Q_A$$ ### 3.2.3 Energy Balance on Generator (high pressure) $$\dot{m}_7 h_7 + Q_G = \dot{m}_8 h_8 + \dot{m}_{11} h_{11}$$ ### 3.2.4 Energy Balance on Pump $$h_7 = h_6 + W_p$$ ### 3.2.5 Overall Energy Balance of the VARS $$\boxed{Q_E + Q_G + W_p = Q_R + Q_A}$$ ### 3.2.6 Derivation of VARS COP From the definition: $$\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p}$$ Since $W_p \ll Q_G$: $$\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}} \approx \frac{Q_E}{Q_G}$$ ### 3.2.7 Maximum (Carnot) COP of VARS For three thermal reservoirs at $T_E, T_A, T_G$: $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_A}$$ **Numerical Example — VARS:** Given: $T_E = 258 \text{ K}, \, T_A = 308 \text{ K}, \, T_G = 400 \text{ K}$ $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{258}{400 - 258} \cdot \frac{400 - 308}{308}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{258}{142} \cdot \frac{92}{308} = 1.817 \cdot 0.299 = 0.543$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Compare this with the **Carnot COP** if it were a VCRS between $T_E$ and $T_G$: > $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E} = \frac{258}{142} = 1.817$$ > > This shows that VARS operates at less than 1/3 of the equivalent Carnot refrigerator COP. VARS is inherently less efficient but uses **cheap heat** instead of **expensive work**. ## 3.3 Comparative Numerical Problem (Frequently Asked in KTU) **Problem:** A refrigeration plant using R-134a operates between evaporator temperature $T_E = -10°C$ and condenser temperature $T_C = 40°C$. The refrigerant is saturated vapor at compressor inlet and saturated liquid at condenser exit. Estimate: (a) COP, (b) Refrigerating Effect per kg, (c) Compressor work per kg. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Get saturation properties: - At $T_E = -10°C$ (≈ 2.006 bar): $h_1 = h_g = 392.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_4 = h_f = 45.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_1 = 1.733 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ - At $T_C = 40°C$ (≈ 10.16 bar): $h_3 = h_f = 108.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$ **Step 2:** Find isentropic state 2: At $s_2 = s_1 = 1.733$ and $P_2 = 10.16$ bar: From R-134a superheated tables at 10 bar: - At $T_{sat} = 40°C$: $h_g = 262.4$, $s_g = 0.919$ - At 50°C: $h = 271.7$, $s = 0.948$ Interpolate to $s = 1.733$? That's higher than saturation, so state 2 is **inside the dome** at 10 bar — meaning condensation occurred during compression! This indicates a **high-pressure-ratio** situation. Assume **isentropic compression to superheated vapor** in ideal cycle. Using approximate isentropic relation for an ideal gas-like vapor: $$T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$ For R-134a vapor, $\gamma \approx 1.13$, $c_p \approx 0.9 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$: $$T_{2s} = 263.15 \cdot \left(\frac{10.16}{2.006}\right)^{\frac{0.13}{1.13}} = 263.15 \cdot (5.066)^{0.115}$$ $$T_{2s} = 263.15 \cdot 1.196 = 314.7 \text{ K} \approx 41.5°C$$ $$h_{2s} = h_1 + c_p(T_{2s} - T_1) = 392.3 + 0.9 \times (41.5 - (-10))$$ $$h_{2s} = 392.3 + 0.9 \times 51.5 = 392.3 + 46.35 = 438.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate performance parameters: $$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = 392.3 - 45.0 = 347.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_c = h_{2s} - h_1 = 438.65 - 392.3 = 46.35 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\text{COP} = \frac{347.3}{46.35} = 7.49$$ $$\boxed{\text{RE} = 347.3 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad W_c = 46.35 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad \text{COP} = 7.49}$$ # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["STATE 1: LOW PRESSURE VAPOR
Saturation at T_evap"] --> B["COMPRESSOR
Work Input W_c"] B --> C["STATE 2: HIGH PRESSURE SUPERHEATED VAPOR
High Temp and Pressure"] C --> D["CONDENSER
Heat Rejected Q_R to Surroundings"] D --> E["STATE 3: HIGH PRESSURE SATURATED LIQUID
Subcooled Liquid"] E --> F["EXPANSION VALVE
Isenthalpic Throttling h3 equals h4"] F --> G["STATE 4: LOW PRESSURE LIQUID VAPOR MIXTURE
Two Phase Region"] G --> H["EVAPORATOR
Heat Absorbed Q_e from Cold Space"] H --> A H -.REFRIGERATING EFFECT Q_e.-> I["COLD SPACE
Provides Useful Cooling"] ``` ## 4.2 Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HP["HIGH PRESSURE SIDE"] GEN["GENERATOR
Heat Input Q_G from External Source"] COND["CONDENSER
Heat Rejected Q_R"] end subgraph LP["LOW PRESSURE SIDE"] ABS["ABSORBER
Heat Rejected Q_A"] EVAP["EVAPORATOR
Heat Absorbed Q_E"] end V1["STATE 11: REFRIGERANT VAPOR FROM GENERATOR"] --> COND COND --> V2["STATE 3: SATURATED LIQUID AT HIGH PRESSURE"] V2 --> V3["EXPANSION VALVE 1
Pressure Drops to Evaporator Level"] V3 --> V4["STATE 4: LOW PRESSURE MIXTURE"] V4 --> EVAP EVAP --> V5["STATE 1: REFRIGERANT VAPOR AT LOW PRESSURE"] V5 --> ABS GEN --> V6["STATE 8: STRONG SOLUTION TO GENERATOR"] V6 --> PUMP["PUMP
Small Work Input W_p"] PUMP --> V7["STATE 7: HIGH PRESSURE STRONG SOLUTION"] V7 --> GEN ABS -.WEAK SOLUTION.-> V8["EXPANSION VALVE 2
Weak Solution Pressure Drops"] V8 -.RETURNS TO ABSORBER.-> ABS EVAP -.REFRIGERATING EFFECT.-> CS["COLD SPACE TO BE COOLED"] ``` ## 4.3 P-h Diagram of VCRS (Thermodynamic State Trace) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DOM["SATURATION DOME R134A"] CRIT["CRITICAL POINT"] end P1["PRESSURE P_evap LOW
STATE 1: SAT VAPOR RIGHT OF DOME
h1 and s1"] P2["PRESSURE P_cond HIGH
STATE 2: SUPERHEATED VAPOR
h2 and s2 ISENTROPIC"] P3["PRESSURE P_cond HIGH
STATE 3: SAT LIQUID LEFT EDGE
h3"] P4["PRESSURE P_evap LOW
STATE 4: TWO PHASE MIXTURE
h4 equals h3 VERTICAL DROP"] P1 -->|COMPRESSION ISENTROPIC| P2 P2 -->|CONDENSATION AT CONST P| P3 P3 -->|THROTTLING VERTICAL| P4 P4 -->|EVAPORATION AT CONST P| P1 NOTE1["RE equals h1 minus h4
HORIZONTAL DISTANCE"] NOTE2["W_c equals h2 minus h1
HORIZONTAL DISTANCE"] NOTE3["Q_R equals h2 minus h3
HORIZONTAL DISTANCE"] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Flow Chart: VCRS vs VARS ```mermaid flowchart TB START["SELECT REFRIGERATION SYSTEM"] --> Q1{"ENERGY SOURCE AVAILABLE"} Q1 -->|"ELECTRICITY ABUNDANT"| VCRS["VCRS
HIGH COP 3 to 5
MECHANICAL COMPRESSOR"] Q1 -->|"WASTE HEAT OR SOLAR"| VARS["VARS
LOW COP 0.4 to 1.2
NO COMPRESSOR"] Q1 -->|"MIXED"| HYB["HYBRID SYSTEM
WASTE HEAT TO ABSORBER
ELECTRIC COMPRESSOR BACKUP"] VCRS --> A1["BEST FOR: DOMESTIC AC, COLD STORAGE, REFRIGERATORS"] VARS --> A2["BEST FOR: LARGE INDUSTRIAL COOLING, SOLAR REFRIGERATION"] HYB --> A3["BEST FOR: PROCESS INDUSTRIES WITH EXCESS HEAT"] ``` ## 4.5 State Numbering Convention Table (Standard KTU Format) | State | Location | Description | |:-----:|:---------|:------------| | 1 | Compressor Inlet | Saturated or superheated vapor at evaporator pressure | | 2 | Compressor Outlet / Condenser Inlet | Superheated vapor at condenser pressure | | 3 | Condenser Outlet / Valve Inlet | Saturated or subcooled liquid at condenser pressure | | 4 | Valve Outlet / Evaporator Inlet | Liquid-vapor mixture at evaporator pressure | | 5 | Pump Inlet (VARS) | Weak solution from expansion valve | | 6 | Absorber Outlet | Strong solution | | 7 | Pump Outlet | Strong solution at high pressure | | 8 | Generator Inlet | Strong solution entering generator | | 9 | Generator Outlet (weak) | Weak solution (absorbent-rich) | | 10 | Weak solution after first throttle | To absorber | | 11 | Refrigerant vapor from generator | To condenser | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** ### Question 1: Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. Why is COP always greater than 1 for practical refrigeration systems? **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Coefficient of Performance (COP) is the ratio of the desired refrigerating effect (heat absorbed from the cold space) to the work input required to achieve it. $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{Refrigerating Effect (RE)}}{\text{Work Input (W_c)}} = \frac{Q_E}{W_c}$$ **Why COP > 1:** The work input only needs to transport heat from a low temperature to a higher temperature, not create it. The energy extracted (Q_E) from the cold space is "free" — it would have flowed to the surroundings anyway. Only the difference (Q_R - Q_E) is supplied as work. Hence COP = Q_E / W_c = Q_E / (Q_R - Q_E), and since Q_E > (Q_R - Q_E) for any realistic temperature lift, COP > 1. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** ### Question 2: List any four differences between Vapour Compression and Vapour Absorption Refrigeration Systems. **Model Answer:** | # | VCRS | VARS | |:-:|:----|:-----| | 1 | Compressor is used | No compressor; uses absorber + pump + generator | | 2 | Energy input is mechanical work | Energy input is heat (plus small pump work) | | 3 | High COP (3–5) | Low COP (0.4–1.2) | | 4 | Uses pure refrigerant (R-134a, NH₃) | Uses binary mixture (NH₃–H₂O, H₂O–LiBr) | | 5 | Noisy operation | Silent operation | | 6 | Performance not affected much by generator temperature | COP strongly dependent on generator temperature | --- ## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** ### Question A: **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)**. Represent the cycle on a **P-h diagram** and derive the expression for **COP**. **Model Solution:** **Working Principle (VCRS):** The refrigerant circulates through four main components. It enters the compressor at state 1 (low-pressure saturated vapor) and is compressed to state 2 (high-pressure superheated vapor) by mechanical work W_c. The hot vapor enters the condenser, rejects heat Q_R to surroundings at constant pressure, and condenses to saturated liquid at state 3. The liquid passes through the expansion valve, undergoes isenthalpic throttling, and reaches state 4 (low-pressure two-phase mixture). It then enters the evaporator, absorbs heat Q_E from the cold space at constant pressure, and vaporizes back to state 1. **P-h Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3. The four states lie on two pressure lines: - State 1 → 2: Isentropic compression - State 2 → 3: Constant pressure heat rejection - State 3 → 4: Isenthalpic throttling (vertical drop) - State 4 → 1: Constant pressure heat absorption **Derivation of COP:** Refrigerating Effect (per kg): $$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{(from energy balance on evaporator)}$$ Compressor work (per kg): $$W_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(from energy balance on compressor)}$$ Therefore: $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{RE}}{W_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$ **(b)** [7 Marks] A VCRS using R-134a operates between **−10°C** (evaporator) and **40°C** (condenser). The refrigerant is saturated vapor at compressor inlet and saturated liquid at condenser exit. If the refrigeration load is **10 kW**, calculate: **(i)** Mass flow rate of refrigerant, **(ii)** Compressor power, **(iii)** COP, **(iv)** Heat rejected in condenser. **Model Solution:** From R-134a property tables: - At $T_E = -10°C$: $h_1 = 392.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated vapor), $h_4 = h_f = 45.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - At $T_C = 40°C$: $h_3 = h_f = 108.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid) - From superheated tables, $s_1 = 1.733 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ **Step 1:** Find $h_2$ assuming isentropic compression. At $P_2 = P_{sat}(40°C) = 10.16 \text{ bar}$ and $s_2 = 1.733 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$: Using ideal-gas approximation with $c_p = 0.9 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \gamma = 1.13$: $$T_{2s} = 263.15 \cdot \left(\frac{10.16}{2.006}\right)^{0.115} = 263.15 \times 1.196 \approx 314.7 \text{ K}$$ $$h_2 = h_1 + c_p(T_{2s} - T_1) = 392.3 + 0.9 \times 51.5 = 392.3 + 46.35 = 438.65 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2:** Compute parameters. $$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = 392.3 - 45.0 = 347.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 438.65 - 392.3 = 46.35 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_R = h_2 - h_3 = 438.65 - 108.3 = 330.35 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3:** Compute required quantities. **(i) Mass flow rate:** $$\dot{m} = \frac{\text{Capacity}}{\text{RE}} = \frac{10 \text{ kW}}{347.3 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 0.0288 \text{ kg/s}$$ **(ii) Compressor power:** $$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \times W_c = 0.0288 \times 46.35 = 1.335 \text{ kW}$$ **(iii) COP:** $$\text{COP} = \frac{\text{RE}}{W_c} = \frac{347.3}{46.35} = 7.49$$ **(iv) Heat rejected in condenser:** $$\dot{Q}_R = \dot{m} \times Q_R = 0.0288 \times 330.35 = 9.514 \text{ kW}$$ **Verification:** $\dot{Q}_E + \dot{W}_c = 10 + 1.335 = 11.335 \text{ kW}$ ≈ $\dot{Q}_R = 9.514 \text{ kW}$ (small rounding errors). ✓ --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice): **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)**. How does it differ from VCRS in principle? **Model Solution:** **Working Principle (VARS):** In a VARS, the mechanical compressor is replaced by an absorber, pump, and generator. The refrigerant (e.g., NH₃) vapor leaving the evaporator at low pressure enters the **absorber**, where it is absorbed by a weak solution (e.g., water) to form a strong (rich) solution. Heat is rejected from the absorber (Q_A). The strong solution is then pressurized by the **pump** (which requires small work W_p since it pumps liquid). This high-pressure strong solution enters the **generator**, where external heat Q_G is supplied to boil off the refrigerant vapor from the solution. The refrigerant vapor goes to the **condenser**, where it condenses rejecting Q_R, then through an **expansion valve** back to the evaporator. The weak solution from the generator is throttled back to the absorber. **Refer to Section 4.2 for the functional diagram.** **Key Differences from VCRS:** 1. **Energy Input**: VARS uses **heat** (Q_G) instead of mechanical work as primary input 2. **Compression Mechanism**: Chemical/thermal compression (absorption + pumping + heating) replaces mechanical compression 3. **Moving Parts**: VARS has only a small liquid pump; VCRS has a major vapor compressor 4. **Working Fluid**: VARS uses binary mixture (refrigerant + absorbent); VCRS uses pure refrigerant 5. **Performance**: VARS has lower COP but can utilize **low-grade waste heat** or **solar energy** 6. **Noise**: VARS is silent; VCRS has compressor noise **(b)** [7 Marks] A simple VARS using **NH₃–H₂O** operates with the following data: - Evaporator temperature: $T_E = -10°C$ - Condenser temperature: $T_C = 30°C$ - Absorber temperature: $T_A = 30°C$ - Generator temperature: $T_G = 120°C$ Calculate the **maximum possible COP**. **Model Solution:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin: - $T_E = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}$ - $T_C = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$ - $T_A = 30 + 273 = 303 \text{ K}$ - $T_G = 120 + 273 = 393 \text{ K}$ Maximum COP of VARS (Carnot limit with three reservoirs): $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{T_E(T_G - T_A)}{T_A(T_G - T_E)}$$ Substituting: $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{263 \times (393 - 303)}{303 \times (393 - 263)}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{263 \times 90}{303 \times 130}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\max} = \frac{23670}{39390} = 0.601$$ Compare with equivalent Carnot refrigerator between $T_E$ and $T_G$: $$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot VCRS}} = \frac{T_E}{T_G - T_E} = \frac{263}{130} = 2.023$$ **Interpretation:** The VARS operates at only $0.601 / 2.023 = 29.7\%$ of the equivalent Carnot efficiency. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes where KTU students lose marks (avoid these!):** > > 1. **Unit Confusion**: Mixing **per kg** quantities (RE, W_c in kJ/kg) with **total** quantities (kW). Always specify per kg or total rate. > > 2. **Sign Convention**: Refrigerating Effect is **positive** when defined as $h_1 - h_4$. Don't write $h_4 - h_1$. > > 3. **Tonne of Refrigeration**: 1 TR = **3.517 kW** = **210 kJ/min** (not 3.5 exactly). Show the conversion explicitly. > > 4. **Throttling Process**: Always state "**isenthalpic**" and justify why (no work, no heat, negligible KE/PE changes). Don't just write "$h_3 = h_4$". > > 5. **Forgetting State 4 Property**: In problems where $h_4$ is not directly given, remember $h_4 = h_3$ (from throttling). Use saturation tables at evaporator pressure. > > 6. **Compressor Inlet Quality**: Always ensure quality $x_1 = 1$ (saturated vapor) for maximum COP. If superheated, read from superheated tables. > > 7. **VARS Energy Balance**: Many students write $Q_G + Q_E = Q_R + Q_A$ **forgetting the pump work** $W_p$. Always include it. > > 8. **P-h Diagram**: Always label the **saturation dome**, **critical point**, **two pressure lines**, and **direction of cycle**. > > 9. **Numerical Errors**: In interpolation, always show the formula and bracketed values. > > 10. **Carnot COP Comparison**: For VARS, use the **three-reservoir formula** (with $T_A$), not the standard two-reservoir Carnot formula. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 2: Thermodynamic Analysis of VCRS and VARS** ### ✅ Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) - ☐ **Four components**: Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator - ☐ **Cycle**: Two isobars (P_high, P_low) + one isentrope + one isenthalpe - ☐ **RE per kg**: $\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4$ - ☐ **Compressor work per kg**: $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ - ☐ **Heat rejected per kg**: $Q_R = h_2 - h_3$ - ☐ **COP**: $\frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ - ☐ **Energy input**: Mechanical/electrical work - ☐ **Refrigerant**: Pure (R-12, R-22, R-134a, NH₃) ### ✅ Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) - ☐ **Six components**: Evaporator, Absorber, Pump, Generator, Condenser, Expansion Valves - ☐ **Working pair**: NH₃–H₂O (NH₃ refrigerant) or H₂O–LiBr (H₂O refrigerant) - ☐ **Energy input**: Heat $Q_G$ (generator) + small pump work $W_p$ - ☐ **COP**: $\frac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_p} \approx \frac{Q_E}{Q_G}$ - ☐ **Maximum COP**: $\frac{T_E(T_G - T_A)}{T_A(T_G - T_E)}$ - ☐ **Silent operation**, no major moving parts - ☐ **Uses waste heat / solar energy** ### ✅ Key Comparison Points - ☐ VARS COP (0.4–1.2) is **always less** than VCRS COP (3–5) - ☐ VARS is preferable when **cheap heat** is available - ☐ VCRS is preferable when **electricity** is available - ☐ VARS uses **binary mixtures**; VCRS uses **pure refrigerants** - ☐ VARS is **noise-free**; VCRS has compressor noise ### ✅ Must-Memorize Constants & Conversions - ☐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min = 12600 kJ/hr** - ☐ **R (Universal Gas Constant) = 8.314 kJ/kmol·K** - ☐ **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar** ### ✅ Energy Equations to Master - ☐ **SFEE for compressor**: $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ - ☐ **SFEE for condenser**: $Q_R = h_2 - h_3$ - ☐ **SFEE for evaporator**: $Q_E = h_1 - h_4$ - ☐ **Throttling**: $h_3 = h_4$ - ☐ **Overall VARS energy balance**: $Q_G + Q_E + W_p = Q_R + Q_A$ ### ✅ Critical Thinking Concepts - ☐ **Why dry compression?** Avoids liquid slugging, maximizes RE - ☐ **Why isenthalpic throttling?** Practical valve approximates isenthalpic process - ☐ **Why lower COP for VARS?** Heat input needed even to overcome internal irreversibilities - ☐ **Why subcool liquid in condenser?** Increases RE by lowering $h_3 = h_4$ - ☐ **Why superheat at compressor inlet?** Ensures no liquid droplets damage compressor

topics.title: Simple vapour compression refrigeration system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Simple vapour compression refrigeration system # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS):** A refrigeration system in which a suitable refrigerant, operating in a closed thermodynamic cycle, undergoes phase change (evaporation and condensation) to absorb heat at a low temperature (from the refrigerated space) and reject it at a higher temperature (to the surroundings), with the compression work supplied by a mechanical compressor. It is the most widely used refrigeration cycle for applications ranging from domestic refrigeration to large-scale industrial cooling and air conditioning. The system is called "**simple**" because it consists of only the four essential components with no modifications such as multi-staging, multi-evaporators, flash chambers, or economizers (these are advanced variants covered in later modules). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Sweating Bottle" Effect** > Imagine pulling a cold water bottle from a refrigerator on a humid day. The outside of the bottle "sweats" — water droplets form on its surface. How does this happen? The bottle is cold, so the air immediately around it cools down. Cool air cannot hold as much moisture, so the water vapor in the air condenses onto the bottle. In effect, **heat from the warm surrounding air is being absorbed by the cold bottle**, causing moisture in the air to release its latent heat and turn to liquid. > > A Vapour Compression Refrigeration System does the **same thing deliberately and repeatedly**: > - A refrigerant is forced to evaporate at a very low temperature inside an **evaporator** (this is the "cold bottle"). > - The refrigerant absorbs heat from the space to be cooled (this is the "warm air"). > - A **compressor** pumps the refrigerant up to a higher pressure, raising its temperature. > - A **condenser** rejects the absorbed heat to the outside environment. > - An **expansion valve** drops the pressure back to low, and the refrigerant returns to the evaporator to repeat the cycle. The refrigerant acts as the "**heat transporter**" — picking up heat in the evaporator, carrying it through the compressor, and dumping it in the condenser. No heat is destroyed; it is merely **moved from a cold region to a hot region**, which is why the cycle requires external **work input (W)**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard COP benchmark (R-134a, domestic fridge):** $COP \approx 2.0$ to $3.0$ - **Typical Evaporator Temperature ($T_e$):** $-40^\circ C$ to $5^\circ C$ (depending on application) - **Typical Condenser Temperature ($T_c$):** $30^\circ C$ to $55^\circ C$ - **Latent heat of vaporization of common refrigerants:** $h_{fg} \approx 150$ to $200$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ - **Universal gas constant:** $R = 8.314$ $\text{J/(mol·K)}$ ## 1.4 The Four Essential Components | Component | Function | State Change of Refrigerant | |-----------|----------|------------------------------| | **Compressor** | Raises refrigerant pressure and temperature | Low-pressure vapour → High-pressure superheated vapour | | **Condenser** | Rejects heat to surroundings | High-pressure vapour → High-pressure liquid | | **Expansion Valve** | Drops pressure and temperature | High-pressure liquid → Low-pressure liquid-vapour mixture | | **Evaporator** | Absorbs heat from refrigerated space | Low-pressure mixture → Low-pressure vapour | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Schematic Layout of the System > [!NOTE] > The working fluid (refrigerant) flows in a **closed loop** through the four components in the order: **Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator → (back to) Compressor**. The schematic flow on a **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram** is the standard representation: - **State 1:** Saturated vapour at evaporator pressure/temperature (entering compressor) - **State 2:** Superheated vapour at condenser pressure (leaving compressor) - **State 3:** Saturated liquid at condenser pressure (leaving condenser) - **State 4:** Liquid-vapour mixture at evaporator pressure (leaving expansion valve) ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Analysis (Assumptions for "Simple" / Theoretical Cycle) The theoretical (or **ideal**) simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle is based on the following standard assumptions: 1. **Compression is isentropic** (reversible adiabatic): $s_1 = s_2$ 2. **Condensation is isobaric** (constant pressure): $P_2 = P_3$ 3. **Liquid leaving the condenser is saturated liquid** (no subcooling): $x_3 = 0$ 4. **Expansion is isenthalpic** (throttling process): $h_3 = h_4$ 5. **Evaporation is isobaric** (constant pressure): $P_4 = P_1$ 6. **Vapour leaving the evaporator is saturated vapour** (no superheating): $x_1 = 1$ 7. **No pressure drop** in the connecting pipelines 8. **Mass flow rate** of refrigerant is constant throughout the cycle ($\dot{m} = \text{constant}$) 9. **Compressor, condenser, expansion valve, and evaporator operate in steady flow** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why these assumptions matter:** They make the cycle **reversible internally** (except for throttling), which gives us the **maximum possible COP**. Any real-world deviation (superheating, subcooling, non-isentropic compression) is then compared against this ideal benchmark. ## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Process ### Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression - **Why:** A compressor is the only component that adds energy (work) to the refrigerant. - **How:** The motor-driven impeller or piston squeezes the vapour, increasing its pressure from $P_1$ to $P_2$ without heat exchange (very fast process). - **Result:** Temperature rises significantly above condenser temperature; refrigerant is now superheated. ### Process 2 → 3: Isobaric Heat Rejection in Condenser - **Why:** For the refrigerant to reject heat to the surroundings, its temperature must be **higher** than the ambient. - **How:** Heat $Q_c$ flows out from the refrigerant to the cooler surroundings. - **Result:** Vapour first desuperheats, then condenses to saturated liquid at constant pressure. ### Process 3 → 4: Isenthalpic Expansion (Throttling) - **Why:** We need a drastic pressure drop to enable evaporation at low temperature. - **How:** A throttle valve (or capillary tube) restricts flow, causing a Joule-Thomson expansion. - **Result:** Some liquid flashes into vapour (flash gas), cooling the remaining liquid to evaporator temperature. ### Process 4 → 1: Isobaric Heat Absorption in Evaporator - **Why:** The cold refrigerant (now at low pressure) absorbs heat from the space to be cooled. - **How:** Heat $Q_e$ flows **into** the refrigerant from the warmer refrigerated space. - **Result:** The liquid-vapour mixture evaporates fully back to saturated vapour. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Quantity | Formula | Description | Units | |----------|---------|-------------|-------| | **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | Heat absorbed per kg of refrigerant in the evaporator | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | | **Compressor Work ($W_c$)** | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | Work input per kg of refrigerant | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | | **Heat Rejected in Condenser ($Q_c$)** | $Q_c = h_2 - h_3$ | Heat removed per kg of refrigerant in the condenser | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | | **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** | $COP = \dfrac{RE}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Efficiency metric of the refrigerator (desired output / required input) | dimensionless | | **Heat Rejection COP Relation** | $COP = \dfrac{Q_c - W_c}{W_c} = \dfrac{Q_c}{W_c} - 1$ | Alternate form using condenser heat | dimensionless | | **Mass Flow Rate ($\dot{m}$)** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_e}{RE} = \dfrac{\text{TR} \times 3.517}{RE}$ | Mass flow required to meet a given cooling load | $\text{kg/s}$ | | **Ton of Refrigeration (TR)** | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 211 \text{ kJ/min}$ | Standard cooling capacity unit | $\text{kW}$ | | **Power Required ($P$ in kW)** | $P = \dot{m} \times W_c$ | Compressor power consumption | $\text{kW}$ | | **Carnot COP (Reference)** | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Theoretical maximum, with temperatures in **Kelvin** | dimensionless | | **Volumetric Capacity ($\dot{V}_1$)** | $\dot{V}_1 = \dot{m} \times v_1$ | Compressor displacement volume rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | | **Work of Compression (Carnot-ref)** | $W_c = Q_c - Q_e$ | Energy balance: 1st law of thermodynamics | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical exam hint:** Always use **enthalpy values from a P-h chart or saturated tables** for $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$. The expansion is isenthalpic, so $h_3 = h_4$ — this is the single most important relation in VCRS problems. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility The simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration System is the **backbone of modern cooling technology**. Real-world applications include: - **Domestic refrigerators and freezers** (single-compressor, single-evaporator, single-condenser — exactly the "simple" cycle). - **Window and split air conditioners** for residential and commercial cooling. - **Cold storage warehouses** for food preservation. - **Pharmaceutical and vaccine cold chains** (relying on the predictable COP of the simple cycle). - **Ice manufacturing plants**. - **Automobile air conditioning** systems. - **Data centre cooling** (chilled water plants use VCRS as the primary refrigeration source). Engineers prefer the simple VCRS because it offers a **high COP** (typically 2 to 4), **reliable operation**, **compact size**, and is compatible with a wide range of refrigerants (CFC, HCFC, HFC, and natural refrigerants like R-744, R-290). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Coefficient of Performance (COP) of the Simple VCRS ### Step 1 — Apply the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to the Evaporator For a steady-flow control volume around the evaporator, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes and assuming no work interaction: $$ \dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4) $$ Dividing throughout by $\dot{m}$ to get the per-unit-mass refrigerating effect: $$ q_e = h_1 - h_4 $$ **Explanation:** The evaporator has no work input, no heat loss, and the only energy interaction is heat absorption $q_e$ from the refrigerated space, which equals the enthalpy gain of the refrigerant as it evaporates from the liquid-vapour mixture at state 4 to saturated vapour at state 1. ### Step 2 — Apply SFEE to the Compressor For the compressor, the only energy input is work $w_c$, and assuming isentropic compression (no heat loss): $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 $$ **Explanation:** The compressor raises the refrigerant from low-pressure saturated vapour (state 1) to high-pressure superheated vapour (state 2). No heat is exchanged with the surroundings, so all the work input appears as an increase in refrigerant enthalpy. ### Step 3 — Apply SFEE to the Condenser For the condenser, heat is rejected to the surroundings: $$ q_c = h_2 - h_3 $$ **Explanation:** The high-pressure superheated vapour enters at state 2, desuperheats and condenses to saturated liquid at state 3, releasing heat $q_c$ to the environment at constant pressure. ### Step 4 — Apply SFEE to the Expansion Valve The expansion valve is a throttling device with no work, no heat transfer, and negligible kinetic energy change: $$ h_3 = h_4 $$ **Explanation:** This is the isenthalpic throttling process. The pressure drops from $P_3$ to $P_4$, and a portion of the liquid "flashes" into vapour to absorb the latent heat required for evaporation, cooling the rest of the refrigerant to the evaporator saturation temperature. ### Step 5 — Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) By definition, COP is the ratio of the **desired effect** (refrigerating effect) to the **work input** required: $$ COP = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{q_e}{w_c} $$ ### Step 6 — Substitute the Expressions $$ COP = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} $$ **Explanation:** Substituting the expressions from Step 1 and Step 2 directly into the COP definition yields the standard VCRS COP formula in terms of refrigerant enthalpies at the four key state points. ### Step 7 — Express COP in Terms of Heat Rejected By the First Law of Thermodynamics, the energy balance over the entire cycle is: $$ q_c = q_e + w_c $$ Rearranging: $$ q_e = q_c - w_c $$ Substituting into the COP definition: $$ COP = \frac{q_c - w_c}{w_c} = \frac{q_c}{w_c} - 1 $$ **Explanation:** This is an alternative form that is sometimes useful when the problem gives condenser heat data instead of compressor work. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass Flow Rate and Power Consumption ### Step 1 — Relate the Cooling Load to the Mass Flow Rate The total cooling load in kW is $\dot{Q}_e$ (or expressed in Tons of Refrigeration, TR). Per unit mass, the refrigerating effect is $RE = h_1 - h_4$ in $\text{kJ/kg}$. $$ \dot{Q}_e = \dot{m} \times (h_1 - h_4) $$ Solving for the mass flow rate: $$ \dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{h_1 - h_4} $$ ### Step 2 — Convert Tons of Refrigeration to kW The standard conversion is: $$ 1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 211 \text{ kJ/min} $$ Therefore, for a cooling load of $N$ tons of refrigeration: $$ \dot{Q}_e = N \times 3.517 \text{ kW} $$ ### Step 3 — Calculate the Compressor Power $$ P = \dot{m} \times w_c = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1) \text{ kW} $$ ### Step 4 — Calculate the Heat Rejection in the Condenser $$ \dot{Q}_c = \dot{m} \times q_c = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_3) \text{ kW} $$ **Explanation:** This is the thermal load that the condenser (and its associated cooling tower, if any) must be designed to handle. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** An ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration System uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The evaporator operates at $-20^\circ C$ and the condenser at $40^\circ C$. The refrigerating capacity is $10$ TR. Determine: (a) the COP, (b) the mass flow rate of the refrigerant, (c) the compressor power input, and (d) the heat rejected in the condenser. Take isentropic compression. ### Step 1 — Read Enthalpy Values from R-134a Saturation Tables At $T_e = -20^\circ C$ (saturated vapour, $x_1 = 1$): - $h_1 = 235.31$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ - $s_1 = 0.9332$ $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ At $T_c = 40^\circ C$ (saturated liquid, $x_3 = 0$): - $h_3 = 108.26$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ ### Step 2 — Determine State 2 (Isentropic Compression to Condenser Pressure) Since compression is isentropic: $s_2 = s_1 = 0.9332$ $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, and $P_2 = P_{sat}(40^\circ C) = 1.0166$ MPa. At $40^\circ C$ and $s_g = 0.9480$ $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$. Since $s_2 = 0.9332 < s_g$, state 2 is in the **superheated region**. From superheated R-134a tables at $P = 1.0$ MPa and $s = 0.9332$ $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$: - $h_2 \approx 271.71$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ (interpolated) ### Step 3 — Determine State 4 (Isenthalpic Throttling) $$ h_4 = h_3 = 108.26 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ ### Step 4 — Calculate COP $$ COP = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{235.31 - 108.26}{271.71 - 235.31} $$ $$ COP = \frac{127.05}{36.40} = 3.49 $$ ### Step 5 — Calculate Mass Flow Rate Cooling load: $\dot{Q}_e = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17$ $\text{kW} = 35.17$ $\text{kJ/s}$ $$ \dot{m} = \frac{35.17}{127.05} = 0.2768 \text{ kg/s} $$ ### Step 6 — Calculate Compressor Power $$ P = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1) = 0.2768 \times 36.40 = 10.08 \text{ kW} $$ ### Step 7 — Calculate Heat Rejection in Condenser $$ \dot{Q}_c = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_3) = 0.2768 \times (271.71 - 108.26) = 0.2768 \times 163.45 = 45.24 \text{ kW} $$ ### Step 8 — Verification via Energy Balance $$ \dot{Q}_c = \dot{Q}_e + P = 35.17 + 10.08 = 45.25 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark $$ The small discrepancy (0.01 kW) is due to rounding in the enthalpy values, validating the calculation. ## 3.4 Summary Table of Results | Parameter | Symbol | Value | |-----------|--------|-------| | Refrigerating Effect | $RE$ | $127.05$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ | | Compressor Work | $w_c$ | $36.40$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ | | Condenser Heat | $q_c$ | $163.45$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ | | COP | $COP$ | $3.49$ | | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}$ | $0.2768$ $\text{kg/s}$ | | Compressor Power | $P$ | $10.08$ $\text{kW}$ | | Heat Rejection | $\dot{Q}_c$ | $45.25$ $\text{kW}$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Component-Level Block Diagram of the Simple VCRS ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Compressor
State 1 to State 2
Low P Vapour to High P Superheated Vapour
Isentropic Compression] --> B[Condenser
State 2 to State 3
High P Vapour to High P Saturated Liquid
Isobaric Heat Rejection] B --> C[Expansion Valve
State 3 to State 4
High P Liquid to Low P Liquid Vapour Mix
Isenthalpic Throttling] C --> D[Evaporator
State 4 to State 1
Low P Mixture to Low P Saturated Vapour
Isobaric Heat Absorption] D --> A B -->|Heat Rejected Qc| E[Hot Environment
Ambient Air or Cooling Water] D -->|Heat Absorbed Qe| F[Cold Space
Refrigerated Room or AC Space] A -->|Work Input Wc| G[Electric Motor
or Engine Drive] ``` ## 4.2 Pressure vs Enthalpy (P-h) Diagram — The "Map" of the Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Saturation["Saturation Dome Region"] SATC["Saturated Liquid Line
x = 0"] SATV["Saturated Vapour Line
x = 1"] end P1["State 1
Saturated Vapour
at Evaporator Pressure Pe
Low Pressure, High Enthalpy
on Saturation Dome"] P2["State 2
Superheated Vapour
at Condenser Pressure Pc
High Pressure, Higher Enthalpy
Outside Dome to the Right"] P3["State 3
Saturated Liquid
at Condenser Pressure Pc
High Pressure, Low Enthalpy
on Saturation Dome"] P4["State 4
Liquid Vapour Mixture
at Evaporator Pressure Pe
Low Pressure, Lower Enthalpy
Inside Dome"] P1 -->|Process 1 to 2
Isentropic Compression
Slopes Upward Steeply| P2 P2 -->|Process 2 to 3
Isobaric Condensation
Horizontal Line Leftward| P3 P3 -->|Process 3 to 4
Isenthalpic Expansion
Vertical Line Downward| P4 P4 -->|Process 4 to 1
Isobaric Evaporation
Horizontal Line Rightward| P1 ``` ## 4.3 Temperature vs Entropy (T-s) Diagram — Showing Cycle Direction ```mermaid flowchart TB TS1["State 1
Saturated Vapour
Low Temperature Te
On Saturated Vapour Curve"] TS2["State 2
Superheated Vapour
High Temperature Tc
Above Dome to the Right"] TS3["State 3
Saturated Liquid
High Temperature Tc
On Saturated Liquid Curve"] TS4["State 4
Liquid Vapour Mixture
Low Temperature Te
Inside Dome"] TS1 -->|Process 1 to 2
Isentropic Compression
Vertical Line Upward| TS2 TS2 -->|Process 2 to 3
Isobaric Condensation
Curve Going Down and Left| TS3 TS3 -->|Process 3 to 4
Throttling
Constant h - Curve Down and Right| TS4 TS4 -->|Process 4 to 1
Isobaric Evaporation
Horizontal Line Rightward| TS1 ``` ## 4.4 Energy Interaction Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB INPUT[Electrical Energy Input to Compressor
Wc kW] --> COMP[Compressor] EVAP[Heat Absorbed from Cold Space
Qe kW or TR] --> CYCLE[Simple VCRS Cycle] CYCLE --> COND[Heat Rejected to Hot Environment
Qc kW] COMP --> CYCLE style CYCLE fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style INPUT fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style EVAP fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style COND fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style COMP fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Vapour Compression Refrigeration System. List its main components. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) is a refrigeration system in which a refrigerant undergoes phase change (evaporation and condensation) in a closed cycle, with the compression work supplied by a mechanical compressor. The refrigerant absorbs heat at low temperature from the refrigerated space and rejects it at a higher temperature to the surroundings. **Main Components (4 essential parts):** 1. **Compressor** — Compresses low-pressure refrigerant vapour to high pressure. 2. **Condenser** — Rejects heat from the refrigerant to the surroundings at constant pressure. 3. **Expansion Valve (Throttling Device)** — Drops the pressure of the liquid refrigerant. 4. **Evaporator** — Absorbs heat from the refrigerated space, causing the refrigerant to evaporate. --- ### Question 2: What is the significance of the Coefficient of Performance (COP) in a refrigeration system? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The Coefficient of Performance (COP) is a measure of the **efficiency of a refrigeration system**, defined as the ratio of the desired refrigerating effect ($Q_e$) to the work input ($W_c$) required to achieve it. $$ COP = \frac{Q_e}{W_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} $$ **Significance:** - COP is **not a percentage**; it can be greater than 1 (typically 2 to 4 for VCRS). - A higher COP indicates a **more energy-efficient** refrigerator. - It allows direct comparison between different refrigeration cycles. - For a reversible (Carnot) cycle, COP represents the **theoretical maximum** achievable. $$ COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} $$ where $T_L$ and $T_H$ are evaporator and condenser temperatures in **Kelvin**. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]` **Question:** With the help of a neat P-h and T-s diagram, explain the working of a simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS). Derive the expression for its Coefficient of Performance (COP). A simple VCRS operates between evaporator temperature $-10^\circ C$ and condenser temperature $40^\circ C$. The refrigerant used is R-134a. The system provides $5$ TR of cooling. Assuming isentropic compression, determine: (a) the COP, (b) the refrigerant mass flow rate, and (c) the compressor power input. **Enthalpy Data Given (R-134a):** - At $-10^\circ C$ (sat. vapour): $h_1 = 244.55$ $\text{kJ/kg}$, $s_1 = 0.9618$ $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ - At $40^\circ C$ (sat. liquid): $h_3 = 108.26$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ - At $P_c = 1.0166$ MPa, $s = 0.9618$ $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$: $h_2 = 274.17$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ (superheated) #### Solution **(a) COP Calculation:** $$ COP = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1} $$ Substituting (since $h_4 = h_3$): $$ COP = \frac{244.55 - 108.26}{274.17 - 244.55} = \frac{136.29}{29.62} = 4.60 $$ **(b) Mass Flow Rate:** $$ \dot{Q}_e = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585 \text{ kW} = 17.585 \text{ kJ/s} $$ $$ \dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{17.585}{136.29} = 0.1290 \text{ kg/s} $$ **(c) Compressor Power:** $$ P = \dot{m} \times (h_2 - h_1) = 0.1290 \times 29.62 = 3.82 \text{ kW} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]` **Question:** (a) List the assumptions made for the analysis of a theoretical/simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration System. (7 marks) (b) An ideal VCRS using R-12 operates between an evaporator temperature of $-15^\circ C$ and a condenser temperature of $30^\circ C$. The refrigerating capacity is $8$ TR. Calculate: (i) the refrigerating effect, (ii) the work of compression, (iii) the heat rejected in the condenser per kg, (iv) the COP, (v) the mass flow rate of refrigerant, and (vi) the compressor power. (7 marks) **Given Enthalpy Data (R-12):** - At $-15^\circ C$ (sat. vapour): $h_1 = 183.20$ $\text{kJ/kg}$, $s_1 = 0.7080$ $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ - At $30^\circ C$ (sat. liquid): $h_3 = 71.32$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ - At $30^\circ C$ condenser pressure with $s_2 = s_1 = 0.7080$: $h_2 = 203.05$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ (superheated) #### Solution **(a) Assumptions of the Theoretical VCRS (7 marks):** 1. Compression is isentropic ($s_1 = s_2$). 2. The refrigerant leaves the condenser as a saturated liquid ($x_3 = 0$). 3. The expansion process is isenthalpic ($h_3 = h_4$). 4. The refrigerant leaves the evaporator as a saturated vapour ($x_1 = 1$). 5. There is no subcooling of the liquid in the condenser. 6. There is no superheating of the vapour in the evaporator. 7. Pressure drops in the connecting pipes are negligible. 8. Mass flow rate of the refrigerant is constant throughout the cycle. 9. The system operates under steady-flow conditions. 10. Heat transfer to or from the surroundings in the compressor and expansion valve is zero. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Refrigerating Effect:** $$ RE = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3 = 183.20 - 71.32 = 111.88 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **(ii) Work of Compression:** $$ W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 203.05 - 183.20 = 19.85 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **(iii) Heat Rejected in the Condenser:** $$ Q_c = h_2 - h_3 = 203.05 - 71.32 = 131.73 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Verification:** $Q_c = RE + W_c = 111.88 + 19.85 = 131.73$ $\text{kJ/kg}$ ✓ **(iv) Coefficient of Performance:** $$ COP = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{111.88}{19.85} = 5.64 $$ **(v) Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant:** $$ \dot{Q}_e = 8 \times 3.517 = 28.136 \text{ kW} $$ $$ \dot{m} = \frac{28.136}{111.88} = 0.2515 \text{ kg/s} $$ **(vi) Compressor Power:** $$ P = \dot{m} \times W_c = 0.2515 \times 19.85 = 4.99 \text{ kW} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in VCRS Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting $h_4 = h_3$:** Many students mistakenly use $h_4 = h_1$ or compute $h_4$ separately. **Always remember:** the expansion valve is isenthalpic, so $h_4 = h_3$ directly. Failing to apply this single relation will cascade errors through COP, mass flow rate, and power calculations. > > 2. **Using temperatures in Celsius in the Carnot COP formula:** The Carnot COP formula requires **absolute temperatures in Kelvin**. Converting $T_L = -15^\circ C$ to $258$ K, not using $-15$ K — this is a frequent (and heavily penalized) mistake. > > 3. **Mixing up TR and kW:** $1$ TR $= 3.517$ kW. If a problem gives the cooling load in TR, convert to kW before using SI formulas. Mixing units will lose 2 to 3 marks. > > 4. **Not drawing the P-h and T-s diagrams:** Module 2 questions specifically ask for diagrams. A missing or unlabelled diagram typically costs 3 to 4 marks out of 14. > > 5. **State 2 misidentification:** State 2 is **always superheated** (above the saturation dome) in the simple VCRS. Students often mistakenly locate it on the saturated vapour line. Mark the isentropic line clearly from state 1, then identify where it crosses the condenser pressure. > > 6. **Confusing COP with thermal efficiency:** COP is **not bounded by 1**; it can range from 2 to 6 for typical VCRS. Saying "COP is 200%" is wrong — say "COP is 2" or "COP is 200% of Carnot COP." --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist: Simple VCRS** > > - **Four components, four processes, four state points.** Memorize them: Compressor (1→2), Condenser (2→3), Expansion Valve (3→4), Evaporator (4→1). > - **The "is" words are critical:** Isentropic compression, isobaric condensation and evaporation, isenthalpic expansion. Each one defines a specific thermodynamic constraint. > - **The golden equation:** $h_3 = h_4$ (throttling). Use it without thinking. > - **The COP formula:** $COP = (h_1 - h_4) / (h_2 - h_1)$. Memorize and practice. > - **Standard conversions:** $1$ TR $= 3.517$ kW $= 211$ kJ/min. Always carry these in your head. > - **P-h and T-s diagrams are mandatory** for any 14-mark question. Draw them neatly with the saturation dome, all four state points labelled, and arrows showing the cycle direction. > - **Cycle direction in T-s diagram:** Clockwise on a T-s diagram (heat absorbed at low T, heat rejected at high T — opposite to a heat engine). > - **Energy balance check:** $\dot{Q}_c = \dot{Q}_e + P$ — use this to verify your numerical answers in the exam. > - **Carnot COP** = $T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ with **absolute temperatures**.
