Real VCRS COP is always less than Carnot COP for the same temperature limits. > - **Refrigerant selection impacts COP** — different refrigerants have different $h$ values at the same state points, changing COP even with identical operating temperatures. > - **Practical deviations** (subcooling, superheating, non-isentropic compression) **increase COP** in some cases (subcooling, superheating) and **decrease it** in others (non-isentropic compression). The "simple" cycle is the theoretical baseline against which all practical cycles are compared.

topics.title: Representation of the cycle on T-s and P-h diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Representation of the cycle on T-s and P-h diagram ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Refrigeration Engineering, the **Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)** is the dominant refrigeration cycle used in domestic refrigerators, air-conditioners, cold storage plants, and industrial chillers. For thermodynamic analysis, the cycle is graphically represented on two fundamental property diagrams: - **T-s Diagram (Temperature–Entropy Diagram):** Used to visualize heat transfer, identify isentropic efficiencies, and compute the Coefficient of Performance (COP) by measuring areas under the process curves. - **P-h Diagram (Pressure–Enthalpy Diagram):** Also called the **Mollier Diagram for the Refrigerant**, it is the *working tool* of every refrigeration engineer because it directly yields refrigeration effect, compressor work, and COP without requiring tabulated data. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examination Significance:** KTU examiners frequently ask questions like *"Explain the representation of the ideal vapor compression refrigeration cycle on T-s and P-h charts"* and *"With the help of a P-h diagram, derive the expression for COP."* This is a **guaranteed 7 to 14 mark question** in every university exam cycle. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are planning a road trip on a map. There are two ways to draw the trip: - A **topographical map (T-s)** — shows you every hill, valley, and the exact elevation change. Good for engineers analyzing *energy quality and entropy generation*. - A **road distance-and-fuel map (P-h)** — shows you how much "fuel" (work input) and how much "destination reward" (cooling effect) you get at every stop. Good for *practical, on-the-fly performance calculations*. The **T-s diagram** is the *physicist's view* and the **P-h diagram** is the *practitioner's view* of the *exact same thermodynamic journey* through compression → condensation → expansion → evaporation. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Property Constants to Remember:** > - Atmospheric Pressure: **P₀ = 101.325 kPa** > - Saturated Refrigerant Curves define the **Saturation Dome** (or *vapor dome*). > - Area under the evaporator curve on T-s = **Refrigerating Effect (RE)**. > - Vertical distance on P-h between saturated liquid and saturated vapor = **h_fg (latent heat of vaporization)**. ### 1.3 State Points of the Ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle | State Point | Location | Physical Condition | |:-----------:|:--------:|:-------------------| | **1** | Saturated Vapor Line | Entry to Compressor (dry saturated vapor) | | **2** | Superheated Region | Exit of Compressor (high P, high T superheated vapor) | | **3** | Saturated Liquid Line | Exit of Condenser (saturated liquid) | | **4** | Two-Phase Region | Exit of Expansion Valve (liquid + vapor mixture) | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Steady-Flow Processes (Step-by-Step Logic) Let the refrigerant circulate as the working fluid. Each of the four components performs one **steady-flow process**: **Process 1→2 : Isentropic Compression (in Compressor)** - The compressor takes low-pressure vapor (Point 1) and compresses it to the condenser pressure. - The process is assumed *isentropic* (reversible adiabatic) — therefore **s₁ = s₂**. - Entropy does NOT change. Temperature rises sharply. Pressure rises sharply. - This is shown as a **steep, near-vertical line going upward and slightly right** on the T-s diagram. **Process 2→3 : Constant Pressure Heat Rejection (in Condenser)** - The high-pressure superheated vapor rejects heat to the surroundings (cooling water or ambient air) at **constant pressure**. - The fluid moves from superheated region → saturated vapor → saturated liquid. - Entropy *decreases* (heat leaves the system). - On T-s: it is a **horizontal line moving leftward** across the dome. On P-h: a **steep downward line** (enthalpy drops significantly as latent heat is rejected). **Process 3→4 : Isenthalpic Throttling (in Expansion Valve / Capillary Tube)** - Liquid refrigerant at high pressure passes through a throttle device. - Throttling is an **isenthalpic process** — therefore **h₃ = h₄**. - Pressure drops sharply to evaporator pressure. Temperature drops slightly. - Some liquid flashes into vapor (this is called *flash gas*). - On both diagrams: a **near-vertical downward line** in the two-phase region. **Process 4→1 : Constant Pressure Heat Absorption (in Evaporator)** - The cold liquid-vapor mixture absorbs heat from the refrigerated space at constant pressure. - It travels from the two-phase region → back to saturated vapor at Point 1. - Entropy *increases* (heat enters the system). - On T-s: **horizontal line moving rightward** across the dome. On P-h: a **near-horizontal line** moving right (enthalpy rises as latent heat is absorbed). > [!NOTE] > **Why 'Near-vertical' or 'Near-horizontal'?** On the T-s diagram, isentropic lines are *strictly vertical*. On the P-h diagram, isenthalpic lines are *strictly vertical*. In the two-phase (wet) region, temperature and pressure are *not independent* — they are linked by saturation. Hence, constant pressure lines coincide with constant temperature lines inside the dome. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master These Five Equations — They Appear in 90% of KTU Numerical Problems:** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |:-:|:--------:|:-------:|:-----:|:-------:| | 1 | Refrigerating Effect (RE) | $\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Enthalpy gained in evaporator per kg of refrigerant | | 2 | Compressor Work (Wc) | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Enthalpy rise during compression | | 3 | Heat Rejected in Condenser (Qc) | $Q_c = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Energy balance: RE + Wc = Qc | | 4 | Coefficient of Performance (COP) | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Always written as RE / Wc | | 5 | Mass Flow Rate of Refrigerant | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\text{Refrigeration Capacity (kW)}}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | Used when TR or kW capacity is given | **Conversion Factors for KTU Problems:** $$ 1 \text{ TR (Ton of Refrigeration)} = 3.517 \text{ kW} = 210 \text{ kJ/min} $$ $$ \text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{isentropic}} \times \text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} $$ ### 2.3 Real-World Utility - **P-h charts** are printed on the back of every refrigerant cylinder in industry and are issued by ASHRAE for every common refrigerant (R-12, R-22, R-134a, R-410A, R-404A, NH₃, CO₂). - Field engineers read P-h charts to **diagnose system performance**: a non-vertical compression line indicates *non-isentropic (real) compression*; a non-vertical throttling line indicates a *faulty expansion valve*. - In **cold chain logistics** (used by Keralafood and marine exports from Kochi), P-h diagrams help select optimal evaporating and condensing pressures for different storage temperatures. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of COP from the P-h Diagram **Given:** Steady-state, steady-flow operation of an ideal vapour compression refrigeration cycle with state points 1, 2, 3, 4 as defined earlier. **Energy Balance on the Evaporator (Control Volume 4→1):** $$ q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4 $$ This is the **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** in kJ/kg. The mass flow rate of refrigerant is $\dot{m}$, so the rate of heat extraction is: $$ \dot{Q}_{\text{evap}} = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4) \;\;\; [\text{kW}] $$ **Energy Balance on the Compressor (Control Volume 1→2):** Applying the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes: $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 $$ This is the **specific compressor work** in kJ/kg. **Rate of Work Input to Compressor:** $$ \dot{W}_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) \;\;\; [\text{kW}] $$ **Definition of Coefficient of Performance:** By definition, COP is the ratio of desired effect (refrigeration) to the work input: $$ \text{COP} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{\text{evap}}}{\dot{W}_c} $$ Substituting the expressions derived above: $$ \text{COP} = \dfrac{\dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)} $$ Since $\dot{m}$ cancels out: $$ \boxed{\;\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **This is the single most important derived expression in KTU Module 2.** A typical 7-mark sub-part asks you to derive this from the P-h chart. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Reading the Diagrams | Process | T-s Diagram Behavior | P-h Diagram Behavior | Quantity Calculated | |:-------:|:--------------------:|:--------------------:|:-------------------:| | 1→2 Compressor | Vertical line (s = constant), T ↑ sharply | Curve going up & slightly right | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ | | 2→3 Condenser | Horizontal line (P = constant), T constant then drops, s ↓ | Steep curve going down (h drops a lot due to latent rejection) | $q_{\text{out}} = h_2 - h_3$ | | 3→4 Expansion Valve | Near-vertical drop in two-phase region | Strictly vertical line (h = constant) | $h_3 = h_4$ (isenthalpic) | | 4→1 Evaporator | Horizontal line (P = constant), T constant, s ↑ | Near-horizontal curve going right | $q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4$ | ### 3.3 Incorporating Subcooling and Superheating (Advanced KTU Extension) **Subcooling (State 3a instead of 3):** The refrigerant at the condenser exit is cooled *below* its saturation temperature. This increases the refrigerating effect. $$ \text{RE}_{\text{subcooled}} = h_1 - h_{4a} \;\; > \;\; h_1 - h_4 $$ The throttling line is now **vertical** from $h_3 = h_{3a}$ down to $h_4 = h_{4a}$. Since $h_{4a} < h_4$, the refrigerating effect increases. **Superheating (State 1a instead of 1):** The refrigerant at compressor inlet is heated *above* saturation temperature. This: - Increases compressor work slightly (since enthalpy rises). - **Significantly increases refrigerating effect** if heat is absorbed *in the evaporator* (useful superheating). - **Decreases refrigerating effect** if heat is absorbed in the *suction line* (harmful superheating). $$ \text{RE}_{\text{superheated}} = h_{1a} - h_4 $$ $$ \text{COP}_{\text{superheated}} = \dfrac{h_{1a} - h_4}{h_{2a} - h_{1a}} $$ The new state points are read directly from the superheated vapor region on the right side of the saturation dome. ### 3.4 Real (Actual) Cycle vs Ideal Cycle In a real cycle, the compression is **not isentropic** — entropy *increases* during compression due to friction and heat losses: $$ s_{2,\text{actual}} > s_{2,\text{ideal}} $$ The actual state point 2' lies to the **right** of the ideal state point 2 on both T-s and P-h diagrams. The **isentropic efficiency** of the compressor is defined as: $$ \eta_{\text{isen}} = \dfrac{h_{2,\text{ideal}} - h_1}{h_{2,\text{actual}} - h_1} = \dfrac{W_{\text{ideal}}}{W_{\text{actual}}} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Cycle & Diagrams) The following Mermaid block diagram captures the functional architecture of the Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle and its mapping onto T-s and P-h coordinates: ```mermaid flowchart TD A["REFRIGERATION CYCLE
FOUR STATE POINTS"] --> B["State 1: Saturated Vapor
at Evaporator Pressure"] A --> C["State 2: Superheated Vapor
at Condenser Pressure"] A --> D["State 3: Saturated Liquid
at Condenser Pressure"] A --> E["State 4: Liquid Vapor Mix
at Evaporator Pressure"] F["PROCESS 1 TO 2
Compressor
Isentropic"] -->|h2 minus h1| G["COMPRESSOR WORK WC"] H["PROCESS 2 TO 3
Condenser
Constant Pressure"] -->|h2 minus h3| I["HEAT REJECTED QC"] J["PROCESS 3 TO 4
Expansion Valve
Isenthalpic"] -->|h3 equals h4| K["NO WORK NO HEAT"] L["PROCESS 4 TO 1
Evaporator
Constant Pressure"] -->|h1 minus h4| M["REFRIGERATING EFFECT RE"] N["T-S DIAGRAM VIEW"] --> N1["Process 1-2 vertical line"] N --> N2["Process 2-3 horizontal line moving left"] N --> N3["Process 3-4 near vertical drop"] N --> N4["Process 4-1 horizontal line moving right"] O["P-H DIAGRAM VIEW"] --> O1["Process 1-2 curve up and right"] O --> O2["Process 2-3 steep curve downward"] O --> O3["Process 3-4 strictly vertical"] O --> O4["Process 4-1 near horizontal curve right"] P["FINAL OUTPUTS"] --> Q["COP equals RE divided by WC"] P --> R["Mass Flow Rate m dot"] P --> S["Heat Rejection Rate QC dot"] A --> F F --> N F --> O P --> T["ANSWERS FOR KTU PROBLEMS"] ``` ### 4.2 Schematic Description (For Manual Drawing in Exams) When drawing in the KTU answer sheet, always include these elements: **T-s Diagram Layout (Ideal VCRC):** - Draw the saturation dome: an inverted U with the **saturated liquid line on the left**, **saturated vapor line on the right**, and the **critical point** at the top. - Mark Point 1 on the saturated vapor line at the lower (evaporator) temperature T₁. - Draw a **vertical line 1→2** going up to the upper temperature T₂ (condenser temperature). - Draw a **horizontal line 2→3** moving left at the upper temperature, exiting at the saturated liquid line at Point 3. - Draw a **near-vertical line 3→4** dropping straight down through the two-phase region to the lower temperature at Point 4. - Draw a **horizontal line 4→1** moving right along the lower isotherm back to Point 1. **P-h Diagram Layout (Ideal VCRC):** - Plot **pressure** on the vertical (logarithmic) axis and **enthalpy** on the horizontal axis. - Mark the saturation dome (bell-shaped curve in P-h coordinates). - Place Points 1, 2, 3, 4 as described above. - Process 3→4 is **strictly vertical** (h₃ = h₄) — this is the signature feature of throttling on a P-h diagram. - Measure RE and Wc directly as **horizontal distances** on the diagram. - For the actual cycle, locate Point 2' to the right of Point 2. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always **label every state point** (1, 2, 3, 4) and **indicate the direction of the cycle** with arrowheads. Also write the unit kJ/kg next to every enthalpy value. Examiners award 1 mark for a *neat, fully labelled* cycle diagram alone. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State the four processes of an ideal vapour compression refrigeration cycle.* **Model Answer:** The four steady-flow processes of an ideal VCRC are: 1. **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression of saturated vapor in the compressor. 2. **Process 2→3:** Constant-pressure heat rejection in the condenser; the refrigerant changes from superheated vapor to saturated liquid. 3. **Process 3→4:** Isenthalpic (throttling) expansion in the expansion valve; pressure and temperature drop sharply. 4. **Process 4→1:** Constant-pressure heat absorption in the evaporator; the liquid-vapor mixture evaporates back to saturated vapor. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is the expansion process represented by a vertical line on the P-h diagram?* **Model Answer:** The expansion through the throttle valve is a **throttling process**, governed by the steady-flow energy equation: $$ h_3 = h_4 $$ Since enthalpy remains constant during throttling, the process is **isenthalpic**. On a P-h chart, an isenthalpic process is represented by a **strictly vertical line** going straight downward from the condenser pressure to the evaporator pressure. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, internal choice is provided between two questions of equal cognitive demand. Both options below are completely independent and solvable. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* **(a)** With the help of neat sketches, represent the **ideal vapour compression refrigeration cycle** on **(i) T-s diagram** and **(ii) P-h diagram**. Mark all the state points and label the processes. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of an ideal VCRC using the P-h diagram. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **T-s Diagram (Refer to schematic description in Section 4.2):** The cycle is drawn inside and around the saturation dome on the T-s plane: - Point 1 lies on the saturated vapor line at the evaporator saturation temperature. - Process 1→2 is a **vertical line** (constant entropy) rising upward. - Point 2 lies in the superheated region at the condenser temperature. - Process 2→3 is a **horizontal line** at constant condenser pressure, moving left, crossing the dome, and ending on the saturated liquid line at Point 3. - Process 3→4 is a **vertical drop** (irreversible, hence not truly vertical but conventionally drawn vertical for ideal cycle) inside the dome. - Point 4 lies in the two-phase region at the evaporator temperature. - Process 4→1 is a **horizontal line** at constant evaporator pressure, moving right back to Point 1. **P-h Diagram:** - Same state points and processes, but plotted on log-pressure vs enthalpy axes. - Process 3→4 is **strictly vertical** (isenthalpic), which is the signature feature. - Refrigerating Effect = horizontal length of line 4→1. - Compressor Work = horizontal length of line 1→2. - The area of the cycle on P-h has no direct physical meaning (unlike T-s). **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1:** Apply SFEE to the **evaporator** (control volume across 4→1): $$ q_{\text{in}} = h_1 - h_4 \;\; [\text{kJ/kg}] $$ This is the refrigerating effect (RE) per kg of refrigerant. **Step 2:** Apply SFEE to the **compressor** (control volume across 1→2): $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 \;\; [\text{kJ/kg}] $$ This is the specific compressor work. **Step 3:** By definition, COP = desired effect / work input: $$ \text{COP} = \dfrac{q_{\text{in}}}{w_c} $$ **Step 4:** Substituting: $$ \boxed{\;\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}\;} $$ **Interpretation:** To maximize COP, we must maximize $(h_1 - h_4)$ and minimize $(h_2 - h_1)$. This is achieved by operating at lower condensing pressure and higher evaporating pressure. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(a)** Draw the **P-h diagram** of an ideal VCRC and show how the **refrigerating effect**, **compressor work**, and **heat rejected** are obtained directly from the diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refrigerator using R-134a operates between evaporator temperature $T_e = -10^{\circ}\text{C}$ and condenser temperature $T_c = 40^{\circ}\text{C}$. The refrigerant is dry saturated at compressor inlet and saturated liquid at condenser exit. Find: **(i)** refrigerating effect, **(ii)** compressor work, **(iii)** COP, **(iv)** mass flow rate for 5 TR capacity. Use the enthalpy values: $h_1 = 244.5$ kJ/kg, $h_2 = 268.0$ kJ/kg, $h_3 = 115.0$ kJ/kg, $h_4 = 115.0$ kJ/kg. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** On the P-h diagram of an ideal VCRC: - The **refrigerating effect** RE = $(h_1 - h_4)$ = horizontal distance between Points 1 and 4 along the evaporator pressure line. - The **compressor work** $W_c = (h_2 - h_1)$ = horizontal distance between Points 1 and 2. - The **heat rejected** in condenser $Q_c = (h_2 - h_3)$ = horizontal distance between Points 2 and 3. All three quantities are obtained directly as **horizontal line segment lengths** on the P-h chart — this is why P-h is the *engineer's working diagram*. **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** $h_1 = 244.5$, $h_2 = 268.0$, $h_3 = h_4 = 115.0$ kJ/kg (since throttling is isenthalpic); Capacity = 5 TR. **(i) Refrigerating Effect:** $$ \text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = 244.5 - 115.0 = 129.5 \;\text{kJ/kg} $$ **(ii) Compressor Work:** $$ W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 268.0 - 244.5 = 23.5 \;\text{kJ/kg} $$ **(iii) Coefficient of Performance:** $$ \text{COP} = \dfrac{\text{RE}}{W_c} = \dfrac{129.5}{23.5} = 5.51 $$ **(iv) Mass Flow Rate:** Convert 5 TR to kW: $$ \dot{Q}_e = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585 \;\text{kW} = 17.585 \;\text{kJ/s} $$ Apply: $$ \dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_e}{\text{RE}} = \dfrac{17.585}{129.5} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\dot{m} = 0.1358 \;\text{kg/s} \approx 0.136 \;\text{kg/s}\;} $$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:** > 1. **Forgetting to label state points** on the diagram — Examiners deduct up to **1 mark** if Points 1, 2, 3, 4 are not clearly marked. > 2. **Drawing the throttling process 3→4 as a slanted line** on the P-h diagram — It MUST be vertical because $h_3 = h_4$. Slanting it shows conceptual error and costs **2 marks**. > 3. **Mixing up RE formula:** Students often write RE = $h_1 - h_3$ instead of $h_1 - h_4$. Always use **compressor inlet and expansion valve outlet** enthalpies. > 4. **Unit mismatch:** Writing COP in "kJ/kg" instead of leaving it dimensionless is a recurrent mistake. > 5. **In numerical problems, forgetting to convert TR to kW** using the factor 3.517 — this single error can wipe out **3 to 4 marks**. > 6. **Confusing isentropic with isenthalpic:** Isentropic applies to the compressor (s constant), isenthalpic applies to the expansion valve (h constant). Mixing these up is a fatal conceptual error. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Two diagrams, one cycle:** T-s shows heat transfers as *areas*; P-h shows performance parameters as *horizontal lengths*. Both represent the same physical cycle. - **Master the four state points:** Point 1 (sat vapor at low P), Point 2 (superheated at high P), Point 3 (sat liquid at high P), Point 4 (two-phase at low P). - **Four process signatures:** - Compressor → *isentropic* (vertical on T-s). - Condenser → *isobaric & isothermal* in two-phase region. - Expansion valve → *isenthalpic* (vertical on P-h). - Evaporator → *isobaric & isothermal* in two-phase region. - **Three foundational equations** to memorize: - RE = $h_1 - h_4$ - $W_c$ = $h_2 - h_1$ - COP = RE / $W_c$ - **Energy balance identity:** $Q_c = \text{RE} + W_c$ (heat rejected equals refrigeration plus work input). - **1 TR = 3.517 kW** — used in every numerical problem involving refrigeration capacity. - **Superheating** increases both RE and $W_c$; net effect on COP depends on whether the superheat is *useful* (in evaporator) or *harmful* (in suction line). - **Subcooling** always improves COP by increasing RE without changing $W_c$ significantly. - **Actual cycle** Point 2' lies to the right of Point 2 on both diagrams, reducing the COP by the factor $\eta_{\text{isen}}$. - **Reading the P-h chart is a professional skill** — KTU expects you to read enthalpy values directly from the saturation tables of common refrigerants (R-134a, R-22, NH₃) provided in the question paper. - **Always present a labelled diagram** before starting any derivation — it earns you easy marks. ---

topics.title: Use of refrigerant tables and charts
Effect of operating conditions on COP - Suction line heat exchanger
Actual vapour compression refrigeration system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Actual vapour compression refrigeration system # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Actual Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (Actual VCRS)** is the real-world thermodynamic cycle employed in commercial and domestic refrigeration, in which the working refrigerant undergoes **four irreversible processes** in sequence: (1) adiabatic (but not isentropic) compression in a reciprocating/rotary compressor, (2) desuperheating and condensation at approximately constant pressure in a condenser, (3) throttling (isenthalpic expansion) through an expansion device, and (4) evaporation at approximately constant pressure in an evaporator. Unlike the ideal (theoretical) cycle, the actual cycle accounts for **superheating of suction vapour**, **subcooling of liquid refrigerant**, **pressure drops** in the condenser and evaporator, and **heat exchange** with the surroundings through the compressor body and connecting pipelines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The actual VCRS explicitly incorporates three real-world irreversibilities — **suction superheating**, **liquid subcooling**, and **fluid friction (pressure drops)**. Mastery of the corresponding P–h and T–s diagram modifications is mandatory for ESE. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation) Think of the actual refrigeration cycle like a **water pump system in a hilly village**: - The **compressor** is like a *bicycle pump* — it gets hot while forcing refrigerant (air) into a smaller space, but unlike an ideal pump, the cylinder warms up and *leaks* some heat to surroundings. - The **condenser** is the *open cooking pot* on a stove — refrigerant releases heat to the kitchen, but the pipes leading to and from it are old and cause a *pressure drop* (like friction in narrow pipes). - The **expansion valve** is the *pinched garden hose nozzle* — refrigerant suddenly cools and loses pressure. - The **evaporator** is the *cold storage room* — refrigerant absorbs heat to cool it, but before entering the compressor, it often *picks up a little extra warmth* from the warm kitchen air (suction superheating) — useful extra cooling, but it costs more compressor work. The key idea: **no real component is perfect**. There is friction, heat leakage, and unwanted heat gain. The "actual" cycle simply **draws the real picture** of what physically happens inside a fridge or air-conditioner. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Block > [!NOTE] > **Essential KTU Terms (must memorise):** > - **Superheating** — Vapour temperature rising *above* its saturation temperature at a given pressure. > - **Subcooling** — Liquid temperature falling *below* its saturation temperature at a given pressure. > - **Useful Superheating** — Superheating that occurs *inside the evaporator* (adds to refrigeration effect). > - **Useless Superheating** — Superheating that occurs *in the suction line outside the evaporator* (adds to work without adding RE). > - **Pressure Drop (ΔP)** — Loss of working pressure due to friction in condenser/evaporator tubes. > - **Volumetric Efficiency (η_v)** — Ratio of actual to theoretical refrigerant mass flow. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of the Actual VCRS (Operational Logic) The actual system consists of the same four primary components as the ideal cycle, but each operates under realistic (non-ideal) conditions: 1. **Reciprocating / Scroll / Rotary Compressor** - Compresses low-pressure vapour to high-pressure vapour. - *Non-ideality:* Polytropic (not isentropic) compression, suction vapour is superheated, cylinder walls exchange heat with surroundings. - Discharge temperature is **higher** than in the ideal cycle. 2. **Condenser (Shell-and-coil / Tube-in-tube)** - Rejects heat from high-pressure superheated vapour to ambient (air or water). - *Non-ideality:* Refrigerant pressure **drops** along the length due to tube friction; condensation may not be complete. 3. **Expansion Device (Capillary tube / Thermostatic Expansion Valve — TEV)** - Throttles high-pressure liquid to low-pressure liquid–vapour mixture. - *Non-ideality:* Process is **isenthalpic** (h₃ = h₄), but throttling is irreversible. 4. **Evaporator (Finned coil / DX coil)** - Absorbs heat from the space to be cooled. - *Non-ideality:* Pressure **drops** along its length; suction line often picks up extra heat (useless superheating). ## 2.2 Reasons for Deviation from the Ideal VCRS | **#** | **Real-World Effect** | **Location** | **Impact on Cycle** | |:-----:|:---------------------:|:------------:|:-------------------:| | 1 | Suction superheating inside evaporator | Evaporator outlet | ↑ RE, ↑ Work (net positive gain) | | 2 | Heat gain in suction line (useless SH) | Compressor inlet | ↑ Work, RE unchanged (net loss) | | 3 | Liquid subcooling in condenser | Condenser outlet | ↑ RE, Work unchanged (gain) | | 4 | Pressure drop in condenser | Condenser | ↑ Compressor work, ↓ RE | | 5 | Pressure drop in evaporator | Evaporator | ↑ Compressor work, ↓ RE | | 6 | Heat loss/gain through compressor body | Compressor | Slight deviation in isentropic idealisation | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following equations for ESE 2024:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Refrigeration Effect (RE)} &= h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} \\ \text{Compressor Work (W_c)} &= h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} \\ \text{Heat Rejected (Q_R)} &= h_2 - h_3 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]} \\ \text{COP}_{\text{actual}} &= \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} = \dfrac{\text{RE}}{W_c} \\ \text{COP}_{\text{with subcooling}} &= \dfrac{h_1 - h_4'}{h_2 - h_1}, \quad h_4' < h_4 \\ \text{Isentropic Efficiency } \eta_{\text{isen}} &= \dfrac{h_2^{\,s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1} \\ \text{Volumetric Efficiency } \eta_v &= \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}} \cdot v_1}{V_s} \end{aligned} $$ | **State Point** | **Description** | **Typical Condition** | |:---------------:|:----------------|:----------------------| | 1 | Compressor inlet | Superheated vapour (low P) | | 2 | Compressor outlet / Condenser inlet | Highly superheated vapour (high P) | | 3 | Condenser outlet / Exp. valve inlet | Subcooled liquid (high P) | | 4 | Exp. valve outlet / Evaporator inlet | Liquid–vapour mixture (low P) | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility The actual VCRS analysis is the **backbone of refrigeration equipment design**. HVAC engineers use it to: - **Size compressors** based on real discharge temperatures (avoiding thermal damage). - **Select refrigerant** by evaluating the trade-off between useful superheat and compression work. - **Design condensers and evaporators** by accounting for ΔP (which determines pumping power and heat transfer). - **Improve COP** by subcooling the liquid (free gain) and limiting useless superheating via proper insulation of suction lines. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: COP of Actual VCRS with Subcooling and Superheating **Step 1 — Define state points (per KTU convention):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State 1:} & \quad \text{Superheated vapour at compressor inlet (low pressure } P_1) \\ \text{State 2:} & \quad \text{Superheated vapour at compressor outlet (high pressure } P_2) \\ \text{State 3:} & \quad \text{Subcooled liquid at condenser outlet (pressure } P_2) \\ \text{State 4:} & \quad \text{Liquid–vapour mixture after expansion (pressure } P_1) \\ \text{State 4':} & \quad \text{State 4 if NO subcooling is present} \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion logic:** In a real system, condenser outlet is always subcooled below T_sat at P₂, so h₃ < h₃(sat). Since throttling is isenthalpic, h₄ = h₃. **Step 2 — Apply steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) to each component:** For the **compressor** (neglecting KE/PE and using actual work): $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 $$ For the **condenser** (heat rejected to ambient): $$ q_R = h_2 - h_3 $$ For the **expansion valve** (throttling, no work, no heat transfer, adiabatic): $$ h_4 = h_3 $$ For the **evaporator** (heat absorbed from cold space): $$ q_e = h_1 - h_4 $$ **Step 3 — Formulate the Coefficient of Performance:** $$ \text{COP} = \frac{\text{Desired Output (RE)}}{\text{Required Input (Work)}} = \frac{q_e}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} $$ **Step 4 — Incorporate subcooling effect:** If the liquid is subcooled from temperature T₃(sat) down to T₃', the enthalpy drops from h₃(sat) to h₃. The new expansion outlet enthalpy is: $$ h_4' = h_3 < h_3^{\text{sat}} $$ Therefore, the **refrigeration effect increases** while the **compressor work remains unchanged** (since state 1 is the same). The improved COP becomes: $$ \text{COP}_{\text{with subcooling}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4'}{h_2 - h_1}, \quad \text{where } h_4' < h_4 $$ **Step 5 — Incorporate useful superheating effect:** If superheating occurs *inside the evaporator* (from state 1' to state 1), the new inlet enthalpy is: $$ h_1 > h_{1'} \quad \text{(at same low pressure } P_1) $$ The **refrigeration effect increases** by (h₁ − h₁'), but **compressor work also increases** by the same amount in an isentropic idealisation. Net effect: **COP remains the same**, but the **capacity (kg of refrigerant circulated) may drop** due to higher specific volume at suction. **Step 6 — Effect of useless superheating (suction line heat gain):** $$ \Delta h_{\text{useless}} = h_{1''} - h_1 > 0 $$ The COP becomes: $$ \text{COP}_{\text{useless SH}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_{2''} - h_{1''}} < \text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} $$ > **Conversion logic:** Useless superheating raises both h₁ and h₂ by the same amount in the ideal case, increasing work without raising RE — so **COP drops**. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Style) > **Problem:** A refrigeration system using R-134a operates between −10 °C (evaporator) and 40 °C (condenser). The refrigerant is subcooled to 30 °C, and the isentropic efficiency of the compressor is 85 %. Determine: (a) COP, (b) refrigeration effect per kg, (c) compressor work per kg. **Given (from R-134a tables):** $$ \begin{aligned} h_1 &= 392.7 \text{ kJ/kg (saturated vapour at } -10\,°\text{C)} \\ h_{2s} &= 426.5 \text{ kJ/kg (after isentropic compression to } 40\,°\text{C)} \\ h_3^{\text{sat}} &= 256.4 \text{ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at } 40\,°\text{C)} \\ c_{p,\text{liq}} &= 1.45 \text{ kJ/kg·K} \end{aligned} $$ **Step (a) — Subcooling effect:** Subcooling from 40 °C to 30 °C (ΔT = 10 K): $$ h_3 = h_3^{\text{sat}} - c_{p,\text{liq}} \cdot \Delta T = 256.4 - 1.45 \times 10 = 241.9 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ Therefore: $$ h_4 = h_3 = 241.9 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step (b) — Actual compressor work using isentropic efficiency:** $$ h_{2s} - h_1 = 426.5 - 392.7 = 33.8 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ w_c = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_{\text{isen}}} = \frac{33.8}{0.85} = 39.76 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ Thus: $$ h_2 = h_1 + w_c = 392.7 + 39.76 = 432.46 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step (c) — COP calculation:** $$ \text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = 392.7 - 241.9 = 150.8 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ w_c = 39.76 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ \text{COP} = \frac{150.8}{39.76} = 3.79 $$ > **Conversion logic:** Without subcooling, RE would be 392.7 − 256.4 = 136.3 kJ/kg, giving COP = 136.3/39.76 = 3.43. Subcooling **boosted COP by ~10.5 %** with **zero extra work** — a free thermodynamic gain. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix | **Modification** | **ΔRE** | **ΔW** | **ΔCOP** | **Verdict** | |:----------------:|:-------:|:------:|:--------:|:-----------:| | Useful superheating (in evaporator) | ↑ | ↑ (same) | ≈ same | Slight capacity benefit | | Useless superheating (in suction line) | = | ↑ | ↓ | **Always avoid** (insulate suction line) | | Liquid subcooling | ↑ | = | ↑ | **Always desirable** | | Pressure drop in condenser | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | Design larger tubes | | Pressure drop in evaporator | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | Design larger tubes | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic of Actual VCRS with State Points (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[State 1: Superheated Vapour
Low Pressure P1] -->|Compressor
Wc = h2 - h1| B[State 2: Highly Superheated Vapour
High Pressure P2] B -->|Condenser
QR = h2 - h3
with pressure drop| C[State 3: Subcooled Liquid
High Pressure P2] C -->|Expansion Valve
Throttling h4 = h3| D[State 4: Liquid-Vapour Mixture
Low Pressure P1 - delta P] D -->|Evaporator
qE = h1 - h4
with pressure drop| A C -.->|Useful Subcooling
Delta Tsc| C A -.->|Useful Superheat
inside evaporator| A A -.->|Useless Superheat
in suction line| A ``` ## 4.2 P–h Diagram of Actual VCRS (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PH["PRESSURE-ENTHALPY (P-h) DIAGRAM — ACTUAL VCRS"] direction TB L1["Saturation Dome
(Liquid + Vapour Curves)"] N1["Point 1: Superheated Vapour
at P1 = Pevap (low)"] N2["Point 2: Actual Compression
(to right of isentropic)"] N2S["Point 2s: Isentropic Compression
(vertical line)"] N3["Point 3: Subcooled Liquid
below saturation line at P2 = Pcond"] N3sat["Point 3sat: Saturated Liquid
on saturation line"] N4["Point 4: Inside Dome
Liquid-Vapour Mix at P1"] N1 -->|h2s - h1 isentropic| N2S N1 -->|h2 - h1 actual| N2 N2 -->|Heat Rejected QR| N3 N2S -.->|Heat Rejected QRs| N3sat N3 -->|h4 = h3 throttling| N4 N3sat -.->|h4sat = h3sat| N4sat["Point 4sat: Slightly higher than 4"] N4 -->|Heat Absorbed RE = h1 - h4| N1 end style PH fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#2c3e50,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 T–s Diagram Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TS["T-s DIAGRAM — ACTUAL VCRS PATH"] T1["1: Slightly above sat. vap. curve (suction SH)"] T2["2: Right of 2s (actual is to the right, higher entropy)"] T2S["2s: Isentropic (vertical from 1)"] T3["3: Below sat. liquid line (subcooled)"] T4["4: Vertical drop from 3 (throttling, s increases)"] T1 -->|Polytropic / non-isentropic| T2 T1 -.->|Isentropic ideal| T2S T2 -->|Heat rejection at P2| T3 T3 -->|Throttling entropy rises| T4 T4 -->|Heat absorption at P1| T1 end style TS fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#d35400,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual interpretation:** In the **P–h diagram**, the actual compression line tilts right of the isentropic line, showing higher h₂ (more work). In the **T–s diagram**, the actual path moves right of vertical, indicating entropy generation due to irreversibility. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Differentiate between useful superheating and useless superheating in a VCRS. Which is desirable and why?** **Model Answer:** - **Useful superheating** occurs *inside the evaporator*, where the refrigerant vapour temperature rises above saturation at evaporator pressure, increasing the refrigeration effect. - **Useless superheating** occurs *in the suction line* between the evaporator and compressor, where the vapour picks up heat from the surroundings; this raises compressor work without adding to the RE. - **Useful superheating is desirable** because it improves coil dehumidification and slightly raises COP. **Useless superheating is undesirable** because it lowers COP and should be minimised by insulating the suction line. **(3 marks)** ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **What is the effect of liquid subcooling on the COP of a VCRS? Justify with the relevant enthalpy relation.** **Model Answer:** Liquid subcooling decreases the enthalpy of refrigerant at the condenser outlet (h₃ < h₃,sat), so the post-throttling enthalpy h₄ also decreases. Since the compressor work (h₂ − h₁) is unaffected, the refrigeration effect (h₁ − h₄) **increases**, thereby **increasing the COP**. $$ \text{COP}_{\text{subcooled}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4'}{h_2 - h_1} > \text{COP}_{\text{saturated}} $$ Subcooling provides a **free thermodynamic gain** with no additional work input. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the actual vapour compression refrigeration cycle with the help of a **P–h diagram**, clearly showing the effects of subcooling, superheating, and pressure drops. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The actual VCRS differs from the ideal cycle on account of the following real-world effects, all of which are visible on the P–h diagram: 1. **Superheating at compressor inlet (State 1):** Located to the right of the saturated vapour line at pressure P₁. The refrigerant absorbs additional heat in the evaporator (useful SH) and in the suction line (useless SH). 2. **Actual compression (1 → 2):** The compression line slopes to the right of the isentropic line (1 → 2s) because real compression is **not isentropic**. State 2 lies at a higher enthalpy than State 2s, indicating greater work input. 3. **Pressure drop in condenser (2 → 3):** The actual condensation path occurs at a pressure slightly lower than the design pressure due to tube friction. The refrigerant exits at State 3, which is **subcooled** below the saturated liquid line at the local pressure. 4. **Throttling (3 → 4):** The expansion process is isenthalpic (h₄ = h₃) and is shown as a vertical downward line on the P–h chart, ending inside the saturation dome. 5. **Pressure drop in evaporator (4 → 1):** Evaporation occurs at a pressure slightly lower than the compressor suction pressure due to friction. **Effect on COP:** Subcooling **increases** RE, useless superheating **decreases** COP, and pressure drops **increase** compressor work — collectively lowering the cycle's COP compared to the ideal Carnot-equivalent cycle. **(b)** A refrigerant R-134a enters the compressor of a VCRS at −5 °C with a superheat of 5 °C, and leaves at 1.0 MPa and 50 °C. The condenser is subcooled to 30 °C. Find: (i) Enthalpy at compressor inlet and outlet, (ii) Refrigeration effect per kg, (iii) Compressor work per kg, (iv) COP. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** T₁ = −5 °C + 5 °C SH = 0 °C, P₂ = 1.0 MPa, T₂ = 50 °C, T₃ = 30 °C (subcooled). **From R-134a property tables:** - h₁ (superheated vapour at 0 °C) = **398.4 kJ/kg** - h₂ (superheated vapour at 1.0 MPa, 50 °C) = **430.0 kJ/kg** - h₃,sat at 1.0 MPa = 255.6 kJ/kg - c_p,liquid ≈ 1.45 kJ/kg·K **Step (i) Enthalpy at compressor inlet and outlet:** $$ h_1 = 398.4 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_2 = 430.0 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step (ii) Subcooled liquid enthalpy:** $$ h_3 = h_{3,\text{sat}} - c_p \cdot \Delta T = 255.6 - 1.45 \times (T_{\text{sat}} - 30) $$ At 1.0 MPa, T_sat ≈ 39.4 °C, so ΔT = 9.4 K: $$ h_3 = 255.6 - 1.45 \times 9.4 = 241.97 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ h_4 = h_3 = 241.97 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step (iii) Compressor work:** $$ w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 430.0 - 398.4 = 31.6 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step (iv) Refrigeration Effect and COP:** $$ \text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = 398.4 - 241.97 = 156.43 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ \text{COP} = \frac{156.43}{31.6} = 4.95 $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common error:** Forgetting to convert subcooling ΔT to actual (T_sat − T_subcooled), not just the difference. Always read T_sat from the refrigerant table at the given condenser pressure. > - **Common error:** Using h₃,sat instead of h₃ (subcooled) — this underestimates RE and COP. > - **Mark-loss tip:** Always mention **units** (kJ/kg) for every enthalpy value; KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units. --- ### Question B [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **(a)** Derive an expression for the **COP of an actual VCRS** incorporating subcooling, and show diagrammatically (on a T–s chart) how it differs from the ideal cycle. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** Let the saturated liquid enthalpy at condenser pressure be h_f, and the subcooled liquid enthalpy be h₃. By definition of subcooling: $$ h_3 = h_f - c_{p,\text{liq}} \cdot (T_{\text{sat}} - T_3) $$ After throttling: h₄ = h₃. The COP of the actual cycle is: $$ \text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} $$ Substituting h₄ = h₃: $$ \text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1} = \frac{h_1 - h_f + c_{p,\text{liq}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{sc}}}{h_2 - h_1} $$ **Gain in COP due to subcooling (ΔCOP):** $$ \Delta \text{COP} = \frac{c_{p,\text{liq}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{sc}}}{h_2 - h_1} $$ This term is always **positive**, confirming that subcooling always improves COP at no extra work cost. **T–s diagram difference:** - In the **ideal cycle**, condensation ends exactly on the saturated liquid line, and expansion reaches state 4sat (at higher entropy than 4 in actual cycle). - In the **actual cycle**, the path extends below the saturated liquid line into the subcooled region (state 3), and expansion reaches a *lower* entropy point 4 inside the dome — closer to the saturated mixture line, yielding higher RE. **(b)** A refrigeration system uses R-12 with evaporator temperature −15 °C and condenser temperature 30 °C. The refrigerant is subcooled by 8 °C below the condenser saturation temperature. Assuming isentropic compression, calculate: (i) Refrigeration effect per kg, (ii) Compressor work per kg, (iii) COP. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **From R-12 saturation tables:** - At T_evap = −15 °C: h₁ = h_g = 180.2 kJ/kg, s₁ = 0.7085 kJ/kg·K - At T_cond = 30 °C: h_f = 64.5 kJ/kg, h_g = 198.5 kJ/kg, s_g = 0.6855 kJ/kg·K - c_p,liquid (R-12) ≈ 0.90 kJ/kg·K **Step (i) Isentropic compression to 30 °C (P_cond = 0.7449 MPa):** Since s₁ = 0.7085 > s_g at 30 °C (0.6855), state 2s is **superheated** at condenser pressure. Interpolating superheated R-12 tables at P = 0.7449 MPa, s = 0.7085 kJ/kg·K: $$ h_{2s} \approx 201.5 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step (ii) Subcooled liquid:** $$ h_3 = h_f - c_p \cdot \Delta T_{\text{sc}} = 64.5 - 0.90 \times 8 = 57.3 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ h_4 = h_3 = 57.3 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step (iii) Refrigeration Effect:** $$ \text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = 180.2 - 57.3 = 122.9 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step (iv) Compressor Work (isentropic):** $$ w_c = h_{2s} - h_1 = 201.5 - 180.2 = 21.3 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step (v) COP:** $$ \text{COP} = \frac{122.9}{21.3} = 5.77 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Question B):** > - **Critical error:** Many students forget that when s₁ > s_g at condenser pressure, the isentropic exit state is **superheated**, not saturated. Always compare entropies before assuming the discharge state. > - **Common error:** Using h_g (condenser) instead of h_{2s} (actual isentropic exit) for work calculation. > - **Mark protection:** Tabulate all intermediate values clearly with units. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - **Actual VCRS** accounts for superheating, subcooling, and pressure drops — unlike the ideal/theoretical cycle. - **State 1** = superheated vapour (low P); **State 2** = highly superheated vapour (high P, real > ideal). - **State 3** = subcooled liquid (high P); **State 4** = liquid–vapour mix (low P, h₄ = h₃). - **Subcooling** always **increases** RE and **COP** (free gain) — strongly recommended. - **Useful superheating** inside evaporator adds to RE; **useless superheating** in suction line adds to work → reduces COP. - **Pressure drops** in condenser and evaporator **decrease** RE and **increase** compressor work → reduce COP. - **Throttling** is always **isenthalpic** (h₃ = h₄) — never isentropic. - **Isentropic efficiency** of compressor: η_isen = (h₂s − h₁)/(h₂ − h₁). - **COP formula (master it):** COP = (h₁ − h₄)/(h₂ − h₁) — the single most important equation in Module 2. - Always check the **saturated vapour entropy** vs **s₁** to determine if compressor discharge is superheated (very common in R-12, R-134a problems). - **Unit discipline:** All enthalpies in **kJ/kg**, temperatures in **°C** or **K**, pressures in **MPa** or **bar** — examiners deduct marks for sloppy units. - **Draw the P–h diagram** in every long answer — it is a compulsory visual aid and carries 1–2 marks on its own.

topics.title: Limitations of single stage refrigeration systems in achieving ultra-low temperature
Cascade refrigeration system and Trans-critical refrigeration system (Numerical problems to be limited to refrigerants R134a, R1234yf, R410A, R744, R718)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cascade refrigeration system and Trans-critical refrigeration system (Numerical problems to be limited to refrigerants R134a, R1234yf, R410A, R744, R718)

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cascade Refrigeration System

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Cascade Refrigeration System** is a multi-stage (typically two-stage) vapour compression refrigeration arrangement in which two or more independent refrigeration cycles operate at different temperature ranges and are thermally coupled through a cascade (intermediate) heat exchanger. The high-temperature cycle rejects heat absorbed from the low-temperature cycle, eliminating the need for a single refrigerant to span an excessively wide temperature glide that would otherwise cause high compressor discharge temperatures and poor volumetric efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Numerical analysis is restricted to refrigerants **R134a, R1234yf, R410A, R744 (CO₂)** and **R718 (Water)**. Cascade systems almost universally pair R744 (low stage) with R134a or R1234yf (high stage) in modern commercial practice.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **two-story building with two separate AC units** stacked together. The lower floor's AC unit cools the upper floor's AC condenser (instead of the outside air). The lower AC works *very* hard to maintain extremely cold temperatures (−40 °C), while the upper AC handles the "intermediate" heat rejection at normal ambient levels. Neither unit has to span the entire impossible temperature gap from −40 °C to +45 °C on its own.

---

## 1.2 Trans-Critical Refrigeration System

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Trans-Critical Refrigeration System** is a vapour compression cycle in which the high-pressure side of the refrigerant operates in the **superheated region above the critical point**, while the low-pressure side remains in the two-phase sub-critical region. The isothermal compression process of the classical cycle is replaced by a high-pressure gas cooling process, and throttling replaces the conventional condensation process. This system is the industry-standard for **Carbon Dioxide (R744)** because CO₂ has a critical temperature of **31.1 °C** — lower than typical condenser ambient temperatures, making traditional sub-critical condensation impossible in warm climates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Point of R744:** Pressure = **73.8 bar**, Temperature = **31.1 °C**, Density = **467 kg/m³**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **pressure cooker** that never lets steam condense back into water inside the cooker — only when the steam is suddenly released through a narrow valve does it cool dramatically (gas cooler effect). This is exactly what happens in a trans-critical CO₂ cycle: the gas is compressed *beyond* its condensation threshold, and cooling is achieved by **gas cooling** followed by **sudden expansion** (like opening the pressure cooker valve), which produces deep refrigeration without any phase change at the high side.

---

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Evaporator Temp (LT) | $T_{evap,LT}$ | **−40 °C** |
| Cascade HX Temp | $T_{int}$ | **0 °C to −5 °C** |
| Condenser Temp (HT) | $T_{cond}$ | **35 °C to 45 °C** |
| Critical Pressure of CO₂ | $P_{cr}$ | **73.8 bar** |
| Critical Temp of CO₂ | $T_{cr}$ | **31.1 °C** |
| Gas Cooler Pressure (optimal) | $P_{gc,opt}$ | **90 – 110 bar** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cascade Refrigeration System — Operating Principle

The system consists of two **independent VCRS loops** connected through a **cascade heat exchanger (CHX)** which acts as:
- The **condenser** of the Low-Temperature (LT) cycle.
- The **evaporator** of the High-Temperature (HT) cycle.

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow
- **Step 1 (LT Compression):** LT compressor takes low-pressure vapour from LT evaporator → compresses it.
- **Step 2 (LT Heat Rejection to CHX):** Hot LT vapour condenses in CHX at intermediate temperature $T_{int}$, releasing heat $\dot{Q}_{int,LT}$.
- **Step 3 (LT Expansion):** Liquid LT refrigerant expands through LT expansion valve → cold two-phase mixture at LT evaporator.
- **Step 4 (LT Evaporation):** LT refrigerant absorbs load $\dot{Q}_L$ at $T_{evap,LT}$.
- **Step 5 (HT Evaporation in CHX):** HT refrigerant evaporates inside CHX at $T_{int}$, absorbing $\dot{Q}_{int,HT}$.
- **Step 6 (HT Compression):** HT compressor compresses HT vapour.
- **Step 7 (HT Condensation):** HT vapour condenses in ambient condenser at $T_{cond}$.
- **Step 8 (HT Expansion):** HT liquid expands through HT expansion valve.

### Energy Balance at Cascade Heat Exchanger

For **steady-state, adiabatic** operation with no heat loss:

$$\dot{Q}_{int,LT} = \dot{Q}_{int,HT}$$

$$\dot{m}_{LT} \cdot (h_{2,LT} - h_{3,LT}) = \dot{m}_{HT} \cdot (h_{5,HT} - h_{8,HT})$$

The **mass flow ratio** governing the relationship:

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{LT}}{\dot{m}_{HT}} = \frac{h_{5,HT} - h_{8,HT}}{h_{2,LT} - h_{3,LT}}$$

### Coefficient of Performance (COP) of Cascade System

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{cascade} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}_{LT} + \dot{W}_{HT}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{m}_{LT}(h_{2,LT}-h_{1,LT}) + \dot{m}_{HT}(h_{6,HT}-h_{5,HT})}}$$

**Conditional Form (when CHX is perfectly balanced):**

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{cascade} = \frac{\text{COP}_{LT} \times \text{COP}_{HT}}{\text{COP}_{LT} + \text{COP}_{HT} + 1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant form assumes the cooling effect of HT cycle equals the heat rejection of LT cycle. It is the **most tested form in KTU University Exams**.

---

## 2.2 Trans-Critical Refrigeration System — Operating Principle

Unlike sub-critical cycles, CO₂ **never condenses** at the high side because the system pressure exceeds $P_{cr}$ = 73.8 bar and ambient air cannot bring it below 31.1 °C during compression cooling alone.

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow
- **Step 1:** CO₂ vapour compressed isentropically to a **high pressure** $P_{gc}$ > 73.8 bar.
- **Step 2 (Gas Cooler):** Superheated CO₂ at high pressure is **cooled** (not condensed) by ambient air. Useful refrigeration effect emerges from sensible cooling of high-pressure gas.
- **Step 3 (Expansion Valve):** High-pressure CO₂ expanded isenthalpically to evaporator pressure. The throttling generates a large **temperature drop** because of CO₂'s high Joule–Thomson coefficient.
- **Step 4 (Evaporator):** CO₂ absorbs heat $\dot{Q}_L$ at $T_{evap}$.

### Why Optimum Pressure Matters

COP is a **strong function** of $P_{gc}$. Too low → insufficient pressure ratio. Too high → excessive compressor work. The **optimum discharge pressure** $P_{gc,opt}$ maximises COP for a given $T_{evap}$ and gas cooler exit temperature $T_{gc,out}$.

**Empirical KTU Relation (for R744):**

$$P_{gc,opt} \approx 2.4 \times P_{evap} + 10 \text{ bar}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q}_{int,LT} = \dot{Q}_{int,HT}$ | Cascade heat exchanger energy balance |
| 2 | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_{LT}}{\dot{m}_{HT}} = \dfrac{h_{5,HT} - h_{8,HT}}{h_{2,LT} - h_{3,LT}}$ | Mass flow ratio between stages |
| 3 | $\text{COP}_{cascade} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{LT}\cdot \text{COP}_{HT}}{\text{COP}_{LT}+\text{COP}_{HT}+1}$ | Cascade COP (matched CHX) |
| 4 | $\text{COP}_{LT} = \dfrac{h_{1,LT}-h_{4,LT}}{h_{2,LT}-h_{1,LT}}$ | LT stage COP |
| 5 | $\text{COP}_{HT} = \dfrac{h_{5,HT}-h_{8,HT}}{h_{6,HT}-h_{5,HT}}$ | HT stage COP |
| 6 | $\text{COP}_{trans} = \dfrac{h_{1}-h_{4}}{h_{2}-h_{1}}$ | Trans-critical COP (gas cooler exit at point 3) |
| 7 | $P_{gc,opt} \approx 2.4 \cdot P_{evap} + 10$ bar | R744 optimum gas cooler pressure |
| 8 | $\text{HPHX Recovery} \Rightarrow \text{COP}_{trans,with\,HPHX} > \text{COP}_{trans,basic}$ | Internal heat exchanger gain |
| 9 | $\eta_{vol} = 1 - C \cdot \left[\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} - 1\right]$ | Volumetric efficiency (used in numericals) |
| 10 | $T_{cr}(\text{R744}) = 31.1\,°C, \; P_{cr}(\text{R744}) = 73.8\,\text{bar}$ | Critical constants — *MUST memorise* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse $h_{4}$ (after expansion) with $h_{3}$ (after gas cooler). In trans-critical cycles, point **3 is supercritical gas**, NOT saturated liquid. This is the single most common valuation mistake.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **Cascade (R744/R134a)** | Supermarket freezers, industrial process chilling (−40 °C to −80 °C) |
| **Trans-Critical R744** | Heat pump water heaters, commercial refrigeration in tropical climates |
| **Cascade with R718 (water)** | Food freeze-drying, pharmaceutical cold storage |
| **Trans-Critical + HPHX** | Modern eco-friendly supermarket refrigeration (CO₂ only) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: COP of Cascade System from First Principles

Let the cooling load be $\dot{Q}_L$ delivered at the LT evaporator.

**Step 1 — LT Cycle Cooling Effect:**
$$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_{LT} \cdot (h_{1,LT} - h_{4,LT})$$

**Step 2 — HT Cycle Cooling Effect at Cascade HX:**
$$\dot{Q}_{CHX} = \dot{m}_{HT} \cdot (h_{5,HT} - h_{8,HT})$$

**Step 3 — LT Cycle Heat Rejection at Cascade HX:**
$$\dot{Q}_{CHX} = \dot{m}_{LT} \cdot (h_{2,LT} - h_{3,LT})$$

**Step 4 — Equating Step 2 and Step 3 (Energy Balance):**
$$\dot{m}_{LT} \cdot (h_{2,LT} - h_{3,LT}) = \dot{m}_{HT} \cdot (h_{5,HT} - h_{8,HT})$$

**Step 5 — Solving for Mass Flow Ratio:**
$$\frac{\dot{m}_{LT}}{\dot{m}_{HT}} = \frac{h_{5,HT} - h_{8,HT}}{h_{2,LT} - h_{3,LT}}$$

**Step 6 — Total Compressor Work Input:**
$$\dot{W}_{tot} = \dot{W}_{LT} + \dot{W}_{HT} = \dot{m}_{LT}(h_{2,LT}-h_{1,LT}) + \dot{m}_{HT}(h_{6,HT}-h_{5,HT})$$

**Step 7 — Substituting and Dividing Numerator & Denominator by $\dot{m}_{HT}$:**

$$\text{COP}_{cascade} = \frac{(h_{1,LT}-h_{4,LT}) \cdot \dfrac{\dot{m}_{LT}}{\dot{m}_{HT}}}{(h_{2,LT}-h_{1,LT}) \cdot \dfrac{\dot{m}_{LT}}{\dot{m}_{HT}} + (h_{6,HT}-h_{5,HT})}$$

**Step 8 — Using the Identity $\text{COP}_{LT} = \dfrac{h_{1,LT}-h_{4,LT}}{h_{2,LT}-h_{1,LT}}$ and similar for HT, after algebraic simplification:**

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{cascade} = \frac{\text{COP}_{LT} \cdot \text{COP}_{HT}}{\text{COP}_{LT} + \text{COP}_{HT} + 1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "+1" in the denominator represents the **thermal coupling inefficiency** between the two cycles through the cascade heat exchanger.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Trans-Critical COP and Optimum Pressure Condition

### COP of Basic Trans-Critical Cycle
$$\text{COP}_{tc} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

Where:
- $h_1$ = enthalpy at evaporator exit (saturated or slightly superheated vapour).
- $h_2$ = enthalpy at compressor exit (function of $P_{gc}$).
- $h_3$ = enthalpy at gas cooler exit (function of $P_{gc}$ and $T_{gc,out}$).
- $h_4 = h_3$ (isenthalpic throttling).

### Optimum Gas Cooler Pressure Criterion

For COP to be maximum with respect to $P_{gc}$ at constant $T_{evap}$ and $T_{gc,out}$:

$$\frac{\partial \text{COP}_{tc}}{\partial P_{gc}} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial P_{gc}}\left[\frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}\right] = 0$$

Since $h_4 = h_3$ depends on $P_{gc}$, applying chain rule:

$$\frac{(h_2 - h_1)\dfrac{\partial h_3}{\partial P_{gc}} - (h_1 - h_3)\dfrac{\partial h_2}{\partial P_{gc}}}{(h_2 - h_1)^2} = 0$$

$$\Longrightarrow (h_2 - h_1)\cdot\frac{\partial h_3}{\partial P_{gc}} = (h_1 - h_3)\cdot\frac{\partial h_2}{\partial P_{gc}}$$

The numerical solution of this equation for R744 yields the **empirical correlation:**

$$P_{gc,opt} \approx 2.4 \cdot P_{evap} + 10 \text{ bar}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Numerical problems typically **give** $P_{gc,opt}$ (or $T_{gc,out}$) directly. You are not expected to solve the differential — just apply the COP formula and compute work/load/COP.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component-by-Component Comparison

| Component | Sub-Critical Cycle | Cascade System | Trans-Critical (R744) |
|---|---|---|---|
| High-side heat rejection | Condensation (latent + sensible) | Same in HT cycle | **Gas cooling only (sensible)** |
| Pressure ratio | 4 – 8 | Each stage: 3 – 5 | **5 – 12** |
| Refrigerant state after cooler | Saturated liquid | Same in HT | **Supercritical fluid** |
| Expansion device | Throttle valve | Throttle valve | Throttle valve (large $\Delta T$) |
| COP for $T_{evap} = -10\,°C$ | 2.5 – 3.5 | 2.0 – 2.8 | **2.5 – 3.2 (with HPHX)** |
| COP for $T_{evap} = -40\,°C$ | 1.0 – 1.4 | **1.5 – 2.0** | 1.0 – 1.3 |
| Compressor discharge temp | High (>120 °C) | Moderate (<90 °C) | Very high (>130 °C) |
| Use of cascade HX | None | Mandatory | None |
| Refrigerant compatibility | Any | Pair carefully | Only R744 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cascade Refrigeration System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LT_Cycle["LOW-TEMPERATURE CYCLE - Refrigerant R744"]
        A1["1: LT Evaporator Exit - Saturated Vapour"] --> A2["2: LT Compressor - Isentropic Compression"]
        A2 --> A3["3: LT Condenser in CHX - Saturated Liquid"]
        A3 --> A4["4: LT Expansion Valve - Flash Gas"]
        A4 --> A5["5: LT Evaporator Inlet - Two-Phase"]
        A5 --> A1
    end

    CHX["CASCADE HEAT EXCHANGER - Couples LT heat rejection to HT evaporation"]

    subgraph HT_Cycle["HIGH-TEMPERATURE CYCLE - Refrigerant R134a or R1234yf"]
        B1["6: HT Evaporator in CHX - Saturated Vapour"] --> B2["7: HT Compressor - Isentropic Compression"]
        B2 --> B3["8: HT Condenser - Saturated Liquid to Ambient"]
        B3 --> B4["9: HT Expansion Valve"]
        B4 --> B5["10: HT Evaporator Inlet - Two-Phase"]
        B5 --> B1
    end

    A3 -. Heat rejected to CHX .-> CHX
    CHX -. Heat absorbed by HT cycle .-> B1
    A5 -. Cools Load QL .-> LOAD["Refrigeration Load - Minus 40 deg C"]
    B3 -. Heat rejected to ambient .-> AMB["Ambient Air 35 to 45 deg C"]
```

## 4.2 Trans-Critical Refrigeration System (R744) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["1: Evaporator Exit - Low Pressure Vapour T_evap"] --> P2["2: Compressor - Isentropic Compression to P_gc above 73.8 bar"]
    P2 --> P3["3: Gas Cooler Exit - Supercritical Fluid T_gc_out"]
    P3 --> P4["4: Expansion Valve - Isenthalpic Throttling - Large Temperature Drop"]
    P4 --> P5["5: Evaporator Inlet - Two Phase at Low Pressure"]
    P5 --> P6["Evaporator Absorbs Heat QL from Load"]
    P6 --> P1

    P2 -. Hot supercritical gas .-> P3
    P3 -. Sensible cooling by ambient .-> AMB2["Ambient Air 25 to 35 deg C"]
    P5 -. Cooling effect QL .-> LOAD2["Refrigeration Load Minus 5 to Minus 40 deg C"]
```

## 4.3 P-h Diagram — Trans-Critical vs Sub-Critical Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SubCritical["SUB-CRITICAL CYCLE p-h DIAGRAM"]
        SC1["1 Saturated Vapour"] --> SC2["2 Superheated Vapour After Compression"]
        SC2 --> SC3["3 Saturated Liquid After Condensation"]
        SC3 --> SC4["4 Two-Phase After Expansion"]
        SC4 --> SC1
    end

    subgraph TransCritical["TRANS-CRITICAL CYCLE p-h DIAGRAM - R744"]
        TC1["1 Saturated Vapour T_evap"] --> TC2["2 Superheated at P_gc well above P_cr"]
        TC2 --> TC3["3 Supercritical Fluid After Gas Cooling"]
        TC3 --> TC4["4 Two-Phase After Throttling"]
        TC4 --> TC1
    end

    CRIT["CRITICAL POINT of R744 - 73.8 bar and 31.1 deg C - Boundary between two domains"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **critical point dome boundary** in the trans-critical diagram is the visual key: state point 3 lies **outside and to the right of the saturation dome**, confirming the refrigerant never condenses.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a cascade refrigeration system. Why is it preferred over a single-stage system for very low temperature applications?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A cascade refrigeration system consists of **two or more independent VCRS cycles** thermally coupled through an intermediate **cascade heat exchanger (CHX)**. The low-temperature (LT) cycle operates at very low evaporator temperature, while the high-temperature (HT) cycle rejects heat to ambient.

**Reasons for preference over single-stage:**
- (i) **Reduces compressor pressure ratio** in each cycle, improving volumetric efficiency.
- (ii) **Lowers discharge temperature**, preventing lubricant breakdown.
- (iii) Allows use of **two different refrigerants**, each optimised for its temperature range (e.g., R744 below, R134a above).
- (iv) **Higher overall COP** for temperature lifts exceeding 60 °C.

---

### **Q2. State the differences between a trans-critical and a sub-critical refrigeration cycle.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sub-Critical Cycle | Trans-Critical Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| High-side process | Condensation (latent + sensible) | Gas cooling (sensible only) |
| State after cooler | Saturated liquid | **Supercritical fluid** |
| Operating pressure | Below $P_{cr}$ | **Above $P_{cr}$** |
| Refrigerant used | Any | **R744 typically** |
| Throttling drop | Moderate | **Very large (high Joule-Thomson coefficient)** |
| COP sensitivity | Low | **Strong function of $P_{gc}$** |

---

## 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Cascade System Numerical** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A cascade refrigeration system uses **R744 in the LT cycle** and **R134a in the HT cycle**. The LT evaporator temperature is **−40 °C**, the cascade heat exchanger temperature is **−5 °C**, and the HT condenser temperature is **40 °C**. 

Assuming both cycles are ideally dry (saturated vapour at compressor inlet) and isentropic compression:
- LT: $h_1$ = 247 kJ/kg, $h_2$ = 280 kJ/kg, $h_3$ = 70 kJ/kg
- HT: $h_5$ = 247 kJ/kg, $h_6$ = 270 kJ/kg, $h_8$ = 110 kJ/kg

**Calculate:**
**(a)** Mass flow ratio $\dot{m}_{LT} / \dot{m}_{HT}$ and the **HT cycle COP**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Overall cascade system **COP** and **total work input per kg of LT refrigerant**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a): Mass Flow Ratio & HT COP

**Step 1 — Cooling effect per kg of LT refrigerant in CHX:**
$$q_{CHX,LT} = h_2 - h_3 = 280 - 70 = 210 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Cooling effect per kg of HT refrigerant in CHX:**
$$q_{CHX,HT} = h_5 - h_8 = 247 - 110 = 137 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Equating heat transfer in CHX:**
$$\dot{m}_{LT}(210) = \dot{m}_{HT}(137)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\dot{m}_{LT}}{\dot{m}_{HT}} = \frac{137}{210} = 0.652}$$

**Step 4 — COP of HT cycle:**
$$\text{COP}_{HT} = \frac{h_5 - h_8}{h_6 - h_5} = \frac{247 - 110}{270 - 247} = \frac{137}{23} = 5.957$$

#### Part (b): Cascade COP & Work

**Step 5 — LT cooling effect (refrigeration provided):**
$$q_L = h_1 - h_4 = h_1 - h_3 = 247 - 70 = 177 \text{ kJ/kg of LT refrigerant}$$

**Step 6 — LT compressor work:**
$$w_{LT} = h_2 - h_1 = 280 - 247 = 33 \text{ kJ/kg of LT refrigerant}$$

**Step 7 — LT cycle COP:**
$$\text{COP}_{LT} = \frac{177}{33} = 5.364$$

**Step 8 — Cascade COP:**
$$\text{COP}_{cascade} = \frac{\text{COP}_{LT}\cdot \text{COP}_{HT}}{\text{COP}_{LT}+\text{COP}_{HT}+1}$$

$$\text{COP}_{cascade} = \frac{5.364 \times 5.957}{5.364 + 5.957 + 1} = \frac{31.95}{12.32}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{cascade} = 2.594}$$

**Step 9 — Total work per kg of LT refrigerant:**
$$w_{tot} = w_{LT} + w_{HT} \cdot \frac{\dot{m}_{HT}}{\dot{m}_{LT}} = 33 + 23 \times \frac{1}{0.652}$$

$$w_{tot} = 33 + 23 \times 1.533 = 33 + 35.26 = 68.26 \text{ kJ/kg of LT refrigerant}$$

$$\boxed{w_{tot} = 68.26 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Trans-Critical Numerical** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A trans-critical CO₂ (R744) refrigeration system operates between evaporator temperature **−10 °C** and gas cooler exit temperature **35 °C**. The compressor isentropically compresses vapour from **30 bar to 90 bar**. Given:
- $h_1$ = 360 kJ/kg, $h_2$ = 480 kJ/kg, $h_3$ = 250 kJ/kg

**Calculate:**
**(a)** Refrigerating effect, **compressor work**, and **COP**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If cooling capacity is **5 kW**, find the **mass flow rate** of CO₂ and **compressor power**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a): RE, Work, COP

**Step 1 — Enthalpy after expansion (isenthalpic):**
$$h_4 = h_3 = 250 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Refrigerating Effect (RE):**
$$\text{RE} = h_1 - h_4 = 360 - 250 = 110 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compressor Work:**
$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 480 - 360 = 120 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — COP:**
$$\text{COP}_{tc} = \frac{\text{RE}}{w_c} = \frac{110}{120}$$

$$\boxed{\text{COP}_{tc} = 0.917}$$

#### Part (b): Mass Flow & Compressor Power

**Step 5 — Mass flow rate:**
$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{RE}} = \frac{5 \text{ kW}}{110 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 0.0455 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 — Compressor Power:**
$$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \times w_c = 0.0455 \times 120 = 5.454 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Verification via COP:**
$$\dot{W}_c = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{COP}} = \frac{5}{0.917} = 5.453 \text{ kW} \;\;\checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 0.0455 \text{ kg/s}, \quad \dot{W}_c = 5.45 \text{ kW}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> 
> 1. **In Trans-Critical cycles: NEVER write "condenser" — always write "GAS COOLER".** Using the wrong term costs 1 mark automatically.
> 
> 2. **In Cascade: ALWAYS show the energy balance at CHX** ($\dot{Q}_{int,LT} = \dot{Q}_{int,HT}$) before computing the mass flow ratio. Skipping this step loses 2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Don't confuse $h_3$ with saturated liquid enthalpy** in trans-critical cycles. Point 3 is **supercritical**, and its enthalpy is read from superheated R744 tables (above $P_{cr}$), NOT from saturation tables.
> 
> 4. **For COP formula** in cascade, the "+1" in the denominator is often missed by students. Mark deduction: 2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Refrigerant pairing** must be justified in design questions. Examiner expects: R744 LT + R134a/R1234yf HT, NOT two identical refrigerants.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **cascade system** uses **two independent VCRS cycles** coupled via an intermediate heat exchanger.
- ✅ Cascade is preferred when **temperature lift > 60 °C** or when evaporator is **colder than −30 °C**.
- ✅ The **mass flow ratio** $\dot{m}_{LT}/\dot{m}_{HT}$ is dictated by the **CHX energy balance**.
- ✅ Cascade COP formula (matched CHX): $\text{COP}_{cascade} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{LT}\cdot\text{COP}_{HT}}{\text{COP}_{LT}+\text{COP}_{HT}+1}$.
- ✅ **Trans-critical cycle** uses **R744 (CO₂)** exclusively because its critical temperature (31.1 °C) is below typical ambient.
- ✅ In trans-critical: **high-pressure side has NO phase change** — only sensible gas cooling.
- ✅ **Gas cooler exit (point 3)** is supercritical fluid — read enthalpy from **supercritical R744 tables**.
- ✅ Trans-critical COP is **highly sensitive to $P_{gc}$** — must optimise for maximum COP.
- ✅ Empirical optimum: $P_{gc,opt} \approx 2.4 P_{evap} + 10$ bar (R744).
- ✅ **Adding an Internal Heat Exchanger (HPHX/IHX)** improves trans-critical COP by 15–25 %.
- ✅ **KTU-approved refrigerants**: R134a, R1234yf, R410A, R744, R718 — no other refrigerants in numericals.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions**: saturated vapour at compressor inlet, isentropic compression, no pressure drop in lines.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of vapour absorption system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principle of vapour absorption system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** is a heat-operated refrigeration cycle that produces cooling by utilizing a *thermodynamic property change* in a working fluid, where the **mechanical compression work** of a conventional vapour compression cycle is replaced by a **thermal compression process** carried out inside an **absorber**, a **pump**, and a **generator** (also called the *boiler* or *reactor*).

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System may be defined as:

> *"A refrigeration system in which a secondary working fluid (the **absorbent**) physically absorbs the primary refrigerant vapour at low pressure, and the resultant rich solution is pumped to a high-pressure generator where heat input liberates the refrigerant vapour, thereby simulating the function of a mechanical compressor through a purely thermal–chemical process."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> The principle of a Vapour Absorption System constitutes a *core fundamental* under **Module 2** of PEMET751. The student is expected to draw the schematic, state the working fluid pair, explain each component, derive the maximum and actual Coefficient of Performance (COP), and solve numerical problems comparing VARS with the equivalent Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS).

### 1.2 Common Working Fluid Pairs (Binary Mixtures)

| Refrigerant | Absorbent | Common Name | Type of System |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ammonia (NH₃) | Water (H₂O) | Aqua-Ammonia System | Used for low temperatures (below 0 °C) |
| Water (H₂O) | Lithium Bromide (LiBr) | Water-LiBr System | Used for air-conditioning (above 0 °C) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **NH₃–H₂O pair**, ammonia is the *refrigerant* (low boiling point ≈ −33.3 °C) and water is the *absorbent* (high boiling point ≈ 100 °C). In the **H₂O–LiBr pair**, water is the *refrigerant* and lithium bromide is the *absorbent* because LiBr is highly *hygroscopic* and has an extremely low vapour pressure at the operating temperatures of an air-conditioning plant.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rechargeable Sponge"

Imagine a *dry kitchen sponge* dropped into a small puddle of water on a kitchen counter:

- The sponge **soaks up** the water (this is the **absorber** doing its job).
- A child can *easily* lift the heavy, wet sponge and place it on a sunny window-sill (this is the **pump** doing its job — moving liquid is energetically cheap).
- The Sun's heat **evaporates** the water out of the sponge (this is the **generator** doing its job — heat input drives the refrigerant out).
- The evaporated water can then be **condensed** back to liquid (this is the **condenser**), **expanded** through a small nozzle (this is the **expansion valve**), and finally made to **evaporate** in a cold tray, drawing heat from the surroundings (this is the **evaporator**, which gives us the refrigeration effect).

The brilliance of the analogy is that the only "work" supplied to move the fluid is the *tiny pump work* required to push a liquid, instead of the *huge compressor work* required to push a vapour. The energy needed to actually lift the refrigerant to high pressure comes from **heat**, not from electricity.

### 1.4 Primary Functional Components (The Six Pillars of VARS)

1. **Absorber** – Low-pressure vessel where the refrigerant vapour is *chemically dissolved* into the absorbent, releasing the *heat of absorption* (Qₐ) to the surroundings.
2. **Pump** – A small *liquid pump* (typically centrifugal or reciprocating) that raises the pressure of the rich solution from the absorber pressure to the generator pressure, supplying a small work input (Wₚ).
3. **Generator** (Boiler/Reactifier) – High-pressure vessel where external heat (Q_g) is supplied (from steam, a gas flame, solar energy, or waste heat) to *boil off* the refrigerant from the rich solution.
4. **Condenser** – High-pressure side, where the pure refrigerant vapour *rejects heat* (Q_c) to a cooling medium and condenses to a saturated or subcooled liquid.
5. **Expansion Valve (Throttling Device)** – Drops the pressure of the condensed refrigerant from condenser pressure to evaporator pressure — analogous to the VCRS throttling process.
6. **Evaporator** – Low-pressure side, where the refrigerant *absorbs heat* (Q_e) from the refrigerated space and produces the desired cooling effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** Unlike the Vapour Compression Refrigeration System where a single shaft-driven *mechanical compressor* is the heart of the cycle, in a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System the "compressor" is actually a **three-component thermal assembly** consisting of the **Absorber + Solution Pump + Generator**. Examiners frequently award bonus marks when students correctly identify this replacement.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The principle of operation of a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System can be described by following the refrigerant and the absorbent through their independent closed loops:

**Loop A — Refrigerant Loop (Pure Refrigerant Path):**
- The pure refrigerant vapour leaving the **generator** is at high pressure and high temperature (Point 1).
- It enters the **condenser**, where it rejects heat (Q_c) to the cooling medium and condenses to a saturated liquid (Point 2).
- The high-pressure liquid passes through the **expansion valve**, where it is throttled to a low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture (Point 3).
- This two-phase mixture enters the **evaporator**, where it absorbs the refrigeration load (Q_e) from the cold space and fully evaporates to a saturated or slightly superheated vapour (Point 4).
- The low-pressure refrigerant vapour then enters the **absorber**, where it meets the weak (dilute) absorbent solution.

**Loop B — Solution Loop (Absorbent + Refrigerant Mixture Path):**
- Inside the **absorber**, the refrigerant vapour is *physically absorbed* into the weak solution, forming a *strong (rich) solution*. The heat of absorption (Qₐ) is rejected to the surroundings (often with the help of a cooling water coil).
- The strong solution leaves the absorber (Point 7) and is pressurised by the **solution pump** (Wₚ) to the high pressure of the generator (Point 8).
- Optionally, the pressurised strong solution is passed through a **solution heat exchanger (SHX)** to recover thermal energy from the *hot weak solution* returning from the generator.
- The high-pressure strong solution enters the **generator**, where external heat (Q_g) drives out the refrigerant vapour, leaving behind a *weak (lean) solution* (Point 6).
- The weak solution is throttled (Point 5) back to the absorber pressure, completing the solution loop.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Solution Heat Exchanger (SHX)** is an energy-recovery sub-assembly that pre-heats the strong solution going to the generator using the heat of the hot weak solution leaving the generator. Although the SHX does **not** alter the COP of the *ideal* cycle (since it conserves energy), it **substantially improves the practical COP** by reducing the external heat input (Q_g) required at the generator.

### 2.2 The Maximum (Ideal) Coefficient of Performance

For a *reversible* Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System, applying the **Second Law of Thermodynamics (entropy balance)** to the combined absorber-generator heat exchange process yields the following elegant result:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{max}} \;=\; \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}$$

where
- $T_E$ = Absolute temperature of the **evaporator** (and the refrigerated space),
- $T_A$ = Absolute temperature of the **absorber**,
- $T_G$ = Absolute temperature of the **generator**.

When the absorber is at the same temperature as the evaporator and the generator is at the same temperature as the condenser (which is the usual case for an *ideal simple VARS*), then $T_A = T_E$ and $T_G = T_C$, which reduces the expression to:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} \;=\; \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}$$
