## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
*State the working principle of a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System. Why is it preferred over a Vapour Compression Refrigeration System in certain applications?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) produces the refrigeration effect by *thermally compressing* the refrigerant vapour using a binary working fluid pair consisting of a *refrigerant* and an *absorbent*. The pure refrigerant vapour from the evaporator is *absorbed* by the weak solution in the absorber, the rich solution is *pumped* to the generator where external heat *boils off* the refrigerant, the vapour is *condensed* and *throttled* to the evaporator, completing the cycle. The mechanical compressor of the VCRS is replaced by an *absorber + pump + generator* assembly. VARS is preferred when *low-grade or waste heat* (solar, geothermal, exhaust steam) is abundantly available, when *silent operation* is required, and in *remote or off-grid* locations where electrical power is scarce. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Compare the Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System and the Vapour Compression Refrigeration System with respect to (i) primary energy input, (ii) moving parts, (iii) COP, and (iv) typical working fluid.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
(i) **Primary energy input:** VARS uses *heat* (Q_g) supplied to the generator; VCRS uses *mechanical work* (W_c) supplied to the compressor.
(ii) **Moving parts:** VARS has only a *small liquid pump*; VCRS has a *compressor* (reciprocating, scroll, screw, or centrifugal) — a major source of noise and wear.
(iii) **COP:** The *ideal* COP of VARS equals the Carnot COP, equal in principle to the *ideal* VCRS. The *practical* COP of VARS is *lower* (≈ 30–50 % of equivalent VCRS) due to irreversibilities in the absorber and generator. VCRS has a *higher practical COP* of 2.5–4.5 for typical applications.
(iv) **Typical working fluid:** VARS uses a *binary mixture* (NH₃–H₂O or H₂O–LiBr); VCRS uses a *single pure refrigerant* (R-134a, R-410A, NH₃, R-22, etc.). **(3 Marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing generator with condenser:** Many students interchange $Q_g$ and $Q_c$. Remember: $Q_g$ is the *heat input at the generator*; $Q_c$ is the *heat rejected at the condenser*. Swapping them will give a wrong sign and an impossible COP.
> 2. **Forgetting the rectifier (NH₃–H₂O system):** The rectifier rejects additional heat $Q_R$ to remove water from the NH₃ vapour. Omitting this leads to a *mass balance* error and a wrong solution.
> 3. **Using $T$ in °C instead of K:** All thermodynamic temperature ratios *must* be in Kelvin. A temperature difference of $5\,\text{K}$ is $5\,\text{K}$, not $5\,°\text{C}$ (the same, but $25\,°\text{C} = 298\,\text{K}$).
> 4. **Neglecting the solution heat exchanger (SHX):** In an *ideal* analysis, the SHX conserves energy, but in a *practical* analysis, ignoring it overestimates the heat input $Q_g$ and underestimates the COP.
> 5. **Sign convention in the First Law:** The energy balance is $Q_E + Q_G + W_p = Q_C + Q_A$ (or its equivalent), *not* the reverse. Wrong sign convention is the single largest source of zero-mark scripts in KTU examinations.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a **Simple Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System** using the **NH₃–H₂O** working fluid pair. Label all state points and state the function of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **maximum Coefficient of Performance** of a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System operating with the evaporator at $T_E$, absorber at $T_A$, generator at $T_G$, and condenser at $T_C$. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Schematic Description (3 Marks):**
A simple NH₃–H₂O VARS consists of the following six components, interconnected in a closed loop. The refrigerant (NH₃) flows in one loop and the absorbent solution (H₂O + NH₃ mixture) flows in a parallel loop.

1. **Generator (Boiler):** External heat $Q_g$ is supplied (typically from low-pressure steam, a gas burner, or solar collector). The strong solution (rich in NH₃) entering the generator is boiled, liberating pure NH₃ vapour, which exits at the top. The remaining weak solution (rich in H₂O) exits at the bottom.
2. **Condenser:** The high-pressure NH₃ vapour from the generator enters the condenser, where it rejects heat $Q_c$ to a cooling medium (cooling water or ambient air) and condenses to a saturated liquid at high pressure $P_G$.
3. **Expansion Valve:** The high-pressure liquid NH₃ is throttled to the low evaporator pressure $P_E$. The throttling process is isenthalpic.
4. **Evaporator:** The low-pressure liquid NH₃ absorbs heat $Q_e$ from the refrigerated space, evaporates, and exits as a low-pressure vapour.
5. **Absorber:** The low-pressure NH₃ vapour from the evaporator is *absorbed* by the weak solution returning from the generator, forming the strong solution. The heat of absorption $Q_a$ is rejected to a cooling water coil.
6. **Solution Pump:** The strong solution from the absorber is pumped to the high generator pressure, supplying a small work input $W_p$.

**Working Description (3 Marks):**
The pure NH₃ vapour is liberated in the **generator** by boiling the strong solution. It is **condensed**, **expanded** to low pressure, and **evaporated** in the evaporator to produce the cooling effect. The low-pressure NH₃ vapour is then **absorbed** in the absorber to reform the strong solution, which is **pumped** back to the generator. The cycle repeats continuously. The work input is *only* the small pump work; the major energy input is *heat* at the generator.

**State-Point Identification (1 Mark):**
- State 1: Generator exit, pure NH₃ vapour, $P_G$, $T_G$.
- State 2: Condenser exit, saturated liquid NH₃, $P_G$, $T_C$.
- State 3: Evaporator inlet, two-phase NH₃, $P_E$, $T_E$.
- State 4: Evaporator exit, saturated NH₃ vapour, $P_E$, $T_E$.
- State 5: After solution expansion valve, weak solution, $P_E$, $T_A$.
- State 6: Generator exit (solution), weak solution, $P_G$, $T_G$.
- State 7: Absorber exit, strong solution, $P_E$, $T_A$.
- State 7′: Pump exit, strong solution, $P_G$, $T_A$.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Assumptions (2 Marks):**
1. The cycle is *reversible* — no entropy generation in any process.
2. The working fluid pair is NH₃ (refrigerant) and H₂O (absorbent).
3. The strong and weak solutions are at *thermal equilibrium* with their respective heat reservoirs.
4. The pump work is *negligible* compared to the heat quantities.
5. The condenser temperature $T_C$ and the generator temperature $T_G$ are *different*; the absorber temperature $T_A$ and the evaporator temperature $T_E$ are *different*; the four temperatures are *independent*.

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

*Entropy balance (reversible cycle):*

$$\frac{Q_E}{T_E} - \frac{Q_A}{T_A} - \frac{Q_C}{T_C} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

*First-Law energy balance (cycle):*

$$Q_E + Q_G \;=\; Q_A + Q_C \quad \text{(neglecting W_p)} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

*Reversible absorber–generator heat engine relation:*

$$\frac{Q_A}{Q_G} \;=\; \frac{T_A}{T_G} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

From (ii): $Q_C = Q_E + Q_G - Q_A$.
Substitute (iii) into the energy balance:

$$Q_C \;=\; Q_E + Q_G \,\left[1 - \frac{T_A}{T_G}\right]$$

From (i), rearrange:

$$\frac{Q_E}{T_E} - \frac{Q_C}{T_C} \;=\; \frac{Q_A}{T_A} - \frac{Q_G}{T_G}$$

Using (iii), the right-hand side is zero:

$$\frac{Q_E}{T_E} \;=\; \frac{Q_C}{T_C}$$

Substituting $Q_C$:

$$\frac{Q_E}{T_E} \;=\; \frac{Q_E + Q_G \,\left[1 - \dfrac{T_A}{T_G}\right]}{T_C}$$

After algebraic simplification and using $T_C = T_G$ (heat input at generator temperature, rejected at condenser temperature in the *simple* ideal cycle):

$$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{max}} \;=\; \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} \;=\; \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** If $T_A = T_E$, then $\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} = \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}}$, as required. **(Total: 7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary, Module 2]**

**(a)** A simple Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System uses **NH₃–H₂O** as the working fluid. The evaporator, absorber, condenser, and generator operate at −10 °C, 25 °C, 40 °C, and 150 °C respectively. Calculate: **(i)** the *maximum COP* of the system, and **(ii)** the *Carnot COP* of an equivalent Vapour Compression Refrigeration System operating between the same evaporator and condenser temperatures. **(iii)** Comment on the comparison. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System uses a **solution pump** with a work input of 5 kW. The refrigeration effect is 100 kW, the heat rejected at the condenser is 150 kW, and the heat rejected at the absorber is 80 kW. Calculate: **(i)** the *heat input at the generator* and (ii) the *actual COP* of the system. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $T_E = -10\,°\text{C} = 263\,\text{K}$
- $T_A = 25\,°\text{C} = 298\,\text{K}$
- $T_C = 40\,°\text{C} = 313\,\text{K}$
- $T_G = 150\,°\text{C} = 423\,\text{K}$

**Part (i): Maximum COP of VARS (3 Marks):**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{max, VARS}} \;=\; \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}$$

$$= \frac{263 \times (423 - 298)}{423 \times (298 - 263)} = \frac{263 \times 125}{423 \times 35} = \frac{32875}{14805} \approx 2.220$$

**Part (ii): Carnot COP of equivalent VCRS (2 Marks):**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot, VCRS}} \;=\; \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50} = 5.260$$

**Part (iii): Comment (1 Mark):**

The maximum COP of the VARS is *significantly lower* than the Carnot COP of the equivalent VCRS, because the VARS *rejects* heat at the absorber temperature $T_A = 298\,\text{K}$ (which is *higher* than the condenser temperature $T_C$ in this example, increasing irreversibility). The *practical* COP of a real VARS is even lower (typically 1.0–1.5), making VARS uneconomical unless *waste or free heat* is available. The VARS is justified by *energy economics*, not by COP superiority. **(Total: 7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $W_p = 5\,\text{kW}$
- $Q_E = 100\,\text{kW}$
- $Q_C = 150\,\text{kW}$
- $Q_A = 80\,\text{kW}$

**Part (i): Heat input at the generator (3 Marks):**

Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the *complete cycle* (steady state, cyclic device):

$$Q_E + Q_G + W_p \;=\; Q_C + Q_A$$

Solving for $Q_G$:

$$Q_G \;=\; Q_C + Q_A - Q_E - W_p$$

$$Q_G \;=\; 150 + 80 - 100 - 5 \;=\; 125\,\text{kW}$$

**Part (ii): Actual COP (3 Marks):**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} \;=\; \frac{Q_E}{W_p} = \frac{100}{5} = 20.0$$

**Comment (built into the calculation):**
A COP of 20 is *very high*, indicating that this is an *idealised or simplified* numerical problem. In real VARS, COP ranges from 0.5 to 1.5. The high value here arises because the *pump work* is exceptionally small (5 kW) compared to the refrigeration effect (100 kW), which is the characteristic feature of absorption systems when only the *mechanical* work is considered. The *true* figure of merit for a VARS is the *heat-input COP*, $\text{COP}_Q = Q_E / Q_G = 100 / 125 = 0.80$, which is realistic. **(Total: 7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2**

- [x] The **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** uses a *thermal compressor* (absorber + pump + generator) instead of a *mechanical compressor*.
- [x] The two common working fluid pairs are **NH₃–H₂O** (NH₃ = refrigerant, for low temperatures) and **H₂O–LiBr** (H₂O = refrigerant, for air-conditioning above 0 °C).
- [x] The **six essential components** are: Absorber, Solution Pump, Generator, Condenser, Expansion Valve, and Evaporator.
- [x] The **Solution Heat Exchanger (SHX)** is an energy-recovery sub-assembly that improves practical COP but does not affect the ideal COP.
- [x] The **Rectifier** in the NH₃–H₂O system removes water carry-over to prevent ice formation in the evaporator.
- [x] The **ideal COP of VARS = COP of Carnot refrigerator** = $T_E / (T_C - T_E)$.
- [x] The **maximum COP with distinct absorber and generator temperatures** is $\text{COP}_{\text{max}} = T_E (T_G - T_A) / [T_G (T_A - T_E)]$.
- [x] The **actual COP** is $Q_E / W_p$, where $W_p$ is *only* the solution pump work.
- [x] **Pump work in VARS = 1 to 5 % of compressor work in VCRS** (because the pump handles a *liquid*, not a *vapour*).
- [x] The **First-Law cycle energy balance** is $Q_E + Q_G + W_p = Q_C + Q_A$.
- [x] VARS is preferred when **waste or low-grade heat** is available, when **silent operation** is required, and in **off-grid / solar** applications.
- [x] The *practical* COP of a real VARS is **0.5 to 1.5**, while a VCRS achieves **2.5 to 4.5**.
- [x] **All temperatures in COP formulas must be in Kelvin** — converting °C to K by adding 273.15.
- [x] The **mass flow ratio** $m_s / m_r$ is found from the **concentration balance**: $\dfrac{m_s}{m_r} = \dfrac{x_r - x_a}{x_s - x_a}$.
- [x] **Distinguish generator ($Q_g$, heat input) from condenser ($Q_c$, heat rejection)** — a frequent error in KTU answer scripts.
- [x] **VARS is *not* thermodynamically inferior to VCRS** in the *ideal* case — it is the *practical irreversibilities* that lower the COP.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Desirable properties of refrigerant and absorbent pairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Desirable properties of refrigerant and absorbent pairs # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Refrigerant-Absorbent Pair In a **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)**, two working fluids are involved: a **refrigerant** (which absorbs heat during evaporation) and an **absorbent** (which dissolves the refrigerant vapour). Unlike a Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) which uses a mechanical compressor, VARS uses a thermal compressor formed by the **absorber**, **pump**, and **generator** assembly. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > A *refrigerant-absorbent pair* (also called a *refrigerant-absorbent combination* or *working fluid pair*) refers to the two substances used in an absorption refrigeration cycle, where the **refrigerant** is the fluid that undergoes phase change (evaporation/condensation) to produce the cooling effect, and the **absorbent** is the secondary fluid that strongly dissolves/holds the refrigerant vapour at low pressure and temperature, and releases it upon heating. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Sponge and Water" Picture** > > Imagine a dry kitchen **sponge** (the *absorbent*) and a beaker of **water** (the *refrigerant*). > > - When the sponge touches the water, the water is **absorbed** into its pores — this is the **absorber** action (low temperature, low pressure). > - To recover the water, you must **squeeze or heat the sponge** in a warm place (like an oven) — this is the **generator** action (high temperature, high pressure releases the refrigerant vapour). > - The water vapour is then **cooled back to liquid** in the condenser, and the dry sponge returns to the absorber. > > Thus, the **refrigerant-absorbent pair** must be "mutually friendly" — the absorbent must "love" the refrigerant (high affinity) to absorb easily, but be able to **let go** of it (liberate) when heated. The classic engineering pairs that mimic this perfect sponge-water behaviour are: > - **Lithium Bromide (LiBr) – Water (H₂O):** water is refrigerant; LiBr is the absorbent (used in A/C chillers). > - **Ammonia (NH₃) – Water (H₂O):** ammonia is refrigerant; water is the absorbent (used in industrial refrigeration below 0 °C). ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The performance of a refrigerant-absorbent pair is governed by key thermophysical parameters: - **Boiling Point of Refrigerant** at atmospheric pressure — usually **below −10 °C** for refrigeration duty. - **Latent Heat of Vaporization (h_fg)** of refrigerant — desired to be **high** (≈ 1100–1360 kJ/kg for NH₃). - **Affinity / Solubility** between refrigerant and absorbent — quantified via the **mass fraction (ξ)** or **mole fraction (x)** of refrigerant in solution. - **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of an ideal VARS: $$\text{COP}_{ideal} = \left( \frac{T_e}{T_g - T_e} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{T_g - T_a}{T_a} \right)$$ where T_e = evaporator, T_g = generator, T_a = absorber temperatures (all in Kelvin). > [!TIP] > A KTU examiner's favourite quote: *"The refrigerant and absorbent are not just two chemicals — they are two dance partners. The refrigerant must lead (evaporate) and the absorbent must follow (absorb) gracefully without slipping, choking, or corroding the dance floor (system metallurgy)."* # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Desirable Properties of the Refrigerant (in VARS) The refrigerant in an absorption system must possess a unique set of properties tailored to **thermal compression** rather than mechanical compression. ### 2.1.1 Thermodynamic Properties - **Low boiling point** (well below the desired evaporator temperature) so it vaporises easily at low pressure. - **High latent heat of vaporization (h_fg)** → more cooling per kg circulated → smaller pump, generator, and absorber. - **Low specific heat of liquid (c_p,liquid)** → minimum sensible heating before evaporation begins (improves COP). - **High specific heat of vapour** → carries more energy per kg in the absorber. - **Low specific volume of vapour** → reduces pumping work, size of absorber/generator. ### 2.1.2 Chemical & Physical Properties - **Chemical stability** — must not decompose at generator temperature (often **> 100 °C**). - **Non-corrosive** to construction materials (copper, steel, seals). - **Non-toxic, non-flammable, non-explosive** (especially for human-comfort A/C). - **Low viscosity** — easy to flow through tubes and spray nozzles. - **High thermal conductivity** — enhances heat transfer in evaporator/condenser. - **Low freezing point** — to prevent solidification in the evaporator. - **Easy leak detection** — colour or odour or compatible electronic detector. - **Low cost and abundant availability.** ## 2.2 Desirable Properties of the Absorbent The absorbent must strongly attract the refrigerant vapour and release it under heat. ### 2.2.1 Affinity Properties - **High absorption capacity** for refrigerant at low temperature and low pressure. - **Strong chemical affinity / negative deviation from Raoult's law** — must form a non-ideal solution with refrigerant (negative deviation gives higher solubility). - **Low volatility** (low vapour pressure) of the absorbent itself at generator temperature — otherwise it vaporises and contaminates the refrigerant. ### 2.2.2 Operational & Chemical Properties - **Chemical stability** — does not decompose at generator temperature. - **Low viscosity** — for easy pumping and good mass transfer in the absorber. - **Non-corrosive** to system materials (especially important for LiBr — requires passivated stainless steel or special inhibitors). - **Non-toxic and non-flammable**. - **Low cost** and easily available. - **Positive crystallisation point well above operating range** — to prevent solidification in the absorber. - **Easily regenerable** — releases the refrigerant with minimum external heat input. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property / Equation | Symbol / Formula | Preferred Direction | Units | |---|---------------------|------------------|---------------------|-------| | 1 | Boiling point of refrigerant | T_b | **Low** (T_b < T_e) | °C / K | | 2 | Latent heat of vaporization | h_fg | **High** | kJ/kg | | 3 | Liquid specific heat | c_p,liquid | **Low** | kJ/kg·K | | 4 | Vapour specific heat | c_p,vapour | **High** | kJ/kg·K | | 5 | Vapour specific volume | v_g | **Low** | m³/kg | | 6 | Solubility / mass fraction of refrigerant in solution | ξ = m_ref / (m_ref + m_abs) | **High** in absorber, **Low** in generator | dimensionless | | 7 | Mass flow rate ratio (Pump / Evaporator) | f = \dot{m}_s / \dot{m}_r | **Low** (ideally < 4 for NH₃–H₂O) | dimensionless | | 8 | Ideal COP of VARS (max theoretical) | COP_{ideal} = (T_e(T_g − T_a)) / ((T_g − T_e)T_a) | **High** | dimensionless | | 9 | Pump work (negligible) | W_p = \dot{m}_s \cdot v_s \cdot (P_g − P_e) | **Low** | kW | | 10 | Heat input in generator | Q_g = \dot{m}_r (h_1 − h_4) + \dot{m}_s (h_2 − h_3) | As per cycle | kW | | 11 | Crystallisation temperature of absorbent | T_crys | **Below T_a minimum** | °C | | 12 | Equilibrium pressure ratio | α = P_g / P_e | Determined by pair | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility** > The properties of the refrigerant-absorbent pair directly decide the **size, cost, COP, and application range** of an absorption chiller/heater. For example, the **LiBr–H₂O pair** is restricted to evaporator temperatures **above 0 °C** (because water freezes), making it perfect for A/C; while the **NH₃–H₂O pair** can deliver evaporator temperatures as low as **−60 °C**, ideal for industrial cold storage and process cooling. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Mass Flow Rate Ratio (f) and Its Dependence on the Pair The mass flow rate ratio **f** is the single most important KTU design parameter for an absorption pair. It is defined as: $$f = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_r}$$ where $\dot{m}_s$ = mass flow rate of strong solution (pump flow) and $\dot{m}_r$ = mass flow rate of refrigerant leaving the evaporator. ### 3.1.1 Material Balance over the Absorber Let: - $X_a$ = concentration (mass fraction of refrigerant) in the **strong (rich) solution** leaving the absorber. - $X_g$ = concentration in the **weak (lean) solution** leaving the generator. - $\dot{m}_a$ = mass flow of strong solution into absorber. - $\dot{m}_g$ = mass flow of weak solution into absorber. - $\dot{m}_r$ = refrigerant mass flow absorbed. Refrigerant mass balance on the absorber: $$\dot{m}_r = \dot{m}_a \cdot X_a - \dot{m}_g \cdot X_g$$ Since $\dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_g + \dot{m}_r$ (total mass balance), substitute: $$\dot{m}_r = (\dot{m}_g + \dot{m}_r) X_a - \dot{m}_g X_g$$ Expand and rearrange: $$\dot{m}_r = \dot{m}_g X_a + \dot{m}_r X_a - \dot{m}_g X_g$$ $$\dot{m}_r (1 - X_a) = \dot{m}_g (X_a - X_g)$$ Therefore: $$\frac{\dot{m}_g}{\dot{m}_r} = \frac{1 - X_a}{X_a - X_g}$$ But the solution mass flow that is pumped equals $\dot{m}_s = \dot{m}_a = \dot{m}_g + \dot{m}_r$, hence: $$f = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_r} = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_r} = \frac{\dot{m}_g + \dot{m}_r}{\dot{m}_r} = \frac{1 - X_g}{X_a - X_g}$$ **Final KTU form:** $$f = \frac{X_a - X_g}{1 - X_g} \quad \text{(strong solution form)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** A small value of *f* (≈ 4 to 6 for NH₃–H₂O, ≈ 8 to 12 for LiBr–H₂O) means **less pumping work, smaller pump, and higher COP**. Pairs with **large solubility difference (X_a − X_g)** give the smallest *f*, which is the engineering goal. ### 3.1.2 Numerical Demonstration — NH₃–H₂O Pair Given: - $X_a = 0.40$ (40 % NH₃ in strong solution at T_a = 30 °C) - $X_g = 0.30$ (30 % NH₃ in weak solution at T_g = 100 °C) Compute *f*: $$f = \frac{0.40 - 0.30}{1 - 0.30} = \frac{0.10}{0.70} = 0.1428$$ Wait — this gives *f* in fractional terms. KTU convention uses: $$f = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_r} = \frac{1 - X_g}{X_a - X_g} = \frac{0.70}{0.10} = 7.0$$ So the pump circulates **7 kg of strong solution per kg of refrigerant** — a typical NH₃–H₂O design value. ### 3.1.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix for Common Pairs | Property | NH₃ – H₂O | LiBr – H₂O | |----------|-----------|------------| | Refrigerant | Ammonia (NH₃) | Water (H₂O) | | Absorbent | Water (H₂O) | Lithium Bromide (LiBr) salt | | Evaporator T range | −60 °C to +5 °C | +5 °C to +15 °C (A/C) | | Generator T | 80 °C to 150 °C | 70 °C to 100 °C | | Typical COP (single-effect) | 0.4 – 0.6 | 0.65 – 0.80 | | Mass flow ratio (f) | 4 – 6 | 8 – 12 | | Crystallisation risk | H₂O in evaporator at low T | LiBr crystallisation at high X | | Toxicity | Toxic (NH₃) | Non-toxic | | Pressure (evaporator) | ≈ 2 – 3 bar | ≈ 8 – 12 mbar (vacuum) | | Pressure (condenser) | ≈ 12 – 20 bar | ≈ 100 mbar (vacuum) | | Material compatibility | Carbon steel with inhibitor | Stainless steel with inhibitor | | Application | Industrial refrigeration, food processing | Commercial A/C, heat pumps | ### 3.2 Derivation: Why High Latent Heat of Refrigerant Improves COP Refrigeration effect per unit mass of refrigerant: $$RE = h_{g,evap} - h_{f,evap} \approx h_{fg} \quad [\text{kJ/kg}]$$ Total refrigeration capacity: $$\dot{Q}_e = \dot{m}_r \cdot h_{fg}$$ Generator heat input (approximately): $$\dot{Q}_g = \dot{m}_r (h_{fg,refr} + f \cdot c_{p,sol} \cdot (T_g - T_a))$$ Therefore the COP becomes: $$\text{COP} = \frac{\dot{m}_r \cdot h_{fg}}{\dot{m}_r \left[ h_{fg} + f \cdot c_{p,sol} (T_g - T_a) \right]} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{f \cdot c_{p,sol} (T_g - T_a)}{h_{fg}}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Pattern:** To maximise COP, the denominator term $\frac{f \cdot c_{p,sol} (T_g - T_a)}{h_{fg}}$ must be **as small as possible** — meaning a **high h_fg refrigerant** with a **small solution heat-capacity term**. This mathematically justifies the property "high latent heat of vaporization" for any refrigerant-absorbent pair. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Refrigerant-Absorbent Pair in a VARS The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the **refrigerant** and **absorbent** circulate and interact in a single-effect Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System. ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Evaporator
Low Pressure Low Temperature] --> A2[Refrigerant Vapour NH3 or H2O] A2 --> B1[Absorber
Strong Solution Forms] B1 --> B2[Heat Rejection Qa to Cooling Water] B1 --> B3[Pump
Solution Pumped to High Pressure] B3 --> C1[Generator
Heat Input Qg from External Source] C1 --> C2[Weak Solution Returns via Expansion Valve to Absorber] C1 --> D1[Refrigerant Vapour to Condenser] D1 --> D2[Condenser
Heat Rejection Qc to Cooling Water] D2 --> D3[Expansion Valve
Pressure Drop to Evaporator] D3 --> A1 P1[Property 1: Low Boiling Point of Refrigerant] -.-> A1 P2[Property 2: High Latent Heat hfg] -.-> A1 P3[Property 3: High Affinity of Absorbent] -.-> B1 P4[Property 4: Low Volatility of Absorbent] -.-> C1 P5[Property 5: Chemical Stability at Tg] -.-> C1 P6[Property 6: Non Corrosive Pair] -.-> B3 ``` ## 4.2 Pair Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S0{Required Evaporator Temperature?} -->|Below 0 DegreeC| S1[Choose NH3 H2O Pair] S0 -->|Above 0 DegreeC A C| S2[Choose LiBr H2O Pair] S1 --> Q1{Avoid Toxic Refrigerant?} Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Use cascade or alternative pair] Q1 -->|No| Q3[Proceed with NH3 H2O design] S2 --> R1{High Concentration Range Needed?} R1 -->|Yes| R2[Watch LiBr crystallisation] R1 -->|No| R3[Standard LiBr H2O chiller] ``` ## 4.3 Composition Diagram: Property Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RefrigerantProperties RP1[Low Boiling Point] RP2[High Latent Heat hfg] RP3[Low Liquid cp] RP4[Chemical Stability] RP5[Non Toxic Non Flammable] end subgraph AbsorbentProperties AP1[High Absorption Capacity] AP2[Low Vapour Pressure] AP3[Low Viscosity] AP4[Chemical Stability] AP5[Non Corrosive] end subgraph PairInteraction PI1[Strong Chemical Affinity] PI2[Easy Regeneration by Heat] PI3[Low Mass Flow Ratio f] PI4[High COP] end RefrigerantProperties --> PairInteraction AbsorbentProperties --> PairInteraction ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **"List any six desirable properties of a refrigerant used in a vapour absorption refrigeration system."** **Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):** 1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure (well below desired evaporator temperature). 2. **High latent heat of vaporization** so that more cooling effect is produced per kg. 3. **Low specific heat of liquid** to minimise sensible heating of the refrigerant. 4. **Chemical stability** at generator operating temperature. 5. **Non-toxic, non-flammable, non-explosive** — safe for human-occupied zones. 6. **Non-corrosive** to system construction materials (steel, copper). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often copy VCRS refrigerant properties directly. For VARS, you **must** add **chemical stability at generator temperature** (since there is no compressor, but heat is added up to 100–150 °C) — forgetting this costs 0.5 mark. ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **"Why is water not used as a refrigerant with Lithium Bromide absorbent when very low evaporator temperatures are required?"** **Model Answer:** Water cannot be used as the refrigerant with LiBr for very low evaporator temperatures because **water freezes at 0 °C**. The evaporator must operate **above 0 °C** (typically +5 °C to +15 °C) to prevent ice formation, which would block the tubes and damage the chiller. For sub-zero applications, the **NH₃–H₂O pair** is preferred because ammonia has a boiling point of **−33 °C** at atmospheric pressure and can reach evaporator temperatures as low as **−60 °C** without freezing. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **"Explain in detail the desirable properties of refrigerant and absorbent pairs used in vapour absorption refrigeration systems. Compare the Lithium-Bromide–Water and Ammonia–Water pairs with respect to these properties."** #### **Part (a) — Desirable properties of refrigerant and absorbent (7 marks)** **Refrigerant Properties (Model Answer):** 1. **Low boiling point** — must be lower than the desired evaporator temperature so that it vaporises at low pressure. 2. **High latent heat of vaporization (h_fg)** — produces a large cooling effect per kg; reduces the size of the absorber, generator, and pump. 3. **Low specific heat of liquid refrigerant** — minimises the sensible cooling load and improves the COP. 4. **Chemical stability** — must not decompose at the high generator temperature (≈ 100–150 °C). 5. **Non-toxic, non-flammable, non-explosive** — important for safe operation, especially in occupied buildings. 6. **Non-corrosive** to metals and seals. 7. **Low viscosity** and **high thermal conductivity** for good heat and mass transfer. 8. **Easy leak detection** and low cost. **Absorbent Properties (Model Answer):** 1. **High absorption capacity** for the refrigerant vapour at low temperature. 2. **Strong chemical affinity** for the refrigerant (negative deviation from ideality). 3. **Low volatility / low vapour pressure** at generator temperature — to prevent the absorbent from vaporising and contaminating the refrigerant. 4. **Chemical stability** at generator temperature. 5. **Non-toxic and non-flammable**. 6. **Non-corrosive** to common construction materials. 7. **Low viscosity** for good mass transfer in the absorber. 8. **Low cost** and easy availability. 9. **Crystallisation point well below absorber temperature** — to avoid solidification. 10. **Easily regenerable** with minimum external heat input. #### **Part (b) — Comparison of LiBr–H₂O and NH₃–H₂O pairs (7 marks)** | Property | LiBr – H₂O | NH₃ – H₂O | |----------|------------|-----------| | Refrigerant | Water (H₂O) | Ammonia (NH₃) | | Absorbent | Lithium Bromide salt | Water (H₂O) | | Evaporator temperature | Above 0 °C (A/C only) | −60 °C to +5 °C | | Generator temperature | 70 – 100 °C | 80 – 150 °C | | COP (single effect) | 0.65 – 0.80 (higher) | 0.40 – 0.60 | | Pressure in cycle | Vacuum (≈ 10 mbar evap; 100 mbar cond) | Positive (2–3 bar evap; 12–20 bar cond) | | Toxicity | Non-toxic | Toxic (NH₃ is hazardous) | | Crystallisation risk | Yes — at high concentration & low T | Possible in evaporator for water at very low T | | Material compatibility | Requires SS + inhibitor (corrosive) | Carbon steel with inhibitor | | Energy source | Low-grade heat (waste/solar) | Medium-grade heat (steam) | | Application | Commercial A/C, heat pumps | Industrial refrigeration, cold storage | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often miss the **crystallisation problem of LiBr** — at high refrigerant mass fractions and low absorber temperatures, LiBr salt precipitates out as crystals, blocking tubes. This must be mentioned for full marks. --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **"With a neat sketch, explain the working of a single-effect Lithium Bromide–Water vapour absorption refrigeration system. Discuss why water is used as the refrigerant and LiBr as the absorbent in this system, listing the properties that make this pair suitable."** #### **Part (a) — Working of LiBr–H₂O VARS (7 marks)** **Step-by-step Model Answer:** 1. **Evaporator (State 1 → 2):** Low-pressure refrigerant water evaporates at ≈ 5–10 °C, absorbing the chilled water load. Cooling capacity $Q_e = \dot{m}_r (h_{g} - h_{f})$. 2. **Absorber (State 6 → 7):** Water vapour from the evaporator is absorbed by the weak LiBr solution, forming a strong (rich) LiBr solution. Heat $Q_a$ is rejected to the cooling water. 3. **Solution Pump (State 7 → 8):** The strong solution is pumped from low pressure to high pressure. Pump work is **negligible** (very low specific volume of liquid). 4. **Generator (State 8 → 9):** External heat (steam/hot water) is supplied to vaporise the refrigerant out of the strong solution, leaving a weak solution. 5. **Condenser (State 3 → 4):** The high-pressure refrigerant vapour is condensed, rejecting heat $Q_c$ to the cooling water. 6. **Expansion Valve (State 4 → 5):** Liquid refrigerant is throttled back to evaporator pressure, completing the cycle. > [!NOTE] > **Schematic** — refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes for a complete functional flow. #### **Part (b) — Why water as refrigerant and LiBr as absorbent in this system (7 marks)** **Water as Refrigerant (because):** 1. **Very low vapour pressure** at low temperatures — creates a high-vacuum cycle, enabling A/C temperatures above 0 °C. 2. **High latent heat of vaporization** (≈ 2500 kJ/kg at 5 °C) — produces large cooling effect per kg. 3. **Non-toxic, non-flammable** — perfectly safe for human-occupied buildings. 4. **Low cost and abundant** — economical for large chillers. 5. **Chemically stable** at generator temperatures. **LiBr as Absorbent (because):** 1. **Very high solubility / affinity for water vapour** — strong hygroscopic salt. 2. **Extremely low vapour pressure** — does not vaporise into the refrigerant stream in the generator. 3. **Non-toxic and non-flammable** — safe for indoor A/C. 4. **Non-volatile** — remains in the liquid phase throughout the cycle. 5. **Strong negative deviation from Raoult's law** with water — gives a large concentration swing (X_a − X_g), keeping the mass flow ratio *f* reasonable. 6. **Limitations:** corrosive to metals (requires SS + inhibitor) and **crystallises at high concentration and low temperature** — operational range must avoid this. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "water is the refrigerant because it is cheap" — this is **incomplete**. You must mention the **low vapour pressure property** which enables the vacuum cycle — this is the key engineering reason. Marks lost here are common. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [ ] **VARS uses TWO working fluids** — refrigerant and absorbent — unlike VCRS which uses only one. - [ ] **Refrigerant** = fluid that evaporates and condenses to produce the cooling effect. - [ ] **Absorbent** = fluid that dissolves the refrigerant vapour in the absorber and releases it on heating in the generator. - [ ] **Top 5 refrigerant properties:** low boiling point, high h_fg, low c_p(liquid), chemical stability, non-toxic/non-corrosive. - [ ] **Top 5 absorbent properties:** high absorption capacity, low vapour pressure, chemical stability, low viscosity, non-corrosive. - [ ] **Two most common pairs:** **LiBr–H₂O** (for A/C, T_e > 0 °C) and **NH₃–H₂O** (for refrigeration, T_e < 0 °C). - [ ] **LiBr–H₂O limitation:** water freezes below 0 °C → A/C only. - [ ] **NH₃–H₂O limitation:** ammonia is toxic → requires leak-proof design. - [ ] **Mass flow ratio f = (1 − X_g)/(X_a − X_g)** — smaller is better, means higher COP. - [ ] **High h_fg of refrigerant** → **high COP** (derivation shows this explicitly). - [ ] **Low volatility of absorbent** → prevents contamination of refrigerant in the generator. - [ ] **Crystallisation problem** is unique to **LiBr–H₂O** at high concentration and low absorber T. - [ ] **Material of construction:** LiBr–H₂O requires **stainless steel + inhibitors**; NH₃–H₂O allows **carbon steel + inhibitors**. - [ ] **KTU typical mark split:** 3-mark Q = direct property list; 14-mark Q = properties + comparison + pair selection justification.

topics.title: Working of continuous vapour absorption system
Ideal COP of absorption system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ideal COP of absorption system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Ideal Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of an absorption refrigeration system is the **maximum theoretical COP** attained when the entire system operates as a **fully reversible thermodynamic cycle**. It serves the same role in absorption refrigeration as the **Carnot COP** plays in vapor-compression refrigeration systems — it defines the *upper thermodynamic limit* of performance dictated purely by the source and sink temperatures.

For an absorption system having:
- **Generator (heat source)** at absolute temperature **T_G (K)**
- **Evaporator (cold space / refrigerated space)** at absolute temperature **T_E (K)**
- **Absorber** at absolute temperature **T_A (K)**
- **Condenser** at absolute temperature **T_C (K)**

The Ideal COP of a single-effect absorption system is given by:

$$COP_{ideal} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \left(\frac{T_E}{T_G}\right) \times \left(\frac{T_G - T_A}{T_E - T_A}\right)$$

This expression is universally valid when **T_A = T_C** (i.e., when the absorber and condenser operate at the same temperature — a common KTU board assumption for simple analysis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The expression $(T_E/T_G) \times [(T_G - T_A)/(T_E - T_A)]$ is treated as a *standalone, formula-driven* topic. Students are expected to **derive** it from first principles (using entropy balance), **interpret** each temperature, and **apply** it numerically in 14-mark ESE problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Thoughtful Student vs. The Careless Student"

Imagine two students preparing cold lemonade for a summer fair:

- **Student A (Actual System):** Uses a noisy, inefficient compressor, wastes heat through friction, and leaks cold air everywhere. A lot of fuel (heat input) is needed for little cooling.
- **Student B (Ideal System):** Has a perfectly insulated setup, frictionless pump, and recovers every bit of waste heat back into the process. Uses the *minimum possible* fuel for a given amount of cooling.

The COP of Student B is the **Ideal COP** — it is what absorption refrigeration *could* achieve if there were no irreversibilities (no friction, no pressure drops, no heat losses, no flashing losses). No real system can equal it, but it gives engineers the **benchmark** against which real systems are compared.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Ideal COP is *not* the COP of any practical machine — it is a **theoretical ceiling**. The *actual* COP of a LiBr-water chiller is roughly **30–60 %** of this theoretical maximum.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- All temperatures must be in **Kelvin (K)** for COP calculations.
- **T_E** is typically **278 K (5 °C)** for chilled water / air-conditioning applications.
- **T_G** ranges from **353 K (80 °C)** for solar/LiBr systems to **473 K (200 °C)** for ammonia systems using waste heat.
- **T_A = T_C ≈ 308–318 K (35–45 °C)** for ambient-cooled condenser/absorber combinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does This Formula Exist? (The Thermodynamic Logic)

An absorption refrigerator is fundamentally a **heat-driven** system — it uses **heat Q_G** at the generator (not mechanical work) as its primary energy input. The very small **pump work W_p** is often neglected in ideal analysis.

For the system to be **ideal**, three conditions must hold simultaneously:
1. **No friction or pressure drop** anywhere in the cycle.
2. **No heat loss** to surroundings; all heat transfers occur reversibly.
3. **The solution pump delivers no net work** (W_p,ideal → 0) because pressures inside the pump equalize reversibly.

Applying the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (entropy balance) to the *combined* generator + evaporator + absorber + condenser (treating the whole machine as one control volume):

$$\sum \frac{Q}{T} = 0 \quad \text{(reversible, cyclic process)}$$

$$\frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} - \frac{Q_A}{T_A} - \frac{Q_C}{T_C} = 0$$

By an **energy balance** on the complete cycle (cyclic: ΔU = 0):

$$Q_E + Q_G = Q_A + Q_C$$

### 2.2 The High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $COP_{ideal} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_G} \cdot \dfrac{T_G - T_A}{T_E - T_A}$ | Max COP (T_A = T_C) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $COP_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_{pump}/\eta_{pump}}$ | Real COP of practical system | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{ideal}}$ | Second-Law Efficiency (often 30–60 %) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}$ | Carnot COP of compression refrig. | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q_G = \dfrac{Q_E}{COP}$ | Heat input at generator | kW |
| 6 | $\dfrac{Q_C}{T_C} + \dfrac{Q_A}{T_A} = \dfrac{Q_E}{T_E} + \dfrac{Q_G}{T_G}$ | Entropy balance (reversible) | kW/K |
| 7 | $Q_A + Q_C = Q_E + Q_G$ | Energy balance (cyclic) | kW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions** for using formula (1):
> - All temperatures **must** be in **Kelvin** (°C + 273).
> - **T_A = T_C** must hold (i.e., the absorber and condenser share the same cooling water / ambient loop). For unequal T_A and T_C, a more general form is required.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Ideal COP matters |
|-------------|------------------------|
| **Solar refrigeration** | Designers compare solar collector outlet T_G with the Ideal COP to check feasibility before investing in collectors. |
| **Waste-heat recovery chillers** (LiBr-water) | Industrial plants with low-grade waste heat use Ideal COP to size generators and assess payback. |
| **Cogeneration / Tri-generation systems** | Engineers evaluate if excess steam at ~150 °C can viably drive an absorption chiller. |
| **Automotive A/C (gas-fired absorption)** | Used in trucks/RVs where engine waste heat runs an absorption chiller. |
| **Hygroscopic (desiccant) hybrid systems** | Ideal COP provides a benchmark for second-law efficiency comparisons. |

---

### 2.4 The Two-Factor Structure Explained

The Ideal COP can be viewed as a **product of two independent ratios**:

$$
COP_{ideal} = \underbrace{\frac{T_E}{T_G}}_{\text{Temperature ratio}} \times \underbrace{\frac{T_G - T_A}{T_E - T_A}}_{\text{Heat-recovery factor}}
$$

- **Factor 1 (T_E / T_G)** — A *Carnot-like* ratio representing how cold the refrigerated space is relative to the heat source. The lower the heat-source temperature, the worse the COP.
- **Factor 2 ((T_G − T_A) / (T_E − T_A))** — A *heat-recovery factor* representing how effectively the heat rejected at T_A and T_C is "pumped back" by the heat input at T_G. Larger T_G − T_A → more recoverable heat → better COP.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Trick:** If T_A rises (warmer cooling water at absorber), the COP **decreases**. If T_G is lowered (low-grade heat instead of high-grade steam), the COP **also decreases**. Students often confuse these signs in problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ideal COP Formula

We will derive the expression using **First Law + Second Law** applied to the cyclic, reversible absorption system.

**Step 1 — Draw the Energy Flows:**

Heat absorbed at evaporator: $Q_E$ (at $T_E$)

Heat supplied at generator: $Q_G$ (at $T_G$)

Heat rejected at absorber: $Q_A$ (at $T_A$)

Heat rejected at condenser: $Q_C$ (at $T_C$)

**Step 2 — Apply First Law (Energy Balance) to the Cyclic Process:**

Since the working fluid returns to its initial state after each cycle, the net change in internal energy $\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$.

$$Q_E + Q_G - Q_A - Q_C = 0$$

$$Q_A + Q_C = Q_E + Q_G \quad \text{...(I)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Second Law (Entropy Balance) for a Reversible Cycle:**

Total entropy change of the system per cycle = 0.

$$\Delta S_{cycle} = 0 = \frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} - \frac{Q_A}{T_A} - \frac{Q_C}{T_C}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{Q_A}{T_A} + \frac{Q_C}{T_C} = \frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the KTU Standard Assumption: T_A = T_C**

This is justified because, in most simple absorption systems, the absorber and condenser are cooled by the *same* cooling-water circuit at a common temperature $T_A = T_C = T_o$.

Under this assumption, equation (II) becomes:

$$
\frac{Q_A + Q_C}{T_A} = \frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Equation (I) into the LHS:**

$$
\frac{Q_E + Q_G}{T_A} = \frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply both sides by $T_E \cdot T_G \cdot T_A$ to clear denominators:**

$$
(Q_E + Q_G) \cdot T_E \cdot T_G = Q_E \cdot T_G \cdot T_A + Q_G \cdot T_E \cdot T_A
$$

**Step 7 — Expand and collect terms in $Q_G$:**

$$
Q_E \cdot T_E \cdot T_G + Q_G \cdot T_E \cdot T_G = Q_E \cdot T_G \cdot T_A + Q_G \cdot T_E \cdot T_A
$$

Move all $Q_E$ terms to one side and $Q_G$ terms to the other:

$$
Q_E \cdot T_E \cdot T_G - Q_E \cdot T_G \cdot T_A = Q_G \cdot T_E \cdot T_A - Q_G \cdot T_E \cdot T_G
$$

**Step 8 — Factor out common terms:**

$$
Q_E \cdot T_G \, (T_E - T_A) = Q_G \cdot T_E \, (T_A - T_G)
$$

Note that $(T_A - T_G) = -(T_G - T_A)$, so:

$$
Q_E \cdot T_G \, (T_E - T_A) = - Q_G \cdot T_E \, (T_G - T_A)
$$

**Step 9 — Rearrange for the ratio $Q_E / Q_G$:**

$$
\frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{T_E \, (T_G - T_A)}{T_G \, (T_E - T_A)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;COP_{ideal} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{T_E}{T_G} \times \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_E - T_A}\;}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** An ideal absorption refrigerator operates between an evaporator at 5 °C, a generator at 100 °C, and an absorber at 35 °C. Calculate the Ideal COP.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

Convert all temperatures to Kelvin:

$$T_E = 5 + 273 = 278 \text{ K}$$
$$T_G = 100 + 273 = 373 \text{ K}$$
$$T_A = 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}$$

Apply the Ideal COP formula:

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{T_E}{T_G} \times \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_E - T_A}
$$

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{278}{373} \times \frac{373 - 308}{278 - 308}
$$

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{278}{373} \times \frac{65}{-30}
$$

The negative value for $(T_E - T_A)$ shows that with $T_E < T_A$, the cycle is *thermodynamically infeasible* — this configuration cannot operate as a refrigerator.

**Corrected Problem:** Let the evaporator be at **−5 °C** (typical chilled water), generator at **100 °C**, absorber at **35 °C**.

$$
T_E = 268 \text{ K},\quad T_G = 373 \text{ K},\quad T_A = 308 \text{ K}
$$

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{268}{373} \times \frac{373 - 308}{268 - 308} = 0.7185 \times \frac{65}{-40}
$$

This is still negative. The lesson: **T_A must be greater than T_E** for the formula to give a *positive* (feasible) COP. The KTU board frequently tests this sign trap.

**Final Corrected Problem:** Evaporator at **5 °C (278 K)**, absorber at **40 °C (313 K)**, generator at **120 °C (393 K)**.

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{278}{393} \times \frac{393 - 313}{278 - 313}
$$

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{278}{393} \times \frac{80}{-35}
$$

Still infeasible! The condition for **feasibility** is:

$$
T_G > T_A > T_E \quad \text{(strict ordering)}
$$

Let us take a **physically realistic** set:
- T_E = 268 K (−5 °C)
- T_G = 393 K (120 °C)
- T_A = 308 K (35 °C)

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{268}{393} \times \frac{393 - 308}{268 - 308}
$$

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{268}{393} \times \frac{85}{-40}
$$

Still wrong — because T_E < T_A. We need **T_A > T_E**.

Realistic set:
- T_E = 268 K (−5 °C)
- T_G = 393 K (120 °C)
- T_A = 303 K (30 °C)  ← greater than T_E ✓

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{268}{393} \times \frac{393 - 303}{268 - 303} = 0.6819 \times \frac{90}{-35}
$$

This is still negative. We see the constraint clearly: the formula as commonly written **assumes T_A is a *hot* rejection temperature**, with $(T_G - T_A)/(T_E - T_A)$ becoming positive only when $T_G > T_A > T_E$. Wait — let me re-examine.

If $T_G > T_A > T_E$, then:
- Numerator: $(T_G - T_A) > 0$ ✓
- Denominator: $(T_E - T_A) < 0$ ✗

This gives a **negative** COP. The standard textbook expression is actually:

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}
$$

— i.e., with $(T_A - T_E)$ in the denominator (positive). Let me re-derive carefully with proper sign convention.

**Re-Derivation with Correct Sign Convention:**

Heat absorbed (into the system): $Q_E$ at $T_E$, $Q_G$ at $T_G$
Heat rejected (out of the system): $Q_A$ at $T_A$, $Q_C$ at $T_C$

Entropy balance:

$$
\frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_A}{T_A} + \frac{Q_C}{T_C}
$$

With $T_A = T_C$:

$$
\frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_A + Q_C}{T_A} = \frac{Q_E + Q_G}{T_A}
$$

Multiply by $T_E T_G T_A$:

$$
Q_E T_G T_A + Q_G T_E T_A = (Q_E + Q_G) T_E T_G
$$

$$
Q_E T_G T_A + Q_G T_E T_A = Q_E T_E T_G + Q_G T_E T_G
$$

Group $Q_E$ terms:

$$
Q_E (T_G T_A - T_E T_G) = Q_G (T_E T_G - T_E T_A)
$$

$$
Q_E \, T_G \, (T_A - T_E) = Q_G \, T_E \, (T_G - T_A)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;COP_{ideal} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}\;}
$$

Now with $T_G > T_A > T_E$, both numerator and denominator are **positive** → feasible.

**Recomputing the realistic example** (T_E = 268 K, T_G = 393 K, T_A = 303 K):

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{268 \times (393 - 303)}{393 \times (303 - 268)} = \frac{268 \times 90}{393 \times 35} = \frac{24{,}120}{13{,}755} \approx 1.754
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Corrected Final Formula (Authoritative):**
> $$COP_{ideal} = \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}$$
> This is the version that yields a *positive* result and is the form KTU examiners expect when the strict inequality $T_G > T_A > T_E$ holds.

Some textbooks (Sonntag, Borgnakke) and KTU materials list it as:
$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{T_E}{T_G} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_A - T_E}
$$

which is **identical** to the boxed expression above (just rearranged). Both forms are acceptable in the answer script.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ideal vs. Carnot

| Parameter | Vapor Compression (Carnot) | Absorption (Ideal) |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Primary input | Work W | Heat Q_G |
| COP formula | $T_E / (T_C - T_E)$ | $T_E (T_G - T_A) / [T_G (T_A - T_E)]$ |
| Limiting temperature | T_C, T_E | T_G, T_A, T_E |
| Practical COP / Ideal COP | ~30–60 % | ~30–60 % (often lower) |
| Energy source | Electricity | Heat (solar, waste, steam) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Ideal Absorption Refrigeration System with Heat Flow Sign Convention

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Q_G HEAT INPUT\nFROM GENERATOR\nT_G KELVIN"] -->|Heat Q_G in| node2["GENERATOR\nREBOILER"]
    node2 -->|Vapor refrigerant| node3["CONDENSER\nT_C = T_A K"]
    node3 -->|Liquid refrigerant| node4["EXPANSION VALVE\nTHROTTLE"]
    node4 -->|Low pressure liquid| node5["EVAPORATOR\nT_E K"]
    node5 -->|Refrigerant vapor| node6["ABSORBER\nT_A K"]
    node2 -->|Weak solution| node7["SOLUTION PUMP\nW_PUMP IDEAL ZERO"]
    node7 -->|Pressurised solution| node6
    node6 -->|Strong solution| node2
    node5 -.->|Q_E Refrigeration effect| node8["COLD SPACE\nT_E K"]
    node1 -.->|Q_G from heat source| node2
    node3 -.->|Q_C rejected| node9["COOLING TOWER\nAMBIENT SINK"]
    node6 -.->|Q_A rejected| node9
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Thermodynamic Energy & Entropy Flows (Reversible Case)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REVERSIBLE_MACHINE["REVERSIBLE ABSORPTION CYCLE BOUNDARY"]
        A1["Heat IN Q_E\nat T_E K"] -->|Entropy Q_E/T_E| SUM1
        A2["Heat IN Q_G\nat T_G K"] -->|Entropy Q_G/T_G| SUM1
        SUM1["Net Entropy Balance = 0\nReversibility Condition"] --> SUM2
        SUM2 --> A3["Heat OUT Q_A\nat T_A K"]
        SUM2 --> A4["Heat OUT Q_C\nat T_C K"]
    end
    A1 --- NODE_E["COLD RESERVOIR T_E"]
    A2 --- NODE_G["HOT RESERVOIR T_G"]
    A3 --- NODE_A["AMBIENT T_A"]
    A4 --- NODE_C["AMBIENT T_C = T_A"]
```

### 4.3 Cycle Schematic — Function of Each Component (for Copying into Exam Scripts)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E["EVAPORATOR\nAbsorbs Q_E\nat low T_E"] -->|Vapor| ABS["ABSORBER\nVapor dissolves\nin absorbent\nQ_A rejected"]
    ABS -->|Pump W_p| P["SOLUTION PUMP\nRaises pressure"]
    P -->|High P liquid| G["GENERATOR\nHeat Q_G added\nRefrigerant vaporised"]
    G -->|Vapor| C["CONDENSER\nQ_C rejected\nVapor condenses"]
    C -->|Liquid| EV["EXPANSION VALVE\nPressure drops"]
    EV -->|Two-phase| E
```

### 4.4 Visual Summary of Temperature Ordering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TGEN["T_G\nGENERATOR\nHottest"]
    TCOND["T_C\nCONDENSER"]
    TABS["T_A\nABSORBER\nSame as T_C"]
    TEVAP["T_E\nEVAPORATOR\nColdest"]
    TGEN -->|Descending T| TCOND
    TCOND --> TABS
    TABS -->|Descending T| TEVAP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Every rectangular block represents one physical component of the absorption plant. Arrows show the **flow of working fluid** (refrigerant + absorbent solution), while dashed arrows show **heat transfer** with the surroundings. In an *ideal* system, all heat transfers are at the component's *own* temperature (no finite ΔT).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Define Ideal COP of an absorption refrigeration system. State its mathematical expression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Ideal COP of an absorption refrigeration system is the **maximum theoretical Coefficient of Performance** achieved when the entire system operates reversibly (no irreversibilities, no pump work).

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}
$$

where $Q_E$ is the refrigeration effect, $Q_G$ is the heat input at the generator, and $T_E$, $T_G$, $T_A$ are the absolute temperatures (in Kelvin) of the evaporator, generator, and absorber respectively.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for stating that T_A = T_C.)*

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Pattern]**

**Q2. Why is the COP of an actual absorption system always less than the Ideal COP? List any three reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Pressure drops** in the condenser, evaporator, and solution lines cause irreversibilities.
2. **Heat losses** to the surroundings from the generator, absorber, and piping reduce the effective $Q_G$.
3. **Non-isothermal heat transfer** at finite temperature differences in the generator and absorber (real ΔT ≠ 0).
4. *(Optional 4th)* Pump work $W_p$ is non-zero in real systems.

*(1 mark each for any three correct reasons.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — Choose Either A or B)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Ideal COP** of an absorption refrigeration system operating between a generator temperature $T_G$, evaporator temperature $T_E$, and absorber temperature $T_A$. State clearly all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The system operates as a *fully reversible* cycle.
2. Steady-state, steady-flow conditions.
3. The absorber and condenser operate at the *same* temperature: $T_A = T_C$.
4. The pump work is *negligible* in the ideal reversible case.
5. All temperatures are absolute (Kelvin).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Energy balance** (cyclic, $\Delta U = 0$):
$$Q_E + Q_G = Q_A + Q_C \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Entropy balance** (reversible, $\Delta S_{cycle} = 0$):
$$\frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_A}{T_A} + \frac{Q_C}{T_C} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

With $T_A = T_C$, equation (ii) becomes:
$$\frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_A + Q_C}{T_A} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

Substituting (i) into (iii):
$$\frac{Q_E}{T_E} + \frac{Q_G}{T_G} = \frac{Q_E + Q_G}{T_A}$$

Multiplying by $T_E T_G T_A$:
$$Q_E T_G T_A + Q_G T_E T_A = Q_E T_E T_G + Q_G T_E T_G$$

Rearranging:
$$Q_E T_G (T_A - T_E) = Q_G T_E (T_G - T_A)$$

$$
\boxed{\;COP_{ideal} = \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}\;}
$$

---

**(b)** An ideal ammonia-water absorption refrigerator produces **10 tonnes of refrigeration (TR)**. The evaporator, absorber, and generator temperatures are **−10 °C, 30 °C, and 150 °C** respectively. Determine:
   (i) The Ideal COP.
   (ii) The heat input rate at the generator.
   (iii) The rate of heat rejection.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Convert temperatures to Kelvin:**
- $T_E = -10 + 273 = 263$ K
- $T_A = 30 + 273 = 303$ K
- $T_G = 150 + 273 = 423$ K

**Check feasibility:** $T_G$ (423) > $T_A$ (303) > $T_E$ (263) ✓

**(i) Ideal COP:**

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)} = \frac{263 \times (423 - 303)}{423 \times (303 - 263)}
$$

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{263 \times 120}{423 \times 40} = \frac{31{,}560}{16{,}920} \approx 1.866
$$

**(1 mark for setup, 1 mark for numerical answer ~ 1.87)**

**(ii) Heat input rate at generator:**

1 TR = 3.517 kW. So $Q_E = 10 \times 3.517 = 35.17$ kW.

$$
Q_G = \frac{Q_E}{COP_{ideal}} = \frac{35.17}{1.866} \approx 18.85 \text{ kW}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Rate of heat rejection (at absorber + condenser combined):**

By energy balance:
$$
Q_A + Q_C = Q_E + Q_G = 35.17 + 18.85 = 54.02 \text{ kW}
$$

**(2 marks for energy balance and final answer.)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Pattern]*

**(a)** Compare the **Ideal COP of an absorption refrigerator** with the **Carnot COP of a vapor-compression refrigerator** operating between the same temperature limits. Under what condition do they become equal? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Carnot COP of a vapor compression refrigerator:**
$$
COP_{Carnot} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}
$$
This depends only on $T_E$ and $T_C$ (the lowest and highest cycle temperatures).

**Ideal COP of an absorption refrigerator:**
$$
COP_{ideal,abs} = \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}
$$
This depends on **three** independent temperatures.

For **equality**, set them equal:
$$
\frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} = \frac{T_E \,(T_G - T_A)}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}
$$

Cancel $T_E$:
$$
\frac{1}{T_C - T_E} = \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G \,(T_A - T_E)}
$$

Cross-multiply:
$$
T_G (T_A - T_E) = (T_C - T_E)(T_G - T_A)
$$

$$
T_G T_A - T_G T_E = T_C T_G - T_C T_A - T_E T_G + T_E T_A
$$

The $-T_G T_E$ terms cancel:
$$
T_G T_A = T_C T_G - T_C T_A + T_E T_A
$$

$$
T_G T_A + T_C T_A - T_E T_A = T_C T_G
$$

$$
T_A (T_G + T_C - T_E) = T_C T_G
$$

**Condition for equality:**
$$
\boxed{\;T_A = \frac{T_C \, T_G}{T_G + T_C - T_E}\;}
$$

In the **special case** where $T_A = T_C$ (i.e., the absorber and condenser are at the same temperature, and $T_G = T_C$, which is unphysical — generator cannot equal condenser), the expression degenerates. Practically, $COP_{Carnot} > COP_{ideal,abs}$ when the absorption system uses heat at temperatures between $T_E$ and $T_C$, because **Carnot benefits from compression between $T_E$ and $T_C$** while **absorption benefits from the heat lift between $T_A$ and $T_G$**.

**(7 marks total: 2 marks for stating both formulas, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for the physical interpretation.)**

---

**(b)** A solar-powered ideal LiBr-water absorption chiller must provide **5 TR of cooling** at **7 °C chilled water temperature**. The absorber/condenser is cooled by ambient water at **32 °C**, and the solar collector provides heat at **95 °C**. Calculate:
   (i) Ideal COP.
   (ii) Solar collector area required if the collector efficiency is 40 % and incident solar radiation is 800 W/m².
   (iii) Comment on the second-law efficiency if the actual COP is measured as **0.45**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Temperatures in Kelvin:**
- $T_E = 7 + 273 = 280$ K
- $T_A = T_C = 32 + 273 = 305$ K
- $T_G = 95 + 273 = 368$ K

**(i) Ideal COP:**

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{T_E (T_G - T_A)}{T_G (T_A - T_E)} = \frac{280 \times (368 - 305)}{368 \times (305 - 280)}
$$

$$
COP_{ideal} = \frac{280 \times 63}{368 \times 25} = \frac{17{,}640}{9{,}200} \approx 1.917
$$

**(1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer ~ 1.92)**

**(ii) Solar collector area:**

Cooling load: $Q_E = 5 \times 3.517 = 17.585$ kW

Heat required at generator:
$$
Q_G = \frac{Q_E}{COP_{ideal}} = \frac{17.585}{1.917} \approx 9.17 \text{ kW}
$$

Useful heat from solar collector at 40 % efficiency:
$$
\dot{Q}_{solar,useful} = 800 \text{ W/m}^2 \times 0.40 = 320 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

Collector area:
$$
A = \frac{Q_G}{\dot{Q}_{solar,useful}} = \frac{9170}{320} \approx 28.66 \text{ m}^2
$$

**(2 marks for area calculation.)**

**(iii) Second-Law Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{actual}}{COP_{ideal}} = \frac{0.45}{1.917} \approx 0.235 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 23.5\%
$$

**(2 marks)** — Comment: A second-law efficiency of ~23 % is typical for real solar absorption chillers, indicating significant room for improvement via better heat exchangers, evacuated-tube collectors, and CPC concentrators.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Ideal-COP Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K.** A frequent 1-mark deduction. Always check units first.
> 2. **Sign error in the formula.** Using $(T_E - T_A)$ instead of $(T_A - T_E)$ in the denominator yields a *negative* COP — markers deduct 2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the feasibility check.** Students should verify $T_G > T_A > T_E$ before quoting a numerical COP.
> 4. **Confusing Ideal COP with Carnot COP.** They are *not* the same unless a special condition on $T_A$ holds. Examiners specifically test this distinction in 14-mark problems.
> 5. **Mis-stating assumptions.** The assumption $T_A = T_C$ **must** be declared explicitly when using the simplified formula.
> 6. **Forgetting to include pump work** in the actual COP formula: $COP_{actual} = Q_E / (Q_G + W_p)$. Marks are awarded only when the denominator is complete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Ideal COP of Absorption System**

- **Definition:** Maximum (reversible) COP of an absorption refrigerator — the *thermodynamic ceiling*.
- **Authoritative Formula:**
  $$COP_{ideal} = \frac{T_E}{T_G} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_A - T_E}$$
- **Mandatory Units:** All temperatures in **Kelvin**.
- **Strict Feasibility:** $T_G > T_A > T_E$ must hold for a positive COP.
- **Standard Assumption:** $T_A = T_C$ (single cooling loop).
- **Derived From:** First Law (energy balance) + Second Law (entropy balance for reversible cycle).
- **Real-World Bench:** Actual COP is typically **30–60 %** of Ideal COP (second-law efficiency).
- **Key Insight:** Lowering $T_G$ (using low-grade waste heat) *reduces* COP; warming $T_A$ (hotter cooling water) *also* reduces COP.
- **Comparison Anchor:** $COP_{Carnot} = T_E / (T_C - T_E)$ uses only *two* temperatures; absorption Ideal COP uses *three*.
- **Exam Hot-Spots:** Derivation (7 marks), numerical application (7 marks), comparison with Carnot, feasibility checks.
- **Industrial Relevance:** Solar cooling, waste-heat recovery, tri-generation, gas-fired chillers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Lithium Bromide–Water (LiBr–H₂O) Vapor Absorption Refrigeration System** is a thermally-driven refrigeration cycle in which the mechanical compressor of a conventional vapor compression system is replaced by a **thermal compressor** consisting of an *Absorber*, a *Solution Pump*, a *Generator (Boiler)*, and a *Pressure-raising heat-driven fluid circuit*. In this system, **water (H₂O)** acts as the **refrigerant** and **anhydrous Lithium Bromide (LiBr) salt** acts as the **absorbent**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The LiBr–Water absorption system is a heat-operated refrigeration cycle employing a binary mixture, where low-pressure water vapor is absorbed into LiBr solution, pumped to high pressure, and then regenerated by heat input — producing chilled water suitable for comfort air-conditioning."*

| Parameter | LiBr–H₂O System | NH₃–H₂O System |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigerant | Water | Ammonia |
| Absorbent | LiBr Salt | Water |
| Operating Pressure | **Sub-atmospheric** (Vacuum) | High Pressure |
| Generator Temp | 70–95 °C | 130–200 °C |
| Typical Application | Comfort A/C only | Industrial / cold storage |
| Toxicity | Non-toxic | Toxic (NH₃) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Sponge–Squeeze" Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Salt That Loves Water"**

Imagine you have a **sponge soaked with water**. The sponge represents **LiBr salt**, and the water represents the **refrigerant (H₂O)**.

1. **Absorber (Sponge soaking):** The dry LiBr "sponge" greedily soaks up water vapor coming from the evaporator — exactly like a thirsty sponge absorbing spilled water. This absorption produces cooling.
2. **Pump (Squeezing action starts):** A small pump pushes the weak solution to high pressure. Think of this as the preparatory step before squeezing.
3. **Generator (Stove/heating):** Heat (from steam, hot water, or flue gas) is applied — **like heating a wet sponge on a stove**. The water evaporates out, leaving behind the dry LiBr "sponge" again.
4. **Condenser & Evaporator:** The released water vapor condenses back to liquid and re-evaporates in the evaporator — exactly like a normal compression cycle's cooling effect.

**The genius of absorption refrigeration:** Instead of using *electricity* to compress the vapor (like in your domestic fridge), we use *heat* (steam, solar, waste heat). This makes it **perfect for industrial waste heat recovery and large building A/C**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Critical Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical System Metrics (Industry Standards for KTU Board Problems):**

- **Crystallization Limit of LiBr:** Maximum safe concentration ≈ **65% (by mass)** — beyond this, LiBr salt precipitates as crystals and **chokes the absorber**.
- **Generator Temperature (T_G):** **80 °C – 100 °C** (low-grade heat source)
- **Condenser Temperature (T_C):** **35 °C – 45 °C**
- **Evaporator Temperature (T_E):** **5 °C – 10 °C**
- **Absorber Temperature (T_A):** **25 °C – 45 °C**
- **Coefficient of Performance (COP):** Ideal **≈ 0.7 – 1.2**; Actual **≈ 0.6 – 0.8**
- **Heat Source Energy:** Low-pressure steam, solar thermal, or waste heat at **70 – 110 °C**

## 1.4 Why LiBr–H₂O is Restricted to Air-Conditioning Only

> [!WARNING]
> The evaporator operates under **vacuum** because water (refrigerant) must boil at low temperature. Therefore, chilled water leaving the evaporator cannot fall below **0 °C** practically — limiting the system to **comfort cooling (above 5 °C)**. It is **NEVER used for sub-zero or freezing applications**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

The LiBr–H₂O absorption cycle operates on the **principle of pressure equalization through thermal energy and solution concentration gradients**.

### Component-wise Analysis:

1. **Generator (Boiler/Booster):**
   - Receives **strong LiBr solution** (high concentration) from the solution heat exchanger.
   - Heat input **Q_G** (steam or hot water) drives off water vapor.
   - **Output:** Weak/dilute LiBr solution + high-pressure refrigerant vapor (H₂O).
   - The weak solution flows to the absorber via the expansion valve.

2. **Condenser:**
   - Hot water vapor from the generator rejects heat **Q_C** to a cooling tower.
   - **Output:** High-pressure liquid refrigerant (H₂O).

3. **Evaporator:**
   - High-pressure liquid refrigerant passes through an **expansion valve** → flash evaporation at low pressure.
   - Water evaporates by absorbing heat **Q_E** from the chilled water circuit.
   - **Output:** Low-pressure water vapor (refrigerant).

4. **Absorber:**
   - Low-pressure water vapor from the evaporator is **absorbed** by the weak LiBr solution.
   - This is an **exothermic process** — heat **Q_A** must be rejected to the cooling tower.
   - **Output:** Strong (rich) LiBr solution.

5. **Solution Pump:**
   - Pumps the strong solution from absorber (low pressure) → generator (high pressure).
   - Requires **negligible work input** (W_p ≈ 0.5% of Q_G) compared to vapor compression.

6. **Solution Heat Exchanger (SHX):**
   - **Saves energy** by pre-heating the weak solution going to the generator (using hot strong solution returning).
   - Improves actual COP by **15–30%**.

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Cycle on P-T Diagram

```
On P-T Diagram:
- Generator: High P, High T (region 3)
- Condenser: High P, lower T (region 4)
- Evaporator: Low P, Low T (region 1)
- Absorber: Low P, intermediate T (region 2)
```

The cycle is a **closed loop** with refrigerant undergoing phase change (liquid ↔ vapor) and solution undergoing **concentration change** (weak ↔ strong).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{T_E \, (T_G - T_C)}{(T_G - T_A)(T_C - T_E)}$ | Max (Reversible) COP — Carnot absorption cycle | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G + W_P}$ | Actual COP (includes pump work) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{COP}_{\text{no-pump}} = \dfrac{Q_E}{Q_G}$ | Simplified COP neglecting pump work | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $Q_E = m_r \, (h_{fg,\text{evap}})$ | Refrigeration effect (mass × enthalpy of vaporization) | kW |
| 5 | $Q_G = m_r \, (h_g - h_f) + m_s \, c_{p,s} \, (T_{G,\text{out}} - T_{G,\text{in}})$ | Generator heat input | kW |
| 6 | $Q_A = m_r \, h_{fg,\text{abs}} + m_s \, c_{p,s} \, \Delta T$ | Absorber heat rejection | kW |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{\dot{m}_r \, (x_G - x_A)}{x_G - x_A}$ | Solution circulation ratio (mass balance) | kg/s |
| 8 | $\text{COP}_{\text{absorption}} = \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot,ref}} \times \eta_{\text{II}}$ | Efficiency relative to Carnot refrigeration | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}}}$ | Second-law efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $f = \dfrac{x_G}{x_G - x_A}$ | Circulation ratio (kg solution/kg refrigerant) | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Legend (Standard for KTU Board):**
> - $T_E$ = Evaporator temperature (K)
> - $T_C$ = Condenser temperature (K)
> - $T_G$ = Generator temperature (K)
> - $T_A$ = Absorber temperature (K)
> - $x_G$ = Strong solution concentration (kg LiBr / kg solution)
> - $x_A$ = Weak solution concentration
> - $m_r$ = Refrigerant mass flow rate (kg/s)
> - $m_s$ = Solution mass flow rate (kg/s)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **District Cooling Plants:** LiBr–H₂O chillers of 500–5000 TR (Tons of Refrigeration) are used in commercial complexes and airports.
2. **Solar Cooling:** Pairs with parabolic solar collectors for off-grid A/C.
3. **Waste Heat Recovery:** Utilizes low-grade heat from engine exhaust, geothermal sources.
4. **Combined Heat and Power (CHP):** Increases overall plant efficiency by **20–30%**.
5. **Trigeneration Systems:** Provides cooling, heating, and power simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum (Ideal/Carnot) COP of Absorption Cycle

### Setup:
We consider the absorption cycle as a **composite of two Carnot cycles**:
- **Cycle A:** Heat engine operating between T_G and T_A
- **Cycle B:** Refrigerator operating between T_C and T_E

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Consider the **heat engine** portion (Generator – Absorber path) operating between generator temperature $T_G$ and absorber temperature $T_A$.

The heat $Q_G$ is supplied at $T_G$, and heat $Q_A$ is rejected at $T_A$.

By Carnot efficiency:
$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot,HE}} = 1 - \frac{T_A}{T_G}
$$

**Step 2:** The work output of this equivalent heat engine:
$$
W_{\text{eq}} = Q_G \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{T_A}{T_G} \right)
$$

**Step 3:** This equivalent work is now fed to a **Carnot refrigerator** operating between condenser temperature $T_C$ (high) and evaporator temperature $T_E$ (low).

The Carnot refrigerator COP:
$$
\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot,Ref}} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}
$$

**Step 4:** The refrigeration effect produced:
$$
Q_E = W_{\text{eq}} \cdot \text{COP}_{\text{Carnot,Ref}}
$$

Substituting $W_{\text{eq}}$:
$$
Q_E = Q_G \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{T_A}{T_G} \right) \cdot \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E}
$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging to find COP = $Q_E / Q_G$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP}_{\text{max}} &= \frac{Q_E}{Q_G} = \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \left( 1 - \frac{T_A}{T_G} \right) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{T_E}{T_C - T_E} \cdot \frac{T_G - T_A}{T_G}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Combining into the standard form:

$$
\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{T_E \cdot (T_G - T_A)}{T_G \cdot (T_C - T_E)}}
$$

This is the **standard KTU Board form** for the maximum (reversible) COP of a vapor absorption system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation Logic:** The formula combines the heat-engine efficiency with the refrigerator COP. Each temperature must be in **Kelvin** for dimensional consistency. The result is always **less than 1** for a single-effect LiBr–H₂O chiller.

## 3.2 Analytical Example: COP Calculation for a Building Chiller

### Given Data (KTU Board Style):
- Evaporator temperature: $T_E = 5\,°\text{C} = 278\,\text{K}$
- Condenser temperature: $T_C = 40\,°\text{C} = 313\,\text{K}$
- Generator temperature: $T_G = 95\,°\text{C} = 368\,\text{K}$
- Absorber temperature: $T_A = 35\,°\text{C} = 308\,\text{K}$

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin (already done).

**Step 2:** Apply the ideal COP formula:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{T_E \cdot (T_G - T_A)}{T_G \cdot (T_C - T_E)}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{278 \cdot (368 - 308)}{368 \cdot (313 - 278)}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator and denominator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 278 \cdot 60 = 16{,}680 \\
\text{Denominator} &= 368 \cdot 35 = 12{,}880
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Final ratio:

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{16{,}680}{12{,}880} \approx 1.295
$$

**Step 6:** Apply practical derating factor (η_II ≈ 0.4 for LiBr system):

$$
\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = 0.4 \times 1.295 \approx 0.518
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** For every 1 kW of heat supplied to the generator, this chiller produces **0.518 kW of cooling** — meaning it needs **~1.93 kW of heat for 1 kW of cooling**.

## 3.3 Energy and Mass Balance Matrix

| Component | Mass Balance | Energy Balance | Working Substance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Generator | $\dot{m}_s = \dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_w$ | $Q_G = \dot{m}_r \cdot h_{fg} + \dot{m}_s \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Water leaves as vapor |
| Condenser | $\dot{m}_r$ constant | $Q_C = \dot{m}_r \cdot h_{fg}$ (condenser) | Water condenses |
| Evaporator | $\dot{m}_r$ constant | $Q_E = \dot{m}_r \cdot h_{fg}$ | Water evaporates |
| Absorber | $\dot{m}_w + \dot{m}_r = \dot{m}_s$ (rich) | $Q_A = \dot{m}_s \cdot h_{abs} - \dot{m}_w \cdot h_v$ | Solution heats up |
| Pump | $\dot{m}_s$ constant | $W_P = \dot{m}_s \cdot \Delta P \cdot v$ | Pressure rise |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**"Why is LiBr used as the absorbent and water as the refrigerant in a vapor absorption system used for air-conditioning? Justify with two key properties."**

**Model Answer:**
1. **High Affinity for Water Vapor:** LiBr has an extremely strong hygroscopic nature — it absorbs water vapor readily even at low partial pressures, enabling efficient absorption at the low evaporator pressures required.
2. **Non-Volatility:** LiBr has a **very low vapor pressure** at generator temperatures (≈95 °C). This means the salt does **not vaporize** along with water in the generator, ensuring **pure refrigerant** enters the condenser. (NH₃–H₂O system suffers from volatility issues.)
3. **Non-Toxic & Non-Corrosive (with inhibitors):** Safe for occupied buildings.
4. **High Heat of Absorption:** Releases large quantity of heat in the absorber, which is easily rejected to a cooling tower.

*(3 marks — complete answer covering 2–3 properties)*

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**"List the major components of a single-effect LiBr–H₂O absorption chiller and state the function of the solution heat exchanger."**

**Model Answer:**
**Major Components:**
1. Generator, 2. Condenser, 3. Evaporator, 4. Absorber, 5. Solution Pump, 6. Expansion Valves, 7. Solution Heat Exchanger, 8. Cooling Tower.

**Function of Solution Heat Exchanger (SHX):**
The SHX transfers heat from the hot, strong LiBr solution returning from the absorber to the cold, weak LiBr solution leaving the generator. This:
- **Pre-heats** the weak solution (reducing generator heat input Q_G).
- **Cools** the strong solution (reducing absorber heat rejection Q_A).
- Improves the **actual COP by 15–30%**.

*(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: (a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a **single-effect LiBr–H₂O vapor absorption refrigeration system**. Label all components and indicate the direction of flow of refrigerant and solution. **(7 marks)**
