**H2O** has **very low volatility** and **strong affinity for NH3** at low temperature, making it an excellent absorber. - **H2O** has a **high boiling point** (100 °C) compared to NH3 ($-33\,°C$) — easy separation in the generator. **(Award 1 mark per correct stage, 2 marks for properties justification, 1 mark for schematic label clarity)** --- **Solution to (b):** **Given:** - $T_L = -15 + 273 = 258\,\text{K}$ - $T_H = 30 + 273 = 303\,\text{K}$ - $T_4 = 150 + 273 = 423\,\text{K}$ **(i) Carnot COP of VARS:** $$\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}} = \left(\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_4 - T_H}{T_4}\right)$$ $$= \left(\dfrac{258}{303 - 258}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{423 - 303}{423}\right)$$ $$= \left(\dfrac{258}{45}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{120}{423}\right)$$ $$= 5.7333 \cdot 0.2837$$ $$\boxed{\;\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}} \approx 1.627\;}$$ **(ii) Carnot COP of VCRS:** $$\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{258}{45} = \boxed{5.733}$$ **(iii) Practical Significance:** - The VARS COP is only **28.4 %** of the VCRS Carnot limit, so VARS appears inefficient. - However, the heat input to VARS is often **free or low-grade waste heat** (e.g., from a solar concentrator, factory exhaust, or geothermal source). - When heat is free, the **primary-energy utilization** of VARS can exceed a VCRS that uses electricity generated from fossil fuels. - For grid-independent or remote applications (Kerala backwaters, rural cold storage), VARS is the only practical choice. **(Award 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) including practical insight)** --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Points in This Topic — KTU Examiner Insights** > > 1. **Confusing COP definitions:** Students often write $\text{COP} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_c}$ instead of $\dfrac{Q_e}{W}$ for VCRS — full 2-mark penalty. > 2. **Skipping the heat-source factor:** When asked to derive the VARS COP, many students write only $T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ and forget the $(T_4 - T_H)/T_4$ term. This loses **3–4 marks**. > 3. **Not stating assumptions:** The Carnot VARS derivation requires you to say *"all processes are reversible"* and *"no pump work is considered."* Examiners explicitly look for this — 1 mark lost if omitted. > 4. **Forgetting to convert °C to K:** Numerical problems like SECTION 3.3 must use Kelvin. Adding 273 incorrectly (using 273.15 sometimes, 273 in others) loses 1 mark. > 5. **Writing "VARS is always less efficient":** The CORRECT statement is *"Carnot VARS COP is less than Carnot VCRS COP between the same temperature limits — but the primary energy advantage can reverse this for free heat sources."* Examiners award the **practical interpretation mark** (often 2 marks) only for this nuanced view. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: VARS vs VCRS** > > ✅ VCRS = **mechanical compression** + condenser + expansion + evaporator. COP = $Q_e/W$. > > ✅ VARS = **absorber + pump + generator + condenser + expansion + evaporator** + rectifier. COP = $Q_e/Q_{\text{gen}}$. > > ✅ Carnot VARS COP = $\left(\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_4 - T_H}{T_4}\right)$. > > ✅ Carnot VARS COP < Carnot VCRS COP for the same $T_L, T_H$ — proven by ratio $(T_4 - T_H)/T_4 < 1$. > > ✅ **NH3–H2O** is the most common VARS pair (NH3 = refrigerant, H2O = absorbent). > > ✅ **LiBr–H2O** is used when H2O is the refrigerant (chillers above 0 °C only). > > ✅ VARS advantages: no moving parts (low noise), low maintenance, runs on heat (waste/solar), suitable for large capacities. > > ✅ VARS disadvantages: low COP, bulky equipment, rectifier needed, hard to modulate capacity. > > ✅ VCRS advantages: high COP, compact, easy capacity control via inverter. > > ✅ VCRS disadvantages: noisy, high maintenance, mechanical wear. > > ✅ **Use °K (Kelvin)** for all temperature ratios in COP calculations — never use °C. > > ✅ State-point notation: VARS uses 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 (eight points); VCRS uses 1, 2, 3, 4 (four points). > > ✅ **Pump work in VARS is negligible** (≈1–2 % of equivalent VCRS compressor work). > > ✅ The **COP ratio** $\dfrac{\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}}}{\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS}}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_H}{T_4}$ is a frequently asked quick numerical. > > ✅ For KTU 2024: Remember the **practical advantage of VARS** — it converts *free or waste heat* into cooling, making it valuable for off-grid, solar, and industrial waste-heat applications. ---

topics.title: Three fluid absorption system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Three fluid absorption system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

The **Three-Fluid Absorption Refrigeration System** (also known as the **Electrolux Refrigerator** or the **Platen-Munters Absorption System**) is a continuous, gas-fired refrigeration cycle that uses **three working fluids** operating in a sealed, pump-less arrangement. The three fluids are:

| Sl. No. | Role of Fluid | Conventional Working Fluid | Alternative |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Refrigerant** | **Ammonia (NH₃)** | — |
| 2 | **Absorbent** | **Water (H₂O)** | — |
| 3 | **Auxiliary / Inert / Pressure-Equalizing Gas** | **Hydrogen (H₂)** | Helium (He) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Block:** A three-fluid absorption system is a heat-operated refrigeration cycle in which an inert non-condensable gas (hydrogen or helium) is introduced into the evaporator–absorber loop to equalize the total pressure between the absorber and the evaporator. This eliminates the requirement of a mechanical pump and allows the entire system to operate on **natural thermosiphon / gravity flow** driven purely by density differences.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sponge + Helper Blower" Picture

Imagine you have a **sponge soaked in perfume (ammonia)** sitting in a sealed room. To get the perfume smell out, you need to push air through the sponge to carry the perfume away. Now imagine instead:

- The **sponge** = water (it absorbs ammonia strongly).
- The **perfume** = ammonia vapor (the actual cooling agent).
- A **silent electric fan** blows a helper gas (**hydrogen**) through the system instead of using a water pump.

Now replace the sponge room with an **evaporator** kept inside your refrigerator. As ammonia evaporates into the helper gas, it absorbs heat (this is the cooling effect). The ammonia-laden helper gas then drifts up to a chamber where the **sponge (water) re-absorbs the ammonia**, releasing the clean helper gas back down to the evaporator. Meanwhile, the saturated sponge (rich ammonia-water solution) is gently heated in a chamber (the generator) — the perfume (ammonia) is boiled out, leaving a weak sponge (weak solution) that drifts back down.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the third fluid?** In the classical two-fluid ammonia-water absorption system, a **pump** is mandatory to push the strong solution from the absorber to the generator. In the three-fluid system, the inert gas acts as a **pressure-equalizer** between the absorber and the evaporator. Because the total pressure is the same in both vessels (only the **partial pressure** of ammonia differs), the liquid ammonia-hydrogen mixture can flow naturally from the high-vapor-pressure evaporator to the low-vapor-pressure absorber. **No pump → No moving parts → Whisper-quiet operation.**

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metric

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** for a three-fluid absorption system is purely **thermal**:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{three-fluid}} = \frac{\text{Desired Refrigerating Effect } (RE)}{\text{Heat Supplied in the Generator } (Q_{\text{gen}})}$$

Because there is **no compressor and no pump work** ($W_{\text{net}} = 0$), the COP depends only on the heat input at the generator. Typical domestic Electrolux units achieve a **COP of approximately 0.20 to 0.30**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Components — Function-by-Function Breakdown

The three-fluid absorption system consists of the following major subassemblies, all hermetically sealed inside a single steel cabinet for domestic refrigerators:

1. **Generator (Boiler / Bubbler)**: A chamber at the bottom heated externally (gas flame or electric heater). It contains the **rich ammonia-water solution**. Heat drives out ammonia vapor.
2. **Rectifier (Analyzer + Separator)**: A small vertical column above the generator. Its job is to **strip residual water vapor** from the ammonia vapor leaving the generator. Without it, water would reach the condenser and freeze, blocking the flow.
3. **Condenser**: Ammonia vapor (now pure) enters coils where it rejects heat to the surroundings and condenses into liquid ammonia.
4. **Evaporator**: Liquid ammonia meets **hydrogen gas** at low ammonia partial pressure. Because the **total pressure is uniform** but ammonia partial pressure is low, ammonia **evaporates at a temperature well below 0 °C**, producing the refrigerating effect.
5. **Absorber**: The ammonia-rich hydrogen mixture rises (or flows) into the absorber, where **weak solution (water)** sprays down. Water greedily absorbs ammonia, leaving pure hydrogen to flow back to the evaporator.
6. **Heat Exchanger (Solution Heat Exchanger, SHX)**: The hot weak solution leaving the generator preheats the cold strong solution entering the generator — a critical efficiency booster.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind every step is the key to scoring in KTU derivations.** Examiners frequently award marks for explaining the *physical reason* a step occurs.

- **Step 1 — Heating the rich solution**: In the generator, external heat $Q_{\text{gen}}$ raises the temperature of the rich NH₃–H₂O solution, causing ammonia to vaporize preferentially (NH₃ is more volatile than H₂O).
- **Step 2 — Rectification**: The vapor mixture passes through the rectifier, where it is cooled just enough to condense and return water droplets, yielding **near-pure ammonia vapor**.
- **Step 3 — Condensation**: The pure ammonia vapor rejects latent heat $Q_{\text{cond}}$ to the ambient and condenses into **liquid ammonia** at high pressure.
- **Step 4 — Evaporation under partial vacuum**: Liquid ammonia enters the evaporator, where it meets hydrogen at the **same total pressure but lower ammonia partial pressure**. The lower partial pressure lowers the saturation temperature, enabling evaporation at the desired low temperature.
- **Step 5 — Absorption**: The NH₃–H₂ mixture rises into the absorber. Weak solution absorbs ammonia, releasing heat $Q_{\text{abs}}$ (which must be rejected to ambient). Hydrogen becomes pure again and returns to the evaporator under gravity (since hydrogen is the lightest gas and naturally rises).
- **Step 6 — Solution circulation**: The **strong solution** (rich in NH₃) formed in the absorber is returned to the generator via the **solution heat exchanger**, completing the loop without any pump.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **high-yield equation bank** for any KTU numerical or derivation on the three-fluid absorption system.

| Sl. No. | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{RE}{Q_{\text{gen}}}$ | COP definition (no work input) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $RE = \dot{m}_{r} \cdot h_{fg,\,evap}$ | Refrigerating effect (mass basis) | kW |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{r} = \dot{m}_{a} (x_{s} - x_{w})$ | Mass of refrigerant circulated per kg of absorbent | kg/s |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{gen}} = \dot{m}_{a} \cdot h_{gs} + \dot{m}_{r} \cdot h_{g,\text{NH}_3} - \dot{m}_{a} \cdot h_{gw}$ | Heat supplied in generator (energy balance) | kW |
| 5 | $h_{gs}, h_{gw}$ | Enthalpy of strong & weak solution at generator pressure | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $h_{g,\text{NH}_3}$ | Enthalpy of ammonia vapor leaving generator | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $Q_{\text{abs}} = \dot{m}_{a} \cdot h_{as} - \dot{m}_{a} \cdot h_{aw} - \dot{m}_{r} \cdot h_{f,\text{NH}_3}$ | Heat rejected in absorber | kW |
| 8 | $P_{\text{total}} = p_{\text{NH}_3} + p_{\text{H}_2}$ | Dalton's law of partial pressures in evaporator | bar |
| 9 | $p_{\text{NH}_3}^{\text{evap}} = P_{\text{total}} \cdot y_{\text{NH}_3}$ | Partial pressure of ammonia in evaporator | bar |
| 10 | $\text{COP}_{\max} = \dfrac{T_{e}}{T_{g}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{g} - T_{a}}{T_{a} - T_{e}}$ | Maximum (reversible) COP for absorption cycle | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation key**: $\dot{m}_{a}$ = mass flow rate of absorbent (water), $\dot{m}_{r}$ = mass flow rate of refrigerant (ammonia), $x_{s}$ = concentration of ammonia in **strong** solution, $x_{w}$ = concentration of ammonia in **weak** solution, $h_{gs}$ = enthalpy of strong solution leaving absorber (entering generator), $h_{gw}$ = enthalpy of weak solution leaving generator (entering absorber).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Domestic Refrigeration**: The classic *Electrolux Servel* gas refrigerator used in off-grid homes, RVs, and remote cabins — runs on LPG without electricity.
- **Marine & RV Applications**: Whisper-quiet operation with no moving parts is ideal where vibration is undesirable.
- **Solar-Powered Cooling**: Since the only energy input is heat, it can be paired with solar thermal collectors for **solar refrigeration** — a sustainable alternative to vapor-compression systems.
- **Historical Significance**: Demonstrated the viability of heat-activated cooling long before vapor-compression refrigerators dominated the market.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: COP of a Three-Fluid Absorption System

### 3.1.1 Energy Balance on the Generator

Consider the generator as the control volume. The strong solution of mass $\dot{m}_{a}$ enters with enthalpy $h_{gs}$ (saturated liquid at generator pressure) and leaves as weak solution of enthalpy $h_{gw}$. The refrigerant ammonia vapor produced has mass $\dot{m}_{r}$ and enthalpy $h_{g,\text{NH}_3}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy In} &= \text{Energy Out} \\[4pt]
Q_{\text{gen}} + \dot{m}_{a} \cdot h_{gs} &= \dot{m}_{a} \cdot h_{gw} + \dot{m}_{r} \cdot h_{g,\text{NH}_3} \\[4pt]
Q_{\text{gen}} &= \dot{m}_{a} \cdot (h_{gw} - h_{gs}) + \dot{m}_{r} \cdot h_{g,\text{NH}_3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** This is the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a steady-flow control volume, neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes.

### 3.1.2 Mass Balance on the Absorbent Loop

The ammonia mass leaving the generator must equal the ammonia mass absorbed in the absorber:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{r} &= \dot{m}_{a} \cdot x_{s} - \dot{m}_{a} \cdot x_{w} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{r} &= \dot{m}_{a} \cdot (x_{s} - x_{w})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The strong solution carries $x_{s}$ kg ammonia per kg water; the weak solution carries only $x_{w}$. The difference is the ammonia boiled out in the generator.

### 3.1.3 Refrigerating Effect in the Evaporator

In the evaporator, ammonia liquid of mass $\dot{m}_{r}$ evaporates at the saturation enthalpy of vaporization $h_{fg}$ corresponding to the evaporator partial pressure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RE &= \dot{m}_{r} \cdot h_{fg,\text{evap}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The latent heat absorbed per kilogram equals the enthalpy difference between the saturated vapor and saturated liquid at the evaporator pressure. This is the *useful cooling*.

### 3.1.4 Assembling the COP

Combining the above expressions into the COP definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{RE}{Q_{\text{gen}}} \\[4pt]
\text{COP} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{r} \cdot h_{fg,\text{evap}}}{\dot{m}_{a} \cdot (h_{gw} - h_{gs}) + \dot{m}_{r} \cdot h_{g,\text{NH}_3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Substitute the expression for $\dot{m}_{r}$ in terms of $\dot{m}_{a}$ if a ratio form is desired:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{(x_{s} - x_{w}) \cdot h_{fg,\text{evap}}}{(h_{gw} - h_{gs}) + (x_{s} - x_{w}) \cdot h_{g,\text{NH}_3}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **board-exam-ready COP expression** for the three-fluid absorption system.

## 3.2 Derivation: Pressure-Equalization Logic (Dalton's Law in Evaporator)

The defining feature of the three-fluid system is that the **total pressure is uniform** between the evaporator and absorber, but the **partial pressure of ammonia** differs drastically.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At evaporator exit (top):} \quad P_{\text{total}} &= p_{\text{NH}_3}^{\text{evap}} + p_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{evap}} \\[4pt]
\text{At absorber exit (bottom):} \quad P_{\text{total}} &= p_{\text{NH}_3}^{\text{abs}} + p_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{abs}} \\[4pt]
\text{Since } P_{\text{total}} \text{ is the same:} \quad p_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{evap}} &< p_{\text{H}_2}^{\text{abs}} \\[4pt]
\text{Therefore:} \quad p_{\text{NH}_3}^{\text{evap}} &> p_{\text{NH}_3}^{\text{abs}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Because ammonia is absorbed by water in the absorber, its partial pressure drops drastically. Hydrogen, being insoluble, retains its high partial pressure. The driving force for hydrogen to flow back to the evaporator is this **partial-pressure imbalance** — no pump needed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component-by-Component Energy & Mass Audit

| Component | Inlets (Mass) | Outlets (Mass) | Energy In | Energy Out | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Generator** | Strong solution $\dot{m}_{a}$ | Weak solution $\dot{m}_{a}$; NH₃ vapor $\dot{m}_{r}$ | $Q_{\text{gen}}$ | None | Boil off NH₃ from solution |
| **Rectifier** | NH₃ + H₂O vapor | Pure NH₃ vapor; liquid H₂O return | None | $Q_{\text{rect}}$ | Remove water from NH₃ |
| **Condenser** | NH₃ vapor $\dot{m}_{r}$ | Liquid NH₃ $\dot{m}_{r}$ | None | $Q_{\text{cond}}$ | Liquefy NH₃ at high pressure |
| **Evaporator** | Liquid NH₃ $\dot{m}_{r}$; H₂ gas | NH₃–H₂ mixture | None | $RE$ (cooling) | Evaporate NH₃ at low partial pressure |
| **Absorber** | NH₃–H₂ mixture; weak solution | Pure H₂; strong solution | None | $Q_{\text{abs}}$ | Absorb NH₃ into water |
| **SHX** | Hot weak solution; cold strong solution | Cooled weak; preheated strong | None | None (internal) | Improve cycle efficiency |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A three-fluid absorption refrigerator uses NH₃–H₂O–H₂. The strong solution has $x_{s} = 0.40$, weak solution $x_{w} = 0.20$, latent heat of ammonia at evaporator pressure $h_{fg} = 1100$ kJ/kg, enthalpy of ammonia vapor from generator $h_{g,\text{NH}_3} = 1450$ kJ/kg, and enthalpy difference $(h_{gw} - h_{gs}) = -200$ kJ/kg. Calculate the COP per kg of water circulated.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{(x_{s} - x_{w}) \cdot h_{fg}}{(h_{gw} - h_{gs}) + (x_{s} - x_{w}) \cdot h_{g,\text{NH}_3}} \\[4pt]
\text{COP} &= \frac{(0.40 - 0.20) \cdot 1100}{(-200) + (0.40 - 0.20) \cdot 1450} \\[4pt]
\text{COP} &= \frac{(0.20)(1100)}{-200 + (0.20)(1450)} \\[4pt]
\text{COP} &= \frac{220}{-200 + 290} \\[4pt]
\text{COP} &= \frac{220}{90} \\[4pt]
\text{COP} &\approx 2.44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** COP ≈ **2.44** (Note: realistic values would yield a lower COP; the numbers above are illustrative. Always cross-check with realistic property tables.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Three-Fluid Absorption System

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional flow of the three fluids through each subassembly. It follows the **Alpha-Rule for Node IDs** (no reserved keywords, purely alphanumeric prefixes) and uses **clean uppercase text** inside double-quoted labels (no markdown formatting).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["GENERATOR: External heat Qgen drives NH3 out of rich NH3-H2O solution"]
    A2["RECTIFIER: Strips residual H2O vapor from NH3 stream"]
    A3["CONDENSER: Pure NH3 vapor rejects Qcond and condenses to liquid"]
    A4["EVAPORATOR: Liquid NH3 evaporates into H2 at low partial pressure producing RE"]
    A5["ABSORBER: Weak H2O solution absorbs NH3 from NH3-H2 mixture releasing Qabs"]
    A6["SOLUTION HEAT EXCHANGER: Preheats strong solution using hot weak solution"]

    B1["NH3 VAPOR FLOW PATH: Generator to Rectifier to Condenser to Evaporator"]
    B2["INERT GAS H2 LOOP: Evaporator to Absorber back to Evaporator driven by partial pressure imbalance"]
    B3["SOLUTION CIRCULATION LOOP: Absorber to SHX to Generator to Absorber driven by density difference and gravity"]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> A1

    A1 -.rich NH3-H2O.-> A6
    A6 -.preheated strong solution.-> A1
    A5 -.strong solution.-> A6
    A4 -.NH3-H2 mixture.-> A5
    A5 -.pure H2 returns.-> A4

    classDef heatNode fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    classDef coolNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef flowNode fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3 heatNode
    class A4,A5 coolNode
    class A6,B1,B2,B3 flowNode
```

## 4.2 State-Point Reference Diagram (Schematic Interpretation)

| State Point | Location | Phase | Typical Composition | Temperature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Generator exit (vapor) | Vapor | NH₃ (saturated) | High (≈ 100–180 °C) |
| **2** | Condenser exit | Liquid | Pure NH₃ | High (≈ 30–40 °C) |
| **3** | Evaporator exit | Vapor + Gas | NH₃ + H₂ | Low (≈ −10 to −30 °C) |
| **4** | Absorber exit (liquid) | Liquid | Strong NH₃-H₂O solution | Moderate (≈ 30 °C) |
| **5** | Generator exit (liquid) | Liquid | Weak NH₃-H₂O solution | High |
| **6** | Absorber exit (gas) | Gas | Pure H₂ | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the schematic in the exam:** The arrows in the Mermaid block represent the *thermodynamic state transitions*, not necessarily physical piping. Always state the **direction of mass flow** and the **direction of heat flow** separately in your answer script. KTU examiners specifically look for the *three distinct loops* (refrigerant, inert gas, solution) being identified.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** List the **three working fluids** used in a three-fluid absorption refrigeration system and state the function of each.

**Model Answer:**
The three fluids are:
1. **Ammonia (NH₃)** — acts as the **refrigerant**; it absorbs heat by evaporating at low pressure inside the evaporator.
2. **Water (H₂O)** — acts as the **absorbent**; it absorbs ammonia vapor in the absorber to form a strong ammonia-water solution.
3. **Hydrogen (H₂) or Helium (He)** — acts as the **auxiliary inert gas**; it equalizes the total pressure between the evaporator and absorber, eliminating the need for a pump and enabling natural circulation.

*(3 marks — 1 mark for each fluid and its role)*

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Why is a **rectifier** necessary in a three-fluid absorption system? What would happen if it were removed?

**Model Answer:**
The rectifier removes **residual water vapor** from the ammonia vapor leaving the generator before it enters the condenser. If the rectifier were absent, water vapor would reach the condenser and **freeze inside the condenser coils** (since the condenser operates at low temperature), blocking the refrigerant flow and causing system failure. The rectifier ensures only **pure ammonia** enters the condenser, maintaining reliable operation.

*(3 marks — 2 marks for function, 1 mark for consequence of removal)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Q:** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **working of a three-fluid absorption refrigeration system**. List its **advantages and disadvantages** compared to a conventional vapor-compression system.

#### Part (a) — Schematic and Working (7 Marks)

**Solution Outline:**

1. **Sketch the schematic** showing the generator, rectifier, condenser, evaporator, absorber, and solution heat exchanger with arrows for refrigerant, inert gas, and solution loops. *(2 marks)*
2. **Working principle** (state the path of each fluid):
   - Heat $Q_{\text{gen}}$ supplied externally vaporizes NH₃ from the rich solution in the generator. *(1 mark)*
   - Vapor passes through the rectifier (water removed) and condenses in the condenser by rejecting $Q_{\text{cond}}$. *(1 mark)*
   - Liquid NH₃ flows to the evaporator where it meets H₂ at low partial pressure and evaporates, producing **refrigerating effect RE**. *(1 mark)*
   - NH₃–H₂ mixture enters the absorber; water absorbs NH₃, releasing $Q_{\text{abs}}$; pure H₂ returns to the evaporator. *(1 mark)*
   - Strong solution returns to the generator via SHX. *(1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — Advantages and Disadvantages (7 Marks)

**Advantages (4 marks):**
- **No moving parts** — completely silent, vibration-free operation.
- **No compressor or pump** — driven entirely by heat; ideal for off-grid solar/LPG operation.
- **Long life** — minimal mechanical wear.
- **Simple maintenance** — no lubrication, no sealing issues.

**Disadvantages (3 marks):**
- **Low COP** (typically 0.20–0.30) compared to vapor-compression systems (3.0–4.0).
- **High initial cost** and large physical size for the same capacity.
- **Slow response** to load changes (thermal inertia of generator).
- **Hydrogen leakage** can be hazardous and degrades performance.

---

### Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Q:** Derive an expression for the **COP of a three-fluid absorption system**. A system uses NH₃-H₂O-H₂ with strong solution concentration $x_s = 0.35$, weak solution $x_w = 0.15$, latent heat at evaporator $h_{fg} = 1050$ kJ/kg, ammonia vapor enthalpy from generator $h_{g,\text{NH}_3} = 1480$ kJ/kg, and $(h_{gw} - h_{gs}) = -180$ kJ/kg. Calculate the COP per kg of water.

#### Part (a) — COP Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step-by-step derivation:**

By mass balance on the absorbent: $\dot{m}_r = \dot{m}_a (x_s - x_w)$ *(2 marks)*

By energy balance on generator: $Q_{\text{gen}} = \dot{m}_a (h_{gw} - h_{gs}) + \dot{m}_r \cdot h_{g,\text{NH}_3}$ *(2 marks)*

Refrigerating effect: $RE = \dot{m}_r \cdot h_{fg,\text{evap}}$ *(1 mark)*

Therefore:

$$\text{COP} = \frac{(x_s - x_w) \cdot h_{fg,\text{evap}}}{(h_{gw} - h_{gs}) + (x_s - x_w) \cdot h_{g,\text{NH}_3}}$$ *(2 marks)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks)

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COP} &= \frac{(0.35 - 0.15) \cdot 1050}{(-180) + (0.35 - 0.15) \cdot 1480} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(0.20)(1050)}{-180 + (0.20)(1480)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{210}{-180 + 296} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{210}{116} \\[4pt]
\text{COP} &\approx 1.81
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Breakdown: 2 marks setup, 3 marks substitution, 2 marks final answer with units)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state "no work input":** Many students write $\text{COP} = RE / (Q_{\text{gen}} + W)$. In a three-fluid system, $W = 0$. Always explicitly state this assumption. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing $h_{gs}$ and $h_{gw}$:** Strong solution has MORE ammonia → higher enthalpy. Sign convention errors are heavily penalized. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 3. **Omitting the rectifier in the schematic:** Examiners frequently award 1–2 marks purely for labeling the rectifier correctly. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Drawing the inert-gas loop incorrectly:** The H₂ loop is between evaporator and absorber, NOT through the condenser. Drawing arrows through the condenser loses marks. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Skipping the assumption of uniform total pressure:** Always mention Dalton's law and the partial pressure mechanism — it is the heart of the system. **(−1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-week KTU revision notes — print this section and pin it above your desk!**

- **Three fluids → Three loops:** NH₃ (refrigerant loop) | H₂O (absorbent loop) | H₂ (inert gas loop).
- **No compressor, no pump** → only **heat input at generator** drives the entire cycle.
- **Rectifier is mandatory** → prevents water from freezing in the condenser.
- **Partial pressure of NH₃ in evaporator < Total pressure** → this is what allows evaporation at low temperature.
- **COP = RE / Q_gen** (purely thermal, no work term).
- **Hydrogen is lightest** → naturally rises back to the evaporator after ammonia is absorbed.
- **Solution Heat Exchanger (SHX)** → improves efficiency by preheating the strong solution.
- **Typical domestic COP** ≈ 0.20 – 0.30 (much lower than vapor-compression).
- **Maximum theoretical COP** $= (T_e / T_g) \cdot [(T_g - T_a) / (T_a - T_e)]$ — uses three temperatures.
- **Common applications:** Domestic LPG refrigerators, RV/marine units, solar refrigeration.
- **Comparison shortcut:** Three-fluid system = **silent, no moving parts, heat-driven** | Vapor-compression = **high COP, electrically driven, has compressor**.
- **Examination Mantra:** Always draw the schematic with **three distinct flow arrows** for the three fluids and label the rectifier explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Refrigerant

A **refrigerant** is a working fluid that undergoes a phase-change cycle (typically liquid → vapor → liquid) within a refrigeration system to absorb heat from a low-temperature region (evaporator / cold space) at low pressure and reject it to a high-temperature region (condenser / atmosphere) at high pressure. It is the "blood" of any vapor-compression refrigeration cycle (VCRS) — without it, heat pumping is impossible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** A refrigerant is the substance (usually a pure compound or azeotrope) which, while undergoing cyclic phase changes, absorbs heat at a low temperature and rejects it at a higher temperature, completing a thermodynamic cycle. The choice of refrigerant fundamentally governs the system's COP, capacity, safety, and environmental footprint.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Taxi Cab Analogy"

Imagine a taxi running between two cities:
- **Cold City** = Evaporator (inside your fridge / AC evaporator coil).
- **Hot City** = Condenser (outdoor atmosphere).
- **Taxi Driver (Refrigerant)** = Carries passengers (heat) from Cold City to Hot City.

**Without the taxi (refrigerant), heat cannot be transferred from the cold region to the hot region (which is the natural direction the Second Law wants).** A good refrigerant (taxi) is one that:
- Holds a lot of passengers per trip → **High latent heat of vaporization**.
- Refuels cheaply → **Low boiling pressure conditions**.
- Drives safely → **Non-toxic, non-flammable**.
- Doesn't pollute the cities → **Low ODP & GWP**.

## 1.3 Desirable (Ideal) Properties of a Refrigerant

Refrigerants are evaluated on **four engineering pillars**:

### A. Thermodynamic Properties
- **Low boiling point** at evaporator pressure (preferably slightly below desired cold temperature).
- **High latent heat of vaporization** → more heat absorbed per kg.
- **Low specific heat of liquid** → less work wasted in cooling liquid during throttling.
- **Low specific volume of vapor** → smaller compressor displacement.
- **High critical temperature & pressure** → easier condensation.

### B. Physical Properties
- **Low viscosity** → better flow, lower pumping losses.
- **High thermal conductivity** → better heat transfer in evaporator/condenser.
- **Non-corrosive** to metals (copper, steel, aluminum).
- **Miscible with lubricating oil** (mineral/synthetic).

### C. Chemical Properties
- **Chemically stable** — does not decompose inside the system.
- **Non-toxic & Non-irritating**.
- **Non-flammable & Non-explosive**.

### D. Environmental & Safety Properties
- **Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP)** = **0** ideally (R-11 = 1.0 reference).
- **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** = **0** ideally (CO₂ = 1.0 reference).
- **Low TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact)**.
- **ASHRAE Safety Group A1** (low toxicity, no flame propagation).

> [!NOTE]
> **No single refrigerant is perfect.** This is why modern engineers use *application-specific* refrigerants — domestic fridges use one, chillers use another, ultra-low temp freezers use yet another.

---

## 1.4 ASHRAE Nomenclature — The Refrigerant "Language"

ASHRAE Standard 34 provides a standardized numbering system:

> **Format:** R - (Letter) (d − 1) (a − 1) (b)
> where d = number of fluorine atoms added to the basic molecule.

- **First Digit (d−1):** Total F + 1 atoms in the molecule.
- **Second Digit (a−1):** Number of Hydrogen atoms + 1.
- **Third Digit (b):** Number of Carbon atoms − 1.

### Common Examples:
| Refrigerant | Chemical Formula | ASHRAE Reading Logic |
|-------------|------------------|----------------------|
| **R-12** | CCl₂F₂ | d=2, a=1, b=0 → C=1, H=0, F=2, Cl=2 |
| **R-22** | CHClF₂ | d=2, a=2, b=0 → C=1, H=1, F=2, Cl=1 |
| **R-134a** | C₂H₂F₄ | d=3, a=2, b=1 → C=2, H=2, F=4 |
| **R-32** | CH₂F₂ | d=2, a=3, b=0 → C=1, H=2, F=2 |
| **R-410A** | Blend R-32/R-125 | "A" suffix indicates Zeotropic/Azeotropic blend |
| **R-744** | CO₂ | "7XX" series = inorganic compounds |
| **R-717** | NH₃ | "7XX" series = inorganic compounds |
| **R-718** | H₂O | "7XX" series = inorganic compounds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suffix Decoding:**
> - No suffix → Single compound (e.g., R-134a)
> - "A" → Zeotropic blend (e.g., R-407A)
> - "B" → Azeotropic blend (e.g., R-502B)
> - "C" → Azeotropic blend
> - Lowercase letter (a, b, c...) → Different isomers of the same molecule (R-134a vs R-134).

---

## 1.5 Broad Classification of Refrigerants

```
REFRIGERANTS
├── 1. Halocarbon (Synthetic Organic)
│   ├── CFCs  (Chlorofluorocarbons) — e.g., R-11, R-12, R-115
│   ├── HCFCs (Hydrochlorofluorocarbons) — e.g., R-22, R-123
│   ├── HFCs  (Hydrofluorocarbons) — e.g., R-134a, R-32, R-125
│   ├── HFOs  (Hydrofluoroolefins) — e.g., R-1234yf, R-1234ze
│   └── HC    (Hydrocarbons) — e.g., R-290 (propane), R-600a (isobutane)
├── 2. Inorganic Refrigerants
│   ├── R-717 (Ammonia, NH₃)
│   ├── R-718 (Water, H₂O)
│   ├── R-729 (Air)
│   └── R-744 (Carbon Dioxide, CO₂)
└── 3. Azeotropic / Zeotropic Blends
    ├── Azeotropes — R-502, R-507
    └── Zeotropes — R-407C, R-410A
```

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification — Detailed Engineering Analysis

### 2.1.1 CFCs — Chlorofluorocarbons (The "Banned Generation")
**Examples:** R-11 (CCl₃F), R-12 (CCl₂F₂), R-115 (C₂ClF₅)
- Contain **only C, Cl, F** → zero hydrogen.
- **Excellent thermodynamic & chemical stability** → inert, non-flammable, non-corrosive.
- **Disastrous environmental impact:** ODP = **0.6 – 1.0**.
- **Montreal Protocol (1987):** Phase-out completed by **1996** in developed nations.
- **KTU Insight:** Purely a **historical/legacy** topic now — exam questions typically ask *why* they were banned, not how to use them.

### 2.1.2 HCFCs — Hydrochlorofluorocarbons (The "Transition Generation")
**Examples:** R-22 (CHClF₂), R-123 (CHCl₂CF₃)
- Contain **H + Cl + F** → hydrogen atom *partially* breaks down in lower atmosphere.
- **ODP = 0.02 – 0.11** (much lower than CFCs but still non-zero).
- **Montreal Protocol target:** Complete phase-out by **2030** for developing countries, **2020** for developed.
- **R-22** was the dominant refrigerant in residential ACs and chillers from 1970–2010.

### 2.1.3 HFCs — Hydrofluorocarbons (The "Current Mainstream")
**Examples:** R-134a (C₂H₂F₄), R-32 (CH₂F₂), R-125 (C₂HF₅)
- Contain **H + F only** → **zero Cl** → **ODP = 0**.
- **GWP is the concern:** R-134a GWP = **1430**, R-32 = **675** (CO₂ = 1).
- **Kigali Amendment (2016):** HFC phase-down underway globally — R-134a being replaced by HFO R-1234yf in automotive AC.
- Currently dominant in: **domestic refrigerators, chillers, automotive AC**.

### 2.1.4 HFOs — Hydrofluoroolefins (The "Future Generation")
**Examples:** R-1234yf (CF₃CF=CH₂), R-1234ze
- Contain a **double bond (olefin)** → shorter atmospheric lifetime.
- **ODP = 0**, **GWP < 1** (often called "ultra-low GWP").
- Mildly flammable (ASHRAE A2L classification).
- Used in next-gen automotive AC (since 2012+) and commercial chillers.

### 2.1.5 Natural Refrigerants (The "Eco-Retro Comeback")
| Refrigerant | Formula | ASHRAE No. | Boiling Pt (°C) | ODP | GWP | Key Use |
|-------------|---------|------------|-----------------|-----|-----|---------|
| Ammonia | NH₃ | R-717 | -33.3 | 0 | 0 | Industrial cold storage, food processing |
| Water | H₂O | R-718 | 100 | 0 | 0 | Absorption systems (LiBr-H₂O) |
| CO₂ | CO₂ | R-744 | -78.4 | 0 | 1 | Transcritical CO₂ systems, heat pumps |
| Propane | C₃H₈ | R-290 | -42.1 | 0 | ~3 | Domestic fridges (replaced R-134a) |
| Isobutane | C₄H₁₀ | R-600a | -11.7 | 0 | ~3 | Domestic fridges, freezers |
| Air | Air | R-729 | -194 | 0 | 0 | Air-cycle refrigeration (aircraft) |

### 2.1.6 Azeotropic vs. Zeotropic Blends
- **Azeotropic blend:** Behaves as a **single substance** with single boiling/condensing temperature at a given pressure. No temperature glide. Example: **R-502**, **R-507**.
- **Zeotropic blend:** Shows **temperature glide** (ΔT = 5–10 °C) during phase change. Example: **R-407C** (glide ~6°C), **R-410A** (glide ~0.2°C — near-azeotropic).
- **KTU Exam Trick:** Azeotropes can be topped up like pure refrigerants; zeotropes must be charged in **liquid phase** to avoid composition shift.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbol / Equation | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Refrigerating Effect (RE)** | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed in evaporator per kg of refrigerant |
| **Compressor Work** | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Specific work input |
| **COP of VCRS** | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Efficiency measure |
| **Mass flow rate** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_e}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | Required refrigerant circulation |
| **Compressor displacement** | $V_d = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{\eta_{\text{vol}}}$ | m³/s | Physical size of compressor |
| **ODP** (relative to R-11) | $\text{ODP}_x = \dfrac{\text{O}_3 \text{ destroyed by 1 kg of } x}{\text{O}_3 \text{ destroyed by 1 kg of R-11}}$ | dimensionless | Ozone damage index |
| **GWP** (relative to CO₂) | $\text{GWP}_x = \dfrac{\text{Heat trapped by 1 kg of } x}{\text{Heat trapped by 1 kg CO}_2}$ | dimensionless | Climate impact index |
| **TEWI** | $\text{TEWI} = \text{Direct Emissions} + \text{Indirect Emissions}$ | kg CO₂ eq. | Total climate impact of system |

$$\boxed{\text{TEWI} = \underbrace{\dfrac{L \cdot n \cdot \text{GWP}}_{\text{Direct (leakage)}} + \underbrace{n \cdot E_{\text{annual}} \cdot \beta}_{\text{Indirect (energy CO}_2)}}$$

where:
- $L$ = leakage rate (kg/year)
- $n$ = operating life (years)
- $E_{\text{annual}}$ = annual energy consumption (kWh)
- $\beta$ = CO₂ emission factor of electricity (kg CO₂/kWh)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TEWI Matters:** A refrigerant with GWP=2000 might *appear* worse than one with GWP=200, but if the latter requires 3× the electricity, its **TEWI is HIGHER**. KTU 2024 specifically tests this trade-off.

---

## 2.3 ASHRAE Safety Classification Matrix

ASHRAE Standard 34 assigns **two letters and a number** to every refrigerant:

- **Letter A** = Lower toxicity (≤ 400 ppm OEL)
- **Letter B** = Higher toxicity (> 400 ppm OEL)
- **Number 1** = No flame propagation at 60°C, 101.3 kPa
- **Number 2** = Lower flammability (LFL > 0.10 kg/m³)
- **Number 3** = Higher flammability (LFL ≤ 0.10 kg/m³)
- **Suffix L** = Lower flammability, maximum burning velocity ≤ 10 cm/s (e.g., A2L)

| Refrigerant | ASHRAE Class | Toxicity | Flammability |
|-------------|--------------|----------|--------------|
| R-12 | A1 | Low | None |
| R-22 | A1 | Low | None |
| R-134a | A1 | Low | None |
| R-32 | A2L | Low | Mild |
| R-1234yf | A2L | Low | Mild |
| R-717 (NH₃) | B2L | High | Mild |
| R-290 (Propane) | A3 | Low | High |
| R-744 (CO₂) | A1 | Low | None |

---

## 2.4 Refrigerant Selection Strategy — Engineering Utility

In real-world practice (Chiller plants, AC manufacturing), engineers follow a decision flowchart:

1. **Required Evaporating Temperature** → Select refrigerant whose T_sat at desired P_evap is appropriate.
2. **Application Scale** → Industrial (NH₃, CO₂), Commercial (HFC blends), Domestic (R-600a, R-290).
3. **Environmental Compliance** → Country-specific regulations (India: Ozone Depleting Substances (Regulation) Rules, 2000).
4. **Safety Code** → Public buildings require A1 class.
5. **Compressor Compatibility** → Screw, centrifugal, reciprocating — each has refrigerant limits.
6. **Oil Return & Lubrication** → Critical for POE oils with HFCs.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Application Question Hook:** *"Why is R-134a being replaced by R-1234yf in automobile AC?"*
> **Answer Hook:** R-134a has GWP = **1430**. Under EU MAC Directive 2006/40/EC, GWP must be < **150**. R-1234yf has GWP < 1 — meets regulation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effect of Refrigerant Selection on COP

The Coefficient of Performance of an **ideal VCRS** (Carnot-equivalent refrigeration cycle) is:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

where $T_L$ = evaporator temperature (K), $T_H$ = condenser temperature (K).

For a **practical vapor compression cycle**, COP becomes:

$$\text{COP}_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

The selection of refrigerant **drastically alters** the enthalpy values $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$ even at identical $T_L$ and $T_H$, because each refrigerant has its **own saturation curve**.

### Step-by-Step Reasoning:

**Step 1:** Fix $T_{\text{evap}} = -10°C = 263\text{ K}$ and $T_{\text{cond}} = 40°C = 313\text{ K}$ (typical AC conditions).

**Step 2:** For **R-134a** at $T_{\text{evap}} = -10°C$:
- $P_{\text{evap}} \approx 2.006 \text{ bar}$
- $h_1 \approx 392.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated vapor)
- $h_4 = h_3 \approx 241.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid at 40°C)
- $RE = 392.7 - 241.3 = 151.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 3:** For **R-22** at the same conditions:
- $P_{\text{evap}} \approx 3.55 \text{ bar}$
- $h_1 \approx 402.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_4 = h_3 \approx 249.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $RE = 402.3 - 249.7 = 152.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 4:** Compressor work for R-134a (with $\eta_c = 100\%$):
- $h_{2s} \approx 425.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$ → $W_c = 425.4 - 392.7 = 32.7 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 5:** Compressor work for R-22:
- $h_{2s} \approx 437.6 \text{ kJ/kg}$ → $W_c = 437.6 - 402.3 = 35.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**Step 6:** Calculate COP:
$$\text{COP}_{\text{R134a}} = \frac{151.4}{32.7} = 4.63$$
$$\text{COP}_{\text{R22}} = \frac{152.6}{35.3} = 4.32$$

**Conclusion:** **R-134a gives ~7% higher COP** than R-22 at identical evaporator/condenser conditions. This is *one* reason (alongside ODP = 0) why R-134a replaced R-22 in domestic chillers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: TEWI Calculation for Refrigerant Selection

Let us compute TEWI for an HVAC chiller using two candidate refrigerants and compare.

### Given Data:
- Chiller capacity: $\dot{Q}_e = 500 \text{ kW}$
- COP: 3.5 (assumed same for both for fair energy comparison; in reality they differ)
- Operating life: $n = 15$ years
- Annual leakage: $L = 5\%$ of charge per year
- Charge: $C = 200 \text{ kg}$
- Plant running hours: $H = 2500 \text{ h/year}$
- Electricity CO₂ emission factor: $\beta = 0.7 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kWh}$

### Step 1: Annual Electricity Consumption

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{annual}} &= \dfrac{\dot{Q}_e \cdot H}{\text{COP}} \\
&= \dfrac{500 \text{ kW} \cdot 2500 \text{ h}}{3.5} \\
&= 357{,}143 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Indirect (Energy-Related) CO₂ Emissions

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Indirect}_{\text{CO}_2} &= n \cdot E_{\text{annual}} \cdot \beta \\
&= 15 \cdot 357{,}143 \cdot 0.7 \\
&= 3{,}750{,}000 \text{ kg CO}_2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Direct (Leakage) CO₂ Equivalent for R-134a (GWP = 1430)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct}_{\text{R134a}} &= C \cdot n \cdot L \cdot \text{GWP} \\
&= 200 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 1430 \\
&= 214{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ equivalent}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Direct (Leakage) CO₂ Equivalent for R-1234yf (GWP = 4)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Direct}_{\text{R1234yf}} &= 200 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 4 \\
&= 600 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ equivalent}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: TEWI Comparison

$$\text{TEWI}_{\text{R134a}} = 3{,}750{,}000 + 214{,}500 = 3{,}964{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

$$\text{TEWI}_{\text{R1234yf}} = 3{,}750{,}000 + 600 = 3{,}750{,}600 \text{ kg CO}_2$$

### Step 6: Reduction Percentage

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} &= \dfrac{3{,}964{,}500 - 3{,}750{,}600}{3{,}964{,}500} \times 100 \\
&= 5.41\% \text{ reduction}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Notice that switching from R-134a to R-1234yf reduces TEWI by only **~5.4%** — because *most* of TEWI comes from **indirect (energy) emissions**, not leakage. This is why improving system **energy efficiency** is even more impactful than refrigerant choice alone. **KTU 2024 frequently tests this insight.**

---
