### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the mechanism of **stratospheric ozone depletion** by CFCs. Include the catalytic cycle and explain why even a small mass of CFCs causes significant damage. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Stability of CFCs in Troposphere:**
CFCs such as R-11 (CCl₃F) and R-12 (CCl₂F₂) have **very high chemical stability** in the lower atmosphere due to the strength of the **C–F bond (~485 kJ/mol)** and the inertness of the molecules. Their atmospheric lifetimes are 52 years (R-11) and 102 years (R-12), giving them ample time to diffuse upward into the stratosphere.

**Step 2 — Photolysis in Stratosphere:**
At altitudes above ~25 km, the molecules encounter **short-wavelength UV-C radiation (λ < 220 nm)**, which is energetic enough to break the **C–Cl bond (~330 kJ/mol)**:

$$CCl_2F_2 + h\nu \ (\lambda < 220 \ nm) \longrightarrow CClF_2^{\bullet} + Cl^{\bullet}$$

This releases **free chlorine radicals (Cl•)** into the stratosphere.

**Step 3 — Catalytic Destruction of Ozone — Propagation Steps:**

$$\text{Initiation: } Cl^{\bullet} + O_3 \longrightarrow ClO^{\bullet} + O_2$$

$$\text{Propagation: } ClO^{\bullet} + O \longrightarrow Cl^{\bullet} + O_2$$

**Step 4 — Net Reaction (Chain Cycles):**
Adding the two propagation steps yields:

$$\text{Net: } O_3 + O \longrightarrow 2O_2$$

**Step 5 — Catalytic Chain Length:**
The crucial feature is that the chlorine radical is **regenerated** in the second step, making it a **true catalyst**. A single Cl• atom can participate in the cycle repeatedly until it is removed by "reservoir species" such as HCl and ClONO₂:

$$Cl^{\bullet} + CH_4 \longrightarrow HCl + CH_3^{\bullet} \quad (\text{terminating})$$

Because the stratospheric mixing times are long and reservoir-formation kinetics are slow, a single chlorine atom has a **catalytic chain length of approximately 10⁵**, meaning one Cl• destroys roughly **100,000 ozone molecules**.

**Step 6 — Why Small Mass Causes Large Damage:**
Thus, even a **kilogram** of leaked CFC-12 can, in principle, destroy on the order of **10⁸ moles of ozone** (~10¹⁰ molecules) over several decades. This explains the historical observation of the **Antarctic Ozone Hole** (discovered 1985 by Joe Farman) and motivates the regulatory action under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Always draw the **two-step catalytic cycle** as a flowchart in your answer — Examiners specifically award credit for clearly identifying the catalytic regeneration of Cl•.

---

**(b)** A commercial cold storage uses **R-404A** with the following data: Charge = **200 kg**, annual leakage = **6%**, annual energy = **120,000 kWh**, grid emission factor = **0.85 kg CO₂/kWh**, equipment life = **20 years**, GWP of R-404A = **3,922**. Calculate the **TEWI** and comment on the result. **[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Charge, $M$ = 200 kg
- Annual leakage, $L$ = 6% × 200 = 12 kg/yr
- Annual energy, $E_{annual}$ = 1,20,000 kWh
- Grid emission factor, $\beta$ = 0.85 kg CO₂/kWh
- Equipment life, $TH$ = 20 years
- $GWP_{R-404A}$ = 3,922

**Step 1 — Direct Emissions (refrigerant leak over lifetime):**

$$m_{\text{leak,total}} = L \times TH = 12 \times 20 = 240 \ \text{kg of R-404A}$$

$$E_{direct} = m_{\text{leak,total}} \times GWP = 240 \times 3{,}922$$

$$E_{direct} = 9{,}41{,}280 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{-eq}$$

**Step 2 — Indirect Emissions (energy-related over lifetime):**

$$E_{indirect} = E_{annual} \times \beta \times TH$$

$$E_{indirect} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.85 \times 20$$

$$E_{indirect} = 20{,}40{,}000 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{-eq}$$

**Step 3 — Total TEWI:**

$$TEWI = E_{direct} + E_{indirect}$$

$$TEWI = 9{,}41{,}280 + 20{,}40{,}000$$

$$\boxed{TEWI = 29{,}81{,}280 \ \text{kg CO}_2\text{-eq} \ \approx \ 2{,}981.28 \ \text{tonnes CO}_2\text{-eq}}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Analysis:**

$$\%_{\text{direct}} = \dfrac{9{,}41{,}280}{29{,}81{,}280} \times 100 \approx 31.57\%$$

$$\%_{\text{indirect}} = \dfrac{20{,}40{,}000}{29{,}81{,}280} \times 100 \approx 68.43\%$$

**Comment:**
The **indirect (energy-related) emissions dominate (≈68%)** for this cold storage. The most effective mitigation strategy would therefore be to **improve the COP** of the refrigeration system through measures such as variable-speed compressors, EC condenser fans, heat recovery, floating-head pressure control, and proper insulation — even before switching the refrigerant. Reducing leak rate from 6% to 2% would lower direct emissions to ~5,27,000 kg CO₂-eq, a ~44% reduction in the direct component.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **greenhouse effect** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of **HFCs** as greenhouse gases. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition:**
The **greenhouse effect** is the natural process by which greenhouse gases (GHGs) in the Earth's troposphere absorb and re-emit the infrared (long-wave) radiation emitted by the Earth's surface, thereby maintaining a habitable average surface temperature of approximately **15°C** (288 K). Without GHGs, Earth's average temperature would be approximately **−18°C** (255 K).

**Step 2 — Mechanism:**

1. **Solar short-wave radiation** (~0.3 µm to 3 µm) from the sun penetrates the atmosphere largely unabsorbed (because N₂, O₂, Ar are transparent to it).
2. The Earth's surface absorbs this energy and warms.
3. The warm Earth emits **infrared radiation** (3 µm to 100 µm, peak around 10 µm).
4. **Polyatomic GHG molecules** (CO₂, H₂O, CH₄, N₂O, O₃, HFCs, PFCs) have vibrational modes that absorb strongly in the IR region.
5. The absorbed IR is **re-emitted isotropically** — roughly half returns to the Earth's surface (downward IR flux), causing additional warming.

**Step 3 — Major Greenhouse Gases and Their Contributions:**

| Gas | Pre-industrial Concentration | Current (2024) | GWP₁₀₀ | Contribution to Global Warming |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂** | 280 ppm | 422 ppm | 1 | ~64% |
| **CH₄** | 700 ppb | 1,895 ppb | 28–34 | ~17% |
| **N₂O** | 270 ppb | 336 ppb | 265–298 | ~6% |
| **HFCs** | 0 | rising | varies | ~2% (but rising rapidly) |

**Step 4 — Role of HFCs as Greenhouse Gases:**
Although HFCs do not deplete stratospheric ozone (ODP = 0), many possess **high Global Warming Potential** values. Examples:
- **R-134a** — used in domestic refrigerators, car AC → GWP = **1,430**
- **R-410A** — used in split AC, VRF → GWP = **2,088**
- **R-404A** — used in commercial refrigeration → GWP = **3,922**

Because refrigerant leaks and end-of-life disposal releases HFCs directly into the atmosphere, even a **1 kg leak of R-404A** is equivalent to releasing **3,922 kg of CO₂**. The **Kigali Amendment (2016)** to the Montreal Protocol now mandates a phase-down of high-GWP HFCs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often incorrectly state that **"ozone depletion causes global warming."** This is **wrong**. Ozone depletion and global warming are **two separate environmental issues** with different chemical mechanisms, different gases, and different regulations (Montreal Protocol vs UNFCCC/Kyoto/Paris). However, some gases (e.g., HCFCs) contribute to **both** problems simultaneously.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **Montreal Protocol** and **Kigali Amendment**. What are the implications for the HVAC&R industry? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Montreal Protocol (1987):**
The **Montrol Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer** was adopted on **16 September 1987** in Montreal, Canada, and entered into force on **1 January 1989**. It is widely regarded as the most successful international environmental treaty in history.

**Key Provisions:**
- Phase-out of **CFCs** (R-11, R-12, R-13, R-113, R-114, R-115)
- Phase-out of **Halons** (fire-fighting agents)
- Phase-out of **Carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄)** and **Methyl Bromide (CH₃Br)**
- Differentiated phase-out schedules for **Developed (Non-Article 5)** and **Developing (Article 5)** countries.

**Step 2 — Kigali Amendment (15 October 2016):**
Adopted at the 28th Meeting of Parties (MOP-28) to the Montreal Protocol in Kigali, Rwanda, this amendment specifically targets the **phase-down of HFCs** — not because of ozone depletion, but because of their **high GWP**.

**Phase-Down Schedule (Freeze & Reduction):**
- **2019** — HFC consumption/production baseline freeze
- **2024** — 10% reduction (developed) / 10% (developing group 1, 2029)
- **2029** — 40–55% reduction
- **2034–2036** — 70–80% reduction
- **2045–2050** — 80–85% reduction

India, as a developing country (Article 5 group), has a baseline of 2030–2032 and a freeze year of 2028, with complete phase-down by **2047**.

**Step 3 — Implications for HVAC&R Industry:**

1. **Refrigerant Transition:** Forced migration from high-GWP HFCs (R-134a, R-410A, R-404A) to **low-GWP alternatives** — R-32 (GWP = 675), R-1234yf (GWP < 1), R-290 (GWP = 3), CO₂ (R-744, GWP = 1), and ammonia (R-717, GWP = 0).

2. **Equipment Redesign:** New refrigerants often have different thermodynamic and safety properties (e.g., R-290 is mildly flammable → requires redesigned heat exchangers and charge limits).

3. **Service & Training:** Existing technicians must be retrained to handle mildly flammable (A2L) and high-pressure (CO₂ transcritical) refrigerants safely.

4. **Recovery, Reclamation & Disposal:** Mandatory recovery of refrigerants before disposal of equipment to prevent atmospheric release.

5. **Energy Efficiency Co-Benefit:** The transition to low-GWP refrigerants is being coupled with **Minimum Energy Performance Standards (MEPS)** and **Star Labelling** programmes to simultaneously reduce indirect CO₂ emissions.

6. **Trade & Technology Transfer:** Developing countries receive financial and technological assistance from the **Multilateral Fund** for compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students incorrectly assume that the **Kigali Amendment is part of the Kyoto Protocol**. It is **not** — it is an amendment to the **Montreal Protocol**. Also, the Kigali Amendment does **not** address ozone depletion (HFCs are ozone-safe); it exclusively addresses **climate change**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam (Module 3 — Ozone Depletion & Global Warming):**
>
> - **ODP** is relative to **R-11 = 1.0**; CFCs → high ODP, HFCs/HCs/Natural → ODP = 0.
> - **GWP** is relative to **CO₂ = 1.0**; over **100-year horizon (GWP₁₀₀)**.
> - One **Cl radical** destroys up to **~10⁵ ozone molecules** before neutralisation.
> - CFCs are stable in troposphere due to **strong C–F bonds**; decomposed by **UV-C (λ < 220 nm)** in stratosphere.
> - **Catalytic cycle:** Cl• + O₃ → ClO• + O₂, followed by ClO• + O → Cl• + O₂.
> - **TEWI** = Direct (refrigerant leaks × GWP) + Indirect (energy × grid factor × lifetime).
> - For most real systems, **indirect (energy-related) emissions dominate (60–80%)**.
> - **Montreal Protocol (1987)** → phase-out of CFCs and HCFCs.
> - **Kigali Amendment (2016)** → phase-down of high-GWP HFCs.
> - **Greenhouse gases**: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O, O₃, HFCs, PFCs, SF₆.
> - **Low-GWP alternatives:** R-32 (675), R-1234yf (<1), R-290 (3), CO₂ (1), NH₃ (0).
> - **Atmospheric lifetime** matters — short-lived gases have lower integrated GWP.
> - Energy efficiency is the **single biggest lever** to reduce total climate impact of refrigeration systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commonly used refrigerants - HFC, HFO and mixed refrigerants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Commonly used refrigerants - HFC, HFO and mixed refrigerants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Refrigerant?

A **refrigerant** is a working fluid used in a refrigeration cycle that absorbs heat from the space to be cooled at low temperature and low pressure, and rejects that heat to the surroundings at high temperature and high pressure by undergoing a phase change (typically vaporization and condensation). In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3), refrigerants are broadly classified based on their chemical composition into **CFC (Chlorofluorocarbon)**, **HCFC (Hydrochlorofluorocarbon)**, **HFC (Hydrofluorocarbon)**, **HFO (Hydrofluoroolefin)**, and **Natural Refrigerants**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET751 Module 3):** *Commonly used refrigerants* covers the modern, post-Montreal-Protocol generation of working fluids — namely **HFCs, HFOs, and mixed (blended) refrigerants** — and their thermodynamic, environmental, safety, and application-based classification.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Refrigerant as the "Blood" of the System

Think of a refrigeration system as the **human circulatory system**:
- The **compressor** is the *heart* — it pumps the fluid.
- The **refrigerant** is the *blood* — it carries heat (the "waste") away from the cold region (the "body cell") and dumps it outside (the "lungs/skin").
- The **expansion valve** is the *vein constriction* — it drops the pressure so the fluid can absorb heat again.

Just as a doctor must choose the right blood type for a patient, an engineer must choose the right refrigerant for an application — one that is **non-toxic, non-flammable, energy-efficient, environmentally benign, and compatible with lubricants and seals**. HFCs, HFOs, and blends are the modern "safe and eco-friendly" choices.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Refrigerant Selection

Every modern refrigerant must satisfy three performance criteria (which KTU examiners love to test):

1. **Environmental Safety** — measured by **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** and **GWP (Global Warming Potential)**.
2. **Operational Safety** — measured by **Toxicity class (A or B)** and **Flammability class (1, 2L, 2, or 3)** as per **ASHRAE Standard 34**.
3. **Thermodynamic Performance** — measured by boiling point, latent heat of vaporization, COP, volumetric refrigerating effect, and discharge temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ASHRAE 34 Safety Classification Matrix** is denoted as **"Letter + Number"**:
> - **Toxicity:** A (lower toxicity) / B (higher toxicity)
> - **Flammability:** 1 (no flame propagation) / 2L (mild flammability, ≤10 cm·s⁻¹) / 2 (flammable) / 3 (highly flammable / explosive)
> 
> **Examples:** R-134a = **A1** (safe + non-flammable), R-1234yf = **A2L** (safe + mildly flammable), R-32 = **A2L**, R-1234ze(E) = **A2L**.

## 1.4 Why HFCs, HFOs, and Blends? The Regulatory Backdrop

The **Montreal Protocol (1987)** phased out CFCs and HCFCs because of their chlorine content (which destroys the ozone layer). The **Kigali Amendment (2016)** further mandates the phase-down of high-GWP HFCs. This regulatory pressure is the *direct reason* why the industry has pivoted to:
- **Low-GWP HFCs** (e.g., R-32, GWP = 675)
- **HFO refrigerants** (e.g., R-1234yf, GWP = 4)
- **HFC/HFO blends** (e.g., R-454B, R-452B)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus explicitly uses the term *"commonly used refrigerants"* — meaning you are expected to know the **trade names, chemical formulae, ODP, GWP, applications, and limitations** of each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Tree of Modern Refrigerants

```
Synthetic Refrigerants
├── HFC (Hydrofluorocarbons)        →  Contain H, F, C only
│   ├── Pure HFCs
│   │   ├── R-134a  (CH₂FCF₃)
│   │   ├── R-32    (CH₂F₂)
│   │   ├── R-125   (CHF₂CF₃)
│   │   ├── R-143a  (CH₃CF₃)
│   │   └── R-152a  (CH₃CHF₂)
│   └── HFC Blends (Zeotropic / Azeotropic)
│       ├── R-410A  (R-32/R-125 — 50/50)
│       ├── R-407C  (R-32/R-125/R-134a — 23/25/52)
│       ├── R-404A  (R-125/R-143a/R-134a — 44/52/4)
│       └── R-507A  (R-125/R-143a — 50/50) — Azeotropic
└── HFO (Hydrofluoroolefins)       →  Contain C=C double bond
    ├── Pure HFOs
    │   ├── R-1234yf  (CF₃CF=CH₂)
    │   ├── R-1234ze(E) (trans-CF₃CH=CHF)
    │   └── R-1234zd(Z) (cis-isomer)
    └── HFO/HFC Blends (Low-GWP 4th Generation)
        ├── R-454B  (R-32/R-1234yf — 68.9/31.1) — A2L
        ├── R-452B  (R-32/R-125/R-1234yf — 67/7/26)
        ├── R-513A  (R-1234yf/R-134a — 56/44)
        └── R-450A  (R-1234ze/R-134a — 58/42)
```

## 2.2 HFC — Hydrofluorocarbons (The 3rd-Generation Workhorse)

HFCs contain **only Hydrogen, Fluorine, and Carbon** — *no chlorine*. Hence ODP = **0** for all HFCs.

### Key Properties of Common HFCs

- **R-134a (1,1,1,2-Tetrafluoroethane, CH₂FCF₃)**
  - ODP = 0, GWP = **1430** (high — being phased down)
  - Boiling point (1 atm) = **−26.07 °C**
  - Application: Domestic refrigerators, automobile AC, chillers
  - Disadvantage: High GWP drives replacement by R-1234yf

- **R-32 (Difluoromethane, CH₂F₂)**
  - ODP = 0, GWP = **675** (about half of R-410A)
  - Boiling point = **−51.7 °C**
  - Application: Mini-split ACs, heat pumps (replaces R-410A)
  - Class: A2L (mildly flammable)

- **R-125 (Pentafluoroethane, CHF₂CF₃)**
  - ODP = 0, GWP = **3500**
  - Boiling point = **−48.1 °C**
  - Used only as a blend component (e.g., R-410A, R-404A)

- **R-152a (1,1-Difluoroethane, CH₃CHF₂)**
  - ODP = 0, GWP = **124** (very low!)
  - Boiling point = **−24 °C**
  - Application: MAC, low-GWP chillers, automotive
  - Class: A2 (flammable — limits its use)

## 2.3 HFO — Hydrofluoroolefins (The 4th-Generation Eco-Refrigerant)

HFOs are **unsaturated** organic compounds containing at least one **carbon–carbon double bond (C=C)**. The double bond makes them **highly reactive in the troposphere**, so they break down within days (atmospheric lifetime ≈ **10.5 days** for R-1234yf) — versus **14 years** for R-134a. Hence, GWP is dramatically low.

### Key Properties of Common HFOs

- **R-1234yf (2,3,3,3-Tetrafluoropropene, CF₃CF=CH₂)**
  - ODP = 0, GWP = **4** (almost negligible!)
  - Boiling point = **−29.5 °C** (very close to R-134a → drop-in replacement)
  - Atmospheric lifetime = 10.5 days
  - Class: **A2L**
  - Application: New-generation automotive AC (replacing R-134a since 2012 EU MAC Directive)

- **R-1234ze(E) (trans-1,3,3,3-Tetrafluoropropene)**
  - ODP = 0, GWP = **7**
  - Boiling point = **−19 °C**
  - Class: **A2L**
  - Application: High-temperature heat pumps, commercial chillers, ORC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are HFOs so environmentally friendly?** The C=C double bond is rapidly attacked by **OH radicals** in the troposphere, producing **TFA (trifluoroacetic acid)** as a residue. This short atmospheric lifetime means minimal infrared trapping → very low GWP. This is a *favourite conceptual question* in KTU exams.

## 2.4 Mixed Refrigerants (Blends)

A blend is a mixture of two or more pure refrigerants to obtain a working fluid with **tuned properties** (e.g., lower GWP, better capacity, better glide). Blends are classified by ASHRAE 34 into:

### 2.4.1 Zeotropic Blends (Non-Azeotropic)

The components have **different boiling points**, so the liquid and vapor phases have **different compositions**, causing a **temperature glide** (ΔT) during phase change.

- **Examples:** R-407C (glide ≈ 7 °C), R-404A (glide ≈ 0.8 °C, *nearly* zeotropic)
- **Glide definition:** 
$$\Delta T_{glide} = T_{dew} - T_{bubble}$$
at a given pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Zeotropic blends require **counter-flow heat exchangers** in the condenser and evaporator to handle glide, otherwise performance degrades by 5–15%.

### 2.4.2 Azeotropic Blends

The mixture behaves like a **single pure substance** — it boils and condenses at **one constant temperature** at a specific composition (the *azeotropic point*). There is **no glide**.

- **Examples:** R-502 (legacy, now banned), R-507A (R-125/R-143a, 50/50)
- **Near-Azeotropic:** R-404A (small but non-zero glide)

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| S.No | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | ODP | $ODP = \frac{\text{Ozone destruction by refrigerant}}{\text{Ozone destruction by CFC-11}}$ | dimensionless | R-11 = 1.0 (reference) |
| 2 | GWP (100-yr) | $GWP = \frac{\int_0^{100} a_x \cdot [x](t)\, dt}{\int_0^{100} a_{CO_2} \cdot [CO_2](t)\, dt}$ | dimensionless | $a_x$ = radiative efficiency |
| 3 | Atmospheric Lifetime | $CF_3CF=CH_2 + OH \cdot \rightarrow \text{products}$ | years | R-1234yf ≈ 10.5 days |
| 4 | TEWI | $TEWI = (m \cdot GWP \cdot 100) + (n \cdot E_{annual} \cdot EF)$ | kg CO₂-eq | Direct + Indirect emissions |
| 5 | Volumetric Refrig. Effect | $VRE = \frac{h_{fg}}{v_{suction}}$ | kJ·m⁻³ | Determines compressor size |
| 6 | COP | $COP = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Carnot upper limit |
| 7 | Temperature Glide | $\Delta T_{glide} = T_{dew} - T_{bubble}$ | K or °C | Zeotropic blends only |
| 8 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \frac{P_{cond}}{P_{evap}}$ | dimensionless | Affects compressor work |
| 9 | Discharge Temp (approx) | $T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ | K | Ideal gas, isentropic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Formula — TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact):**
> The TEWI is the **single number that captures the global warming footprint of a refrigerant over the lifetime of a system**. It has two parts:
> - **Direct emissions** (refrigerant leakage to atmosphere, weighted by GWP).
> - **Indirect emissions** (CO₂ emitted at the power plant to generate the electricity the compressor consumes, integrated over the equipment's lifetime).
> 
> This appears in KTU problems where you compare two refrigerants (e.g., R-410A vs R-32) on environmental grounds.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Refrigerant Selection

- **Automotive AC:** R-134a → R-1234yf (driven by EU MAC Directive 2006/40/EC).
- **Domestic AC (split/window):** R-22 (banned) → R-410A → R-32 (currently in India and Japan).
- **Commercial refrigeration (supermarkets):** R-404A → R-449A, R-452A, or CO₂ (R-744) cascade.
- **High-temp heat pumps (water heating 80 °C):** R-1234ze(E) or R-152a.
- **Chillers:** R-134a → R-513A or R-1234ze(E).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TEWI (Total Equivalent Warming Impact)

The TEWI is the **gold-standard environmental metric** in refrigeration. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to **write the TEWI equation, identify each term, and apply it to a numerical problem**. Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step derivation** starting from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the Two Physical Sources of Warming

Over the operational lifetime of a refrigeration system (typically $n$ years), the system contributes to global warming through two physically distinct pathways:

1. **Direct Emissions (Direct GWP Effect):** The refrigerant mass that leaks into the atmosphere (during operation, servicing, and end-of-life disposal) traps infrared radiation. This warming is measured in **kg CO₂-equivalent** by multiplying leaked mass by GWP and a 100-year integration factor.

2. **Indirect Emissions (Indirect CO₂ Effect):** The compressor consumes electrical energy, and at the power-plant level, burning fossil fuels (coal/gas) generates CO₂. The CO₂ emitted at the power plant, weighted over the system's lifetime, is the indirect contribution.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Write the Direct Emission Term

Let:
- $m$ = total mass of refrigerant charged in the system (kg)
- $L$ = annual leakage rate (fraction, typically 0.06 for commercial, 0.04 for domestic)
- $GWP_x$ = 100-year Global Warming Potential of refrigerant $x$
- $R$ = recovery factor at end-of-life (typically 0.80 → 80% recovered)

The total leaked mass over the lifetime is:
$$
m_{leak} = m \cdot L \cdot n + m \cdot (1 - R)
$$
**Explanation:**
- The first term captures **gradual leakage** during each of the $n$ years of operation: $m \cdot L$ leaks every year → $m \cdot L \cdot n$ over $n$ years.
- The second term captures the **end-of-life loss**: the unrecovered fraction $(1 - R) \cdot m$ is released to the atmosphere when the system is scrapped.

The **direct warming contribution** in kg CO₂-equivalent is:
$$
\text{Direct} = m_{leak} \cdot GWP_x \cdot 100
$$
**Explanation:** Multiplying by 100 converts GWP from "kg CO₂-eq per kg refrigerant" to "kg CO₂-eq per 100 kg refrigerant" — this is the **standard IPCC AR4/AR5 convention** used in TEWI calculations to keep numbers tractable.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Write the Indirect Emission Term

Let:
- $E_{annual}$ = annual electricity consumption by the compressor (kWh/year)
- $EF$ = average CO₂ emission factor of the grid (kg CO₂ per kWh)
  - India grid average ≈ 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh (CEA 2023 data)
  - EU average ≈ 0.30 kg CO₂/kWh
- $n$ = operational lifetime (years)
- $\alpha$ = efficiency factor of power generation + transmission (typically 0.40 for thermal)

The annual CO₂ emitted at the power plant is:
$$
CO_{2,annual} = \frac{E_{annual} \cdot EF}{\alpha}
$$
**Explanation:** Since only $\alpha$ fraction of fuel energy becomes electricity at the consumer end, the fuel-side CO₂ is $E_{annual}/\alpha$ in primary energy, and the carbon emitted is proportional to it.

The **indirect warming contribution** over $n$ years is:
$$
\text{Indirect} = n \cdot E_{annual} \cdot EF
$$
**Explanation:** This is the total fuel-side CO₂ emitted over the system lifetime, in kg CO₂.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Combine Both Terms

$$
TEWI = \underbrace{m \cdot \left[ L \cdot n + (1 - R) \right] \cdot GWP_x \cdot 100}_{\text{Direct Contribution}} + \underbrace{n \cdot E_{annual} \cdot EF}_{\text{Indirect Contribution}}
$$

This is the **canonical TEWI equation** per **IPCC TEWI Manual (2006)** and **IIR (International Institute of Refrigeration) guidelines**.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

**Problem:** A supermarket refrigeration system uses 50 kg of R-404A. Annual leakage rate is 8%, lifetime is 15 years, recovery factor is 80%. The annual electricity use is 60,000 kWh. Grid emission factor is 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh. Calculate TEWI and compare with the same system using R-744 (CO₂, GWP = 1) where the leakage rate reduces to 3% (CO₂ systems are tighter).

**Given:**
- R-404A: $m = 50$ kg, $L = 0.08$, $n = 15$ yr, $R = 0.80$, $GWP = 3922$, $E_{annual} = 60{,}000$ kWh, $EF = 0.82$.
- R-744: same $m, n, R, E_{annual}, EF$ but $L = 0.03$, $GWP = 1$.

**Solution for R-404A:**

Direct term:
$$
m_{leak} = 50 \cdot [0.08 \cdot 15 + (1 - 0.80)] = 50 \cdot [1.20 + 0.20] = 50 \cdot 1.40 = 70 \text{ kg}
$$
$$
\text{Direct}_{R-404A} = 70 \cdot 3922 \cdot 100 = 2.7454 \times 10^7 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

Indirect term:
$$
\text{Indirect} = 15 \cdot 60{,}000 \cdot 0.82 = 7.38 \times 10^5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

Total:
$$
TEWI_{R-404A} = 2.7454 \times 10^7 + 0.0738 \times 10^7 = 2.8192 \times 10^7 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Solution for R-744:**

Direct term:
$$
m_{leak} = 50 \cdot [0.03 \cdot 15 + 0.20] = 50 \cdot [0.45 + 0.20] = 50 \cdot 0.65 = 32.5 \text{ kg}
$$
$$
\text{Direct}_{R-744} = 32.5 \cdot 1 \cdot 100 = 3250 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

Indirect term (assume same):
$$
\text{Indirect}_{R-744} = 7.38 \times 10^5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

Total:
$$
TEWI_{R-744} = 3250 + 7.38 \times 10^5 = 7.4133 \times 10^5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Conclusion:** Switching from R-404A to R-744 reduces TEWI by a factor of approximately **38×**, dominated by the direct emission term.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to "compare two refrigerants on environmental grounds", always show the TEWI calculation in *both* cases and identify which term dominates. Students who write only GWP comparison lose 2–3 marks because the *energy efficiency* (indirect term) is also part of environmental performance.

## 3.2 Derivation: Discharge Temperature (Isentropic Compression)

The discharge temperature determines whether a refrigerant is **suitable for a compressor** (too high → oil breakdown, valve damage).

Starting from the **isentropic relation for an ideal gas**:
$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}
$$
Multiplying both sides by $T_1$:
$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}
$$

For **R-134a** ($\gamma \approx 1.13$), evaporating at $T_1 = -10$ °C = 263 K, condensing at $P_2/P_1 = 4$ (typical):
$$
T_2 = 263 \cdot (4)^{\frac{0.13}{1.13}} = 263 \cdot (4)^{0.115} = 263 \cdot 1.144 = 300.9 \text{ K} \approx 27.7 \text{ °C}
$$

For **R-1234yf** ($\gamma \approx 1.10$):
$$
T_2 = 263 \cdot (4)^{\frac{0.10}{1.10}} = 263 \cdot (4)^{0.0909} = 263 \cdot 1.119 = 294.2 \text{ K} \approx 21.0 \text{ °C}
$$

**Result:** R-1234yf has a **lower discharge temperature** → cooler compressor head, longer compressor life.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

    B1 --> D[Direct Warming Term]
    B2 --> E[Indirect Warming Term]

    D --> F[TEWI = Direct + Indirect]
    E --> F

    F --> G((Final kg CO2 eq))
