# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any two HFC refrigerants with their chemical formulae and GWP values. Why are HFCs considered environmentally superior to CFCs?**

**Model Answer:**

| Refrigerant | Chemical Formula | ODP | GWP (100-yr) | ASHRAE Class |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| R-134a | CH₂FCF₃ | 0 | 1430 | A1 |
| R-32 | CH₂F₂ | 0 | 675 | A2L |
| R-152a | CH₃CHF₂ | 0 | 124 | A2 |

HFCs are considered **environmentally superior to CFCs** because:
1. **Zero ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** HFCs contain no chlorine atoms, so they do not react with stratospheric ozone. CFCs (e.g., R-12, R-11) contain chlorine which catalytically destroys O₃ molecules.
2. **Lower GWP than CFCs:** While HFCs still have GWP > 1, they are significantly lower than CFCs (e.g., R-12 has GWP = 10,900, whereas R-134a has GWP = 1430 — about 7.6× lower).
3. **Shorter atmospheric lifetime:** HFCs break down in decades, not centuries like CFCs.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is an HFO refrigerant? Why is R-1234yf called a "fourth-generation" refrigerant? State its ASHRAE safety classification.**

**Model Answer:**

**HFO Definition:** A Hydrofluoroolefin (HFO) is an **unsaturated** organic refrigerant containing at least one **carbon–carbon double bond (C=C)**, along with hydrogen and fluorine atoms. The double bond gives it a very short atmospheric lifetime (days to weeks).

**R-1234yf as a 4th-Generation Refrigerant:** The evolution of refrigerants is conventionally classified as:
- 1st Generation: CFCs (R-12, R-11) — high ODP, high GWP.
- 2nd Generation: HCFCs (R-22) — transitional, still has chlorine.
- 3rd Generation: HFCs (R-134a, R-410A) — zero ODP but high GWP.
- **4th Generation: HFOs (R-1234yf, R-1234ze) — zero ODP AND ultra-low GWP (GWP = 4 for R-1234yf).**

**ASHRAE 34 Safety Classification of R-1234yf:** **A2L**
- **A** = lower toxicity
- **2L** = mild flammability (burn velocity ≤ 10 cm/s, very hard to ignite)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the classification of refrigerants based on chemical composition with suitable examples. Discuss the role of HFO refrigerants in reducing global warming. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial refrigeration system uses 80 kg of R-410A. Annual leakage rate is 6%, lifetime is 12 years, end-of-life recovery is 75%, and the grid emission factor is 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh. The annual electricity consumption is 45,000 kWh. Calculate the **TEWI** for this system. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Classification of Refrigerants (by Chemical Composition):**

1. **CFC (Chlorofluorocarbons)** — contain C, Cl, F. Example: R-12 (CCl₂F₂), R-11 (CCl₃F). Banned under Montreal Protocol.

2. **HCFC (Hydrochlorofluorocarbons)** — contain H, C, Cl, F. Example: R-22 (CHClF₂). Transitional refrigerant, being phased out.

3. **HFC (Hydrofluorocarbons)** — contain H, C, F only. No chlorine. Examples: R-134a, R-32, R-410A (a blend). Zero ODP.

4. **HFO (Hydrofluoroolefins)** — unsaturated, contain C=C double bond. Examples: R-1234yf, R-1234ze. Zero ODP, ultra-low GWP.

5. **Natural Refrigerants** — occur in nature. Examples: NH₃ (R-717), CO₂ (R-744), propane (R-290), isobutane (R-600a), water (R-718).

**Role of HFOs in Reducing Global Warming:**

- HFOs have **GWP values between 1 and 10** (vs 1000–4000 for HFCs), a reduction of 100–1000×.
- The C=C double bond makes them **highly reactive with tropospheric OH radicals**, leading to atmospheric lifetimes of **days to weeks** (vs decades for HFCs).
- R-1234yf (GWP = 4) and R-1234ze(E) (GWP = 7) are already deployed as drop-in replacements for R-134a in automotive and commercial chillers.
- HFOs also form the basis of **4th-generation low-GWP blends** (R-454B, R-452B, R-513A) which combine HFO performance with HFC handling characteristics.
- They help industries comply with the **Kigali Amendment (2016)** phase-down of high-GWP HFCs.
- *Limitation:* HFOs can decompose to TFA (trifluoroacetic acid) and other PFAS — environmental research is ongoing.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b) — TEWI Calculation:**

**Given Data:**
- $m = 80$ kg
- $L = 0.06$ (6% leakage per year)
- $n = 12$ years
- $R = 0.75$ (75% recovery)
- $E_{annual} = 45{,}000$ kWh/year
- $EF = 0.82$ kg CO₂/kWh
- $GWP_{R-410A} = 2088$ (standard value)

**Step 1: Total Leaked Mass**
$$
m_{leak} = m \cdot [L \cdot n + (1 - R)] = 80 \cdot [0.06 \cdot 12 + 0.25] = 80 \cdot [0.72 + 0.25] = 80 \cdot 0.97 = 77.6 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2: Direct Emissions**
$$
\text{Direct} = m_{leak} \cdot GWP \cdot 100 = 77.6 \cdot 2088 \cdot 100 = 1.6203 \times 10^7 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 3: Indirect Emissions**
$$
\text{Indirect} = n \cdot E_{annual} \cdot EF = 12 \cdot 45{,}000 \cdot 0.82 = 4.428 \times 10^5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 4: Total TEWI**
$$
TEWI = 1.6203 \times 10^7 + 0.04428 \times 10^7 = 1.6646 \times 10^7 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{TEWI = 1.6646 \times 10^7 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} \approx 16{,}646 \text{ tonnes CO}_2\text{-eq}}$

**Interpretation:** The direct emissions dominate (~97% of total), confirming that **refrigerant leakage is the primary environmental concern**, not just energy efficiency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Differentiate between **zeotropic** and **azeotropic** refrigerant blends. Give two examples of each, and explain why zeotropic blends require special heat exchanger design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A window air conditioner uses 1.2 kg of R-32 (GWP = 675) instead of 1.2 kg of R-410A (GWP = 2088). Annual leakage is 3%, lifetime is 10 years, recovery is 80%, and grid factor is 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh. The annual electricity use is 1200 kWh. Compute and compare the **direct emission** of the two refrigerants. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

| Property | Zeotropic Blend | Azeotropic Blend |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Composition of liquid and vapor phases | **Different** | **Identical** |
| Boiling/condensation temperature | **Varies** (has a temperature glide) | **Constant** (single temperature) |
| Phase change behavior | Glide $\Delta T = T_{dew} - T_{bubble}$ (typically 2–10 °C) | No glide ($\Delta T = 0$) |
| Composition shift during leakage | **Yes** (fractionation) | No |
| Examples | R-407C, R-404A, R-410A | R-502, R-507A |
| Heat exchanger design | **Counter-flow** required | Parallel flow acceptable |

**Examples of Zeotropic Blends:**
- **R-407C:** R-32 (23%) / R-125 (25%) / R-134a (52%) — glide ≈ 7 °C, used in retrofit of R-22 AC.
- **R-404A:** R-125 (44%) / R-143a (52%) / R-134a (4%) — glide ≈ 0.8 °C, used in low/medium temp commercial refrigeration.

**Examples of Azeotropic Blends:**
- **R-507A:** R-125 (50%) / R-143a (50%) — true azeotrope, no glide, replaces R-502.
- **R-502** (legacy, now banned): R-22 / R-115.

**Why Zeotropic Blends Need Counter-Flow Heat Exchangers:**

Because of the temperature glide, the **saturated temperature** at the inlet of the evaporator/condenser is different from the outlet. In a **parallel-flow** exchanger, the temperature difference between the refrigerant and the secondary fluid decreases rapidly along the length, reducing the **log-mean temperature difference (LMTD)** and causing **non-uniform heat transfer** in different zones. A **counter-flow** arrangement allows the secondary fluid to track the changing refrigerant temperature along the length, maintaining a more uniform ΔT and improving the **heat transfer effectiveness** by 10–20%. Failure to use counter-flow results in **glide losses** of 5–15% in capacity and COP.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b) — Direct Emission Comparison:**

**Given Data (same for both):**
- $m = 1.2$ kg
- $L = 0.03$ (3% per year)
- $n = 10$ years
- $R = 0.80$
- $GWP_{R-32} = 675$, $GWP_{R-410A} = 2088$

**Step 1: Total Leaked Mass (same for both)**
$$
m_{leak} = 1.2 \cdot [0.03 \cdot 10 + (1 - 0.80)] = 1.2 \cdot [0.30 + 0.20] = 1.2 \cdot 0.50 = 0.60 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2: Direct Emissions for R-410A**
$$
\text{Direct}_{R-410A} = 0.60 \cdot 2088 \cdot 100 = 1.2528 \times 10^5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} = 125{,}280 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 3: Direct Emissions for R-32**
$$
\text{Direct}_{R-32} = 0.60 \cdot 675 \cdot 100 = 0.405 \times 10^5 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} = 40{,}500 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq}
$$

**Step 4: Comparison**
$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{125{,}280 - 40{,}500}{125{,}280} \times 100\% = 67.7\%
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\text{Switching from R-410A to R-32 reduces direct emissions by approximately 67.7\% (from 125,280 to 40,500 kg CO}_2\text{-eq)}.$

**Comment:** R-32 has **about one-third the GWP of R-410A**, and in this case the direct emission reduction is 67.7%. However, R-32 is mildly flammable (A2L) while R-410A is non-flammable (A1), so charging procedures and leak detection must follow **ASHRAE 15 and 34** safety guidelines. Also, R-32 has slightly higher discharge pressure and **requires re-sizing of the compressor** in some retrofit cases.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the GWP × 100 factor in TEWI:** Some students write `m_leak × GWP` and lose 2 marks. The standard IPCC convention is **multiply by 100** to keep numbers in the 10⁵–10⁷ range.
> 2. **Confusing ODP and GWP:** ODP is *zero* for ALL HFCs and HFOs. Don't write ODP = 0.5 for R-32 — it's **0**.
> 3. **Mixing up A2L and A2 flammability classes:** A2L = *mildly* flammable, A2 = *flammable*. The "L" makes a critical difference for safety design.
> 4. **Ignoring the recovery factor (1−R) term:** Many students forget that end-of-life release is ALSO a direct emission. Always include it.
> 5. **Not stating the units in TEWI:** Always write **kg CO₂-equivalent** in the final answer.
> 6. **Calling R-410A "azeotropic":** R-410A is **near-azeotropic** (very small glide, ~0.1 °C) — true azeotropes are rare (R-502, R-507A).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 / HFC, HFO & Mixed Refrigerants**

- ☐ **HFC = Hydrofluorocarbon** → contains H, F, C → **ODP = 0** always, but **GWP is high** (1430 for R-134a, 675 for R-32, 2088 for R-410A, 3922 for R-404A).
- ☐ **HFO = Hydrofluoroolefin** → contains **C=C double bond** → ODP = 0 AND GWP ≈ 1–10 → atmospheric lifetime = days (R-1234yf = 10.5 days).
- ☐ **ASHRAE 34 classification:** Toxicity (A or B) + Flammability (1, 2L, 2, 3). R-134a = A1, R-32 = A2L, R-1234yf = A2L, R-152a = A2, propane (R-290) = A3.
- ☐ **Zeotropic blend** = temperature glide + fractionation → needs **counter-flow** heat exchangers (R-407C, R-404A).
- ☐ **Azeotropic blend** = behaves like a pure fluid, **no glide** (R-507A, R-502).
- ☐ **R-410A** = R-32 (50%) + R-125 (50%), near-azeotropic, glide ≈ 0.1 °C, used in modern split ACs.
- ☐ **R-407C** = R-32 (23%) + R-125 (25%) + R-134a (52%), glide ≈ 7 °C, R-22 retrofit.
- ☐ **R-404A** = R-125 (44%) + R-143a (52%) + R-134a (4%), used in commercial low-temp refrigeration.
- ☐ **R-1234yf** = drop-in replacement for R-134a in automotive AC, GWP = 4, A2L.
- ☐ **R-1234ze(E)** = used in high-temp heat pumps and chillers, GWP = 7, A2L.
- ☐ **TEWI equation** = `m × [L×n + (1−R)] × GWP × 100 + n × E_annual × EF`. Direct term (refrigerant leakage) typically dominates.
- ☐ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** → banned CFCs; **Kigali Amendment (2016)** → phase-down of HFCs.
- ☐ **Favourite 3-mark questions:** (i) Define ODP & GWP, (ii) State ASHRAE class of R-32, (iii) HFO = "4th generation" because of C=C bond.
- ☐ **Favourite 7-mark sub-parts:** (i) Compare R-410A vs R-32 on environmental grounds, (ii) Explain why HFOs decompose rapidly in troposphere, (iii) Zeotropic vs Azeotropic with examples.
- ☐ **Favourite 14-mark questions:** TEWI calculation comparing two refrigerants (numerical), or classification of refrigerants + HFO discussion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Desirable physical, chemical and thermodynamic properties of refrigerants
Types of expansion devices - Constant pressure valve, Thermostatic expansion valve and capillary tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types of expansion devices - Constant pressure valve, Thermostatic expansion valve and capillary tube # Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Expansion Device? An **expansion device** (also called a **throttling device** or **expansion valve**) is a critical component of a **vapor compression refrigeration system (VCRS)**. It is a deliberate constriction placed between the **high-pressure liquid receiver** (outlet of condenser) and the **low-pressure evaporator inlet**. It performs **two simultaneous, indispensable functions**: 1. **Pressure Reduction:** Drops the refrigerant pressure from the **condensing pressure ($P_c$)** down to the **evaporating pressure ($P_e$)**. 2. **Refrigerant Metering:** Accurately regulates the rate at which liquid refrigerant is fed into the evaporator to match the cooling load. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is expansion (throttling) necessary?** > Throttling is an **isenthalpic process** ($h_3 = h_4$). When a high-pressure saturated/sub-cooled liquid passes through the restriction, a small amount of liquid **flash-evaporates** (typically 20–25%), absorbing its **latent heat of vaporization** from the remaining liquid. This causes a sharp temperature drop — the refrigerant enters the evaporator **cold** (typically 0°C to 5°C for air-conditioning duty), enabling it to absorb heat from the cooled space. --- ## 1.2 Classification of Expansion Devices The KTU 2024 syllabus groups expansion devices into two broad families: | Family | Type | Examples | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **I. Adjustable / Modulating Valves** | Pressure-controlled | **Constant Pressure (Automatic) Expansion Valve** | | | Thermal-controlled | **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV/TXV)** | | **II. Fixed / Non-adjustable** | Capillary action | **Capillary Tube** | | | Orifice type | Fixed orifice, Float valve | --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Garden Tap at Three Different Speeds"** > - Imagine you have a **high-pressure water pipeline** and you want to drip-feed a small garden plant. > - **Constant Pressure Valve:** A **pressure-regulated faucet** that opens wider whenever the plant's soil dries out (pressure drops) and shuts slightly when the soil is wet (pressure rises). It cares *only* about maintaining constant pressure downstream. > - **Thermostatic Expansion Valve:** A **smart thermostatic shower valve**. It has a temperature sensor (bulb) at the showerhead outlet. If water gets too cold (under-fed → too much evaporation → superheat rises), it opens more. If too hot (over-fed → liquid reaches outlet → superheat drops), it closes. It modulates to maintain **constant superheat**. > - **Capillary Tube:** A **fixed-length straw** inserted into the pipe. The diameter and length are cut once-and-for-all; you cannot adjust it. Friction alone throttles the flow. --- ## 1.4 Core Definitions (Syllabus Highlights) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Constant Pressure Expansion Valve (CPEV / AEV):** A pressure-actuated modulating valve that maintains the evaporator pressure **constant** at a pre-set value by balancing evaporator pressure against an adjustable spring force. > - **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV / TXV):** A thermally-actuated modulating valve that maintains a **constant degree of superheat** at the evaporator outlet, ensuring the evaporator is fully utilized without any liquid carry-over (flood-back) to the compressor. > - **Capillary Tube:** A fixed-bore, small-diameter, long copper tube that acts as both the metering and pressure-reducing device purely through **frictional resistance** — it has **no moving parts**. --- ## 1.5 Standard Refrigerant Metrics (KTU Benchmarks) - **R-22** (legacy HCFC): Evaporator $T_e = -5°C$ to $5°C$; Condenser $T_c = 40°C$ to $50°C$. - **R-410A, R-134a** (modern HFC/HFC blends): Operating pressures typically **15–25 bar** on high side, **2–6 bar** on low side. - **Capillary Tube:** Internal diameter **$d = 0.5$ to $2.5\ \text{mm}$**; Length **$L = 1$ to $6\ \text{m}$** (coiled). - **TEV Superheat Setting:** typically **$4°C$ to $10°C$** static superheat for air-conditioning duty. --- ## 1.6 Position in the VCRS Cycle (Thermodynamic Anchor) In the **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-h) diagram**, the expansion device executes the process **3 → 4**: - Point **3**: Saturated or sub-cooled liquid leaving condenser. - Point **4**: Two-phase mixture (quality $x \approx 0.20$ to $0.25$) entering evaporator. - **$h_3 = h_4$** (isenthalpic throttling). # Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Constant Pressure (Automatic) Expansion Valve (AEV) ### 2.1.1 Working Principle The AEV operates on **force balance** between two opposing forces acting on a diaphragm: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Closing Force:} \quad F_c &= P_e \cdot A_{top} + F_{spring} \\ \text{Opening Force:} \quad F_o &= P_s \cdot A_{top} \end{aligned}$$ where: - $P_e$ = Evaporator pressure (acting on underside of diaphragm via pressure equalizer tube) - $A_{top}$ = Effective diaphragm area - $F_{spring}$ = Spring force (adjustable via set-screw) - $P_s$ = Set pressure (constant) ### 2.1.2 Operating Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. When cooling load **increases** → more refrigerant evaporates → $P_e$ drops below set value. 2. The **spring pushes the diaphragm up** → **needle lifts** → valve opens wider. 3. More refrigerant flows in → $P_e$ rises back to set value → equilibrium restored. 4. When cooling load **decreases** → liquid refrigerant accumulates → $P_e$ rises above set value. 5. The **higher $P_e$ pushes diaphragm down** → needle moves toward seat → valve throttles closed. > [!NOTE] > **Net Effect:** Evaporator pressure is held **constant** irrespective of load fluctuations. ### 2.1.3 Advantages & Limitations | Advantages | Limitations | | :--- | :--- | | Simple, rugged, cheap | Evaporator is only **partially filled** at part-load → poor utilization | | Quick response to load change | Risk of liquid carry-over to compressor at sudden load reduction | | Protects compressor from high back-pressure | Not suitable for systems with widely varying load (e.g., multi-evaporator systems) | --- ## 2.2 The Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV / TXV) ### 2.2.1 Working Principle The TEV operates on a **three-force balance** on the diaphragm: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Opening Force (downward):} \quad F_o &= P_{bulb} \cdot A_{eff} \\ \text{Closing Forces (upward):} \quad F_c &= P_e \cdot A_{eff} + F_{spring} \end{aligned}$$ At equilibrium (valve position fixed): $$P_{bulb} = P_e + \frac{F_{spring}}{A_{eff}}$$ Since $P_{bulb}$ is a function of **bulb temperature** ($T_{bulb}$, which equals the evaporator outlet temperature), the TEV effectively modulates flow to keep $T_{bulb}$ (and hence **superheat**) constant. ### 2.2.2 Definition of Superheat $$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_{sh} &= T_{evap,outlet} - T_{sat}(P_e) \end{aligned}$$ where $T_{sat}(P_e)$ is the saturation temperature corresponding to evaporator pressure. ### 2.2.3 Operating Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. As cooling load **increases** → more refrigerant evaporates before reaching outlet → $T_{bulb}$ rises → bulb pressure rises → diaphragm pushed **down** → **valve opens wider**. 2. As cooling load **decreases** → liquid refrigerant reaches outlet → $T_{bulb}$ falls → bulb pressure falls → spring pushes diaphragm **up** → **valve closes**. 3. Result: **Constant superheat** maintained → **all liquid is guaranteed to evaporate** before the compressor inlet → **no liquid slugging**. ### 2.2.4 Types of TEV > [!IMPORTANT] > **I. Internally Equalized TEV** — The evaporator pressure is sensed at the **valve body** itself. Suitable for small evaporators with low pressure drop ($< 0.5\ \text{bar}$). > > **II. Externally Equalized TEV** — A separate **external equalizer line** senses pressure at the evaporator outlet. Essential for **large evaporators** with significant internal pressure drop ($> 0.5\ \text{bar}$), multiple distributors, or long circuit runs. > > **III. Charged Bulb Variants** — Liquid-charged, gas-charged, cross-charged (liquid + adsorber), and charge-coupled bulbs — selected based on temperature range and response time. ### 2.2.5 Advantages & Limitations | Advantages | Limitations | | :--- | :--- | | Full utilization of evaporator surface at all loads | More expensive than AEV | | Prevents liquid slugging of compressor | Bulb must be properly clamped at evaporator outlet | | Adapts well to varying loads | Slightly slower response than AEV | | Provides better COP at part-load | Requires external equalizer for high pressure-drop evaporators | --- ## 2.3 The Capillary Tube ### 2.3.1 Construction & Geometry A **capillary tube** is a seamless copper tube with: - Inner diameter: **$d = 0.5$ to $2.5\ \text{mm}$** - Outer diameter: **$D = 2$ to $5\ \text{mm}$** - Length: **$L = 1$ to $6\ \text{m}$** (coiled into a helix or laid in serpentine path) ### 2.3.2 Operating Principle The capillary tube has **no moving parts**. Pressure drop occurs due to: 1. **Viscous friction** along the tube wall. 2. **Sudden contraction** at the inlet. 3. **Sudden expansion** at the outlet. 4. **Acceleration** of refrigerant as its specific volume increases (due to flashing). The tube is sized so that the **frictional + accelerational pressure drop** exactly equals the difference $(P_c - P_e)$. ### 2.3.3 Optimal Subcooling Requirement > [!IMPORTANT] > For stable operation, the liquid entering the capillary tube **must be sub-cooled by at least $5°C$** below $T_{sat}(P_c)$. Without sub-cooling, two-phase flow inside the tube causes unstable mass flow rate. ### 2.3.4 Critical (Choked) Flow Condition At the **capillary tube exit**, if the local pressure drops to or below the **critical pressure** $P_{cr}$: $$P_{cr} = \eta_c \cdot P_c$$ where $\eta_c \approx 0.546$ (for R-12; ranges 0.53–0.58 for most refrigerants). Beyond this point, **choked flow** occurs — mass flow rate becomes independent of downstream (evaporator) pressure. ### 2.3.5 Advantages & Limitations | Advantages | Limitations | | :--- | :--- | | Cheapest of all expansion devices | Cannot compensate for load variation | | No moving parts → high reliability | Capacity depends on condenser/evaporator pressures | | Compact, no adjustment needed | Sensitive to moisture/contamination (must have filter-drier) | | Ideal for hermetically sealed, pre-charged units | Limited to small capacity (< 10–15 kW) | | Self-cleaning due to high velocity | Equalization on off-cycle can over-load compressor (requires accumulator) | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear regularly in KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.** | # | Equation | Description | | :-: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $h_3 = h_4$ | Isenthalpic throttling | | 2 | $\Delta T_{sh} = T_{out} - T_{sat}(P_e)$ | Static superheat definition | | 3 | $P_{bulb} = P_e + F_{spring}/A_{eff}$ | TEV force balance | | 4 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{in} (P_c - P_{cr})}$ | Capillary tube mass flow rate (choked flow, simplified) | | 5 | $P_{cr} = \eta_c \cdot P_c \approx 0.546\ P_c$ | Critical pressure for choked flow | | 6 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \mu L \dot{m}}{\rho d^2 A}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille viscous loss (laminar) | | 7 | $\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{d} \cdot \dfrac{\dot{m}^2}{2 \rho A^2}$ | Frictional pressure drop (Darcy-Weisbach form) | | 8 | $\dot{m} = C \cdot \dfrac{d^{2.6}}{L^{0.5}}$ | Empirical capillary sizing (correlated form) | | 9 | $\text{Sub-cooling requirement:} \quad \Delta T_{sc} \geq 5°C$ | For stable capillary operation | | 10 | $\text{COP} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_c - T_e}$ | Carnot COP (theoretical benchmark) | > **Units reminder:** All pressures in **kPa** or **bar**, temperatures in **°C** or **K**, mass flow in **kg/s**, lengths in **m**, diameters in **m**, density in **kg/m³**. # Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Mass Flow Rate Through a Capillary Tube (Analytical Form) We treat the capillary tube as a **control volume** of length $L$ and constant cross-section $A_c = \pi d^2 / 4$. ### Step 1 — Apply the Steady-Flow Energy Equation (Neglecting KE except at exit) $$\begin{aligned} h_{in} &= h_{out} + \Delta h_{friction} \end{aligned}$$ Because the process is isenthalpic in the absence of friction, any deviation from $h_{in} = h_{out}$ represents the irreversible friction loss. ### Step 2 — Express Enthalpy Difference in Terms of Pressure & Specific Volume Using the Maxwell relation for a compressible fluid: $$\begin{aligned} dh &= T\,dS + v\,dP \\ \text{For an isenthalpic process with friction:} \quad dh &= -\Delta h_f \\ \text{But also (from entropy generation):} \quad T\,dS &= -v\,dP \quad \text{(along the tube)} \end{aligned}$$ Combining, the **pressure gradient** along the tube due to friction and acceleration is: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{dP}{dx} &= -\frac{f}{d} \cdot \frac{\dot{m}^2}{2 \rho A_c^2} - \frac{\dot{m}^2}{\rho^2 A_c^2} \cdot \frac{dv}{dx} \end{aligned}$$ where: - $f$ = Darcy friction factor (function of Reynolds number and roughness) - $v$ = specific volume of refrigerant - $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s) ### Step 3 — Integrate from Inlet to Exit The total pressure drop across the capillary is: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta P = P_c - P_e = \int_0^L \left[ \frac{f}{d} \cdot \frac{\dot{m}^2}{2 \rho A_c^2} + \frac{\dot{m}^2}{\rho^2 A_c^2} \cdot \frac{dv}{dx} \right] dx \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Define the Two-Phase Density Model Assuming the refrigerant follows the homogeneous-equilibrium model with quality $x$ at any section: $$\begin{aligned} \rho &= \frac{1}{v_f + x(v_g - v_f)} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5 — Solve the Implicit Equation Iteratively Since $\rho$ varies along the length (refrigerant flashes as pressure drops), the integral above **cannot be solved analytically** in closed form. The standard KTU approach uses the **empirical correlation**: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m} &= K \cdot \frac{d^{2.6}}{L^{0.5}} \quad \text{[kg/s]} \end{aligned}$$ where: - $K$ = empirical constant depending on refrigerant and operating conditions (typically $K \approx 0.013$ for R-134a, R-410A) - $d$ = internal diameter (mm) - $L$ = length (m) ### Step 6 — Choked-Flow Limiting Case When the exit Mach number reaches unity ($M = 1$), the mass flow becomes: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{max} &= A_c \cdot P_c \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R T_c}} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma+1}{2(\gamma-1)}} \end{aligned}$$ The corresponding exit pressure (the critical pressure): $$\begin{aligned} P_{cr} &= \left(\frac{2}{\gamma+1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}} \cdot P_c \approx 0.546\,P_c \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** If $P_e < P_{cr}$, the capillary is in **choked flow** — increasing evaporator load further will NOT increase refrigerant flow. The system becomes load-limited. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical) > **Problem:** A refrigeration system using **R-134a** has a capillary tube of $d = 1.5\ \text{mm}$, $L = 3.5\ \text{m}$. Condensing temperature $T_c = 40°C$ ($P_c = 1017\ \text{kPa}$). Evaporating temperature $T_e = -10°C$ ($P_e = 200.6\ \text{kPa}$). Sub-cooling $= 5°C$. Estimate the mass flow rate using the empirical formula. ### Solution **Step 1:** Compute sub-cooled inlet enthalpy: $$h_3 = h_f\,@\,40°C - c_p \cdot \Delta T_{sc} = 256.41 - 1.42 \times 5 = 249.31\ \text{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2:** Check if choked flow occurs: $$P_{cr} = 0.546 \times 1017 = 555.3\ \text{kPa}$$ Since $P_e = 200.6\ \text{kPa} < P_{cr} = 555.3\ \text{kPa}$ → **Flow is CHOKED**. **Step 3:** Apply empirical correlation (with $K \approx 0.013$ for R-134a): $$\dot{m} = 0.013 \times \frac{(1.5)^{2.6}}{(3.5)^{0.5}}$$ Compute numerator: $$(1.5)^{2.6} = e^{2.6 \ln 1.5} = e^{2.6 \times 0.4055} = e^{1.0543} = 2.870$$ Compute denominator: $$(3.5)^{0.5} = 1.871$$ Therefore: $$\dot{m} = 0.013 \times \frac{2.870}{1.871} = 0.013 \times 1.534 = 0.01994\ \text{kg/s} \approx 71.8\ \text{kg/h}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** A 1.5 mm × 3.5 m capillary delivers ~72 kg/h of R-134a — sufficient for a **1 TR (3.5 kW)** household refrigerator. --- ## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: AEV vs TEV vs Capillary Tube | Parameter | Constant Pressure Valve (AEV) | Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV) | Capillary Tube | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Actuation** | Pressure | Thermal (bulb) | None (passive) | | **Moving parts** | Yes (diaphragm, needle, spring) | Yes (diaphragm, needle, spring, bulb) | No | | **Maintained variable** | Constant $P_e$ | Constant superheat | Constant $L$ and $d$ (geometry) | | **Load adaptability** | Moderate | Excellent | None | | **Evaporator utilization** | Partial at part-load | Full at all loads | Full at design load only | | **Liquid slugging risk** | High | Negligible | Moderate | | **Initial cost** | Low | Medium–High | Very low | | **Capacity range** | Small–Medium (1–100 kW) | Medium–Large (5 kW–MW) | Small (<15 kW) | | **Applications** | Older domestic refrigerators, ice-makers | Modern split ACs, chillers, supermarkets | Window AC, domestic fridge, dehumidifier | | **Equalizer needed?** | Usually no | Yes, external equalizer for large evaps | N/A | | **Maintenance** | Periodic | Periodic + bulb positioning | Replace if clogged | --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Tree — Which Device Should Be Used? ``` ┌── Single evaporator? ──────────┐ │ │ YES ────┤ ├─── NO → Use TEV per │ │ evaporator Load highly variable? │ (multi-circuit) │ │ YES ────┼─── NO │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ Use TEV (TXV) │ │ Use AEV or │ │ Maintains │ │ Capillary │ │ superheat → │ │ depending on │ │ full utilization│ │ capacity │ └──────────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ │ Capacity < 15 kW? │ YES ────┴─── NO │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌───────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ Use Capillary │ │ Use AEV (simple) │ │ Tube (cheap, │ │ or TEV (modern) │ │ no moving pt) │ │ │ └───────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ ``` # Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — All Three Expansion Devices Compared ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VCRS["Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle"] COMP["Compressor
Low P Vapor to High P Vapor"] COND["Condenser
High P Vapor to High P Liquid"] RX1{"Expansion Device
SELECT ONE BELOW"} EVAP["Evaporator
Low P Liquid to Low P Vapor"] ACC["Accumulator
Optional safety"] COMP --> COND --> RX1 --> EVAP --> ACC --> COMP end subgraph DEV1["Option 1: Constant Pressure Valve AEV"] AEV1["Diaphragm senses Evaporator Pressure Pe"] AEV2["Spring Force Fs opposes Pe"] AEV3["Force balance Pe x A = Fs"] AEV4["Output: Constant Pe maintained"] AEV1 --> AEV2 --> AEV3 --> AEV4 end subgraph DEV2["Option 2: Thermostatic Expansion Valve TEV"] TEV1["Bulb senses Evap Outlet Temperature Tb"] TEV2["Pb converts Tb to Bulb Pressure"] TEV3["Diaphragm: Pb vs Pe + Fs"] TEV4["Output: Constant Superheat dTsh"] TEV1 --> TEV2 --> TEV3 --> TEV4 end subgraph DEV3["Option 3: Capillary Tube"] CAP1["No moving parts"] CAP2["Friction plus Acceleration drop"] CAP3["Sub cooling atleast 5C required"] CAP4["Critical choked flow at Pcr"] CAP1 --> CAP2 --> CAP3 --> CAP4 end RX1 -. assigns .-> DEV1 RX1 -. assigns .-> DEV2 RX1 -. assigns .-> DEV3 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Flow — TEV Modulation Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Cooling Load
Increases"] --> B["More Refrig Evaporates"] B --> C["Bulb Temperature Tb Rises"] C --> D["Bulb Pressure Pb Rises"] D --> E["Diaphragm Pushes Down"] E --> F["Needle Lifts
Valve Opens More"] F --> G["More Refrigerant Flows In"] G --> H["Superheat Returns
to Set Value"] X["Cooling Load
Decreases"] --> Y["Liquid Reaches Outlet"] Y --> Z["Tb Falls"] Z --> AA["Pb Falls"] AA --> AB["Spring Pushes Up"] AB --> AC["Needle Closes
Valve Throttles"] AC --> AD["Superheat Returns
to Set Value"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Functional Flow — AEV Pressure Regulation ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Load Increases"] --> P2["Pe Falls Below Set"] P2 --> P3["Spring Pushes Diaphragm Up"] P3 --> P4["Valve Opens Wider"] P4 --> P5["More Refrig In"] P5 --> P6["Pe Rises to Set"] Q1["Load Decreases"] --> Q2["Pe Rises Above Set"] Q2 --> Q3["Pe Pushes Diaphragm Down"] Q3 --> Q4["Valve Closes"] Q4 --> Q5["Less Refrig In"] Q5 --> Q6["Pe Falls to Set"] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Process Map — Refrigerant Path Through Capillary Tube ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["High P Subcooled
Liquid at Inlet"] --> S2["Inlet Contraction
Minor Loss"] S2 --> S3["Frictional Flow
Along Length L"] S3 --> S4["Pressure and Temp Drop
Flashing Begins"] S4 --> S5["Two Phase Flow
Quality x Increases"] S5 --> S6["Exit Pressure Pe
or Critical Pcr"] S6 --> S7["Two Phase Mixture
Enters Evaporator"] ``` ## 4.5 Schematic Layout — Expansion Device Location in a Refrigerator ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Refrigerator["Domestic Refrigerator Schematic"] C["Compressor
Hermetically Sealed"] F1["Filter Drier"] CD["Condenser
External Coil"] CT["Capillary Tube
1.5 mm x 3 m
Coiled Near Compressor"] EV["Evaporator
Internal Coil"] AC["Accumulator
Before Compressor"] C --> F1 --> CD --> CT --> EV --> AC --> C end subgraph Notes["Critical Design Notes"] N1["Capillary tube is usually soldered to compressor suction line for heat exchange"] N2["Subcooling minimum 5C required"] N3["Filter drier upstream removes moisture"] end Refrigerator --> Notes ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** The schematics above are **functional block diagrams** rather than photorealistic drawings — this format is optimal for KTU board examinations because it isolates operating logic and makes force/pressure/flow paths explicit. # Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Explain the working of a thermostatic expansion valve with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV) is a **temperature-actuated modulating valve** that maintains a **constant degree of superheat** at the evaporator outlet. Its key components are: **bulb** (clamped at evaporator outlet), **diaphragm**, **spring**, **needle/pin**, **valve body**, and optionally an **external equalizer line**. - The bulb senses the **outlet temperature** of the refrigerant leaving the evaporator. This temperature is converted into a **bulb pressure ($P_{bulb}$)** acting on the top of the diaphragm. - The bottom of the diaphragm experiences the **evaporator pressure ($P_e$)** plus the **adjustable spring force ($F_{spring}$)**. - The force balance at equilibrium is $P_{bulb} = P_e + F_{spring}/A_{eff}$. - When the **load increases**, more refrigerant evaporates, superheat rises, $P_{bulb}$ rises, the diaphragm is pushed down, the needle lifts, and **more refrigerant flows in**. - When the **load decreases**, superheat falls, $P_{bulb}$ falls, the spring pushes the diaphragm up, the needle closes, and **refrigerant flow is throttled**. - The valve thus modulates flow continuously to keep **superheat constant** (typically 4–10 °C), ensuring complete evaporation and preventing liquid slugging at the compressor. **Diagram credit:** (Sketch showing bulb at evaporator outlet, diaphragm chamber, spring, valve body with refrigerant flow paths.) --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a capillary tube and an expansion valve.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Sl. | Capillary Tube | Expansion Valve (AEV/TEV) | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **No moving parts** — fixed geometry | Has moving parts (diaphragm, needle, spring) | | 2 | Pressure drop via **friction** | Pressure drop via **flow restriction** through needle valve | | 3 | Cannot adapt to load variation | Modulates flow with load change | | 4 | Very **cheap** and compact | Costlier; TEV most expensive | | 5 | Used in **small systems** (< 15 kW): domestic fridges, window AC | Used in **medium/large systems**: split AC, chillers | | 6 | Sensitive to clogging; needs filter-drier | Robust; requires only periodic servicing | | 7 | Equalization during off-cycle may overload compressor | Self-regulating; safer during cycling | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. The student answers **ONE of the two alternatives.** --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working of a thermostatic expansion valve (TEV)**. Discuss the role of the **external equalizer line**. **(b) [7 marks]** A refrigeration system using **R-22** has a TEV set to maintain a superheat of **6 °C**. Evaporator pressure is **$P_e = 3.8\ \text{bar}$** (saturation temp $T_{sat} = -5°C$). The bulb pressure is **$P_{bulb} = 4.6\ \text{bar}$**. If the bulb temperature drops by **2 °C** such that $P_{bulb}$ falls to **4.3 bar**, calculate the **new superheat** (assuming $P_e$ remains constant). Comment on the valve action. #### Model Solution to (a) 1. **Construction [2 marks]:** List and label components — bulb, capillary tube, diaphragm chamber, spring, needle, valve seat, external equalizer. 2. **Working [3 marks]:** Explain three-force balance: $P_{bulb} \cdot A_{eff} = P_e \cdot A_{eff} + F_{spring}$. 3. **External equalizer [2 marks]:** Required when evaporator has significant internal pressure drop ($\Delta P > 0.5$ bar). Without it, the valve would sense a fictitious low $P_e$, over-feed the evaporator, and cause liquid carry-over. #### Model Solution to (b) **Given:** - $P_e = 3.8\ \text{bar}$, $T_{sat}(P_e) = -5°C$ - Initial $P_{bulb,1} = 4.6\ \text{bar}$, $T_{bulb,1} = T_{sat}(P_e) + 6 = +1°C$ - New $P_{bulb,2} = 4.3\ \text{bar}$, find $T_{bulb,2}$ and new superheat. **Step 1:** Using R-22 saturation tables, $dP/dT \approx 0.12\ \text{bar/°C}$ near $-5°C$. **Step 2:** Approximate change in saturation temperature: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_{bulb} &= \frac{\Delta P_{bulb}}{dP/dT} = \frac{4.3 - 4.6}{0.12} = \frac{-0.3}{0.12} = -2.5°C \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3:** New bulb temperature: $$T_{bulb,2} = +1°C - 2.5°C = -1.5°C$$ **Step 4:** New superheat: $$\Delta T_{sh,2} = T_{bulb,2} - T_{sat}(P_e) = -1.5 - (-5) = 3.5°C$$ **Step 5:** Valve action [1 mark]: Since superheat dropped from **6 °C → 3.5 °C**, the bulb pressure is now **lower than** $P_e + F_{spring}/A_{eff}$. The **spring pushes the diaphragm up**, the needle moves toward the seat, and the **valve throttles closed** — reducing refrigerant flow to restore superheat to 6 °C. --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]` **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **construction and working of a capillary tube** as an expansion device. Discuss its **limitations** and state the **sub-cooling requirement** for stable operation. **(b) [7 marks]** A capillary tube of **length 2.5 m** and **inside diameter 1.2 mm** is used in an R-134a refrigeration system. The condenser pressure is **$P_c = 900\ \text{kPa}$** and evaporator pressure is **$P_e = 180\ \text{kPa}$**. Using the empirical correlation $\dot{m} = K \cdot d^{2.6}/L^{0.5}$ with $K = 0.012$ (R-134a), determine the **mass flow rate**. Check if the flow is **choked**. #### Model Solution to (a) 1. **Construction [2 marks]:** Seamless copper tube, $d = 0.5$–$2.5\ \text{mm}$, $L = 1$–$6\ \text{m}$, coiled. 2. **Working [3 marks]:** Frictional + acceleration pressure drop; no moving parts; passive metering. 3. **Limitations [1 mark]:** No load adaptability; choking risk; sensitivity to contaminants; equalization on off-cycle. 4. **Sub-cooling requirement [1 mark]:** $\Delta T_{sc} \geq 5°C$ for stable two-phase flow. #### Model Solution to (b) **Step 1:** Compute $d^{2.6}$: $$d^{2.6} = (1.2)^{2.6} = e^{2.6 \ln 1.2} = e^{2.6 \times 0.1823} = e^{0.4741} = 1.607$$ **Step 2:** Compute $L^{0.5}$: $$L^{0.5} = (2.5)^{0.5} = 1.581$$ **Step 3:** Mass flow rate: $$\dot{m} = 0.012 \times \frac{1.607}{1.581} = 0.012 \times 1.0165 = 0.01220\ \text{kg/s} = 43.9\ \text{kg/h}$$ **Step 4:** Choking check: $$P_{cr} = 0.546 \times 900 = 491.4\ \text{kPa}$$ Since $P_e = 180\ \text{kPa} \ll 491.4\ \text{kPa}$ → **Flow is fully choked**. **Step 5:** Interpretation [1 mark]: Mass flow is now independent of $P_e$. To increase capacity, the system must raise $P_c$ (increase condenser saturation temperature) or use a **larger diameter** capillary. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students LOSE marks on expansion-device questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the isenthalpic assumption** ($h_3 = h_4$) when drawing P-h diagrams — examiners award 1 mark specifically for this. > 2. **Confusing AEV and TEV:** Many students write "AEV maintains constant superheat" — this is **wrong**. AEV maintains constant **pressure**; TEV maintains constant **superheat**. > 3. **Not labeling the bulb location:** The TEV bulb must be clamped at the **evaporator OUTLET**, not inlet. Markers deduct 0.5–1 mark for this. > 4. **Skipping the external equalizer discussion:** Any TEV question worth ≥ 7 marks expects explicit mention of when an **external equalizer** is required (pressure drop > 0.5 bar). > 5. **Using wrong refrigerant property units:** R-134a tables give saturation pressure in **kPa**, not bar. Mixing units causes 30–40% numerical error. > 6. **Forgetting the $\Delta T_{sc} \geq 5°C$** sub-cooling rule for capillary tubes — frequently asked as a 1-mark Part-A question. > 7. **Not stating "choked flow"** when $P_e < 0.546 P_c$ — examiners expect this qualitative conclusion. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Expansion Devices** > > ✅ Expansion device performs **two functions**: pressure reduction + flow metering. > > ✅ Process 3→4 in VCRS is **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$); ~20–25% of liquid flash-evaporates. > > ✅ **Constant Pressure Valve (AEV):** Maintains **constant $P_e$**; force balance between $P_e$ and spring; **partial evaporator utilization** at part load. > > ✅ **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TEV):** Maintains **constant superheat** ($\Delta T_{sh} = 4°\text{–}10°C$); three-force balance $P_{bulb} \cdot A = P_e \cdot A + F_{spring}$; **prevents liquid slugging**. > > ✅ TEV **bulb** is filled with the **same refrigerant** as the system; clamped at evaporator **outlet**. > > ✅ **External equalizer** required when evaporator pressure drop **> 0.5 bar** (large evaporators, multiple distributors). > > ✅ **Capillary tube:** $d = 0.5$–$2.5\ \text{mm}$, $L = 1$–$6\ \text{m}$; **no moving parts**; pressure drop via friction + acceleration; sub-cooling $\geq 5°C$ mandatory. > > ✅ **Choked flow** in capillary occurs when $P_e < P_{cr} \approx 0.546\,P_c$; mass flow becomes independent of $P_e$. > > ✅ Empirical capillary sizing: $\dot{m} = K \cdot d^{2.6}/L^{0.5}$ (for R-134a, $K \approx 0.012$–$0.013$). > > ✅ Capillary tube is always paired with a **filter-drier** (upstream) and an **accumulator** (downstream) for safety. > > ✅ TEV > AEV > Capillary tube in **load adaptability**. > > ✅ Capillary tube > TEV > AEV in **simplicity and cost** (capillary cheapest, TEV most expensive). > > ✅ All three devices must convert the high-pressure liquid into a low-quality **two-phase mixture** entering the evaporator.

topics.title: Electronic expansion valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electronic expansion valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV)** is an actuated, electrically-controlled refrigerant throttling device that precisely regulates the mass flow rate of refrigerant entering the evaporator in a vapor-compression refrigeration cycle, by continuously modulating the refrigerant flow in response to real-time feedback signals from pressure and temperature sensors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** EEV is a *modulating flow control device* in which an electronic controller (microprocessor-based) commands an actuator (solenoid or stepper motor) to position the valve stem, based on inputs from a **pressure transducer** at the evaporator outlet and **temperature sensors** at the evaporator inlet and outlet. The objective is to maintain the **optimal degree of superheat** at the evaporator exit and to maximize the **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of the system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding)

Think of an EEV as a **"smart, self-adjusting kitchen faucet"** connected to a *digital thermometer and pressure gauge*:

- The **cold water tap** is like the **refrigerant flow** entering the evaporator.
- The **temperature gauge on the tap** is like the **thermistor at the evaporator inlet**.
- The **pressure gauge on the nozzle** is like the **pressure transducer at the compressor suction**.
- The **"smart sensor chip"** built into the faucet constantly reads the temperature and pressure, then a tiny **electric motor** automatically turns the knob to open/close the tap.
- Result: The faucet always delivers *just the right amount* of water at the *right temperature* — never too much (which would waste water/flood) and never too little (which would starve the system).

In a refrigeration system, the EEV continuously "fine-tunes" itself to ensure that **liquid refrigerant does not reach the compressor** (which would cause damage) and that the **evaporator is fully utilized** for maximum cooling effect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from TXV:** A Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) uses a *mechanical diaphragm and a feedback spring* to control superheat. An EEV replaces this mechanical feedback with *electronic sensors + a microcontroller + a motorized actuator*, giving faster response, higher accuracy, and the ability to communicate with building management systems (BMS).

## 1.3 Classification of EEV (As Per KTU Module 3 Syllabus)

EEVs are broadly classified based on the **type of actuator** used:

1. **Electromagnetic (Solenoid) Pulse Expansion Valve** — Operates on ON/OFF pulses; uses a solenoid coil.
2. **Electric Motor-Driven (Stepper Motor) Expansion Valve** — Uses a stepper/DC motor with gear reduction; provides continuous modulation.
3. **Piezo-Electric Expansion Valve** — Uses piezoelectric crystals for ultra-fine control (advanced application).
4. **Pulse Width Modulated (PWM) Expansion Valve** — Uses solenoid with variable pulse width to approximate continuous flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 examinations, the **Stepper Motor-Driven EEV** and the **Pulse (Solenoid) EEV** are the two most frequently tested variants.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Reference Metrics

- **Standard superheat target:** $\Delta T_{sh} = 4^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $6^{\circ}\text{C}$ (typical for refrigeration); higher for AC systems ($5^{\circ}\text{C}$–$8^{\circ}\text{C}$).
- **Response time of stepper motor EEV:** $1\text{ s}$ to $5\text{ s}$ full stroke.
- **Operating voltage of stepper motor:** typically **$12\text{ V DC}$** or **$24\text{ V DC}$**.
- **Sensor accuracy:** Temperature sensor ($\pm 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$), Pressure transducer ($\pm 0.5\%$ full scale).
- **Pulsation frequency of solenoid EEV:** typically **$1\text{ Hz}$ to $10\text{ Hz}$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of an Electronic Expansion Valve

A complete EEV system consists of **three integrated sub-systems**:

### 2.1.1 The Valve Assembly (Mechanical Body)
- A **throttling body** with a needle/plunger orifices arrangement.
- A **movable spindle/stem** that varies the effective flow area.
- A **spring-loaded seat** to provide the default closing force (fail-safe closed).
- **Inlet and outlet ports** for refrigerant flow.

### 2.1.2 The Actuator
- **Stepper motor (most common):** Receives discrete electrical pulses; each pulse rotates the motor by a fixed angular step (e.g., $0.9^{\circ}$ per pulse). A gear train converts this rotation into linear motion of the valve stem.
- **Solenoid coil (in pulse-type EEV):** Energized/de-energized rapidly to open/close a pilot valve.

### 2.1.3 The Electronic Controller & Sensors
- **Microprocessor-based controller** executing PID or fuzzy-logic algorithms.
- **Pressure transducer** at the **evaporator outlet** (measures evaporating pressure $P_e$).
- **Temperature sensors (thermistors):** One at the **evaporator inlet**, one at the **evaporator outlet**.
- **Optional sensors:** Suction line temperature, ambient temperature, discharge temperature.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The EEV operates on a **closed-loop feedback control strategy**. The control objective is to maintain a **target superheat** at the evaporator outlet:

$$\Delta T_{sh,\ target} = T_{evap\ outlet} - T_{sat}(P_e)$$

where $T_{sat}(P_e)$ is the saturation temperature corresponding to the measured evaporating pressure $P_e$.

**Logic Flow (How the EEV "Thinks"):**

- **Step 1 — Sense:** The pressure transducer measures $P_e$ and the thermistor measures $T_{out}$.
- **Step 2 — Compute Superheat:** The controller calculates actual superheat $\Delta T_{sh,\ actual} = T_{out} - T_{sat}(P_e)$.
- **Step 3 — Compare with Setpoint:** Error $e(t) = \Delta T_{sh,\ target} - \Delta T_{sh,\ actual}$.
- **Step 4 — Compute Control Action:** Using a **PID algorithm**, the controller computes the required valve opening change $\Delta u(t)$:

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$ are the proportional, integral, and derivative gains respectively.

- **Step 5 — Actuate:** The stepper motor receives a specific number of pulses (e.g., 50 pulses = 0.5 mm lift) to open/close the valve.
- **Step 6 — Repeat:** The cycle continues at intervals of typically $1$–$2$ seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PID Control?** Pure proportional control leads to a *steady-state offset* (valve never fully reaches the setpoint). Integral action eliminates this offset, and derivative action improves response to sudden load changes (e.g., door opening of a refrigerator).

## 2.3 Why and How — The "Engineering Reasoning"

| Operating Condition | Superheat Reading | Controller Action | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low load (less heat to absorb) | $\Delta T_{sh,\ actual} > \Delta T_{sh,\ target}$ | **Close valve** (reduce flow) | Less refrigerant needed; excess flow would over-cool and reduce superheat below target. |
| High load (more heat to absorb) | $\Delta T_{sh,\ actual} < \Delta T_{sh,\ target}$ | **Open valve** (increase flow) | More refrigerant needed to absorb the higher heat load; prevent liquid carry-over to compressor. |
| Liquid refrigerant detected at outlet (danger) | $\Delta T_{sh,\ actual} \approx 0$ | **Rapidly close valve** (safety override) | Protect compressor from liquid slugging. |

## 2.4 Comparison: EEV vs. Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)

This is a **high-yield KTU comparison question** that appears regularly in university exams.

| Parameter | Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) | Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV) |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback mechanism | Mechanical (diaphragm + spring) | Electronic (sensors + microcontroller) |
| Response speed | Slow (thermal lag) | Fast (milliseconds to seconds) |
| Superheat control accuracy | $\pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $2^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.1^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Adjustability | Limited (fixed superheat setting) | Programmable (adaptive setpoint) |
| Communication with BMS | Not possible | Possible (Modbus, BACnet) |
| Energy efficiency | Lower (mismatch losses) | Higher (typically $5\%$–$15\%$ COP improvement) |
| Cost | Low | Higher (initial) |
| Maintenance | Simple | Requires sensor calibration |
| Suitable for variable load | Limited | Excellent |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta T_{sh} = T_{out} - T_{sat}(P_e)$ | Actual superheat at evaporator outlet | ${}^{\circ}\text{C}$ or $\text{K}$ |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d\,de(t)/dt$ | PID controller output | Dimensionless / pulses |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{ref} = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{2 \rho (P_c - P_e)}$ | Mass flow rate through EEV (Bernoulli-based) | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 4 | $\text{COP} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{comp}} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Coefficient of Performance | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $A_{eff} = A_{max} \cdot f(N_{pulses})$ | Effective flow area (function of valve position) | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 6 | $N_{step} = \frac{\theta_{required}}{\theta_{step}}$ | Number of stepper motor pulses | Pulses |
| 7 | $T_{sat}(P_e) = f(\text{refrigerant})$ | Saturation temperature (from refrigerant tables) | ${}^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 8 | $Q_L = \dot{m}_{ref} (h_1 - h_4)$ | Evaporator cooling capacity | $\text{W}$ or $\text{kW}$ |
| 9 | $\text{EEV Opening}\ (\%) = \frac{N_{pulses\ applied}}{N_{pulses\ max}} \times 100$ | Valve position indicator | $\% $ |
| 10 | $\Delta u(n) = K_p [e(n) - e(n-1)] + K_i\,e(n) + K_d [e(n) - 2e(n-1) + e(n-2)]$ | Discrete PID (digital form) | Pulses per cycle |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the question asks for a *numerical answer*, always substitute the **discrete (digital) PID form** since the EEV operates in sampled time, not continuous time. Marks are often lost for using the continuous form without justification.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic refrigerators and freezers:** Variable-speed compressors paired with EEVs achieve $20\%$–$30\%$ energy savings (inverter technology).
- **Automotive air-conditioning:** EEVs compensate for rapidly varying compressor speeds ($800$–$6000$ rpm) where TXVs would lag.
- **Chillers and commercial HVAC:** EEVs enable capacity modulation and integration with Building Management Systems (BMS).
- **Cold storage and food processing:** Critical for maintaining precise humidity and temperature control.
- **Heat pumps:** EEVs allow reversal of flow direction in cooling/heating modes without manual adjustment.
- **Supercritical CO₂ systems:** EEVs are essential because TXVs cannot operate at supercritical pressures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Flow Rate Through an EEV (Based on Bernoulli's Equation)

The refrigerant undergoes an **isenthalpic throttling process** from condenser pressure $P_c$ to evaporator pressure $P_e$ across the EEV. Applying the steady-flow energy equation across the valve:

$$h_{in} = h_{out} \quad \text{(throttling is isenthalpic)}$$

For an **incompressible approximation** (valid for liquids and two-phase mixtures at moderate velocities), the mass flow rate is derived from Bernoulli's equation:

$$\dot{m}_{ref} = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{2\, \rho_{in}\, (P_c - P_e)}$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Apply the steady-flow energy equation between EEV inlet (state $5'$) and outlet (state $6$):

$$h_{5'} + \frac{V_{5'}^2}{2} + g z_{5'} = h_6 + \frac{V_6^2}{2} + g z_6 + q - w_s$$

**Step 2:** Apply valid assumptions for throttling:
- No shaft work: $w_s = 0$
- No heat transfer (adiabatic, fast process): $q = 0$
- Negligible change in elevation: $z_{5'} = z_6$
- The process is isenthalpic: $h_{5'} = h_6$

This simplifies to:

$$\frac{V_{5'}^2}{2} = \frac{V_6^2}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Bernoulli's equation (mechanical energy balance, including pressure):

$$\int \frac{dP}{\rho} + \frac{V_6^2 - V_{5'}^2}{2} = 0$$

**Step 4:** For a liquid-like fluid with approximately constant density $\rho$:

$$\frac{P_c - P_e}{\rho} = \frac{V_6^2 - V_{5'}^2}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Using continuity, $V_{5'} A_{in} = V_6 A_{eff}$, and assuming $A_{in} \gg A_{eff}$ (vena contracta effect), $V_{5'} \ll V_6$, so $V_{5'} \approx 0$:

$$\frac{P_c - P_e}{\rho} \approx \frac{V_6^2}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for exit velocity $V_6$:

$$V_6 = \sqrt{\frac{2\,(P_c - P_e)}{\rho}}$$

**Step 7:** Multiply by effective area $A_{eff}$ and a discharge coefficient $C_d$ (to account for viscous losses and vena contracta):

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{ref} = C_d \cdot A_{eff} \cdot \sqrt{2\, \rho\, (P_c - P_e)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The mass flow rate is proportional to (i) the effective flow area (controlled by the EEV's actuator), (ii) the square root of the pressure differential across the valve, and (iii) the square root of the inlet density. Hence, **$A_{eff}$ is the primary control variable** in an EEV.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Valve Position and Superheat

Let $x(t)$ be the **valve stem position** (dimensionless, $0 \le x \le 1$). The effective area is:

$$A_{eff}(t) = A_{max} \cdot x(t)$$

The mass flow rate equation becomes:

$$\dot{m}_{ref}(t) = C_d \cdot A_{max} \cdot x(t) \cdot \sqrt{2\, \rho\, (P_c - P_e)}$$

The evaporator cooling load is given by:

$$Q_L(t) = \dot{m}_{ref}(t) \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$$

For **steady-state** operation, the refrigerant mass flow rate entering the evaporator equals the rate at which the load is absorbed. If the load increases suddenly, the superheat will initially **drop** (more heat available per kg of refrigerant), prompting the controller to **increase $x(t)$** (open the valve more) until superheat returns to the setpoint.

The **steady-state balance** in the evaporator gives:

$$\dot{m}_{ref} \cdot (h_{1} - h_{in,evap}) = \dot{Q}_{L}$$

For a subcooled or two-phase inlet, the energy balance yields the superheat relation. For a saturated inlet (typical assumption):

$$h_{in,evap} = h_f(P_e) \quad \text{(saturated liquid at evaporator pressure)}$$

The **outlet enthalpy** (after superheating by $\Delta T_{sh}$):

$$h_{out,evap} = h_g(P_e) + c_{p,v} \cdot \Delta T_{sh}$$

where $c_{p,v}$ is the specific heat of superheated vapor.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PID Controller Steady-State Error Analysis

Consider the closed-loop superheat control system. Define the error:

$$e(t) = \Delta T_{sh,set} - \Delta T_{sh,actual}(t)$$

The valve position update (in continuous time) is:

$$x(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**In steady state**, if a *constant disturbance* (e.g., constant heat load) is present, the derivative term vanishes ($de/dt = 0$). For a P-only controller, a residual error $e_{ss}$ would persist (offset). With PI control:

$$K_p\,e_{ss} + K_i \int_0^{\infty} e_{ss}\,dt = x_{required}$$

For steady constant error, the integral term accumulates and drives $e_{ss} \to 0$, which is the **key benefit of integral action** in EEV control.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** An EEV controls refrigerant R-134a flow into an evaporator. The pressure transducer reads $P_e = 3.5\text{ bar}$ and the outlet thermistor reads $T_{out} = 8^{\circ}\text{C}$. The target superheat is $5^{\circ}\text{C}$. The condenser pressure is $P_c = 12\text{ bar}$. Calculate:
1. Actual superheat (use R-134a saturation table: at $3.5\text{ bar}$, $T_{sat} \approx 3.5^{\circ}\text{C}$).
2. Should the EEV open or close? By how many pulses (assume $K_p = 8$ pulses/K, integral contribution $= 2$ pulses)?

**Step 1: Actual superheat calculation:**

$$\Delta T_{sh,actual} = T_{out} - T_{sat}(P_e) = 8^{\circ}\text{C} - 3.5^{\circ}\text{C} = 4.5^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Error calculation:**

$$e = \Delta T_{sh,set} - \Delta T_{sh,actual} = 5 - 4.5 = +0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$$

Since the actual superheat is **less than** the target, the evaporator is **over-fed** with refrigerant. The EEV should **close** (reduce flow).

**Step 3: Valve pulse command:**

$$\Delta u = K_p \cdot e + K_i \cdot \int e\, dt = 8 \times 0.5 + 2 = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ pulses (close)}$$

**Final answer:** The EEV should **close by 6 pulses** to bring superheat back to the $5^{\circ}\text{C}$ target.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EEV System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **closed-loop control architecture** of an Electronic Expansion Valve system, including all sensors, the controller, the actuator, and the physical refrigerant flow path.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Evaporator Outlet - Superheated Vapor] --> B[Pressure Transducer PT]
    A --> C[Temperature Sensor T2 - Thermistor]
    D[Evaporator Inlet - Two-Phase Refrigerant] --> E[Temperature Sensor T1 - Thermistor]
    B --> F[Microprocessor Controller MCU]
    C --> F
    E --> F
    G[Target Superheat Setpoint - User BMS Input] --> F
    F --> H[PID Control Algorithm - Kp Ki Kd]
    H --> I[Stepper Motor Driver Circuit - PWM Signals]
    I --> J[Stepper Motor Actuator]
    J --> K[Electronic Expansion Valve - Throttling Element]
    K --> L[High Pressure Liquid from Condenser]
    K --> M[Low Pressure Two-Phase to Evaporator]
    M --> D
    F --> N[Safety Interlock - Liquid Floodback Detector]
    N --> J
    F --> O[Display and Communication Module - BMS Modbus]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style K fill:#87CEEB
    style F fill:#90EE90
    style J fill:#FFB6C1
    style H fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: EEV Closed-Loop Control Decision Logic

The diagram below maps the **decision tree** followed by the controller each sampling cycle to determine whether to open, close, or hold the EEV position.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Start of Sampling Cycle - Every 1 to 2 seconds] --> Q[Read Pressure Transducer Pe]
    Q --> R[Read Outlet Temperature Tout]
    R --> S[Look up Tsat from Refrigerant Property Table]
    S --> T[Compute Actual Superheat SH_actual = Tout - Tsat]
    T --> U[Compute Error e = SH_set - SH_actual]
    U --> V{Is Liquid Floodback Detected SH_actual less than 1 degree C}
    V -->|Yes| W[EMERGENCY CLOSE - Force Valve to 0 percent Opening]
    V -->|No| X{Is Error within Plus or Minus 0.2 degree C Deadband}
    X -->|Yes| Y[HOLD - No Valve Action this Cycle]
    X -->|No| Z[Apply PID Equation - Compute Delta_u]
    Z --> AA{Is Error Positive SH too Low}
    AA -->|Yes| AB[CLOSE Valve by Delta_u Pulses]
    AA -->|No| AC[OPEN Valve by Delta_u Pulses]
    AB --> AD[Wait for Next Sampling Period]
    AC --> AD
    Y --> AD
    W --> AD
    AD --> P

    style P fill:#FFD700
    style W fill:#FF6347
    style AB fill:#FFA07A
    style AC fill:#98FB98
    style Y fill:#D3D3D3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparison of EEV vs. TXV Control Philosophy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TXV[Thermostatic Expansion Valve System]
        T1[Evaporator Outlet Temperature] --> T2[Diaphragm and Spring Feedback]
        T2 --> T3[Mechanical Needle Position]
        T3 --> T4[Throttling Action]
    end

    subgraph EEV[Electronic Expansion Valve System]
        E1[Pressure Transducer and Thermistor] --> E2[Microcontroller with PID]
        E2 --> E3[Stepper Motor Driver]
        E3 --> E4[Stepper Motor with Gear Reduction]
        E4 --> E5[Precision Needle Position]
        E5 --> E6[Throttling Action]
        E2 --> E7[BMS Communication and Data Logging]
    end

    style T2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style E2 fill:#90EE90
    style E7 fill:#87CEEB
```

## 4.4 Component-Level Layout of a Stepper-Motor-Driven EEV

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Stepper Motor - 12V DC 0.9 degree per step] --> B[Gear Reduction Unit - 50 to 100:1 ratio]
    B --> C[Lead Screw and Threaded Spindle]

    D --> E[Throttling Orifice - Variable Aperture]
    F[Inlet Port - High Pressure Liquid] --> E
    E --> G[Outlet Port - Low Pressure Two-Phase]
    H[Return Spring - Fail Safe Closed] --> D

    style A fill:#FFB6C1
```
