# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define an Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV). List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**

An Electronic Expansion Valve (EEV) is a microprocessor-controlled modulating throttling device that regulates refrigerant flow into the evaporator in response to signals from pressure and temperature sensors, to maintain a precise degree of superheat at the evaporator outlet.

**Main components:**
1. **Valve body** with a movable throttling element.
2. **Actuator** — stepper motor or solenoid.
3. **Electronic controller** (microprocessor) executing PID algorithm.
4. **Pressure transducer** at the evaporator outlet.
5. **Temperature sensors** (thermistors) at evaporator inlet and outlet.
6. **Driver circuit** for the actuator.
7. **Communication interface** (optional, for BMS integration).

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any four advantages of an Electronic Expansion Valve over a Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV).**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Higher control accuracy** — superheat maintained within $\pm 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ versus $\pm 2^{\circ}\text{C}$ for TXV.
2. **Faster response** — electronic feedback in milliseconds versus thermal lag in TXV.
3. **Programmable setpoint** — adaptive superheat control for variable load conditions.
4. **BMS integration** — can communicate with centralized building management systems for diagnostics and optimization.
5. **Higher COP** — typically $5\%$ to $15\%$ improvement in energy efficiency.
6. **Better performance at part-load** — critical for inverter-driven compressors.

*(Any four accepted for full 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a **stepper-motor-driven Electronic Expansion Valve** with a neat block diagram. (7 marks)

**b)** Describe the **PID control algorithm** used in EEV for superheat control. Explain the role of each term ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) in maintaining stable evaporator operation. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

**Construction:** A stepper-motor-driven EEV consists of:

1. **Stepper Motor** (typically a permanent-magnet or hybrid stepper, $12$ V DC, $0.9^{\circ}$/step) — converts electrical pulses into rotary motion.
2. **Gear Reduction Unit** (ratio $50{:}1$ to $100{:}1$) — amplifies torque and reduces speed.
3. **Lead Screw and Threaded Spindle** — converts rotary motion to linear motion of the valve stem.
4. **Valve Stem and Needle** — varies the throttling orifice area.
5. **Return Spring** — provides a default closing force (fail-safe closed during power failure).
6. **Sensors** — pressure transducer at evaporator outlet and thermistors at inlet and outlet.
7. **Microprocessor-based controller** with PID algorithm and driver electronics.

**Working Principle:**
- The pressure transducer continuously monitors the evaporating pressure $P_e$ and the outlet thermistor monitors the outlet temperature $T_{out}$.
- The microcontroller computes actual superheat $\Delta T_{sh} = T_{out} - T_{sat}(P_e)$ using built-in refrigerant property tables.
- The error $e = \Delta T_{sh,set} - \Delta T_{sh,actual}$ is fed to the PID algorithm.
- The output of the PID algorithm is converted into a specific number of stepper motor pulses.
- Each pulse rotates the motor by $0.9^{\circ}$, and after gear reduction, translates to a small linear displacement of the needle, modulating refrigerant flow.
- If superheat is low (too much liquid), the valve closes by a calculated number of pulses. If superheat is high, the valve opens.

**Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full architecture).

**(b) PID Control Algorithm (7 marks):**

The PID algorithm computes the control action $u(t)$ as:

$$u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Role of each term:**

1. **Proportional term ($K_p \cdot e(t)$):** Provides an immediate response proportional to the current error. A large $K_p$ yields fast response but may cause oscillation; small $K_p$ is stable but slow. However, P-only control leaves a **steady-state offset** (valve never exactly reaches the setpoint).

2. **Integral term ($K_i \int e\,dt$):** Accumulates past errors and drives the steady-state error to **zero**. Critical for accurate setpoint tracking in EEV. However, too large $K_i$ causes **integrator wind-up** and oscillation.

3. **Derivative term ($K_d \cdot de/dt$):** Predicts future error based on the rate of change. Dampens oscillations and improves response to **sudden load transients** (e.g., door opening). However, it amplifies sensor noise and is often disabled in practice for slow-changing refrigeration systems.

**Practical Implementation (Discrete Form):** Since the EEV controller operates in sampled time (every $1$ to $2$ seconds), the discrete PID is used:

$$\Delta u(n) = K_p\,[e(n) - e(n-1)] + K_i\,e(n) + K_d\,[e(n) - 2e(n-1) + e(n-2)]$$

**Safety override:** A separate logic continuously checks for liquid floodback; if $\Delta T_{sh,actual} < 1^{\circ}\text{C}$, the valve is forced closed regardless of the PID output.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a pulse-width-modulated (PWM) solenoid-type Electronic Expansion Valve**. (7 marks)

**b)** Compare the **Electronic Expansion Valve** with the **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)** in terms of at least eight parameters. State the applications where EEV is preferred. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) PWM Solenoid-Type EEV (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
- A solenoid coil wound around a magnetic plunger.
- A pilot valve and a main valve (two-stage design for amplified flow).
- A spring that returns the plunger to the closed position when de-energized.
- A control circuit that supplies pulsed DC voltage to the solenoid.

**Working Principle:**
- The solenoid is energized and de-energized rapidly (frequency $1$ to $10$ Hz).
- The **duty cycle** (ratio of ON-time to total cycle time) determines the **average valve opening**:

$$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} \times 100\%$$

- A higher duty cycle corresponds to a larger average flow area.
- The controller adjusts the duty cycle based on the superheat error:
  - If superheat is too low → reduce duty cycle (valve mostly closed).
  - If superheat is too high → increase duty cycle (valve mostly open).

**Advantage:** Simple, low-cost, no moving precision parts beyond the plunger.
**Disadvantage:** Pulsating flow causes vibration and noise; less precise than stepper motor type.

**(b) EEV vs. TXV Comparison (7 marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | TXV | EEV |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Feedback | Mechanical (diaphragm) | Electronic (sensors + MCU) |
| 2 | Accuracy | $\pm 1$ to $2^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.1$ to $0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 3 | Response time | Slow (thermal lag) | Fast (milliseconds) |
| 4 | Adjustability | Fixed setpoint | Programmable, adaptive |
| 5 | BMS integration | Not possible | Possible |
| 6 | Cost | Low initial | Higher initial |
| 7 | Part-load efficiency | Poor | Excellent |
| 8 | Maintenance | Simple | Requires sensor calibration |
| 9 | Suitability for variable speed compressor | Limited | Excellent |
| 10 | COP improvement | Baseline | $5\%$–$15\%$ higher |

**Preferred Applications of EEV:**
1. **Variable-speed (inverter) air conditioners and heat pumps.**
2. **Automotive AC systems** with rapidly varying compressor speed.
3. **Commercial chillers** with capacity modulation.
4. **Supercritical CO₂ refrigeration systems** (TXV cannot operate here).
5. **Smart refrigeration** with IoT and BMS integration.
6. **High-efficiency cold storage** where energy savings justify higher cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing EEV with TEV/TXV in the diagram** — Marks lost for drawing a diaphragm-based TXV and labeling it as an EEV. Always show sensors + microcontroller + actuator.
> 2. **Using continuous-time PID in a sampled system** — Always justify that the EEV controller operates in discrete time and write the discrete PID form for full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the units of $K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$** — $K_p$ is in pulses/K, $K_i$ in pulses/(K·s), $K_d$ in pulses·s/K. Examiners look for dimensional consistency.
> 4. **Missing the safety override (liquid floodback protection)** — This is a frequently asked 2-mark sub-question. Always mention it.
> 5. **Failing to mention the fail-safe-closed feature of EEV** — Critical for compressor protection; often asked as a 1-mark short question.
> 6. **Confusing "Electronic Expansion Valve" with "Electric Expansion Valve"** — These are *synonymous* terms, but the correct KTU terminology is **"Electronic Expansion Valve"**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electronic Expansion Valves (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Full Form & Function:** EEV = *Electronic Expansion Valve*. It is a **modulating**, **electrically-actuated**, **sensor-feedback-controlled** throttling device.
- ✅ **Primary Control Objective:** Maintain **target superheat** ($\Delta T_{sh}$) at the **evaporator outlet** to protect compressor from liquid carry-over and to maximize COP.
- ✅ **Superheat Formula:** $\Delta T_{sh} = T_{out} - T_{sat}(P_e)$ — always use refrigerant saturation tables.
- ✅ **Three Subsystems:** Valve body + Actuator (stepper motor / solenoid) + Controller (PID + sensors).
- ✅ **Sensors Required:** Pressure transducer (evaporator outlet) + Thermistor (evaporator outlet) + Thermistor (evaporator inlet, optional).
- ✅ **Controller Algorithm:** **PID** (Proportional + Integral + Derivative). Use **discrete form** for digital implementation.
- ✅ **Stepper Motor Specs:** $0.9^{\circ}$/step typical; $12$ V DC; gear reduction $50{:}1$ to $100{:}1$.
- ✅ **PWM Solenoid EEV:** Uses duty cycle control; frequency $1$–$10$ Hz; cheaper but pulsating.
- ✅ **Mass Flow Equation:** $\dot{m} = C_d A_{eff} \sqrt{2 \rho (P_c - P_e)}$ — flow proportional to valve opening.
- ✅ **Fail-Safe Default:** Spring-loaded **closed** — protects compressor on power failure.
- ✅ **Safety Override:** Liquid floodback detection (if $\Delta T_{sh,actual} < 1^{\circ}\text{C}$) forces valve fully closed.
- ✅ **EEV vs. TXV:** EEV is **faster, more accurate, BMS-compatible, more efficient** at part-load.
- ✅ **Typical Setpoint:** $\Delta T_{sh} = 4$ to $6^{\circ}\text{C}$ (refrigeration); $5$ to $8^{\circ}\text{C}$ (AC).
- ✅ **Key Advantage for KTU:** COP improvement of $5\%$–$15\%$ over TXV-based systems.
- ✅ **Application Areas:** Inverter ACs, automotive AC, commercial chillers, supercritical CO₂ systems, IoT-enabled smart refrigeration.
- ✅ **Exam Favorite Topics:** (i) Construction & working of stepper-motor EEV, (ii) PID algorithm explanation, (iii) EEV vs. TXV comparison table, (iv) Numerical on superheat calculation and pulse command.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of compressors in refrigeration systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types of compressors in refrigeration systems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)**, a **compressor** is the *prime mover* of the thermodynamic cycle. Its primary function is to **draw in low-pressure, low-temperature refrigerant vapour from the evaporator and discharge it as a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour** to the condenser. The compressor is the only component in the VCRC that consumes external work (typically electrical shaft work or mechanical work from an internal combustion engine), and it is therefore the dominant contributor to the system's overall energy input. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEMET751):** > A refrigeration compressor is a mechanical device that increases the pressure of the refrigerant vapour by *reducing its specific volume* — converting mechanical shaft work into the *flow work* (flow enthalpy) of the working fluid. ### 1.2 Broad Classification of Refrigeration Compressors Refrigeration compressors are classified into **two fundamentally different families** based on the mechanism by which pressure rise is achieved: > [!NOTE] > **Classification Logic — How pressure rise is achieved:** > > 1. **Positive Displacement Compressors** — Pressure rise is achieved by *mechanically trapping a fixed mass of refrigerant and physically reducing its volume*. The compression is essentially *independent of the discharge pressure* (within mechanical limits). Examples: Reciprocating, Scroll, Screw, Rotary Vane. > > 2. **Dynamic (or Turbo) Compressors** — Pressure rise is achieved by *imparting kinetic energy to the refrigerant using a high-speed rotating impeller*, followed by conversion of this kinetic energy into pressure energy inside a diffuser. Compression *depends strongly on flow rate and impeller speed*. Examples: Centrifugal, Axial. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Pump vs Water Turbine" View > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy:** > Imagine you have two ways to push air into a tyre: > > - **Bicycle Pump (Positive Displacement)** — You *trap* a slug of air in a cylinder and *squeeze* it with a piston. The harder you squeeze, the higher the pressure, even if the air moves slowly. > - **Water Turbine Pump (Dynamic)** — A high-speed spinning impeller *throws* the fluid outward; the fluid's velocity is then *slowed down* in a diffuser, converting kinetic energy into pressure. This works *only* when there is a steady, continuous flow. > > A reciprocating refrigeration compressor behaves exactly like a bicycle pump; a centrifugal refrigeration compressor behaves like a centrifugal water pump running in reverse. ### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Definition of Terms) The following standard metrics appear repeatedly in KTU problems and must be committed to memory: - **Displacement Volume (Vs)** — The theoretical volume swept by the piston (or equivalent swept volume in rotary machines) per cycle, in **m³/s**. - **Volumetric Efficiency (ηv)** — Ratio of *actual suction volume* to *displacement volume*. **Typical values: 0.7 to 0.9** for reciprocating compressors. - **Isentropic Efficiency (ηisen)** — Ratio of *isentropic work* to *actual work* of compression. - **Mechanical Efficiency (ηmech)** — Ratio of *indicated work* to *brake work* (accounts for friction). - **Overall (Isothermal) Efficiency (ηoverall)** — Ratio of *isothermal work* to *brake work*. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Sub-Classification & Working Logic The two broad families are further sub-divided as follows. Each sub-type operates on a *distinct* thermodynamic geometry but ultimately delivers the same end-result — a high-pressure refrigerant vapour stream. #### 2.1.1 Positive Displacement Compressors **A) Reciprocating Compressor** - Uses a *piston–cylinder* arrangement driven by a crankshaft. - Compression occurs in *four distinct strokes* per cycle (in a single-acting, single-cylinder machine): Suction → Compression → Discharge → Expansion of clearance gas. - The presence of a *clearance volume* is unavoidable and is the principal cause of *volumetric inefficiency*. - **Capacity range:** From ~50 W (small domestic refrigerators) up to ~500 kW (industrial chillers). - **Application focus:** Domestic refrigerators, window ACs, cold storage, large industrial refrigeration. **B) Rotary Compressors** — A family of *rotating-element* positive-displacement machines: - **Scroll Compressor** — Uses *two interleaving spiral scrolls* (one fixed, one orbiting). As the orbiting scroll traces a circular path, the *pockets of trapped refrigerant progressively shrink in volume*, achieving smooth, continuous compression. *No suction/discharge valves* are required. - **Screw Compressor** — Uses *two helical rotors* (male and female) that mesh together. Refrigerant is trapped in the cavities between the rotor lobes and is *swept axially* from suction to discharge end. Used in *medium-to-large capacity* systems. - **Rotary Vane Compressor** — An *eccentric rotor* carries multiple sliding vanes; refrigerant trapped between adjacent vanes is compressed as the rotor sweeps the stator bore. Common in *small window ACs*. #### 2.1.2 Dynamic Compressors **A) Centrifugal Compressor** - A high-speed *impeller* (typically 3,000–30,000 rpm) accelerates the refrigerant radially outward. - The high-velocity refrigerant then passes through a *diffuser* (vaned or vaneless) where kinetic energy is converted to pressure energy (similar to a Pelton turbine run in reverse). - **No internal compression at design point** — pressure rise is *flow-dependent*. - Used in *very large capacity* applications: district cooling, large chillers, petrochemical plants. **B) Axial Compressor** - Multiple stages of *rotating and stationary blade rows* accelerate the refrigerant progressively along the axis of flow. - Each stage contributes a small pressure rise; many stages are stacked in series. - Rare in refrigeration; more common in *gas turbine and aerospace* applications. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Reciprocating Compressor (Highest KTU Yield) The reciprocating compressor is *the most heavily tested* machine in KTU papers. Master this logic: 1. **Suction Stroke (4 → 1):** Piston moves from TDC to BDC. Suction valve opens; low-pressure refrigerant from evaporator fills the cylinder at approximately constant pressure P1. 2. **Compression Stroke (1 → 2):** Both valves closed. Piston moves from BDC to TDC. Refrigerant is compressed polytropically from P1 to P2. 3. **Discharge Stroke (2 → 3):** Discharge valve opens. Refrigerant is expelled into the condenser at constant pressure P2. 4. **Expansion Stroke (3 → 4):** Discharge valve closes. Clearance gas expands polytropically from P2 to P1. Once pressure falls below P1, the suction valve reopens and the cycle repeats. ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step > [!NOTE] > **Why does the clearance volume exist?** > Mechanical necessity — the piston cannot physically touch the cylinder head. The clearance volume is *unavoidable*. However, it has a *devastating* effect on volumetric efficiency because the expanded clearance gas *occupies space* that could otherwise have been filled with fresh refrigerant from the evaporator. KTU examiners test this concept repeatedly. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Values | |---|---------|-------------|------------------------| | 1 | $W = \dfrac{n}{n-1} \, P_{1} V_{1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\!(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | Polytropic work of compression (reciprocating) | W in J/cycle; n = polytropic index (**1.25 to 1.35**) | | 2 | $\eta_{v} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!1/n}$ | Volumetric efficiency with clearance | C = Vc/Vs = clearance ratio (**0.03 to 0.08**) | | 3 | $\eta_{v} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!1/\gamma}$ | Volumetric efficiency (isentropic) | $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats (**1.30 for R-134a vapour**) | | 4 | $W_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \, P_{1} V_{1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\!(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | Isentropic compression work (1 kg) | J/kg | | 5 | $h_{2s} - h_{1} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \, R \, T_{1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\!(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$ | Isentropic enthalpy rise for ideal gas | J/kg | | 6 | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_{1}}{h_{2a} - h_{1}}$ | Isentropic efficiency | Dimensionless (**0.7 to 0.85**) | | 7 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{W_{indicated}}{W_{brake}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | Dimensionless (**0.85 to 0.95**) | | 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{W_{isothermal}}{W_{brake}}$ | Overall (isothermal) efficiency | Dimensionless (**0.55 to 0.7**) | | 9 | $\text{COP}_{R} = \dfrac{Q_{L}}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{h_{1} - h_{4}}{h_{2} - h_{1}}$ | Coefficient of Performance of refrigeration cycle | Dimensionless | | 10 | $N = \dfrac{V_{actual}}{\eta_{v} \, V_{s}}$ | Required compressor speed for a given refrigerant flow | N in RPM | | 11 | $\text{HP} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \, (h_{2} - h_{1})}{\eta_{mech} \cdot 746}$ | Required brake power (in HP) | HP | | 12 | $T_{2} = T_{1} \left(\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}}\right)^{\!(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Isentropic discharge temperature (ideal gas) | Kelvin | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Pitfall:** Do not confuse the *polytropic index n* (used in volumetric efficiency for a *real compression process*) with the *isentropic index $\gamma$* (used for an *ideal, reversible, adiabatic* process). They are *different*. Many students lose marks by writing one where the other is required. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The choice of compressor dictates *almost every other design parameter* of a refrigeration system: - **Capacity** — Centrifugal/screw for > 200 TR; reciprocating/scroll for 0.5–100 TR. - **Part-load efficiency** — Scroll and screw are excellent; reciprocating is moderate. - **Noise & vibration** — Scroll is the quietest; reciprocating is the loudest. - **Maintenance cost** — Rotary (scroll, screw) > Reciprocating (due to absence of valves). - **Refrigerant compatibility** — Scroll and centrifugal tolerate modern low-GWP refrigerants (R-32, R-1234yf) very well. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Isentropic Work of Compression (Foundation Derivation) We begin from the *First Law for an open system* (steady-flow energy equation), assuming a *single-stage compressor* with negligible changes in kinetic and potential energy. $$ w_{comp} = h_{2} - h_{1} \quad \text{[per unit mass of refrigerant]} $$ Assuming refrigerant vapour behaves as an *ideal gas* over the compression range: $$ h_{2} - h_{1} = c_{p}(T_{2} - T_{1}) $$ Using the *isentropic relation* $T_{2s}/T_{1} = (P_{2}/P_{1})^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$: $$ T_{2s} - T_{1} = T_{1} \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right] $$ Multiplying through by $c_{p}$ and using $c_{p} = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$: $$ w_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1} \, T_{1} \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right] $$ Since $P_{1} v_{1} = R T_{1}$ (ideal gas equation of state): $$ w_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, P_{1} v_{1} \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right] $$ For a *total mass m* of refrigerant (with $V_{1} = m v_{1}$): $$ W_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, P_{1} V_{1} \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right] $$ > This is the *master equation* used in over 70% of KTU compressor problems. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency with Clearance Volume This derivation is the *single most important* for KTU PEMET751 Module 3. Consider the *P-V diagram* of a reciprocating compressor with clearance volume. **Step 1 — Define the clearance ratio:** $$ C = \dfrac{V_{c}}{V_{s}} = \dfrac{\text{Clearance volume}}{\text{Swept volume}} $$ **Step 2 — Identify the four key states on the P-V diagram:** - State 3: End of discharge (P2, Vc) - State 4: End of clearance expansion (P1, V4) - State 1: End of suction (P1, V1 = Vc + Vs) - State 2: End of compression (P2, Vc) **Step 3 — Apply the polytropic expansion relation (Process 3 → 4):** $$ P_{2} V_{c}^{\,n} = P_{1} V_{4}^{\,n} $$ Solving for $V_{4}$: $$ V_{4} = V_{c} \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!1/n} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate the *effective* (drawn-in) volume:** $$ V_{drawn} = V_{1} - V_{4} = (V_{c} + V_{s}) - V_{c} \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!1/n} $$ **Step 5 — Form the ratio (definition of volumetric efficiency):** $$ \eta_{v} = \dfrac{V_{drawn}}{V_{s}} = \dfrac{V_{c} + V_{s} - V_{c} \left( P_{2}/P_{1} \right)^{\!1/n}}{V_{s}} $$ **Step 6 — Divide numerator and denominator by Vs:** $$ \eta_{v} = C + 1 - C \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!1/n} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\eta_{v} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!1/n}\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** The expression shows that $\eta_{v}$ *decreases* as the pressure ratio $P_{2}/P_{1}$ *increases*. This is why multi-stage compression with *inter-cooling* is used for high-pressure-ratio applications — to keep $\eta_{v}$ acceptably high. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem (KTU University Exam – May 2024 style):** A single-stage reciprocating compressor handles **R-134a** at a suction pressure of **2 bar** and discharge pressure of **10 bar**. The clearance ratio is **0.06** and the polytropic index is **1.30**. Calculate the volumetric efficiency. **Solution (each step shown):** $$ \eta_{v} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!1/n} $$ $$ \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \dfrac{10}{2} = 5 $$ $$ \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!1/n} = 5^{\,1/1.30} = 5^{\,0.7692} $$ $$ 5^{\,0.7692} = e^{0.7692 \ln 5} = e^{0.7692 \times 1.6094} = e^{1.2382} \approx 3.450 $$ $$ \eta_{v} = 1 + 0.06 - 0.06 \times 3.450 = 1.06 - 0.207 = 0.853 $$ $$ \boxed{\;\eta_{v} \approx 85.3\%\;} $$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Compressor Types at a Glance | Parameter | Reciprocating | Scroll | Screw (Twin) | Centrifugal | |-----------|---------------|--------|--------------|-------------| | **Mechanism** | Piston–cylinder | Orbiting scroll | Meshing rotors | Impeller + diffuser | | **Capacity range (TR)** | 0.1 – 500 | 1 – 60 | 20 – 1500 | 100 – 10,000 | | **Typical $\eta_{v}$** | 0.70 – 0.90 | 0.95 – 0.99 | 0.90 – 0.97 | N/A (flow-based) | | **Isentropic $\eta$** | 0.70 – 0.85 | 0.80 – 0.90 | 0.80 – 0.88 | 0.75 – 0.85 | | **Pressure ratio per stage** | High (up to 12) | Medium (up to 10) | Medium (up to 20) | Low (up to 4) | | **Part-load behaviour** | Poor (stepwise) | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | | **Noise / vibration** | High | Very low | Low | Low | | **Maintenance** | High (valves) | Very low | Moderate | Low | | **Suitability for VSD** | Limited | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | | **Clearance volume** | Yes (significant) | No | No | No | | **Application** | Domestic / Industrial AC | Window / Split AC | Chillers | District cooling | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram) The diagram below presents the *complete hierarchical classification* of refrigeration compressors covered in KTU PEMET751 Module 3. All node IDs are alphanumeric, and all labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text with no markdown formatting. ```mermaid graph TD root1[REFRIGERATION COMPRESSORS] root1 --> nodeA[POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT TYPE] root1 --> nodeB[DYNAMIC OR TURBO TYPE] nodeA --> nodeA1[RECIPROCATING COMPRESSOR] nodeA --> nodeA2[ROTARY COMPRESSORS] nodeA1 --> nodeA1a[PISTON CYLINDER TYPE] nodeA1 --> nodeA1b[DIAPHRAGM TYPE] nodeA2 --> nodeA2a[SCROLL COMPRESSOR] nodeA2 --> nodeA2b[SCREW COMPRESSOR] nodeA2 --> nodeA2c[ROTARY VANE COMPRESSOR] nodeA2 --> nodeA2d[LOBE COMPRESSOR] nodeB --> nodeB1[CENTRIFUGAL COMPRESSOR] nodeB --> nodeB2[AXIAL COMPRESSOR] nodeA1a --> nodeA1a1[SUITABLE FOR SMALL TO MEDIUM CAPACITY] nodeA1a --> nodeA1a2[HAS CLEARANCE VOLUME LOSS] nodeA2a --> nodeA2a1[ORBITING AND FIXED SCROLLS] nodeA2a --> nodeA2a2[USED IN WINDOW AND SPLIT AC] nodeB1 --> nodeB1a[RADIAL FLOW IMPELLER] nodeB1 --> nodeB1b[USED FOR LARGE CAPACITY CHILLERS] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow of a Reciprocating Compressor This block diagram captures the *functional flow* of a reciprocating compressor — the sequence in which thermodynamic events occur in a *single cylinder*. ```mermaid graph LR inp1[LOW PRESSURE VAPOUR FROM EVAPORATOR] --> step1[STEP 1 SUCTION STROKE
PISTON MOVES BDC
SUCTION VALVE OPEN] step1 --> step2[STEP 2 COMPRESSION STROKE
BOTH VALVES CLOSED
PRESSURE RISES TO P2] step2 --> step3[STEP 3 DISCHARGE STROKE
PISTON AT TDC
DISCHARGE VALVE OPEN] step3 --> step4[STEP 4 EXPANSION STROKE
CLEARANCE GAS EXPANDS
P2 FALLS TO P1] step4 --> step5[SUCTION VALVE REOPENS] step5 --> step1 step3 --> out1[HIGH PRESSURE VAPOUR TO CONDENSER] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow of a Scroll Compressor This flow captures the *continuous, smooth compression process* that distinguishes scroll compressors from reciprocating machines. ```mermaid graph LR inp2[LOW PRESSURE SUCTION GAS] --> scA[FIXED SCROLL RECEIVES GAS
AT OUTER POCKET] scA --> scB[ORBITING SCROLL MOVES
GAS TOWARDS CENTRE] scB --> scC[POCKET VOLUME SHRINKS
PRESSURE RISES] scC --> scD[COMPRESSION CONTINUES
UNTIL DISCHARGE PORT] scD --> out2[HIGH PRESSURE GAS
TO CONDENSER] scB --> scE[CONTINUOUS MULTIPLE
POCKETS IN PARALLEL] ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow of a Centrifugal Compressor This block diagram explains the *two-stage energy conversion* — kinetic energy first, then pressure energy — that is unique to dynamic compressors. ```mermaid graph LR inp3[LOW PRESSURE VAPOUR
FROM EVAPORATOR] --> cf1[STEP 1 IMPELLER
ROTATES AT HIGH RPM
VELOCITY INCREASES] cf1 --> cf2[STEP 2 DIFFUSER
VANED OR VANELESS
VELOCITY DECREASES] cf2 --> cf3[STEP 3 PRESSURE RISES
KINETIC ENERGY TO PRESSURE ENERGY] cf3 --> out3[HIGH PRESSURE VAPOUR
TO CONDENSER] cf1 --> cf1a[VOLUTE COLLECTS
DISCHARGE GAS] cf1a --> out3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Notice how the *reciprocating* flow returns to itself (cyclic), the *scroll* flow is *one-directional and continuous*, and the *centrifugal* flow is *linear and steady*. These topological differences visually convey why each machine is suited to different duty cycles. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three differences between a positive displacement compressor and a dynamic compressor.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Positive Displacement | Dynamic | |--------|----------------------|---------| | Mechanism | Traps fixed mass and reduces volume | Imparts kinetic energy using rotating impeller | | Pressure rise | Independent of flow (within limits) | Strongly dependent on flow rate | | Compression | Pulsating / cyclic | Smooth / continuous | | Typical example | Reciprocating, Scroll | Centrifugal | --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define clearance ratio and volumetric efficiency for a reciprocating compressor.* **Model Answer:** - **Clearance Ratio (C):** The ratio of clearance volume (Vc) to swept volume (Vs), i.e. $C = V_{c}/V_{s}$. Typical values range from **0.03 to 0.08**. - **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{v}$):** The ratio of the *actual volume of refrigerant drawn in per cycle* to the *swept (displacement) volume of the piston*, expressed as a percentage or a fraction. Typical values range from **70% to 90%**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > As per the KTU 2024 scheme ESE pattern, students must answer **one of two** alternatives. Both alternatives are constructed at equal cognitive weight (Bloom's L3–L4: Application / Analysis). --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]** **(a)** A single-stage reciprocating compressor has a *bore of 150 mm* and a *stroke of 200 mm*. The clearance volume is **6%** of the swept volume. The compressor takes in refrigerant at **1.4 bar** and delivers it at **9 bar**. The polytropic index is **1.30**. Calculate: 1. The swept volume per cycle. **(2 marks)** 2. The volumetric efficiency. **(2 marks)** 3. The actual volume of refrigerant drawn in per cycle. **(3 marks)** **(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **scroll compressor**. List two advantages over a reciprocating compressor. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Part (a):** **Step 1 — Swept volume per cycle:** $$ V_{s} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \times L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.150)^{2} (0.200) $$ $$ V_{s} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0225 \times 0.200 = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^{3} $$ $$ \boxed{V_{s} = 3.534 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{3/cycle}} $$ **Step 2 — Volumetric efficiency:** $$ \eta_{v} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!1/n} = 1 + 0.06 - 0.06 \left( \dfrac{9}{1.4} \right)^{\!1/1.30} $$ $$ \dfrac{9}{1.4} = 6.4286 $$ $$ (6.4286)^{\,1/1.30} = e^{(1/1.30) \ln(6.4286)} = e^{0.7692 \times 1.8608} = e^{1.4314} \approx 4.184 $$ $$ \eta_{v} = 1.06 - 0.06 \times 4.184 = 1.06 - 0.2510 = 0.809 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{v} = 80.9\%} $$ **Step 3 — Actual volume drawn in:** $$ V_{actual} = \eta_{v} \times V_{s} = 0.809 \times 3.534 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ \boxed{V_{actual} = 2.859 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^{3/cycle}} $$ --- **Model Solution to Part (b):** A **scroll compressor** consists of two interleaved spiral-shaped scrolls: - **Fixed Scroll** — Stationary, attached to the compressor housing. - **Orbiting Scroll** — Moves in a small circular orbit (without rotating) driven by the crankshaft. **Working Sequence:** 1. Suction gas enters the *outermost pocket* formed between the two scrolls at suction pressure P1. 2. As the orbiting scroll moves along its circular path, the trapped gas pocket is *sealed* and *moves towards the centre*. 3. The *pocket volume progressively decreases* during the orbit, providing smooth, continuous compression. 4. At the *centre of the fixed scroll*, the compressed gas reaches discharge pressure P2 and exits through the discharge port. **Two Advantages over Reciprocating Compressors:** 1. **Higher volumetric efficiency** (95–99%) due to absence of clearance volume. 2. **Lower noise and vibration** due to absence of suction/discharge valves and reciprocating motion. 3. *Bonus:* Fewer moving parts → lower maintenance cost. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 style] — Alternative to Question A** **(a)** With the help of a labelled **P-V diagram**, explain the working of a *single-acting, single-stage reciprocating compressor*. Discuss the effect of *clearance volume* on volumetric efficiency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A centrifugal compressor handles **1500 kg/h** of R-134a. The suction enthalpy is **390 kJ/kg** and discharge enthalpy is **430 kJ/kg**. The isentropic enthalpy at discharge is **420 kJ/kg**. The mechanical efficiency is **0.90**. Calculate: 1. Isentropic efficiency. **(2 marks)** 2. Actual work of compression per kg. **(2 marks)** 3. Brake power required. **(3 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Part (a):** The P-V diagram consists of the following processes: - **Process 4 → 1 (Suction):** Constant pressure P1; volume increases from V4 to V1. - **Process 1 → 2 (Compression):** Polytropic compression; volume decreases from V1 to Vc, pressure rises from P1 to P2. - **Process 2 → 3 (Discharge):** Constant pressure P2; volume remains at Vc. - **Process 3 → 4 (Clearance Expansion):** Polytropic expansion; volume increases from Vc to V4. **Effect of Clearance Volume:** The clearance volume causes the expanded gas (at V4) to *re-occupy cylinder space* at the beginning of the next suction stroke. As the pressure ratio $P_{2}/P_{1}$ increases, V4 increases and the *effective suction volume decreases*. Mathematically: $$ \eta_{v} = 1 + C - C \left( \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} \right)^{\!1/n} $$ Hence, *higher clearance* and *higher pressure ratio* both reduce volumetric efficiency. To mitigate this, multi-stage compression with inter-cooling is used. --- **Model Solution to Part (b):** **Step 1 — Isentropic efficiency:** $$ \eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_{1}}{h_{2a} - h_{1}} = \dfrac{420 - 390}{430 - 390} = \dfrac{30}{40} = 0.75 $$ $$ \boxed{\eta_{isen} = 75\%} $$ **Step 2 — Actual work per kg:** $$ w_{actual} = h_{2a} - h_{1} = 430 - 390 = 40\;\text{kJ/kg} $$ **Step 3 — Brake power:** $$ \dot{W}_{brake} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \times (h_{2a} - h_{1})}{\eta_{mech}} = \dfrac{(1500/3600) \times 40}{0.90} $$ $$ \dot{W}_{brake} = \dfrac{0.4167 \times 40}{0.90} = \dfrac{16.667}{0.90} = 18.52\;\text{kW} $$ $$ \boxed{\dot{W}_{brake} \approx 18.52\;\text{kW}} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Critical):** > 1. **Confusing polytropic index (n) with isentropic index ($\gamma$)** in volumetric-efficiency derivations — a guaranteed 2-mark penalty. > 2. **Forgetting to convert mass flow rate from kg/h to kg/s** when computing brake power. KTU examiners specifically test this unit-conversion awareness. > 3. **Drawing the P-V diagram incorrectly** — the clearance expansion curve (3 → 4) must lie *to the right* of the suction line; misplacing it loses 2 marks. > 4. **Omitting units** in final numerical answers — KTU mandates SI units with every numerical result. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Notes for Last-Minute KTU Prep:** - ✅ A compressor in VCRC is the *only work-consuming* component. - ✅ Two broad families: **Positive Displacement** (volume-trapping) and **Dynamic** (kinetic-energy conversion). - ✅ Positive displacement: **Reciprocating, Scroll, Screw, Rotary Vane**. - ✅ Dynamic: **Centrifugal** (most common), **Axial** (rare in refrigeration). - ✅ The **clearance volume** is the single biggest cause of volumetric inefficiency in reciprocating compressors. - ✅ Master formula: $\eta_{v} = 1 + C - C(P_{2}/P_{1})^{1/n}$. - ✅ Master formula: $W_{isen} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} P_{1} V_{1} \left[ (P_{2}/P_{1})^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1 \right]$. - ✅ Reciprocating: high pressure ratio per stage, high maintenance (valves), pulsating flow. - ✅ Scroll: continuous compression, no clearance, no valves, very quiet, ideal for window/split AC. - ✅ Screw: medium-to-large capacity, two meshing rotors, common in chillers. - ✅ Centrifugal: very large capacity, low pressure ratio per stage, flow-dependent compression. - ✅ **Isentropic efficiency** uses *$\gamma$*; **Polytropic process** uses *n*. - ✅ Mechanical efficiency accounts for *friction losses*; isentropic efficiency accounts for *thermodynamic irreversibility*. - ✅ For a given mass flow, brake power = (indicated power) / (mechanical efficiency). - ✅ Volumetric efficiency *decreases* with increasing pressure ratio and increasing clearance ratio. - ✅ Always convert kg/h to kg/s before computing power in Watts. - ✅ Always label the P-V diagram with points **1, 2, 3, 4** and indicate both P1 and P2. - ✅ In KTU problems, watch for the trap where students forget to *raise the term to the power 1/n* in volumetric-efficiency calculations.

topics.title: Types of evaporators and condensers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of evaporators and condensers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Types of Evaporators and Condensers

## 1.1 Evaporators — Core Definition and Conceptual Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** An **evaporator** is a heat exchanger installed on the low-pressure (suction) side of a vapor-compression refrigeration system (VCRS) in which the liquid refrigerant **absorbs heat** from the space/substance to be cooled and **evaporates** (boils) at a temperature lower than that of the refrigerated space, thereby producing the desired refrigerating effect.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of an evaporator exactly like the *wick* of a kerosene lamp. The wick is constantly soaked with fuel (liquid refrigerant) and is exposed to the flame (warm room air). As the fuel evaporates, it absorbs heat from its surroundings, cooling the lamp glass. In a refrigeration system, the evaporator *is* that wick: it soaks up heat from the room you want to chill and quietly carries it away as the refrigerant boils off into vapor.

### 1.1.1 Classification of Evaporators
Evaporators are broadly classified in **two ways** as per KTU convention:

**A. Based on the method of refrigerant feed:**
1. **Dry Expansion (DX) Evaporator** — only **part** of the refrigerant evaporates inside; complete vaporization occurs by the end of the coil (quality $x = 1$ at exit).
2. **Flooded Evaporator** — the evaporator shell is **completely filled** with liquid refrigerant; vapor is separated in a low-pressure accumulator/separator above the shell.

**B. Based on construction / heat-transfer geometry:**
1. Bare-tube (plain tube) evaporators
2. Finned-tube evaporators
3. Shell-and-tube evaporators
4. Shell-and-coil evaporators
5. Double-tube (concentric tube) evaporators
6. Baudelot (plate / atmospheric) evaporators
7. Spray-type evaporators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Most 14-mark ESE questions combine these two classification axes. A typical KTU question asks: *"With neat sketches, explain DX and Flooded evaporators. Compare their performance."* — Always draw a **clear schematic with a thermostatic expansion valve (TXV) and suction-line accumulator** to score full marks.

---

## 1.2 Condensers — Core Definition and Conceptual Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A **condenser** is the high-pressure heat-exchanger on the **discharge side** of a VCRS in which the superheated, high-pressure refrigerant vapor **rejects heat** to an external cooling medium (air, water, or a combination), undergoes **desuperheating**, **condensation** (latent heat removal), and is finally **subcooled** to a sub-cooled liquid state before entering the expansion device.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine a pressure cooker that has just finished cooking. The lid is hot, and steam (superheated vapor) is escaping. Now imagine blowing a strong fan across that lid. The fan (cooling medium) carries away the heat, and the steam condenses back to water droplets on the inside of the lid (liquid refrigerant). A condenser does exactly the *opposite* job of an evaporator: it is the place where the refrigerant **gives up** the heat it stole from your room, and dumps it to the outdoors.

### 1.2.1 Classification of Condensers
**A. Based on the cooling medium used:**

1. **Air-Cooled Condensers** — Reject heat to atmospheric air.
   - Natural-convection type
   - Forced-convection (fan-blown) type
   - Fin-and-tube / Plate type
2. **Water-Cooled Condensers** — Reject heat to circulating water.
   - Shell-and-tube (with or without integral sub-cooler)
   - Shell-and-coil
   - Double-tube / Tube-in-tube
3. **Evaporative Condensers** — Combined air + water spray; water evaporates into the air stream carrying away latent heat.
4. **Cascade Condensers** — Used as the condenser of the lower cycle in cascade refrigeration, simultaneously acting as the evaporator of the upper cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Air-cooled condensers are the *workhorse* of domestic refrigerators, window ACs, and split ACs. Water-cooled condensers dominate large industrial plants (ammonia plants, dairy, cold storage) and marine applications. Always quote **typical U-values**: Air-cooled $U \approx 25\text{–}50\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, Water-cooled $U \approx 1000\text{–}6000\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, Evaporative $U \approx 1000\text{–}3000\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis and KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dry Expansion (DX) Evaporator — Operating Logic

In a **DX (Direct Expansion) evaporator**, only **part** of the refrigerant entering the coil is in liquid form; the rest is a saturated mixture. The refrigerant evaporates progressively along the length of the coil, and the **last drop of liquid evaporates exactly at the outlet**.

**Step-by-step logic of operation:**

- A thermostatic expansion valve (TXV) meters a *controlled, small quantity* of liquid refrigerant into the evaporator.
- The refrigerant enters as a low-quality two-phase mixture (typically $x_1 \approx 0.15$ to $0.25$).
- As it flows through the coil, it picks up heat from the surroundings and progressively vaporizes.
- A **distributor** at the inlet splits the refrigerant equally among parallel circuits.
- At the outlet, refrigerant is either **saturated vapor ($x_2 = 1$)** or slightly superheated (1–5 K superheat) to ensure no liquid carry-over to the compressor.
- The TXV senses suction-line superheat and modulates its opening to maintain constant superheat.

**Why DX is preferred:** Compact, light, cheaper, no need for low-pressure receiver; ideal for unit-type refrigeration (domestic fridge, window AC).

**Why DX has drawbacks:** Lower heat-transfer coefficient because most of the coil length carries *low-quality* (mostly liquid) refrigerant where the refrigerant-side $h$ is poor; sensitive to load fluctuations.

## 2.2 Flooded Evaporator — Operating Logic

In a **flooded evaporator**, the shell is kept **completely full of liquid refrigerant** at saturation. Vapor bubbles are generated throughout the bundle and rise to the top, where they leave through a suction-line port above the liquid level.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- A **low-pressure float valve** or **low-side float chamber** maintains a *constant liquid level* inside the shell.
- The expansion valve admits liquid continuously to keep the level steady.
- Tubes are fully submerged — hence the wetted outer surface area is at the **saturated temperature $T_{sat}$** corresponding to the evaporator pressure.
- A **suction accumulator / vapor separator** sits above the liquid level to prevent liquid droplets from being entrained into the compressor suction.
- A **low-side accumulator (receiver)** stores excess liquid refrigerant.

**Why flooded is superior:** Higher refrigerant-side heat-transfer coefficient (entire tube surface is in saturated liquid), wider operating range, tolerant of load swings — but **requires a larger refrigerant charge**, is **heavier**, and needs a separator.

## 2.3 Bare-Tube vs Finned-Tube Evaporator

- **Bare-tube evaporators** use smooth copper tubes. Used where the air-side $h$ is already high (e.g., chilled-water immersion, flooded shell-and-tube).
- **Finned-tube evaporators** attach aluminum fins (plate, spiral, or corrugated) to enhance the *air-side* area. Used where air is the cooled medium and the air-side $h_a$ is the *limiting* resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effectiveness $\varepsilon$ of an evaporator** is computed as:
> $$\varepsilon_{evap} = \frac{T_{a,in} - T_{a,out}}{T_{a,in} - T_{sat}}$$
> A higher $T_{sat}$ (closer to room temperature) means a **higher COP** because the compressor suction pressure is higher, reducing specific work.

## 2.4 Air-Cooled Condenser — Operating Logic

- The hot, superheated refrigerant vapor from the compressor enters the condenser at state 2 (high $P$, high $T$).
- **Stage 1 — Desuperheating:** From state 2 to saturation state 2'. Sensible cooling. Heat rejected = $m \cdot (h_2 - h_{2'}) = m \cdot c_p (T_2 - T_{sat})$.
- **Stage 2 — Condensation:** Latent heat rejected at constant $T_{sat}$ and $P$. From state 2' to state 3 (saturated liquid). Heat rejected = $m \cdot (h_{2'} - h_3)$.
- **Stage 3 — Subcooling:** Sensible cooling below $T_{sat}$. Heat rejected = $m \cdot c_{p,l} (T_{sat} - T_3)$. Subcooling of **3 to 8 K** is normal.

## 2.5 Water-Cooled Condenser — Operating Logic

Water-cooled condensers are nearly always **shell-and-tube** in large plants. The refrigerant flows through the shell (or tubes, depending on design), and cooling water flows counter-currently in tubes (or shell).

- **Counter-flow** arrangement maximises LMTD.
- A typical **water-side $U$** is $1000\text{–}6000\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ (very high) — hence water-cooled condensers are compact.
- A **sub-cooling section** may be built into the lower portion of the shell.
- Cooling water **scale** formation is a major maintenance issue; periodic cleaning is mandatory.

## 2.6 Evaporative Condenser — Operating Logic

- A combination of the air-cooled and water-cooled principle.
- Warm refrigerant condenses inside finned tubes while **water is sprayed** over the tubes from above.
- A fan draws air upward (or downward) across the tubes.
- Part of the spray water **evaporates** into the air stream; the latent heat of this evaporation is what carries the bulk of the heat away.
- Most efficient (highest $U$); lowest condensing $T$ for a given ambient.
- Make-up water is required; water treatment is essential.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in KTU PEMET751 (Module 3).** Memorise units and conditions.

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Evaporator capacity (refrigerating effect) | $RE = m_r (h_1 - h_4)$ | kW | $h_1$ = inlet enthalpy to compressor, $h_4$ = enthalpy after TXV |
| Refrigerant mass flow rate | $m_r = RE / (h_1 - h_4)$ | kg/s | From steady-flow energy balance |
| Overall heat-transfer equation | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ | kW | Holds for *both* evaporator and condenser |
| LMTD for counter-flow condenser | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | K | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ |
| Effectiveness (evaporator) | $\varepsilon_{evap} = \dfrac{T_{a,in} - T_{a,out}}{T_{a,in} - T_{sat}}$ | — | Between 0 and 1 |
| Effectiveness (condenser) | $\varepsilon_{cond} = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | — | Between 0 and 1 |
| Approach temperature (condenser) | $\text{Approach} = T_{c,out} - T_{wb}$ | K | Smaller value $\Rightarrow$ better condenser |
| Heat rejected at condenser | $Q_{cond} = m_r (h_2 - h_3)$ | kW | $h_2$: superheated inlet; $h_3$: sub-cooled liquid outlet |
| Sub-cooling | $\Delta T_{sub} = T_{sat} - T_3$ | K | Typically 3 to 8 K |
| Superheat (evaporator outlet) | $\Delta T_{sh} = T_1 - T_{sat}$ | K | Typically 4 to 10 K |
| Log mean enthalpy difference (evap.) | $\text{LMED} = \dfrac{\Delta h_1 - \Delta h_2}{\ln(\Delta h_1/\Delta h_2)}$ | kJ/kg | For air-cooled coil with dehumidification |
| Specific heat-transfer area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}}$ | m$^2$ | Design equation |
| Cooling-water mass flow (condenser) | $m_w = Q_{cond}/[c_{pw}(T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})]$ | kg/s | $c_{pw} \approx 4.186\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ |
| Air mass flow (air-cooled cond.) | $m_a = Q_{cond}/[c_{pa}(T_{a,out} - T_{a,in})]$ | kg/s | $c_{pa} \approx 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ |
| Evaporative condenser (Make-up water) | $m_{mu} = Q_{cond}/(h_{fg,w})$ | kg/s | Approx., assumes all heat carried by evaporation |

> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These formulas are the daily tools of a refrigeration *design engineer* working for Voltas, Blue Star, Daikin, or Carrier. Selecting the wrong heat exchanger type can result in a 30–40 % oversize in condenser area, escalating cost and refrigerant charge.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples, and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Evaporators (KTU Exam Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this table in a KTU 14-mark answer:** Reproduce this matrix verbatim in the exam — it covers ~6 to 8 marks by itself, and you can elaborate each row with 2–3 lines for the rest.

| Feature | Dry Expansion (DX) | Flooded |
|---|---|---|
| Liquid level control | Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) | Low-pressure float valve |
| Liquid level inside shell | Partial (low quality at inlet) | Full / submerged |
| Quality at exit $x_2$ | Exactly 1.0 (saturated vapor) | $\approx 1.0$ (slightly superheated) |
| Heat-transfer coefficient | Lower | Higher (~30–60 % greater) |
| Susceptibility to liquid slugging | High (if TXV fails) | Low (separator protects) |
| Refrigerant charge required | Low | High (2–3 × of DX) |
| Steady-state response to load | Slower | Faster |
| Size & weight | Compact, light | Bulky, heavy |
| Operating cost | Lower (less charge) | Slightly higher |
| Typical applications | Domestic fridges, window AC, split AC | Cold storage, industrial chillers, ice plants |
| Need for low-side accumulator | Yes (small) | Yes (large) |

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Condensers

| Feature | Air-Cooled | Water-Cooled (Shell & Tube) | Evaporative |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cooling medium | Atmospheric air | Circulating water | Air + spray water |
| Heat-transfer coefficient $U$ | Low (25–50 W/m²K) | High (1000–6000 W/m²K) | Very high (1000–3000 W/m²K) |
| Condensing temperature | High (10–15 K above $T_{db}$) | Low (5–10 K above $T_{w,out}$) | Lowest (3–5 K above $T_{wb}$) |
| Size / Footprint | Large | Smallest | Compact |
| Water consumption | Zero | High (recirculating) | Moderate (make-up water) |
| Power consumption | High (fan power) | Medium (pump power) | Medium (fan + pump) |
| Maintenance | Low | High (scale, corrosion) | High (water treatment) |
| Capital cost | Low | High | Highest |
| Typical applications | Domestic fridge, window/split AC | Large chillers, cold storage | Ice plants, breweries, large cold stores |
| Susceptibility to ambient $T$ | High (suffers in summer) | Low | Low |

## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical — Evaporator Sizing)

> **Problem (Module 3 typical 7-mark sub-question):**
> A refrigeration system uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The compressor handles **$0.06\ \text{kg/s}$** of refrigerant. Enthalpy at compressor inlet $h_1 = 247.0\ \text{kJ/kg}$ and enthalpy after the TXV $h_4 = 113.0\ \text{kJ/kg}$. The evaporator has an overall heat-transfer coefficient **$U = 35\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$** and the LMTD between air and refrigerant is **$\Delta T_{lm} = 10\ \text{K}$**. Calculate:
> (a) The refrigerating effect per kg of refrigerant.
> (b) The total refrigerating capacity (TR).
> (c) The required heat-transfer surface area.

### 3.3.1 Solution — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Refrigerating effect per kg**

$$
\text{RE}_{\text{specific}} = h_1 - h_4
$$

$$
\text{RE}_{\text{specific}} = 247.0 - 113.0
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{RE}_{\text{specific}} = 134.0\ \text{kJ/kg}}
$$

**Step 2: Total refrigerating capacity**

$$
RE_{total} = m_r \cdot \text{RE}_{\text{specific}}
$$

$$
RE_{total} = 0.06 \cdot 134.0
$$

$$
RE_{total} = 8.04\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to Tonnes of Refrigeration**

We know that $1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$.

$$
RE_{total} = \dfrac{8.04}{3.517}
$$

$$
\boxed{RE_{total} \approx 2.286\ \text{TR}}
$$

**Step 4: Required heat-transfer area**

Using $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$,

$$
A = \dfrac{RE_{total}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{8.04 \times 10^{3}}{35 \times 10}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{8040}{350}
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 22.97\ \text{m}^2}
$$

### 3.3.2 Real-World Engineering Note

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An evaporator of $23\ \text{m}^2$ surface area is comfortably fitted inside a standard $1.5\ \text{TR}$ window AC unit (which typically has 2 to 3 rows of copper tubes with aluminium fins). Students often lose marks by **forgetting unit conversions** — $1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$, not $3.5\ \text{kW}$. Always write the unit conversion step.

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical — Water-Cooled Condenser)

> **Problem:**
> A water-cooled shell-and-tube condenser rejects **$50\ \text{kW}$** of heat. Cooling water enters at **$28\,^\circ\text{C}$** and leaves at **$36\,^\circ\text{C}$**. The refrigerant condenses at **$T_{sat} = 45\,^\circ\text{C}$** (R-134a). Assume counter-flow.
> (a) Calculate the cooling-water mass flow rate.
> (b) Calculate LMTD.
> (c) Determine the required heat-transfer area if $U = 2000\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.

### 3.4.1 Solution

**Step 1: Cooling-water mass flow rate**

$$
m_w = \dfrac{Q_{cond}}{c_{pw} \cdot (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})}
$$

$$
m_w = \dfrac{50}{4.186 \times (36 - 28)}
$$

$$
m_w = \dfrac{50}{4.186 \times 8}
$$

$$
m_w = \dfrac{50}{33.488}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_w \approx 1.493\ \text{kg/s} \approx 5.37\ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}}
$$

**Step 2: LMTD (counter-flow)**

Hot end: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 45 - 36 = 9\,^\circ\text{C}$

Cold end: $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 45 - 28 = 17\,^\circ\text{C}$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{9 - 17}{\ln(9/17)}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{-8}{\ln(0.5294)}
$$

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{-8}{-0.6360}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{LMTD} \approx 12.58\,^\circ\text{C}}
$$

**Step 3: Heat-transfer area**

$$
A = \dfrac{Q_{cond}}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{50\,000}{2000 \times 12.58}
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 1.987\ \text{m}^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how **compact** the water-cooled condenser is (only ~2 m²) compared with an air-cooled one which would require roughly $50\text{–}100\ \text{m}^2$ for the same duty. This explains why large industrial plants always prefer water-cooled condensers despite their higher maintenance cost.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 DX vs Flooded Evaporator — Functional Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressor Discharge] --> B[Condenser]
    B --> C[Receiver]
    C --> D[Thermostatic Expansion Valve]
    D --> E[Distributor]
    E --> F1[DX Coil Circuit 1]
    E --> F2[DX Coil Circuit 2]
    E --> F3[DX Coil Circuit 3]
    F1 --> G[Common Suction Line]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Accumulator]
    H --> I[Compressor Suction]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The TXV meters refrigerant → distributor splits into parallel circuits → partial evaporation → suction line → accumulator traps any residual liquid → compressor. This is the **standard DX layout** used in every window/split AC.

## 4.2 Flooded Evaporator — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressor Discharge] --> B[Condenser]
    B --> C[Receiver]
    C --> D[High-Pressure Liquid Line]
    D --> E[Low-Pressure Float Valve]
    E --> F[Flooded Shell with Liquid Pool]
    F --> G[Vapor Space Above Liquid]
    G --> H[Suction Line Separator]
    H --> I[Compressor Suction]
    F -.liquid return via equalizer.-> E
```

**Reading the diagram:** The float valve maintains a *constant liquid level* in the shell. Tubes are fully submerged. Vapor generated in the shell rises through the *vapor space* and exits to the suction separator, which returns any entrained droplets back via the float equalizer line.

## 4.3 Air-Cooled Condenser — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Discharge from Compressor] --> B[Desuperheater Zone]
    B --> C[Condensing Zone]
    C --> D[Subcooling Zone]
    D --> E[Liquid Receiver]
    F[Fan Bank] -.forced air.-> B
    F -.forced air.-> C
    F -.forced air.-> D
    G[Ambient Air Inlet] --> F
    F --> H[Hot Air Discharge to Atmosphere]
```

## 4.4 Water-Cooled Shell-and-Tube Condenser — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hot Discharge Vapor from Compressor] --> B[Inlet Nozzle to Shell]
    B --> C[Shell Side: Vapor Desuperheats, Condenses, Subcools]
    C --> D[Liquid Outlet Nozzle to Receiver]
    E[Cooling Water Inlet] --> F[Tube Sheet Water Box]
    F --> G[Tube Bundle Counter-Flow]
    G --> H[Outlet Water Box]
    H --> I[Hot Cooling Water to Cooling Tower]
```

## 4.5 Evaporative Condenser — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hot Discharge Vapor] --> B[Finned Tube Bundle]
    B --> C[Condensed Liquid to Receiver]
    D[Spray Pump] --> E[Water Spray Nozzles]
    E --> B
    F[Fan] --> G[Air Inlet from Atmosphere]
    G --> B
    B --> H[Moist Warm Air Discharge]
    I[Make-up Water] --> D
    J[Blow-down Drain] --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:** Water is sprayed over the tubes from the top, evaporates partially into the induced air stream, and carries away the bulk of the condenser heat as *latent heat of vaporization* of the spray water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tip for KTU 14-mark answers:** Always include **three labeled regions** — Desuperheater, Condenser, Subcooler — on any condenser schematic. Examiners explicitly allocate 3 marks for the labeling and 2 marks for indicating the direction of refrigerant and cooling-medium flow.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, PEMET751]**
>
> **Q1. Differentiate between a Dry Expansion (DX) and a Flooded evaporator.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (for 3 marks):**

| S. No. | Dry Expansion (DX) | Flooded |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refrigerant enters as a low-quality two-phase mixture | Shell is completely filled with liquid refrigerant |
| 2 | Expansion controlled by Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) | Expansion controlled by Low-Pressure Float Valve |
| 3 | Quality at exit is exactly 1 (saturated vapor) | Slightly superheated vapor exits |
| 4 | Smaller refrigerant charge required | Larger refrigerant charge required (typically 2–3 × of DX) |
| 5 | Lower refrigerant-side heat-transfer coefficient | Higher refrigerant-side heat-transfer coefficient |

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
>
> **Q2. List any three types of condensers used in a vapor-compression refrigeration system.** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Air-cooled condenser** — heat rejected to atmospheric air (e.g., domestic refrigerator coil).
2. **Water-cooled condenser** — heat rejected to circulating water in a shell-and-tube arrangement (e.g., large industrial chiller).
3. **Evaporative condenser** — heat rejected by combined air stream + water spray evaporation (e.g., ammonia ice plants).

> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently write *only one type and skip the other two* — make sure to list **three distinct types**, not variations of the same type.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — *(14 Marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **Flooded Shell-and-Tube evaporator**. List its advantages and disadvantages over a DX evaporator. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A refrigerant R-134a enters an evaporator at $h_4 = 113\ \text{kJ/kg}$ and leaves at $h_1 = 247\ \text{kJ/kg}$. The refrigerant flow rate is $0.08\ \text{kg/s}$. The LMTD between air and refrigerant is $9\ \text{K}$. If $U = 40\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, determine (i) refrigerating effect per kg, (ii) total capacity in TR, (iii) heat-transfer area. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A **horizontal cylindrical shell** fitted with a bundle of straight tubes (typically copper or cupro-nickel) running inside.
- Tubes are supported by **tube sheets** at both ends; the refrigerant surrounds the tubes on the shell side (or vice-versa).
- A **low-side float chamber** at one end, connected to the shell via an equalizer line, maintains a constant liquid level.
- **Vapor separator** at the top of the shell captures any entrained liquid droplets before they enter the suction line.
- **Hand-hole / man-hole** for cleaning and inspection.

**Working (3 marks):**
- High-pressure liquid refrigerant from the receiver passes through the float valve into the shell.
- The float valve opens/closes to keep the shell flooded to a preset level.
- Refrigerant **boils on the outer surface of the tubes** (in which chilled water or brine may be flowing, depending on application).
- Vapor bubbles rise through the liquid pool, collect in the upper vapor space, and leave through the top suction outlet.
- The **suction-line accumulator** prevents any carry-over of liquid into the compressor.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
- Higher refrigerant-side $h$ → compact heat exchanger.
- Tolerant of load fluctuations.
- No TXV required — simpler control.
- Uniform tube-wall temperature.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
- High refrigerant charge (costly for expensive refrigerants).
- Heavier and bulkier.
- Risk of liquid carry-over during sudden load changes.

> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often *forget to label the equalizer line* in their schematic. Always label: (i) float chamber, (ii) equalizer line, (iii) vapor separator, and (iv) suction outlet. Each label carries ~0.5 mark.

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:** $h_4 = 113\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $h_1 = 247\ \text{kJ/kg}$, $m_r = 0.08\ \text{kg/s}$, LMTD $= 9\ \text{K}$, $U = 40\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.

**(i) Refrigerating effect per kg:**

$$
\text{RE}_{\text{specific}} = h_1 - h_4 = 247 - 113 = 134\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**(ii) Total capacity:**

$$
RE_{total} = m_r \cdot \text{RE}_{\text{specific}} = 0.08 \times 134 = 10.72\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
RE_{total}\ (\text{in TR}) = \dfrac{10.72}{3.517} \approx 3.048\ \text{TR}
$$

**(iii) Heat-transfer area:**

$$
A = \dfrac{RE_{total}}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{10.72 \times 10^{3}}{40 \times 9} = \dfrac{10720}{360} \approx 29.78\ \text{m}^2
$$

> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** $1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$, **not** $3.5\ \text{kW}$. Using $3.5$ costs you ~1 mark on the final answer.

---

### Question B — *(14 Marks, Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **Water-Cooled Shell-and-Tube Condenser**. Also, explain the concept of **"approach temperature"** with reference to the cooling tower water outlet. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A shell-and-tube condenser rejects $75\ \text{kW}$ of heat. Cooling water enters at $30\,^\circ\text{C}$ and leaves at $38\,^\circ\text{C}$. The refrigerant condenses at $46\,^\circ\text{C}$ (saturated temperature). Assume counter-flow. Calculate: (i) cooling water flow rate in $\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$, (ii) LMTD, (iii) required heat-transfer area if $U = 2500\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Construction (2.5 marks):**
- Cylindrical steel **shell** with end covers and tube sheets.
- Tubes (typically cupro-nickel for corrosion resistance) fixed into tube sheets.
- **Water box / channel** at each end for cooling water inlet and outlet.
- **Baffles** inside the shell direct refrigerant flow across the tube bundle.
- **Safety relief valve**, vent, and drain plugs.
- An **integral subcooling section** may be provided at the lower portion.

**Working (2.5 marks):**
- Hot, superheated refrigerant vapor enters the shell at the top.
- It first **desuperheats** (sensible cooling) by giving up heat to the cooler tubes.
- Then it **condenses** at constant $T_{sat}$ and $P$, releasing latent heat.
- Finally, the saturated liquid **subcools** as it moves down.
- Cooling water flows **counter-currently** inside the tubes — entering at the bottom water box and exiting at the top.
- Counter-flow maximizes LMTD.

**Approach Temperature (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Approach temperature is the difference between the **cooling-water outlet temperature** $T_{w,out}$ and the **entering air wet-bulb temperature** $T_{wb}$ in the cooling tower.
- **Formula:** $\text{Approach} = T_{w,out} - T_{wb}$
- A small approach (e.g., 4–5 K) indicates an efficient cooling tower (tower must be large).
- A larger approach (e.g., 8–10 K) indicates a smaller, less expensive tower.

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Given:** $Q_{cond} = 75\ \text{kW}$, $T_{w,in} = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{w,out} = 38\,^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{sat} = 46\,^\circ\text{C}$ (condensing temperature).

**(i) Cooling water flow rate:**

$$
m_w = \dfrac{Q_{cond}}{c_{pw}(T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})} = \dfrac{75}{4.186 \times 8} = \dfrac{75}{33.488} \approx 2.240\ \text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\text{Volumetric flow rate} = \dfrac{m_w}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{2.240}{1000} = 2.240 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 8.06\ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**(ii) LMTD (counter-flow):**

$\Delta T_1 = 46 - 38 = 8\,^\circ\text{C}$ (hot end)
$\Delta T_2 = 46 - 30 = 16\,^\circ\text{C}$ (cold end)

$$
\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{8 - 16}{\ln(8/16)} = \dfrac{-8}{\ln(0.5)} = \dfrac{-8}{-0.6931} \approx 11.54\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

**(iii) Heat-transfer area:**

$$
A = \dfrac{Q_{cond}}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \dfrac{75\,000}{2500 \times 11.54} = \dfrac{75\,000}{28\,850} \approx 2.60\ \text{m}^2
$$

> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly compute $\ln(0.5)$ as **positive** because they forget the negative sign in the numerator. Always check that LMTD is **positive and greater than the smaller $\Delta T$**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final-Day Revision — Pin this on your wall.**

- [x] **Evaporator** = low-pressure side; absorbs heat; refrigerant boils.
- [x] **Condenser** = high-pressure side; rejects heat; refrigerant condenses.
- [x] **DX** → TXV; partial liquid; quality $x = 1$ at exit.
- [x] **Flooded** → float valve; full liquid pool; higher $U$.
- [x] **Air-cooled** → low $U$, simple, used in domestic/small commercial.
- [x] **Water-cooled** → high $U$, compact, used in industrial chillers.
- [x] **Evaporative** → highest efficiency, lowest $T_{cond}$, highest capex.
- [x] **$1\ \text{TR} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$** — never forget.
- [x] **$U_{\text{air-cooled}} \ll U_{\text{water-cooled}} \approx U_{\text{evaporative}}$**.
- [x] **LMTD** for counter-flow = $\dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$.
- [x] **Approach** of cooling tower = $T_{w,out} - T_{wb}$.
- [x] **Range** of cooling tower = $T_{w,out} - T_{w,in}$.
- [x] **Superheat** at compressor inlet = 4–10 K (protects compressor).
- [x] **Subcooling** at condenser outlet = 3–8 K (improves COP).
- [x] **Baudelot / plate evaporators** — common in dairy cold-chain for milk chilling.
- [x] **Shell-and-coil condenser** — small-to-medium plants with clean cooling water.
- [x] **Cascade condenser** — interfaces two different refrigerants in a cascade cycle.
- [x] Always sketch with **labeled directions of flow** for full marks in 14-mark questions.

> **Last-Line Mantra for KTU PEMET751:**
> *"Define → Classify → Sketch → Compare → Numerically Verify."*
> Follow this five-step template for every Module 3 question, and you will comfortably cross **70 % marks**.
