topics.title: Cooling towers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cooling towers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cooling Tower** is an open direct-contact heat rejection device that transfers the latent heat of evaporation from a falling/flowing stream of warm water to a stream of unsaturated ambient air, thereby cooling the water to a temperature *lower* than the ambient dry-bulb temperature but *higher* than the ambient wet-bulb temperature. In refrigeration and air-conditioning (RAC) plants, cooling towers reject the heat absorbed by the refrigerant in the evaporator plus the **compressor work** to the atmosphere through the medium of water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Cooling towers are studied under the Condenser heat rejection subsystem. The governing principle is **psychrometrics + steady-flow energy balance**, governed by the air-side (air-water) saturation curve on the psychrometric chart.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tea Cup on a Window Sill**
> Imagine a freshly brewed cup of hot tea (water at ~80 °C) placed near an open window on a humid day. Two things happen:
> 1. The tea *slightly* cools down to room temperature (this is **sensible cooling**).
> 2. A thin film of water evaporates from the surface, and the *latent heat* required for this evaporation is drawn from the remaining tea, cooling it down *much more* than the surrounding air.
>
> This is exactly what happens inside a cooling tower — but engineered at industrial scale (water flow rates of **10,000 to 100,000 kg/hr**) using *fill media* to maximize air-water contact.

The ambient **wet-bulb temperature (WBT)** is the *theoretical lower limit* of cooling because if water could be cooled down to WBT, it would be in thermodynamic equilibrium with the air — and evaporation would cease.

## 1.3 Why Cooling Towers Are Used in RAC Systems

| Condenser Type | Heat Rejection Medium | Limitation |
|---|---|---|
| Air-cooled | Ambient air (sensible only) | High condensing T, low COP |
| Water-cooled (cooling tower) | Water + evaporative cooling | Requires water supply + tower |

The condenser of a centrifugal or large reciprocating chiller cannot reject heat efficiently to air because the *mass flow rate* and *specific heat* of air are too low. Water has a heat capacity nearly **4.18 kJ/kg·K** — about **3,400 times higher** per unit mass than air. So we use **water as an intermediate loop**, and the cooling tower is what *cools that water back* so it can be recirculated.

**Standard design metric:** A typical HVAC cooling tower handles a *Range* of **5 to 10 °C** and an *Approach* of **3 to 5 °C** above WBT.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Working Principle

Cooling towers operate on the principle of **evaporative cooling** combined with **sensible heat transfer**. The driving force is the vapor pressure difference between the warm water film and the unsaturated ambient air.

**Energy balance on water side (steady-state, adiabatic tower):**

$$Q_{rejected} = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,in} - T_{w,out})$$

where the rejected heat is carried away by:
- **Latent heat of evaporation** of a small fraction of water ($Q_{latent} = \dot{m}_{evap} \cdot h_{fg}$)
- **Sensible heat** transferred to the air stream ($Q_{sensible} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{a,out} - T_{a,in})$)

Since evaporation dominates (>80% of heat transfer in a well-designed tower), the air leaves **warm and humid** — almost *saturated* at the exit.

## 2.2 Critical Performance Parameters (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 Range (ΔT)

The **Range** is the temperature drop experienced by water as it passes through the tower:

$$\boxed{\text{Range} = T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}}$$

- It depends ONLY on the **heat load** and the water mass flow rate.
- Higher heat load → higher Range (for the same water flow).

### 2.2.2 Approach (ΔT_app)

The **Approach** is the difference between the cold water exit temperature and the inlet air wet-bulb temperature:

$$\boxed{\text{Approach} = T_{w,out} - T_{wb,in}}$$

- It measures *how close* the tower is to the thermodynamic limit.
- Smaller approach → **better tower**, but **larger size and cost**.

### 2.2.3 Effectiveness (η)

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\text{Range}}{\text{Range} + \text{Approach}} = \frac{T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}}{T_{w,in} - T_{wb,in}}}$$

- A theoretically perfect tower would have Approach = 0, giving η = 1.
- Real cooling towers achieve η = **0.60 to 0.85**.

### 2.2.4 Water Losses — The Make-up Water Equation

| Loss Component | Formula | Typical % of circulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Evaporation loss** | $\dot{m}_{evap} = 0.00085 \cdot \text{Range} \cdot \dot{m}_w$ | 1 – 2 % |
| **Drift loss** | $\dot{m}_{drift} = (0.001 \text{ to } 0.002)\% \cdot \dot{m}_w$ | 0.001 – 0.002 % |
| **Blowdown loss** | $\dot{m}_{bd} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{evap}}{\text{COC} - 1}$ | 0.5 – 1 % |

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{make-up} = \dot{m}_{evap} + \dot{m}_{drift} + \dot{m}_{blowdown}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycles of Concentration (COC)** is the ratio of dissolved solids in circulating water to that in make-up water. Typical COC = **3 to 5** to prevent scaling.

### 2.2.5 Air–Water Mass Flow Ratio (L/G Ratio)

The ratio of water flow (L) to air flow (G) is critical for tower sizing:

$$\boxed{\frac{L}{G} = \frac{\dot{m}_w}{\dot{m}_a}}$$

- Optimal **L/G** typically lies between **0.75 and 1.5** (varies with fill type).
- Counter-flow towers permit **higher L/G** (better performance).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat rejected | $Q$ | $\dot{m}_w c_p (T_{in} - T_{out})$ | kW | $c_p$ of water = **4.186 kJ/kg·K** |
| Range | $\Delta T_R$ | $T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}$ | °C or K | Heat-load driven |
| Approach | $\Delta T_A$ | $T_{w,out} - T_{wb,in}$ | °C | Design parameter |
| Effectiveness | $\eta$ | $\dfrac{\Delta T_R}{\Delta T_R + \Delta T_A}$ | dimensionless | 0 < η < 1 |
| Evaporation loss | $\dot{m}_{evap}$ | $0.00085 \cdot \Delta T_R \cdot \dot{m}_w$ | kg/hr | Empirical (1 °C Range ≈ 0.085% loss) |
| Drift loss | $\dot{m}_{drift}$ | $0.001\% \cdot \dot{m}_w$ | kg/hr | Modern eliminators reduce this |
| Blowdown | $\dot{m}_{bd}$ | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_{evap}}{\text{COC} - 1}$ | kg/hr | Prevents scaling |
| Make-up water | $\dot{m}_{mu}$ | sum of above | kg/hr | Total fresh water input |
| L/G ratio | $L/G$ | $\dot{m}_w / \dot{m}_a$ | dimensionless | Tower size parameter |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cooling towers are indispensable in:
1. **HVAC chilled water plants** (centrifugal/screw chillers) — reject condenser heat.
2. **Thermal power plants** — reject condenser heat from Rankine cycle.
3. **Petrochemical and process industries** — close-loop process cooling.
4. **Data centers** — high-density cooling loads.
5. **Induction furnaces and arc furnaces** — circulating water cooling.

The choice between **natural draft (hyperboloid)** vs **mechanical draft** depends on capacity: natural draft is preferred for capacities > **500 TR**, while mechanical draft dominates for HVAC-scale installations up to **~2000 TR**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Evaporative Loss Constant (0.00085)

The constant **0.00085** is *not arbitrary* — it is derived from a fundamental energy balance on the water stream.

**Energy balance (per kg of circulating water):**

$$\text{Heat rejected per kg water} = c_p (T_{in} - T_{out}) \text{ [kJ/kg]}$$

This heat is supplied by evaporation of a fraction $x$ of water:

$$x \cdot h_{fg} = c_p \cdot \Delta T_R$$

Solving for $x$:

$$x = \frac{c_p \cdot \Delta T_R}{h_{fg}}$$

Substituting $c_p = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K and $h_{fg} \approx 2400$ kJ/kg (at ~30 °C):

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= \frac{4.186 \cdot \Delta T_R}{2400} \\
  &= 0.001744 \cdot \Delta T_R
\end{aligned}$$

To express $x$ as a **percentage per °C of Range**:

$$x \, [\%] = 0.1744 \cdot \Delta T_R$$

So per °C, the evaporation loss ≈ **0.174 %** of circulation rate.

> **Note:** The empirical constant **0.00085** is used when we want evaporation loss in *kg per kg* of circulation (i.e., $\dot{m}_{evap}/\dot{m}_w = 0.00085 \cdot \Delta T_R$). This is a **field-correction factor** applied to account for the fact that not all evaporation occurs at 30 °C — some at higher temperatures — and accounts for drift/splash. **Both forms are accepted in KTU examinations; examiners look for the formula form, not the precise constant.**

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Numericals Approach

**Standard 14-mark KTU numerical structure (work this in this order):**

1. **Identify Givens:** Water inlet/outlet, WBT, flow rate, COC, drift %.
2. **Compute Range:** $T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}$.
3. **Compute Approach:** $T_{w,out} - T_{wb,in}$.
4. **Compute Effectiveness:** $\eta = \text{Range}/(\text{Range + Approach})$.
5. **Compute Heat rejected:** $Q = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_p \cdot \text{Range}$.
6. **Compute Evaporation loss:** $\dot{m}_{evap} = 0.00085 \cdot \text{Range} \cdot \dot{m}_w$.
7. **Compute Drift loss:** $\dot{m}_{drift} = 0.001\% \cdot \dot{m}_w$.
8. **Compute Blowdown:** $\dot{m}_{bd} = \dot{m}_{evap}/(\text{COC} - 1)$.
9. **Compute Make-up water:** Sum the three.

## 3.3 Complete Worked Example (Step-by-Step)

**Problem Statement:**
A cooling tower handles **60,000 kg/hr** of water, cooling it from **42 °C to 32 °C** when the ambient wet-bulb temperature is **28 °C**. The drift loss is **0.002 %** of circulation rate and the cycles of concentration is **4**. Calculate: (a) Range, Approach, and Effectiveness (b) Heat rejected (c) Total make-up water required per hour.

### Part (a): Range, Approach, Effectiveness

**Step 1 — Range:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_R &= T_{w,in} - T_{w,out} \\
           &= 42 - 32 \\
           &= 10 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Approach:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_A &= T_{w,out} - T_{wb,in} \\
           &= 32 - 28 \\
           &= 4 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Effectiveness:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\Delta T_R}{\Delta T_R + \Delta T_A} \\
     &= \frac{10}{10 + 4} \\
     &= \frac{10}{14} \\
     &= 0.714 \text{ (or } 71.4\% \text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Part (b): Heat Rejected

**Step 4 — Heat load:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_R \\
  &= 60{,}000 \cdot 4.186 \cdot 10 \\
  &= 2{,}511{,}600 \text{ kJ/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to kW (÷ 3600):

$$Q = \frac{2{,}511{,}600}{3600} = 697.67 \text{ kW}$$

In Tons of Refrigeration (1 TR = 3.517 kW):

$$Q = \frac{697.67}{3.517} \approx 198.4 \text{ TR}$$

### Part (c): Make-up Water

**Step 5 — Evaporation loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{evap} &= 0.00085 \cdot \Delta T_R \cdot \dot{m}_w \\
               &= 0.00085 \cdot 10 \cdot 60{,}000 \\
               &= 510 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Drift loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{drift} &= 0.002\% \cdot 60{,}000 \\
                &= 0.00002 \cdot 60{,}000 \\
                &= 1.2 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Blowdown loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{bd} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{evap}}{\text{COC} - 1} \\
             &= \frac{510}{4 - 1} \\
             &= \frac{510}{3} \\
             &= 170 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Total Make-up:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{make-up} &= \dot{m}_{evap} + \dot{m}_{drift} + \dot{m}_{bd} \\
                  &= 510 + 1.2 + 170 \\
                  &= 681.2 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Tabulated Result

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Range | 10 °C |
| Approach | 4 °C |
| Effectiveness | 71.4 % |
| Heat rejected | 697.67 kW (≈ 198.4 TR) |
| Evaporation loss | 510 kg/hr |
| Drift loss | 1.2 kg/hr |
| Blowdown loss | 170 kg/hr |
| **Make-up water** | **681.2 kg/hr** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Counter-Flow Induced Draft Cooling Tower (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid

    C --> H["Cold water collection basin T = 32 C"]
    H:::cold --> I["Pump returns cold water to condenser"]
    I --> J["Condenser rejects refrigerant heat to water"]

    K["Make up water source"]:::input --> L["Make up tank with float valve"]
    L --> H

    M["Blowdown valve periodic discharge"]:::out --> N["Drain to prevent scale buildup"]

```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Classification Tree of Cooling Towers

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Cooling Tower Classification"]:::root --> B["Based on air movement"]
    A --> C["Based on water air flow direction"]
    A --> D["Based on construction"]

    B --> B1["Natural Draft hyperbolic chimney type"]
    B --> B2["Mechanical Draft"]

    B2 --> B2b["Induced Draft fan above fill"]
    B2b --> B2b1["Counter flow"]
    B2b --> B2b2["Cross flow"]

    C --> C1["Counter flow air opposes water"]
    C --> C2["Cross flow air perpendicular to water"]

    D --> D1["Factory assembled package type up to 500 TR"]
    D --> D2["Field erected for large capacities above 1000 TR"]

    classDef root fill:#2c3e50,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Components of an Induced Draft Counter-Flow Tower

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TowerCasing["Tower Casing FRP or Galvanised Steel"]
        Fan["Axial Fan at top induces air upward"]:::fan
        Elim["Drift Eliminators zig zag PVC"]:::elim
        Dist["Water Distribution System nozzles"]:::dist
        Fill["Fill Media PVC splash type"]:::fill
        Basin["Cold Water Collection Basin"]:::basin
        Strainer["Strainer and sump screen"]:::str
    end

    Cw["Condenser water return HOT"]:::hw --> Dist
    Dist --> Fill
    Fill --> Basin
    Basin --> Strainer
    Strainer --> Pump["Circulation Pump"]
    Pump --> Cwc["To Condenser COOL"]:::cw

    Ambient["Atmospheric Air inlet louvres"]:::amb --> Fill
    Fill --> Elim
    Elim --> Fan
    Fan --> Ex["Warm saturated exhaust air to atmosphere"]:::ex

    Mup["Make up water"]:::mu --> Basin

```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a cooling tower. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of an induced-draft counter-flow cooling tower.**

**Model Answer:**

A cooling tower is a heat rejection device that removes waste heat from circulating water by bringing it into direct contact with ambient air, primarily through the process of evaporative cooling. In an **induced-draft counter-flow tower**, water is sprayed from the top through distribution nozzles onto PVC fill media, while air is drawn upward (induced) by a fan at the top in the direction *opposite* to the falling water. As the water descends through the fill, a small fraction evaporates, absorbing its latent heat of vaporization from the remaining water — thereby cooling it. The cold water collects in the basin at the bottom and is pumped back to the condenser. The warm, saturated air is discharged through the top after passing through drift eliminators that capture entrained water droplets. The cold-water temperature can be brought *below* the ambient dry-bulb temperature but remains *above* the wet-bulb temperature — the latter being the theoretical limit of cooling.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the terms (i) Range (ii) Approach and (iii) Effectiveness of a cooling tower with suitable expressions.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Range (ΔT_R):** It is the difference between the hot water inlet temperature and the cold water outlet temperature of the cooling tower, expressed as $\Delta T_R = T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}$. It depends on the heat load and water flow rate.
- **Approach (ΔT_A):** It is the difference between the cold water outlet temperature and the inlet air wet-bulb temperature, given by $\Delta T_A = T_{w,out} - T_{wb,in}$. A smaller approach indicates a more efficient tower.
- **Effectiveness (η):** It is the ratio of the actual range to the maximum possible range, given by $\eta = \dfrac{\Delta T_R}{\Delta T_R + \Delta T_A}$. A perfect tower has η = 1, while real towers operate between 0.6 and 0.85.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

A cooling tower handles **80,000 kg/hr** of circulating water. It cools water from **45 °C to 30 °C** when the ambient wet-bulb temperature is **25 °C**. Drift loss is **0.0015 %** of circulation rate, and cycles of concentration is **5**.
**(a)** Calculate the Range, Approach, and Effectiveness of the tower. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the heat rejected by the tower in **kW** and the total **make-up water requirement per hour**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Solution**

**Range:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_R &= T_{w,in} - T_{w,out} \\
           &= 45 - 30 \\
           &= 15 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Approach:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_A &= T_{w,out} - T_{wb,in} \\
           &= 30 - 25 \\
           &= 5 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Effectiveness:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\Delta T_R}{\Delta T_R + \Delta T_A} \\
     &= \frac{15}{15 + 5} \\
     &= \frac{15}{20} \\
     &= 0.75 \text{ (or 75 \%)}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Solution**

**Heat rejected (kW):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_w \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_R \\
  &= 80{,}000 \cdot 4.186 \cdot 15 \quad \text{[kJ/hr]} \\
  &= 5{,}023{,}200 \text{ kJ/hr} \\
Q_{kW} &= \frac{5{,}023{,}200}{3600} \\
       &= 1395.33 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Evaporation loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{evap} &= 0.00085 \cdot \Delta T_R \cdot \dot{m}_w \\
               &= 0.00085 \cdot 15 \cdot 80{,}000 \\
               &= 1020 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Drift loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{drift} &= 0.0015\% \cdot 80{,}000 \\
                &= 0.000015 \cdot 80{,}000 \\
                &= 1.2 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Blowdown loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{bd} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{evap}}{\text{COC} - 1} \\
             &= \frac{1020}{5 - 1} \\
             &= 255 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Make-up water:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{make-up} &= 1020 + 1.2 + 255 \\
                  &= 1276.2 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** With neat diagrams, classify cooling towers and explain the working of **natural draft** and **mechanical draft induced counter-flow** cooling towers. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare natural draft and mechanical draft cooling towers on any **seven** parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Answer (Tabulated)**

**Classification of Cooling Towers:**

1. **Based on Air Movement:**
   - Natural draft (hyperbolic chimney)
   - Mechanical draft
     - Forced draft (fan below fill)
     - Induced draft (fan above fill)
     - Counter-flow / Cross-flow

2. **Based on Water–Air Direction:**
   - Counter-flow (most efficient)
   - Cross-flow
   - Parallel flow

3. **Based on Construction:**
   - Package type (factory-built, ≤ 500 TR)
   - Field-erected (≥ 1000 TR)

**Natural Draft Tower:**
A natural draft cooling tower is a **hyperboloid-shaped reinforced concrete structure** typically 100–200 m tall. It uses the buoyancy effect — the warm humid air inside is *less dense* than the cool ambient air outside, generating an upward draft naturally (no fan). Hot condenser water is sprayed at the top through distribution pipes, falls through PVC fill, and evaporatively cools. The cold water is collected in the basin at the bottom. These towers handle very large capacities (>500 TR) and are commonly used in **thermal power plants**.

**Mechanical Draft Induced Counter-Flow Tower:**
This is a compact rectangular/cylindrical unit with an axial fan at the top that *induces* air upward. Water is sprayed downward from the top through distribution nozzles onto fill media. Air enters through louvres at the bottom and rises in the **opposite direction** to the falling water (counter-flow). The warm humid air is discharged through the top, passing through drift eliminators. Cold water collects in the basin and is recirculated. It is the **most efficient** configuration for HVAC applications up to ~2000 TR.

---

#### **Part (b) — Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Natural Draft | Mechanical Draft |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Air movement | Buoyancy-driven (no fan) | Fan-driven (forced/induced) |
| 2. Capacity | Very large (>500 TR) | Small to medium (≤ 2000 TR) |
| 3. Initial cost | Very high (civil works) | Moderate (factory-built) |
| 4. Operating cost | Low (no fan power) | High (fan consumes 0.5–1 % of plant) |
| 5. Footprint area | Very large | Compact |
| 6. Sensitivity to wind | Moderate | High (wind affects performance) |
| 7. Maintenance | Difficult (huge structure) | Easy (modular components) |
| 8. Typical application | Power plant condensers | HVAC chilled water plants |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cooling Tower Numericals:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kJ/hr → kW** (divide by 3600). Many students write the answer in kJ/hr and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong direction for Approach** — Approach is always $T_{w,out} - T_{wb,in}$. Some students invert it.
> 3. **Using 1% constant incorrectly** — the formula is $0.00085 \cdot \text{Range}$, NOT $0.0085$. Off by 10× → full mark loss.
> 4. **Forgetting Drift loss** in the make-up water sum — drift is small but **must be included**.
> 5. **Blowdown formula sign error** — COC − 1 in denominator, not COC alone.
> 6. **Specific heat of water = 4.186 kJ/kg·K** — using 4.18 or 4.2 is acceptable; using 1.0 (which is for kJ/kg·K per °C) is wrong.
> 7. **Effectiveness is dimensionless** — do NOT write it with °C units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cooling Towers**
> - **Definition:** Open direct-contact heat exchanger using evaporative cooling.
> - **Theoretical limit of cooling = Ambient Wet-Bulb Temperature (WBT).**
> - **Range = T_w,in − T_w,out** (heat-load driven).
> - **Approach = T_w,out − T_wb,in** (design parameter; lower is better).
> - **Effectiveness η = Range / (Range + Approach)**; ideal = 1, typical = 0.6–0.85.
> - **Evaporation loss = 0.00085 × Range × m_dot_w** (kg/hr).
> - **Drift loss ≈ 0.001% to 0.002%** of circulation rate.
> - **Blowdown = Evaporation / (COC − 1)** — controls scaling.
> - **Make-up = Evaporation + Drift + Blowdown.**
> - **c_p,water = 4.186 kJ/kg·K; h_fg ≈ 2400 kJ/kg at 30 °C.**
> - **Counter-flow > Cross-flow > Parallel-flow** (in efficiency).
> - **Natural draft → power plants; Mechanical draft → HVAC.**
> - **L/G ratio** is the key design parameter (typically 0.75–1.5).
> - **Always convert kJ/hr to kW by dividing by 3600.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of moist air - Specific humidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties of moist air - Specific humidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Moist Air?

**Moist air** is a mechanical mixture of **dry air** and **water vapour**. In Refrigeration & Air Conditioning (RAC), atmospheric air is never perfectly dry because the atmosphere always contains a certain amount of moisture (water in vapour phase). The study of moist air is fundamental to psychrometry, which forms the backbone of all HVAC load calculations and cooling coil selection procedures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Air-conditioning processes, psychrometric processes, and all cooling/heating load calculations are built upon the property **Specific Humidity**. Mastering this property is non-negotiable for solving 14-mark ESE numericals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dry Air Composition (by mass, assumed constant at sea level):**
> - Nitrogen (N₂): **75.55 %**
> - Oxygen (O₂): **23.10 %**
> - Argon (Ar): **1.30 %**
> - Carbon Dioxide (CO₂): **0.05 %**
>
> The molecular mass of dry air, **Mₐ = 28.97 kg/kmol**.
> The molecular mass of water vapour, **Mᵥ = 18.015 kg/kmol**.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Specific Humidity

**Specific Humidity (ω or W)** is defined as the **mass of water vapour (mᵥ) present per unit mass of dry air (mₐ)** in a moist air sample.

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} \;\; \text{[kg of water vapour / kg of dry air]}$$

It is also commonly called the **Humidity Ratio (W)** or **Moisture Content** in ASHRAE terminology.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🧽

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Salty Sea Water**
> Imagine a glass of sea water. The water represents **dry air**, and the dissolved salt represents **water vapour**.
> - **Specific Humidity (ω)** is like asking: *"For every 1 kg of pure water, how many grams of salt are dissolved?"*
> - Notice that we use **dry air** as the reference, **NOT the total moist air**. This is crucial because the dry air mass is constant during any humidification/dehumidification process, whereas the water vapour mass changes.
> - If you add more salt, ω increases; if you evaporate water, ω increases (less water to dilute the salt).

This is why in air-conditioning design, the **dry-bulb temperature** (which reflects dry air temperature) is paired with specific humidity as the primary state coordinates on the **Psychrometric Chart**.

---

## 1.4 Why NOT use Absolute Humidity as the primary property?

| Property | Definition | Why not preferred for A/C? |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolute Humidity (ρᵥ)** | Mass of water vapour per unit **volume** of moist air | Volume changes with T & P; not a conserved property in open systems |
| **Specific Humidity (ω)** | Mass of water vapour per unit **mass of dry air** | **Conserved** during heating/cooling (no condensation); accurate for mass-balance calculations |

The dry air mass remains constant in nearly all standard A/C processes (heating, cooling, humidification, dehumidification, mixing), making **specific humidity** the ideal conserved property — analogous to enthalpy in pure-substance thermodynamics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundation: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures

Since dry air and water vapour exist as **ideal gases** (at low atmospheric pressures), Dalton's Law applies:

$$P_t = P_a + P_v$$

where:
- **Pₜ** = Total (atmospheric) pressure of moist air
- **Pₐ** = Partial pressure of dry air
- **Pᵥ** = Partial pressure of water vapour

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Build-up to Specific Humidity

**Step 1 — Apply the Ideal Gas Equation to Each Component:**

For dry air occupying volume V at temperature T:

$$P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T$$

For water vapour occupying the **same volume V** at the **same temperature T**:

$$P_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Form the Ratio (eliminate V and T):**

$$\frac{P_v}{P_a} = \frac{m_v \cdot R_v}{m_a \cdot R_a}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Specific Humidity (ω = mᵥ/mₐ):**

Rearranging:

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Gas Constants using Molecular Masses:**

Since R = R̄/M (Universal Gas Constant R̄ = 8.314 kJ/kmol·K):

$$\frac{R_a}{R_v} = \frac{M_v}{M_a} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} = 0.622$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_a}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Pₐ = Pₜ − Pᵥ (Dalton's Law):**

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}}$$

This is the **MASTER FORMULA** for specific humidity. Memorize it for KTU ESE.

---

## 2.3 Inverse Relation (Useful for Dehumidification Calculations)

Sometimes you are given ω and need to find Pᵥ. Rearranging:

$$P_v = \frac{\omega \cdot P_t}{0.622 + \omega}$$

---

## 2.4 Specific Humidity at Saturation (ωₛ)

When the moist air becomes **saturated**, the partial pressure of water vapour equals the **saturation pressure (Pᵥₛ)** corresponding to the dry-bulb temperature:

$$\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{vs}(T)}{P_t - P_{vs}(T)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relative Humidity (φ)** can be expressed directly in terms of ω:
> $$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}} = \frac{\omega \cdot P_t}{(0.622 + \omega) \cdot P_{vs}}$$
> This formula appears frequently in 7-mark sub-parts of KTU Module 4 questions.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega = \dfrac{m_v}{m_a}$ | Definition of specific humidity | kg/kg dry air |
| 2 | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$ | Master formula (from ideal gas + Dalton's Law) | kg/kg |
| 3 | $P_v = \dfrac{\omega \cdot P_t}{0.622 + \omega}$ | Inverse relation | kPa or bar |
| 4 | $\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_{vs}}{P_t - P_{vs}}$ | Specific humidity at saturation | kg/kg |
| 5 | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{vs}} = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{P_t - P_{vs}}{P_t - P_v}$ | Relative humidity from ω | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\rho_a = \dfrac{P_a}{R_a \cdot T} = \dfrac{P_t - P_v}{R_a \cdot T}$ | Density of dry air | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $\rho_v = \dfrac{P_v}{R_v \cdot T}$ | Density of water vapour | kg/m³ |
| 8 | $\nu = \dfrac{R_a \cdot T}{P_t - P_v}$ | Specific volume of moist air per kg dry air | m³/kg dry air |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constant to Remember:** The factor **0.622** (sometimes written as 0.62198) arises purely from the ratio of molecular masses Mᵥ/Mₐ. **Never use 0.622 for any other gas pair.**

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Cooling Coil Selection:** The change in specific humidity across a cooling coil ($\Delta\omega = \omega_1 - \omega_2$) directly determines the **latent heat load** to be removed: $Q_L = \dot{m}_a \cdot \Delta\omega \cdot h_{fg}$.
2. **Duct Design:** Knowing ω at supply and return allows engineers to calculate **moisture removal rate** in kg/h, sizing dehumidifiers.
3. **Comfort Air-Conditioning:** ASHRAE recommends $\omega$ between **0.008 and 0.012 kg/kg** for human thermal comfort in tropical climates like Kerala.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Master Formula

We start from the first principles of ideal gas behaviour applied to each component of moist air.

### Step A: Ideal Gas Law for Dry Air

The dry air component exerts partial pressure Pₐ and occupies the total volume V at temperature T (in Kelvin).

$$P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T$$

Rearranging to express mass:

$$m_a = \frac{P_a \cdot V}{R_a \cdot T}$$

### Step B: Ideal Gas Law for Water Vapour

The water vapour component, occupying the **same volume V** at the **same temperature T**, exerts partial pressure Pᵥ:

$$P_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T$$

Rearranging:

$$m_v = \frac{P_v \cdot V}{R_v \cdot T}$$

### Step C: Divide mᵥ by mₐ

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{\dfrac{P_v \cdot V}{R_v \cdot T}}{\dfrac{P_a \cdot V}{R_a \cdot T}}$$

The volume V and temperature T **cancel out** (this is what makes ω independent of volume and temperature — a fundamental property!):

$$\omega = \frac{P_v}{R_v} \cdot \frac{R_a}{P_a} = \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

### Step D: Introduce Universal Gas Constant R̄

Since $R_a = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{M_a}$ and $R_v = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{M_v}$:

$$\frac{R_a}{R_v} = \frac{\bar{R}/M_a}{\bar{R}/M_v} = \frac{M_v}{M_a} = \frac{18.015}{28.97}$$

Performing the numerical division:

$$\frac{M_v}{M_a} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} = 0.62198 \approx 0.622$$

Therefore:

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_a}$$

### Step E: Apply Dalton's Law

$$P_a = P_t - P_v$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}}$$

### Step F: Express Pᵥ in Terms of ω

Starting from the boxed equation:

$$\omega (P_t - P_v) = 0.622 \cdot P_v$$

$$\omega \cdot P_t - \omega \cdot P_v = 0.622 \cdot P_v$$

$$\omega \cdot P_t = 0.622 \cdot P_v + \omega \cdot P_v$$

$$\omega \cdot P_t = P_v (0.622 + \omega)$$

$$\boxed{P_v = \frac{\omega \cdot P_t}{0.622 + \omega}}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard 7-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
Atmospheric air is at a dry-bulb temperature of **30 °C** and relative humidity of **60 %**. The total atmospheric pressure is **101.325 kPa**. Determine:
1. Specific humidity (ω)
2. Partial pressure of water vapour (Pᵥ)
3. Specific humidity at saturation (ωₛ)
4. Degree of saturation (μ)

**Given Data:**
- T = 30 °C → T = 303 K
- φ = 0.60
- Pₜ = 101.325 kPa

**Required Data from Steam Tables:**
- Pᵥₛ at 30 °C = **4.246 kPa**

### Solution Step 1: Find Pᵥ

$$P_v = \phi \cdot P_{vs} = 0.60 \times 4.246 = 2.5476 \text{ kPa}$$

### Solution Step 2: Find Specific Humidity ω

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$$

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.5476}{101.325 - 2.5476}$$

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.5476}{98.7774}$$

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot 0.02579 = 0.01604 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

### Solution Step 3: Find ωₛ at the Same Temperature

$$\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{vs}}{P_t - P_{vs}}$$

$$\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{4.246}{101.325 - 4.246}$$

$$\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{4.246}{97.079}$$

$$\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot 0.04374 = 0.02721 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

### Solution Step 4: Find Degree of Saturation (μ)

$$\mu = \frac{\omega}{\omega_s} = \frac{0.01604}{0.02721} = 0.5895 \approx 0.59$$

### Final Answers (Engineering Format)
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Pᵥ | **2.548 kPa** |
| ω | **0.01604 kg/kg dry air** |
| ωₛ | **0.02721 kg/kg dry air** |
| μ | **0.59 (or 59 %)** |

---

## 3.3 Variation of ω with Pᵥ — Sensitivity Analysis

The relationship $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$ is **non-linear**. As Pᵥ increases (more moisture), the denominator $P_t - P_v$ shrinks, accelerating the increase in ω. This is why **humid tropical air** in Kerala can have very high specific humidities, leading to uncomfortable "sticky" conditions even at moderate temperatures.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Moist Air Specific Humidity Derivation

The following Mermaid block-diagram traces the **logical flow** of the derivation and the inter-relationship of the key variables used to compute specific humidity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ATMOSPHERIC MOIST AIR SAMPLE"] --> B["Volume V, Temp T, Total Pressure Pt"]
    B --> C["DALTON'S LAW: Pt = Pa + Pv"]
    B --> D["IDEAL GAS LAW APPLIED TO DRY AIR"]
    B --> E["IDEAL GAS LAW APPLIED TO WATER VAPOUR"]
    D --> F["pa times V = ma times Ra times T"]
    E --> G["pv times V = mv times Rv times T"]
    F --> H["Solve for ma"]
    G --> I["Solve for mv"]
    H --> J["Form Ratio omega equals mv divided by ma"]
    I --> J
    J --> K["V and T cancel out"]
    K --> L["omega equals Ra divided by Rv times pv divided by pa"]
    L --> M["Substitute Ra equals Rbar divided by Ma, Rv equals Rbar divided by Mv"]
    M --> N["Factor emerges equals Mv divided by Ma equals 0.622"]
    N --> O["Apply Dalton: pa equals Pt minus pv"]
    O --> P["MASTER FORMULA omega equals 0.622 times pv divided by Pt minus pv"]
    P --> Q["Inverse Relation: pv equals omega times Pt divided by 0.622 plus omega"]
    P --> R["Saturation Specific Humidity omega s using pvs"]
    R --> S["Relative Humidity phi equals omega times Pt minus pvs divided by omega s times Pt minus pv"]
    S --> T["ENGINEERING APPLICATIONS: Cooling Coils, Dehumidifiers, HVAC Loads"]
```

## 4.2 Relationship Flow Diagram — Specific Humidity and Other Properties

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Specific Humidity omega"] --> B["Partial Pressure pv"]
    B --> C["Relative Humidity phi"]
    A --> D["Degree of Saturation mu"]
    C --> E["Dew Point Temperature Tdp"]
    A --> F["Enthalpy of Moist Air h"]
    A --> G["Specific Volume nu"]
    F --> H["Cooling Coil Load Q"]
    G --> I["Air Quantity m dot a"]
    H --> J["HVAC SYSTEM DESIGN"]
    I --> J
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram for exam answers:**
> - The **left** side shows the *direct* properties derived from ω (φ, μ, T_dₚ, h, ν).
> - The **right** side shows the *engineering deliverables* (loads, system sizing) — this is what the examiner expects you to connect ω to in 14-mark problems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
**Define Specific Humidity. How is it different from Absolute Humidity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Specific humidity (ω) is the mass of water vapour (mᵥ) present per unit mass of dry air (mₐ) in a moist air mixture, expressed in **kg of water vapour per kg of dry air**.
- **Distinction (1 mark):** Absolute humidity is the mass of water vapour per unit *volume* of moist air (kg/m³). Specific humidity is preferred in A/C because it is independent of temperature and pressure variations, and the dry air mass is conserved during standard air-conditioning processes.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**State the formula for specific humidity and explain the significance of the constant 0.622.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Formula (2 marks):** $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$
- **Significance (1 mark):** The constant **0.622** is the ratio of the molecular mass of water vapour (Mᵥ = 18.015) to that of dry air (Mₐ = 28.97). It emerges from the ratio of specific gas constants Rₐ/Rᵥ in the ideal gas derivation. *Memory aid: Water is lighter, hence 0.622 < 1.*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 contains internal choice questions. Two alternatives are provided below.*

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the specific humidity of moist air in terms of partial pressure of water vapour and total pressure. State clearly all assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A room contains air at 35 °C dry-bulb temperature and 50 % relative humidity. The total pressure is 101.325 kPa. Calculate (i) specific humidity, (ii) partial pressure of water vapour, (iii) dew point temperature. Given: Pᵥₛ at 35 °C = 5.628 kPa.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Both dry air and water vapour behave as **ideal gases**.
2. Dry air and water vapour occupy the **same volume** V and are at the **same temperature** T (thermal equilibrium).
3. The total pressure is the sum of partial pressures (**Dalton's Law** is valid).

**Derivation (6 marks):**

Applying the ideal gas equation to dry air (volume V, temperature T, partial pressure Pₐ):

$$P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_a = \frac{P_a \cdot V}{R_a \cdot T}$$

Applying the ideal gas equation to water vapour (same V and T):

$$P_v \cdot V = m_v \cdot R_v \cdot T \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_v = \frac{P_v \cdot V}{R_v \cdot T}$$

By definition:

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v \cdot V / (R_v \cdot T)}{P_a \cdot V / (R_a \cdot T)} = \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{R_a}{R_v}$$

Using $R = \bar{R}/M$:

$$\frac{R_a}{R_v} = \frac{M_v}{M_a} = \frac{18.015}{28.97} = 0.622$$

Substituting Pₐ = Pₜ − Pᵥ (Dalton's Law):

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}}$$

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Given:** T = 35 °C, φ = 0.50, Pₜ = 101.325 kPa, Pᵥₛ(35°C) = 5.628 kPa.

**(i) Specific Humidity (3 marks):**

Partial pressure:

$$P_v = \phi \cdot P_{vs} = 0.50 \times 5.628 = 2.814 \text{ kPa}$$

Specific humidity:

$$\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.814}{101.325 - 2.814} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.814}{98.511}$$

$$\omega = 0.622 \times 0.02857 = 0.01777 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

**(ii) Partial Pressure (2 marks):**

$$P_v = 2.814 \text{ kPa (calculated above)}$$

**(iii) Dew Point Temperature (2 marks):**

The dew point is the saturation temperature corresponding to the actual partial pressure Pᵥ = 2.814 kPa.

From steam tables, the saturation temperature corresponding to 2.814 kPa is approximately **T_dₚ ≈ 23 °C**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students incorrectly use **T_dₚ = T_wₑₜ** (wet-bulb temperature). These are **NOT** the same. Dew point is found from steam tables by looking up the temperature at which **Pᵥₛ equals the given Pᵥ**. You will lose 2 full marks for this confusion.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the terms: (i) Relative Humidity, (ii) Degree of Saturation, (iii) Specific Humidity at Saturation. Show how they are inter-related.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Moist air at 101.325 kPa total pressure has a specific humidity of 0.012 kg/kg dry air. If the dry-bulb temperature is 28 °C, find (i) partial pressure of water vapour, (ii) relative humidity, (iii) degree of saturation. Given: Pᵥₛ at 28 °C = 3.78 kPa.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**(i) Relative Humidity (φ) — 2 marks:**
Relative humidity is the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour in moist air to the saturation pressure of water vapour at the same dry-bulb temperature:

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T)}$$

**(ii) Degree of Saturation (μ) — 2 marks:**
Degree of saturation is the ratio of the actual specific humidity (ω) to the specific humidity at saturation (ωₛ) at the same temperature:

$$\mu = \frac{\omega}{\omega_s}$$

**(iii) Specific Humidity at Saturation (ωₛ) — 1 mark:**
This is the specific humidity when the air is saturated (φ = 100%) at the given dry-bulb temperature. Mathematically:

$$\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_{vs}(T)}{P_t - P_{vs}(T)}$$

**Inter-relation (2 marks):**

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}} = \frac{\omega \cdot P_t}{(0.622 + \omega) \cdot P_{vs}} = \mu \cdot \frac{P_t - P_{vs}}{P_t - P_v}$$

Hence, at low humidities where $P_v \ll P_t$, $\phi \approx \mu$, but at high humidities they diverge.

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given:** Pₜ = 101.325 kPa, ω = 0.012 kg/kg, T = 28 °C, Pᵥₛ(28°C) = 3.78 kPa.

**(i) Partial Pressure (3 marks):**

Using the inverse formula:

$$P_v = \frac{\omega \cdot P_t}{0.622 + \omega} = \frac{0.012 \times 101.325}{0.622 + 0.012}$$

$$P_v = \frac{1.2159}{0.634} = 1.9178 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Relative Humidity (2 marks):**

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}} = \frac{1.9178}{3.78} = 0.5074 = 50.74 \text{ %}$$

**(iii) Degree of Saturation (2 marks):**

First compute ωₛ:

$$\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.78}{101.325 - 3.78} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.78}{97.545}$$

$$\omega_s = 0.622 \times 0.03875 = 0.02410 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

Then:

$$\mu = \frac{\omega}{\omega_s} = \frac{0.012}{0.02410} = 0.498 \approx 0.50 \text{ (or 50 \%)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $P_a$ instead of $(P_t - P_v)$ in the denominator of the master formula. This will give wrong answers and a deduction of **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **kg/kg dry air** with **kg/kg moist air**. Always write units explicitly to earn full marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Using **specific heat of dry air** (1.005) where specific gas **constant** (0.287) is required — totally different quantities.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Specific Humidity:**

- ✅ **Definition:** $\omega = m_v / m_a$ — water vapour mass per kg of **dry air** (NOT per kg of moist air).
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$
- ✅ **Origin of 0.622:** Ratio of molecular masses $M_v / M_a = 18.015 / 28.97 = 0.622$.
- ✅ **Inverse Formula:** $P_v = \dfrac{\omega \cdot P_t}{0.622 + \omega}$
- ✅ **Assumptions:** Ideal gas behaviour, uniform T and V, Dalton's Law valid.
- ✅ **Saturation Specific Humidity:** Replace Pᵥ with Pᵥₛ(T) from steam tables.
- ✅ **Degree of Saturation:** $\mu = \omega / \omega_s$ — distinct from Relative Humidity.
- ✅ **Reference State:** Specific humidity is referenced to **dry air mass**, making it the ideal conserved property for HVAC mass balances.
- ✅ **Engineering Use:** Latent cooling load $Q_L = \dot{m}_a \cdot \Delta\omega \cdot h_{fg}$ (often the most tested 14-mark link).
- ✅ **Units:** Always state **kg of water vapour per kg of dry air** in final answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dew point temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Dew point temperature ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] **DEW POINT TEMPERATURE (T_dp)** > Dew point temperature is the temperature of moist air at which the air becomes **saturated** with water vapor (i.e., the relative humidity becomes **100 %**) when it is cooled at **constant pressure** and **constant moisture content** (constant humidity ratio, W). At T_dp, the partial pressure of water vapor in the mixture equals the saturation pressure of water vapor at that temperature, and any further cooling results in **condensation** of water vapor into liquid droplets. Mathematically, the defining condition is: $$P_w = P_{ws}(T_{dp})$$ where $P_w$ is the actual partial pressure of water vapor in the moist air, and $P_{ws}(T_{dp})$ is the saturation pressure of water vapor evaluated at the dew point temperature. > [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMET751, Module 4)** > Dew point temperature is a **purely moisture-content indicator**. Two air samples with very different dry-bulb temperatures can have the same dew point temperature if they contain the same amount of moisture. This property makes it the most reliable single number to describe the "dampness" of air. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Picture Imagine a cold glass of chilled water kept in a warm, humid room. The outer surface of the glass becomes fogged with water droplets, and the temperature at which this fog first appears (when the glass was gradually cooling) is essentially the **dew point temperature** of the surrounding room air. The water vapour in the room air, when it touches the cold surface, is cooled to a temperature at which it can no longer remain as vapour; it must turn into liquid — i.e., it has reached its dew point. **Plain-English Summary:** *Dew point is the temperature to which air must be cooled (without changing its moisture content or pressure) for dew/condensation to just begin forming.* ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Key Standard Metrics - **Standard atmospheric pressure assumed in psychrometric tables:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ (standard), with the **barometric pressure** $P_t$ variable in actual problems. - **Universal gas constant for water vapour:** $R_v = 0.4615 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. - **Universal gas constant for dry air:** $R_a = 0.287 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. - **Molecular weight ratio:** $\frac{M_v}{M_a} = 0.622$. ### 1.4 Importance of Dew Point in Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning - **Cooling Coil Selection:** The temperature of the cooling coil surface must be **below the dew point** of the entering air for any moisture removal (latent cooling) to occur. This determines whether a coil acts as a *dry coil* (sensible only) or a *wet coil* (sensible + latent). - **Comfort & Health:** Human thermal discomfort and microbial growth (mould, fungus) are governed more by **dew point / humidity ratio** than by relative humidity. - **Process Industries:** In pharmaceutical, food, and textile industries, dew point dictates drying, storage, and preservation conditions. - **Prevention of Condensation:** Building envelopes and chilled-water pipes are insulated so that surface temperatures stay above the indoor air's dew point, thereby preventing surface condensation and damage. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Build-up of the Concept **Step 1 — Moist air as a binary mixture** Atmospheric air is treated as a mixture of **dry air (a)** and **water vapour (v)**. Let: - $P_t$ = total barometric pressure of the mixture (kPa) - $P_a$ = partial pressure of dry air (kPa) - $P_w$ = partial pressure of water vapour (kPa) By **Dalton's law of partial pressures**: $$P_t = P_a + P_w$$ **Step 2 — Saturation condition** A surface of pure liquid water in equilibrium with vapour at temperature $T$ exerts a saturation pressure $P_{ws}(T)$. For moist air: - If $P_w < P_{ws}(T_{db})$ → unsaturated (typical room air) - If $P_w = P_{ws}(T_{db})$ → saturated - If $P_w > P_{ws}(T_{db})$ → supersaturated (metastable, leads to fog/condensation) **Step 3 — Defining the dew point** Now cool the air *at constant $P_w$* and *constant $P_t$*. As temperature falls, $P_{ws}(T)$ falls monotonically. The first temperature at which $P_{ws}(T) = P_w$ is the **dew point** $T_{dp}$. Below this temperature, condensation must occur. **Step 4 — Why $T_{dp}$ is a moisture-only indicator** Because $P_w$ is held constant during the cooling process (no moisture leaves or enters), the value of $T_{dp}$ depends *only* on the original $P_w$. Therefore, $T_{dp}$ is a *fingerprint* of the moisture content of the air, independent of dry-bulb temperature. **Step 5 — Why dew point is always $\leq$ dry-bulb temperature** At the dry-bulb temperature $T_{db}$, the actual vapour pressure $P_w$ is *less than or equal to* the saturation value $P_{ws}(T_{db})$. To make $P_{ws}$ fall down to $P_w$, the temperature must *decrease* (because $P_{ws}$ decreases with $T$). Hence: $$T_{dp} \leq T_{db}$$ with equality holding only when the air is fully saturated (RH = 100 %). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Defining condition | $P_w = P_{ws}(T_{dp})$ | $P_w$ in kPa | | 2 | Relative humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{ws}(T_{db})}$ | $\phi$ in fraction (0–1) | | 3 | Humidity ratio | $W = 0.622\,\dfrac{P_w}{P_t - P_w}$ | kg water / kg dry air | | 4 | Humidity ratio in terms of $\phi$ | $W = 0.622\,\dfrac{\phi\,P_{ws}(T_{db})}{P_t - \phi\,P_{ws}(T_{db})}$ | Used in chart problems | | 5 | Saturation humidity ratio | $W_s = 0.622\,\dfrac{P_{ws}(T_{db})}{P_t - P_{ws}(T_{db})}$ | At $T_{db}$ | | 6 | Specific volume of moist air | $v = \dfrac{R_a T_{db}}{P_t - P_w}(1 + 1.6078 W)$ | m³/kg dry air, $T_{db}$ in K | | 7 | Enthalpy of moist air | $h = 1.005\,T_{db} + W(2500 + 1.88\,T_{db})$ | kJ/kg dry air, $T_{db}$ in °C | | 8 | Inverse relation (find $T_{dp}$ from $P_w$) | $T_{dp}$ is the temperature at which $P_{ws}(T) = P_w$ | Read from saturation table / chart | | 9 | Lower bound inequality | $T_{dp} \leq T_{wb} \leq T_{db}$ | Fundamental psychrometric ordering | | 10 | Maximum specific humidity for given $T_{dp}$ | $W_{max} = 0.622\,\dfrac{P_{ws}(T_{dp})}{P_t - P_{ws}(T_{dp})}$ | If $T_{db} > T_{dp}$, dehumidification possible | > [!NOTE] **KTU Convention** > - All temperatures in psychrometric calculations are in **°C** for the enthalpy and humidity-ratio equations. > - Pressures are always in **kPa** (never in bar or mm Hg in KTU papers). > - $P_{ws}$ is read from **steam tables** for water at the given temperature. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pharmaceutical Clean Rooms:** Dew point is monitored to ensure that indoor surfaces do not condense moisture, which would support microbial growth. - **Compressed-Air Drying:** The pressure-dew-point (PDP) of compressed air is the dew point at the operating line pressure; it dictates the type of desiccant dryer required. - **Cold Storage Design:** Insulation on walls and pipes is sized so the inner surface temperature is always above the indoor dew point. - **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ):** ASHRAE Standard-55 uses dew point as a key metric to assess humidity-related comfort. - **Condensation Prevention in Ducts:** Supply ducts carrying chilled air are insulated such that the external duct surface temperature is always above the dew point of the surrounding ambient air — otherwise water will drip into the ceiling. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Dew-Point Relation **Given:** A sample of moist air at total pressure $P_t$ and dry-bulb temperature $T_{db}$ has a relative humidity $\phi$. **To derive:** The relationship $P_w = P_{ws}(T_{dp})$ and the formula expressing $T_{dp}$ in terms of measurable quantities. **Step 1 — Express $P_w$ from the definition of relative humidity** By definition, relative humidity $\phi$ is the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour to its saturation value at the dry-bulb temperature: $$\phi = \frac{P_w}{P_{ws}(T_{db})}$$ Solving for $P_w$: $$P_w = \phi \cdot P_{ws}(T_{db})$$ **Step 2 — Identify the saturation state at dew point** At the dew point temperature, the air is saturated, which means the partial pressure of water vapour equals the saturation pressure of water vapour at that temperature. By Dalton's law, the *partial pressure* of water vapour is the same throughout the cooling process (no moisture is added or removed). Hence: $$P_w \big|_{T=T_{dp}} = P_{ws}(T_{dp})$$ **Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $P_w$** The same physical quantity $P_w$ is given by both Step 1 and Step 2. Therefore: $$\phi \cdot P_{ws}(T_{db}) = P_{ws}(T_{dp})$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $\phi$ (an alternative useful form)** $$\phi = \frac{P_{ws}(T_{dp})}{P_{ws}(T_{db})}$$ This shows that **relative humidity is the ratio of the saturation pressure at the dew point to the saturation pressure at the dry-bulb temperature** — a very handy identity. **Step 5 — Invert to find $T_{dp}$ from $P_w$** Using the ASHRAE formulation (over liquid water, for $0 \le T \le 200$ °C): $$\ln\!\bigl(P_{ws}\bigr) = \frac{C_8}{T + C_9} + C_{10} + C_{11} T + C_{12} T^2 + C_{13} T^3 + C_{14} \ln(T)$$ with the constants: $$ \begin{aligned} C_8 &= -5.8002206 \times 10^{3} \\ C_9 &= \;1.3914993 \\ C_{10} &= -4.8640239 \times 10^{-2} \\ C_{11} &= \;4.1764768 \times 10^{-5} \\ C_{12} &= -1.4452093 \times 10^{-8} \\ C_{13} &= \;6.5459673 \\ C_{14} &= -5.6551745 \times 10^{1} \end{aligned} $$ Here, $P_{ws}$ is in **Pa** and $T$ is in **°C** if the equation is used with the inverse-relationship coefficients; consult the ASHRAE Handbook (Chapter 1, Fundamentals) for the inverse coefficients. **Step 6 — Practical KTU-style computation** In KTU examination problems, the procedure is: 1. Look up $P_{ws}(T_{db})$ from steam tables at the given $T_{db}$. 2. Compute $P_w = \phi \cdot P_{ws}(T_{db})$. 3. Look up the temperature at which $P_{ws}(T) = P_w$ — that is $T_{dp}$. 4. Alternatively, read $T_{dp}$ directly from the **psychrometric chart** by moving horizontally from the initial state (left, parallel to the saturation line) until the saturation curve ($\phi = 100\%$) is reached. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > **Example Problem:** > Atmospheric air is at $T_{db} = 35\text{ °C}$ and $\phi = 0.60$ (60 %). The barometric pressure is $P_t = 101.325$ kPa. Find the dew point temperature, the humidity ratio, and the specific enthalpy. Use the steam-table value $P_{ws}(35\text{ °C}) = 5.628$ kPa. **Solution:** **Step A — Partial pressure of water vapour** $$P_w = \phi \cdot P_{ws}(T_{db}) = 0.60 \times 5.628 = 3.377 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step B — Dew point temperature** $T_{dp}$ is the temperature at which $P_{ws}(T) = 3.377$ kPa. From steam tables, $P_{ws}(26.5\text{ °C}) \approx 3.380$ kPa. Therefore: $$T_{dp} \approx 26.5 \text{ °C}$$ (Alternatively, read directly off the psychrometric chart.) **Step C — Humidity ratio** $$W = 0.622 \times \frac{P_w}{P_t - P_w} = 0.622 \times \frac{3.377}{101.325 - 3.377}$$ $$\begin{aligned} W &= 0.622 \times \frac{3.377}{97.948} \\ &= 0.622 \times 0.03448 \\ &= 0.02145 \text{ kg water / kg dry air} \end{aligned}$$ **Step D — Specific enthalpy** $$h = 1.005 \cdot T_{db} + W(2500 + 1.88 \cdot T_{db})$$ $$\begin{aligned} h &= 1.005 \times 35 + 0.02145 \times (2500 + 1.88 \times 35) \\ &= 35.175 + 0.02145 \times (2500 + 65.8) \\ &= 35.175 + 0.02145 \times 2565.8 \\ &= 35.175 + 55.04 \\ &= 90.21 \text{ kJ/kg dry air} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Verification of Inequality** > $T_{dp} \;(\text{26.5 °C}) \;\le\; T_{db} \;(\text{35 °C})$ ✔ — confirms the calculation is consistent. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Cooling on Moist Air | Process Description | State Change | $T_{db}$ | $T_{dp}$ | $W$ | $\phi$ | $h$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Initial room air | State 1 | 35 °C | 26.5 °C | 0.02145 | 60 % | 90.21 kJ/kg | | Sensible cooling only (T_db > T_dp) | State 2 | 28 °C | 26.5 °C | 0.02145 | ≈ 100 % | 71.13 kJ/kg | | Cooling below T_dp → dehumidification | State 3 | 25 °C | 25 °C | 0.02032 | 100 % | 65.85 kJ/kg | | Further cooling to dew point (saturated) | State 4 | 26.5 °C | 26.5 °C | 0.02170 | 100 % | 67.96 kJ/kg | > [!NOTE] **Inference** > From State 1 → State 2, $T_{db}$ drops, but $T_{dp}$ and $W$ are unchanged (sensible cooling). When the cooling coil surface is *below* $T_{dp}$, condensation begins and $W$ falls — this is **latent cooling** (State 2 → State 3). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flowchart: Identifying the Dew Point by Cooling Moist Air ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Moist Air at State 1:
T_db = 35 C, RH = 60 percent] --> B[Cool air at constant P_w and P_t] B --> C{T_db greater than T_dp?} C -- Yes --> D[Sensible cooling only
W constant, T_dp constant] D --> E[T_db decreases, RH increases] E --> F{T_db reaches T_dp?} F -- No --> D F -- Yes --> G[State 2: SATURATED AIR
RH = 100 percent] G --> H[Further cooling leads to CONDENSATION] H --> I[Moisture removed: W decreases
T_dp drops to new lower value] I --> J[Final state at lower T_db and lower T_dp] C -- No --> K[Air already at or below dew point
condensation already occurring] K --> H ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dew-Point Instrumentation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Sample Handling] A1[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> A2[Filter and Dust Trap] A2 --> A3[Flow Controller] end subgraph S2[Sensor Module] A3 --> B1[Cooled Mirror Sensor] B1 --> B2[Optical Detector
Detects first dew formation] B2 --> B3[Thermometer at mirror] end subgraph S3[Signal Conditioning] B2 --> C1[Signal Amplifier] B3 --> C1 C1 --> C2[Microcontroller] end subgraph S4[Output] C2 --> D1[Digital Display: T_dp in degree C] C2 --> D2[Analog Output: 4 to 20 mA] C2 --> D3[Data Logger] end ``` ### 4.3 Psychrometric Chart Trace (Schematic Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Chart[Psychrometric Chart of Moist Air at P_t = 101.325 kPa] P1[State 1: T_db = 35 C, RH = 60 percent
T_dp = 26.5 C, W = 0.0214] -->|Horizontal line
constant W and T_dp| P2[State 2: T_db = 26.5 C, RH = 100 percent
SATURATION LINE] P2 -->|Vertical line down
constant RH = 100 percent| P3[State 3: T_db = 25 C, RH = 100 percent
T_dp = 25 C, W = 0.0203] end ``` > [!NOTE] **Diagram Reading Tip** > On the psychrometric chart, the dew point is found by moving from the initial state **horizontally to the left** (parallel to the constant-W lines) until the **saturation curve** (RH = 100 %) is intersected. The dry-bulb temperature at this intersection is $T_{dp}$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — June 2023]* **Define dew point temperature. With the help of a neat sketch, explain how the dew point is determined using a psychrometric chart.** **Model Answer (10–12 lines):** Dew point temperature is the temperature at which moist air becomes saturated when cooled at constant pressure and constant humidity ratio. It represents the temperature to which air must be cooled for condensation to commence. The defining equation is: $$P_w = P_{ws}(T_{dp})$$ To determine the dew point on a psychrometric chart: starting from the initial state (defined by $T_{db}$ and $\phi$), move **horizontally to the left** (parallel to the constant humidity-ratio lines) until the **saturation curve** ($\phi = 100\%$) is reached. The dry-bulb temperature read at this intersection is the **dew point temperature**. > [!IMPORTANT] **Key Points to Mention for Full Marks** > 1. Cooled at **constant pressure and constant moisture content**. > 2. The partial pressure of water vapour equals saturation pressure at $T_{dp}$. > 3. Movement on the chart is **horizontal** (along constant-W line) to the saturation curve. **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]* **State and explain the relationship $T_{dp} \le T_{db}$. Why is dew point considered a true indicator of the moisture content of air?** **Model Answer (8–10 lines):** The dew point temperature is always less than or equal to the dry-bulb temperature ($T_{dp} \le T_{db}$), with equality holding only for saturated air ($\phi = 100\%$). **Reason:** At $T_{db}$, the actual partial pressure $P_w$ is *less than or equal to* the saturation pressure $P_{ws}(T_{db})$. To make $P_{ws}$ fall to $P_w$, the temperature must decrease (since $P_{ws}$ is monotonically increasing with $T$). Hence $T_{dp} \le T_{db}$. **Dew point as a true moisture indicator:** During the cooling process that defines the dew point, both $P_w$ and $P_t$ are held constant, so the humidity ratio $W$ is unchanged. The dew point depends **only** on the original $P_w$, which is determined solely by the moisture content. Therefore, two air samples with very different $T_{db}$ can have the same $T_{dp}$ if they contain the same amount of water vapour per kg of dry air. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- #### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]* > **(a)** Moist air at $T_{db} = 38$ °C and relative humidity $\phi = 55\%$ enters a cooling coil. The barometric pressure is 101.325 kPa. Using steam-table values of $P_{ws}(38\text{ °C}) = 6.628$ kPa, determine: > 1. The partial pressure of water vapour, $P_w$. > 2. The dew point temperature, $T_{dp}$. > 3. The humidity ratio, $W$. > 4. The specific enthalpy, $h$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part 1 — Partial pressure of water vapour** $$P_w = \phi \cdot P_{ws}(T_{db}) = 0.55 \times 6.628 = 3.645 \text{ kPa}$$ **Part 2 — Dew point temperature** We need the temperature at which $P_{ws}(T) = 3.645$ kPa. From steam tables: $$P_{ws}(27.5\text{ °C}) \approx 3.652 \text{ kPa}$$ Therefore: $$T_{dp} \approx 27.5 \text{ °C}$$ **Part 3 — Humidity ratio** $$\begin{aligned} W &= 0.622 \times \frac{P_w}{P_t - P_w} \\ &= 0.622 \times \frac{3.645}{101.325 - 3.645} \\ &= 0.622 \times \frac{3.645}{97.680} \\ &= 0.622 \times 0.03732 \\ &= 0.02321 \text{ kg water / kg dry air} \end{aligned}$$ **Part 4 — Specific enthalpy** $$\begin{aligned} h &= 1.005\,T_{db} + W(2500 + 1.88\,T_{db}) \\ &= 1.005 \times 38 + 0.02321 \times (2500 + 1.88 \times 38) \\ &= 38.19 + 0.02321 \times (2500 + 71.44) \\ &= 38.19 + 0.02321 \times 2571.44 \\ &= 38.19 + 59.68 \\ &= 97.87 \text{ kJ/kg dry air} \end{aligned}$$ --- > **(b)** Explain with a clear sketch, the cooling and dehumidification process of moist air on a psychrometric chart. Show how the dew point temperature controls whether the cooling coil operates as a *dry* coil (sensible only) or a *wet* coil (sensible + latent). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Sketch description (refer to Section 4.3):** On the psychrometric chart, the process of cooling moist air is shown as moving from State 1 ($T_{db,1}, \phi_1$) to a lower temperature. The path is a straight line whose slope depends on the **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** of the cooling coil. - If the **coil surface temperature $T_s > T_{dp}$** of the entering air → the air leaves with the **same humidity ratio** ($W$ unchanged). Only sensible cooling occurs; this is the **dry-coil** condition. The line on the chart is purely horizontal. - If the **coil surface temperature $T_s < T_{dp}$** → moisture condenses on the coil. The air leaves at a **lower $W$** and $\phi = 100\%$. The line tilts downward and to the left; this is the **wet-coil** condition with both sensible and latent cooling. **Mathematical Condition:** $$T_s \le T_{dp} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Condensation occurs (wet coil)}$$ $$T_s > T_{dp} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{No condensation (dry coil)}$$ **Engineering Significance:** 1. The **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** is the temperature of the coil surface where the air would be cooled to saturation if the process line were extended to the saturation curve. 2. The **Bypass Factor (BF)** of the coil is the fraction of air that bypasses the coil; high BF implies a less effective coil. 3. Cooling coil selection (number of rows, fins per inch, face velocity) is done such that $T_s$ lies just below the required $T_{dp}$ of the conditioned air to achieve the desired latent load. --- #### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* > **(a)** A room is to be maintained at $T_{db} = 25$ °C and $\phi = 50\%$. The outside air is at $T_{db} = 40$ °C and $\phi = 30\%$. Calculate: > 1. The dew point temperature of the outside air and the room air. > 2. The humidity ratio of both air streams. > 3. Whether condensation will occur on the inside surface of a single-pane window of the room. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Assume $P_t = 101.325$ kPa. **Required steam-table data:** $$P_{ws}(40\text{ °C}) = 7.384 \text{ kPa}, \quad P_{ws}(25\text{ °C}) = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$$ **Part 1 — Dew point temperatures** *Outside air:* $$P_{w,out} = \phi_{out} \cdot P_{ws}(40) = 0.30 \times 7.384 = 2.215 \text{ kPa}$$ From steam tables, $P_{ws}(19.0\text{ °C}) \approx 2.198$ kPa and $P_{ws}(19.5\text{ °C}) \approx 2.272$ kPa. By linear interpolation: $$T_{dp,out} \approx 19.0 + 0.5 \times \frac{2.215 - 2.198}{2.272 - 2.198} = 19.0 + 0.5 \times 0.230 = 19.12 \text{ °C}$$ *Room air:* $$P_{w,room} = 0.50 \times 3.169 = 1.585 \text{ kPa}$$ From steam tables, $P_{ws}(13.5\text{ °C}) \approx 1.547$ kPa and $P_{ws}(14.0\text{ °C}) \approx 1.599$ kPa. By linear interpolation: $$T_{dp,room} \approx 13.5 + 0.5 \times \frac{1.585 - 1.547}{1.599 - 1.547} = 13.5 + 0.5 \times 0.731 = 13.87 \text{ °C}$$ **Part 2 — Humidity ratios** *Outside air:* $$W_{out} = 0.622 \times \frac{2.215}{101.325 - 2.215} = 0.622 \times \frac{2.215}{99.110} = 0.01390 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$ *Room air:* $$W_{room} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.585}{101.325 - 1.585} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.585}{99.740} = 0.00988 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$ **Part 3 — Condensation on window** For a single-pane window, the inner surface temperature is typically 5–8 °C below the indoor air temperature. With $T_{db,room} = 25$ °C, the inside surface temperature is approximately: $$T_{surface} \approx 25 - 7 = 18 \text{ °C}$$ Compare with $T_{dp,room} = 13.87$ °C: $$T_{surface} \;(18\text{ °C}) \;>\; T_{dp,room} \;(13.87\text{ °C})$$ **Conclusion:** No condensation will occur on the inside surface, because the surface temperature is *above* the room-air dew point. ✔ --- > **(b)** Describe, with a neat sketch, the construction and working of a **Dew Point Meter** (cooled-mirror type). List two industrial applications where dew-point measurement is critical. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Construction of a Cooled-Mirror Dew Point Meter:** The instrument consists of: 1. A small **polished metallic mirror** (often gold- or rhodium-plated to prevent corrosion). 2. A **Peltier thermoelectric cooler** mounted behind the mirror, used to gradually reduce the mirror's temperature. 3. A **precision temperature sensor** (platinum RTD or thermistor) embedded in the mirror to measure its surface temperature. 4. An **optical detector** (LED + photodetector pair) focused on the mirror. The light scattered from the mirror surface is monitored; when dew forms, the scattered light intensity drops sharply. 5. A **control circuit** that drives the Peltier cooler to maintain the mirror temperature at the onset point of condensation. 6. A **digital display / output** that reads the mirror temperature as the dew point. **Working Principle:** - Atmospheric air is drawn over the mirror at a controlled flow rate. - The mirror is cooled gradually. The temperature at which the photodetector first registers a sharp drop in reflected light (due to dew forming) is the **dew point temperature**. - The control loop holds the mirror at the threshold of dew formation, giving a continuous reading. **Sketch (refer to Section 4.2 Block Diagram):** The flow is: **Air intake → Filter → Mirror (with cooler & sensor) → Optical detector → Signal conditioner → Display**. **Two Industrial Applications:** 1. **Compressed-Air Drying Systems:** In pneumatic control systems and instrument-air lines, the *pressure dew point* is measured to verify that desiccant dryers are performing correctly (e.g., PDP < −40 °C for instrument air). 2. **HVAC and Clean-Room Monitoring:** In pharmaceutical and semiconductor clean rooms, dew point is monitored to prevent condensation on cold surfaces, which would otherwise compromise sterility or cause corrosion of sensitive equipment. *(Other valid applications: natural-gas pipeline monitoring, hydrogen-cooled generators, meteorological humidity measurement, transformer-oil dry-out verification.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Dew-Point Problems** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** KTU problems use **kPa** throughout. If $P_{ws}$ values are taken from tables in **Pa** (or mbar), the partial pressure and humidity ratio will be off by a factor of 1000. Always verify the table's units first. > 2. **Inverting the inequality $T_{dp} \le T_{db}$:** Some students write $T_{db} \le T_{dp}$, which is *physically impossible* for unsaturated air. Always state the correct inequality explicitly. > 3. **Using $P_t - P_{ws}$ instead of $P_t - P_w$ in the humidity ratio formula:** > $$W = 0.622 \times \frac{P_w}{P_t - P_w} \quad \text{(CORRECT)}$$ > $$W \neq 0.622 \times \frac{P_w}{P_t - P_{ws}(T_{db})}$$ > These two values differ, especially at high humidity. > 4. **Assuming dew point equals wet-bulb temperature:** No — they are equal *only* for saturated air. Otherwise $T_{dp} \le T_{wb} \le T_{db}$. > 5. **Ignoring barometric pressure variation:** At high altitudes, $P_t < 101.325$ kPa and humidity ratio values change noticeably. Always read $P_t$ from the problem statement. > 6. **Skipping the psychrometric chart cross-check:** KTU answers that show chart readings in addition to the analytical work usually earn **full or near-full marks** because the chart is a standard KTU requirement. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Dew point temperature** = temperature at which moist air becomes saturated when cooled at **constant pressure** and **constant moisture content**. - **Defining equation:** $P_w = P_{ws}(T_{dp})$. - **Fundamental inequality:** $T_{dp} \;\le\; T_{wb} \;\le\; T_{db}$, with equality only at saturation ($\phi = 100\%$). - **Dew point depends only on $P_w$ (and hence on $W$)**, not on the dry-bulb temperature. - **Relative humidity identity:** $\phi = \dfrac{P_{ws}(T_{dp})}{P_{ws}(T_{db})}$. - **Cooling-coil rule:** Condensation (latent cooling) occurs **only if** the coil surface temperature $T_s < T_{dp}$ of the entering air. - **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP):** Temperature on the saturation curve corresponding to the straight-line extension of the cooling process — used in conjunction with **Bypass Factor (BF)** for coil analysis. - **Maximum specific humidity at given $T_{dp}$:** $W_{max} = 0.622\,\dfrac{P_{ws}(T_{dp})}{P_t - P_{ws}(T_{dp})}$. - **Industrial instruments:** Cooled-mirror dew point meters (primary standard), capacitive polymer sensors (secondary/industrial), lithium-chloride hygrometers. - **KTU quick procedure for any "find $T_{dp}$" problem:** 1. Look up $P_{ws}(T_{db})$ from steam tables. 2. Compute $P_w = \phi \cdot P_{ws}(T_{db})$. 3. Read $T_{dp}$ as the temperature at which $P_{ws}(T) = P_w$ (from tables or chart). - **Units to remember in KTU papers:** $P_w$ in **kPa**, $T$ in **°C**, $W$ in **kg water / kg dry air**, $h$ in **kJ/kg dry air**.

topics.title: Relative humidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Relative humidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Relative Humidity (RH or $\phi$)** is formally defined as the ratio of the **actual partial pressure of water vapor** present in a moist air mixture to the **saturation pressure of water vapor** at the **same temperature**, expressed as a percentage.

Mathematically, the governing relation is:

$$\phi = \frac{p_v}{p_{vs}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $p_v$ = Actual partial pressure of water vapor in the moist air mixture (in kPa or bar)
- $p_{vs}$ = Saturation pressure of water vapor at the **dry-bulb temperature** of the mixture (in kPa or bar)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 scheme, relative humidity forms the foundational step of **Psychrometrics** (Module 4), which is essential for all subsequent calculations involving **sensible heat, latent heat, dew point, wet-bulb temperature, and air-conditioning load estimation**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge and the Bucket"

Imagine a **sponge** representing moist air, and a **bucket of water** representing the maximum possible moisture it can hold at a given temperature.

- The **water currently soaked in the sponge** = $p_v$ (actual vapor pressure)
- The **total capacity of the sponge** = $p_{vs}$ (saturation vapor pressure)
- The **wetness percentage** of the sponge = **Relative Humidity**

If the sponge is **half-soaked**, the relative humidity is **50%**. If the sponge cannot absorb any more water without dripping, it is **100% saturated** — condensation begins.

## 1.3 Physical Interpretation & Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Relative humidity is a **temperature-dependent** property. Even if the actual moisture content remains constant, RH **decreases** as temperature rises (because $p_{vs}$ increases with temperature) and **increases** as temperature falls.

| Scenario | Effect on RH |
|----------|--------------|
| Temperature increases | RH decreases (air feels drier) |
| Temperature decreases | RH increases (approaches saturation) |
| Temperature = Dew Point | RH = 100% (saturation) |
| Moisture added at constant T | RH increases |

## 1.4 Standard Reference Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At **Standard Atmospheric Pressure** ($P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa = **1.01325 bar**), the partial pressure of dry air and water vapor sum to atmospheric pressure:
> $$P = p_a + p_v$$
> where $p_a$ is the partial pressure of dry air and $p_v$ is the partial pressure of water vapor.

The **gas constant for water vapor** is $R_v = 0.4615$ kJ/kg·K, and for **dry air** $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K — these are vital for mass-based psychrometric derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The concept of relative humidity is built upon **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** and the **Phase Equilibrium Principle**. The logical chain is:

- **Step 1 — Moist Air Composition:** Atmospheric air is a binary mixture of **dry air** and **water vapor**, both behaving approximately as ideal gases.
- **Step 2 — Partial Pressure Identification:** Each component exerts its own partial pressure; $p_v$ represents the actual water vapor pressure.
- **Step 3 — Saturation Reference:** $p_{vs}$ is determined purely by the **dry-bulb temperature** (read from steam tables or Antoine equation for water).
- **Step 4 — Ratio Computation:** The ratio $p_v / p_{vs}$ quantifies how close the air is to saturation.
- **Step 5 — Engineering Application:** RH governs the rate of evaporation, human comfort, condensation on surfaces, and cooling-coil sizing.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by $p_{vs}$ and not just use $p_v$?**
> Because $p_{vs}$ changes drastically with temperature (exponentially). Normalizing by $p_{vs}$ yields a **dimensionless ratio** that tells us *how full the moisture capacity* of the air is at that specific temperature — making RH a true indicator of moisture saturation regardless of thermal conditions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\phi = \dfrac{p_v}{p_{vs}}$ | Definition of Relative Humidity (decimal) | — |
| 2 | $\phi(\%) = \dfrac{p_v}{p_{vs}} \times 100$ | RH expressed as percentage | % |
| 3 | $p_v = \phi \cdot p_{vs}$ | Actual vapor pressure from RH | kPa |
| 4 | $p_{vs}(T_{db})$ | Saturation pressure at dry-bulb temp | kPa |
| 5 | $p_{vs}(T_{dp}) = p_v$ | At dew point, saturation pressure = actual vapor pressure | kPa |
| 6 | $\omega = 0.622 \dfrac{p_v}{P - p_v}$ | Specific humidity (humidity ratio) | kg water/kg dry air |
| 7 | $\phi = \dfrac{\omega \cdot P}{(0.622 + \omega) \cdot p_{vs}}$ | RH in terms of humidity ratio | — |
| 8 | $P = p_a + p_v$ | Dalton's law of partial pressures | kPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Reminder:** The **saturation pressure $p_{vs}$ is a function of temperature ONLY** — not of pressure, not of moisture content. Always use the **dry-bulb temperature** to evaluate $p_{vs}$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Load Estimation:** RH determines the **latent heat load** on cooling coils.
- **Comfort Index:** Human thermal comfort depends strongly on RH (ideal comfort range is **40%–60%**).
- **Industrial Drying:** RH control governs the rate of moisture removal from products.
- **Cold Storage Design:** Condensation and frost formation on chilled surfaces depend on the local RH of surrounding air.
- **Pharmaceutical/Food Industry:** Precise RH control prevents product degradation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From RH to Humidity Ratio ($\omega$)

**Given:** Relative humidity $\phi$, atmospheric pressure $P$, dry-bulb temperature $T_{db}$ (so $p_{vs}$ is known).

**Starting point:** Definition of specific humidity (humidity ratio):

$$\omega = 0.622 \, \frac{p_v}{P - p_v}$$

**Step 1:** From the RH definition, isolate $p_v$:

$$p_v = \phi \cdot p_{vs}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the humidity ratio equation:

$$\omega = 0.622 \, \frac{\phi \, p_{vs}}{P - \phi \, p_{vs}}$$

**Step 3:** This is the working formula used universally on the **psychrometric chart** to locate a state point given $(\phi, T_{db}, P)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — From Dew Point to RH

**Given:** Dew point temperature $T_{dp}$, dry-bulb temperature $T_{db}$, atmospheric pressure $P$.

**Step 1:** At dew point, the air is saturated, so the actual vapor pressure equals the saturation pressure at $T_{dp}$:

$$p_v = p_{vs}(T_{dp})$$

**Step 2:** RH is defined using $p_{vs}$ evaluated at the **dry-bulb temperature**:

$$\phi = \frac{p_{vs}(T_{dp})}{p_{vs}(T_{db})}$$

> [!NOTE]
