# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q:** Define **Bypass Factor (BPF)** of a cooling coil. State its range for a good coil design.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> The Bypass Factor (BPF) of a cooling coil is defined as the ratio of the difference between the leaving air temperature and the Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) temperature to the difference between the entering air temperature and the ADP temperature.
>
> $$\text{BPF} = \frac{T_2 - T_{\text{ADP}}}{T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}}}$$
>
> For a well-designed cooling coil, the BPF typically ranges between **0.05 and 0.30**. A lower BPF indicates better thermal contact between the air and the coil surface, leading to more effective cooling and dehumidification.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q:** What is **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)**? How is it located on the psychrometric chart?

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> The Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) is the temperature of the air at the surface of the cooling coil. It is the temperature to which the air would be cooled if it came into perfect thermal contact with the coil surface (i.e., with zero bypass).
>
> **Location on Psychrometric Chart:** The ADP lies on the **saturation curve (100% RH line)** of the psychrometric chart. It is determined by drawing the **RSHF (Room Sensible Heat Factor) line** from the room condition point. The point where this line intersects the saturation curve is the ADP.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q:** A simple air conditioning system is designed for a lecture hall. The system has the following data:
- Outdoor air: $38^\circ C$ DBT, $26^\circ C$ WBT
- Room air: $26^\circ C$ DBT, $50\%$ RH
- Supply air: $18^\circ C$ DBT, $90\%$ RH
- Outdoor air quantity: $30\%$ of supply air
- Air quantity supplied: $1.2 \ \text{kg/s}$ of dry air

**Determine:**
- **(a)** [7 Marks] The mixed air condition (DBT and humidity ratio), the cooling load in kW (sensible, latent, and total), and the RSHF.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] The Bypass Factor (BPF) of the cooling coil, and the Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) using the psychrometric chart method. Comment on the coil effectiveness.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

---

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Step 1: Humidity Ratios from Chart Data**

From standard psychrometric tables or chart:
- Outdoor air (38°C DBT, 26°C WBT): $\omega_o = 0.0148 \ \text{kg/kg DA}$
- Room air (26°C DBT, 50% RH): $\omega_r = 0.01075 \ \text{kg/kg DA}$

**Step 2: Mixed Air (MA) Calculation**

Basis: 1 kg of supply air (dry air basis).

$$\dot{m}_o = 0.30, \quad \dot{m}_r = 0.70$$

$$T_{\text{MA}} = (0.70)(26) + (0.30)(38) = 18.2 + 11.4 = 29.6^\circ C$$

$$\omega_{\text{MA}} = (0.70)(0.01075) + (0.30)(0.0148) = 0.007525 + 0.00444 = 0.01197 \ \text{kg/kg DA}$$

**Step 3: Sensible Heat Load (SH)**

Room sensible heat: from room condition to supply condition
$$\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_s \, c_p \, (T_r - T_s) = 1.2 \times 1.005 \times (26 - 18)$$
$$\dot{Q}_s = 1.2 \times 1.005 \times 8 = 9.648 \ \text{kW}$$

**Step 4: Latent Heat Load (LH)**

$$\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m}_s \, h_{fg} \, (\omega_r - \omega_s)$$

For supply air at 18°C DBT, 90% RH:
- $p_{ws} = 2.064 \ \text{kPa}$, $p_w = 1.857 \ \text{kPa}$
- $\omega_s = 0.622 \times \dfrac{1.857}{101.325 - 1.857} = 0.01163 \ \text{kg/kg DA}$

$$\dot{Q}_L = 1.2 \times 2501 \times (0.01075 - 0.01163) = 1.2 \times 2501 \times (-0.00088)$$

Since $\omega_s > \omega_r$, there is actually humidification here. The latent heat is **negative**, indicating the coil is adding moisture (this is uncommon and indicates the supply RH is too high).

$$\dot{Q}_L = -2.641 \ \text{kW}$$

**Step 5: Total Heat and RSHF**

$$\dot{Q}_T = \dot{Q}_s + \dot{Q}_L = 9.648 + (-2.641) = 7.007 \ \text{kW}$$

$$\text{RSHF} = \frac{\dot{Q}_s}{\dot{Q}_T} = \frac{9.648}{7.007} = 1.377$$

> [!WARNING]
> **RSHF > 1** indicates that the supply air is being **humidified** (latent heat is negative). This is unusual for a cooling application and suggests the supply RH should be reduced.

---

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1: Locate MA point on chart** at $T_{\text{MA}} = 29.6^\circ C$, $\omega_{\text{MA}} = 0.01197 \ \text{kg/kg DA}$.

**Step 2: Draw the RSHF line** from Room point (26°C DBT, 50% RH) with slope $= \dfrac{c_p}{h_{fg} \cdot \text{RSHF}}$.

Using chart, the RSHF line intersects the saturation curve at approximately **ADP ≈ 12.4°C**.

**Step 3: Calculate BPF**

$$\text{BPF} = \frac{T_{\text{MA}} - T_s}{T_{\text{MA}} - T_{\text{ADP}}} = \frac{29.6 - 18.0}{29.6 - 12.4} = \frac{11.6}{17.2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BPF} = 0.674}$$

**Step 4: Contact Factor and Coil Effectiveness**

$$\text{CF} = 1 - 0.674 = 0.326$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Comment:** A BPF of 0.674 is **too high** for an efficient cooling coil. The ideal BPF should be in the range **0.05 – 0.30**. The coil is underperforming, possibly due to insufficient coil surface area, low fin density, or inadequate air velocity across the coil. Recommendations include increasing the number of rows, increasing fins per inch (FPI), or reducing face velocity.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q:** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the working of a **simple summer air conditioning system** for a hot and humid climate. Discuss the role of the **cooling coil, blower, filter, and expansion valve** in the system.

**Solve:**
- **(a)** [7 Marks] Draw the schematic and explain each component with its function. Sketch the psychrometric process on the chart.
- **(b)** [7 Marks] A simple AC system has a cooling coil with BPF = 0.20. The entering air is at $32^\circ C$ DBT, $24^\circ C$ WBT, and the apparatus dew point is $10^\circ C$. Calculate: (i) the temperature of air leaving the coil, (ii) the contact factor, and (iii) the total heat removed per kg of dry air if the supply air humidity ratio is $0.0085 \ \text{kg/kg DA}$.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

---

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Schematic Explanation:**

A simple summer air conditioning system for hot-humid climate (e.g., Kerala) consists of:

1. **Return Air Grille:** Recirculates air from the conditioned space.
2. **Filter:** Removes dust, pollen, and contaminants.
3. **Blower (Fan):** Provides the necessary air velocity (typically 2.5 m/s across the coil).
4. **Cooling Coil (Direct Expansion - DX type):** Refrigerant evaporates inside, absorbing heat and moisture from the air. Surface temperature is the ADP.
5. **Compressor (Outdoor):** Compresses refrigerant vapor; raises pressure and temperature.
6. **Condenser Coil (Outdoor):** Rejects heat to ambient air using a condenser fan.
7. **Expansion Valve / Capillary Tube:** Throttles the liquid refrigerant to create a low-pressure, low-temperature mixture.
8. **Supply Air Grille/Duct:** Distributes conditioned air uniformly.

**Psychrometric Process:**

On the psychrometric chart:
- **Point O:** Outdoor air (32°C DBT, 24°C WBT).
- **Point M:** Mixed air (combination of return + outdoor air).
- **Point S:** Supply air (cooled, dehumidified).
- **Point ADP:** On saturation curve, where RSHF line meets.
- **Process M → S:** Straight line representing simultaneous sensible and latent cooling.

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Given:**
- BPF = 0.20
- $T_1 = 32^\circ C$ (entering DBT)
- $T_{\text{ADP}} = 10^\circ C$
- $\omega_s = 0.0085 \ \text{kg/kg DA}$

**Step (i): Temperature of air leaving the coil ($T_2$)**

$$\text{BPF} = \frac{T_2 - T_{\text{ADP}}}{T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}}}$$

$$0.20 = \frac{T_2 - 10}{32 - 10} = \frac{T_2 - 10}{22}$$

$$T_2 - 10 = 0.20 \times 22 = 4.4$$

$$\boxed{T_2 = 14.4^\circ C}$$

**Step (ii): Contact Factor (CF)**

$$\text{CF} = 1 - \text{BPF} = 1 - 0.20 = \boxed{0.80}$$

**Step (iii): Total Heat Removed**

First, find the entering humidity ratio $\omega_1$ at $T_1 = 32^\circ C$, WBT $= 24^\circ C$ (from chart):
$$\omega_1 = 0.0152 \ \text{kg/kg DA}$$

Enthalpies (using chart approximation):
- $h_1 = 1.005(32) + 0.0152(2501 + 1.88 \times 32) = 32.16 + 38.93 = 71.09 \ \text{kJ/kg DA}$
- $h_2 = 1.005(14.4) + 0.0085(2501 + 1.88 \times 14.4) = 14.47 + 21.49 = 35.96 \ \text{kJ/kg DA}$

$$\Delta h = h_1 - h_2 = 71.09 - 35.96 = \boxed{35.13 \ \text{kJ/kg DA}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing BPF and RSHF:** BPF applies to the **cooling coil**; RSHF applies to the **room load**. These are NOT the same value. Drawing an RSHF line and calling it the "coil process line" will result in **3-4 marks deduction**.
>
> 2. **Incorrect mixing calculations:** Forgetting that mass fractions (not volume fractions) must be used for $\omega$ and $h$ mixing. Outdoor air fraction must be on a mass basis.
>
> 3. **Wrong humidity ratio calculation:** Using relative humidity values directly in the $\omega$ formula instead of saturation pressure values. Always use $p_w = \text{RH} \times p_{ws}$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Always write units with numerical answers. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
>
> 5. **Not drawing the psychrometric chart process:** Even if a calculation is correct, KTU expects a clear labeled diagram of all four points (O, R, M, S) with the RSHF line and the coil process line clearly indicated.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Simple AC System = Recirculating air + DX cooling coil + refrigeration cycle**.
- **Four key states on psychrometric chart:** Outdoor (O), Room (R), Mixed (M), Supply (S).
- **Bypass Factor (BPF)** = $(T_2 - T_{\text{ADP}}) / (T_1 - T_{\text{ADP}})$; range = **0.05 – 0.30** for good coils.
- **Contact Factor (CF)** = $1 - \text{BPF}$; represents cooling coil effectiveness.
- **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** lies on the **saturation curve** at the intersection of the **RSHF line** drawn from the room point.
- **RSHF** = RSH / (RSH + RLH); slope on chart = $c_p / (h_{fg} \cdot \text{RSHF})$.
- **SHF** = SH / (SH + LH) for coil performance; **RSHF** is for the room.
- **Mixed air temperature** uses weighted average: $T_{\text{MA}} = (\dot{m}_o T_o + \dot{m}_r T_r) / \dot{m}_{\text{total}}$.
- **For Monsoon / Humid regions:** Cooling + Dehumidification + **Reheat** cycle is needed to maintain supply temperature.
- **Winter AC system:** Predominantly heating + humidification (no cooling coil active).
- **Sensible heat removed:** $\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$; **Latent heat:** $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} h_{fg} \Delta \omega$.
- **Air quantity formula:** $\dot{V} = \dot{Q}_s / (\rho c_p \Delta T)$ for duct sizing.
- **Standard comfort envelope:** $22-26^\circ C$ DBT, $40-60\%$ RH, $0.1-0.5 \ m/s$ air velocity.
- **ASHRAE standard ventilation:** $7.5-9 \ \text{L/s per person**}$ of fresh outside air.
- **Common refrigerant used in simple AC systems:** R-22 (legacy), R-410A, R-32 (modern eco-friendly).
- **BPF depends on:** coil rows, fins per inch (FPI), face velocity, and air distribution uniformity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Summer air-conditioning system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Summer air-conditioning system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Summer Air-Conditioning System** (also referred to as *Comfort Air-Conditioning System* or *Warm-Weather Air-Conditioning System*) is a closed-loop thermal management system designed to maintain indoor environmental conditions within the human comfort envelope during hot and humid ambient conditions. The system simultaneously performs the functions of **temperature reduction (sensible cooling), humidity removal (latent cooling/dehumidification), air circulation, air filtration, and ventilation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus-Aligned):**
> *"A summer air-conditioning system is one which maintains the desired internal conditions of temperature, humidity, velocity and purity of air inside a building against the external conditions of high temperature and high relative humidity that exist during summer months."*

The reference indoor design conditions typically maintained are:

| Parameter | Standard Comfort Range |
|---|---|
| Inside Dry Bulb Temperature | **24 °C to 27 °C** |
| Inside Relative Humidity | **40% to 50%** |
| Inside Air Velocity | **5 to 10 m/min (≈ 0.083 to 0.167 m/s)** |
| Air Purity | Dust-free, CO₂ < 800 ppm |
| Effective Temperature | ~**25 °C** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Cool Bathroom After a Hot Shower"**
> Imagine stepping out of a hot shower on a humid summer day. Your bathroom mirror is fogged (high humidity), the air feels heavy, and you're sticky. Now picture a system that quietly sucks that muggy air out, passes it through a block of ice-cold metal pipes, and pushes the resulting cool, dry air back into the room. That, in essence, is exactly what a **Summer Air-Conditioning System** does at a building scale. The cooling coil is the "ice block," the blower is the "quiet fan," and the chilled water (or refrigerant) inside the coil is the "invisible cold shower" for the air.

The system can be visualized as a **5-task machine**:

- **Task 1 — Sensible Cooling:** Lower the dry-bulb temperature of the air.
- **Task 2 — Dehumidification (Latent Cooling):** Condense and remove water vapour from the air.
- **Task 3 — Filtration:** Trap dust, pollen, and contaminants.
- **Task 4 — Circulation:** Move air uniformly through the conditioned space.
- **Task 5 — Ventilation:** Introduce a controlled fraction of fresh outside air.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** A Summer AC system = **"Cool + Dry + Clean + Moving + Fresh"** air — remember the 5-Cs: *Cooling, Dehumidification, Cleaning, Circulation, Controlled Fresh Air*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Components of a Summer AC System

A standard **centralized summer air-conditioning plant** consists of the following components in series along the air path:

1. **Outdoor Air Intake (Fresh Air Damper):** Introduces a controlled volume of ventilation air.
2. **Return Air Mixing Section:** Where outdoor air blends with recirculated return air from the conditioned space.
3. **Filter Section:** Removes dust and particulate matter (typically dry or viscous filters).
4. **Cooling Coil (Evaporator of the Refrigeration Cycle):** Performs simultaneous sensible and latent cooling. Chilled water or refrigerant flows inside tubes; air passes over them.
5. **Blower / Fan:** Provides the motive force to drive air through ducts and overcome static pressure.
6. **Humidifier / Reheater (Optional):** In precision applications, reheat coils restore temperature without adding moisture to achieve desired RH.
7. **Supply Duct + Diffusers:** Distribute conditioned air to the conditioned space.
8. **Return Grille + Return Duct:** Recirculate room air back to the AHU (Air Handling Unit).
9. **Exhaust Damper:** Vitiate stale air to maintain.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Flow

The air follows a deterministic sequence through the system:

- **Step 1 (Ventilation):** Outside air (O.A.) at state **0** — hot, humid, contaminated — enters via the fresh-air damper.
- **Step 2 (Mixing):** Return air (R.A.) from the room at state **4** mixes with O.A. at state **0** to give a **Mixed Air** at state **M**.
- **Step 3 (Filtration):** Air at state **M** is cleaned; no psychrometric change occurs (filtration is non-thermal).
- **Step 4 (Cooling & Dehumidification):** Air at state **M** is passed over the cooling coil. It leaves at state **2** (supply air state), with reduced DBT and lower specific humidity.
- **Step 5 (Distribution):** Supply air at state **2** enters the conditioned space.
- **Step 6 (Room Heat Gain):** Inside the room, the air absorbs sensible heat (Qₛ) and latent heat (Qₗ) from occupants, equipment, walls, and infiltration. It rises to state **4** (return air state).
- **Step 7 (Return & Recirculate):** A portion of return air is exhausted (typically to balance fresh-air intake), while the rest re-enters the AHU.

### 2.3 Bypass Factor (BPF) — The Core Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bypass Factor (BPF)** is defined as the ratio of the difference between leaving and coil surface temperature to the difference between entering and coil surface temperature. It quantifies the *inefficiency* of a real cooling coil.

Mathematically, for sensible cooling:

$$
\text{BPF} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}
$$

Where:
- $T_1$ = Temperature of cooling coil surface (≈ **Apparatus Dew Point, ADP**).
- $T_2$ = Temperature of air **leaving** the coil.
- $T_3$ = Temperature of air **entering** the coil.

The **Contact Factor** is the complement:

$$
\text{Contact Factor (CF)} = 1 - \text{BPF}
$$

### 2.4 Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)

The **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** is the saturation temperature corresponding to the surface temperature of the cooling coil, assuming all entering air contacted the coil long enough to reach saturation along a straight-line path on the psychrometric chart.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ADP matters:** Every cooling coil has a characteristic BPF (typically 0.05 to 0.30). Given the entering state and coil surface temperature, ADP is what governs the leaving state of air.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{BPF} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}$ | Bypass Factor (DBT basis) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{CF} = 1 - \text{BPF}$ | Contact Factor | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $Q_s = \dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_3 - T_2)$ | Sensible Heat Removed | kW |
| 4 | $Q_L = \dot{m} \, (h_3 - h_2)$ | Latent Heat Removed | kW |
| 5 | $Q_t = \dot{m} \, (h_3 - h_2)$ | Total Heat Removed (Eq. of Qₗ for air enthalpy) | kW |
| 6 | $Q_{room,s} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_4 - T_2)$ | Sensible Room Heat Gain | kW |
| 7 | $Q_{room,L} = \dot{m} \, (h_4 - h_2)$ | Latent Room Heat Gain | kW |
| 8 | $\text{ADP} = T_1$ | Coil Surface / Saturation Temperature | °C |
| 9 | $W_2 = \text{BPF} \cdot W_3 + (1-\text{BPF}) \cdot W_s$ | Leaving humidity ratio | kg/kg d.a. |
| 10 | $\text{SHF} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_L}$ | Sensible Heat Factor | Dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reality:** A well-designed cooling coil has BPF ≤ 0.10. A BPF of 0.30 or higher indicates coil fouling or insufficient coil surface area.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

Summer AC systems are deployed in:

- **Commercial Buildings:** Offices, malls, hotels, hospitals.
- **Residential HVAC:** Window AC, split AC, central plant.
- **Data Centres & Server Rooms** (precision cooling variants).
- **Educational & Healthcare Facilities** under KTU-relevant built-environment engineering.
- **Industrial Operator Cabins** (textile mills, foundry operator rooms).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bypass Factor (BPF) from First Principles

Consider air entering a cooling coil at temperature $T_3$ and leaving at temperature $T_2$. Let the coil surface (ADP) temperature be $T_1$.

**Assumption:** Air leaving the coil lies on a straight line on the psychrometric chart between the entering condition (3) and the coil surface saturation point (1). This is the classical **straight-line cooling-dehumidification assumption**.

**Step 1:** The temperature drop of air that actually *contacts* the coil is:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{actual}} = T_3 - T_2
$$

**Step 2:** The maximum possible temperature drop (if BPF = 0, i.e., every molecule of air reached the coil surface) is:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{max}} = T_3 - T_1
$$

**Step 3:** The fraction of air that did *not* contact the coil (bypassed) retains its original temperature. The temperature of leaving air is a weighted average of bypassed air ($T_3$) and contacted air ($T_1$):

$$
T_2 = (\text{BPF}) \cdot T_3 + (1 - \text{BPF}) \cdot T_1
$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging the equation to isolate BPF:

$$
T_2 = \text{BPF} \cdot T_3 + T_1 - \text{BPF} \cdot T_1
$$

$$
T_2 - T_1 = \text{BPF} \cdot (T_3 - T_1)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{BPF} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}}
$$

This is the canonical KTU formula for BPF on a DBT basis.

### 3.2 Derivation of Leaving Specific Humidity

Using the same straight-line assumption applied to humidity ratio:

$$
W_2 = (\text{BPF}) \cdot W_3 + (1 - \text{BPF}) \cdot W_{s}(T_1)
$$

Where $W_{s}(T_1)$ is the saturation humidity ratio at the ADP temperature.

**Derivation:** Let $W_1 = W_s(T_1)$ (saturation at coil surface). Then by analogy with temperature:

$$
W_2 - W_1 = \text{BPF} \cdot (W_3 - W_1)
$$

$$
W_2 = \text{BPF} \cdot W_3 + W_1 - \text{BPF} \cdot W_1
$$

$$
W_2 = \text{BPF} \cdot W_3 + (1 - \text{BPF}) \cdot W_s(T_1)
$$

This is critical for computing the **dehumidified moisture content** and hence the latent cooling load.

### 3.3 Derivation of Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) for Room

The room Sensible Heat Factor is the ratio of sensible to total heat absorbed by the air in the conditioned space:

$$
\text{SHF}_{\text{room}} = \frac{Q_{s,\text{room}}}{Q_{s,\text{room}} + Q_{L,\text{room}}} = \frac{c_p (T_4 - T_2)}{(h_4 - h_2)}
$$

For approximate calculations where latent heat ≈ $h_{fg} \cdot (W_4 - W_2)$:

$$
\text{SHF} \approx \frac{c_p (T_4 - T_2)}{c_p (T_4 - T_2) + h_{fg}(W_4 - W_2)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For typical comfort applications in Kerala's tropical climate:** SHF is usually between **0.65 and 0.85**. This ratio dictates whether a single cooling coil suffices or whether a separate dehumidifier + reheat coil is required.

### 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Worked Problem)

**Problem:** Outside air at 35 °C DBT and 60% RH (state 0) is mixed with return air at 26 °C DBT and 50% RH (state 4) in the ratio 1:3 (OA:RA by mass). The mixed air (state M) is passed over a cooling coil with ADP at 10 °C. The coil has a bypass factor of 0.15. Determine:

1. The condition of supply air (state 2).
2. The total cooling load per kg of dry air.
3. The room SHF.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Find enthalpy at state 0 and state 4 using $h = 1.005 T + W(2501 + 1.88 T)$ kJ/kg d.a.

$$
W_0 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_a - P_v}
$$

At 35 °C, $P_{s,0} = 5.628$ kPa (from steam tables); $P_v = 0.60 \times 5.628 = 3.377$ kPa.

$$
W_0 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.377}{101.325 - 3.377} = 0.0214 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

$$
h_0 = 1.005(35) + 0.0214[2501 + 1.88(35)] = 35.18 + 54.91 = 90.09 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

At 26 °C, 50% RH: $P_{s,4} = 3.36$ kPa; $P_v = 1.68$ kPa.

$$
W_4 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.68}{101.325 - 1.68} = 0.01051 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

$$
h_4 = 1.005(26) + 0.01051[2501 + 1.88(26)] = 26.13 + 26.79 = 52.92 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Mass-weighted mixing (OA:RA = 1:3, so OA fraction = 0.25):

$$
T_M = 0.25(35) + 0.75(26) = 8.75 + 19.5 = 28.25 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
W_M = 0.25(0.0214) + 0.75(0.01051) = 0.00535 + 0.00788 = 0.01323 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

$$
h_M = 0.25(90.09) + 0.75(52.92) = 22.52 + 39.69 = 62.21 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply BPF to find supply air state:

$$
T_2 = T_1 + \text{BPF}(T_M - T_1) = 10 + 0.15(28.25 - 10) = 10 + 2.7375 = 12.74 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
W_2 = W_{s}(10) + \text{BPF}[W_M - W_{s}(10)]
$$

$W_s(10) = 0.00766$ kg/kg d.a.

$$
W_2 = 0.00766 + 0.15(0.01323 - 0.00766) = 0.00766 + 0.000836 = 0.00850 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

$$
h_2 = 1.005(12.74) + 0.00850[2501 + 1.88(12.74)] = 12.80 + 21.46 = 34.26 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Total cooling load per kg of dry air:

$$
Q_t = h_M - h_2 = 62.21 - 34.26 = 27.95 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}
$$

**Step 5:** Sensible and latent breakdown:

$$
q_s = c_p (T_M - T_2) = 1.005(28.25 - 12.74) = 15.58 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}
$$

$$
q_L = h_{fg}(W_M - W_2) = 2501(0.01323 - 0.00850) = 11.83 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}
$$

**Verification:** $q_s + q_L = 15.58 + 11.83 = 27.41$ ≈ $Q_t = 27.95$ (minor rounding difference). ✓

**Step 6:** Room SHF:

$$
\text{SHF} = \frac{q_s}{q_s + q_L} = \frac{15.58}{27.41} = 0.568
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Condition | Recommendation for System Design |
|---|---|
| SHF > 0.80 | Simple cooling coil sufficient |
| SHF = 0.65 – 0.80 | Cooling coil + reheat coil needed |
| SHF < 0.65 | Dedicated dehumidifier with chemical absorption (e.g., LiBr) |
| BPF > 0.20 | Increase coil rows or face velocity; service filters |
| Outside W > 0.018 kg/kg (Kerala monsoon) | Pre-cooler / desiccant wheel recommended |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Psychrometric Process Diagram (Mermaid Block Schematic)

The following Mermaid block represents the **thermodynamic state transitions** of air as it moves through a typical summer air-conditioning system. State numbers are consistent with the derivations in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OA["State 0: Outside Air - 35 C DBT, 60 percent RH"] --> MX
    RA["State 4: Return Air - 26 C DBT, 50 percent RH"] --> MX
    MX["State M: Mixed Air - 28.25 C, W 0.0132"] --> FLT["Filter Section - No Psychrometric Change"]
    FLT --> CC["Cooling Coil - ADP at 10 C"]
    CC --> SUP["State 2: Supply Air - 12.74 C, W 0.0085"]
    SUP --> RM["Conditioned Room - Sensible and Latent Heat Pickup"]
    RM --> RA
    RA --> EXH["Exhaust Damper - Vitiate to Atmosphere"]
    SUP -. Supply Duct .-> RM
    RM -. Return Duct .-> RA
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of a Central Summer AC Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AHU["Air Handling Unit AHU"]
        D1["Fresh Air Damper"] --> D2["Return Air Damper"]
        D2 --> D3["Mixing Chamber"]
        D3 --> D4["Pre Filter"]
        D4 --> D5["Fine Filter"]
        D5 --> D6["Cooling Coil Chilled Water"]
        D6 --> D7["Blower Centrifugal Fan"]
        D7 --> D8["Optional Reheat Coil"]
    end
    D8 --> D9["Supply Duct"]
    D9 --> D10["Conditioned Room Space"]
    D10 --> D11["Return Grille"]
    D11 --> D12["Return Duct"]
    D12 --> D2
    D1 -. Exhaust Balance .-> EXT["Exhaust to Atmosphere"]
    D11 -. Exhaust Path .-> EXT
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Refrigeration Cycle Coupled with AHU

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMP["Compressor - Low Pressure Vapor to High Pressure Vapor"] --> CND["Condenser - Rejects Heat to Atmosphere"]
    CND --> EXV["Expansion Valve - Throttles to Low Pressure"]
    EXV --> EVP["Evaporator - Same as Cooling Coil of AHU"]
    EVP --> CMP
    EVP -. Cold refrigerant .-> AHU_AIR["Conditioned Air Stream"]
    AHU_AIR -. Warm return air .-> EVP
    CND -. Hot refrigerant .-> COOL_TWR["Cooling Tower"]
    COOL_TWR -. Heat rejected .-> ATM["Atmosphere"]
```

### 4.4 BPF Geometric Interpretation (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CHART["On Psychrometric Chart"]
        P3["Point 3 - Entering Air"]
        P1["Point 1 - ADP on Saturation Curve"]
        P2["Point 2 - Leaving Air - On Line 3 to 1"]
        P3 --> P2
        P2 --> P1
    end
    T3["T3 = DBT at Point 3"] -.- P3
    T1["T1 = DBT at Point 1 ADP"] -.- P1
    T2["T2 = DBT at Point 2"] -.- P2
    BPF["BPF = Distance 3 to 2 divided by Distance 3 to 1"] -.- P3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Rule:** On the psychrometric chart, the leaving state **always** lies on the straight line joining the entering state and the ADP, at a fraction equal to BPF from ADP. A larger BPF shifts the leaving point toward the entering point.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define **Bypass Factor (BPF)** of a cooling coil. State its typical range for a well-designed coil.

**Model Answer:**
Bypass Factor is defined as the ratio of the difference between the temperatures of air leaving and entering the cooling coil surface to the difference between the temperatures of air entering the coil and the coil surface (ADP).

$$
\text{BPF} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}
$$

It is dimensionless. For a properly designed and maintained cooling coil, BPF typically ranges between **0.05 and 0.15**. *(3 Marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
List the **four primary functions** performed by a summer air-conditioning system.

**Model Answer:**
A summer air-conditioning system performs the following four primary functions:

1. **Temperature Control (Sensible Cooling):** Reducing the dry-bulb temperature of indoor air.
2. **Humidity Control (Latent Cooling):** Dehumidification by condensing moisture from the air.
3. **Air Circulation:** Distributing conditioned air uniformly throughout the space.
4. **Air Filtration and Ventilation:** Removing dust and contaminants while introducing a controlled quantity of fresh outdoor air. *(3 Marks)*

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat psychrometric chart, describe the **complete summer air-conditioning cycle** and mark all state points clearly. Discuss the role of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Air at 32 °C DBT and 70% RH is passed through a cooling coil whose surface temperature is 12 °C. The bypass factor is 0.18. Determine:
   1. Temperature and humidity ratio of leaving air.
   2. Sensible and latent heat removed per kg of dry air.
   3. ADP verification. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

A summer air-conditioning system operates between **four principal states** on the psychrometric chart:

- **State 0** → Fresh outside air.
- **State M** → Mixed air (outside + return).
- **State 2** → Supply air after cooling coil.
- **State 4** → Return air leaving the conditioned space.

**Process Path (3 → 2 → 4):**

$$
3 \xrightarrow{\text{Cooling + Dehumidification}} 2 \xrightarrow{\text{Room Heat Gain}} 4
$$

**Role of Each Component:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Fresh Air Damper | Introduces outside air for ventilation |
| Mixing Chamber | Blends O.A. and R.A. for energy economy |
| Filter | Removes dust and particulates |
| Cooling Coil | Performs sensible and latent cooling |
| Blower | Drives air through ducts |
| Supply Ducts + Diffusers | Even distribution of conditioned air |
| Return Grille + Duct | Recirculation of room air |
| Exhaust Damper | Maintains fresh air balance |

*(7 Marks: 2 for description, 2 for state-point identification, 3 for component roles)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $T_3 = 32$ °C, $\phi_3 = 70\%$
- Coil surface temp $T_1 = 12$ °C
- BPF = 0.18

**Step 1:** Find saturation pressure at 32 °C: $P_{s,3} = 4.76$ kPa (from steam tables).
$P_{v,3} = 0.70 \times 4.76 = 3.332$ kPa.

$$
W_3 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.332}{101.325 - 3.332} = 0.02112 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply BPF to find $T_2$:

$$
T_2 = T_1 + \text{BPF}(T_3 - T_1) = 12 + 0.18(32 - 12) = 12 + 3.6 = 15.6 \text{ °C}
$$

**Step 3:** Find leaving humidity ratio:

$W_s(T_1) = W_s(12)$ = 0.01065 kg/kg d.a. (from steam tables at 12 °C).

$$
W_2 = 0.01065 + 0.18(0.02112 - 0.01065) = 0.01065 + 0.00188 = 0.01253 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

**Step 4:** Sensible heat removed:

$$
q_s = c_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (32 - 15.6) = 16.48 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}
$$

**Step 5:** Latent heat removed:

$$
q_L = h_{fg} (W_3 - W_2) = 2501 \times (0.02112 - 0.01253) = 21.47 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}
$$

**Step 6:** ADP Verification:
ADP = Coil surface temperature = 12 °C (given). The leaving state lies on the straight line between (3) and (1), confirmed by the linear interpolation above. ✓

**Final Results:**
- $T_2 = 15.6$ °C, $W_2 = 0.01253$ kg/kg d.a.
- $q_s = 16.48$ kJ/kg d.a., $q_L = 21.47$ kJ/kg d.a.
- Total heat removed: $q_t = 37.95$ kJ/kg d.a.
- SHF = $16.48 / 37.95 = 0.434$

*(7 Marks: 2 for setup, 3 for computation, 2 for ADP verification + answer)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** (Option 2 — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** and **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)**. Explain the **straight-line law** of the cooling-dehumidification process on the psychrometric chart. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Outside air at 38 °C DBT and 55% RH is mixed with return air at 25 °C DBT and 50% RH in the volume ratio 2:3 (OA:RA). The mixed air passes over a coil with ADP at 14 °C and BPF = 0.20. Calculate the cooling load in TR per kg/s of mixed air. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Apparatus Dew Point (ADP):**
ADP is the saturation temperature corresponding to the surface temperature of the cooling coil, determined by the intersection of the straight line through the entering and leaving air states with the saturation curve on the psychrometric chart. *(2 Marks)*

**Sensible Heat Factor (SHF):**
SHF is the ratio of sensible heat to total heat (sensible + latent) involved in a process. For a conditioned space:

$$
\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_L}
$$

*(2 Marks)*

**Straight-Line Law:**
According to the straight-line law of cooling-dehumidification, the path followed by air during its passage over a cooling coil is approximately a straight line on the psychrometric chart between the entering state and the ADP point (on the saturation curve). This assumption allows direct interpolation for intermediate states using the BPF. *(3 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- State 0: $T_0 = 38$ °C, $\phi_0 = 55\%$
- State 4: $T_4 = 25$ °C, $\phi_4 = 50\%$
- Mix ratio OA:RA = 2:3 (so OA = 0.40, RA = 0.60)
- ADP $T_1 = 14$ °C, BPF = 0.20

**Step 1:** Find properties at State 0:

$P_{s,38} = 6.63$ kPa; $P_{v,0} = 0.55 \times 6.63 = 3.647$ kPa.

$$
W_0 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.647}{101.325 - 3.647} = 0.02322 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

$$
h_0 = 1.005(38) + 0.02322[2501 + 1.88(38)] = 38.19 + 59.66 = 97.85 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2:** Properties at State 4:

$P_{s,25} = 3.169$ kPa; $P_{v,4} = 0.50 \times 3.169 = 1.585$ kPa.

$$
W_4 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.585}{101.325 - 1.585} = 0.00990 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

$$
h_4 = 1.005(25) + 0.00990[2501 + 1.88(25)] = 25.13 + 25.22 = 50.35 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Mix state M (mass-weighted):

$$
T_M = 0.40(38) + 0.60(25) = 15.2 + 15.0 = 30.2 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
W_M = 0.40(0.02322) + 0.60(0.00990) = 0.00929 + 0.00594 = 0.01523 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

$$
h_M = 0.40(97.85) + 0.60(50.35) = 39.14 + 30.21 = 69.35 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply BPF to find State 2:

$$
T_2 = T_1 + \text{BPF}(T_M - T_1) = 14 + 0.20(30.2 - 14) = 14 + 3.24 = 17.24 \text{ °C}
$$

$W_s(14) = 0.01207$ kg/kg d.a.

$$
W_2 = 0.01207 + 0.20(0.01523 - 0.01207) = 0.01207 + 0.000632 = 0.01270 \text{ kg/kg d.a.}
$$

$$
h_2 = 1.005(17.24) + 0.01270[2501 + 1.88(17.24)] = 17.33 + 32.18 = 49.51 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 5:** Cooling load per kg of dry air:

$$
q_t = h_M - h_2 = 69.35 - 49.51 = 19.84 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to TR per kg/s:

1 TR = 3.517 kW = 3.517 kJ/s.

For 1 kg/s of dry air:

$$
Q_t = 19.84 \text{ kJ/s} = \frac{19.84}{3.517} = 5.642 \text{ TR per kg/s}
$$

**Final Answer:** Cooling load ≈ **5.64 TR per kg/s of mixed air**.

*(7 Marks: 2 for property tables, 2 for mixing, 2 for BPF application, 1 for unit conversion)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing Formula Confusion:** Students often forget that mass-weighted averaging requires **mass or weight** ratios, not volume ratios — unless air density is constant (≈ 1.2 kg/m³ at standard conditions, where the two are nearly equal). Always state your assumption.
> 2. **BPF Direction Sign Error:** When leaving temperature is *lower* than entering temperature (cooling), the formula $T_2 = T_1 + \text{BPF}(T_3 - T_1)$ is correct. Reversing signs is the #1 source of zero marks.
> 3. **Forgetting $h_{fg}$ in Latent Heat:** Many students use $c_p \Delta T$ for both sensible and latent calculations, leading to wrong SHF values.
> 4. **Not Converting to TR:** KTU examiners **always deduct marks** if the final answer is left in kJ/kg without converting to TR or kW.
> 5. **Ignoring Ventilation Load:** Sometimes QL includes latent load from fresh air — students frequently compute room load only and miss outdoor air latent contribution.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Summer Air-Conditioning System:**

- ☐ A summer AC system performs **5 functions:** Sensible Cooling, Dehumidification, Circulation, Filtration, and Ventilation.
- ☐ The **psychrometric cycle** involves 4 states: **Outside (0), Mixed (M), Supply (2), Return (4)**.
- ☐ **Bypass Factor (BPF)** is dimensionless and ranges from **0 to 1**; ideal = 0, completely bypassed = 1.
- ☐ **Contact Factor = 1 − BPF** represents the actual fraction of air that participated in heat exchange.
- ☐ **ADP** is the coil surface temperature corresponding to the saturation curve intersection of the straight-line process path.
- ☐ The **straight-line law** states that air leaves the coil on a straight line joining entry state to ADP on the psychrometric chart.
- ☐ Standard comfort conditions: **DBT 25 °C, RH 50%, Air velocity 5–10 m/min.**
- ☐ **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** = $Q_s / (Q_s + Q_L)$. If SHF < 0.65, use a **reheat coil**.
- ☐ **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 211 kJ/min.**
- ☐ Air enthalpy formula: $h = 1.005T + W(2501 + 1.88T)$ in kJ/kg d.a.
- ☐ In **Kerala's tropical climate**, OA conditions often reach **38 °C / 80% RH**, demanding coils with **ADP ≤ 12 °C**.
- ☐ Typical cooling coil BPF in KTU-evaluated design problems lies between **0.10 and 0.30**.
- ☐ Sensible cooling **does not change W**; only dehumidification does — but in a real coil, both occur simultaneously.
- ☐ Always compute **SHF of the room** to decide whether a single cooling coil suffices or reheat is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Winter air-conditioning system
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Winter air-conditioning system

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Winter Air-Conditioning System** is a year-round air-conditioning plant designed to operate in cold climatic conditions by simultaneously performing the dual functions of **heating** and **humidification** of the supply air, so as to maintain the desired indoor comfort conditions (typically $21^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $50\%$ RH or $\omega \approx 0.0085 \text{ kg/kg of dry air}$) during winter months.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Winter air-conditioning systems include both the **heated air-only** configurations (dry heating) and the **heated + humidified** configurations (wet heating). Students must be able to plot the process on the **psychrometric chart** and compute the heating coil load, humidification load, and mixture conditions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine stepping out of a cold, dry winter morning into a warm, gently misted bathroom after a hot shower. That pleasant warmth-with-moisture is exactly what a winter AC delivers to an entire building.

- The **heating coil** behaves like the hot shower — it warms the cold outdoor air.
- The **humidifier** behaves like the steam from the shower — it adds the missing moisture back into the air.
- The **bypass damper** is like partially opening a window on a cold day — it lets some of the cold outdoor air sneak in so the room is not over-heated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Standard Reference Comfort Conditions used for design are **$21^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $50\%$ RH** (also $W = 0.0085 \text{ kg/kg of dry air}$). All winter AC calculations are anchored to this design point.

### 1.3 Why Winter Air-Conditioning is Needed

- Outdoor air in winter is **cold and dry** (low DBT, very low specific humidity).
- If this cold, dry air is directly supplied to the conditioned space, occupants will feel **chilled** and suffer from **dry skin, throat irritation, and static shocks**.
- Therefore, the system must:
  1. **Heat** the air to the desired supply temperature.
  2. **Humidify** the air to the desired moisture level.

### 1.4 Essential Components of a Winter AC System

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Return / Mixing Dampers** | Mix return air with fresh outdoor air |
| **Filter** | Remove dust and contaminants from air |
| **Pre-heater (optional)** | Prevents freezing at humidifier and improves efficiency |
| **Heating Coil** | Sensibly heats the air using hot water, steam, or electricity |
| **Humidifier (Spray / Steam)** | Adds moisture to the air |
| **Bypass Damper** | Allows part of air to bypass the heating coil |
| **Fan (Blower)** | Circulates conditioned air through ductwork |
| **Return Air Duct** | Carries room air back to AHU |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Sequence in a Winter AC Plant

The sequence of operations (shown on the psychrometric chart from state 1 to state 3) is:

1. **Outside Air (State 1)** — Cold and dry outdoor air enters the system.
2. **Mixing with Return Air (State M)** — Fresh air mixes with recirculated room air for energy economy.
3. **Pre-heating (State 2')** — Optional pre-heating to avoid coil freezing.
4. **Heating (State 2 → State 3)** — Sensible heating across the heating coil (humidity ratio remains constant).
5. **Humidification (State 3 → State 4)** — Moisture is added by the humidifier (DBT may drop slightly).
6. **Supply to Room (State 4)** — Conditioned air delivered at design comfort conditions.

### 2.2 Process Path on Psychrometric Chart

- **Heating** : Horizontal line moving to the **right** (constant $\omega$, increasing DBT).
- **Humidification** : Line moving **upward and slightly left** (increasing $\omega$, DBT may drop due to evaporative cooling).

> [!TIP]
> If a **bypass factor (BPF)** exists, the actual leaving temperature is less than the heating coil surface temperature. This is the same BPF logic used for cooling coils — only the direction changes.

### 2.3 Detailed Component-Level Theory

#### 2.3.1 Heating Coil
- Acts as a sensible heater only.
- Operates along the constant humidity line ($\omega = \text{const}$).
- Leaving DBT lies between inlet DBT and coil surface temperature.
- Typical heating media: hot water, steam, electric resistance.

#### 2.3.2 Humidifier
Two principal types:
- **Spray-type (water) humidifier** — adds both moisture and may add evaporative cooling.
- **Steam injection humidifier** — adds pure moisture without temperature drop; preferred for clean applications.

#### 2.3.3 Bypass Damper Arrangement
- A fraction of air bypasses the heating coil.
- Improves control flexibility and reduces energy consumption during part-load conditions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible Heat Added | $Q_s = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ | $\text{kW}$ or $\text{kJ/s}$ |
| 2 | Humidification Load (Steam) | $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a \cdot (W_2 - W_1)$ | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| 3 | Bypass Factor | $\text{BPF} = \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Coil Surface Temperature | $T_3 = T_1 + \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{\text{BPF}}$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 5 | ADP / Leaving DBT after heating | $T_2 = T_1 + \text{BPF} \cdot (T_3 - T_1)$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 6 | SHF | $\text{SHF} = \dfrac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Mixture (Mixed Air) DBT | $T_m = \dfrac{\dot{m}_o T_o + \dot{m}_r T_r}{\dot{m}_o + \dot{m}_r}$ | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 8 | Mixture Humidity Ratio | $W_m = \dfrac{\dot{m}_o W_o + \dot{m}_r W_r}{\dot{m}_o + \dot{m}_r}$ | $\text{kg/kg da}$ |
| 9 | Specific Heat of Dry Air | $c_p = 1.005$ | $\text{kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$ |
| 10 | Latent Heat of Vaporization | $h_{fg} = 2501$ | $\text{kJ/kg}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to memorize for KTU Board Exam:** $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $h_{fg} = 2501 \text{ kJ/kg}$, design comfort = **$21^\circ\text{C}$ DBT, $50\%$ RH**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Heating Coil Leaving Temperature Using Bypass Factor

Consider air entering a heating coil at temperature $T_1$ (DBT) with humidity ratio $W_1$.

The coil surface is maintained at a uniform temperature $T_3$ (greater than $T_1$). Part of the air makes perfect contact with the coil surface and leaves at $T_3$, while the rest bypasses the coil unchanged.

Let $\text{BPF} =$ fraction of air that bypasses.

Mass balance on the leaving air:

$$
\dot{m}_a \, T_2 = \text{BPF} \cdot \dot{m}_a \, T_1 + (1 - \text{BPF}) \cdot \dot{m}_a \, T_3
$$

Dividing throughout by $\dot{m}_a$:

$$
T_2 = \text{BPF} \cdot T_1 + (1 - \text{BPF}) \cdot T_3
$$

$$
T_2 = T_1 - \text{BPF} \cdot T_1 + T_3 - \text{BPF} \cdot T_3
$$

$$
T_2 = T_1 + T_3 - \text{BPF}(T_1 + T_3)
$$

$$
T_2 - T_1 = (1 - \text{BPF})(T_3 - T_1)
$$

Since $(1 - \text{BPF}) \to \text{contact factor}$:

$$
\boxed{T_2 = T_1 + (1 - \text{BPF})(T_3 - T_1)}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\boxed{\text{BPF} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** Outside air at $5^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $W_1 = 0.002 \text{ kg/kg da}$ is mixed with return air at $22^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $W_2 = 0.0085 \text{ kg/kg da}$ in the ratio $1:3$ (OA:RA by mass). The mixed air is passed through a heating coil with $\text{BPF} = 0.2$, and then through a steam humidifier. The required supply condition is $25^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $W_3 = 0.0105 \text{ kg/kg da}$. Calculate:
> (a) Mixed air condition
> (b) Required heating coil surface temperature
> (c) Steam to be added per kg of dry air
> (d) Heating load per kg of dry air

**Given:**
- $T_o = 5^\circ\text{C}$, $W_o = 0.002$, $T_r = 22^\circ\text{C}$, $W_r = 0.0085$
- Mixing ratio OA:RA = $1:3$ → $m_o = 1$, $m_r = 3$, total $m = 4$
- $\text{BPF} = 0.2$
- $T_{\text{supply}} = 25^\circ\text{C}$, $W_{\text{supply}} = 0.0105$

#### Step (a) Mixed Air Condition

$$
T_m = \frac{m_o T_o + m_r T_r}{m_o + m_r} = \frac{1 \times 5 + 3 \times 22}{4}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{5 + 66}{4} = \frac{71}{4} = 17.75^\circ\text{C}
$$

$$
W_m = \frac{m_o W_o + m_r W_r}{m_o + m_r} = \frac{1 \times 0.002 + 3 \times 0.0085}{4}
$$

$$
W_m = \frac{0.002 + 0.0255}{4} = \frac{0.0275}{4} = 0.006875 \text{ kg/kg da}
$$

**Mixed Air State:** $T_m = 17.75^\circ\text{C}$, $W_m = 0.006875$.

#### Step (b) Required Heating Coil Surface Temperature

After heating, the air leaves at $T_h$ (before humidification). Assume humidification does not change DBT (steam injection). Thus $T_h = T_{\text{supply}} = 25^\circ\text{C}$.

$$
\text{BPF} = \frac{T_h - T_m}{T_s - T_m}
$$

$$
0.2 = \frac{25 - 17.75}{T_s - 17.75}
$$

$$
T_s - 17.75 = \frac{7.25}{0.2} = 36.25
$$

$$
\boxed{T_s = 53.75^\circ\text{C}}
$$

#### Step (c) Steam to be Added per kg of Dry Air

$$
\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a (W_{\text{supply}} - W_m) = 1 \times (0.0105 - 0.006875)
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_w = 0.003625 \text{ kg steam per kg of dry air}}
$$

#### Step (d) Heating Load per kg of Dry Air

$$
Q_s = m_a c_p (T_h - T_m) = 1 \times 1.005 \times (25 - 17.75)
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_s = 7.287 \text{ kJ per kg of dry air}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Process | Constant | Property that Changes | Sensible/Latent |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heating | $W$ | DBT increases | Sensible |
| Steam humidification | DBT (approx) | $W$ increases | Latent (pure moisture addition) |
| Spray humidification | RHB | $W$ ↑, DBT ↓ | Latent + Evaporative cooling |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System Architecture — Winter AC with Recirculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[OUTSIDE AIR\nCold Dry 5C, W0.002] --> DMP1[FRESH AIR DAMPER]
    A2[RETURN AIR\nFrom Room 22C, W0.0085] --> DMP2[RETURN AIR DAMPER]
    DMP1 --> MIX[MIXING CHAMBER\nTm 17.75C]
    DMP2 --> MIX
    MIX --> FLT[FILTER\nDust Removal]
    FLT --> HEAT[HEATING COIL\nSensible Heat Qs]
    HEAT --> HUM[HUMIDIFIER\nSteam Injection mw]
    HUM --> FAN[SUPPLY FAN\nBlower]
    FAN --> RM[CONDITIONED ROOM\nComfort 21C 50 percent RH]
    RM --> DMP2
```

### 4.2 System Architecture — Winter AC with Bypass Damper

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ODA[OUTDOOR AIR Inlet] --> DMP3[BYPASS DAMPER]
    DMP3 --> PTH1[Path A Bypass Route]
    DMP3 --> PTH2[Path B Through Coil]
    PTH1 --> MIX2[MIXING PLAENUM]
    PTH2 --> COIL[HEATING COIL Ts 53.75C]
    COIL --> MIX2
    MIX2 --> HUM2[STEAM HUMIDIFIER]
    HUM2 --> BLWR[FAN]
    BLWR --> ROOM2[CONDITIONED SPACE]
```

### 4.3 Process Path on Psychrometric Chart (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["State 1\nOutside Air\n5C, W0.002"] --> S2["State M\nMixed Air\n17.75C, W0.0069"]
    S2 --> S3["State 3\nHeated Air\n25C, W0.0069"]
    S3 --> S4["State 4\nAfter Humidifier\n25C, W0.0105"]
    S4 --> S5["State R\nRoom Condition\n21C, W0.0085"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Block of Operational Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    BLOCK_IN[Air Inlet Condition] --> BLOCK_CTRL{Is Supply Temp\nAbove Setpoint}
    BLOCK_CTRL -- YES --> BLOCK_BYP[Open Bypass Damper More]
    BLOCK_CTRL -- NO --> BLOCK_CLOSE[Close Bypass Damper]
    BLOCK_BYP --> BLOCK_OUT[Conditioned Supply Air]
    BLOCK_CLOSE --> BLOCK_OUT
    BLOCK_HUM[Humidifier Logic] --> BLOCK_OUT
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
*What is a winter air-conditioning system? Why is humidification essential in it?*

**Model Answer:**  
A winter air-conditioning system is designed to maintain indoor comfort during cold weather by **heating** and **humidifying** the supply air. Outdoor air in winter is cold and dry; heating alone would cause discomfort due to excessive dryness (skin irritation, throat problems, static discharge). Humidification restores the moisture content, ensuring the relative humidity stays in the comfort range of **40%–60%**. The reference comfort condition is **$21^\circ\text{C}$ DBT, 50% RH**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
*Define Bypass Factor (BPF) of a heating coil and write its expression.*

**Model Answer:**  
The Bypass Factor of a heating coil is defined as the ratio of the **unheated air that bypasses the coil** to the **total air passing through the coil section**. Mathematically,

$$
\text{BPF} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_1}
$$

where $T_1$ = inlet DBT, $T_2$ = leaving DBT, $T_3$ = coil surface temperature. BPF is **dimensionless** and lies between 0 and 1. Lower BPF indicates better coil effectiveness.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

> Outside air at $4^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $W = 0.0018 \text{ kg/kg da}$ is mixed with recirculated air at $24^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $W = 0.0092 \text{ kg/kg da}$ in the ratio $1:2$ by mass. The mixture is heated and then humidified using steam to a final supply condition of $26^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $W = 0.0110 \text{ kg/kg da}$. The heating coil has a Bypass Factor of $0.25$. Determine:
> **(a)** The condition of mixed air and the sensible heat added by the heating coil per kg of dry air. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The required coil surface temperature and the mass of steam injected per kg of dry air. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T_o = 4^\circ\text{C}$, $W_o = 0.0018$; $T_r = 24^\circ\text{C}$, $W_r = 0.0092$; ratio OA:RA = $1:2$; supply $T_s = 26^\circ\text{C}$, $W_s = 0.0110$; BPF = $0.25$; $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.

**Part (a): Mixed Air Condition & Sensible Heat**

Mixed DBT:

$$
T_m = \frac{1 \times 4 + 2 \times 24}{3} = \frac{4 + 48}{3} = \frac{52}{3} = 17.33^\circ\text{C}
$$

Mixed humidity ratio:

$$
W_m = \frac{1 \times 0.0018 + 2 \times 0.0092}{3} = \frac{0.0018 + 0.0184}{3} = \frac{0.0202}{3} = 0.00673 \text{ kg/kg da}
$$

Sensible heat added (heating raises DBT from $17.33^\circ\text{C}$ to $26^\circ\text{C}$):

$$
Q_s = m_a \cdot c_p \cdot (T_s - T_m) = 1 \times 1.005 \times (26 - 17.33)
$$

$$
Q_s = 1.005 \times 8.67 = 8.71 \text{ kJ per kg of dry air}
$$

**Part (b): Coil Surface Temperature & Steam Mass**

For the heating coil (humidification after heating is assumed to be pure steam — no DBT change):

$$
\text{BPF} = \frac{T_s - T_m}{T_{\text{coil}} - T_m}
$$

$$
0.25 = \frac{26 - 17.33}{T_{\text{coil}} - 17.33}
$$

$$
T_{\text{coil}} - 17.33 = \frac{8.67}{0.25} = 34.68
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{coil}} = 52.01^\circ\text{C}}
$$

Mass of steam per kg of dry air:

$$
m_w = W_s - W_m = 0.0110 - 0.00673 = 0.00427 \text{ kg steam per kg da}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_w = 0.00427 \text{ kg steam per kg of dry air}}
$$

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

> A winter air-conditioning system supplies $1000 \text{ kg/hr}$ of dry air at $30^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $W = 0.012 \text{ kg/kg da}$. The outdoor air enters at $8^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $W = 0.0035 \text{ kg/kg da}$. The return air is at $22^\circ\text{C}$ DBT and $W = 0.0085 \text{ kg/kg da}$. The heating coil has BPF = $0.3$. Steam is added after the heating coil. Determine:
> **(a)** The proportion of fresh air and return air, and the mixed air condition. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The heating coil surface temperature and the total heating load in kW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\dot{m}_a = 1000 \text{ kg/hr}$, supply $T_s = 30^\circ\text{C}$, $W_s = 0.012$; OA: $T_o = 8^\circ\text{C}$, $W_o = 0.0035$; RA: $T_r = 22^\circ\text{C}$, $W_r = 0.0085$; BPF = $0.3$.

**Part (a): Fresh Air Fraction & Mixed Air Condition**

Let $x$ = fraction of fresh air. Mass balance on humidity ratio (since heating does not change $W$, and assuming pure steam addition after heating):

$$
W_s = x W_o + (1-x) W_r \text{ ... (not valid; humidification changes W)}
$$

**Correct approach:** Mixed air passes through heating coil (constant $W$), then steam is added. So the mixed air $W_m = W_{\text{after heating}} = $ supply $W$ minus steam addition.

For mass balance between OA, RA and the air just before steam injection:

$$
\dot{m}_a W_m = \dot{m}_o W_o + \dot{m}_r W_r
$$

Let $\dot{m}_o = x \dot{m}_a$, $\dot{m}_r = (1-x) \dot{m}_a$:

$$
W_m = x W_o + (1-x) W_r
$$

Assume steam humidification adds moisture **after** heating and does not change DBT, so $T_{\text{after heating}} = T_s = 30^\circ\text{C}$. Similarly $W_m = W_{\text{after heating}}$.

We have two unknowns ($x$ and $W_m$). Apply DBT balance:

$$
T_m = x T_o + (1-x) T_r = 30^\circ\text{C} \text{ ... (since T_m = T after heating = T_s)}
$$

$$
30 = 8x + 22(1-x) = 8x + 22 - 22x = 22 - 14x
$$

$$
14x = -8 \rightarrow x = -0.571
$$

This negative value indicates that the heating coil **raises** DBT — so $T_m < T_s$. The correct relationship is:

$$
T_m = x T_o + (1-x) T_r
$$

$$
x = \frac{T_r - T_m}{T_r - T_o}
$$

Need $T_m$. Using BPF:

$$
T_s - T_m = \text{BPF}(T_{\text{coil}} - T_m)
$$

Without $T_{\text{coil}}$ directly given, we use mass balance:

Solving simultaneously:

Assume $T_{\text{coil}} = 55^\circ\text{C}$ (typical). Then:

$$
T_s - T_m = 0.3 (55 - T_m)
$$

$$
30 - T_m = 16.5 - 0.3 T_m
$$

$$
30 - 16.5 = T_m - 0.3 T_m = 0.7 T_m
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{13.5}{0.7} = 19.29^\circ\text{C}
$$

Fraction of fresh air:

$$
x = \frac{T_r - T_m}{T_r - T_o} = \frac{22 - 19.29}{22 - 8} = \frac{2.71}{14} = 0.1936 \approx 19.36\%
$$

Mixed humidity ratio:

$$
W_m = x W_o + (1-x) W_r = 0.1936 \times 0.0035 + 0.8064 \times 0.0085
$$

$$
W_m = 0.000678 + 0.006854 = 0.00753 \text{ kg/kg da}
$$

**Part (b): Coil Surface Temperature and Heating Load**

Coil surface temperature (already assumed = $55^\circ\text{C}$; verifying):

$$
T_{\text{coil}} = T_m + \frac{T_s - T_m}{\text{BPF}} = 19.29 + \frac{30 - 19.29}{0.3} = 19.29 + 35.7 = 54.99 \approx 55^\circ\text{C} \checkmark
$$

Mass flow rate of dry air:

$$
\dot{m}_a = \frac{1000}{3600} = 0.2778 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Heating load:

$$
\dot{Q}_s = \dot{m}_a c_p (T_s - T_m) = 0.2778 \times 1.005 \times (30 - 19.29)
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_s = 0.2778 \times 1.005 \times 10.71 = 2.989 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_s \approx 2.99 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Mixing humidity ratio after steam addition** — Steam is added *after* heating; therefore mixed air $W_m = W$ at coil exit, NOT the final supply $W$.
> 2. **Confusing ADP and Coil Surface Temp** — In heating coils, the coil surface temperature = ADP (since the process is purely sensible, $\Delta W = 0$).
> 3. **Wrong direction of BPF** — BPF for heating coil uses $(T_2 - T_1)$ where $T_2 > T_1$ — students sometimes invert the formula.
> 4. **Forgetting $c_p = 1.005$** — Always use $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ for dry air in sensible heat calculations; using 1.006 (moist air approx) loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Units mismatch** — Express final answer in kW (or TR for cooling, but here kW for heating).
> 6. **Missing constant humidity line** — On the psychrometric chart, **heating is a horizontal line**; not following this loses process-path marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **Winter AC = Heating + Humidification** (not just heating).
- 🎯 **Reference comfort condition:** $21^\circ\text{C}$ DBT, $50\%$ RH ($\omega = 0.0085 \text{ kg/kg da}$).
- 🎯 **Heating** is a **sensible** process — line is horizontal on psychrometric chart.
- 🎯 **Steam humidification** is a **pure latent** process — DBT unchanged, $\omega$ increases.
- 🎯 **BPF formula (heating):** $\text{BPF} = (T_2 - T_1)/(T_s - T_1)$.
- 🎯 **Sensible heat:** $Q_s = m_a c_p \Delta T$ with $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.
- 🎯 **Steam required:** $m_w = m_a (W_2 - W_1)$ kg per kg da.
- 🎯 **Mixed air condition:** Mass-weighted average of OA and RA.
- 🎯 **Bypass damper** allows energy-efficient part-load operation.
- 🎯 **Process sequence on chart:** State 1 (OA) → State M (Mix) → State 2 (Heated) → State 3 (Humidified) → State R (Room).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Year round air-conditioning systems - Representation on the psychrometric chart and estimation of quantities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET751 - REFRIGERATION AND AIRCONDITIONING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Year round air-conditioning systems - Representation on the psychrometric chart and estimation of quantities ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal KTU 2024 Definition A **Year-Round Air-Conditioning System** is a centralized air-handling installation designed to maintain prescribed indoor conditions of temperature, relative humidity, air purity, and air motion in an enclosed space during **all seasons of the year** — namely summer (high temperature, high humidity), winter (low temperature, low humidity), and the monsoon seasons (moderate temperature, very high humidity). The system is capable of simultaneously performing **sensible cooling or heating, latent cooling (dehumidification) or latent heating (humidification), and filtration** as the atmospheric load conditions dictate. The system is often called an "all-seasons air conditioning plant" and uses the **psychrometric chart as a graphical computational tool** to size the apparatus (cooling coil, heating coil, spray washer, reheater) and to determine the **quantities of air, water, heating steam, refrigeration, and reheat energy** required. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):** > A year-round AC system is the **superset** of summer AC and winter AC. The student is expected to draw the **complete plant schematic** (with dampers, filters, preheater, washer, reheater, fans) and trace **summer, winter, and monsoon cycles** on a single psychrometric chart, then compute the mass flow rates of air and water for the specified design conditions. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **smart wardrobe** that automatically adjusts its internal climate for your clothes: - In **summer**, you want a cool, dry compartment (think: refrigerator mode with moisture removal). - In **winter**, you want a warm, slightly moist compartment (think: room heater with a steam kettle). - In **monsoon**, you want it cool but extremely dry (think: air conditioner running at maximum dehumidification). A year-round AC system is this "smart wardrobe" for a building. It mixes outdoor air with the air already in the room, then passes it through a chain of "machines" (filter → heater/washer → cooler → reheater) until it becomes perfectly suited to the room's needs. The **psychrometric chart** is the **map of this climate transformation** — every dot on the map is a "weather condition" and every line is a "treatment process." ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Specific heat of dry air: **$c_{pa} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$** - Specific heat of water vapour: **$c_{pw} = 1.88 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$** - Approximate specific heat of moist air: **$c_{pm} = 1.005 + 1.88 \, W$ (kJ/kg dry air·K)** - Latent heat of vaporisation at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$: **$h_{fg} = 2501 \text{ kJ/kg}$** (often taken as **$h_{fg} = 2470 \text{ kJ/kg}$** at room temperature) - Standard human comfort: **$25^{\circ}\text{C DBT}$, $50\% \text{ RH}$** (Inside Design Condition – IDC) > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Context:** Year-round AC systems are mandatory in **hospitals, operation theatres, server rooms, semiconductor fabs, pharmaceutical clean rooms, and luxury hotels** where indoor conditions must remain stable irrespective of the outdoor season. The capital cost is high but the *energy bills* are dominated by **refrigeration (TR)** in summer and **steam (kg/hr)** in winter, both of which are computed using the psychrometric method described in this note. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Year-Round AC Plant A typical central plant (in the order of airflow) contains: 1. **Outdoor (Fresh) Air Damper** – admits ventilation air. 2. **Return Air Damper + Mixing Box** – blends fresh and return air. 3. **Pre-Filter + Fine Filter** – removes dust/bacteria. 4. **Pre-Heater (Winter Use)** – warms incoming air above $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to prevent washer freezing. 5. **Spray Washer (Adiabatic Saturator / Cooling & Humidifying Tower)** – sprays recirculated water for sensible cooling + humidification (winter) or for contact cooling + dehumidification (summer, if chilled water is sprayed). 6. **Eliminator Plates (Baffles)** – removes entrained water droplets. 7. **Cooling Coil (Chilled Water / DX / Brine)** – cools and dehumidifies the air in summer. 8. **Re-Heater (Steam Coil or Electric)** – raises the temperature of supply air to the desired value without adding moisture (sensible-only heating). 9. **Humidifier (Steam Pan / Atomiser)** – adds moisture in winter to reach desired RH. 10. **Supply Fan + Motor** – drives the air through ducts. 11. **Return Fan + Motor** – pulls used air from the room. 12. **Automatic Dampers + Control Valves** – modulated by thermostat, humidistat, and dew-point sensor. ### 2.2 Working Philosophy — Why Each Block Exists - **Why a Mixing Box?** It saves energy by reusing conditioned air; only a small fraction of fresh air is required for **ventilation ($V_{fresh} \geq 8.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ per person, ASHRAE 62.1)**. - **Why a Pre-Heater in Winter?** When outdoor air is at $-5^{\circ}\text{C}$, spraying water on it would form **ice on the eliminators** and freeze the chilled-water coil if back-up cooling is on. Pre-heating to $5\text{–}7^{\circ}\text{C}$ eliminates this risk. - **Why a Re-Heater after the Cooling Coil?** Pure cooling-coil output is *over-cooled* (to remove moisture) — this would be uncomfortably cold in the room. The reheater restores the **dry-bulb temperature** to the desired supply value *without* changing the moisture content. This is called the **"cooling and reheat"** process and is *the* signature operation of a comfort-AC plant. - **Why Bypass Factor matters?** A real cooling coil does not cool the air down to its **Apparatus Dew Point (ADP)** — a fraction of the air slips through untreated. This fraction, $\text{BPF}$, governs the leaving air condition and is essential for **sizing the coil and the tonnage of refrigeration**. ### 2.3 Psychrometric Processes Used in Year-Round AC | # | Process on Chart | What Happens Thermodynamically | Where in the Plant | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Mixing of two air streams** (rectilinear) | Conservation of mass & enthalpy between OA & RA | Mixing Box / Plenum | | 2 | **Sensible Heating** (horizontal line, $W$ constant) | DBT ↑ , WBT ↑ , RH ↓ | Pre-Heater, Re-Heater | | 3 | **Sensible Cooling** (horizontal line, $W$ constant) | DBT ↓ , WBT ↓ , RH ↑ | Chilled water coil, DX coil | | 4 | **Cooling & Dehumidification** (line sloping from inlet to ADP) | DBT ↓, W ↓ | Cooling coil in summer | | 5 | **Adiabatic Humidification** (along WBT line / constant $h$ approximately) | DBT ↓, W ↑ | Spray washer (summer & winter) | | 6 | **Heating & Humidification** | DBT ↑, W ↑ | Pre-heater + humidifier in winter | | 7 | **Room Process** (heat & moisture load) | DBT ↑, W ↑ (summer); DBT ↓, W ↓ (winter) | Space being conditioned | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below use **dry air as the basis** ($kg_{da}/\text{s}$). This is the **KTU 2024 convention** for all psychrometric calculations. Always specify the basis in your answer script for full marks. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Mass flow rate of dry air from sensible heat | $\dot{m}_{a} = \dfrac{Q_{s}}{c_{p}(t_{R} - t_{S})}$ | kg/s | $t_R > t_S$ in summer | | 2 | Mass flow rate of dry air from latent heat | $\dot{m}_{a} = \dfrac{Q_{l}}{h_{fg}(W_{R} - W_{S})}$ | kg/s | $W_R > W_S$ in summer | | 3 | Mass flow rate of water to be supplied (humidification) | $\dot{m}_{w} = \dot{m}_{a}(W_{2} - W_{1})$ | kg/s | $W_2 > W_1$ | | 4 | Mass flow rate of water removed (dehumidification) | $\dot{m}_{w} = \dot{m}_{a}(W_{1} - W_{2})$ | kg/s | $W_1 > W_2$ | | 5 | Total enthalpy change of air | $\Delta h = c_{p}(t_{2} - t_{1}) + h_{fg}(W_{2} - W_{1})$ | kJ/kg | Used for coil load | | 6 | Sensible Heat Factor | $\text{SHF} = \dfrac{Q_{s}}{Q_{s} + Q_{l}} = \dfrac{c_{p}\Delta t}{c_{p}\Delta t + h_{fg}\Delta W}$ | dimensionless | $\text{SHF} + \text{LHF} = 1$ | | 7 | Bypass Factor of a cooling coil | $\text{BPF} = \dfrac{t_{2} - t_{\text{ADP}}}{t_{1} - t_{\text{ADP}}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \text{BPF} \le 1$ | | 8 | Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) for cooling coil | Solve $h_{2} = h_{\text{ADP}}$ on saturation curve | °C | Condition 2 on $\phi = 100\%$ line | | 9 | Cooling coil load (Total) | $Q_{\text{coil}} = \dot{m}_{a}(h_{1} - h_{2})$ | kW | $h_1 > h_2$ | | 10 | Re-heater load (Sensible only) | $Q_{\text{reheat}} = \dot{m}_{a} \cdot c_{p}(t_{H} - t_{C})$ | kW | $W$ constant | | 11 | Pre-heater load (Winter) | $Q_{\text{pre}} = \dot{m}_{a} \cdot c_{p}(t_{P} - t_{\text{mix}})$ | kW | $W$ constant | | 12 | Humidifier load (Steam) | $Q_{\text{steam}} = \dot{m}_{a} \cdot h_{fg,steam}(W_{2} - W_{1})$ | kW | Live steam quality assumed | | 13 | Refrigeration Tonnage Required | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{coil}}}{3.517}$ | TR | $1 \text{ TR} = 3.517 \text{ kW}$ | | 14 | Specific Volume of Moist Air | $v = \dfrac{R_{a}T}{p_{a}}$ where $p_{a} = p_{t} - p_{v}$ | m³/kg da | Ideal-gas psychrometric relation | | 15 | Volumetric flow rate of supply air | $\dot{V} = \dot{m}_{a} \cdot v$ | m³/s | Used for duct sizing | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The **SHF (Sensible Heat Factor)** is the single most important number a consulting HVAC engineer computes first. It tells the designer which type of AC system to choose — a *fan-coil unit* (SHF $\ge 0.8$), a *high-velocity induction system* (SHF $\approx 0.6$–$0.8$), or a *low-velocity double-duct system* (SHF $< 0.6$). The bypass factor tells the manufacturer how many rows of coil tubing to use. Both parameters are extracted directly from the psychrometric chart. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Mass Flow Rate of Air from Sensible Heat Load Consider a conditioned room of mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{a}$ kg of dry air per second. Let the supply air enter at $t_S$ °C and the return air leave at $t_R$ °C. The **sensible heat picked up by this air** in the room is: $$Q_{s} = \dot{m}_{a} \cdot c_{p} \cdot (t_{R} - t_{S})$$ Solving for the mass flow rate (which is what KTU numerical problems ask for): $$\dot{m}_{a} = \frac{Q_{s}}{c_{p}(t_{R} - t_{S})}$$ **Conversion logic step by step:** - $Q_s$ in $\text{kJ/s} = \text{kW}$ (after dividing by $1000$ if heat is given in watts). - $c_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ for dry air. - $\Delta t = t_R - t_S$ in Kelvin (= °C difference). - Result is in $\text{kg/s}$ of dry air. ### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Flow Rate of Air from Latent Heat Load The latent heat associated with moisture pickup between $W_S$ and $W_R$ is: $$Q_{l} = \dot{m}_{a} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot (W_{R} - W_{S})$$ Therefore: $$\dot{m}_{a} = \frac{Q_{l}}{h_{fg}(W_{R} - W_{S})}$$ **Cross-check:** Equating the two expressions of $\dot{m}_a$ gives the **Sensible Heat Factor**: $$\frac{Q_{s}}{c_{p}(t_{R} - t_{S})} = \frac{Q_{l}}{h_{fg}(W_{R} - W_{S})}$$ $$\therefore \frac{Q_{s}}{Q_{l}} = \frac{c_{p}(t_{R} - t_{S})}{h_{fg}(W_{R} - W_{S})}$$ $$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_{s}}{Q_{s} + Q_{l}}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) and Bypass Factor Let an air stream at state 1 enter the cooling coil and leave at state 2. The portion of the coil surface that contacts the air intimately and brings it to saturation is the *ideal* condition. The mixture of the *ideal exit air* and the *bypassed (untreated) air* is the actual exit air. Let ADP temperature be $t_{\text{ADP}}$ (and $W_{\text{ADP}}$ on the saturation line). The bypass factor is defined as: $$\text{BPF} = \frac{\text{amount of air that bypasses the coil}}{\text{total air handled}}$$ By an **enthalpy-mass balance**, it is also given by the temperature ratio (on a $h$–$t$ or psychrometric chart): $$\text{BPF} = \frac{t_{2} - t_{\text{ADP}}}{t_{1} - t_{\text{ADP}}}$$ **Step-by-step conversion to coil leaving condition:** $$t_{2} = t_{\text{ADP}} + \text{BPF} \cdot (t_{1} - t_{\text{ADP}})$$ $$W_{2} = W_{\text{ADP}} + \text{BPF} \cdot (W_{1} - W_{\text{ADP}})$$ $$h_{2} = h_{\text{ADP}} + \text{BPF} \cdot (h_{1} - h_{\text{ADP}})$$ ### 3.4 Worked Example (Model Solution Style — Sourced from KTU Pattern) **Problem (KTU December 2023 – Modified):** An auditorium of seating capacity 800 has a total summer load of $Q_s = 80{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$ sensible and $Q_l = 20{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$ latent. The outdoor air is at $35^{\circ}\text{C DBT}$, $60\% \text{ RH}$ and the room is to be maintained at $25^{\circ}\text{C DBT}$, $50\% \text{ RH}$. Fresh air supply is $20\%$ of total air circulated. The cooling coil has a BPF of $0.20$. Determine: (a) The mass flow rate of supply air and fresh air. (b) The refrigeration load on the cooling coil. (c) The re-heater load if supply air temperature is $15^{\circ}\text{C}$. (d) The spray water requirement for dehumidification. **Given Property Values (read from standard psychrometric chart or table):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{State O (Outdoor)} &: t_O = 35^{\circ}\text{C}, \phi_O = 60\% \\ &\Rightarrow W_O = 0.0218 \text{ kg/kg da}, h_O = 90.2 \text{ kJ/kg da} \\ \text{State R (Room)} &: t_R = 25^{\circ}\text{C}, \phi_R = 50\% \\ &\Rightarrow W_R = 0.0099 \text{ kg/kg da}, h_R = 50.0 \text{ kJ/kg da} \\ \text{State S (Supply)} &: t_S = 15^{\circ}\text{C} \text{ (given)}, W_S = W_C = 0.0080 \text{ kg/kg da} \\ \text{State C (Coil Exit)} &: W_C = 0.0080 \text{ kg/kg da}, \text{ADP} = 8^{\circ}\text{C} \end{aligned} $$ **Part (a): Mass flow rate of supply air** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_a &= \frac{Q_s}{c_p(t_R - t_S)} = \frac{80{,}000/3600}{1.005 \times (25 - 15)} \\ &= \frac{22.222}{10.05} = 2.211 \text{ kg/s} \end{aligned} $$ Cross-check using latent heat: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_a &= \frac{Q_l}{h_{fg}(W_R - W_S)} = \frac{20{,}000/3600}{2501 \times (0.0099 - 0.0080)} \\ &= \frac{5.556}{4.752} = 1.169 \text{ kg/s} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Note:** The two answers *do not match* because the assumed supply state $t_S = 15°C$ does not satisfy **both** sensible and latent loads simultaneously. The **correct procedure** is to use the more restrictive value, OR to perform a chart-based graphical solution where the supply condition is the *intersection* of the room process line with the coil-slope line. In this model solution we use the sensible value (dominant load). Therefore, adopt: $$\dot{m}_a = 2.21 \text{ kg/s of dry air}$$ Fresh air fraction $= 0.20$: $$\dot{m}_{\text{fresh}} = 0.20 \times 2.21 = 0.442 \text{ kg/s}$$ **Part (b): Refrigeration (Cooling Coil) Load** The coil cools the air from $t_M$ (mixed) to $t_C$ (coil exit). First, find mixed air enthalpy using: $$ h_M = 0.20 \cdot h_O + 0.80 \cdot h_R = 0.20(90.2) + 0.80(50.0) = 58.04 \text{ kJ/kg da} $$ For BPF $= 0.20$ and ADP $= 8°C$, the coil leaving enthalpy is: $$ h_C = h_{\text{ADP}} + 0.20 \cdot (h_M - h_{\text{ADP}}) $$ From saturation table, $h_{\text{ADP}}$ at $8°C$ $\approx 29.4 \text{ kJ/kg da}$ (using $h = c_p t + W h_{fg}$, with $W$ at saturation): $$ h_C = 29.4 + 0.20(58.04 - 29.4) = 29.4 + 5.728 = 35.13 \text{ kJ/kg da} $$ Cooling coil load: $$ Q_{\text{coil}} = \dot{m}_a (h_M - h_C) = 2.21 \times (58.04 - 35.13) = 2.21 \times 22.91 = 50.63 \text{ kW} $$ In **tonnes of refrigeration (TR):** $$\text{TR} = \frac{50.63}{3.517} = 14.40 \text{ TR}$$ **Part (c): Re-heater Load** The supply air must leave the reheater at $t_S = 15°C$, while the coil exit is at the temperature corresponding to $h_C = 35.13 \text{ kJ/kg da}$. To find $t_C$: For $h = 35.13 \text{ kJ/kg da}$ and $W = W_C = 0.0080 \text{ kg/kg da}$: $$t_C = \frac{h_C - W_C \cdot h_{fg}}{c_p} = \frac{35.13 - 0.0080 \times 2501}{1.005} = \frac{35.13 - 20.008}{1.005} = 15.06^{\circ}\text{C}$$ Hmm, this is *already* at the desired supply temperature. Reheater load $\approx 0 \text{ kW}$ in this case — the BPF was already set to give the right leaving condition. In general: $$ Q_{\text{reheat}} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p (t_S - t_C) = 2.21 \times 1.005 \times 0 = 0 \text{ kW} $$ (For non-coincidental cases, this term is non-zero.) **Part (d): Spray Water Requirement for Dehumidification** The water removed in the cooling coil: $$ \dot{m}_{w,\text{removed}} = \dot{m}_a (W_M - W_C) $$ We need $W_M$. By mass balance of moisture: $$W_M = 0.20 \cdot W_O + 0.80 \cdot W_R = 0.20(0.0218) + 0.80(0.0099) = 0.01228 \text{ kg/kg da}$$ Therefore: $$\dot{m}_{w} = 2.21 \times (0.01228 - 0.0080) = 2.21 \times 0.00428 = 9.46 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$ Or in $\text{kg/hr}$: $$\dot{m}_{w} = 0.00946 \times 3600 = 34.05 \text{ kg/hr}$$ ### 3.5 Summary Table of Computed Quantities | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Mass flow of dry air | $\dot{m}_a$ | $2.21$ | kg/s | | Mass flow of fresh air | $\dot{m}_{\text{fresh}}$ | $0.442$ | kg/s | | Mixed air enthalpy | $h_M$ | $58.04$ | kJ/kg da | | Coil exit enthalpy | $h_C$ | $35.13$ | kJ/kg da | | Cooling coil load | $Q_{\text{coil}}$ | $50.63$ | kW | | Refrigeration tonnage | TR | $14.40$ | TR | | Re-heater load | $Q_{\text{reheat}}$ | $\approx 0$ | kW | | Dehumidification water | $\dot{m}_w$ | $34.05$ | kg/hr | ## 4.
