The block diagram shows the **hardware** that realises these processes; the psychrometric chart shows the **mathematical map** of state changes. The KTU 2024 exam expects you to draw **both** — the hardware schematic for 4–5 marks and the chart representation for 4–5 marks in a 14-mark question. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions #### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – July 2023] **Define a year-round air-conditioning system. List the essential components used in such a system.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A year-round air-conditioning system is an air-handling installation that maintains desired indoor conditions of temperature, relative humidity, air purity, and motion throughout **all seasons** (summer, winter, monsoon) by performing simultaneous heating, cooling, humidification, dehumidification, and filtration as required. **Essential components (1½ marks):** 1. Mixing box with fresh and return air dampers 2. Pre-filter and fine filter 3. Pre-heater (for winter operation) 4. Spray washer / humidifier 5. Cooling coil (chilled water / DX) 6. Re-heater 7. Eliminator plates 8. Supply and return fans 9. Controls — thermostat, humidistat, motorized dampers (Any 5 components = 1½ marks; definition = 1½ marks) #### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – December 2022] **What is meant by "cooling and reheat" process? Why is it necessary in a comfort AC system?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **cooling and reheat** process is one in which air is first passed over a cooling coil where it is cooled below the desired supply temperature to remove excess moisture (dehumidification), and then passed over a reheater to raise the temperature back to the desired supply value without adding or removing moisture. **Necessity (1½ marks):** 1. To achieve the desired *humidity ratio* (low RH) the air must be **over-cooled** below its dew point. 2. The resulting over-cooled air would be uncomfortably cold in the room. 3. **Reheating** restores the *sensible temperature* to the comfort level while keeping the moisture content unchanged. 4. It also compensates for variations in outdoor conditions and room load. (Process explanation = 1½ marks, reasons = 1½ marks) --- ### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 regulations, every Module-level question paper carries a **mandatory internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. Both questions are given below with complete model solutions. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Draw the **schematic layout of a year-round air-conditioning system** and label all components. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A public hall has a sensible heat gain of $Q_s = 65{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$ and latent heat gain of $Q_l = 18{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$. The room conditions are $24°C \text{ DBT}, 50\% \text{ RH}$. The supply air is at $14°C$ DBT, $W_S = 0.0076 \text{ kg/kg da}$. The outdoor air is at $32°C$ DBT, $55\% \text{ RH}$. Fresh air is $25\%$ of total supply. Cooling coil has $\text{BPF} = 0.15$. Determine: (i) Mass flow rate of supply air. (ii) Mixed air enthalpy and the cooling coil load in kW. (iii) Spray water removal rate. **(7 marks)** **Property values to use (from standard psychrometric chart):** $$ \begin{aligned} W_O &= 0.0160 \text{ kg/kg da}, \quad h_O = 76.0 \text{ kJ/kg da} \\ W_R &= 0.0094 \text{ kg/kg da}, \quad h_R = 47.9 \text{ kJ/kg da} \end{aligned} $$ **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** A neat labelled schematic showing: *Return air damper → Mixing box → Pre-filter → Fine filter → Pre-heater → Spray washer with pump and eliminator → Cooling coil → Re-heater → Humidifier → Supply fan → Conditioned room → Fresh air damper at the entry.* All 5–7 components must be labelled to get full marks. *(Diagram 4 marks, labelling 3 marks)* **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **(i) Mass flow rate of supply air (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_a &= \frac{Q_s}{c_p(t_R - t_S)} = \frac{65{,}000/3600}{1.005 \times (24 - 14)} \\ &= \frac{18.056}{10.05} = 1.797 \text{ kg/s} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) Mixed air enthalpy and coil load (3 marks):** $$ h_M = 0.25 \cdot h_O + 0.75 \cdot h_R = 0.25(76.0) + 0.75(47.9) = 19.0 + 35.925 = 54.93 \text{ kJ/kg da} $$ For BPF $= 0.15$ and assuming $\text{ADP} \approx 7°C$, $h_{\text{ADP}} \approx 27.5 \text{ kJ/kg da}$: $$ h_C = 27.5 + 0.15(54.93 - 27.5) = 27.5 + 4.12 = 31.62 \text{ kJ/kg da} $$ $$ Q_{\text{coil}} = \dot{m}_a (h_M - h_C) = 1.797 \times (54.93 - 31.62) = 1.797 \times 23.31 = 41.89 \text{ kW} $$ **(iii) Spray water removal rate (2 marks):** $$ W_M = 0.25 W_O + 0.75 W_R = 0.25(0.0160) + 0.75(0.0094) = 0.0040 + 0.00705 = 0.01105 \text{ kg/kg da} $$ Coil exit humidity $W_C$: At $h_C = 31.62$ and assuming temperature near supply $t_C \approx 14°C$: $$ W_C = \frac{h_C - c_p t_C}{h_{fg}} = \frac{31.62 - 1.005 \times 14}{2501} = \frac{31.62 - 14.07}{2501} = \frac{17.55}{2501} = 0.00702 \text{ kg/kg da} $$ $$ \dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a (W_M - W_C) = 1.797 \times (0.01105 - 0.00702) = 1.797 \times 0.00403 = 0.00724 \text{ kg/s} $$ In $\text{kg/hr}$: $\dot{m}_w = 0.00724 \times 3600 = 26.07 \text{ kg/hr}$ **Final Answer Box — Question A:** - $\dot{m}_a = 1.797 \text{ kg/s}$ - $h_M = 54.93 \text{ kJ/kg da}$, $Q_{\text{coil}} = 41.89 \text{ kW}$ - $\dot{m}_w = 26.07 \text{ kg/hr}$ (dehumidification) --- #### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – December 2023] **(a)** Represent the **summer air-conditioning cycle** on a psychrometric chart, clearly labelling all the state points and the processes. Derive the expression for **Bypass Factor (BPF)** of a cooling coil. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A conference room requires the following winter conditions: Indoor $22°C \text{ DBT}, 45\% \text{ RH}$; Outdoor $8°C \text{ DBT}, 30\% \text{ RH}$. The total heating load is $Q_s = 45{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$ sensible and $Q_l = 6{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$ latent. Fresh air is $30\%$ of total supply. Determine: (i) Mass flow rate of supply air. (ii) Mass of water evaporated in the humidifier per hour. (iii) Capacity of the pre-heater in kW if mixed air temperature is raised from $t_M$ to $15°C$ before the washer. **(7 marks)** **Property values to use:** $$ \begin{aligned} W_O &= 0.0019 \text{ kg/kg da}, \quad h_O = 12.8 \text{ kJ/kg da} \\ W_R &= 0.0073 \text{ kg/kg da}, \quad h_R = 40.4 \text{ kJ/kg da} \\ W_S &= W_O = 0.0019 \text{ kg/kg da (if no humidity added) OR set by chart intersection} \end{aligned} $$ For this winter problem, supply $W_S$ is determined by room process and coil/washer. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** *Psychrometric chart representation (4 marks):* - Plot Point O (outdoor $32–35°C, 60\%$) - Plot Point R (room $25°C, 50\%$) - Plot Point M on straight line OR joining O and R, at 20% from R (i.e., 0.20 O + 0.80 R) - Process M → C: cooling and dehumidification along line from M to ADP - Process C → S: re-heat (sensible) horizontally to supply state - Process S → R: room sensible + latent heat gain (slope = SHF) - Label all axes (DBT, W, RH lines, WBT) *BPF derivation (3 marks):* Let total mass flow $= \dot{m}_a$ kg/s. Of this, fraction $(1 - \text{BPF})$ is cooled to ADP and fraction BPF remains at inlet temperature. By enthalpy balance: $$ \dot{m}_a h_2 = \text{BPF} \cdot \dot{m}_a h_1 + (1 - \text{BPF}) \cdot \dot{m}_a h_{\text{ADP}} $$ Rearranging: $$ h_2 - h_{\text{ADP}} = \text{BPF} \cdot (h_1 - h_{\text{ADP}}) $$ Since for sensible-dominant cooling, the same relation holds for DBT: $$ \boxed{\text{BPF} = \frac{h_2 - h_{\text{ADP}}}{h_1 - h_{\text{ADP}}} = \frac{t_2 - t_{\text{ADP}}}{t_1 - t_{\text{ADP}}}} $$ **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **(i) Mass flow rate of supply air (2 marks):** For winter, $t_R > t_S$ (room warmer than supply). The room loses sensible heat to the cold supply air: $$ \dot{m}_a = \frac{Q_s}{c_p(t_R - t_S)} $$ Assume $t_S = 15°C$ (typical winter supply): $$ \dot{m}_a = \frac{45{,}000/3600}{1.005 \times (22 - 15)} = \frac{12.5}{7.035} = 1.777 \text{ kg/s} $$ **(ii) Mass of water evaporated in humidifier (2 marks):** The room needs latent heat $= 6{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr}$. From latent heat: $$ \dot{m}_a = \frac{Q_l}{h_{fg}(W_R - W_S)} \Rightarrow W_R - W_S = \frac{Q_l}{\dot{m}_a h_{fg}} $$ $$ W_R - W_S = \frac{6000/3600}{1.777 \times 2501} = \frac{1.667}{4444.3} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/kg da} $$ Therefore $W_S = W_R - 3.75 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0073 - 0.000375 = 0.00693 \text{ kg/kg da}$ The fresh air moisture is $W_O = 0.0019 \text{ kg/kg da}$ — much drier. The spray washer + humidifier must raise the moisture to $W_S$. Mass of water added per kg dry air $= W_S - W_O = 0.00693 - 0.0019 = 0.00503 \text{ kg/kg da}$ *(if all moisture came from the washer)* However, in winter the **mixed air** is the actual inlet to the washer: $$ W_M = 0.30 W_O + 0.70 W_R = 0.30(0.0019) + 0.70(0.0073) = 0.00057 + 0.00511 = 0.00568 \text{ kg/kg da} $$ Moisture to be added to reach $W_S$: $$ \Delta W = W_S - W_M = 0.00693 - 0.00568 = 0.00125 \text{ kg/kg da} $$ Total water evaporated per hour: $$ \dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a \cdot \Delta W \cdot 3600 = 1.777 \times 0.00125 \times 3600 = 7.99 \text{ kg/hr} $$ **(iii) Capacity of pre-heater (3 marks):** $$ t_M = 0.30 \cdot t_O + 0.70 \cdot t_R = 0.30(8) + 0.70(22) = 2.4 + 15.4 = 17.8°C $$ Pre-heater must raise mixed air to $15°C$? That's a *cooling* operation — but in winter the mixed air is *already* above $15°C$. So the pre-heater is needed **only if the outdoor air is very cold** (e.g., $t_O < 10°C$) and the mixed air falls below $5°C$ (to prevent washer icing). For this problem, the mixed air at $17.8°C$ is fine; pre-heater load $= 0$ kW. If hypothetically the pre-heater *was* needed: $$ Q_{\text{pre}} = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_p \cdot (t_P - t_M) = 1.777 \times 1.005 \times (15 - 17.8) = 1.777 \times 1.005 \times (-2.8) = -5.0 \text{ kW} $$ The negative sign indicates *cooling* is required (or the pre-heater is bypassed). Conclusion: **pre-heater is not active in this mild-winter scenario**. **Final Answer Box — Question B:** - $\dot{m}_a = 1.777 \text{ kg/s}$ - $\dot{m}_w = 7.99 \text{ kg/hr}$ - $Q_{\text{pre}} \approx 0 \text{ kW}$ (mixed air already above $15°C$) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Year-Round AC):** > > 1. **Mixing vs Sensible Heat:** Many students confuse *mixing* of OA and RA (a straight line on the chart) with the *room sensible heat process* (a slope equal to SHF). Always remember: **mixing is weighted by mass flow fraction**; **room process is weighted by $Q_s$ and $Q_l$**. > > 2. **Forgetting to include Fresh Air:** When the problem says "20% fresh air", students often compute the mass flow from $Q_s$ alone but use the *return air* properties to find mixed air properties. The mixed air enthalpy and humidity must be weighted by **$\dot{m}_O = 0.20 \dot{m}_a$** and **$\dot{m}_R = 0.80 \dot{m}_a$**. > > 3. **BPF Misapplication:** The BPF formula $\text{BPF} = (t_2 - t_{\text{ADP}})/(t_1 - t_{\text{ADP}})$ is *valid only when the process is along a straight line* on the chart. If the process is strongly curved, use the **enthalpy form** $\text{BPF} = (h_2 - h_{\text{ADP}})/(h_1 - h_{\text{ADP}})$. > > 4. **Sign of $W$ direction:** In winter, $W_S > W_M$ (humidification); in summer, $W_M > W_C$ (dehumidification). Always show the **direction of water transfer** explicitly — a common source of −2 marks. > > 5. **Units of $\dot{m}_a$:** KTU expects either $\text{kg/s}$ or $\text{kg/hr}$ — *be consistent*. Don't mix $\text{kg/s}$ for $\dot{m}_a$ and $\text{kg/hr}$ for $\dot{m}_w$ in the same answer block. > > 6. **ADP Determination:** When BPF is given, you can *back-calculate* $h_{\text{ADP}}$ and the coil leaving state; when BPF is **not** given, you must *read* the ADP from the intersection of the room process line and the saturation curve. The latter is more common in KTU university papers. > > 7. **Summer vs Winter Process Labelling:** A common mistake is to draw the **winter cycle** when the question asks for summer, or vice versa. Always begin by writing *"Summer cycle"* or *"Winter cycle"* as a header on the chart. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [x] **Year-round AC** = combination of summer AC (cooling + dehumidification) **AND** winter AC (heating + humidification), with the *same* air-handling unit. - [x] **Key Hardware Chain:** Mixing Box → Filter → Pre-Heater (winter) → Spray Washer → Cooling Coil → Re-Heater → Humidifier (winter) → Supply Fan → Room. - [x] **Summer Psychrometric Path:** O → M → C (cooling + dehumidification) → S (re-heat) → R (room gain) → back to M. - [x] **Winter Psychrometric Path:** O → M → P (pre-heat) → W (washer/humidify) → S (re-heat) → R (room loss) → back to M. - [x] **Mixing equation (mass basis):** $h_M = x \cdot h_O + (1-x) \cdot h_R$ and $W_M = x \cdot W_O + (1-x) \cdot W_R$, where $x$ = fresh air mass fraction. - [x] **Air mass flow from sensible heat:** $\dot{m}_a = Q_s / [c_p(t_R - t_S)]$. **From latent heat:** $\dot{m}_a = Q_l / [h_{fg}(W_R - W_S)]$. - [x] **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF):** $\text{SHF} = Q_s / (Q_s + Q_l)$ — determines the slope of the room process line on the psychrometric chart. - [x] **Bypass Factor (BPF):** $\text{BPF} = (h_2 - h_{\text{ADP}})/(h_1 - h_{\text{ADP}})$. Typical comfort AC: $\text{BPF} = 0.10\text{–}0.30$. - [x] **Cooling Coil Load:** $Q_{\text{coil}} = \dot{m}_a (h_M - h_C)$. In TR: divide by 3.517. - [x] **Re-Heater Load:** $Q_{\text{reheat}} = \dot{m}_a c_p (t_S - t_C)$ — sensible only. - [x] **Pre-Heater Load (winter):** $Q_{\text{pre}} = \dot{m}_a c_p (t_P - t_M)$ — sensible only, used to prevent washer icing. - [x] **Humidifier Water:** $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a (W_S - W_M)$ in winter; **Dehumidifier water (condensate):** $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_a (W_M - W_C)$ in summer. - [x] **Standard comfort conditions (India):** $25 \pm 1°C \text{ DBT}, 50 \pm 5\% \text{ RH}$, indoor air velocity $\le 0.25 \text{ m/s}$. - [x] **Minimum fresh air:** $\ge 8.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$ per person (ASHRAE) — for comfort; higher for hospitals. - [x] **Three-decimal precision** for $W$ values in numerical answers (KTU marking scheme). - [x] **Always state your assumptions:** BPF value, fresh air percentage, $\text{ADP}$, and whether the spray washer uses chilled water or adiabatic water. - [x] **Plot to scale** on the psychrometric chart — examiners penalise for unreadable or unlabelled diagrams (−2 to −3 marks). - [x] **Box your final answer** with units — this is the **first thing** the examiner scans for awarding 80–90% of the marks.
